title: Rust rewrite: make pane orchestrator the source of tab-local session truth
description: Move authoritative tab-local workspace/session truth closer to the Rust pane orchestrator instead of re-deriving it across Nushell and Yazi integration caches. This is the highest-value next Rust rewrite slice after the helper-backed config/render-plan work because it deletes a real multi-runtime correctness seam rather than porting more renderers.
design: Align with docs/specs/cross_language_runtime_ownership.md and docs/specs/rust_migration_matrix.md: keep renderers in Nushell, but move pane identity, focus/sidebar state, and tab-local workspace truth behind a typed orchestrator-owned seam. Lua/Yazi should remain an adapter, not a second owner. The goal is to delete duplicated state derivation outside the plugin, especially where tab-locality bugs can reappear.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear plan and child slices for exposing typed orchestrator-owned tab/session state, migrating Nushell/Yazi consumers to that seam, and deleting duplicate non-authoritative owners. The backlog record makes the owner boundary explicit: pane/session truth in the plugin, renderers and user-facing UX still in Nushell.
notes: 2026-04-20 prioritization update: keep this epic as the correctness-and-complexity lane for pane/session truth, not as the primary Nushell-LOC reduction lane. The new parent for deletion-first Rust ownership and shipped Nushell reduction is yazelix-ulb2. Work here should still prefer deleting duplicate state owners, but it is justified mainly by architecture safety and tab-local correctness rather than by raw control-plane LOC removal.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Pane orchestrator: expose a typed tab-local session-state report/query seam
description: Define and implement the first typed plugin-owned read surface for current-tab workspace/session truth: workspace root, managed pane ids/roles, focused owner, and sidebar visibility state. This creates the seam other layers can consume instead of inferring those facts from scattered state files or shell-side heuristics.
design: Keep the surface narrow and contract-first. Prefer one report/query shape that can be consumed by Nushell commands and sidebar/Yazi integrations. Do not drag full CLI ownership into Rust; this is a plugin/session-truth slice only. Reuse existing pane orchestrator ownership rather than inventing a second Rust state store.
acceptance_criteria: The pane orchestrator exposes a documented typed surface for tab-local session truth with focused tests for tab locality and pane-role identity. At least the current tab workspace root, managed editor/sidebar identity, focused pane role, and sidebar open/collapsed state are represented or explicitly scoped out with recorded reasons.
notes:
2026-04-20 Composer-2 prep:
- linked spec docs/specs/pane_orchestrator_tab_local_session_state_seam.md
- choose a new stable pipe command `get_active_tab_session_state` instead of promoting `debug_editor_state`
- v1 payload: schema_version, active_tab_position, workspace {root, source}, managed_panes {editor_pane_id, sidebar_pane_id}, focus_context, layout {active_swap_layout_name, sidebar_collapsed}, sidebar_yazi {yazi_id, cwd}
- current Rust sources to start from: main.rs State maps, panes.rs DebugEditorState/FocusContext, workspace.rs WorkspaceState, sidebar_yazi.rs get_active_sidebar_yazi_state_snapshot, layout.rs is_sidebar_closed
- first Nushell consumers: integrations/zellij.nu workspace-root readers plus sidebar/Yazi callers that still parse debug_editor_state or split sidebar state reads
- crate gate: no new production crates expected; stay on existing serde/serde_json + zellij_tile unless implementation proves otherwise
- doc drift fixed: workspace bootstrap root is plugin initial_cwd, not HOME
close_reason: Implemented get_active_tab_session_state pipe with versioned JSON (schema v1); Nushell read_current_tab_workspace_root and doctor use stable seam; synced wasm; lib unit test for snapshot keys; pane_orchestrator_component.md updated; fixed plugin_build.nu str join quoting.

title: Sidebar and Yazi integration: consume orchestrator-owned tab truth instead of re-deriving it
description: Once the plugin exposes typed tab-local session truth, migrate sidebar/path-sync/Yazi-facing integrations to consume that seam instead of relying on recency-based state selection, ad-hoc pane lookups, or duplicated focus/sidebar inference. This directly targets the cross-tab locality class that already caused sidebar cwd leaks.
design: Treat this as a delete-first migration. Replace non-authoritative shell/Yazi derivations with plugin-owned answers where they overlap. Keep Lua as an adapter and keep config/rendering ownership in Nushell. Use the postmortem in yazelix-f0w as a guardrail against session-global or recency-based shortcuts.
acceptance_criteria: Relevant sidebar and Yazi integration flows use the orchestrator-owned seam for tab-local targeting wherever the plugin can answer authoritatively. The implementation removes or clearly narrows the older duplicated derivation paths, and regression checks defend against cross-tab mis-targeting.
close_reason: Completed: sidebar and Yazi integrations now consume pane-orchestrator session truth via get_active_tab_session_state, the duplicate raw sidebar-state seam was deleted, and shared post-retarget sync behavior is defended by focused regressions.

title: Retire get_active_sidebar_yazi_state and make the session snapshot the only live sidebar and Yazi read seam
description: The pane orchestrator now exposes sidebar_yazi inside get_active_tab_session_state, but Nushell still carries a second live read seam through get_active_sidebar_yazi_state_from_plugin and yazi_sidebar_state.nu. Migrate live consumers to the typed session snapshot and delete the duplicate active-tab read path.
design: Delete first. Reuse sidebar_yazi from the versioned session payload instead of adding another compatibility adapter. Keep ya emit-to execution and Yazi or Lua UI work outside Rust. If consumers still need readiness or failure detail, extend the versioned session seam rather than preserving a parallel raw pipe contract.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Live sidebar and Yazi consumers read active-tab identity from get_active_tab_session_state. 2. yazi_sidebar_state.nu and duplicate helper paths are deleted or materially reduced. 3. get_active_sidebar_yazi_state stops acting as a production ownership seam and is removed or clearly compatibility-only. 4. Regression checks defend missing-sidebar and cross-tab targeting behavior.
close_reason: Completed: live sidebar/Yazi consumers now read sidebar_yazi from get_active_tab_session_state, the duplicate Nushell sidebar-state bridge file was deleted, the raw get_active_sidebar_yazi pipe command was removed from the pane orchestrator, and Yazi regressions now defend the session-snapshot seam directly.

title: Collapse duplicated post-retarget sidebar and editor sync orchestration across open and zoxide flows
description: managed_editor.nu and zoxide_open_in_editor.nu both run the same retarget_workspace_for_path to optional editor handling to sidebar sync sequence. Collapse that duplicated live-state orchestration so one narrow path owns post-retarget editor and Yazi sync behavior.
design: Prefer deleting duplicated Nushell branches over adding another abstraction layer. If a small new orchestrator pipe materially simplifies ordering or error handling, keep it narrow to plugin-owned state transitions only. Path resolution, editor choice, and ya emit-to execution should stay outside Rust unless a broader contract changes.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The repeated retarget then sidebar-sync sequences in managed_editor.nu and zoxide_open_in_editor.nu materially shrink. 2. One post-retarget sync path remains for shared behavior. 3. Remaining Nushell clearly owns only intent, path resolution, and external tool execution. 4. Tests defend shared retarget and sidebar-sync behavior instead of per-call duplication.
close_reason: Completed: managed_editor.nu and zoxide_open_in_editor.nu now share one post-retarget sync path for optional missing-editor follow-up plus sidebar/current-Yazi sync behavior, and focused Yazi regressions defend the shared retarget-sync contract.

title: Audit and delete duplicate pane/focus/sidebar state owners outside the plugin
description: After the new orchestrator seam exists and key consumers move to it, audit remaining non-authoritative owners of pane identity, focus context, sidebar visibility, and tab-local workspace state across Nushell, Yazi glue, and caches. Remove duplicates that no longer need to exist, or explicitly demote them to cache/adapter status.
design: Do not create a second generic state abstraction. The point is to shrink ownership and future bug surface, not to wrap old and new sources in more indirection. Preserve only data that is truly cache, transport, or renderer input; make the pane orchestrator the durable owner for live tab/session truth.
acceptance_criteria: The surviving sources of pane/focus/sidebar/tab-local state are documented and smaller than before. Duplicate or ambiguous owners are removed where possible, and any remaining cache/adapters are labeled as non-authoritative with focused rationale and tests guarding the intended boundary.
notes: Completed by finishing the remaining duplicate-state audit slice: the old `debug_editor_state` inspection payload no longer survives as a public-looking session-truth seam and is now exposed only as maintainer-only `maintainer_debug_editor_state`. The surviving live boundary is the typed pane-orchestrator session snapshot consumed by shipped Yazi/sidebar/editor flows, with docs updated to mark caches/adapters as non-authoritative.
close_reason: Completed: duplicate sidebar/Yazi/debug session-state owners were removed or demoted and the surviving session-truth boundary is documented

title: Demote debug_editor_state to a maintainer-only surface after typed session consumers migrate
description: The stable get_active_tab_session_state seam now exists, but debug_editor_state still survives as a second broad inspection payload. Once live consumers stop depending on it, narrow or demote the debug surface so it no longer acts like an accidental session-truth contract.
design: Do not wrap debug and typed state together behind another abstraction. Keep debug payloads only for maintainer inspection and troubleshooting. Production Nushell and Yazi flows should consume versioned typed payloads or explicit narrow commands.
acceptance_criteria: 1. No shipped live consumer relies on debug_editor_state for tab-local session truth. 2. debug_editor_state is removed, materially reduced, or clearly labeled maintainer-only. 3. Docs reflect the surviving debug versus contract boundary. 4. Tests defend real consumer contracts, not debug payload shape trivia.
notes: Implemented by renaming the pane-orchestrator debug pipe from `debug_editor_state` to `maintainer_debug_editor_state` and renaming the exported Nushell helper to match. No shipped live consumer still depends on the old broad debug payload for tab-local session truth; active Yazi/sidebar/editor flows remain on `get_active_tab_session_state`. Updated workspace/session docs and pane-orchestrator specs to label the surviving debug surface as maintainer-only and to remove stale references to the deleted sidebar read seam. Verified with pane-orchestrator wasm rebuild/sync, targeted Yazi/session-retarget tests, Rust plugin lib tests, syntax/spec validation, and `git diff --check`.,workdir:/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix,yield_time_ms:1000,max_output_tokens:4000}
close_reason: Completed: debug_editor_state demoted to maintainer-only and no shipped live consumer still depends on it

title: Define ratconfig terminal UI hardening checklist
description: Define a small Yazelix/yazelix-ratconfig hardening checklist for terminal UI correctness, borrowing useful external TUI-kernel ideas without adopting a new framework.
design: Focus on Yazelix's actual config UI surface. Evaluate one-writer discipline, RAII/raw-mode/alt-screen cleanup, panic/error cleanup behavior, deterministic snapshot fixtures for key screens, PTY smoke coverage where it buys confidence, resize/flicker behavior under Zellij, and a minimal accessibility/focus metadata model. Keep this as a checklist or narrow tests first; do not replace ratconfig's Ratatui foundation or import a broad UI runtime.
acceptance_criteria: The decision states which hardening checks belong in yazelix-ratconfig, which belong in the main Yazelix adapter, and which are unnecessary. Accepted follow-up implementation beads are concrete and small: e.g. cleanup regression, snapshot fixture, resize smoke, focus/a11y metadata, or Zellij popup behavior. The checklist avoids framework churn and keeps ratconfig usable for vanilla users.

title: Define the runtime dependency contract and launch preflight checker scope
description: Specify the runtime dependency contract that normal user-facing entrypoints such as yzx launch actually rely on, and define the scope of a preflight checker that should validate that contract before launch. Cover required versus optional dependencies, config-conditioned requirements, runtime assets versus host tools, and the boundary between lightweight preflight checks and heavier doctor flows. The result should be explicit enough that launch, doctor, install smoke, and any future Core edition can share one dependency story instead of ad hoc checks.
close_reason: Duplicate of yazelix-j4qv created during parallel bead creation.

title: yzx tutor
description: Add a tutor surface under `yzx tutor` that helps users learn both the native tools Yazelix ships and the Yazelix-specific workflow layered on top of them.
design: Split the feature in two parts. Part one: thin wrappers for native tutors that already exist, specifically `yzx tutor hx` -> `hx --tutor` and `yzx tutor nu` -> Nushell `tutor`. Part two: Yazelix-specific markdown tutors for `yzx`, `yazi`, and `zellij`. `yzx tutor yzx` should teach the Yazelix mental model and core commands. `yzx tutor yazi` should explain what changes in the Yazelix-managed Yazi experience compared with vanilla Yazi. `yzx tutor zellij` should explain what changes in the Yazelix-managed Zellij experience compared with vanilla Zellij. Prefer opening concise curated markdown guides over building a live demo or interactive checklist system first.
acceptance_criteria: `yzx tutor hx` and `yzx tutor nu` launch the existing upstream tutors. `yzx tutor yzx`, `yzx tutor yazi`, and `yzx tutor zellij` open maintained Yazelix-specific guides. The tutor surface feels coherent: every subcommand either teaches a real upstream tutor or opens curated Yazelix-specific onboarding content. Avoid shallow website-link wrappers with no Yazelix adaptation. Add unit tests for command routing and target resolution, docs-existence checks for the markdown guides, and end-to-end smoke tests that log the resolved tutor path or launched command.
close_reason: Completed: shipped the yzx tutor MVP with a Yazelix overview plus hx/nu upstream tutor wrappers; richer structured tutor flow moved to yazelix-3jw

title: Filter large GIFs from git history
description: Remove oversized GIF history from the repository in a deliberate way so the repo is lighter to clone without breaking the current docs and preview experience.
design: This is history hygiene, not just deleting files from HEAD. Identify which demo GIF blobs are disproportionately large, decide what should stay in git versus move to releases or external hosting, and plan the rewrite carefully so active docs, screenshots, and README references still work afterward. Document the migration and force-push implications before rewriting history.
acceptance_criteria: Large legacy GIF blobs are removed from git history, the current repo still has working preview assets, and contributors have a clear migration note if history rewriting is required. Include a verification script or logged before-and-after measurements for repo size, clone size, and asset reference integrity.
close_reason: Not worth the disruption. GIFs are already deleted from HEAD, not shipped in the flake package, and only affect full-clone size. History rewriting would force-push main and break every existing clone for ~54 MB of dead blobs. Shallow clones (the common user path) don't pay for them.

title: Define curated LSP/formatter/linter defaults for every Yazelix-supported language pack
description: Define and document the default LSP, formatter, and linter for every Yazelix-supported language pack so each pack feels like a curated development environment rather than a loose bundle of packages.
design: Keep this opinionated and pack-first. For each supported language pack, decide the default LSP, formatter, and linter Yazelix stands behind, document the reasoning, and make sure the pack actually installs the chosen tools. Avoid turning this into an explosion of alternatives; the point is a clear supported default for each pack.
acceptance_criteria: Every supported language pack has a documented default LSP, formatter, and linter, and the pack implementation matches that documentation. Add unit tests for the pack-to-tool mapping, integration tests for config generation, and end-to-end fixture checks for a representative set of languages, with detailed logs of the resolved tools and launched language-service commands.
close_reason: Stale v14-era issue. v15 dropped language packs (yazelix_packs.toml, yzx packs) entirely. Yazelix no longer manages curated LSP/formatter/linter defaults — users bring their own editor tooling.

description: Imported GitHub issue #527. Pressing Alt+t shows the yzx_popup_toggle pane briefly and the floating popup pane flashes closed immediately.
close_reason: Fixed on main in 347dc07 (popup env serialization) and verified live after runtime reinstall

title: Reduce high-signal nu-lint findings across the Yazelix Nushell codebase
description: Reduce the remaining high-signal nu-lint findings that still fire under Yazelix's curated .nu-lint.toml, without regressing intentional patterns or creating broad style churn. This bead is about paying down real correctness and maintainability warnings after the tool has already been adopted as an explicit maintainer command.
design: Work from the curated nu-lint surface, not upstream defaults. The starting point is the narrowed finding set that remained useful after disabling noisy groups and incompatible rules: unsafe_dynamic_record_access, do_not_compare_booleans, unused_parameter, check_typed_flag_before_use, unchecked_get_index, non_final_failure_check, unused_helper_functions, unused_variable, assign_then_return, fragile_last_exit_code, and unchecked_cell_path_index. Treat this as defect-reduction work, not style cleanup. Preserve intentional process-isolation patterns and repo conventions already defended by the nu-lint policy bead. Prefer fixing classes of warning where the resulting code is actually clearer or safer; if a rule still produces low-value churn during cleanup, capture that evidence and narrow the policy instead of forcing busywork.
acceptance_criteria: The curated nu-lint warning count is materially reduced on the Yazelix Nushell tree, with fixes concentrated on the highest-signal rule families. Every retained warning class is either intentionally deferred with a reason or judged not worth the churn. The cleanup does not re-enable noisy rules that were already rejected, and the resulting code remains consistent with Yazelix conventions and behavior. The bead records the before/after warning counts plus any rule-policy adjustments discovered during cleanup.
notes: Completed cleanup pass from current baseline: nu-lint started at 67 warnings + 100 hints with the existing config, and now reports 0 violations through the maintained yzx dev lint_nu entrypoint. Added pkgs.nu-lint to the flake maintainer shell so the command is self-contained. Code cleanup focused on typed flag defaults/null-safety, safe optional indexing, explicit external-command exit checks, the fragile installer activation exit-code path while preserving streamed install logs, and delete-first removal of unused v15-obsolete Nix-bootstrap helpers plus a dead doctor helper/no-op yzx config --full flag. Policy adjustment: disabled rules that proved to be low-signal or false-positive-heavy in the current v15 codebase: spread_list_to_external on typed internal helper list args, redundant_ignore, chained_str_transform, error_make_for_non_fatal, if_null_to_default, unused_parameter, unused_variable, unused_helper_functions, and assign_then_return.
close_reason: Completed: yzx dev lint_nu is self-contained in the flake maintainer shell, current curated lint output is reduced from 67 warnings + 100 hints to 0 violations, real safety/dead-code findings were fixed, and noisy/false-positive rule classes are documented in .nu-lint.toml.

title: Let popup_program use the configured editor via an explicit editor token
close_reason: Completed

title: Align runtime-owned devenv with the locked source and replace false skew warning
description:
Yazelix currently has two authorities for `devenv`: the runtime/package outputs ship `pkgs.devenv` from nixpkgs, while `devenv shell` still evaluates against the separately pinned `cachix/devenv` source in `devenv.lock`. That split can drift in either direction and is currently user-visible in `yzx env` as the upstream warning that the CLI is newer than the locked input.

Investigation also showed that the warning itself is not a reliable contract check for Yazelix. In the currently locked upstream `devenv` source, the CLI package version is `2.0.7` while the module-side `src/modules/latest-version` file is still `2.0.6`, so upstream can warn even when the CLI and module tree come from the same locked Git revision. The bead should therefore fix both problems: remove Yazelix's multi-authority packaging seam, and stop relying on upstream's warning semantics as if they were the real source-of-truth check.
design:
Treat this as an authority/alignment cleanup, not as a warning-string tweak. The accepted fix should make `devenv.lock` the authoritative source for the runtime-owned `devenv` CLI, package that same locked source into the flake/runtime/nixpkgs-style outputs, and keep `devenv_cli.nu` preferring the runtime-owned path.

Because upstream `devenv.warnOnNewVersion` currently compares the CLI version to `src/modules/latest-version` rather than to the locked revision identity, Yazelix should not depend on that warning to model its own contract. The likely shape is:
- add a small Nix helper that derives the runtime `devenv` package from the locked `cachix/devenv` source in `devenv.lock`
- use that helper in `yazelix_runtime_package.nix` and `mk_yazelix_package.nix` instead of `pkgs.devenv`
- disable the upstream new-version warning in Yazelix's `devenv.nix`
- add Yazelix-owned validation that the packaged runtime `bin/devenv` comes from the same locked source as the shipped `devenv.lock`, and that `yzx env` no longer surfaces the upstream skew warning

This should also reduce the maintainer command surface instead of preserving a special-case bump path. `yzx dev update` should become the single intentional maintainer flow for updating Yazelix inputs, running canaries, and syncing follow-up artifacts. As part of centralizing that surface, `yzx dev update devenv` should be removed rather than retained as a blessed targeted alias, because it implies there is a separate devenv-specific sync concept beyond the authoritative lockfile. If a raw targeted upstream debug/update flow is still occasionally useful, it should stay an explicit manual repo-maintainer operation, not part of the supported Yazelix command surface.

Keep flake install, `yzx env`, `yzx refresh`, `yzx doctor`, version reporting, maintainer `yzx dev update`, and the local nixpkgs-style package coherent under that single-source contract. Do not solve this by merely updating `devenv.lock` to a quieter state or by only suppressing text without fixing the packaging seam.
acceptance_criteria: A freshly installed or updated Yazelix runtime no longer prints the upstream `devenv ... is newer than devenv input ... in devenv.lock` warning during `yzx env`. The runtime-owned `bin/devenv` in the flake/runtime/package outputs is demonstrably built from the same locked upstream source as the shipped `devenv.lock`, not merely from a matching semver. `yzx env`, `yzx refresh`, `yzx doctor`, flake install smoke, and the nixpkgs-style package smoke remain coherent under the new contract. `yzx dev update` remains the single intentional maintainer path for updating Yazelix inputs, and the targeted `yzx dev update devenv` surface is removed. The repo gains a Yazelix-owned regression check for CLI/lock source parity instead of relying on the upstream warning behavior.
notes:
Investigation details:
- `yazelix_runtime_package.nix` and `mk_yazelix_package.nix` currently wire runtime `devenv` from `pkgs.devenv`
- runtime entrypoints then prefer that runtime-local CLI via `devenv_cli.nu` / `environment_bootstrap.nu`
- maintainer updates currently run `devenv update` via `yzx dev update`, but the current `input_name?` surface still exposes a special `yzx dev update devenv` path that should be deleted if the lockfile becomes the one authority
- the currently locked upstream `cachix/devenv` tree has `Cargo.toml` version `2.0.7` but `src/modules/latest-version` still `2.0.6`
- upstream warning semantics therefore do not reliably express Yazelix's desired single-authority contract
close_reason: Closed as superseded by the v15 runtime trim: live runtime/package code no longer ships or prefers a runtime-local devenv binary, installed-runtime validation now defends that absence, and only historical/docs references remain.

title: Allow Yazi user keymap sections beyond mgr to merge
description: Investigation from yazelix-tjc7 found an adjacent config gap: configs/yazi/user/README.md documents input.append_keymap and cmp.append_keymap as supported, and Yazi itself can layer append/prepend keymaps for those modes, but Yazelix merge_yazi_keymap currently iterates only top-level sections present in the Yazelix base keymap. Since the base keymap currently only contains mgr, user keymap sections like [input] and [cmp] are silently dropped during materialization. Decide whether the supported contract is manager-only or all Yazi keymap layers; if supporting them, update merge_yazi_keymap and its regression test so new top-level keymap sections are preserved and merged.
notes: Implemented 2026-05-03: merge_yazi_keymap now iterates user keymap sections and inserts top-level sections absent from the bundled base, while preserving the existing array-append behavior for base sections such as [mgr]. This makes documented [input] and [cmp] append/prepend keymaps survive generation. Verification: yzx dev rust fmt --check; yzx dev rust check core; focused keymap merge unit regression; focused yazi_materialization.generate regression; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_core_yazi_materialization.
close_reason: Preserved documented Yazi keymap sections beyond [mgr] in materialization

title: support for https://github.com/just-every/code and pi

title: Enforce per-test strength scoring across all governed test lanes

title: investigate making the game of life clickable
description: Imported GitHub issue #543.
close_reason: Not planned: making Game of Life clickable would turn the welcome/screen animation into an interactive terminal toy, adding mouse/input complexity across raw terminal mode and Zellij without advancing the core Yazelix workspace. Keep yzx screen as an animated preview/screen-saver surface that exits on keypress; any future interactive cellular-automata toy should be a separate explicit product decision.

title: Recover usage periods when status-bar refresh loses config env
description: Status-bar agent usage refresh can run with only the cache path visible. In that case it falls back to default daily periods even when the launch config snapshot contains day/month/last.
design: Recover the session config snapshot path from the launch-scoped status_bar_cache.json path, matching the existing parent-process env recovery used for cache path discovery.
acceptance_criteria: A window whose snapshot has codex_usage_periods = ["day", "month", "last"] refreshes codex_daily, codex_monthly, and codex_session even when direct YAZELIX_SESSION_CONFIG_PATH is absent.
close_reason: Completed: refresh config now recovers the sibling config snapshot from status_bar_cache.json and tests cover grouped period target selection without direct config env.

title: Add Tailwind CSS LSP to JS/TS pack
description: Add the Tailwind CSS language server to the JavaScript and TypeScript language-pack story so modern frontend projects have the expected completion and diagnostics out of the box.
design: Scope this to the curated pack model rather than ad-hoc per-user tweaks. Decide whether Tailwind LSP belongs directly in the JS or TS pack or in a narrower frontend or web pack, then wire the package, editor integration, and docs accordingly. Be explicit about how this behaves for projects that do not use Tailwind so the default does not feel like noisy overreach.
acceptance_criteria: Projects that use Tailwind get a working LSP path from the relevant Yazelix pack, and users can understand why it is included. Add unit tests for pack membership and resolver logic, config-generation checks for the chosen editor integration, and an end-to-end smoke test in a minimal frontend fixture with detailed LSP startup logs.
notes: Implemented by adding tailwindcss-language-server to the ts pack in both yazelix_default.toml and home_manager/module.nix, documenting the pack change, and adding a ts-pack parity test. Broader frontend-pack/toolchain questions were split into yazelix-7zs.
close_reason: Completed

title: add option of silent refresh on a specific time (like 4am)
description: Imported GitHub issue #389.
close_reason: Too hard and out of scope for Yazelix right now

title: Prevent Yazelix runtime PATH from breaking host-distributed apps like Steam
description: Yazelix currently prepends its runtime libexec/bin paths ahead of the host PATH for `yzx env` and normal Yazelix shells. That lets Nix-built userland tools such as coreutils shadow host-distributed tools for arbitrary external applications launched from the Yazelix shell. Steam on Ubuntu/Pop!OS is a concrete reproduction: `steamwebhelper` fails to render when it resolves Yazelix-provided `dirname` linked against a newer glibc than the host runtime provides.
design:
Investigation findings:
- `shells/posix/runtime_env.sh` prepends `$runtime_dir/libexec:$runtime_dir/bin` ahead of the inherited PATH for POSIX launchers
- `nushell/scripts/utils/runtime_env.nu` does the same for `yzx env`, startup, popup, and other managed runtime flows
- `packaging/mk_runtime_tree.nix` populates `libexec/` by symlinking every binary from every package in `packaging/runtime_deps.nix`
- `packaging/runtime_deps.nix` currently includes both real user-facing tools and incidental helper/userland packages such as `coreutils`, `findutils`, `gnugrep`, `gnused`, `util-linux`, `procps`, and `nix`
- This means a Yazelix shell currently exports far more than the intended interactive tool surface. Any host-distributed app launched from that shell can resolve Nix-built helper binaries before `/usr/bin`, which is unsafe on non-NixOS Linux systems where those binaries may require a newer glibc than the host runtime provides
- Steam is only the clearest reproduction. The boundary bug is broader: any host app that shells out through PATH and expects host-compatible core userland tools is in scope

Preferred fix direction:
1. Delete-first: stop treating the full runtime dependency closure as the interactive PATH surface
2. Split the environment contract into a curated user PATH and an internal helper resolution path
3. Keep only first-class interactive tools on the exported PATH; resolve helper tools needed by Yazelix internals via absolute runtime paths or a helper-only PATH used inside managed wrappers/scripts
4. Audit all PATH-exporting seams together: `runtime_env.sh`, `runtime_env.nu`, `yzx env`, startup, popup/editor wrappers, and any launcher that re-exports the runtime environment
5. Add regression coverage for the boundary itself, not just Steam specifically: a host command launched from a Yazelix shell should not resolve incidental runtime-owned helper binaries such as `dirname` ahead of the system tool on Linux

Relevant tension to keep in mind:
- current docs market `yzx env` as a way to load Yazelix tools into ordinary shells and editor terminals
- current package/spec prose also talks about a fixed runtime toolset, but that does not require exporting the entire dependency closure as first-class interactive commands
acceptance_criteria: A Yazelix shell or `yzx env` session must not break host-distributed applications because runtime-owned helper binaries shadow system tools through PATH ordering. The exported interactive PATH should contain only the curated user-facing tool surface Yazelix intentionally owns, while incidental helper binaries needed by Yazelix internals are resolved through a separate internal path or absolute runtime paths. Steam on Linux should launch from a Yazelix shell without the `dirname` / glibc mismatch, and docs should describe the resulting PATH boundary honestly.
notes:
Reproduction from user report: with Yazelix active, PATH begins with the Yazelix runtime and coreutils store paths, Steam logs `dirname: ... GLIBC_2.42 not found`, `steamwebhelper` becomes zombie/defunct, and the UI never appears. Launching Steam with a clean system PATH fixes the issue immediately. This likely affects more than Steam: any host-distributed app that shells out through PATH and expects host-glibc-compatible userland tools is in scope.
Additional investigation: the packaging reshuffle that moved runtime tools into `libexec/` reduced profile/Home Manager collisions, but it also made the whole dependency closure easy to prepend as a single PATH segment. The real follow-up is to narrow what counts as the exported interactive tool surface, not to special-case Steam.
close_reason: Completed: exported interactive PATH now comes from curated runtime toolbin while libexec stays runtime-private, docs/specs describe the boundary, and runtime/package regressions cover toolbin-vs-libexec behavior.

title: Improve Helix support for Ghostty cursor effects
description: Track work needed to improve Ghostty cursor-effect support when using Helix inside Yazelix. Cover which trail effects currently fail or degrade in Helix, what editor/runtime limitations are involved, and what Yazelix-side adaptations or fallbacks could close the gap with Neovim support.
design: Treat this as a capability-gap analysis between Neovim-style cursor effects and the Helix environment Yazelix actually supports. Identify which Ghostty effects fail in Helix, whether the limitation is in Ghostty triggers, Helix state visibility, or Yazelix-side integration, and whether the right answer is parity, graceful degradation, or explicit unsupported status. Avoid promising full parity if the editor/runtime stack cannot expose the right events reliably.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear support decision for Ghostty cursor effects in Helix: which effects work, which do not, why, and what Yazelix-side adaptation or fallback is justified. If there is a viable improvement path, the bead leaves behind narrower implementation work instead of keeping the problem generic.

title: Design runtime light/dark theme switching
description: A tester asked for dynamic theme switching without rebuilding or running home-manager switch just to move between light and dark themes. Evaluate a runtime theme mode that accepts light/dark theme choices and can switch manually or follow system preferences where credible.
acceptance_criteria: Design defines the supported theme inputs, runtime state owner, manual switch command if any, and whether automatic system-preference following is supported per platform. It covers Zellij, Yazi, Helix, terminal theme boundaries, and a theme-aware zjstatus/widget palette so CPU/RAM/usage/custom widgets remain legible in both light and dark modes. It avoids requiring Nix/Home Manager rebuilds for day/night changes and creates implementation follow-ups for the accepted scope.
notes:
Issue comment 2026-05-03: tester's working external script is useful product evidence for the desired runtime surface. Their shape is switch-theme light|dark|toggle, with one operation updating a theme cache plus Ghostty, Kitty, Helix, Yazelix Yazi/Zellij/theme TOML, Atuin, native Yazi, Starship runtime config, native Zellij, and macOS appearance. They use Catppuccin Latte/Mocha specifically to reduce eye strain. This suggests Yazelix should evaluate a small yzx theme light|dark|toggle command and a persisted runtime theme state, with tool-specific adapters where Yazelix owns generated config and documented boundaries where tools only pick up changes on next launch. Automatic system-appearance following should be optional/platform-scoped rather than assumed for every tool.
Issue comment/image 2026-05-03: screenshot also shows the CPU widget rendering ?? on macOS; track that data-source bug separately as yazelix-co9j. The same screenshot/light-theme report also means status widgets need theme-aware color tokens rather than hard-coded orange/purple/green values. Runtime theme switching should include a small palette contract for zjstatus segments so CPU/RAM/usage/custom widgets can render legibly in both light and dark modes.
Issue 580 comment 2026-05-03 follow-up: reset made the setup no longer usable in light theme until the tester manually customized theme files under ~/.config/yazelix. This reinforces that runtime theme switching needs a coherent Yazelix-owned palette/update path, not advice to reset config, because reset can erase local visual preferences while leaving adjacent per-tool overrides intact.

title: Experiment: use Nushell job freeze/unfreeze to reuse pane (no-sidebar)
description: Investigate whether Nushell job freeze/unfreeze can be used under the hood to let Helix and Yazi share the same pane in the no_sidebar layout instead of paying the full reopen cost every time.
design: This is an experiment, not a guaranteed feature. The target flow is the no_sidebar layout only: freeze one program, swap in the other, and unfreeze it later so the pane feels reused rather than recreated. Evaluate correctness first: terminal state, shell/editor cleanup, focus behavior, log noise, and what happens when a frozen process crashes or the pane is closed. If the technique is brittle, capture that clearly and reject it rather than forcing it into production.
acceptance_criteria: The bead ends with either a working prototype for no_sidebar or a written rejection based on concrete evidence. If implemented, add unit tests around the state machine that tracks frozen vs active processes, plus e2e coverage for repeated Helix/Yazi swaps, crash/recovery paths, and pane-close behavior with detailed timing and state-transition logs.
close_reason: Superseded by the v15 pane orchestrator architecture. The Rust plugin manages separate editor/sidebar panes with distinct identities and swap layout families handle visibility. Freeze/unfreeze pane sharing would fight the plugin's pane-discovery model and the reopen cost it aimed to avoid is no longer the bottleneck.

title: Replace Yazelix transient popup/menu/config adapter with yzpp specs
description: Why: once yzpp owns generic popup identity and close hooks, Yazelix no longer needs a bespoke transient adapter for lazygit, command/menu, and config UI panes. What: generate or ship yzpp specs for lazygit, menu, and config UI, switch semantic keybindings to yzpp messages, and remove transient adapter code that only duplicated generic popup behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix Zellij config contains yzpp specs for popup/menu/config; popup/menu/config semantic actions pipe to yzpp; lazygit uses the sidebar refresh close hook; pane-orchestrator transient adapter code and Nu wrappers are deleted or reduced to genuine command entrypoints.
close_reason: Completed: popup, menu, and config UI panes now route through generated yzpp specs/raw requests; pane-orchestrator transient open/toggle code and Nushell popup wrappers were deleted.

title: Make Codex usage widget fast on large local histories
description: Investigate and fix ccusage-codex status-bar refresh performance when ~/.codex is large. Current provider calls can exceed the bar safety timeout, so codex_usage remains empty even though ccusage-codex is installed. Evaluate narrower date windows, provider caching, a longer background-only timeout, or an independent summary cache that does not block frequent widget refreshes.
notes: Implementation update 2026-04-30: removed the ccusage-codex fallback per provider contract. Claude and Codex widgets now use exactly one provider binary, tu, with source-specific tokenusage commands; OpenCode remains on ccusage-opencode. Added tokenusage 1.5.2 to the opt-in runtime/package surface, exported tu through the runtime toolbin, removed ccusage/ccusage-codex from #yazelix_agent_tools, and updated Home Manager/docs to use agent_usage_programs = [ "tokenusage" "ccusage-opencode" ]. Verified runtime_agent_tools builds and contains tu + ccusage-opencode while ccusage-codex is absent; direct codex widget probe rendered through tu.
close_reason: Completed: Codex usage widgets use packaged tokenusage without fallback; OpenCode remains on ccusage-opencode.

title: Investigate Ctrl+y conflict between Yazelix and Yazi picker recommendations
description: Investigate the possible conflict between Yazelix's current keybinding model and Yazi's documented Tips and Tricks recommendation for using Ctrl+y to drive Yazi as a picker from Zellij/Helix.
design: Treat this as an input-model and docs-coherence investigation, not just a keybinding bikeshed. Verify what Yazi's website currently recommends for picker usage from Zellij or Helix, how that overlaps with Yazelix's existing Ctrl+y or related bindings, what escape sequences and ownership layers are involved, and whether Yazelix should preserve the upstream recommendation, intentionally diverge from it, or document a different integrated workflow. The goal is to avoid a confusing mismatch between Yazelix and the mental model users will pick up from Yazi's own docs.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear documented decision about the Ctrl+y flow for Yazi-as-picker usage in Yazelix. If the current mapping is wrong or confusing, update the keybinding/docs accordingly. Add validation or end-to-end coverage for the affected key path and log the emitted sequence, target action, and final documented behavior.
close_reason: Aligned Yazelix keybindings with Ctrl+y => Yazi mental model and removed stale Space-y Helix default

title: Organize the pane orchestrator as an internal separately-owned component boundary
description: The pane orchestrator already owns real behavior: transient popup/menu toggles, sidebar focus and visibility, workspace retargeting, and layout-family motions. The repo still spreads that ownership across Rust plugin code, Nushell client wrappers, generated Zellij config, and maintainer build/sync notes in ways that make the subsystem feel more scattered than it is. Tighten the in-repo organization and contract so the wasm/component can be reasoned about as one unit while it still lives in the monorepo.
design: Do not split repos or genericize behavior yet. Work from first principles: if the plugin and its consumer interfaces still change together, a separate repo adds versioning and coordination cost without buying real clarity. Instead, define the hard contract in-tree and organize around it. Likely outputs: a short component doc/README for the pane orchestrator; one explicit catalog of command names, plugin-configuration keys, wrapper/runtime path assumptions, and pane identity assumptions; clearer ownership boundaries between Rust plugin internals, Nushell client transport, generated Zellij keybind/config wiring, and Yazi/sidebar adapters; and deletion of stale cross-layer convenience surfaces where possible. Success means future extraction would be straightforward, but extraction itself is not the goal.
acceptance_criteria: 1. There is one explicit in-repo pane-orchestrator component document or spec covering command surface, plugin configuration keys, runtime and wrapper path assumptions, and pane identity invariants. 2. Source, build, test, and doc ownership are organized so the plugin can be understood as one owned subsystem without hunting across unrelated files. 3. At least one cross-layer or convenience coupling is deleted or internalized rather than merely documented. 4. Popup, menu, sidebar, workspace, and layout-family behavior remain unchanged, and focused regressions still defend the surviving contract.
notes: Implemented pane-orchestrator boundary cleanup: added docs/specs/pane_orchestrator_component.md, registered it in the spec inventory, removed obsolete Rust pipe commands set_workspace_root and set_workspace_root_and_cd_focused_pane, trimmed the workspace session contract to the surviving retarget_workspace command, rebuilt/synced the wasm artifact, and verified popup/menu/sidebar/workspace/layout command coverage through the default suite.
close_reason: Completed: documented the internal pane-orchestrator component boundary, removed stale workspace pipe commands, rebuilt/synced the wasm, and verified the surviving workspace/popup/menu/sidebar/layout contract.

title: Decide cursor settings and preset registry consolidation
description: Decide whether ~/.config/yazelix/cursors.toml should remain a permanent sidecar or be split/consolidated into the future canonical Yazelix settings surface. The sidecar currently mixes simple semantic settings, such as active trail/effects/fallback toggles, with a larger preset registry that behaves more like editable visual assets.
design: Initial direction from yazelix-8x3x: cursor selection/effects/fallback toggles are canonical settings candidates because Yazelix owns and validates them. Custom cursor preset definitions need deeper investigation before embedding them in the main settings file because they are bulky, asset-like, and may also relate to standalone Ghostty cursor preset distribution work. Preserve the current cursors.toml sidecar until the config UI can offer a structured cursor editor/import/export flow or this decision explicitly chooses another model.
acceptance_criteria:
- Decision classifies cursor selection/effects and custom preset definitions separately.
- Decision accounts for hand-editing ergonomics, schema/UI editing, Home Manager generation, standalone Ghostty cursor shader distribution, and import/export of custom presets.
- Decision defines whether cursors.toml stays permanent, is split into settings plus preset file, or is migrated into the canonical settings file.
- Follow-up implementation beads exist for the accepted cursor path.
close_reason: Superseded by yazelix-8x3x: cursors are first-class config/UI settings with a dedicated Cursors tab, so a separate cursor classification decision is not needed now.

title: Add a scored strength rubric for default-lane tests
design: Add a per-test strength rubric for the default suite so test quality is judged explicitly instead of only through prose. Use a small 0-10 score made from five 0-2 dimensions: failure significance, behavior proximity, refactor resilience, cost efficiency, and uniqueness. Require a nearby '# Strength: N/10' marker for every canonical default-lane test entry and enforce a minimum 7/10 bar in the existing default-suite validator. This should remain a decision aid with mechanical enforcement, not fake precision for every test in the repo: default-lane tests carry the score because that lane is the one whose quality bar matters most in CI.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The test governance spec documents the 0-10 default-test strength rubric and the meaning of the score bands. 2. AGENTS.md tells agents to add a nearby '# Strength: N/10' marker for new canonical default-lane tests and respect the minimum 7/10 bar. 3. validate_default_test_traceability.nu rejects canonical default-lane tests that are missing a score or fall below 7/10. 4. The current default canonical suite is scored under the new rubric and passes the validator. 5. The runtime budget remains a separate guard from the quality score.
close_reason: Completed: added a 0-10 strength rubric for canonical default-lane tests, enforced nearby # Strength: N/10 markers with a minimum 7/10 bar in CI, and scored the current default suite under the new model.

title: Implement true minibrot Mandelbrot zoom loop
description: Replace the fake/palindrome Mandelbrot animation with a monotonic zoom into a baby Mandelbrot/minibrot. The visual loop should restart only when the end frame is a self-similar mini-copy of the start, so runtime playback reads as an endless zoom-in rather than zoom-in/zoom-out. Use in-house/std math, document the chosen minibrot path, keep terminal CPU cost bounded, and defend monotonic zoom/cycle semantics with tests. Sources checked: Mandelbrot set self-similarity around minibrots/Misiurewicz points, Seahorse Valley examples, and fractal-artist guidance that zooms repeat when aimed at baby Mandelbrot copies.
notes: 2026-04-26 implementation: no new crates. Web/context finding: true terminal-scale loop should be a monotonic zoom into a baby Mandelbrot/minibrot, not a zoom-in/zoom-out palindrome. Chosen target is the large real-axis period-3 minibrot centered near -1.7548776662466927 + 0i because it is shallow enough for f64/terminal rendering and exposes the self-similar reset idea without arbitrary precision.
close_reason: Implemented: Mandelbrot screen now uses a monotonic zoom into the real-axis period-3 minibrot and resets only from the minibrot frame; screen tests and Rust budget checks pass

title: Replace ascii_art_mode with welcome_style selector
design: Use one welcome_style setting instead of separate mode/style knobs. The allowed values should be static, logo, boids, life, mandelbrot, and random. Random must choose only from the animated styles and must never select static. This bead should own the config/schema/default/Home Manager migration from ascii_art_mode to welcome_style, plus the shared style-selection helper used by the welcome renderer. The renderer contract may support either lightweight live frame generation or pre-rendered frame playback behind the same style surface.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has one supported welcome_style config surface with values static, logo, boids, life, mandelbrot, and random. Random never resolves to static. Existing config paths and Home Manager are migrated cleanly, and tests defend schema parsing, default generation, Home Manager emission, and the random-pool contract.
close_reason: Completed

title: Review docs on IDE integration and document both default-terminal modes
description: Review the editor and IDE integration docs and document both recommended terminal-profile setups: one where yzx env is the default integrated terminal and one where it remains an explicit non-default profile.
design: Treat this as a docs-clarity pass grounded in real user confusion. The docs should show both modes cleanly for VS Code or Cursor: a vanilla terminal as the default with an optional Yazelix profile, and a setup where the integrated terminal defaults directly to yzx env. Keep the examples consistent with the current command flow and explain the tradeoff between preserving a raw shell and dropping straight into the Yazelix environment.
acceptance_criteria: The IDE integration docs clearly present both supported VS Code/Cursor terminal configurations and when to choose each one. The JSON examples are current, minimal, and internally consistent. Validate the documented commands or config snippets and leave logs or notes showing which terminal profile becomes default in each example.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add Ghostty and WezTerm runtime variants without heavy media tools
description: Replace broad runtime feature-matrix ideas with two simple install-time runtime variants: Ghostty for Yazelix identity/cursor shaders and WezTerm for better image/Sixel compatibility. Keep the baseline helper toolset broad enough for normal Yazi/Yazelix use, but do not add heavy media preview dependencies yet.
design: Expose only terminal-centered package variants for now: ghostty and wezterm. Include existing small/medium helpers in both variants: jq, fd, ripgrep, fzf, zoxide, p7zip, poppler, resvg, xclip, wl-clipboard, and add xsel on Linux if it is small enough in nixpkgs closure. Keep ffmpeg and ImageMagick out of both variants. Keep Nerd Fonts user/system-owned. Do not revive v14 arbitrary packs or runtime package mutation. Home Manager and flake package selection own the install closure; yazelix.toml only orders/chooses already-installed terminal behavior.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Flake exposes ghostty and wezterm package variants with clear names and keeps default mapped to the Ghostty variant. 2. Home Manager can select the runtime variant without changing mutable yazelix.toml ownership. 3. Both variants include the existing small preview/navigation/search helpers; Linux variants include xclip, wl-clipboard, and xsel if accepted by closure-size review. 4. Neither variant includes ffmpeg or ImageMagick. 5. Docs explain Ghostty identity/cursor-shader path versus WezTerm image/Sixel compatibility path. 6. yzx doctor/inspect can report the active runtime variant or a follow-up bead is created if that surface is not yet available.
notes: Dependency decision before implementation: packaging-only variant work; no Rust crates expected. Use existing Nix package inputs and in-repo package functions. Rejected alternatives: reviving v14-style mutable packs or adding heavy media dependencies. Packaging impact: flake/Home Manager variant selection and docs only.
close_reason: Completed: packaged runtime now has Ghostty default and WezTerm variants, records runtime_variant, keeps heavy media tools out, and includes xsel for Linux clipboard coverage.

title: Require per-dimension test strength scoring to reduce score inflation
close_reason: Completed

title: Spec default multi-window Yazelix behavior for non-persistent sessions
description: Specify the default cross-window behavior when `zellij.persistent_sessions = false`.
design:
This task should pin down the normal Yazelix behavior for users who open more than one window without enabling persistent sessions.

In this mode, each launch should create its own live Yazelix/Zellij session. Windows share durable runtime/materialized state, but they do not share one live session. That means one window may still be running an older activated profile while a newly launched window uses fresher runtime/config state.

This task should also define the expected lifecycle of those independent sessions: closing the last client for a non-persistent window should end that session rather than leaving a long-lived detached server behind. That is the product contract that detached-session bugs should be measured against.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The docs state that non-persistent Yazelix windows are independent live sessions, even when they consume the same config/runtime/materialized state.
2. The docs state that new launches may use newer durable state than older still-open non-persistent windows.
3. The docs state that non-persistent windows are not automatically upgraded or hot-swapped when another window refreshes or updates the runtime.
4. The docs state that closing the last client for a non-persistent Yazelix session should not leave a detached long-lived session behind.
5. The resulting contract is specific enough to support later fixes for `yzx restart`, desktop launch, and detached-session cleanup in non-persistent mode.
notes:
This is the default user-facing behavior because `persistent_sessions = false` is the shipped default.

Spec delivered:
- `docs/specs/nonpersistent_window_session_contract.md`

Current defense:
- `nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu`
- `nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu; test_launch_here_path_uses_requested_directory_for_nonpersistent_sessions'`
close_reason: Completed: documented the default non-persistent multi-window session contract.

title: Add maintainer session-state inspect surface
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done

The workspace/session model is now explicitly plugin-owned, but there is still no first-class maintainer inspection surface that prints the active tab session state in one deliberate format. Debugging workspace bugs still pushes maintainers toward ad hoc pipes, raw plugin responses, or code reading.

Add a maintainer-facing inspect command that dumps the active workspace/session snapshot clearly enough to debug managed pane identity, focus context, workspace root, layout/sidebar state, and sidebar Yazi identity without bespoke probes.
close_reason: Added yzx dev inspect_session backed by pane-orchestrator session state

title: README: clarify owner runtime vs materialized runtime
description: Document the distinction between the installed owner runtime and the materialized runtime under ~/.local/share/yazelix so maintainers understand why syncing a rebuilt pane-orchestrator wasm alone does not guarantee fresh launches use it. Clarify that fresh launches and yzx restart rebuild from the owner runtime, and that local dogfooding requires activating the local repo package before relying on relaunch behavior.
close_reason: Completed: README now explains owner runtime vs materialized runtime, the ~/.local/share/yazelix split, and why local repo dogfooding needs owner activation before relaunch behavior is trusted.

title: Remove the Kiro spec file and directory

title: Clarify and verify the zsh/fish managed-shell availability boundary
description: Record and evaluate the current shell boundary: Yazelix-managed zsh/fish hook surfaces exist and work in managed shells, but zsh and fish are not currently shipped in the installed runtime bin/ and are instead provided through the managed devenv environment. Decide whether that is the intended product contract or whether extra shells should eventually become runtime-owned too.
design: Keep this as a low-priority clarification bead, not a pressure to runtime-own zsh or fish. The goal is to make the boundary explicit: Yazelix-managed hook surfaces exist for zsh and fish, but the shells themselves currently come from the managed devenv environment rather than the installed runtime bin. Verify the real behavior, record the intended support boundary, and only add tests or docs that defend that boundary clearly.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The repo has a clear documented statement of where zsh and fish are expected to come from in managed Yazelix sessions today. 2. The support boundary between managed hook surfaces and runtime-owned shell binaries is verified instead of guessed. 3. Any added validation or docs defend that actual boundary without turning this into pressure to runtime-ship more shells immediately.
close_reason: Obsolete: the repo now runtime-ships fish and zsh as part of the fixed v15 shell set, and the current docs plus install validation already describe and defend that boundary.

title: Ship v15 as the only supported Yazelix line
description: Finalize the public docs, release notes, and version surfaces so Yazelix can ship `v15` as the live supported release and treat v14 as a historical, unsupported line. This is the release-cut planning layer for the README/history cleanup, the GitHub release bodies, the structured upgrade notes, and the actual version bump/tag/publication work.
design: Treat this as one coherent release story, not a set of disconnected edits. Public surfaces should all tell the same truth: Yazelix is now the trimmed workspace-focused product, fast popup/menu and the first-party Ghostty path are part of the core experience, dynamic runtime management was out of scope, v14 is no longer a supported line, and `v15` is the exact release identifier being cut now. Do not tag until the front-door docs, narrative history, changelog/upgrade notes, GitHub releases, and version surfaces stop drifting from each other.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear release-cut task graph for shipping `v15` as the only supported Yazelix line. The graph covers README/history alignment, release-note and GitHub-release alignment, and the exact `v15` bump/tag/publication step, with explicit blockers so the tag is not cut before the public story is coherent.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Align GitHub releases and release notes for the v14 closeout and v15 launch
description: Update the release-facing surfaces around the v14 closeout and the v15 launch. That includes revising the existing v14 GitHub release so it reads as the final historical Classic release, creating the new v15 GitHub release body, and aligning the release-facing docs and note sources that those GitHub releases should reflect.
design: Keep the release story consistent across GitHub releases, CHANGELOG.md, docs/upgrade_notes.toml, and any release-facing history copy. The v14 release should stop implying ongoing support or bug-fix maintenance and should frame the broader dynamic-runtime-management scope as historical. The v15 release should present the current trimmed product honestly: workspace-focused scope, first-party Ghostty, fast popup/menu panes, and the end of the old runtime-manager story. Prefer one coherent note set rather than hand-maintained drift between GitHub and repo docs.
acceptance_criteria: The v14 GitHub release is updated to historical final-release framing, the v15 GitHub release notes exist in a form ready to publish, and the related release-facing note sources in the repo are aligned with those bodies. No public release surface still claims that v14 remains supported or that dynamic runtime management is part of Yazelix scope.
close_reason: Completed: release-facing repo note sources now carry the publish-ready v15 launch body, and the live v14 GitHub release was updated to final historical Classic framing.

title: Cut and publish the v15 release tag with synced version surfaces
description: Once the public docs and release notes are coherent, cut and publish the exact `v15` release. This includes syncing `YAZELIX_VERSION` and the other version-sensitive repo surfaces, validating the release-sensitive commands and docs, creating the git tag, and publishing the GitHub release from the prepared notes.
design: Treat this as release execution, not content drafting. Use the exact version string `v15` consistently across the repo and the public release surfaces. Do not tag while the README, docs/history.md, CHANGELOG.md, docs/upgrade_notes.toml, or GitHub release bodies still tell the old v14-maintained story or disagree about the release identifier. Validate version-sensitive surfaces such as `YAZELIX_VERSION` and `yzx whats_new` against the final note set before tagging and publishing.
acceptance_criteria: The repo and public release surfaces consistently use the exact `v15` identifier, the relevant validators/tests pass, the git tag exists and is pushed, and the GitHub release is published from the final aligned notes. The tag is not cut before the README, history, and release-note prep beads are complete.
close_reason: Completed: bumped repo surfaces to v15, validated README/upgrade notes/whats_new, pushed the v15 tag, and published the GitHub v15 release from the aligned note set.

title: Cache Beads contract workflow tooling
description: The Bead issue-contract workflow spends most of its runtime installing bd from source. Cache the bd binary and Go module/build caches around the pinned BD_VERSION so scheduled and manual contract checks avoid repeated cold installs.
acceptance_criteria: Validate Bead Issue Contract workflow restores/caches the bd binary and Go caches; Install bd reuses an existing binary when present and still verifies bd --version; cache key includes BD_VERSION; workflow YAML parses locally.
close_reason: Completed: added actions/cache for ~/go/bin/bd, Go modules, and Go build cache keyed by BD_VERSION and runner OS. Install step now puts GOPATH/bin on PATH, skips go install when bd is already cached, and still runs bd --version. Verified both touched workflow YAML files parse with python3/PyYAML and the install script body passes bash -n.

title: Allow controlling sidebar width in % from yazelix.toml
close_reason: GitHub issue #495 is closed; tracker synced.

title: Add emoji support (status/UI)
description: Add an emoji insertion path inside Yazelix instead of treating emoji support as a vague cosmetic request. The main user value is being able to insert emoji quickly from the terminal workspace, either through a lightweight emoji picker keybinding or by exposing a good emoji CLI in the environment if that gives a cleaner result.
design: Prefer the smallest UX that feels native in Yazelix. First evaluate whether a dependable emoji CLI is enough or whether Yazelix should wire a dedicated keybinding that opens an emoji menu or picker. Keep the feature opt-in or low-friction, avoid disturbing existing keymaps, and make sure the experience works in the managed shell/editor workflow instead of only in isolation.
acceptance_criteria: Users can insert emoji from a Yazelix session without leaving the workspace. The chosen flow is documented and ergonomic enough to use regularly. If a keybinding or menu approach is used, it avoids obvious key conflicts and fails clearly when the underlying emoji tool is unavailable. Add unit tests for picker or command-selection logic, integration tests for the picker or CLI wiring, and an end-to-end script that records the invoked path and resulting insertion behavior with detailed logs.
close_reason: Not planned: low-value compared with other work; any useful residue is better handled as optional docs rather than a first-class Yazelix feature.

title: Deliver a superb upgrade UX for config changes and releases
description: Build a coherent upgrade UX for Yazelix config and release changes across startup, doctor, migration commands, changelog surfaces, and first run post-upgrade messaging. Users should never have to diff templates or guess why an update broke their setup.
design: Treat this as one product system, not four unrelated tasks. Use one shared migration knowledge layer for known config changes, one clear human facing upgrade notes surface, and explicit user journeys for failure and recovery: startup failure, doctor diagnosis, safe migrate preview and apply, and first run after an upgrade. Startup must stay read only and fail fast with actionable guidance; explicit commands such as yzx config migrate or yzx doctor --fix may perform safe deterministic repairs with backup. Changes that define supported user visible behavior should leave behind focused specs under docs/specs with traceability to the relevant bead and defended tests. Add a cheap validator lane for this contract: repo-consistency checks should run locally through prek and in CI, while diff-aware enforcement for upgrade-sensitive changes should run in CI so version bumps and config-contract changes cannot quietly bypass migration or release-note obligations.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a coherent upgrade UX: known stale config cases produce precise actionable diagnostics; safe deterministic config migrations can be previewed and applied with backup; maintainers have a stable changelog and upgrade-notes contract for recording breaking changes; and the first interactive run after a real version bump can show a concise once per version summary with a way to reopen it later. Add comprehensive unit coverage for rule logic and state handling, plus high signal end to end scripts with detailed logging for stale config launch failures, doctor flows, migrate preview and apply flows, changelog or release-note validation, and version-bump summary behavior. Add cheap contract validation in the repo validator lane and wire it into both prek and CI so upgrade-sensitive work cannot silently skip the required notes or migration metadata.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Make stale config diagnostics migration-aware across startup and doctor
description: When Yazelix hits a known stale config value or field, surface a precise migration-aware diagnostic at the exact failure boundary instead of collapsing into a generic startup or generation failure. The same classification should also appear in yzx doctor so users can understand what changed and what the safest next action is.
design: Consume the shared migration registry from the migrate work instead of inventing a separate error taxonomy here. Startup must stay read only: no silent rewrites, no hidden fallbacks, and no generic wrap up that hides the real stale config problem. Diagnostics should name the exact config path and stale value, state what changed and in which real Yazelix version or date when known, distinguish safe auto-fixable cases from manual-only cases, and point to the right next step such as yzx config migrate, yzx doctor --fix when explicitly invoked, a one line manual edit, or the changelog surface. Cover startup, refresh, and doctor paths with the same underlying classification and copy contracts, even if the surrounding wrapper command differs.
acceptance_criteria: A stale config such as zellij.widget_tray containing layout fails with a targeted actionable message instead of a generic Failed to generate Zellij configuration wrapper. yzx doctor reports the same migration-aware finding with the same path, rationale, and next steps, and safe cases clearly advertise whether yzx config migrate or yzx doctor --fix can resolve them while startup remains read only. Unknown or unsupported schema problems still fail clearly without pretending to have a migration. Add comprehensive unit tests for finding classification, message formatting, multi-finding aggregation, and safe versus manual guidance. Add end to end scripts with detailed logging that launch or refresh Yazelix from temp homes with stale configs, capture the full failure output, and verify the doctor output for both a known migration and an unknown invalid config case.
notes:
Planning refinements:
- Create a focused spec such as docs/specs/stale_config_diagnostics.md.
- Keep startup read only; explicit repair happens only via dedicated commands.
- Prefer one message-rendering layer so start_yazelix, refresh, and doctor do not drift in terminology or suggested actions.
- Preserve a clear distinction between known migrations, unknown invalid values, missing fields, and non-config runtime failures.
- Verification plan: unit coverage for classified findings and message rendering, plus end to end Nushell scripts that log full launch and refresh stderr or stdout, doctor output, and the exact message differences between known migration cases and unsupported schema errors.
close_reason: Completed: added shared stale-config diagnostics, migration-aware startup and refresh failures, doctor reporting and doctor --fix integration, plus spec-backed unit and e2e coverage for known migrations versus unsupported config problems.

title: Add a root CHANGELOG.md and a structured upgrade-notes contract
description: Add the user facing upgrade-notes surface for Yazelix: a concise root CHANGELOG.md that highlights user visible changes, breaking config changes, and migration notes, with a clean relationship to the deeper docs history. If the first-run summary or diagnostics need structured note data, define that contract here instead of relying on brittle markdown scraping.
design: Treat this as a product contract, not just a markdown file. The system needs one short human facing changelog surface and, if required by the upgrade summary flow, one canonical structured data source that records version, date, highlights, breaking changes, and migration actions. Prefer a simple Nushell-friendly structured file such as docs/upgrade_notes.toml rather than scraping markdown. Keep docs/history.md as the long narrative history, but make CHANGELOG.md the practical upgrade document users can find immediately. Do not invent fake versions; keep YAZELIX_VERSION, changelog entries, and any structured upgrade-note metadata aligned. Link the changelog from the obvious user surfaces and define what maintainers must record whenever a config or behavior change can break upgrades. This bead also owns the upgrade contract validator that should run cheaply in prek for repo consistency and more strictly in CI for diff-aware enforcement on upgrade-sensitive changes.
acceptance_criteria: The repo has a root CHANGELOG.md with a stable format that emphasizes Added, Changed, Fixed, Breaking, and Migration notes for real Yazelix versions, and it is clearly connected to the deeper docs history without duplicating vague prose. A structured upgrade-note source such as docs/upgrade_notes.toml is canonical for machine consumption and the markdown surface is derived from or explicitly aligned with it rather than parsed heuristically. Add a validator such as validate_upgrade_contract.nu and wire it into both .pre-commit-config.yaml and .github/workflows/ci.yml. In prek it must perform the cheap current-version and metadata cross-checks defined in this bead. In CI it must additionally perform diff-aware enforcement for the exact guarded files listed in this bead. The validator must reject current-version note drift, missing required fields, bad upgrade_impact combinations, missing migration coverage, and guarded config-contract changes that land without either matching migration metadata or an explicit acknowledged manual-only or no-user-action note. Leave behind detailed logs for both the passing and failing validator paths.
notes:
Planning refinements:
- Create a focused spec such as docs/specs/upgrade_notes_contract.md.
- Prefer one canonical structured source of upgrade metadata and treat CHANGELOG.md as the human-facing projection of that contract or as an explicitly synchronized sibling, not an unrelated document.
- Record for each version the release date, headline summary, optional highlights, optional breaking section, and explicit migration actions when relevant.
- Keep the enforcement narrow and high-signal: use prek for cheap repo-consistency checks and CI for diff-aware checks against curated upgrade-sensitive surfaces.
- Non-goals: dumping every internal refactor into the changelog, inventing placeholder versions, maintaining two drifting sources of truth, or using noisy diff heuristics that trigger on unrelated edits.

Concrete validator spec:
- Canonical structured source: prefer docs/upgrade_notes.toml.
- Human-facing surface: root CHANGELOG.md.
- Version source of truth: nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu.
- Migration registry source of truth from yazelix-cr3: prefer nushell/scripts/utils/config_migrations.nu.

Exact initial guarded files for CI diff-aware enforcement:
- nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu
- yazelix_default.toml
- home_manager/module.nix
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_schema.nu
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_migrations.nu
- docs/upgrade_notes.toml
- CHANGELOG.md

Files intentionally excluded from the first hard guard set to avoid noisy false positives:
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_parser.nu unless a later pass can prove a narrow high-signal rule for parser-only contract drift.

Structured upgrade-note contract for the current version entry:
- version = current YAZELIX_VERSION
- date = real release date
- headline = short summary
- upgrade_impact = one of no_user_action, migration_available, manual_action_required
- acknowledged_guarded_changes = list of guarded file paths intentionally changed for this version
- migration_ids = list of migration rule ids when upgrade_impact = migration_available
- manual_actions = list of explicit user actions when upgrade_impact = manual_action_required

What validate_upgrade_contract.nu should run in prek:
- verify CHANGELOG.md exists
- verify docs/upgrade_notes.toml exists
- verify current YAZELIX_VERSION has an entry in docs/upgrade_notes.toml
- verify current YAZELIX_VERSION appears in CHANGELOG.md
- verify current version entry has non-empty date, headline, upgrade_impact, and acknowledged_guarded_changes fields
- verify upgrade_impact enum is valid
- verify migration_ids are present only when upgrade_impact = migration_available
- verify manual_actions are present only when upgrade_impact = manual_action_required
- verify every migration_id referenced by upgrade notes exists in the migration registry
- verify every migration rule id that is flagged as user-visible for the current version is either referenced by upgrade notes or explicitly marked internal-only in the migration registry schema

What validate_upgrade_contract.nu should additionally run in CI:
- inspect the git diff against the PR base or previous pushed commit
- if nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu changes YAZELIX_VERSION, fail unless the same diff updates both docs/upgrade_notes.toml and CHANGELOG.md for the new version
- for each changed file in the guarded set {yazelix_default.toml, home_manager/module.nix, nushell/scripts/utils/config_schema.nu, nushell/scripts/utils/config_migrations.nu}, fail unless that exact path is listed in acknowledged_guarded_changes for the current version entry
- if any guarded config surface changed and upgrade_impact = no_user_action, allow it only when the guarded-file acknowledgements are present and both migration_ids and manual_actions are empty
- if upgrade_impact = migration_available, fail unless migration_ids is non-empty and every referenced rule exists
- if upgrade_impact = manual_action_required, fail unless manual_actions is non-empty
- fail if docs/upgrade_notes.toml changed the current version entry but CHANGELOG.md did not change in the same diff
- fail if CHANGELOG.md changed the current version entry but docs/upgrade_notes.toml did not change in the same diff

Preferred repo wiring:
- add validate_upgrade_contract.nu to .pre-commit-config.yaml as a cheap local hook
- add validate_upgrade_contract.nu as an explicit CI step in .github/workflows/ci.yml
- keep validator logs detailed enough to show current version, guarded files touched in the diff, acknowledged_guarded_changes, selected migration ids, and the exact missing requirement when failing.

Verification plan: validator coverage for version alignment and required fields, plus an end to end validation script with detailed logging that shows the current version lookup, rendered markdown entry presence, the guarded files touched in a synthetic diff, and the exact failure when upgrade-note metadata is incomplete or a guarded config surface changed without the required migration coverage.
close_reason: Completed: added the root CHANGELOG, structured upgrade notes, local and CI upgrade-contract validation, and e2e coverage so guarded upgrade-sensitive changes cannot bypass notes or migration metadata.

title: Show and reopen a first-run upgrade summary after Yazelix version changes
