description: Persist the last seen Yazelix version and, on the first relevant interactive run after an upgrade, show a concise summary of what changed, what might break, and what action the user should take. Users must also have a way to reopen that summary later instead of losing it after the first display.
design: Build this on top of the structured upgrade-note contract and shared migration registry rather than inventing another freeform message path. The summary should trigger once per real version change in the right interactive surfaces, stay quiet on repeated launches or non interactive command flows, and highlight breaking changes or migration actions without turning startup into noisy release marketing. Prefer a dedicated reopen command such as yzx whats_new so users can intentionally revisit the current version summary without dumping the full changelog. If the current config is affected by known migrations, the summary should point to the exact docs or command surfaces that help, without attempting hidden rewrites.
acceptance_criteria: After simulating a version bump, the first relevant interactive run shows a concise upgrade summary that is keyed to the actual new version, includes any breaking or migration notes, and does not repeat on the second run for the same version. Users can reopen the same summary on demand through a supported command surface. The feature ignores non interactive flows that should stay quiet, and it does not claim notes for versions that are not represented in the release-note contract. Add comprehensive unit tests for version-state persistence, note selection, and suppression logic, plus end to end scripts with detailed logging that simulate previous and current version state, capture the first run and second run outputs, and verify the manual reopen path.
notes:
Planning refinements:
- Create a focused spec such as docs/specs/first_run_upgrade_summary.md.
- Define the state boundary explicitly: store last-seen version in a Yazelix-managed state location, separate from the user config file.
- Trigger only in relevant interactive entrypoints; keep env-only or scripted flows quiet unless the user explicitly asks for notes.
- Prefer yzx whats_new as the reopen surface, with behavior scoped to the current installed version rather than the whole historical changelog.
- Verification plan: unit coverage for state persistence and suppression logic, plus end to end Nushell scripts that simulate first run, second run, and manual reopen while logging version state files, selected note data, and rendered output.
close_reason: Completed: added shared upgrade-summary state and rendering, a new yzx whats_new reopen command, interactive first-run upgrade summaries, and spec-backed unit/e2e coverage for suppression and migration-aware guidance.

title: Revamp docs/history.md around generated recent releases and narrative eras
description: Rework docs/history.md so it tells the current release story instead of preserving the older branch-era or Classic-maintenance narrative. The document should keep its long-form narrative role, but recent release facts should stay aligned with the structured upgrade notes and with the new decision that v14 is a historical, unsupported line while v15 is the live trimmed release.
design:
Treat this as role clarity plus factual cleanup, not a history wipe. docs/upgrade_notes.toml should remain the canonical recent-release fact source, CHANGELOG.md should remain the concise upgrade-facing surface, and docs/history.md should be the narrative companion. But the narrative itself must now reflect the current product stance clearly: dynamically managed runtimes and the broader Classic runtime-manager scope were out of Yazelix scope, v14 is historical context rather than a maintained fallback line, and v15 is the live workspace-focused release with the slimmer runtime surface, first-party Ghostty path, and fast popup/menu-centered UX.

Do not preserve wording that recommends staying on v14 or frames v14 as a line that will continue receiving support. Keep the Rust-forward framing as a later roadmap, not the identity of the v15 release itself. Prefer a recent-release block sourced from or tightly validated against upgrade notes so the history page cannot drift from the GitHub releases, changelog, and whats_new data.
acceptance_criteria: docs/history.md has a clear long-form role distinct from CHANGELOG.md, and its maintained recent-release narrative matches the current product stance: v14 is historical and unsupported, v15 is the live trimmed release, dynamic runtime management is explicitly out of scope, and the v15-era workspace UX is described accurately. Recent release facts are sourced from or tightly validated against docs/upgrade_notes.toml so the history page does not silently drift from the canonical release surfaces.
close_reason: Completed: docs/history.md now has a distinct narrative role, adds the live v15 release story, and frames v14 as historical and unsupported in line with upgrade notes.

title: Define next Rust/Nu control-plane deletion tranche
description:
Define the next architectural simplification tranche after the closed lj7z Nu-deletion wave.

Design:
Re-inventory the surviving Nushell, POSIX, and Rust bridge/control-plane boundaries after recent runtime, doctor, install-owner, launch, and validation work. Identify which surfaces still own deterministic config/state/materialization policy outside Rust, which shell files are irreducible process entrypoints, which validators or docs checks have grown into new control-plane mass, and which old lj7z targets are truly complete versus reopened by later work. Prefer deletion and owner transfer over wrappers.

Acceptance Criteria:
A current deletion map names exact files or modules, target owners, stop conditions, and verification gates. It creates implementation follow-ups only for cuts that remove real ownership or reduce main-repo runtime/maintainer surface. It records rejected candidates where shell/process boundaries should intentionally remain, and it updates any relevant Nu/Rust budget ceilings or owner notes instead of relying on the closed lj7z tranche.
notes:
Inventory snapshot 2026-05-23:
- Current tracked Nu surface from wc: 1,651 lines across 12 .nu files. Runtime/product Nu floor excluding the Ghostty shader build helper is 1,360 lines across 11 files.
- Current POSIX shell surface under shells/posix is 869 lines. runtime_env.sh and yzx_cli.sh are real current-shell/bootstrap owners, not immediate Rust targets.
- The old broad lj7z map is complete and should not be reopened as a generic Rust rewrite. The live tranche should target exact concepts that can disappear.

Accepted deletion map:
1. Rust-own yzx menu and delete the internal Nu public route. Files: nushell/scripts/yzx/menu.nu, rust_core/yazelix_core/src/internal_nu_runner.rs, rust_core/yazelix_core/src/bin/yzx.rs, rust_core/yazelix_core/src/public_command_surface.rs. Payoff: delete the last public yzx internal_nu route and remove the generic Nu module trampoline concept. Verification: menu metadata/category tests, argument rendering parity replaced by direct Rust menu tests, popup and pane mode behavior checks.
2. Split startup handoff planning out of start_yazelix_inner.nu. Files: nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix_inner.nu, nushell/scripts/setup/welcome.nu, nushell/scripts/utils/yzx_core_bridge.nu, Rust startup/session/materialization helpers. Payoff: Nu keeps human prompt and final zellij exec only; Rust returns a typed startup handoff plan and owns config snapshot/materialization/handoff recording details. Verification: yzx_control_runtime_surface startup tests, runtime materialization tests, startup profile evidence.
3. Collapse shellhook setup into one Rust-owned prepare command. Files: nushell/scripts/setup/environment.nu, nushell/scripts/utils/runtime_paths.nu, nushell/scripts/utils/yzx_core_bridge.nu, initializer and extern sync Rust commands. Payoff: environment.nu stops orchestrating multiple helper calls and becomes a tiny shellhook wrapper around one typed result. Verification: shell initializer tests, extern sync tests, validate-nushell-syntax, runtime build.
4. Delete remaining Nu runtime helper modules after callers shrink. Files: runtime_paths.nu, runtime_commands.nu, constants.nu, runtime_defaults.nu, surviving parts of yzx_core_bridge.nu. Payoff: remove helper bags only after no caller depends on them; do not replace them with equivalent wrappers. Verification: rg import search, validate-contracts, validate-nushell-syntax, focused launch/menu/startup tests.
5. Rebaseline shell floor and Rust ownership budgets after accepted cuts. Files/docs: docs/subsystem_code_inventory.md, docs/contracts/runtime_shell_floor_contract.md, docs/rust_code_inventory.md, config_metadata/rust_ownership_budget.toml. Payoff: ratchet ceilings down and prevent later agent code from spending the savings silently.

Rejected for this tranche:
- Port shells/posix/runtime_env.sh wholesale to Rust: it mutates the current shell environment and must stay sourceable POSIX until an equivalent shell contract exists.
- Port nushell/config/config.nu or stack_prompt_guard.nu to Rust: they are interactive shell-local config/prompt behavior, not control-plane owners.
- Touch configs/terminal_emulators/ghostty/shaders/build_shaders.nu: it is a shader asset build helper and outside this runtime control-plane tranche.
- Move final zellij process exec to Rust immediately: only worthwhile if start_yazelix_inner.nu can disappear or become a tiny wrapper; otherwise it adds a wrapper without deleting an owner.
close_reason: Completed current control-plane deletion map. Inventory recorded current Nu/POSIX surface, accepted menu/startup/shellhook/helper/budget cut sequence, rejected false-positive targets that should stay shell-local or out of scope, created implementation children yazelix-2ak5y.1 through yazelix-2ak5y.5, and added blocking dependencies so helper deletion and budget rebaseline wait for the caller cuts.

title: Rust-own yzx menu and delete the internal Nu route
description:
Move the yzx menu command palette out of nushell/scripts/yzx/menu.nu and into Rust so the public yzx root no longer needs an internal Nu route.

Design:
Implement the menu surface as a Rust-owned command that reads the existing Rust command metadata directly, presents the same fzf palette, supports direct pane mode and popup open behavior, and dispatches selected commands through the stable yzx entrypoint. Delete the generic internal_nu_runner trampoline if yzx menu is the last caller. Keep this as behavior preservation, not a redesign of the command palette.

Acceptance Criteria:
yzx menu, yzx menu --popup, and yzx menu --pane preserve the current user-visible behavior. nushell/scripts/yzx/menu.nu is deleted. rust_core/yazelix_core/src/internal_nu_runner.rs and the InternalNu route variant are deleted if no other caller remains. Command metadata remains the single category/source of truth. Tests cover palette item construction, popup response handling, selected-command dispatch argv, and unknown/bad flag errors. Runtime validation includes yzx_repo_validator validate-contracts, validate-nushell-syntax, and focused yzx public command tests.
close_reason: Completed: yzx menu is Rust-owned via yzx_control, nushell/scripts/yzx/menu.nu and internal_nu_runner.rs are deleted, the InternalNu public route is gone, ownership docs/contracts reflect the smaller Nu floor, and stale validator hooks were removed. Verification passed: cargo check -p yazelix_core --all-targets; cargo check -p yazelix_maintainer --all-targets; cargo test -p yazelix_core menu_commands; cargo test -p yazelix_core public_command_surface; cargo test -p yazelix_core command_owners_include_rust_and_nu_routes; cargo test -p yazelix_core --test yzx_core_command_metadata runtime_ownership_graph_includes_command_and_manifest_owners; yzx_control menu --help; validate-nushell-syntax; validate-contracts; cargo fmt --check. validate-rust-ownership-budget still fails without stale internal_nu_runner/menu ownership errors; residual ceiling/unbudgeted-file work is recorded on yazelix-2ak5y.5.

title: Split startup handoff planning out of Nushell
description:
Shrink nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix_inner.nu by moving deterministic startup handoff planning to Rust.

Design:
Create a Rust-owned startup handoff plan that performs or coordinates runtime materialization, session config snapshot writing, upgrade summary lookup, layout/default-shell resolution, and handoff-context capture. Nushell should keep only startup-shell UX that genuinely needs the interactive shell: welcome prompt sequencing, optional macchina display, startup profile boundary labels if still needed, and the final zellij process exec. Do not move final shell behavior to Rust unless that deletes a whole owner.

Acceptance Criteria:
start_yazelix_inner.nu no longer assembles deterministic JSON requests for materialization, session snapshot, or handoff capture. The Rust plan returns typed fields for layout path, default shell, working directory, session env, welcome facts, and visible warnings. Existing startup behavior, skip-welcome behavior, setup-only/profile behavior, and failure messages remain covered by focused tests. Verification includes yzx_control_runtime_surface startup tests, runtime materialization tests, startup/session snapshot tests, validate-nushell-syntax, and one packaged runtime build.

title: Delete or narrow automatic shellhook setup
description: Re-evaluate whether Yazelix needs a product shellhook at all. The current runtime setup path invokes nushell/scripts/setup/environment.nu before launch, and managed shell configs source generated initializer files. This is not the same as silently editing a user's global shell rc files, but it still bundles launch preflight, initializer generation, extern sync, log cleanup, and runtime asset checks behind a broad shellhook concept.
design:
Delete-first target: normal Yazelix launch/setup should not have a product shellhook abstraction. Move launch/runtime preflight work to Rust-owned launch preparation before Zellij and delete nushell/scripts/setup/environment.nu if no current-shell caller remains. Preserve the existing managed-pane UX: zoxide, starship, mise, atuin, carapace, and the generated yzx extern bridge are still auto-generated for Yazelix-owned shell configs and sourced inside Yazelix-managed panes. Keep yzx run/env child-process environment computation in Rust; any current-shell loading remains explicit and opt-in, scoped to Yazelix-owned paths, and must not mutate a user's global shell rc files.

Implementation boundary: Rust launch/setup preflight may call existing Rust initializer and extern-sync modules directly, ensure Yazelix-owned state/log directories, trim Yazelix-owned setup/welcome logs, and check packaged runtime assets. It should not add a broader shellhook.prepare wrapper, a new shell trampoline, or another Nushell helper layer.

Dependency decision: no new production or dev crates. Use std plus existing yazelix_core modules for initializer generation, startup facts/config, state paths, command metadata extern sync, and runtime asset checks. Rejected alternatives: a Rust wrapper that shells out to environment.nu, a new shellhook command surface, or a third-party setup/orchestration crate. Packaging impact should be deletion of one runtime Nu script plus Rust-owned setup behavior in the existing core package.
acceptance_criteria: Normal launch and --setup-only no longer invoke nushell/scripts/setup/environment.nu. The remaining shellhook/current-shell requirement is stated explicitly: normal launch has none; any current-shell loading is explicit/opt-in and has no runtime preflight/log cleanup/extern sync policy. Managed Yazelix panes keep the same automatic tool UX by generating the same aggregate shell initializer files and yzx Nushell extern bridge under Yazelix-owned config/state paths. Call sites for environment.nu, managed shell configs, yzx env, yzx run, and launch setup are audited. Verification covers launch/setup-only behavior, yzx run/env behavior, generated initializer/extern refresh, validate-nushell-syntax, validate-contracts, and focused Rust checks/tests for changed setup code.
notes:
Pivot from the initial shellhook.prepare approach after maintainer review: broad shellhooks are invasive and should not become the next abstraction layer by default. I reverted the uncommitted shellhook.prepare prototype and left this bead as a delete-first audit/implementation slice.

Maintainer UX requirement from 2026-05-23: preserve automatic zoxide/starship/etc. setup inside Yazelix-managed panes. The deletion target is the broad shellhook owner, not the generated initializer UX.
close_reason: Completed: normal launch/setup no longer invokes nushell/scripts/setup/environment.nu, and that runtime Nu setup script is deleted. yzx enter now runs Rust-owned setup preflight directly: selected-shell initializers, managed shell stubs, yzx extern bridge refresh, state/log directory creation, and zjstatus runtime-asset validation. Managed pane UX is preserved for zoxide/starship/etc. via the same generated aggregate initializer files; yzx run and yzx env remain explicit Rust child/current-shell environment surfaces. Updated docs/contracts, ownership graph, maintainer shell setup, and stale shellHook wording. Verification passed: cargo fmt --all; cargo check -p yazelix_core --all-targets; cargo check -p yazelix_maintainer --all-targets; cargo test -p yazelix_core initializer_commands; cargo test -p yazelix_core command_metadata::tests::sync_yzx_extern_bridge; cargo test -p yazelix_core --test yzx_control_runtime_surface; validate-nushell-syntax; validate-contracts; validate-rust-test-traceability; nix eval .#devShells.x86_64-linux.default.drvPath; git diff --check. LOC scorecard HEAD..temp: runtime -53 LOC, maintainer -1, docs -4, tests +96, total code +48; budget follow-up recorded on yazelix-2ak5y.5.

title: Delete remaining Nu runtime helper modules after caller cuts
description: Final helper-bag deletion pass after the menu, startup, and shell setup caller cuts land. This bead should remove or inline surviving Nushell utility helpers only where the caller graph proves they no longer own distinct shell-local behavior.
design:
Delete or inline the remaining Nu helper modules only after menu, startup, and shell setup callers have been reduced. After yazelix-2ak5y.3, environment.nu is expected to be gone from normal launch/setup, so this pass should focus on surviving imports from start_yazelix_inner.nu, welcome.nu, managed Nushell config, and any genuine shell-local callers.

Target nushell/scripts/utils/runtime_paths.nu, runtime_commands.nu, constants.nu, runtime_defaults.nu, and surviving yzx_core_bridge.nu helpers. Treat this as a final helper-bag deletion pass, not a place to add equivalent wrappers. Surviving behavior should live either in Rust typed owners, POSIX current-shell bootstrap, or the one remaining Nu caller that genuinely needs it.
acceptance_criteria: Every exported helper in the targeted Nu utility modules has either no caller and is deleted, is inlined into a single retained caller, or is replaced by an existing Rust/POSIX owner without behavior drift. The pass deletes any now-empty modules and updates docs/contracts that list the retained shell floor. Verification includes rg import search, validate-nushell-syntax, validate-contracts, focused launch/startup/setup tests, and a runtime build if any packaged entrypoint changed.

title: Rebaseline shell-floor and Rust ownership budgets after control-plane cuts
description:
Update the retained shell floor and Rust ownership budgets after the accepted control-plane deletion cuts land.

Design:
Refresh docs/subsystem_code_inventory.md, docs/contracts/runtime_shell_floor_contract.md, docs/rust_code_inventory.md, config_metadata/rust_ownership_budget.toml, and any related validator expectations. Ratchet ceilings downward when code is deleted. If Rust grows temporarily to delete larger Nu/POSIX ownership, record the budget debt and child owner instead of silently raising ceilings.

Acceptance Criteria:
The inventory and budget files match the measured tree after the menu/startup/shellhook/helper cuts. Runtime-side Nu LOC and file counts are current, shell-survivor reasons are explicit, Rust family ceilings are lowered where possible, and validators pass or report only intentionally documented warnings. Verification includes validate-contracts, validate-nushell-syntax, validate-rust-ownership-budget, and the repo LOC scorecard for the tranche.
notes:
After yazelix-2ak5y.1, the stale internal_nu_runner.rs budget path was replaced with rust_core/yazelix_core/src/menu_commands.rs without raising ceilings. validate-rust-ownership-budget still fails in the current tree: existing unbudgeted popup_runtime_command.rs, repo_canary_session.rs, repo_child_release.rs; total measured 67243 LOC > allowed 65704; core_workspace_and_pane_integration measured 14484 LOC > 13869 after the menu Rust owner lands. Treat this as the tranche budget/payback audit, not as permission to raise ceilings silently.

After yazelix-2ak5y.3, nushell/scripts/setup/environment.nu is deleted and normal launch/setup preflight is Rust-owned in launch_commands/enter.rs. This preserves automatic generated shell initializers and yzx extern refresh for managed panes, but it adds Rust setup code and focused runtime-surface tests. Rebaseline this explicitly during the budget pass rather than silently absorbing the temporary Rust growth.

title: Rename Yazelix Terminal id to yzxterm
close_reason: Renamed the Yazelix Terminal selector/runtime id from yazelix_terminal to yzxterm across CLI docs, supported terminal lists, config schema/metadata, Home Manager runtime_variant values, flake outputs (#yzxterm/#runtime_yzxterm), launch/runtime materialization, and focused tests. No legacy alias kept because the surface has not been pushed.

title: Migrate roadmap planning into Beads and remove docs_explore/roadmap.md
description: Create Beads issues/dependencies that replace the exploratory roadmap, then delete docs_explore/roadmap.md once the migration is complete.
close_reason: Roadmap migrated into Beads

title: Main roadmap sequence
description: Top-level roadmap track for the major product and packaging work that should shape Yazelix after the current stabilization pass.
design: This bead is a planning container, not a feature to implement directly. Its job is to keep the major streams visible without forcing fake blocker edges between unrelated work. The child beads under this epic should use real technical dependencies where they exist and otherwise remain parallelizable so Beads triage stays honest.
acceptance_criteria: The main roadmap children are well-scoped, their blockers reflect actual prerequisites rather than aesthetic ordering, and the graph remains usable for bv triage. Revisit this epic whenever major roadmap themes change rather than stuffing execution detail into the container itself.
close_reason: Obsolete grouping container; replaced by labels and direct dependencies

title: Harden config and build errors
description: Make Yazelix consistently fail fast with high-signal errors around launch, config validation, rebuilds, refreshes, and other common startup or recovery failures.
design: Treat this as a reliability umbrella, not a single patch. One important slice is already done: the maintainer update flow now uses canary-gated `yzx dev update` behavior so broken lockfile updates stay local instead of propagating. The remaining work is to harden the broader user-facing reliability surface: startup and launcher failures, classification of config versus generated-state versus host-environment problems, and consistent rebuild or refresh failure reporting. Keep this epic as a parent tracker and push concrete work into child issues rather than letting it absorb unrelated error-quality ideas.
acceptance_criteria: The remaining high-risk launch, config, refresh, and rebuild flows surface actionable errors instead of silent or low-signal failures. The child issues under this epic should cover startup error hardening, failure classification, and rebuild or refresh reporting with focused tests and logs for each surface.
close_reason: Completed the reliability slices for hermetic tests, startup/launch hardening, failure classification, refresh/rebuild reporting, and CI/Home Manager coverage

title: Make yzx command tests hermetic in CI
description: Refactor nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_commands.nu so it can run on a clean CI runner without assuming the repo is mounted at ~/.config/yazelix, without assuming generated Yazelix state already exists, and without hard-failing when optional host tools like nix, devenv, zoxide, zellij, yazi, or helix are absent. The suite should provision any config or generated state it needs, use repo-relative entrypoints, and explicitly skip or stub checks whose external toolchain is not available.
close_reason: Completed via hermetic test harness, split suites, and blocking CI gate

title: Launch and startup error hardening
description: Harden Yazelix launch and startup paths so missing runtime roots, missing generated assets, terminal-launch failures, and startup precondition failures fail fast with actionable messages instead of looking hung or broken.
design: Focus on the user-facing startup path first. Distinguish between bad invocation context, missing generated runtime state, missing external tools, and terminal-launch failures. Error messages should say what failed, what command or asset was involved when relevant, and the shortest recovery action.
acceptance_criteria: Common startup failure modes surface clear, consistent errors with recovery hints. Add focused tests for bad runtime roots, missing generated assets, and terminal-launch failure handling, plus logs that preserve the failing command or asset path.
close_reason: Hardened POSIX launchers, desktop launcher runtime checks, and startup regression coverage for missing runtime scripts and launch preconditions

title: Classify config, generated-state, and host-dependency failures
description: Make Yazelix explicitly distinguish bad user config, generated-state drift, and missing host dependencies so failures are classified correctly and recovery guidance matches the actual problem.
design: Build a small failure-classification model instead of ad-hoc messaging. The important boundary is whether the problem comes from the user config, generated Yazelix state, or missing host capabilities. Doctor, launch, and refresh paths should converge on the same classification so users get consistent guidance.
acceptance_criteria: Representative failures are classified correctly as config, generated-state, or host-dependency problems, and the surfaced recovery hint matches the class. Add focused tests for invalid config values, stale generated state, and absent external tools.
close_reason: Added explicit config/generated-state/host-dependency failure classes with focused regressions for each representative case

title: Standardize rebuild and refresh failure reporting
description: Make rebuild and refresh failures consistently surface the failing command, a useful stderr tail, and a concrete recovery hint instead of uneven command-specific messages.
design: Unify the way refresh, rebuild, and update helpers report failures. Instead of bespoke command-specific output, preserve the failing command, the most useful stderr tail, and a short recovery hint. Keep the summaries compact, but make the underlying failure reconstructable from logs.
acceptance_criteria: Refresh and rebuild failure paths produce consistent high-signal summaries and detailed logs. Add focused tests for failed rebuild and refresh scenarios, verifying that the command, stderr tail, and recovery hint are all surfaced.
close_reason: Standardized refresh and rebuild failure summaries with focused regressions

title: Fix Home Manager desktop entry evaluation regression
description: The Home Manager module declares the Yazelix desktop entry with an invalid Home Manager option path for StartupWMClass, causing module evaluation to fail before activation. Reproduce with a minimal Home Manager configuration evaluation against home_manager/module.nix. Keep the fix focused on restoring a working supported entrypoint, while leaving the broader question of Home Manager ownership and package-ready path modeling to the existing Home Manager packaging work.
design: Use the Home Manager desktop entry API correctly for desktop-file metadata instead of assuming the nixpkgs makeDesktopItem field names are accepted directly. Add a focused regression check that evaluates a minimal Home Manager configuration and verifies the Yazelix desktop entry exposes StartupWMClass through settings. Keep this as a Nix/Home Manager evaluation test, because the failure is in the Home Manager module contract rather than in the devenv runtime path. Surface it through yzx dev test by default so the supported Home Manager path stays covered in the normal maintainer loop.
acceptance_criteria: A minimal Home Manager evaluation with programs.yazelix.enable = true no longer fails on the Yazelix desktop entry. The desktop entry still carries the Yazelix StartupWMClass metadata. Add a focused regression test that reproduces the evaluation path and fails if the invalid option path comes back.
notes: Implementation direction now landed locally: home_manager/flake.nix exposes a Linux-only desktop_entry_smoke check whose derivation carries passthru.startupWMClass. The regression test resolves the current system and runs nix eval --raw --read-only --no-write-lock-file against that flake check attr instead of embedding a long inline expression. After pinning home_manager/flake.lock and measuring the cached path, the default suite remained fast enough, so the temporary --fast opt-out was removed rather than carrying extra CLI surface area.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Align CI with Home Manager smoke checks
description: The main CI workflow currently sets up Nushell and runs the yzx command tests, but the default test suite now includes a Home Manager smoke check that evaluates the home_manager flake via nix eval. CI should install Nix explicitly and keep the supported Home Manager path covered instead of depending on runner luck or environment drift. Consider whether to add a broader home_manager flake check step as dedicated CI coverage after the minimal setup is in place.
design: Install Nix explicitly in the main CI workflow so the default yzx dev test coverage has the tools it now requires. Keep the existing yzx command suite as the primary smoke path, and add a dedicated nix flake check ./home_manager --no-build step for broader Home Manager flake validation without forcing builds.
acceptance_criteria: CI installs Nix before running Yazelix tests. The main yzx command suite continues to run in CI. CI also evaluates the home_manager flake through nix flake check --no-build so Home Manager flake regressions are caught independently of the shell-level smoke tests.
notes: CI now installs Nix explicitly and sets determinate: false on DeterminateSystems/nix-installer-action@v21 so the workflow does not try to authenticate with FlakeHub for features Yazelix is not using. The remaining follow-up is action-runtime maintenance: GitHub warns that the Determinate actions in use still target Node.js 20, so version refresh or replacement is tracked separately.
close_reason: CI now installs Nix, runs the default yzx suite, and checks the Home Manager flake with nix flake check --no-build

title: Refresh CI actions for Node 24 compatibility
description: The current CI workflow uses DeterminateSystems/nix-installer-action@v21 and DeterminateSystems/magic-nix-cache-action@v13, and GitHub now warns that these actions are still on Node.js 20. Track the follow-up needed to verify newer tags or alternate actions that support Node.js 24 before GitHub flips the default runtime.
design: Use the latest available Determinate action tags, but stop waiting for them to switch runtimes on their own. Force JavaScript actions onto Node.js 24 in CI now so Yazelix discovers compatibility problems before GitHub flips the default globally. Keep FlakeHub disabled in both installer and cache setup because Yazelix does not need it for this workflow.
acceptance_criteria: CI no longer relies on the deprecated Node.js 20 action runtime. The workflow explicitly opts into Node.js 24 for JavaScript actions, keeps Nix installation and cache setup working, and avoids unnecessary FlakeHub auth paths.
notes: At implementation time, the latest available tags were nix-installer-action v21 and magic-nix-cache-action v13, and both still emitted the Node.js 20 deprecation warning. Since there was no newer compatible major tag to upgrade to immediately, the workflow now uses FORCE_JAVASCRIPT_ACTIONS_TO_NODE24=true as the concrete mitigation.
close_reason: Opted CI into Node 24 and disabled unnecessary FlakeHub paths in Determinate actions

title: Agent usage widgets in the Zellij widget tray
description:
Expose agent usage information in the Zellij zjstatus bar so AI tooling feels like a first-class workspace surface.

v15 ships no packs or llm-agents input. The ccusage family of binaries (ccusage, ccusage-codex, ccusage-amp, ccusage-opencode) must be opt-in, sourced from numtide's llm-agents flake. Both standalone flake users and Home Manager users must be supported.
design:
Layered opt-in across both flake and Home Manager paths:

**1. Flake input (packages layer)**
Add llm-agents as a new flake input. Provide an opt-in package variant or overlay (e.g. packages.${system}.yazelix-with-agent-tools) so standalone flake users can get ccusage binaries in their runtime PATH. numtide's binary cache is already trusted in the build policy.

**2. Home Manager option (config layer)**
Expose a typed option (e.g. yazelix.agentUsagePrograms) defaulting to []. When non-empty, the HM module pulls the matching packages from the llm-agents input and adds them to the runtime — same underlying packages, HM-friendly surface with validation.

**3. Widget tray (zjstatus layer)**
Add new widget tray entries (e.g. "claude_usage", "codex_usage"). The zjstatus command slots shell out to a wrapper script that checks whether the binary is in PATH at runtime. If present, it runs the binary and formats output. If absent, it emits nothing — zjstatus renders empty, tray stays clean. Use a long interval (60-300s) since usage data changes slowly.

This means the widget tray config works regardless of whether the user opted in via flake or HM — it only needs the binary present in PATH.

**Seams to modify:**
1. flake.nix — add llm-agents input, wire into package variants
2. home_manager/module.nix — add agentUsagePrograms option
3. configs/zellij/layouts/fragments/zjstatus_tab_template.kdl — add command_* entries
4. nushell/scripts/utils/layout_generator.nu:28 — extend allowed list and match block
5. config_metadata/main_config_contract.toml:239 — extend allowed_values
6. New wrapper script or zjstatus_widget.nu handler for runtime detection and formatting
acceptance_criteria:
- llm-agents flake input is added and ccusage binaries are available in the opt-in package variant
- HM option (agentUsagePrograms or similar) wires the same binaries when non-empty, defaults to []
- Adding "claude_usage" to zellij.widget_tray shows a compact usage indicator when ccusage is in PATH
- When the binary is missing, the widget renders nothing (no errors, no placeholder)
- Same pattern works for ccusage-codex, ccusage-amp, ccusage-opencode
- Allowlist, contract, and layout generator updated consistently
- Existing widget tray entries unaffected
- Both nix run / nix profile install and Home Manager paths work
notes:
Dependency decision, 2026-04-28:
- Production Rust crates: none new. Use existing serde_json and std::process/path/env for the yzx_control agent-usage command.
- Dev-only Rust crates: none new. Existing cargo tests and Nix eval/build smoke are enough for this slice.
- Nix dependency: add the numtide/llm-agents.nix flake input because ccusage binaries are deliberately opt-in external packages, not part of the default Yazelix runtime.
- In-house logic: small provider-to-binary mapping, runtime PATH detection, compact JSON summary parsing/formatting, widget allowlist entries, package extra-runtime plumbing.
- Rejected alternatives: vendoring ccusage, making AI usage tools default, reviving pack-style dependency groups, shell wrappers for zjstatus, or making missing binaries render placeholder text.
- Packaging impact: default package remains unchanged; opt-in package/Home Manager selections add ccusage, ccusage-codex, ccusage-amp, and/or ccusage-opencode to the Yazelix runtime tool surface.
Implementation result, 2026-04-28:
- Added llm-agents flake input and opt-in #yazelix_agent_tools / #runtime_agent_tools outputs. Default #yazelix remains unchanged.
- Added Home Manager programs.yazelix.agent_usage_programs as a typed opt-in list for ccusage, ccusage-codex, ccusage-amp, and ccusage-opencode; selected binaries are included inside the Yazelix runtime package.
- Added zellij.widget_tray entries claude_usage, codex_usage, amp_usage, and opencode_usage across default config comments, contract allowlist, Home Manager description, onboarding, Rust render plan, and yazelix_bar.
- Added yzx_control zellij agent-usage <provider>, which detects the matching ccusage binary on PATH, runs blocks --active --json, formats a compact colored segment, and emits an empty line when the binary is missing or has no usable data.
- Added zjstatus command slots with format {stdout}, so missing binaries do not leave static labels in the tray.
- Verification: cargo fmt --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml --all -- --check; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_bar; yzx_repo_validator validate-contracts; nix eval .#packages.x86_64-linux.yazelix_agent_tools.name; Home Manager module eval with agent_usage_programs; nix build --print-out-paths --no-link .#runtime_agent_tools; flake show lists runtime_agent_tools/yazelix_agent_tools; runtime_agent_tools exposes ccusage binaries in toolbin and libexec.
close_reason: Implemented opt-in agent usage widgets and runtime packaging

title: Evaluate a broader Rust/clap rewrite as a v16-or-later path after boundaries are proven
description: Keep the possibility of a broader Rust or clap rewrite on the table as a v16-or-later path, but only after the current v15.0 product, runtime model, and UX seams are proven well enough that a rewrite would clarify rather than scramble the system.
design: This remains intentionally late and skeptical. A broader Rust or clap rewrite should not be a reflexive response to complexity that really comes from fuzzy product semantics. Revisit it only after v15.0 has shipped as the trimmed non-Rust reboot, the runtime boundaries are proven, the Rust migration matrix exists, and the Rust/Nushell bridge strategy is explicit. The default assumption for v15.x should be incremental Rust helpers, not an immediate whole-CLI rewrite; v16 is the earliest release framing where a much broader Rust/clap move should become a visible product direction.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear justification document for any future broader Rust/clap rewrite path, including what problems it would solve, what parity must be preserved, whether it belongs in v16 or later, and what benchmarks or maintainability wins would justify the cost. Any eventual implementation must come with unit tests for rewritten helper logic, end-to-end parity comparison scripts against the current behavior, and detailed logs showing what changed and what remained equivalent.
notes:
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before any broader Rust/clap rewrite work, record the crate-vs-in-house plan. In particular, justify clap or any public-CLI framework against the private-helper bridge path and the current Nushell UX owner contract.
2026-04-19 guardrail:
- A broader rewrite is only worth considering if earlier beads have already deleted substantial Nushell owner seams rather than accumulating permanent parity and glue layers.
- Any future v16 proposal must include a deletion budget: which Nushell modules disappear, which bridges vanish, and which parity tests are transition-only and can be removed.
- If the case for a broader rewrite depends on carrying the current helper and bridge stack forward indefinitely, that is evidence against doing it.
2026-04-19 planning start:
- using this bead as the next Rust-forward planning slice after kt5.2, kt5.3, kt5.4, and the launch/startup stop-condition audit
- goal: write the justification/deletion-budget document for any broader v16-or-later Rust CLI path instead of forcing more v15.x helper ports
- dependency gate for planning: default assumption remains no public CLI framework in v15.x; any v16 proposal must justify clap against keeping the current helper-backed private bridge and must spell out which Nushell owners disappear
2026-04-19 planning completion:
- added docs/specs/v16_rust_cli_rewrite_evaluation.md as the go/no-go and deletion-budget document for any broader v16 public Rust CLI path
- anchored the recommendation in the current public command families, bridge contract, launch/startup stop condition, v15 trimmed runtime contract, and cross-language ownership map
- conclusion: a broader rewrite is only justified if Rust becomes the single public command-tree owner for the control-plane families and deletes the public Nushell registry plus extern-bridge seams; clap-as-thin-shim is explicitly rejected
- added the spec to docs/specs/spec_inventory.md
- validated with nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu
close_reason: Completed: broader v16 Rust/clap path now has a justification and deletion-budget spec in docs/specs/v16_rust_cli_rewrite_evaluation.md with explicit go/no-go gates and scope boundaries

title: Packaging-readiness refactor
description: Decouple the runtime from the repo checkout so Yazelix can be packaged and installed cleanly instead of assuming it lives inside ~/.config/yazelix as a writable source tree.
design: This is the architectural prerequisite behind serious packaging work. Inventory the hardcoded path assumptions, separate shipped assets from user config and generated state, and centralize the runtime path model so core flows work from an installed runtime just as well as from a git checkout. Be explicit about what becomes packaged data, what remains user-owned config, and what Yazelix should generate or cache at runtime.
acceptance_criteria: Core launch, env, refresh, and managed-tool flows no longer require a repo checkout as their runtime root. Add unit tests for path resolution helpers and config/state root derivation, plus end-to-end smoke tests that run the runtime from an installed or relocated path with detailed logs showing resolved asset, config, and state locations.
close_reason: Core packaging-readiness work is complete: split config/runtime roots, routed core launch/config/runtime helpers through the runtime model, removed repo-shaped callers, and added relocated-runtime smoke coverage proving core commands work away from the repo checkout. Remaining FlakeHub evaluation stays open as an orthogonal follow-up.

title: Remove repo-shaped ~/.config bootstrap from CI and runtime helpers
description: Eliminate the remaining expectation that Yazelix lives at ~/.config/yazelix during tests, CI, and helper scripts. The current CI workflow bootstraps a ~/.config/yazelix symlink only as a temporary compatibility layer. Follow through on the broader packaging/path-model work so command entrypoints, helper scripts, and validation paths resolve from the installed runtime or repo checkout explicitly instead of relying on HOME-relative repo paths.
close_reason: Completed: removed CI symlink bootstrap, resolved runtime helpers/tests via YAZELIX_DIR, and verified nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_commands.nu passes against a temp fixture without ~/.config bootstrap

title: Evaluate FlakeHub adoption for Yazelix
description: Evaluate whether FlakeHub would bring enough value to Yazelix to justify the additional platform coupling and paid features. Current immediate cache needs are better served by Cachix, while FlakeHub may still become useful as a future distribution/channel layer once Yazelix has a more mature package-first flake interface. Include current pricing, free/open-source eligibility, CI/cache use cases, authentication/public-cache behavior, and decision criteria for when adoption would become worthwhile.
design: Treat FlakeHub as a distribution and maintenance decision, not a generic ecosystem checkbox. Compare it against Yazelix's actual packaging maturity, expected install paths, binary-cache needs, CI ergonomics, open-source eligibility, and future flake-first direction. The decision should be time-aware: a no-for-now answer is valid if the product shape is not ready yet.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit adopt, defer, or reject decision on FlakeHub, with current value, costs, and future trigger conditions documented. If adoption is deferred, the bead records what would need to change in Yazelix's packaging model for FlakeHub to become worthwhile later.
notes:
Prime-beads 2026-05-04: Cachix now owns the immediate binary-cache story through yazelix-r1ax.2. Keep this FlakeHub bead focused on future distribution/channel value, not as the default cache fix. Recent local Home Manager output also showed an unauthenticated cache.flakehub.com 401, so adopting any FlakeHub cache path needs explicit auth/public-cache evidence before recommending it to users.
Prime-beads 2026-05-05: this bead predates the package-first/settings.jsonc direction. Keep any FlakeHub evaluation focused on distribution/channel value after the current Cachix and package work, not on an old repo+devenv+yazelix.toml installation story.

title: Packaging: stamp runtime-root into generated Zellij layouts and wrapper scripts
description: Remove repo-shaped runtime assumptions from generated Zellij layouts and wrapper scripts so the merged Zellij runtime can point at an installed runtime root instead of hardcoding ~/.config/yazelix.
close_reason: Generated Zellij layouts now stamp the configured runtime root, the wrapper scripts use relative imports, and the generated-config regression suite covers the packaging contract.

title: Packaging: route shell and terminal entrypoints through the runtime root
description: Make generated shell hooks, shipped shell runtime assets, generated terminal configs, and POSIX launchers resolve Yazelix scripts from the configured runtime root instead of hardcoding ~/.config/yazelix.
close_reason: Generated shell hooks, shipped shell runtime assets, generated terminal configs, and POSIX launchers now resolve internal Yazelix scripts from the runtime root, with shell/terminal regressions covering the packaging contract.

title: Packaging: adopt split config/runtime roots in command-side helpers
description: Move remaining command-side helpers onto the split path model so config reset, packs, launch re-entry, and pane-orchestrator path helpers stop bypassing get_yazelix_config_dir/get_yazelix_runtime_dir.
close_reason: Config reset, packs, launch re-entry, and pane-orchestrator path helpers now use the split config/runtime helpers, with focused regressions for split-root config reset, runtime-root .devenv lookup, and pane-orchestrator wasm path resolution.

title: Packaging: clean residual runtime-path callers and sweep helpers
description: Finish the remaining runtime-path cleanup in residual callers like yzx gen_config, sweep helpers, legacy bash launcher, and sweep layouts so they stop hardcoding ~/.config/yazelix as a runtime location.
close_reason: Updated residual runtime-path callers including yzx gen_config, sweep helpers, the legacy bash launcher, and sweep layouts to use the runtime-root model, with direct regressions for yzx gen_config and generated sweep/runtime layouts.

title: Rollback system
description: Add a real rollback story so users can recover from bad updates or broken generated state without manually reconstructing their environment.
design: The rollback model should match Yazelix's actual failure modes: bad config merges, broken generated assets, failed updates, and rebuild regressions. Define what gets snapshotted, where rollback metadata lives, how users discover the feature, and how safety is preserved when the rollback target spans config, generated state, or environment artifacts. Avoid a fake rollback that only restores one narrow file while leaving the rest of the runtime inconsistent.
acceptance_criteria: Users have a documented rollback path that can restore the supported runtime state after a bad change. Add unit tests for snapshot metadata and retention logic, plus e2e scripts that create a broken state, roll back, and verify the recovered launch path with detailed logs of the stored snapshot and restored targets.
close_reason: No longer needed; Yazelix is sturdy enough without a dedicated rollback system

title: Packaging and distribution umbrella
description: Umbrella for the remaining packaging and distribution coordination work. Keep this small and planning-focused while concrete child issues track the real implementation work such as nixpkgs packaging and any packaging-surface consolidation follow-ons.
design: Keep this as the packaging and distribution coordination layer, not as another implementation issue. The children should capture the concrete work: Home Manager alignment, flake interface, and nixpkgs packaging. Use real blockers only where one packaging path truly depends on another.
acceptance_criteria: The packaging follow-on work is represented by concrete child beads with honest dependencies and clear deliverables. This umbrella stays small and planning-focused instead of accumulating implementation detail.
close_reason: Umbrella no longer needed; remaining public packaging work continues in #402 and follow-on tasks stay separate

title: Packaging: rework Home Manager integration for the package-ready runtime model
description: Rework the Home Manager module so it matches the package-ready runtime model instead of assuming a repo checkout and repo-owned runtime assets.
design: Define exactly what the Home Manager module should own versus what Yazelix should generate at runtime. The module should set up the install path, stable config hooks, and any package/runtime references cleanly, while avoiding assumptions that only make sense for a live clone under ~/.config/yazelix. Keep option parity with the default config model where that still makes sense, but do not drag repo-first behavior into the packaged world.
acceptance_criteria: The Home Manager path remains first-class under the package-ready runtime model. Add unit tests for option translation and path derivation, evaluation tests for module options and defaults, and end-to-end Home Manager install or activation smoke tests with detailed logs showing where configs, assets, and runtime state land.
notes: The emerging testing model is: runtime behavior stays in the normal Yazelix/devenv lane, while Home Manager module correctness is exercised through Nix/Home Manager evaluation. A flake-native smoke check under home_manager/flake.nix provides a clean bridge: yzx dev test can call nix eval on a focused attr for the default regression path, while broader nix flake check usage can remain a CI or dedicated package-readiness tool. After measuring the cached smoke check, there is no current need for a separate fast-path toggle in yzx dev test.
close_reason: Completed

title: Packaging: add a first-party flake interface around Yazelix
description: Add a thin first-party flake interface around the existing Yazelix runtime so users can consume it through a standard Nix surface without creating a second environment definition.
design: Keep devenv.nix and the runtime model as the source of truth. The flake should expose useful outputs such as a dev shell, launcher or app entrypoints, and module exports where appropriate, but it should stay thin and avoid reimplementing the runtime separately. The point is discoverability and integration, not a forked architecture.
acceptance_criteria: Users can consume Yazelix through a first-party flake surface that wraps the real runtime instead of duplicating it. Add unit tests for any flake-output helper logic, flake evaluation tests, and smoke tests for the exported outputs, with logs that make it obvious which runtime assets and entrypoints are being exercised.
notes:
This bead is now the implementation track for the install decision captured in `yazelix-aa24` and `docs/specs/one_command_install_ux.md`.

Target front door:
  nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install

Keep the flake thin: it should expose installer/launcher surfaces around the existing runtime model, not create a second environment definition.
close_reason: Top-level flake interface and install front door implemented

title: Packaging: define the top-level flake output contract and installed runtime layout
description: Define the exact flake outputs Yazelix should expose and the installed runtime layout those outputs target, so the flake stays thin and the install command does not accidentally treat the flake source path as the runtime root.
design: This is the contract bead that keeps the flake honest. Specify which outputs exist first, which ones are optional later, where the persistent runtime tree lives, how yzx points at it, and how desktop integration relates to that runtime. Keep devenv.nix and the runtime-root contract as the source of truth; the flake should wrap them, not fork them.
acceptance_criteria: A concrete output contract and installed-runtime layout are documented well enough that the implementation beads can add a thin flake.nix and an install app without reopening basic path-ownership questions.
notes:
Recorded in docs/specs/flake_interface_contract.md.

Phase 1 required outputs:
- apps.<system>.install
- packages.<system>.runtime
- packages.<system>.default = runtime

Phase 1 installed-runtime model:
- stable runtime pointer at ~/.local/share/yazelix/runtime/current
- stable yzx entrypoint at ~/.local/bin/yzx
- user config remains under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/
- bootstrap flake source path is not the installed runtime
close_reason: Flake output and installed-runtime contract recorded

title: Packaging: add the top-level flake skeleton and expose the install app
description: Add a thin top-level flake.nix that exposes the initial Yazelix outputs, starting with the canonical installer app selected by the install-UX decision.
design: Keep the flake minimal. It should expose only the outputs needed for the chosen front door and future packaging discoverability, while delegating the real runtime behavior to the existing runtime assets and the devenv.nix-backed model.
acceptance_criteria: The repo has a working top-level flake.nix whose initial outputs match the documented contract, including an install app entrypoint.
close_reason: Top-level flake skeleton and install app exposed

title: Packaging: implement the install app and persistent runtime materialization
description: Implement the nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install flow so it materializes a persistent runtime, initializes user config if missing, and installs the stable yzx entrypoint without requiring a live clone.
design: Bootstrap only. The install app should not duplicate the runtime definition or point stable entrypoints at the bootstrap flake source path. It should install or materialize a persistent runtime and wire yzx and optional desktop assets to that runtime.
acceptance_criteria: Running the install app from the flake creates a persistent Yazelix runtime, leaves user config under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/, and makes yzx available as a stable command without relying on a clone path.
close_reason: Install app now materializes the runtime and stable yzx entrypoint

title: Packaging: rewrite install docs and smoke-check the flake front door
description: Update the installation story to lead with the flake install command and verify the basic install surface works end-to-end.
design: Do not over-document transitional clone flows once the flake front door exists. Make the guide short, copy-paste friendly, and honest about Nix prerequisites and runtime ownership. Keep smoke checks focused on the real front door, not broad test churn.
acceptance_criteria: The install guide leads with nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install, and the flake front door has at least one maintained smoke path that proves the installed yzx command and runtime layout are coherent.
close_reason: Install docs now lead with the flake front door and the smoke path is verified

title: Packaging: prepare and submit a nixpkgs package for Yazelix
description: Prepare Yazelix for nixpkgs submission once the runtime and packaging boundaries are clean enough that the remaining work is primarily packaging and review.
design: This is a distribution milestone, not a hidden architecture refactor. Define the package shape, wrapper behavior, runtime assets, and installation story expected by nixpkgs reviewers after the package-ready path model exists. Be explicit about Linux and macOS implications, update strategy, and what remains outside scope for a first nixpkgs submission.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix reaches a state where a nixpkgs submission is mostly packaging work. Add unit tests for package metadata or wrapper-generation helpers where applicable, packaging smoke tests that build and run the wrapped entrypoints, and logged validation of install paths, runtime asset availability, and user-facing launch behavior.
notes: Upstream nixpkgs submission is no longer a planned project goal. Keep the completed contract and local package work as historical reference, but do not treat upstream submission as a downstream milestone for the current package-runtime-first simplification lane.

title: Define the nixpkgs package contract for Yazelix
description: Define the package shape expected for a nixpkgs submission now that Yazelix has a package-ready runtime and flake install surface.
design: Treat this as a packaging contract bead, not implementation churn. Be explicit about package outputs, wrapper behavior, runtime asset layout, required dependencies, desktop integration expectations, and what stays out of scope for a first nixpkgs submission. The result should make the later implementation bead mostly translational work.
acceptance_criteria: Record the nixpkgs-facing package contract in a spec or equivalent packaging note. Be explicit about Linux and macOS scope, wrapper/runtime behavior, installed files, update story, and what a first submission will intentionally not provide.
close_reason: Defined the nixpkgs package contract in docs/specs/nixpkgs_package_contract.md and linked it from the architecture map.

title: Implement and smoke-test a local nixpkgs-ready Yazelix package expression
description: Translate the nixpkgs package contract into a local package expression and smoke-test the wrapped Yazelix entrypoints before any upstream PR is prepared.
design: Do the real packaging work locally first: package files, runtime assets, wrapper behavior, desktop integration expectations, and smoke paths that match how nixpkgs users would actually invoke Yazelix. Keep this bead repo-local; do not conflate it with opening the upstream PR.
acceptance_criteria: A local nixpkgs-style package expression builds successfully and the wrapped Yazelix entrypoints are smoke-tested. The package shape should match the contract from yazelix-2ex.1.4.3.1 closely enough that the remaining upstream step is mainly adaptation and review.
close_reason: Completed

title: Prepare the nixpkgs PR patch and submission notes for Yazelix
description: Once the local package shape is stable, prepare the actual nixpkgs-facing patch, metadata, and submission notes for the upstream PR.
design: This is the public-facing packaging bead: adapt the proven local package work into the patch shape expected by nixpkgs, capture reviewer-relevant notes, and make the upstream submission path straightforward. Keep policy and implementation disputes out of this stage as much as possible by resolving them earlier.
acceptance_criteria: The upstream-facing patch and submission notes are prepared from the proven local package work. The remaining work to open the nixpkgs PR should be mechanical or review-driven rather than unresolved product design.
close_reason: Prepared local nixpkgs draft package, validation harness, and submission notes

title: Docs experience pass
description: Improve the docs experience so users can discover the right guide quickly and the written surface matches the current product instead of feeling like repo archaeology.
design: This is broader than fixing one page. Review navigation, entrypoints, installation flow, customization docs, troubleshooting, and how examples map to the current runtime model. The result should keep docs maintainable while making the first path through Yazelix much easier to follow.
acceptance_criteria: The docs have clearer entrypoints, more coherent structure, and fewer stale or confusing paths. Add unit tests for any generated docs-index or navigation helpers, end-to-end link and command-reference validation scripts for the main docs routes, and a logged checklist covering the examples and entrypoints that were verified.
notes:
Dependency decision, 2026-04-28:
- Production Rust crates: none new. Docs route validation uses std::fs/path and the existing ValidationReport shape.
- Dev-only crates: none new. Existing tempfile dependency covers validator fixtures.
- In-house logic: small docs-front-door validator plus route/command marker table.
- Rejected alternatives: generating a full docs site, adding markdown parser dependencies, or creating broad link crawling that would be noisy for external links.
- Packaging impact: none. Maintainer validator only.
Implementation result, 2026-04-28:
- Added docs/README.md as the docs front door with current user guide, architecture, maintainer, and historical routes.
- Linked README.md to the docs front door and recorded docs/README.md in docs/documentation_architecture.md as the route map, not a behavior contract owner.
- Updated customization docs to explain the new lightweight managed user-config stubs and why Zellij/Helix behavior-owning files are not created automatically.
- Added `yzx_repo_validator validate-docs-experience`, which validates the docs front door route targets, root README route, current yzx command-reference markers, and stale current-user-doc markers such as `yzx cwd`.
- Verified checklist: docs front door points to installation, yzx CLI, keybindings, customization, troubleshooting, terminal docs, editor docs, Yazi docs, Zellij docs, layouts, architecture map, documentation architecture, and contract inventory; command reference includes launch, enter, warp, tutor begin, edit cursors, update upstream, and update home_manager; current user docs do not route users to `yzx cwd` or `yazelix_packs.toml`.
- Verification: cargo fmt --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml --all -- --check; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_maintainer repo_docs_validation::tests::; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_maintainer; yzx_repo_validator validate-docs-experience; yzx_repo_validator validate-readme-version; yzx_repo_validator validate-contracts.
close_reason: Added docs front door and docs experience validator

title: Website launch at yazelix.com
description: Create a thin but credible public website for Yazelix so the project has a clear front door beyond the repository README.
design: Keep the site focused on what Yazelix is, who it is for, how it differs from a plain editor or shell setup, and where new users should go next. This should complement the repo docs instead of competing with them. Favor maintainability and strong messaging over building a complex web app.
acceptance_criteria: yazelix.com becomes a credible first stop for new users, with clear installation and docs entrypoints and visuals that match the current product. Add unit tests for any generated route or content-selection helpers, end-to-end route and outbound-link validation, and detailed logs for the final validated site structure, screenshots, and key flows.
notes:
2026-04-26 direction update: the website should become the live documentation home, not just a marketing splash page. Keep the first launch thin, but structure it so docs routes can replace GitHub README as the detailed user guide over time.

2026-05-22 idea triage: treat the website as a docs-first front door sourced from the repo docs where practical. The first routes should make install, update, recovery, keybindings, and the workspace model easy to reach before adding broader marketing polish.

title: Site docs: first-run, update, and recovery path
description: Make the upcoming Yazelix site the primary live documentation surface for the first successful user path: install, launch, edit config, update, diagnose, and recover.
design: Design this as site-first documentation, not a README expansion. Keep it short and route-oriented: Start, Configure, Update, Recover. The site should explain the happy path and the few common recovery paths without exposing architecture up front. GitHub README should become a concise project front door that points to the live docs for details.
acceptance_criteria:
- Site docs cover first-run starter behavior, installation paths, update paths, Home Manager ownership, and recovery without relying on README sprawl.
- ~/.config/yazelix/settings.jsonc is documented as the canonical semantic config surface.
- Old yazelix.toml / cursors.toml references are limited to migration or recovery context.
- Docs include the current guidance for Cachix opt-in and what 0 B cache storage means before CI pushes build artifacts.
- Docs explain how to recover from clobbered external config files without Yazelix silently taking ownership of them.
- Manual verification confirms the rendered site keeps the first-run/update/recovery path easy to follow.
notes: Prime-beads 2026-05-05: site docs should present ~/.config/yazelix/settings.jsonc as the canonical semantic config file. Old TOML names should appear only in migration/recovery context, with the current contract framed as either automatic migration during the supported window or a clear hard error once that window is retired.

title: Workspace UX and Yazi ergonomics
description: Umbrella for the workspace-model and Yazi ergonomics work that makes Yazelix feel more coherent and less fiddly in day-to-day use.
design: This cluster is about the lived workspace experience: keybindings, Yazi-to-editor transitions, pane cwd behavior, status surfaces, and other small seams that shape whether the environment feels magical or awkward. Keep the child beads concrete and user-facing, and avoid using this umbrella as a dumping ground for unrelated UI tweaks.
acceptance_criteria: The workspace-UX child beads cover the main friction points without duplicating each other, and their dependencies reflect real interaction-model prerequisites where those exist. The umbrella itself remains a coordination bead, not an execution target.
close_reason: Obsolete grouping container; replaced by labels and direct dependencies

title: Yazi: remove extra step after zoxide jump
description: Remove the awkward extra step after a Yazi zoxide jump so users can jump to a directory and act on it immediately instead of backing out one level first.
design: Preserve the speed of the current zoxide integration but fix the UX mismatch. After a jump, the user should be able to open the selected target naturally instead of manually navigating back to a parent just to re-select the folder. The design should stay consistent with Yazi semantics and avoid surprising behavior for file versus directory targets.
acceptance_criteria: After a zoxide jump, the next action on the selected target feels direct and natural. Add unit tests for jump-resolution or target-selection helpers, integration tests for the jump-resolution path, and end-to-end Yazi flow coverage with logs showing the resolved target, resulting cursor location, and open action.
close_reason: Completed via direct-open Yazi zoxide flow on Alt+z with managed-editor reuse/new-pane fallback.

title: Zellij keybindings: replace awkward Ctrl+Alt combos with easier workspace bindings
description: Replace the ugliest Ctrl+Alt workspace bindings with easier combinations where possible without wrecking the editor-first feel of Yazelix.
design: Audit the current Zellij-facing bindings, identify the high-frequency awkward ones, and remap only the places where the ergonomic win is real. Favor easier workspace-oriented bindings such as Alt combinations or other low-conflict keys, but keep Helix, Yazi, shells, and terminal-emulator behavior as hard constraints. This should be a surgical ergonomics pass, not a full keybinding philosophy rewrite.
acceptance_criteria: The highest-friction workspace bindings become easier to use without introducing major conflicts. Add unit tests for keymap generation or binding-selection helpers, keymap validation checks where possible, and end-to-end tests in the supported terminal stack that exercise the changed bindings and log the emitted sequences, Zellij actions, and any conflict notes.
close_reason: Stale: superseded by the Yazelix-owned semantic action keymap design tracked in yazelix-hg3a. Do not continue a one-off Ctrl+Alt remapping pass separately from the new action registry/config model.

title: Define the behavior for editors opened from the shell instead of from Yazi
description: Decide how Yazelix should treat editors opened directly from the shell instead of through the managed Yazi-to-editor flow, and implement the chosen behavior coherently.
design: This is a workspace-model decision, not just a bug fix. Figure out what state Yazelix can and cannot assume when a user starts Helix or another editor manually from a shell pane: cwd ownership, pane identity, managed editor tracking, sidebar interactions, and open-file routing. The result may be explicit support, explicit non-support, or a narrower managed-handoff model, but it must be intentional.
acceptance_criteria: The behavior of shell-opened editors is explicitly defined and documented: only Yazelix-managed `editor` panes are reused by managed editor flows, while shell-opened editors remain ordinary panes. Add pane-policy unit tests for managed pane detection and focus context, routing regression checks for Yazi open behavior, and user-facing doctor/docs wording that matches the contract.
close_reason: Defined the shell-opened-editor contract, documented it, added Yazi routing regressions, and added plugin pane-policy unit tests for managed editor detection and focus context.

title: Workspace: keep a stable workspace cwd for Yazelix-managed tabs and pane creation
description: Workspace/Yazi ergonomics sub-item that is already complete.
close_reason: Already shipped

title: Make new panes open on the active Yazi project path
description: Make new Yazelix-managed panes inherit the project path implied by the current Yazi context when that is what the user is clearly working on.
design: The feature should strengthen the workspace model instead of creating hidden cwd magic. Define when a clicked or entered Yazi directory should become the effective workspace root for new panes, how that interacts with the stable tab workspace cwd, and when the user's current shell/editor state should override the file-browser context. Keep the behavior predictable and document the mental model clearly.
acceptance_criteria: New managed panes open on the expected project path when the user is working through Yazi. Add unit tests for workspace-root selection helpers, integration tests for workspace-root resolution, and end-to-end coverage that clicks into directories, opens new panes, and logs the Yazi path, tab workspace root, and resulting pane cwd.
notes: Root cause: Zellij with default_shell=nu did not reliably preserve the requested pane cwd for this path, which matches upstream bug zellij-org/zellij#3068. Final fix: resolve the live sidebar/Yazi cwd in Nushell, then ask the pane orchestrator to open a normal pane directly in that cwd. Rejected approaches: command panes caused the exit prompt UI, and injected cd commands worked but were too ugly.
close_reason: Fixed Alt+m pane opening to resolve the live Yazi/sidebar cwd in Nushell and delegate normal pane creation to the pane orchestrator, avoiding both wrong cwd fallback and command-pane exit UI

title: replace bottom bar with hideable one
description: Replace the current bottom bar with a hideable alternative that keeps the useful workspace status without permanently stealing screen space.
design: This is a layout and information-density problem, not just a cosmetics tweak. Evaluate approaches such as a second zjstatus instance, a hideable bar, or a more context-sensitive status surface, and optimize for narrow layouts, clarity, and discoverability. The result should preserve the important information users rely on while making it easier to reclaim vertical space.
acceptance_criteria: Users can hide or collapse the bottom status surface without losing the important workspace cues permanently. Add unit tests for layout-selection or visibility-state helpers, layout or rendering regression checks for narrow widths, and end-to-end validation of show and hide behavior with detailed logs of the active layout state and visible widgets.
notes:
2026-04-26 subsystem review: hideable bar work should build on yazelix_bar after the static widget registry/zjstatus boundary exists. Do not lose current workspace cues, CPU/RAM widgets, or custom text while reclaiming vertical space.
2026-04-28 follow-up from yazelix-dr06: scope this as reclaiming the resident native Zellij bottom hint row, not replacing Yazelix's top zjstatus surface. Preferred direction is bottom-bar-free generated layouts plus an on-demand first-party hints/help popup through the pane orchestrator. Keep compact-bar tooltip code as an inspiration/prototype candidate; do not default to zjstatus-hints, zellij-forgot, ghost, zellij-cb, or archived zj-status-bar.
2026-04-28 design correction: a bar-first solution is acceptable if it is genuinely toggleable and reclaims the row when hidden. Do not frame the implementation as popup-only. Preferred UX options are: default hidden bottom hint bar toggled on demand, or explicit mode-aware hint bar that appears only outside base mode. The key constraint is no permanent resident bottom row unless the user opts into always-on hints.
2026-04-28 deeper Zellij code/upstream investigation: do not rely on hide_pane_with_id/show_pane_with_id for a second tiled zjstatus or hint row unless a runtime prototype proves stable geometry. Zellij's plugin API sends SuppressPane and later UnsuppressOrExpandPane; the tab code extracts the pane into suppressed_panes and re-adds it through add_tiled_pane rather than restoring a named bottom-row slot. Upstream issue zellij-org/zellij#694 now points users at override-layout for dynamic UI chrome changes, which matches a layout-arithmetic approach. Product direction: keep this bead focused on reclaiming bottom hint/status space, and track the broader top-bar + bottom-bar + sidebar focused-work idea separately in yazelix-rknh.
Roadmap correction 2026-05-08: maintainer does not want to prioritize hideable bottom bar work in the LOC/extraction lane. Treat as low-priority UX/storage work, not a prerequisite for near-term main-repo slimming unless a later Zellij materialization pass proves the dependency is still necessary.

title: POC toggleable bottom bar with override-layout
description: Prove the smallest useful surface before committing to full Yazelix zen mode: toggle the bottom Zellij hints/status row off and back on for the active tab while preserving existing terminal panes.
design: Use Zellij override-layout or equivalent Yazelix-owned layout arithmetic. Do not use hide_pane_with_id/show_pane_with_id as the primary mechanism for tiled bottom-row geometry. Start with two explicit active-tab layouts: one with the bottom hint/status row, one without it. Preserve existing terminal panes with Zellij's retain-existing-terminal-panes option where possible. Record any limitations around plugin panes, top bar retention, swap-layout damage, and sidebar interaction.
acceptance_criteria: A maintainer can run one command or key path to hide the bottom row and reclaim its terminal height, then run the inverse to restore the bottom row. Verification captures list-panes --all or equivalent geometry before hide, after hide, and after restore. The POC documents whether Zellij replaces/reflows panes, whether plugin panes survive restore, and whether this is suitable as the foundation for a later zen mode.
notes:
2026-05-07 decision start: resolving as part of yazelix-pomi layout ownership gate. Scope is ownership decision, not live override-layout POC.
2026-05-07 decision: deferred with the override-layout branch. A bottom-row/zen POC built on override-layout is not the right next surface while override-layout resurrection is rejected. Existing Zellij fullscreen remains the supported focused-work fallback; any future barless/status toggle should start from the accepted bar/layout ownership boundary, not this POC.
close_reason: Deferred: bottom-bar override-layout POC depends on the rejected override-layout branch; revisit only through a bar/layout ownership design.

title: Fix pane drift by adding pane pinning mechanics
description: Add pane pinning mechanics so the managed editor pane keeps a stable position instead of drifting around the stack during normal Yazelix workspace activity. The main user-facing goal is to keep the editor anchored as the top pane in the relevant stack so the workspace remains predictable.
design: Treat this as workspace-structure behavior, not just a one-off layout patch. Define what it means for a pane to be pinned, which pane types deserve pinning first, when pinning should be re-applied after layout changes or pane creation, and how this interacts with user-initiated pane moves. The first target is the managed editor pane: it should remain the top pane in its stack unless the user explicitly breaks that contract. Avoid hidden fights against intentional user actions, but prevent routine Yazelix actions from causing gradual pane drift.
acceptance_criteria: The managed editor pane no longer drifts out of its intended top position during normal workspace operations. Add unit tests for the pinning decision logic and pane-order helpers, integration tests for pane-target selection, and end-to-end scripts that exercise pane creation, focus changes, workspace retargeting, and layout transitions with detailed logs of pane ids, stack order, pin state, and any re-anchoring actions.
close_reason: Not planned: local pane-pinning mechanics would compensate for an upstream stacked-pane ordering bug. Track Zellij #4084 instead and choose between waiting, contributing upstream, or temporarily carrying a thin Zellij fork with only that fix.

title: Support a separate user-managed devenv file
