Retain the existing Neovim command-mode path until a separate editor bridge contract exists. Packaging impact: child repo commit must be pushed and then consumed by the main Yazelix flake.lock before this bead is closed.
close_reason: Completed pane-orchestrator cleanup in published child commit aafd0257. Removed Helix command-mode :open/:cd construction from yazelix-zellij-pane-orchestrator, direct Helix open_file/set_managed_editor_cwd/retarget editor requests now resolve as unsupported_editor instead of injecting terminal text, and Neovim remains the command-mode editor path. Main flake.lock now consumes the published child revision. Validation: child cargo fmt --all --check; child cargo test --lib; nix build ../yazelix-zellij-pane-orchestrator#yazelix_zellij_pane_orchestrator --no-link; nix build .#runtime --override-input yazelixZellijPaneOrchestrator ../yazelix-zellij-pane-orchestrator --no-link --no-write-lock-file; nix build .#runtime --no-link --no-write-lock-file; yzx_repo_validator validate-workspace-session-contract; yzx_repo_validator validate-child-release-transaction.

title: Make Helix action bridge transport native and cross-platform
description: Replace the Unix-only Helix action bridge transport contract with a native local IPC abstraction: Unix domain sockets on Unix/macOS and best-effort Windows named pipes on native Windows. Keep the JSON action protocol and no-keystroke-fallback policy unchanged. Acceptance: registry records transport object instead of Unix-only socket_path; Unix/macOS behavior still uses Unix sockets and Linux tests/smoke pass; Windows has cfg(windows) named-pipe server/client code; unsupported transports fail clearly; docs reject loopback TCP as the default; child repo is pushed and main flake.lock consumes the published child revision.
notes: Crate-vs-in-house decision before implementation: use in-house/std for the transport abstraction and Unix backend. Add windows-sys as a Windows-only direct dependency only where Windows named pipe APIs are needed, reusing the version family already present transitively in the Helix lock when possible. Rejected alternatives: loopback TCP default, broad interprocess-style IPC abstraction crates, terminal text fallback, and Unix-only releasable bridge support. Packaging impact: yazelix-helix child package changes first, then main flake.lock must consume the published child revision. Windows runtime behavior is best-effort from Linux; do not claim native Windows validation until Windows CI or manual testing exists.
close_reason: Implemented native Helix action bridge transport schema v2. Child yazelix-helix now advertises typed transports and uses Unix sockets on Unix plus best-effort Windows named-pipe server code behind cfg(windows). Main Yazelix client consumes transport objects, keeps Unix socket behavior on Linux, includes best-effort Windows named-pipe client code behind cfg(windows), rejects v1 socket_path registries as schema mismatches, and documents loopback TCP rejection. Linux validation: cargo test -p helix-term yazelix_bridge --features steel; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core helix_bridge; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_control_helix_bridge; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_control_workspace_surface yzx_control_zellij_open_editor; yzx_repo_validator validate-contracts via cargo run; nix build .#runtime --no-link --no-write-lock-file. Windows named pipes remain best-effort and unvalidated on native Windows.

title: Track managed Helix wishlist: folding sticky headers Copilot inline blame
description:
Track the next managed-Helix product wishlist beyond the initial Steel/runtime work. This is a roadmap bead, not an implementation bead.

Wishlist:
- Code folding: real fold/unfold UI for syntax and LSP folding ranges, with clear indicators and Helix-native keybindings.
- Sticky headers: pinned context lines for the current function, type, module, or section while scrolling.
- Copilot: first-class AI completion inside managed Helix without requiring users to assemble their own plugin/runtime setup.
- Inline git blame: commit/author/context annotations shown inline in the editor, not only a one-shot shell command.

Current state notes:
- The Helix runtime includes many tree-sitter fold query files and LSP folding range types, but Yazelix does not yet expose a polished managed folding UI.
- Yazelix managed Helix now ships A-g.b as a lightweight git blame shell helper; the wishlist item is inline blame rendered in the editor buffer or editor UI.
- These features probably belong in yazelix-helix or upstream Helix/Steel work, with the main repo owning config/docs/integration once the editor capability exists.

Acceptance Criteria:
1. Each wishlist item has a feasibility note naming whether it belongs upstream, in yazelix-helix, in a Steel plugin, or in main-repo integration.
2. The first implementation slice is chosen and split into child beads with concrete verification.
3. Any default keybindings/config surfaces are documented before landing.
4. Inline git blame distinguishes the existing A-g.b shell helper from the desired editor-rendered blame annotations.
5. Folding and sticky-header work define behavior for unsupported languages or missing parser/LSP support.

title: Add eye-watering welcome screen
description: Redesign the default Yazelix welcome screen so it has a visually striking hero treatment without making startup feel heavy or slow. The default experience should feel beautiful, surprising, and recognizably Yazelix instead of just showing plain welcome text.
design: Preferred direction: use an abstract animated visual, gif, or lightweight animation that frames the welcome content rather than obscuring it. The motion should either stay out of the text area or gracefully darken or fade once the reveal is done so the welcome information remains readable. The Yazelix logo should play a central role if possible, ideally through an animation that resolves into the final Yazelix mark. A strong direction would be a logo-based animation that nods to the Yazi, Helix, and Zellij influences already present in the current logo while landing on the final Yazelix icon form.
acceptance_criteria: The default welcome screen has a visually distinctive animation or image path that feels fast, branded, and readable, with a static fallback users can choose explicitly. The final design should work in narrow and split layouts, preserve welcome-text readability, and feel like Yazelix rather than a random screensaver. Add unit tests for animation-selection or layout-decision helpers, end-to-end startup/render validation for normal and narrow layouts, performance measurements for startup impact, and detailed capture logs for whichever animation or asset pipeline is chosen.
notes:
Candidate visual directions: a magician casting colorful ASCII magic; a colored liquid animation, ASCII or non-ASCII depending quality/perf tradeoffs; a Game of Life style simulation; particle-life style motion similar to OfficialCodeNoodles/Particle-Life-Simulation; and continuous-life variants like Lenia or SmoothLife for a more fluid organic feel. Longer-term direction: ship a small curated set of beautiful default welcome animations and choose one at random on each start. Existing context: Yazelix already has a star animation that looks nice, but it degrades badly in vertical splits or lower-width layouts, so any new direction should adapt better to constrained dimensions. Imported overlap from GitHub issue #263: replacing the current welcome art with the Yazelix logo and making the result work well on half screens and narrow layouts is part of this same feature, not a separate track.
2026-04-20 tracker hygiene: moved out of in_progress. This broader welcome-screen feature is still dependency-driven and there is no active implementation in the current tree, so it should remain unclaimed until one of its concrete visual children is actually being worked.

title: Normalize Escape behavior for popup and menu panes
description:
Popup pane and popup menu now both use the shared toggleable transient-pane model. Escape behavior should be normalized across both surfaces instead of letting one surface close/hide while the other behaves differently.

Current context: `nushell/scripts/yzx/menu.nu` still advertises `Enter/Esc: close` and handles `esc` in the menu popup loop. Audit the popup pane path as well, including Zellij keybindings and pane-orchestrator transient-pane behavior, before changing it.

Preferred direction from product discussion: because popup and menu are now toggles, remove popup/menu-specific Escape closing if it is not needed. If Escape remains, it must have exactly the same user-visible behavior on both the popup pane and popup menu.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The popup pane and popup menu have one documented Escape-key contract.
2. Either Escape does nothing on both surfaces, or Escape hides/closes both surfaces through the same transient-pane path.
3. If the toggle path is the canonical close path, remove popup/menu-specific Escape close handling and stale prompt text such as `Enter/Esc: close` or `Esc to cancel`.
4. Update focused tests for the surviving behavior, covering both `yzx popup` and `yzx menu --popup` where practical.
5. Do not reintroduce helper panes or separate popup/menu control paths.
close_reason: Completed: yzx menu no longer advertises or handles Escape as its own close path; popup/menu close semantics stay toggle-owned, with regression coverage in the core yzx suite.

title: Harden Yazelix-managed file writes with shared atomic replace helpers
description:
Imported GitHub issue #515.

## Problem

Yazelix writes several managed artifacts that users depend on immediately:

- generated configs under `~/.local/share/yazelix/configs/`
- managed files under `~/.config/yazelix/`
- copied plugin artifacts such as Zellij wasm files
- small state/cache files that are part of the managed Yazelix contract

Some of these writes are already atomic, but the repo does not use one consistent pattern everywhere. That means a runtime refresh, install, or config regeneration can still leave some managed files briefly torn, partially written, or replaced through ad-hoc logic.

This is not a request for a full transaction system. The likely problem is narrower: Yazelix needs one shared atomic replace helper for Yazelix-owned file writes, and then it should apply that helper consistently where torn writes would create real user-visible breakage.

## Proposed direction

Treat this as a hardening pass on **managed Yazelix-owned writes only**.

Goals:

- inventory the important managed write sites
- define one shared helper for atomic file replacement
- use that helper for generated configs, runtime-managed plugin copies, and other Yazelix-owned state where partial writes are unacceptable
- avoid widening this into multi-file transactions or generic filesystem abstraction

A likely helper contract is:

1. create parent directories if needed
2. write to a temporary file in the same directory
3. atomically rename into place
4. fail clearly on error

## Important boundary

This bead is **not** about automatically modifying user-owned external config outside Yazelix-owned paths. It is only about making Yazelix's own managed writes safer and more consistent.

## Acceptance criteria

- There is one shared atomic write helper for Yazelix-owned managed files instead of repeated ad-hoc temp-file logic.
- The important user-visible managed write sites use that helper consistently.
- The implementation stays narrow: atomic replace for managed artifacts, not a fake transaction layer.
- Verification shows that installs / refreshes / managed config generation still work and no managed write sites are left using weaker ad-hoc patterns without justification.
notes: 2026-04-09 implementation: added shared atomic replace helpers for Yazelix-managed text and file writes; migrated generated Zellij, Yazi, Helix, terminal, desktop-entry, shell bridge, initializer, launch-state, materialized-state, extern-bridge, upgrade-summary, restart bootstrap, config-surface copy, migration staging, and runtime plugin-copy paths to the shared helper; intentionally left maintainer-only write sites, append-only logs, and host-shell append flows outside this bead.
close_reason: Completed: Yazelix-managed file writes now use shared atomic replace helpers across the main runtime/config/state artifact paths.

title: Windows support: verify native and WSL paths and document caveats
description: Re-evaluate what level of Windows support Yazelix can credibly claim now that Zellij supports Windows, and document the caveats honestly instead of leaving the answer implicit.
design: Treat this as a support-boundary verification task, not a promise that native Windows is automatically first-class. Validate both realistic paths: native Windows, now that Zellij supports it, and WSL as an alternative/fallback path. Check the core flows that matter: install, launch, terminal integration, shell/path handling, clipboard and browser/file openers, fonts, file-manager interop, plugin/runtime caveats, and any assumptions in Yazelix that still depend on Unix-only behavior. The output should separate supported, works-with-caveats, and unsupported cases for each path.
acceptance_criteria: Users can tell whether Yazelix is a credible native-Windows option, a WSL-only option, or a mixed-support story with specific caveats. The docs include concrete caveats and recovery tips for both native Windows and WSL where relevant, and the validation process leaves behind a logged checklist or script so support can be re-verified later as Zellij and Yazelix evolve.

title: Support Emacs as a low-priority compatibility target
description: Keep Emacs support on the backlog mainly so Yazelix continues to cover the three big model editors, not because this is a current priority area.
design: This should stay aspirational for now. When revisited, verify the modes people actually use, especially Doom Emacs and similar real-world setups, and evaluate whether tools like chemacs2 help keep integration manageable. Do not let this expand into a broad editor-support project before higher-value workspace and packaging work is done.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a documented path or verified compatibility story for mainstream Emacs setups when this eventually gets picked up. Until then, this should remain clearly marked as low-priority backlog work rather than active roadmap pressure.

title: Prepare the public yzx command surface so a future Clap rewrite could be honest
description: Successor planning lane after yazelix-rxym closed as a no-go. Before Clap is reconsidered, Yazelix needs one shared public command-surface schema, stronger root parity coverage, and either one more real public-family deletion beyond yzx_control or an explicit proof that no such family exists.
design: Do not treat this as pre-approval for Clap. Success means the public yzx surface becomes cleaner and more single-source first. Preserve all current command behavior, aliases, grouped subcommands, direct internal Nu module routing, error policy, and argv passthrough. If these prerequisites still do not make Clap deletion-positive, keep the no-go.
acceptance_criteria: 1. A shared-schema lane exists for public command metadata and parser shape. 2. A parity-hardening lane exists before schema/router rewrites. 3. A next-family deletion lane exists beyond yzx_control. 4. A future Clap re-evaluation bead is blocked on those prerequisites instead of being assumed ready.
notes:
2026-04-21 progress

Completed the shared-schema and parity-hardening sub-lanes. Remaining work in this epic is the next public-family deletion decision (2p32.3) and the downstream Clap re-evaluation gate (2p32.4).
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Unify the public yzx command-surface schema
description: Create one shared source of truth for the public yzx command surface instead of keeping command_metadata.rs and yzx.rs route definitions as separate surfaces.
design: The shared surface must represent top-level commands, grouped subcommands, aliases, parameter/help shape, route ownership, internal Nu module path and command name where relevant, and error/help policy. Do not lose features or silently widen Rust ownership.
acceptance_criteria: 1. One shared schema owns public command metadata and parser-shape information. 2. yzx.rs route planning can consume that schema without behavior drift. 3. Root help, externs, and menu/palette metadata stay single-source. 4. Hand-written duplicated route tables become deletable.
close_reason: Completed

title: Decide the shared public yzx schema owner and dependency gate
description: Decide whether command_metadata.rs should grow into the shared command-surface owner or whether a new shared schema module should exist.
design: This bead must satisfy the Rust dependency gate for later Rust implementation work: list production crates, dev-only crates, in-house logic, rejected alternatives, and packaging impact. Compare at least: extending command_metadata.rs in place; introducing a new shared schema module that feeds both metadata and routing; and generating one surface from the other. The chosen shape must preserve a single source for help, externs, and menu metadata.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Records a crate-vs-in-house decision for the shared-schema lane. 2. Chooses the schema owner shape explicitly. 3. Names the fields the shared schema must represent. 4. Names the current code that should disappear once the schema lands. 5. Rejects any shape that creates a second public command registry.
notes:
2026-04-21 dependency gate decision

Chosen owner shape
- Add a new shared Rust module for the public yzx command surface instead of growing command_metadata.rs in place
- command_metadata.rs should shrink to rendering/export helpers plus extern-bridge sync that consume the shared surface
- yzx.rs route planning should consume the same shared surface instead of keeping a second handwritten registry

Why this shape
- command_metadata.rs already mixes flat public metadata with extern rendering and bridge sync; adding routing policy, owner state, module targets, and subcommand error policy there would make it a grab-bag owner
- A neutral shared surface can feed both metadata renderers and route planning without creating a second registry
- Generating one surface ad hoc from the other still leaves one side authoritative and the other derived by convention instead of by explicit shared structure
- Clap must not become the schema owner; that would pre-approve the parser choice before the delete-first prerequisites land

Crate gate
- Production crates: no new crates; reuse current std, serde, serde_json, sha2, and existing bridge/control-plane crates already in yazelix_core
- Dev-only crates: no new crates required for this lane; reuse the current Rust/Nu test harnesses already in tree
- In-house logic: command-family schema, alias/subcommand matching, internal Nu route planning, help/extern/menu projections
- Rejected alternatives: extend command_metadata.rs directly; make yzx.rs remain authoritative and regenerate metadata from it; make Clap the schema owner; keep separate flat metadata and manual route tables
- Packaging impact: none expected because this is an internal Rust ownership refactor inside the existing yzx/yzx_core helpers

Shared surface fields that must exist
- public command id/full name
- category
- description and optional menu-facing override text
- parameter list
- route owner kind: root-help, root-version, rust-control, or internal-nu
- family root token
- alias/subcommand tokens after the family root
- internal Nu module path when route owner is internal-nu
- concrete exported Nu command name when route owner is internal-nu
- family fallback/error policy for missing or unknown subcommands
- menu visibility/category policy so the palette stops reverse-engineering categories from command names

Current code that should disappear once the shared surface lands
- the hard-coded top-level command allowlist inside classify_root_route in rust_core/yazelix_core/src/bin/yzx.rs
- the manual per-family match tree in plan_internal_nu_route in rust_core/yazelix_core/src/bin/yzx.rs
- the manually curated command vector in yzx_command_metadata() in rust_core/yazelix_core/src/command_metadata.rs
- the menu-side category guessing and command-shape reconstruction in nushell/scripts/yzx/menu.nu where the Rust metadata already knows the command surface

Guardrail
- Do not land any shape that creates a second public command registry or a schema that only covers help/externs while yzx.rs keeps its own route table.
close_reason: Completed

title: Model public yzx families, aliases, subcommands, and route ownership in the shared schema
description: Represent the current public yzx command surface in one shared schema without changing behavior.
design: Model top-level commands, grouped subcommands, aliases, help/version flags, route ownership, internal Nu module path, internal Nu command name, and any required error-policy distinctions. Preserve every current command and alias; this is a modeling cut, not a simplification cut.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The shared schema covers the current public root surface completely. 2. Grouped subcommands and aliases are represented explicitly. 3. Route ownership is explicit for Rust-owned versus internal Nu-owned families. 4. No current user-facing command or alias disappears.
notes:
2026-04-21 implementation

Added rust_core/yazelix_core/src/public_command_surface.rs as the single Rust-owned public yzx schema. It now models command metadata, menu visibility/category, family root tokens, alias/subcommand tokens, route ownership, internal Nu module targets, and family fallback/error policy. No new crates were added.
close_reason: Completed

title: Move yzx.rs route planning onto the shared schema without behavior drift
description: Move yzx.rs route planning onto the shared schema once the schema and parity surface are ready.
design: Replace hand-written top-level matching and direct route planning with schema-driven planning, while preserving exact behavior: help/version handling, unknown-command policy, grouped subcommands, aliases, direct internal Nu module routing, and argv passthrough boundaries.
acceptance_criteria: 1. yzx.rs route planning consumes the shared schema. 2. Behavior does not change. 3. Explicit internal Nu routes remain explicit and honest. 4. Hand-written route-selection logic shrinks materially.
notes:
2026-04-21 implementation

yzx.rs now delegates public root classification and internal Nu route planning to classify_yzx_root_route from the shared surface. The old top-level allowlist and per-family plan_internal_nu_route match tree were removed.
close_reason: Completed

title: Move root help, externs, and menu metadata onto the shared schema
description: Move root help, externs, and menu/palette metadata onto the shared schema without creating duplication.
design: Keep one source for render_yzx_help, yzx-command-metadata.externs, and the command-palette/menu inventory. The shared schema may extend command_metadata.rs or feed it, but must not create a separate Clap-only or router-only metadata surface.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Root help, externs, and menu/palette metadata derive from the shared schema. 2. There is still one public metadata source. 3. Existing metadata behavior remains intact. 4. Duplicated metadata definitions become deletable.
notes:
2026-04-21 implementation

Root help and extern rendering now consume the shared surface through command_metadata.rs, and nushell/scripts/yzx/menu.nu now reads menu visibility/category/description from Rust-owned command metadata instead of reconstructing them in Nushell.
close_reason: Completed

title: Delete duplicated hand-written route tables after shared-schema ownership lands
description: Delete the duplicated hand-written route-definition surfaces once the shared schema owns both metadata and routing.
design: This is the first honest deletion step in the command-surface lane. Remove only the hand-written parser/help/router tables that the shared schema genuinely replaces. Do not delete helper resolution, Nu quoting, child-process execution, or explicit internal Nu routing unless another owner cut removes them too.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The duplicated hand-written route-definition surfaces are removed or materially reduced. 2. The remaining yzx.rs code is smaller and still feature-complete. 3. No helper-resolution or quoting behavior is lost by accident.
notes:
2026-04-21 implementation

Deleted the duplicated handwritten metadata vector from command_metadata.rs, the manual route planner from yzx.rs, and the menu-side exclusion/category override tables. The remaining public command registry now lives in public_command_surface.rs.
close_reason: Completed

title: Lock down yzx root parity before schema and parser rewrites
description: Lock down the current Rust yzx root behavior before schema or parser rewrites so later deletion work cannot silently drop aliases, subcommand handling, direct module routing, or argv behavior.
design: Prefer focused Rust unit tests for pure parser/planner behavior and existing Nushell CLI probe tests for launcher and direct-module behavior. Defend real behavior rather than command-list snapshots.
acceptance_criteria: 1. High-signal parity coverage exists for root behavior before schema/router rewrites. 2. Aliases, grouped subcommands, errors, direct module routing, and argv passthrough are defended. 3. The parity surface is good enough to support future schema and router deletions safely.
close_reason: Completed

title: Defend root help, version, and unknown-command behavior
description: Defend root help, version, and unknown-command behavior for the public Rust yzx root.
design: Cover no-arg help, help/-h/--help, version flags -V/--version/-v/--version-short, and unknown top-level command error class/code. Use focused assertions that protect user-visible behavior.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Root help and version behavior are defended. 2. Unknown top-level command behavior is defended. 3. The tests are high-signal rather than command-inventory snapshots.
notes:
2026-04-21 verification

Added shared-surface Rust tests for no-arg help, help aliases, all supported version flags, and unknown top-level command behavior.
close_reason: Completed

title: Defend grouped subcommand and alias behavior
description: Defend grouped subcommands and aliases that the current yzx root planner must preserve.
design: Cover required and unknown subcommand behavior for grouped families like desktop, dev, and import, plus current alias behavior such as config reset, edit config, home_manager prepare, keys aliases, and tutor aliases. Preserve all features; do not simplify the surface.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Grouped subcommand behavior is defended. 2. Alias behavior is defended. 3. Error-policy differences between missing and unknown subcommands are defended.
notes:
2026-04-21 verification

Added shared-surface Rust tests covering config reset, edit config, home_manager prepare, keys helix, tutor nushell, desktop missing-subcommand behavior, and dev unknown-subcommand behavior.
close_reason: Completed

title: Defend direct internal Nu route planning and argv passthrough
description: Defend direct internal Nu route planning and argv passthrough behavior for the current Rust yzx root.
design: Cover direct internal Nu module path selection, internal Nu command-name selection, tail argv planning, and rendered Nu invocation quoting for spaces, quotes, and --. Keep existing CLI probe tests aligned with the route plan instead of replacing them with weaker Rust-only checks.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Direct internal Nu route planning is defended. 2. argv passthrough and quoting are defended. 3. Existing launcher and direct-module CLI probe tests remain aligned with the route plan.
notes:
2026-04-21 verification

Kept the focused CLI probe tests green for reveal/menu/popup/enter direct-module routing and strengthened the Rust invocation-script test to cover -- passthrough, spaces, and embedded quotes.
close_reason: Completed

title: Define the next honest public-family deletion beyond yzx_control
description: To make a future Clap rewrite deletion-positive, Yazelix likely needs one more real public-family owner cut beyond yzx_control or an explicit proof that no such family currently qualifies.
design: Keep this delete-first. Do not choose the next family by convenience. Choose it only if Rust can become the public owner while deleting a real public Nushell registry or route role and without dragging shell-heavy or renderer-heavy behavior into Rust by habit.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The next candidate public family is evaluated with an explicit deletion budget. 2. The repo records either a real next family-level cut or an explicit no-go. 3. If a family is chosen, there is a bounded implementation bead for deleting that public owner.
close_reason: Completed. The next public-family deletion lane selected `yzx home_manager`, delivered the Rust owner cut, and removed the surviving public Nushell wrapper.

title: Define the deletion budget for the next public yzx family
description: Define the deletion budget for the next public yzx command family beyond env/run/update/status.
design: Compare candidate families by real owner deletion, not by which one is easiest to parse in Rust. The decision should name surviving Nu owners, shell heaviness, renderer heaviness, direct-route table shrink value, and whether the cut would make a future Clap rewrite materially more honest.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The candidate families are compared explicitly. 2. The deletion budget is named in terms of real owner removal. 3. The decision rejects families that are mostly shell-heavy or renderer-heavy without a real deletion story.
notes:
Deletion budget decision:
- Candidate families reviewed: home_manager, doctor, config, plus heavier shell/UI families such as launch, desktop, edit, and import
- Chosen target: `yzx home_manager`
- Why this is the next honest public-family deletion: the install-ownership evaluation and takeover-artifact classification are already Rust-owned in `install_ownership_report`, while the remaining public Nushell owner is a small wrapper plus archive/apply behavior
- Rejected for this lane: `yzx doctor` still fronts a materially larger interactive repair surface; `yzx config` still owns more of its behavior directly and would delete less of an already-Rust seam; launch/desktop/edit/import are too shell-heavy for an honest scoped public-owner cut
- Exact deletion budget: move `yzx home_manager` and `yzx home_manager prepare` into Rust control-plane ownership, preserve preview/apply/archive/confirmation semantics, switch the shared public command surface to Rust control routing for this family, then delete `nushell/scripts/yzx/home_manager.nu` as the public owner
- Rust dependency gate: no new production crates; use std plus existing `yazelix_core` modules because this work is filesystem moves, timestamp formatting, env/config path resolution, and existing install-ownership report consumption rather than domain logic that justifies another crate
close_reason: Completed. The deletion-budget review chose `yzx home_manager` as the next honest public-family Rust owner cut because install-ownership evaluation was already Rust-owned and the remaining Nushell surface was only a thin public wrapper plus archive/apply behavior.

title: Choose the next public family or record a no-go
description: Choose the next public yzx family to migrate, or record an explicit no-go if no family currently makes sense.
design: Use the deletion budget from the previous bead. The answer may be a concrete family or a no-go, but it must be honest. Do not force a family just to keep a Clap story alive.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Records one concrete next family or an explicit no-go. 2. Names the exact public-owner seam to delete if a family is chosen. 3. Keeps no-go as an acceptable outcome.
notes:
Decision: go with `yzx home_manager` as the next honest public-family Rust owner cut.

Selection criteria applied:
- real end-to-end deletion of a surviving public Nushell owner
- minimal shell-only UI logic that would just migrate wrappers instead of ownership
- meaningful shrink of the remaining direct internal-Nu family surface in the shared public command schema
- no duplication with existing Rust metadata/help/schema ownership

Stop condition for this bead: either `yzx home_manager` becomes Rust-owned and the Nu family owner is deleted, or the implementation reveals a hidden shell-bound blocker serious enough to record a no-go. Current expectation is go.
close_reason: Completed. The go/no-go review stayed go: `yzx home_manager` delivered real public-owner deletion value without creating a second metadata or route registry.

title: Delete or explicitly reject the chosen next public-family owner cut
description: Delete the chosen next public-family owner once the family decision is explicit, or close with the no-go stop condition.
design: If the previous decision chooses a family, this bead owns the actual public-owner deletion lane for that family. If the previous decision is no-go, close this bead by recording the stop condition instead of doing speculative Rust work.
acceptance_criteria: 1. If a family is chosen, the public-owner deletion lane is implemented or decomposed honestly. 2. If no family is chosen, the bead closes with the explicit stop condition. 3. The result either shrinks the direct yzx.rs route table materially or proves why that cannot happen yet.
notes:
Implementation lane opened with `yzx home_manager` as the chosen public-family cut. Scope for completion:
- add Rust control-plane ownership for `yzx home_manager` and `yzx home_manager prepare`
- preserve preview output, empty-state output, `--apply`, `--yes`, timestamped archive suffixes, and next-step guidance
- route the home_manager family through Rust control in the shared command surface
- delete `nushell/scripts/yzx/home_manager.nu` after parity is defended
Implemented the chosen public-family cut:
- added Rust control-plane ownership for `yzx home_manager` and `yzx home_manager prepare`
- extracted a shared `install_ownership_request_from_env` helper so `yzx update` and `yzx home_manager` consume the same install-ownership report request path
- moved the shared public command surface to route `home_manager` through Rust control instead of direct Nu modules
- deleted `nushell/scripts/yzx/home_manager.nu` and removed its runtime-contract expectation
- updated focused CLI tests so the defended contract now runs through the public `yzx` route, not a direct Nushell import
- updated migration docs/spec notes so `yzx home_manager` is no longer described as a surviving Nu owner
close_reason: Completed. `yzx home_manager` is now Rust-owned end to end at the public owner layer, and the surviving Nushell family owner was deleted.

title: Re-evaluate Clap after shared-schema and public-owner prerequisites land
description: Re-evaluate Clap only after the shared schema, parity surface, and next-family deletion work land. This is the first future point where a Clap rewrite might become deletion-positive.
design: Use the closed yazelix-rxym no-go as the baseline. Reopen Clap only if the shared schema now owns public metadata and parser shape, parity coverage is strong enough to protect behavior, and one more public-owner cut has materially reduced the surviving direct internal route table. If those gates still are not met, keep the no-go.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Re-evaluates Clap against the post-prerequisite command surface, not the old router shape. 2. Either creates a new honest Clap implementation lane with an exact deletion budget or records that Clap still does not make sense. 3. Does not reopen Clap by default just because the prerequisites exist.
notes:
Re-evaluation after shared-schema ownership and one more public-family cut:
- satisfied prerequisite: the shared public command surface is now the single Rust-owned source for public metadata, menu visibility, aliases, subcommand shape, and root route ownership
- satisfied prerequisite: one more public family (`yzx home_manager`) moved from a surviving public Nushell owner to Rust control
- conclusion: Clap is still a no-go for now
- why: the remaining complexity is still the direct internal-Nu family planner and owner split, not ad-hoc top-level parsing. The current Rust root/parser path is already small and schema-driven, so adding Clap now would mostly wrap or duplicate a working route planner instead of deleting enough code
- honest future gate: revisit only after another substantial direct internal-Nu family collapse, or if a future command-surface plan can let Clap replace the current family-routing rule engine rather than sit beside it
close_reason: Completed with a no-go. The shared-schema and public-owner prerequisites are now better, but Clap still would not delete enough of the surviving family-routing rule engine to justify itself yet.

title: Add Claude Code statusline quota bridge
description: Implement Claude quota ingestion without periodic OAuth polling. Prefer an opt-in Claude Code `statusLine` command that receives Claude's official statusline JSON, extracts `rate_limits.five_hour` and `rate_limits.seven_day`, and writes normalized facts into the shared Yazelix quota cache for `claude_quota` widgets.
acceptance_criteria:
- `claude_quota` widget has a documented source that does not require Yazelix to drive `claude /usage` repeatedly
- A small `yzx`/`yzx_control` ingestion command accepts Claude statusline stdin JSON and writes shared quota-cache facts
- User-facing setup is explicit and reversible, likely through a managed config surface or documented Claude statusLine snippet
- Widget reads cache only and renders compact remaining-oriented text such as `[claude q 5h85 wk62]`
- Missing, stale, or unsupported Claude statusline data does not produce bar errors
- Tests cover Claude statusline JSON parsing and stale-cache rendering
close_reason: Not planned: Claude quota is out of scope for now; quota work should focus on Codex only.

title: Figure out how to install Yazelix in an existing app on Android
description: Figure out whether Yazelix can be installed inside an existing Android app environment and what the credible support story would look like if that path is worth pursuing.
design: This is an exploration bead, not a promise of a mobile app. Focus on realistic hosts such as Termux or other existing app shells that can run the needed Nix or terminal stack, and identify what would break first: rendering, keyboard input, terminal multiplexing, install size, or package assumptions. The result should distinguish technically possible from supportable.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation about whether Android-in-an-existing-app is viable, what the supported host would be, and what major caveats remain. Leave behind a documented validation path and detailed logs of the tested environment so the conclusion is reproducible.

title: add to yzx keys the alt <number> motions yazelix has
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Fix collection docs pane orchestrator link for CI checkout
description: CI docs validation fails because docs/yazelix_collection.md links to sibling checkout ../../yazelix-zellij-pane-orchestrator, which does not exist in a clean GitHub Actions checkout. Use a repository-stable link for the child project.
acceptance_criteria: docs/yazelix_collection.md no longer links to a missing sibling checkout. validate-docs-experience passes locally. CI is re-run after push.
close_reason: Fixed. Replaced the clean-checkout-broken sibling link in docs/yazelix_collection.md with the GitHub child-repo URL. Verified with validate-docs-experience and git diff --check.

title: UX: floating pane option for lazygit/AI TUI
description: Offer an optional floating-pane workflow for transient TUIs such as lazygit or AI terminals when that better fits the workspace than dedicating a persistent split.
design: Keep this focused on the tools that benefit from temporary, attention-stealing use rather than making every pane type floatable by default. Evaluate how floating panes should interact with focus, sizing, closing, cwd inheritance, and the existing managed-pane model so the feature feels intentional instead of bolted on.
acceptance_criteria: Users can launch the supported transient TUIs in a predictable floating-pane flow without breaking normal pane management. Add unit tests for the command and config routing and end-to-end scripts that open, focus, and close the floating TUI path while logging pane ids, cwd, and focus transitions.
close_reason: Shipped configurable managed floating popup workflow for transient TUIs

title: detect first time running yazelix and give a helpful message explaining the zjstatus permission thingy
description: Imported GitHub issue #327.
close_reason: First-run permission guidance is now covered in the welcome flow and docs

title: Stop failing when native and managed Zellij configs both exist
description: Imported GitHub issue #514. After Zellij 0.44 migration, ~/.config/zellij/config.kdl can reappear even when Yazelix already has ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/zellij/config.kdl. Yazelix should not hard fail on every launch in that state. The managed user_configs file must stay canonical; only auto-relocate the native config when the managed file is missing. If both exist, prefer the managed file and make import/sync an explicit user action rather than a launch blocker.
close_reason: Completed

title: Evaluate whether Television belongs in Yazelix
description: Evaluate whether the Television fuzzy finder (tv) adds enough value to Yazelix to justify packaging, integration, or documentation. The question is not whether Television is interesting in general, but whether it improves Yazelix workflows enough to deserve a maintained place in the product.
design: Television is a fast terminal fuzzy finder with channels, shell integration, and editor integrations. Judge it against Yazelix's actual workflow surfaces: Yazi, Helix, popup flows, shell search, and any future picker surfaces. Determine whether Television would be redundant with existing tools, complementary as a generic fuzzy-search surface, or valuable only as an optional pack tool. Prefer a concrete go, no-go, or optional-integration decision over vague enthusiasm for another terminal tool.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit decision on whether Television should be ignored, documented, added to a pack, or integrated more deeply into Yazelix. The bead records which user workflows it would improve, what it duplicates, and what the lightest worthwhile adoption shape would be if it is kept.
notes:
Investigation decision, 2026-04-28:
- Reviewed upstream alexpasmantier/television as of 2026-04-28: Television (`tv`) is active, fast, and channel-oriented, with current visible release 0.15.6 from 2026-04-15, shell integration, editor integrations, and Nix availability via `nix run nixpkgs#television`. Source: https://github.com/alexpasmantier/television
- Yazelix already has focused picker surfaces: Yazi for file-tree workflows, Helix/Neovim for editor pickers, zoxide/fzf for workspace jumps, `yzx menu` for commands, and `yzx warp` for project switching into a new tab. Adding Television as default would duplicate fzf/zoxide/Yazi before it owns a clear Yazelix workflow.
- Product fit: Television is interesting as an optional power-user picker or future backend for a generalized picker abstraction, especially channels like files, text, and git-repos. It does not currently justify default packaging, deeper integration, or docs emphasis.
- Decision: no default dependency and no first-party integration now. Revisit only when Yazelix defines a picker abstraction or a package surface where `tv` replaces a concrete existing path better than fzf/zoxide/Yazi. Lightest worthwhile future shape would be opt-in package/docs, not runtime coupling.
- No code change needed for this bead.
close_reason: Recorded no-adopt decision for Television

title: Regenerate desktop fast-path terminal configs instead of trusting existing files
description: Desktop launch can reuse generated terminal configs purely because the files exist. If a generated Ghostty or WezTerm config is stale, corrupt, or from the wrong runtime, the desktop fast path can hand it to the terminal before the in-terminal runtime materializer gets a chance to repair anything.
design: Prefer the delete-first/simple shape: generated terminal configs are cheap Yazelix-owned text artifacts, so desktop launch should regenerate the selected Yazelix terminal configs before terminal handoff instead of validating a cache. Make terminal config writes atomic as part of the same work so interruption cannot leave partial configs.
acceptance_criteria: Desktop fast-path launch regenerates selected Yazelix-owned terminal configs even when files already exist; terminal config writes are atomic for Ghostty and WezTerm at minimum; focused tests cover stale existing generated terminal config on desktop fast path; no new compatibility fallback or duplicate terminal path logic is introduced.
close_reason: Completed: desktop fast-path launch now regenerates selected Yazelix-owned terminal configs instead of trusting existing generated files. Terminal config writes now go through a shared same-directory atomic writer, covering Ghostty and WezTerm plus the other generated terminal configs. Regression coverage updated for stale existing Ghostty/WezTerm configs on the desktop fast path. Verification: cargo fmt --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml --all --check; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core launch_materialization --lib; cargo check --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core; git diff --check.

title: Import remaining open GitHub issues into Beads
description: Create Beads issues for all open GitHub issues that are not yet represented in .beads, without importing closed history.
close_reason: Imported remaining open GitHub issues into Beads

title: Investigate Warp as an open-source terminal UX reference
description:
## Decision
Pending. Investigate Warp for Yazelix-shaped terminal, agent, config, workspace, and shell-integration lessons without cloning Warp or assuming code reuse.

## Rationale
Warp is solving adjacent UX problems at a different application layer. The research should separate reusable concepts from ideas that would make Yazelix less terminal-native or harder to package.

## Alternatives Considered
Ignore Warp; use only conceptual UX lessons; adapt small architecture patterns subject to license review; or create follow-up beads for specific high-value features.

## Existing Context
Investigate what Yazelix can directly use from, adapt from, or be inspired by in Warp, the open-source terminal/agentic development environment. This is a research bead, not approval to vendor Warp code or make Yazelix a Warp clone. The outcome should identify practical Yazelix-shaped lessons from Warp's terminal UX, command blocks, AI/agent surfaces, shell integration, workspace model, launch/update story, settings model, and Rust/application architecture.
design:
Approach from first principles: Yazelix is a terminal workspace built on existing terminals, Zellij, Yazi, editors, shells, and generated config; Warp is a terminal/application layer. Compare only the parts that solve similar user problems without weakening Yazelix's terminal-native design.

Evaluate at least:
- Command block model, command history, and output navigation
- Agent/AI workflow placement and safety boundaries
- Shell integration model and prompt/command detection
- Workspace/session/project model
- Settings/config discoverability and sync/import/export behavior
- Keybinding and command palette/action model
- Terminal rendering/input architecture and performance lessons
- Collaboration/share/export features, if relevant
- Onboarding/update/recovery UX
- Any reusable crates or small code patterns, subject to AGPL/license and packaging review

Classify findings as: use directly, adapt conceptually, inspire only, reject, or defer. Be explicit about AGPL/license compatibility, Nix/package impact, complexity cost, and whether each idea belongs in Yazelix core, pane orchestrator, config UI, status bar, docs, or a future standalone component.
acceptance_criteria: There is a written recommendation with a concise table of Warp areas reviewed, what Yazelix should learn from each, and why. The recommendation must separate direct code reuse from architectural/product inspiration, include AGPL/license and packaging risk, identify 3-7 high-value follow-up beads when applicable, and reject ideas that would make Yazelix less portable, less terminal-native, or too proprietary-app-like. If no direct reuse is worthwhile, the bead still closes with concrete design lessons for command workflows, AI/agent surfaces, config UI, terminal integration, onboarding, or Rust architecture.

title: Show a focused stale-desktop-entry repair error at launch time
description: Teach the desktop launcher to validate the desktop-entry freshness metadata at startup and fail with a short, actionable repair message when the installed desktop entry is stale.
design: At launch time, validate the desktop-entry freshness metadata against the current installed runtime and fail fast with a short repair message when they differ. Keep the error focused: say the desktop entry is stale and recommend yzx desktop install. Avoid generic stack traces or broader reinstall guidance unless the runtime is genuinely missing.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Desktop launches can detect a stale Yazelix desktop entry before continuing into broken runtime paths. 2. The failure message names the problem as a stale desktop entry. 3. The repair path is the minimal one: yzx desktop install.

title: add links to the overview
description: Imported open GitHub issue #276.
close_reason: README overview now links out to the main docs entrypoints

title: Benchmark: clean install using Nix (cold/warm)
description: Benchmark clean Yazelix installation and first-run behavior under Nix so the project can talk concretely about cold and warm setup costs instead of guessing.
design: Measure the path users actually care about: cold install, warm install, first launch, and any major cache effects. Capture the environment, cache state, hardware notes, and the exact commands used so the numbers remain meaningful instead of becoming one-off anecdotes. The README or docs update should present the results honestly rather than as marketing theater.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has reproducible cold and warm install benchmarks that can be referenced in docs. Add unit tests for any benchmark parsing or summarization helpers, end-to-end benchmark scripts or documented procedures with detailed logs for timing, cache state, and machine context, and update the relevant docs or README with the measured results.
close_reason: Stale local backlog item. The linked GitHub issue #92 is already closed, and the useful install-performance outcome is now owned by yazelix-r1ax and yazelix-r1ax.2 rather than this old standalone benchmark/docs bead.

title: Move workspace retarget, reveal, and sidebar-editor targeting truth into the pane orchestrator
description: Continue shrinking cross-language correctness by moving more active workspace targeting truth behind the pane orchestrator. Sidebar identity is one part of the problem, but the broader goal is that current-tab workspace retargeting and reveal behavior stop depending on Nushell-side reconstruction of live session state.
design: Build on the cross-language ownership map and the sidebar-identity cleanup. The pane orchestrator should become the durable owner for hot-path workspace targeting truth, while Nushell remains a command/config layer and Lua stays a thin Yazi adapter. Prefer deleting cache-shaped or reconstruction-shaped correctness paths rather than layering more state synchronizers on top.
acceptance_criteria: Workspace retarget, reveal, and editor-sidebar targeting use a stronger pane-orchestrator-owned source of truth than they do today. Nushell-side reconstruction and cache dependence are materially reduced, and the remaining ownership boundary is explicit.
notes: Pipeline stage 2B on the workspace-truth branch. After sidebar identity is plugin-owned, extend that ownership to workspace retarget/reveal/sidebar-editor targeting so Nushell no longer reconstructs live session truth on the hot path.
close_reason: Completed: pane-orchestrator retarget now returns current-tab editor/sidebar targeting truth, yzx cwd and workspace-retarget integrations consume that single plugin-owned response instead of reconstructing state through separate calls, and the synced wasm carries the stronger retarget contract while Yazi adapter execution stays explicit in Nushell.

title: Collapse the remaining yzx_core Nushell bridge layer
description: The yzx_core Rust helper owns typed computation for config, state, preflight, materialization, and reports, but a thick Nushell bridge layer still shapes requests, translates errors, and assembles per-command report shims around those Rust owners. This bead tracks collapsing that bridge toward thin transport only.
close_reason: All three children complete: zl3f collapsed config_parser.nu wrappers (-17 lines), fikz split generated_runtime_state.nu into pure bridge transport (-109 lines), a2ex extracted shared helpers into common.nu (-35 lines). Total bridge layer reduction: 161 lines. The remaining bridge files are now thin transport modules.

title: Document NO_COLOR inheritance can make launched Yazelix sessions look white
description: Investigation note: when Yazelix is launched from an environment that has NO_COLOR=1, the managed terminal session inherits it through the launch environment. Many tools then intentionally suppress ANSI colors, so Nushell output, Yazi listings, and other CLI surfaces can appear mostly white even though Ghostty/Zellij still have some colors. This was observed after launching Yazelix from the Codex/tool environment; a normal desktop launch may not carry NO_COLOR. Quick confirmation: compare 'yzx launch --terminal ghostty' with 'env -u NO_COLOR yzx launch --terminal ghostty'. Product decision left open: decide whether managed graphical terminal launches should scrub NO_COLOR while plain yzx CLI commands continue respecting it.
acceptance_criteria: Information captured for future launch/color investigations; no implementation required until this is promoted into a bug or decision.
close_reason: Documented

title: Add broader install-artifact staleness detection to yzx doctor
description: Teach yzx doctor to detect stale or broken repairable Yazelix artifacts beyond the desktop entry, such as shell hooks, ~/.local/bin/yzx, runtime/current, and generated config references. Each diagnosis should point to the smallest repair command that fixes the specific artifact.

title: Extend semantic keybinding remaps beyond Yazelix-owned Zellij actions
description: The first semantic keybinding implementation covers Yazelix-owned Zellij pane-orchestrator actions. A broader remap model should decide how far Yazelix should go for native Zellij actions, Helix, Yazi, terminal-emulator conflicts, and app-local keymaps without pretending every layer has the same ownership model.
design: Start from the zellij.keybindings action-map contract and define separate ownership scopes for native Zellij actions, managed Yazi keymaps, editor-local helpers, and terminal-emulator shortcuts. Prefer explicit semantic action ids where Yazelix owns the behavior, and keep native sidecar files as the escape hatch where another app owns the keymap. Add conflict metadata only where Yazelix can detect or prevent the conflict reliably.
acceptance_criteria: There is a defended contract for which keybinding layers Yazelix can semantically remap and which remain sidecar/native-owned. Users get documented paths for native Zellij, Yazi, and editor remapping. Any implemented extra remap surface has defaults, schema/contract metadata, Home Manager parity, conflict validation, and behavior tests. The existing zellij.keybindings action map remains backward-compatible.
close_reason: Completed: reconciled broader semantic remap scope with the new keybinding_action_ownership contract, documented native paths for Zellij/Yazi/Helix/terminal remaps in docs/keybindings.md, kept zellij.keybindings backward-compatible, and created registry/Yazi follow-ups yazelix-poy6 and yazelix-ep4s.

title: Add a Helix note-taking section
description: Add a focused documentation section on note taking in Helix for Yazelix users, with special attention to Markdown-heavy workflows, Obsidian-style vaults, and markdown-oxide support.
design: This should not become a generic Helix note-taking essay. Focus on the practical Yazelix angle: how to work on Markdown notes inside the managed editor, what language tooling matters, how markdown-oxide fits in, and what an Obsidian user needs to know when living in a terminal-first workspace instead of a GUI vault app.
acceptance_criteria: The docs give a clear path for a Yazelix user who wants a serious Markdown or Obsidian-adjacent workflow in Helix. The section explains the role of markdown-oxide, shows a realistic note-taking setup, and avoids hand-wavy editor advice. Verify the referenced tooling and commands in the current environment and keep examples and logs concrete enough that regressions in docs examples would be obvious.
close_reason: Not planned

title: Improve README layouts section
description: Refresh the README Layouts section so it explains the layout model without recommending three_column for Claude Code or AI tools, and keep the detailed layouts link at the end of the section.
close_reason: Improved the README Layouts section around the managed sidebar/editor pane model, config knobs, built-in layout cycling, and customization boundary. Removed the three_column Claude Code/AI tools recommendation. Verified: rg for removed wording, git diff --check, validate-docs-experience, validate-readme-version.

title: Cut v17.3 release
close_reason: Completed: cut v17.3 release metadata, updated YAZELIX_VERSION/README/changelog/upgrade notes, and validated with git diff --check, validate-upgrade-contract, validate-readme-version, and validate-docs-experience.

title: add process viewer popup
description:
Imported GitHub issue #602.

pick a free keybinding, maybe alt t

likely use the bottom program. evaluate alternatives

maybe have this as a commented out option? but then it's hard to discover from ratconfig UI

check the storage size and evaluate
notes: Rust dependency gate: no new Rust crates. The implementation should reuse the existing action registry, Zellij materialization, popup command, config schema, and runtime tool registry. New package dependency is the Nix runtime tool package bottom, exposed as command btm and hostable like lazygit.
close_reason: Completed first-class btm process viewer popup. Added bottom/btm to the runtime tool registry as hostable, generated zellij.popup_commands.btm and zellij.keybindings.btm defaults, wired the zellij.btm action through yzpp with Alt Shift B, exposed it in yzx keys/config/docs/Home Manager, and verified with cargo fmt --check, git diff --check, focused key/zellij/config/popup tests, yzx_control keys output, runtime tool registry eval, docs/config/upgrade/nix/flake/contract validators, and nix build .#runtime_ghostty --no-link --no-write-lock-file.

title: Guard doctor config creation from overwriting existing settings
description: doctor --fix can overwrite an existing valid ~/.config/yazelix/settings.jsonc because the config-creation fix path is keyed off fuzzy result text: status == info and message contains default. The install-owner informational finding "default Nix profile" satisfies that condition, so a profile-owned runtime can cause the fix flow to render settings_default.jsonc over the live user config. Replace the fuzzy match with an explicit fix_action for missing config creation and fail/skip if the target already exists.
acceptance_criteria: doctor --fix only creates settings.jsonc when a finding explicitly requests config creation. Existing settings.jsonc is never overwritten by the default-config creation path, including when install-owner output mentions the default Nix profile. A regression test covers the exact profile-info collision. Verification covers focused doctor tests and relevant Rust checks.
close_reason: Fixed. doctor config creation now requires explicit fix_action=create_default_settings_config and writes settings.jsonc with create_new semantics, so default-profile info findings cannot overwrite existing user config. Verified with cargo fmt --check, git diff --check, cargo test -p yazelix_core doctor_commands, cargo test -p yazelix_core doctor_config_report, cargo check -p yazelix_core --all-targets, and yzx_repo_validator validate-rust-test-traceability.

title: pin editor pane zellij plugin
description:
Imported GitHub issue #510.

maybe top or bottom, or configurable
close_reason: Not planned: local editor-pane pinning is the wrong layer. Upstream Zellij #4084 owns the stacked-pane ordering bug; Yazelix should wait for that fix, contribute a PR upstream, or temporarily carry a thin Zellij fork with only that fix rather than building local pane-pinning mechanics.

title: Home Manager activation validator misses generated runtime hook regressions
notes: Added a cheap static guard for deleted Home Manager runtime-hook helpers and a real installed-runtime validator path that builds a temporary Home Manager generation, runs its activate script, and checks generated Yazelix runtime artifacts. Local execution on this machine hit a disk-space ceiling during the Nix rebuild, but the validator is wired into validate-installed-runtime-contract for CI.
close_reason: Completed

title: Yazi: open the current file or directory in the system file manager
description: Add a Yazelix-managed Yazi action that opens the current file or directory in the host system file manager, on Linux and ideally macOS too.
design: This should start with an investigation because Yazi may already support part of this flow natively. Verify what Yazi already provides, then decide whether Yazelix only needs to surface it through a keybinding or whether Yazelix needs a small wrapper for Linux and macOS. The user-facing goal is simple: from Yazi, hit a keybinding and open the current location in the platform file manager. This is likely a low-hanging fruit and a good candidate to tackle soon once planning cleanup is done.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear supported way from Yazi to open the current file or directory in the system file manager. If Yazi already supports it cleanly, Yazelix documents or binds that flow. If not, Yazelix adds the minimal integration needed for Linux and macOS. Add unit tests for opener selection and platform dispatch helpers, integration tests for keybinding routing, and end-to-end validation that logs the selected path, platform opener invoked, and fallback behavior when the opener is unavailable.
close_reason: Not planned

title: Adopt Nushell-style tutor flow for yzx tutor
description:
Follow up to the shipped `yzx tutor` MVP.

Keep this bead strictly about a richer tutor flow, not config onboarding. Config-first setup belongs under `yzx onboard`, not here.
design:
Adopt the shape of Nushell's built-in `tutor`: explicit subcommands like `begin`, `list`, and short guided steps instead of a single static overview. Keep the scope Yazelix-specific rather than copying Nushell literally. The goal is a small guided learning flow with mini quests that help users practice the managed workspace model.

Potential shape:
- `yzx tutor begin` starts the first Yazelix lesson
- `yzx tutor list` shows available lessons
- lessons are short, structured, and action-oriented
- mini quests ask the user to try concrete actions like `yzx keys`, `yzx menu`, `yzx cwd`, and Yazi-driven editor opens

Non-goal:
- guided `yazelix.toml` creation or interactive setup questionnaires; that belongs to `yzx onboard`
acceptance_criteria: The tutor surface can present multiple short lessons with explicit entrypoints instead of only a flat overview. At least one lesson includes a mini quest with a concrete Yazelix workflow to try. The flow stays distinct from `yzx help` \(command reference\), `yzx keys` \(keybinding discovery\), and `yzx onboard` \(config creation\).
notes:
Dependency decision, 2026-04-28:
- Production Rust crates: none new. Tutor flow is static front-door rendering and parser logic.
- Dev-only crates: none new. Existing yzx_control integration tests cover the CLI boundary.
- In-house logic: enum-backed lesson registry, parser, help/list rendering, and short guided lesson copy.
- Rejected alternatives: interactive terminal state machine, markdown lesson files, copying Nushell tutor internals, or merging tutor with config onboarding.
- Packaging impact: none.
Implementation result, 2026-04-28:
- `yzx tutor` now keeps the guided overview but points users into an explicit lesson flow.
- Added `yzx tutor begin`, `yzx tutor list`, `yzx tutor workspace`, `yzx tutor discovery`, and `yzx tutor tool_tutors`; Helix/Nushell tutor commands remain external tutor bridges.
- The first lesson includes a concrete workspace mini quest around `yzx warp .`, `yzx keys yazi`, and `yzx status`.
- Updated CLI metadata, command-palette contract docs, `yzx_control` usage, and `docs/yzx_cli.md`.
- Verification: cargo fmt --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml --all; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core front_door_commands::tests::; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_control_front_door; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_core_command_metadata; yzx_repo_validator validate-contracts.
close_reason: Added guided yzx tutor lesson flow

title: Allow and document Starship customization
description: Allow users to customize Starship in a supported way and document where the boundary lies between Yazelix-managed prompt behavior and user-owned prompt configuration.
design: This should preserve Yazelix's managed defaults while giving users a real override path. Decide whether the right model is merge-based, file-inclusion-based, or a simpler user hook, then document exactly what is stable and what can drift. Avoid a design where Starship becomes a second uncontrolled config island with no compatibility story.
acceptance_criteria: Users can apply Starship customization without forking core Yazelix files, and the docs explain the supported customization path clearly. Add unit tests for merge or override helpers, integration tests for the chosen generation path, and end-to-end checks that verify the final prompt config is generated as expected, with detailed logs of the merge source and output path.
close_reason: Not planned

title: Yazi: open multiple selected files
description: Let Yazi open multiple selected files into the managed editor workflow instead of forcing one-at-a-time opens when a multi-file edit session is the natural next step.
design: The feature should respect the existing managed editor model rather than bypassing it. Decide how selections map into Helix or other supported editors, how pane reuse and focus behave, and what should happen when the current editor already has an active workspace context. Keep the first version predictable even if that means limiting which editors or selection counts are supported initially.
acceptance_criteria: Users can select multiple files in Yazi and open them through Yazelix in one action. Add unit tests for selection aggregation and editor-command construction helpers, integration tests around selection collection and editor-command construction, and end-to-end coverage for multi-file open behavior with detailed logs showing the selected paths, target pane, and resulting editor invocation.
notes:
Dependency decision for multi-file Yazi open: use no new Rust crates. Production crates stay unchanged; dev-only crates stay unchanged. Build selection aggregation, payload compatibility, and editor command construction in-house because this is small path/vector formatting logic. Rejected alternatives: shell-specific argument parsers or broader command-building crates, which would add packaging surface without buying meaningful safety. Packaging impact: none beyond rebuilding and syncing the existing pane-orchestrator wasm after source changes.
Implemented multi-file Yazi open by changing the bundled opener from legacy "" to Yazi's %s selected-file expansion, allowing yzx_control zellij open-editor to accept one or more paths, carrying file_paths through the pane-orchestrator open_file payload while preserving legacy file_path compatibility, and opening all selected files in managed Helix/Neovim panes or a new editor pane when the managed editor is missing. Verification: pane-orchestrator editor_open_contract tests, yzx_control multi-open workspace tests, yazi materialization test, yzx dev rust fmt --check, yzx dev rust check, cargo check for pane orchestrator, build_pane_orchestrator --sync, validate-pane-orchestrator-sync, validate-workspace-session-contract, validate-specs, validate-rust-test-traceability, validate-package-rust-test-purity, git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: Yazi now passes selected files through %s to yzx_control, the control path accepts multiple target paths, the pane-orchestrator payload and editor command helper open all selected files, and focused tests plus wasm sync validators pass.

title: Ratconfig private boundary deletion and extraction pass
description: Use the existing config UI model/editor/render/apply split to decide what can be deleted, what is generic ratatui config editing, and what must remain Yazelix adapter policy. Keep this private unless the adapter is thin enough for a real yazelix_ratconfig child repo.
design: Delete-first. Start from config_ui.rs and rust_core/yazelix_core/src/yazelix_ratconfig/*. Remove redundant model/editor/render glue if the split exposed dead wrappers. Keep Yazelix settings, Home Manager status, JSONC patching, read-only/native status, and runtime apply local.
acceptance_criteria: The ratconfig boundary is smaller or clearly justified, redundant config UI glue is deleted where possible, public extraction is accepted or deferred with reasons, behavior-backed config UI tests pass, Rust inventory/budget are updated, and LOC movement is recorded.
notes: Implemented private ratconfig boundary pass. Moved UiRowRef plus shared render labels/styles/truncation/detail-line helpers under rust_core/yazelix_core/src/yazelix_ratconfig, keeping Yazelix-specific model loading, Home Manager/read-only/native status, JSONC patching, keybinding detail, and runtime apply in config_ui.rs. Deleted model_accepts_non_yazelix_field_namespace because it only proved Rust structs can store arbitrary strings for a future crate split and did not defend shipped behavior. Public yazelix_ratconfig extraction remains deferred: ConfigUiApp and detail rendering still carry Yazelix-specific request/apply/keybinding policy, so the adapter is not thin enough yet. Scorecard: net -47 raw Rust lines, tokei Rust code LOC -43, budget ratcheted to 72,832 tracked raw Rust lines. Verification: cargo fmt --all -- --check; cargo check -p yazelix_core --all-targets; cargo test -p yazelix_core --test yzx_core_config_ui config_ui; cargo test -p yazelix_core config_ui; validate-rust-ownership-budget; validate-docs-experience; git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed
