description: Keep the yzx CLI deep documentation link at the end of the README CLI section instead of opening the section with it, matching the preferred README structure for these reference links.
close_reason: Moved the README yzx CLI deep-link from the section opener to the section closing line. Verified: git diff --check; validate-docs-experience; validate-readme-version.

title: Delete or demote legacy maintainer wrapper scripts
description: Audit outcome: several dev scripts are now thin compatibility wrappers around canonical maintainer owners or manual-only tooling. Examples include update_zellij_pane_orchestrator.nu, update_zjstatus.nu, record_demo.nu, record_demo_fonts.nu, and packaging-specific nixpkgs validators.
design: Retained behavior: pane-orchestrator wasm build/sync, zjstatus vendoring, demo recording when intentionally kept, and nixpkgs smoke validation. Candidate surviving owner: maintainer/plugin_build.nu, maintainer/update_workflow.nu, public docs, or explicit manual scripts that are not shipped as product runtime contract. Stop condition: do not delete scripts that still provide the only documented command for a maintained release/package workflow; demote to manual docs if deletion would hide an intentional maintainer entrypoint.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Each candidate script is classified as canonical owner, thin wrapper, manual/exploratory, packaging validator, or delete candidate. 2. Thin wrappers are deleted or replaced by direct calls to canonical maintainer modules. 3. Manual-only scripts are documented as manual or moved out of normal runtime expectations. 4. Release, vendoring, wasm sync, and nixpkgs validation workflows keep a supported entrypoint. 5. README or maintainer docs stop presenting deleted wrappers as live product commands.
close_reason: Completed

title: Align Ghostty override discovery with upstream config paths
description: Investigate the report that Ghostty may have ignored a discovered user override. Re-evaluate against the current flat Yazelix config contract: ~/.config/yazelix/settings.jsonc owns semantic settings, and Ghostty sidecar overrides live at ~/.config/yazelix/terminal_ghostty.conf when enabled. Historical nested user_configs paths are not the current target.
design: Check whether Ghostty override discovery is supposed to read a sidecar, a settings.jsonc field, or both. The desired contract should be explicit: config_mode selects generated defaults vs user-owned sidecar materialization, and the Ghostty target path should be flat under ~/.config/yazelix. Avoid adding compatibility for old nested paths unless a real released migration requires it.
acceptance_criteria:
- Reproduce or rule out the Ghostty override miss with current ~/.config/yazelix/settings.jsonc and ~/.config/yazelix/terminal_ghostty.conf paths.
- Document the current Ghostty override contract in the relevant config docs or bead notes.
- If a bug exists, fix discovery/materialization so Ghostty behaves like other terminal sidecars.
- Add focused coverage for the actual contract if code changes are needed.
- Do not reintroduce nested user_configs paths as a supported surface.
notes:
Investigation 2026-05-05:
- Current managed override path works: a flat ~/.config/yazelix/terminal_ghostty.conf file or symlink is referenced by generated Ghostty config as config-file = ?"<flat path>". A temp-dir probe with a symlinked terminal_ghostty.conf generated the expected optional config-file line.
- The reported ~/.config/yazelix/user_config/terminal/ghostty path is not the current contract, and differs from the old supported legacy spelling user_configs/terminal/ghostty. Legacy symlinks are intentionally rejected by resolve_flat_config_file so external owners move to the flat path explicitly.
- Do not make managed override discovery read upstream Ghostty native configs by default; docs/contracts/terminal_override_layers.md says native config takeover belongs to terminal.config_mode = "user".
- Real adjacent gap: terminal.config_mode = "user" still hard-codes ~/.config/ghostty/config for Ghostty. Current upstream Ghostty docs use config.ghostty first, config as pre-1.2.3 fallback, and macOS also loads ~/Library/Application Support/com.mitchellh.ghostty/config.ghostty then config after XDG. Fix should target native Ghostty user-config lookup, not the managed terminal_ghostty.conf override layer.
close_reason: Completed: Ghostty terminal.config_mode=user now checks upstream native config candidates including config.ghostty, legacy config, and macOS Application Support paths, while managed terminal_ghostty.conf override discovery remains unchanged.

title: Collapse integration config reads into explicit integration facts
description: Audit outcome: yazi.nu and managed_editor.nu still parse the full Yazelix config at live integration time to answer small questions such as sidebar enablement, Yazi/ya command names, and managed editor kind. That keeps config_parser.nu in the integration path even when the pane orchestrator already owns live session state.
design: Retained behavior: configured yazi_command and yazi_ya_command, enable_sidebar behavior, managed Helix wrapper detection, Neovim fallback, and clear no-sidebar/outside-Zellij messages. Candidate surviving owner: caller-provided integration facts or a Rust-owned config/control-plane facts request, with Nu retaining only external-tool adapter behavior. Stop condition: do not move process execution or ya/zellij action calls into Rust just to reduce config reads; the cut only counts if it removes duplicated config ownership from integration glue.
acceptance_criteria: 1. All parse_yazelix_config use in integrations is inventoried. 2. A narrow facts source is chosen and documented before implementation. 3. Live pane-orchestrator session state remains the only sidebar identity truth. 4. Config reads are removed or reduced without losing configured Yazi/ya/editor behavior. 5. Verification covers Yazi command resolver overrides, managed editor kind detection, sidebar-disabled copy, and plugin-owned sidebar sync.
notes: Rust dependency gate 2026-04-22: no new crates. Reuse existing in-house config normalization/control-plane/workspace command logic inside rust_core. Rejected adding serde-adjacent helper crates or CLI frameworks for this cut because the seam is a narrow facts request over existing JSON/env plumbing, and extra crates would not delete the Nu owner.
close_reason: Completed

title: make startup faster
description:
Use this as the umbrella for startup-performance work now that desktop and managed-launch profiling is available from both repo checkouts and installed runtimes.

The latest installed-runtime profiles on 2026-04-18 show three common startup choke points worth tracking explicitly:
- `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe` at roughly 1034-1039ms
- `shellhook.sync_yzx_extern_bridge` at roughly 604-618ms
- `inner.materialize_runtime_configs` at roughly 509-513ms

This umbrella should stay broad and outcome-oriented while the concrete work happens in child beads with explicit rewrite sequencing.
design:
Keep this issue as the performance umbrella, not the implementation bead. The current evidence suggests a split rather than one big 'make startup faster' project:

- Do before the broader Rust rewrite: the detached terminal spawn/probe tax, because it is a large live UX hit and defines the launch-success versus early-failure contract the rewrite must preserve.
- Evaluate before the rewrite with a hard simplicity bar: runtime config materialization, because a slim invalidation fix could pay off now and clarify boundaries, but a heavy caching layer would be the wrong trade.
- Do during the Rust rewrite seam work: extern-bridge synchronization, because its ownership/timing should follow the Rust/Nushell bridge contract instead of accumulating transitional Nushell complexity.

Do not create a separate 'after the rewrite' bucket for these three choke points. If they are worth doing, they are either current UX debt that should be trimmed now or design inputs that belong inside the rewrite sequencing itself.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The main measured startup choke points have focused child beads with clear before-versus-during rewrite sequencing.
2. This umbrella remains the GitHub-visible startup-performance tracker while implementation details live in the child beads.
3. Follow-up optimization work is driven by measured startup profiles rather than vague startup-speed complaints.
notes: 2026-04-18 profiling split: child beads yazelix-4xp1.1, yazelix-4xp1.2, and yazelix-4xp1.3 were created from the installed-runtime desktop/managed launch reports. Use those children for concrete implementation and keep this issue as the umbrella and GitHub-facing tracker.
close_reason: Completed: startup-performance umbrella is done; concrete child beads for terminal spawn/probe tax, runtime config materialization, and extern-bridge startup cost are closed.

title: Trim the detached terminal spawn/probe startup tax
description:
Latest installed-runtime startup profiles show both desktop launch and managed new-window launch paying roughly one full second inside `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe`. This is now one of the clearest user-visible startup taxes in the measured launch path.

The current probe is valuable because it makes early terminal-launch failure visible instead of silently assuming success. But the profiler suggests the current policy is overly coarse for the steady-state success path. The goal is to reduce the fixed success-path tax without regressing the failure visibility that the desktop-launch work just restored.
design:
Treat this as pre-Rust-rewrite work. It is worth fixing before the rewrite because it is both highly user-visible today and a boundary the later Rust launch path must preserve: how Yazelix decides a detached terminal launch is truly alive versus how it reports early death.

Keep this delete-first. First separate actual terminal spawn cost from deliberate probe waiting. Prefer a smaller readiness criterion, a tighter early-death window, or a less blunt success policy over adding a long-lived supervisor, daemon, or large Nushell state machine. If the only robust solution requires substantial new launch orchestration machinery, stop and reframe that larger ownership change under the later Rust launch/bootstrap slice instead of growing transitional Nushell complexity.

Current seam to target:
- `nushell/scripts/utils/terminal_launcher.nu::run_detached_terminal_launch` currently writes a launch log, starts `nohup bash -lc ... &`, sleeps for a fixed `1` second, treats `kill -0 $pid` after that sleep as success, and only then removes the log / returns success.
- That means the measured `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe` step currently includes a deliberate one-second success-path wait, not just real terminal spawn time.
- `nushell/scripts/core/launch_yazelix.nu` depends on this helper to decide whether to keep the chosen terminal candidate or fall through to the next candidate.

Constraints:
- preserve early-death visibility and useful stderr tail reporting when a terminal exits immediately
- preserve terminal fallback behavior in `launch_yazelix.nu`
- preserve the startup-profile contract that this remains a first-class `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe` step
- do not add terminal-specific readiness machinery unless it is obviously tiny and justified

Likely acceptable solution shapes:
- replace the blunt `sleep 1` with a shorter bounded poll loop that exits early on stable success or early death
- split “early death detection window” from “steady-state success confirmation” more precisely
- keep the launch log only long enough to capture actionable failure output, then delete it on success

Likely non-acceptable solution shapes for this bead:
- a resident supervisor/daemon
- a broad background process manager
- a terminal-specific protocol matrix just to shave this tax
- a solution that hides failures and makes launch feel faster only by becoming less honest

Sequencing decision: do before the broader Rust rewrite, because this defines the launch contract and should not remain as a known one-second tax while waiting for later architectural work.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Startup profiles for both `yzx dev profile --desktop` and `yzx dev profile --launch --terminal <name>` show a materially smaller `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe` step on successful launch.
2. Early terminal-launch failure is still surfaced clearly enough that desktop/managed launch errors do not become silent again.
3. The implementation keeps the launch contract understandable and does not add broad new background-management machinery just to save this probe time.
4. Focused regression coverage defends both the faster success path and the preserved early-failure reporting behavior of `run_detached_terminal_launch`.
notes:
2026-04-19 code and profile context:

Measured choke point:
- installed-runtime startup profiles reviewed on 2026-04-18 showed `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe` around `1034-1039ms` on both desktop and managed-launch scenarios
- that makes this one of the clearest fixed user-visible startup taxes left in the measured path

Relevant files:
- `nushell/scripts/utils/terminal_launcher.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/core/launch_yazelix.nu`
- `docs/specs/startup_profile_scenarios.md`
- `nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_maintainer.nu`

Existing defended contract:
- `docs/specs/startup_profile_scenarios.md` requires detached launch probing to remain visible as `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe`
- `test_startup_profile_records_detached_terminal_probe` already proves the step exists as a first-class measured phase
- desktop/managed profile tests already prove profiling waits through `inner.zellij_handoff_ready`

Current testing gap worth addressing in this bead:
- there is currently good coverage that the probe step is recorded, but weak direct coverage for the success-vs-early-failure behavior of `run_detached_terminal_launch` itself
- this bead should preferably add focused behavior coverage for both:
  - a success path that no longer waits the full old one-second tax
  - an early-failure path that still surfaces actionable output

GLM-5.1 instructions:
1. Read before editing:
   - `nushell/scripts/utils/terminal_launcher.nu`
   - `nushell/scripts/core/launch_yazelix.nu`
   - `docs/specs/startup_profile_scenarios.md`
   - `nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_maintainer.nu`
2. Start with the delete-first question: which part of the current ~1s cost is deliberate policy rather than required safety?
3. Keep the probe as a single understandable helper. Do not spread launch-state machinery across multiple modules unless the current helper truly cannot carry the simpler policy.
4. Preserve the profiling step name `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe`.
5. Add focused regression coverage for both success and failure behavior, not just profile-record presence.
6. Prefer a bounded poll/early-exit shape over a hardcoded fixed sleep.
7. Stop and report back instead of landing a large orchestration rewrite if the only robust fix seems to require a supervisor or heavy terminal-specific logic.

Suggested verification after the patch:
- `yzx run nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_maintainer.nu`
- `yzx run nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu`
- `yzx run nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_syntax.nu`
- `nix develop -c nu -c 'use nushell/scripts/yzx/dev.nu *; yzx dev lint_nu'`
- manual comparison with `yzx dev profile --desktop` and `yzx dev profile --launch --terminal ghostty`
close_reason: Completed: trimmed the detached terminal spawn/probe tax by replacing the old one-second-style wait with a bounded early-death probe, preserved actionable early launch failure reporting, added focused maintainer regressions for the fast success path and delayed early failure visibility, and verified the updated runtime manually plus with maintainer tests, syntax validation, and nu-lint.

title: Avoid redundant runtime config materialization on startup
description:
Recent installed-runtime startup profiles show `inner.materialize_runtime_configs` still costing roughly 500ms on both desktop launch and managed launch. That is large enough to matter, but it sits in a part of the system where it would be easy to overreact with a heavy caching layer that Yazelix would later have to rewrite or defend forever.

The point of this bead is to find out whether there is a slim delete-first win: unconditional rewrites, over-broad invalidation, or repeated work that can be narrowed without making config correctness mysterious.
design:
Treat this as a pre-Rust-rewrite candidate with a hard complexity budget. It is worth evaluating before the rewrite because runtime-config materialization is a live hot path and any slimmer invalidation boundary will help both current users and the later migration matrix. But do not build a generalized cache or migration engine here.

Prefer deleting unnecessary rewrites or narrowing invalidation over introducing fingerprints for every file, wide persistent cache state, or other machinery that mainly shifts complexity around. If profiling shows the only meaningful win requires broad caching/state management, stop and defer the deeper solution to the later Rust runtime layer instead of landing a lot of transitional Nushell code.

Sequencing decision: investigate and potentially land before the broader Rust rewrite only if the solution stays small and clarifies the invalidation contract. Otherwise explicitly defer the deeper optimization into the runtime rewrite lane.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The materialization step is broken down enough to identify which substeps dominate the current ~500ms cost.
2. If a pre-rewrite optimization lands, warm unchanged startup avoids clearly redundant runtime-config work while preserving config correctness.
3. The resulting implementation stays slim; if the only winning shape is broad cache machinery, the bead is deferred rather than forcing that machinery into Nushell.
close_reason: Completed: runtime materialization now reports generated_runtime_state substeps, warm Yazi generation skips unchanged managed file rewrites with stable headers, start_yazelix_inner reuses the computed config state for record_runtime_state, and repo-driven startup profiling now pins YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR to the selected source root instead of accidentally measuring the installed runtime. Verified with focused generated-config/profiler regressions, syntax validation, nu-lint, and warm repo-backed profiles that reduced inner.record_runtime_state from the old ~126-133ms range to ~3-5ms and cut inner.materialize_runtime_configs + record_runtime_state to roughly ~425-440ms on unchanged warm runs.

title: Fold extern-bridge startup cost into the Rust/Nushell bridge seam
description:
Recent installed-runtime startup profiles show `shellhook.sync_yzx_extern_bridge` costing roughly 600ms on both desktop launch and managed launch. That is a meaningful startup tax, but this step sits directly on the command-bridge seam that Yazelix already plans to revisit as part of incremental Rust insertion.

This bead exists to keep that cost visible and intentional instead of either forgetting it or papering over it with ad hoc Nushell caching before the bridge contract is decided.
design:
Treat this as a during-the-Rust-rewrite seam, not as default standalone pre-rewrite Nushell work. The right question is not just how to shave time off the current sync step, but whether this bridge work belongs eagerly on startup at all once the Rust/Nushell integration contract is defined.

Use `yazelix-kt5.1.1` as the gating contract work. Only land a pre-rewrite change here if it is obviously tiny and delete-first, such as removing an eager sync that is clearly unnecessary today. Otherwise solve it when the bridge contract answers where generated extern surfaces should live, when they should be refreshed, and how startup should avoid paying this cost unnecessarily.

Sequencing decision: do during the Rust rewrite planning/bridge-slice work, not after. Waiting until after the rewrite would turn a known bridge hot spot into cleanup instead of a design input.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The Rust/Nushell bridge contract explicitly states when extern bridge synchronization happens and why that timing is required.
2. Startup no longer pays the full current eager-sync cost unless that cost is explicitly justified by the preserved contract.
3. User-facing command discovery, completion, and bridge behavior remain correct after the timing/ownership change.
notes:
Implementation notes:
- Added fingerprinted generated-state for the Nushell yzx extern bridge at initializers/nushell/yazelix_extern.fingerprint.json.
- Warm sync now verifies the command-surface fingerprint plus generated extern hash and returns without spawning Nushell when current.
- Refresh failure is non-destructive: an existing generated bridge is kept; only first-run/missing bridge gets an empty sourceable placeholder.
- Updated docs/specs/rust_nushell_bridge_contract.md with Extern Bridge Timing so future Rust bridge work preserves this timing.

Verification:
- nu-lint nushell/scripts/utils/nushell_externs.nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_shell_managed_config_contracts.nu nushell/scripts/setup/environment.nu: 0 violations.
- nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_shell_managed_config_contracts.nu: 11/11 passed.
- nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu: passed.
- nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_default_test_traceability.nu: passed.
- git diff --check: passed.
- Local direct probe: cold generation about 560ms, warm fingerprinted reuse about 6ms.
close_reason: Completed: extern bridge sync now uses fingerprinted warm reuse, keeps previous generated bridge on refresh failure, and the Rust/Nushell bridge spec documents the timing contract.

title: Evaluate standalone yazelix_screen distribution for terminal users
description:
Evaluate whether yazelix_screen should become usable outside Yazelix and outside Zellij, as a standalone terminal welcome/splash/status experience for any terminal user

## Context
yazelix_screen already has product value as a terminal-native visual surface
The experience may not need to be tied to a Zellij plugin or a full Yazelix session
A standalone path could make the screen useful for Ghostty, WezTerm, Kitty, plain shell startup, demos, and other terminal workflows
design:
Start by separating renderer, runtime host, configuration, and session integration concerns
Evaluate whether standalone use should be a binary, flake output, shell hook, terminal integration snippet, or future separate repo
Keep Zellij integration as one host target rather than the core identity if the boundary is clean
Do not extract until ownership, release cadence, and config compatibility are explicit
acceptance_criteria:
Decision identifies which parts of yazelix_screen are generic terminal UI and which are Yazelix or Zellij specific
Decision compares in-repo standalone binary, flake output, shell integration, and separate repository options
Decision defines a minimal supported standalone contract for terminal users
Decision records whether extraction is worth doing now, later, or not at all
notes:
2026-04-28 decision: keep standalone yazelix_screen distribution as a staged package-first possibility, not a separate extraction now.

Generic terminal UI today:
- rust_core/yazelix_screen owns animation state, frame generation, alternate-screen rendering primitives, terminal-size reads, and ANSI row-addressed output.
- The crate depends only on crossterm, so the renderer itself is not Zellij-specific.

Yazelix-specific surface today:
- The public command is yzx screen, implemented in yazelix_core front-door code.
- yzx screen resolves Yazelix style names, reads session/config facts for Game of Life cell style, and belongs to the Yazelix command surface.
- The supported standalone-ish surface already available is running package-provided yzx screen [STYLE] outside Zellij in any terminal.

Zellij-specific coupling:
- None in yazelix_screen itself.
- Zellij only consumes the style contract indirectly for the pane-orchestrator screen saver config.

Option comparison:
- In-repo binary/flake output: best next step if we want a true standalone preview, because it can reuse the crate while making config defaults explicit.
- Current yzx screen command: good enough as the minimal supported contract for Yazelix package users outside Zellij.
- Shell hook: too invasive for first distribution; it would create startup ownership questions before the binary contract is proven.
- Terminal-specific snippets: poor fit because the screen is an interactive process, not static terminal config.
- Separate repo: premature until an in-repo standalone binary proves config, release, and support boundaries.

Minimal supported contract now: yzx screen [STYLE] from the Yazelix package may be used outside Zellij, enters the alternate screen, exits on keypress, adapts to terminal size, and uses Yazelix style/config semantics. No separate any-terminal package or separate repo is promised yet.
close_reason: Completed: evaluated the renderer/runtime/config/session boundaries. Decision is to keep yzx screen as the current package-provided outside-Zellij surface, avoid a separate repo now, and only consider an in-repo standalone binary/flake output after the config/release contract is explicit.

title: Add in-repo standalone yazelix_screen binary and flake output
description: Create the first concrete standalone yazelix_screen surface inside the Yazelix repo. This follows the yazelix-4xt8 decision: keep yzx screen as the current outside-Zellij surface for now, and only add a true standalone surface as an in-repo binary/flake output before considering any separate repo.
design: Package-first and in-repo. Add a small standalone binary that reuses the yazelix_screen crate and exposes the retained screen styles without requiring a Yazelix session, Zellij, or the full yzx command surface. Prefer a flake package/app such as .#yazelix_screen over shell hooks or terminal-specific snippets. Keep config minimal and explicit: terminal size, style selection, keypress exit, resize behavior, and defaults should be owned by this binary rather than borrowed implicitly from user Yazelix config. Do not mutate terminal configs or user files. Do not extract to a separate repository in this slice.
acceptance_criteria: 1. A user can build or run an in-repo standalone yazelix_screen package/app from the flake. 2. The binary renders at least the retained animated styles without requiring Zellij or a Yazelix session. 3. The binary exits cleanly on keypress and preserves terminal state. 4. The package/app boundary is documented as a preview or supported surface with explicit non-goals. 5. Existing yzx screen behavior remains unchanged.
notes: Dependency decision before implementation: no new production or dev crates. Reuse the existing yazelix_screen crate and its existing crossterm dependency for raw mode, key polling, terminal size, and alternate-screen output. Build CLI parsing, style dispatch, and frame loop in-house/std because the standalone surface is small and adding a CLI framework would be unnecessary package surface. Rejected alternatives: depend on yazelix_core front-door command code, because that would pull the standalone binary back into Yazelix session/config semantics; add clap, because the command has one optional style plus help; shell wrapper, because the requested surface is a real binary/flake output. Packaging impact: add one Rust package output and app, not to the default Yazelix runtime closure.
close_reason: Completed: added standalone yazelix_screen Rust binary, .#yazelix_screen flake package/app, and canonical distribution contract. Verified cargo test -p yazelix_screen, cargo fmt --check, nix build .#yazelix_screen, nix run .#yazelix_screen -- --help, and validate-contracts.

title: Seed agent usage widgets from recent cache for fast first paint
description: New Yazelix windows can paint agent usage widgets empty or late because the per-window status-bar cache starts without agent_usage facts and waits for the delayed background provider refresh. Seed a new window's cache from a recent sibling session cache so zjstatus can render last-known usage immediately, while normal refresh still updates in the background.
design: Use recent sibling status_bar_cache.json files as a bounded first-paint seed. Preserve current-window agent_usage if present; otherwise scan the newest sibling cache candidates, require non-empty agent_usage plus a recent agent_usage_updated_at_unix_seconds, and let the normal throttled provider refresh replace the seed.
acceptance_criteria: New window status-cache writes render a configured Codex usage widget from a recent sibling cache without running provider binaries from zjstatus. Stale sibling usage facts are ignored. Existing current-window cache facts remain preserved across status-bus writes.
close_reason: Completed: status-cache writes now seed agent_usage from recent sibling session caches for fast first paint, reject stale sibling seeds, and keep zjstatus provider-free; verified with status_cache/agent_usage Rust tests and contract validation.

title: Evaluate local child-repo integration workflow
description: Evaluate how Yazelix maintainers should test main-repo runtime behavior against local child-repo changes before any child or main push. This covers local flake input overrides, temporary lock overrides, a possible yzx dev wrapper, and the minimum smoke checks needed to prove generated runtime integration without publishing child commits first.
design:
Recommendation: use explicit local flake overrides as the default maintainer workflow for child repos that already publish package outputs, and keep copied-wasm sync commands only for first-party Zellij plugin artifacts until those plugins migrate to locked child packages.

Preferred package workflow:
1. Build and test the child repo locally first, using its own package or check target, for example `nix build ../yazelix-zellij-bar#yazelix_zellij_bar --no-link` or `nix build ../yazelix-yazi-assets#yazelix_yazi_assets --no-link`.
2. Smoke the main repo against the adjacent checkout with an explicit override, without editing or committing path inputs:
   - `nix build .#runtime --override-input yazelixZellijBar ../yazelix-zellij-bar --no-link`
   - `nix build .#runtime --override-input yazelixYaziAssets ../yazelix-yazi-assets --no-link`
   - `nix build .#runtime --override-input yazelixScreen ../yazelix-screen --no-link`
   - `nix build .#runtime --override-input yazelixGhosttyCursors ../yazelix-ghostty-cursors --no-link`
3. Run the focused main-repo verifier for the integration surface that changed, then do any necessary manual runtime smoke in a fresh Yazelix session before pushing non-trivial behavior.
4. After the child commit is pushed, update the main `flake.lock` to the pushed child revision and re-run the same main-repo package or validator gate without local path overrides.

Transitional copied-wasm workflow:
- `yazelix-zellij-pane-orchestrator` currently uses `yzx dev build_pane_orchestrator --sync` plus `yzx_repo_validator validate-pane-orchestrator-sync`.
- `yazelix-zellij-popup` already has a package artifact, but the main repo still uses `yzx dev sync_yzpp_wasm` plus `yzx_repo_validator validate-yzpp-sync`.
- These commands are proof only for the copied-artifact transition. They should not become the long-term provenance model.

Rejected or limited strategies:
- Do not commit local path inputs or local-only `flake.lock` state. They make reviewer and CI behavior differ from the maintainer machine.
- Do not rely on `cargo check`, `cargo test`, or a child package build alone as proof of Yazelix runtime behavior. Main-repo generated configs and runtime placement need their own smoke or validator.
- Do not add a `yzx dev` wrapper yet. The useful primitive is clear enough: child package/check first, main `nix build .#runtime --override-input ...`, then focused validator/manual runtime smoke. A wrapper is worth adding only after the pane-orchestrator and yzpp package migrations stabilize the command set.
acceptance_criteria: Produce an evaluation-only recommendation that compares at least two local integration strategies; identifies which child repos need local override support; specifies the exact commands a maintainer would run; defines what counts as enough proof for generated Zellij/bar/plugin runtime behavior; calls out failure modes such as stale flake.lock, local-only path leakage, and unsynced wasm; does not implement the workflow.
notes:
Child repos and local override support:
- Already lockable package inputs in the main flake: `yazelix-screen`, `yazelix-ghostty-cursors`, `yazelix-zellij-bar`, `yazelix-yazi-assets`.
- Needs package input migration: `yazelix-zellij-pane-orchestrator`, tracked by follow-ups `yazelix-sdgz` and `yazelix-hion`.
- Already package-capable but still consumed by a copied-wasm sync path in this repo: `yazelix-zellij-popup`.

Enough proof by surface:
- Runtime package integration: `nix build .#runtime --override-input <input> ../<child> --no-link`, followed after child publication by the same build without the override against the committed lock.
- Zellij bar integration: child package build, main runtime build with `--override-input yazelixZellijBar`, and focused workspace/session or zellij materialization validation such as `yzx_repo_validator validate-workspace-session-contract` or the relevant `cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core zellij_materialization` filter.
- Yazi assets integration: child package/check, main runtime build with `--override-input yazelixYaziAssets`, and focused materialization/asset validation for the changed path.
- First-party Zellij plugin wasm while copied artifacts remain: sync command plus sync validator, then a fresh Yazelix/Zellij session for live plugin behavior when behavior changed.
- Future packaged first-party Zellij plugin wasm: child package build, main runtime build with `--override-input yazelixZellijPaneOrchestrator ../yazelix-zellij-pane-orchestrator` or the popup equivalent once the input exists, workspace/session validation, and fresh-session manual smoke for live behavior.

Failure modes to watch:
- Stale `flake.lock` after pushing a child commit.
- Accidental committed path input or local-only lock state.
- Dirty child checkout hidden behind a local override.
- Copied wasm or sync stamp drift for pane-orchestrator or yzpp.
- Package artifact path drift, especially `share/.../*.wasm` paths consumed by the runtime builder.
- Runtime sessions using old generated state after an artifact change; use a fresh session or `yzx restart` for behavior checks.
- Binary cache hits or child build success masking a main-repo integration break.
close_reason: Completed: recorded the local child-repo integration recommendation. Default workflow is child package/check, main runtime build with explicit --override-input against the adjacent checkout, focused integration validation, manual fresh-session smoke when runtime behavior changes, then lock update to the pushed child commit. Copied-wasm sync commands remain transitional for pane-orchestrator and yzpp only.

title: Split yzx status and doctor into machine-readable core plus Nushell rendering
notes:
Created from docs/specs/v16_rust_cli_rewrite_evaluation.md as the first likely prerequisite mixed-family narrowing task before any broader Rust public CLI move.

Intent:
- split concise machine-readable status/findings from user-facing prose rendering
- make `yzx status` and `yzx doctor` less mixed so a future Rust public owner can delete a real Nushell family instead of wrapping the same UX tree
- keep current doctor text and remediation quality unless a narrower Rust renderer contract is explicitly approved

Likely owners to inspect first:
- nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu
- nushell/scripts/utils/doctor.nu
- nushell/scripts/utils/runtime_contract_checker.nu
- rust_core/yazelix_core runtime-contract surfaces
2026-04-19 implementation direction:
- no new Rust helper in this bead: after reading status/doctor, the remaining logic is mostly host probes, config reconciliation, install-artifact checks, plugin session checks, and user-facing remediation assembly
- adding a Rust helper here would mostly wrap the same Nushell check owners instead of deleting a real decision layer
- implementation seam for this bead: build structured status and doctor reports first, keep human rendering in Nushell, and expose JSON output on `yzx status` and `yzx doctor`
- this is the prerequisite narrowing step for any later Rust public-CLI move, not a new v15 helper-port slice
2026-04-19 implementation completion:
- extracted structured status collection and rendering into nushell/scripts/utils/status_report.nu
- status report now drives both the default table rendering and `yzx status --json`
- version info now has collect/render split in nushell/scripts/utils/version_info.nu
- doctor now has collect/render/apply stages in nushell/scripts/utils/doctor.nu
- `yzx doctor --json` emits the collected report and summary counts; `yzx doctor --json --fix` is rejected as an explicit read-only boundary
- added docs/specs/status_doctor_machine_readable_reports.md and registered it in docs/specs/spec_inventory.md
- validation: targeted status tests passed, full doctor canonical suite passed, validate_specs passed, git diff --check passed
close_reason: Completed: status and doctor now collect structured reports first, keep Nushell rendering separate, expose JSON inspection mode, and document the contract in docs/specs/status_doctor_machine_readable_reports.md

title: Decide yazelix_screen dependency boundary for animation engines
description: Record the dependency boundary for future yzx screen animation work: keep shipped terminal runtime animations in the in-house yazelix_screen crate instead of adopting broad game engines or a terminal UI framework prematurely.
design: Runtime yzx screen animations should stay in-house with simple data models: Vec<Boid> for boids, grids/bitsets for Game of Life, and CPU-generated frame producers for Mandelbrot. Add a tiny in-house spatial hash only if boid counts grow enough to require it. Add Ratatui only if yzx screen becomes an interactive app with controls, inspectors, panels, or widgets. Consider Bevy/Fyrox only for separate desktop visualizers, demo recorders, social-preview/gif generators, or other non-runtime tooling; do not put them in the shipped terminal runtime.
acceptance_criteria: The architecture decision is recorded in Beads; future boids, Game of Life, and Mandelbrot work can cite this bead for dependency decisions; any future Ratatui, Bevy, or Fyrox proposal must state which gate it satisfies and why the in-house renderer is insufficient.
close_reason: Decision recorded: keep shipped yzx screen animations in-house; gate Ratatui, Bevy, Fyrox, and spatial hashing behind explicit future needs.

title: Make Yazelix config migrations land atomically across managed surfaces
description: Imported GitHub issue #520.
design: Treat this as config-transition atomicity, not merely filesystem atomicity. The goal is to make migrations across Yazelix-managed config surfaces land as one coherent state transition: stage the migrated result, validate it before commit, update related managed config files coherently, and preserve explicit backup/recovery paths. Scope this to Yazelix-owned managed paths such as user_configs and generated state that depends on migrated config, while preserving the existing boundary against taking ownership of external user config.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Yazelix has a clearer staged migration model across managed config surfaces. 2. A migration either preserves the previous valid managed state or lands the new valid managed state coherently, rather than leaving partial cross-surface updates behind. 3. yzx config migrate and startup migration preflight follow the same staged transition model. 4. Backups and failure recovery remain explicit and understandable.
notes: Planning refinement: this work is broad enough to deserve explicit child beads for (1) the staged migration contract, (2) staged apply semantics in yzx config migrate, and (3) reuse of that staged transition model in startup or entrypoint preflight. The goal is to keep the architecture coherent without collapsing design and implementation into one vague task.
close_reason: Completed: child beads 53zx.1, 53zx.2, and 53zx.3 landed the staged transaction contract, staged apply semantics, and entrypoint preflight reuse.

title: Define the staged migration transaction contract for managed config surfaces
description: Specify the staged transaction model for Yazelix-managed config migrations: which managed surfaces participate, where staged output lives, what gets validated before commit, what backup or rollback artifacts are created, and which failures must leave the previous valid state untouched.
design: Keep this contract-focused. Define the exact managed surfaces, staging boundary, validation boundary, backup semantics, and generated-state handling that together make a migration coherent instead of partially applied. Preserve the boundary against touching external non-Yazelix config.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The repo has an explicit staged migration transaction contract for managed config surfaces. 2. The contract names the participating surfaces, staging location or mechanism, validation-before-commit rules, and backup or rollback behavior. 3. The contract is specific enough that both yzx config migrate and startup preflight can follow the same model later without reinterpreting it differently.
close_reason: Completed: added the staged managed-config migration transaction contract and aligned the migration engine spec with it.

title: Implement staged apply semantics in yzx config migrate
description: Replace the direct-write backup/copy-back path in `apply_config_migration_plan` with a narrow managed-config transaction helper that stages rewritten TOML payloads and a JSON manifest under `user_configs`, validates the staged main/pack set, commits via same-directory rename, and rolls back cleanly on failure or stale journal recovery. Even though this bead is framed around `yzx config migrate --apply`, the helper is already shared by doctor and entrypoint call sites, so the implementation must preserve those callers while keeping the explicit migrate path as the main proving UX.
design:
Delete-first:
- do not invent a generic file-transaction framework
- do not broaden the surface beyond the canonical managed config pair

Implementation seam:
- extract a small helper, likely under `nushell/scripts/utils/`, that owns `prepare -> validate -> commit -> rollback` for the canonical managed surfaces
- have `apply_config_migration_plan` delegate to it instead of writing final targets directly

First implementation should:
- stage rewritten TOML payloads in the same `user_configs` filesystem as their final targets
- persist a hidden JSON manifest with `transaction_id`, `schema_version`, `caller`, `phase`, target, staged, backup, and `existed_before` metadata
- validate the staged final pair with the same config-surface loading rules before any canonical target swap
- preserve explicit backup files as the rollback source of truth
- clean up staged files and manifest on success
- on a subsequent explicit migrate apply, detect any stale manifest and roll back conservatively before starting a new transaction

Keep out of scope for this bead:
- startup/entrypoint recovery wiring beyond what already shares `apply_config_migration_plan`
- generic transaction support for runtime state, desktop entries, shell hooks, or external configs
- forward-completion heuristics for interrupted transactions
acceptance_criteria:
1. `apply_config_migration_plan` no longer writes canonical targets directly with `save --force` followed by copy-back rollback.
2. Main-only and main-plus-pack migrations are staged, validated, and committed coherently through the managed-config transaction helper.
3. Safe migrations that create a new `yazelix_packs.toml` sidecar record that the file did not exist before and remove it again on rollback.
4. `yzx config migrate --apply` detects and rolls back an unfinished managed-config transaction before starting a new apply attempt.
5. Successful apply leaves backups in place but removes staged files and manifest artifacts.
6. High-signal regression coverage exists for at least one interrupted or failing apply path, and the existing migrate/doctor fix behaviors continue to pass.
close_reason: Completed: staged yzx config migrate apply through a managed-config transaction helper with stale-journal recovery and regression coverage.

title: Finish reusing the staged migration transaction model in entrypoint preflight and legacy-root relocation
description: 53zx.2 moved deterministic rewrites onto the shared staged transaction engine, but the startup and entrypoint family still has one important ad hoc path left: legacy root-level config relocation is still performed before the transaction boundary. This bead should finish that integration so entrypoint migration preflight uses one coherent prepare, recover, and apply model for both deterministic rewrites and any managed-surface relocation it owns, instead of mixing staged writes with separate pre-transaction moves.
design:
Keep this bead narrow and entrypoint-focused. Start from nushell/scripts/utils/entrypoint_config_migrations.nu, nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix.nu, nushell/scripts/core/launch_yazelix.nu, nushell/scripts/yzx/launch.nu, and nushell/scripts/yzx/env.nu.

Delete-first boundary:
- do not build a second transaction engine for startup
- do not reopen yzx config migrate apply or doctor semantics beyond shared helper reuse
- do not broaden into generic config-surface ownership work already covered elsewhere

The real remaining seam is that build_preflight_context currently performs legacy-root relocation through reconcile_primary_config_surfaces before the staged transaction lifecycle begins. That means entrypoint preflight already reuses staged apply for deterministic TOML rewrites, but not yet for the full managed-surface transition it is responsible for.

The implementation should either:
1. move legacy-root relocation under the same staged prepare, recover, and apply boundary as entrypoint migration preflight, or
2. introduce one small shared helper that truthfully wraps relocation plus staged apply as one managed-surface transition contract for entrypoints.

Keep the caller distinction explicit:
- entrypoint_preflight may commit safe deterministic changes and relocation
- it may still block launch afterward if manual-only items remain
- interrupted transaction recovery should remain conservative and rollback-first

High-signal code seams to review:
- nushell/scripts/utils/entrypoint_config_migrations.nu
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_migration_transactions.nu
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_migrations.nu
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_surfaces.nu
- existing entrypoint regressions in nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_core_commands.nu
acceptance_criteria:
1. Entry-point migration preflight no longer relies on an ad hoc legacy-root relocation step outside the shared managed-config transition model.
2. Safe deterministic rewrites and managed legacy-root relocation for the entrypoint family use one coherent prepare, recover, and apply lifecycle, or one clearly shared wrapper around that lifecycle.
3. Startup and entrypoint code does not maintain a second incompatible migration-commit model beside yzx config migrate apply.
4. Entry-point behavior remains explicit: safe deterministic changes may commit, but remaining manual-only items still block continuation afterward.
5. There is at least one regression that defends the remaining seam this bead closes, not just the already-covered happy path.
notes: Current state after 53zx.2: entrypoint preflight already reuses staged apply for deterministic rewrites and already recovers interrupted staged transactions. The main remaining mismatch with the transaction spec is legacy-root relocation happening early in build_preflight_context via reconcile_primary_config_surfaces, before the staged transaction boundary. Existing tests already cover auto-apply, manual-follow-up blocking, and basic relocation; this bead should focus on the still-unified transition boundary rather than re-testing generic migration behavior.
close_reason: Implemented entrypoint relocation through the shared managed-config transaction lifecycle and added regressions for stale recovery ordering and pack-only relocation reporting.

title: Add optional yazelix_packs.toml sidecar config
description: Add an optional ~/.config/yazelix/yazelix_packs.toml sidecar so dense pack configuration can move out of yazelix.toml without removing any existing pack capability or turning packs into a folder forest.
design: Treat this as a configuration-clarity feature, not a pack redesign. Keep yazelix.toml as the front door and add yazelix_packs.toml as an optional structured sidecar for denser pack enablement and pack-specific settings. Preserve all current pack features; the sidecar must not remove any capability currently expressible in yazelix.toml. Define one sharp ownership rule so users are never left wondering which file wins: either the sidecar owns dense pack config while yazelix.toml keeps only simple top-level references, or Yazelix fails fast when the same pack keys are declared in both places. Prefer explicit duplicate-source errors over silent precedence surprises. Keep this limited to packs; do not turn it into a generic many-file config explosion. Because packs are supported through both direct config and Home Manager today, the sidecar design must preserve a coherent story for both paths.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix supports an optional yazelix_packs.toml without regressing existing pack behavior from yazelix.toml. The final design clearly documents what pack data belongs in each file, how duplicate or conflicting definitions are handled, and how the feature works for both direct config users and Home Manager users. Add focused unit tests for sidecar discovery, parse or merge behavior, duplicate-source errors, and pack-resolution behavior. Add end-to-end tests with detailed logs for main-file-only pack config, sidecar-only pack config, a conflicting dual-definition case that fails clearly, a direct-config path, a Home Manager path, and a representative generated-config run showing the resolved pack set and emitted tooling or config output.
close_reason: Completed

title: Define Yazelix Nushell tooling strategy
description: Umbrella for deciding how Yazelix should package and govern Nushell-specific maintainer tooling. Scope includes a dedicated Nushell pack, nufmt formatting policy, and nu-lint lint policy. Keep tool availability separate from enforcement: shipping tools in an opt-in pack must not by itself decide canonical formatter status, maintainer workflows, or CI gates.
design: Reason from first principles: Yazelix needs a coherent Nushell-tooling story, not an ad hoc accumulation of commands. There are three separate questions that should not be collapsed into one: 1. what Nushell-specific tools belong in a first-party opt-in pack; 2. whether nufmt becomes the canonical formatter and what rollout it should have; and 3. whether nu-lint is repo-compatible enough to justify any maintained command surface. The parent bead should stay an umbrella for those planning decisions rather than directly implementing a single tool addition.
acceptance_criteria: The subtree under this bead cleanly separates pack composition, formatting policy, and lint policy; each child bead has an explicit boundary and success criteria; and maintainers can make progress on one area without accidentally forcing the others. The parent can close once the dedicated pack direction and the nufmt and nu-lint policy decisions are each resolved.
close_reason: Completed: all three strategy decisions resolved — Nushell pack shipped, nufmt rejected, nu-lint adopted as maintainer command.

title: Add a dedicated Nushell tooling pack
description: Create a dedicated Nushell pack instead of overloading the generic maintainer pack. Initial scope should include nufmt and should consider nu-lint based on the outcome of the repo-fit evaluation. The pack bead is about package availability and ergonomics, not about forcing formatter or linter enforcement by itself.
design: Keep pack composition separate from policy enforcement. A first-party opt-in Nushell pack is valuable even if nu-lint remains experimental or nufmt adoption is staged. The pack should stay small and purposeful: prefer clear Nushell authoring and hygiene tools over a grab-bag of loosely related utilities. Any change here must preserve the normal Yazelix config model by keeping the pack available through both the pack sidecar/default config path and the Home Manager module, without creating a second-class configuration path.
acceptance_criteria: A dedicated Nushell pack exists in both Yazelix config surfaces, with justified initial contents and no ambiguity about what the pack is for. The pack can be enabled through direct config and Home Manager. Its contents do not silently imply CI enforcement or canonical formatter/linter status beyond what the separate policy beads decide.
close_reason: Completed

title: Evaluate nufmt and define Yazelix Nushell formatting policy
description: Decide whether nufmt should be the canonical formatter for Yazelix Nushell code, define the formatting policy and command surface, and determine how adoption should happen: repo-wide reformat, touched-files-only rollout, or rejection. This bead owns formatter policy, migration story, and enforcement decisions, not just tool installation.
design: The important question is not merely whether nufmt exists, but whether Yazelix wants one authoritative Nushell formatting policy. Resolve formatter ambiguity explicitly: if nufmt is adopted, decide whether it becomes the canonical formatter, where it is invoked from, and whether maintainers are expected to reformat the whole repo or only touched files. The rollout should preserve review quality and avoid accidental churn. If nufmt is rejected or deferred, capture the reasons so the repo does not drift into an undocumented quasi-policy.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit maintainers-facing decision on nufmt: adopt, defer, or reject. If adopted, the bead records the canonical invocation path, the enforcement lane if any, and the migration strategy for existing files. If deferred or rejected, the reasons and blocking concerns are captured clearly enough to prevent accidental partial adoption.
notes: Decision: do not adopt nufmt for Yazelix right now because the current formatter output was observed to rewrite at least one real Yazelix Nushell file into invalid syntax.
close_reason: Closed: current nufmt repo-fit evaluation found unsafe formatter output on real Yazelix Nushell files, so Yazelix is not adopting a Nushell formatting policy around nufmt right now

title: Evaluate nu-lint and define Yazelix Nushell lint policy
description: Evaluate nu-lint against the Yazelix codebase, identify incompatible or noisy rules, and decide what role it deserves: pack-only availability, editor/LSP assistance, explicit maintainer command, pre-commit lane, CI lane, or rejection. This bead owns repo-fit evaluation and lint policy, not just checking that the tool exists.
design: Judge nu-lint by repo fit, but optimize for extracting real value rather than defaulting to a timid pack-only posture. Current evidence says the upstream default rule set is too noisy for blind enforcement: on real Yazelix files it reports parse errors for normal relative `use` imports, and several prominent rules conflict with repo conventions or priorities, especially `kebab_case_commands`, `add_doc_comment_exported_fn`, `missing_output_type`, and broad style rules like `string_may_be_bare`. Pack inclusion is already solved; this bead should now determine the highest-value maintained role nu-lint can play in Yazelix. That may be editor/LSP use, an explicit maintainer command, a tuned pre-commit lane, or a narrower CI lane, but only with a repo-specific config that disables or scopes noisy rules. Do not assume upstream defaults are acceptable, but do aggressively explore whether a constrained rule set yields strong signal.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit decision on nu-lints role in Yazelix that aims for maximum useful signal, not minimum ambition. The bead records the concrete repo-fit blockers already found: relative-import parse noise, rule conflicts with underscore-oriented naming, and high-churn style/documentation warnings. If adoption is chosen beyond pack availability, the bead defines the exact lane, the repo-specific config or disabled-rule policy, and why that scoped rule set is worth the maintenance cost. If broader adoption is rejected, the bead explains why no high-value narrowed configuration was good enough.
notes: Evaluation complete. Raw nu-lint produces 8565 violations (dominated by noise: string_may_be_bare 2466, kebab_case_commands 845, missing_output_type 784, nu_parse_error 433 from relative imports). With a curated .nu-lint.toml disabling 10 groups and ~10 noisy individual rules, output drops to ~170 findings of genuine signal: unsafe_dynamic_record_access (49), do_not_compare_booleans (23), unused_parameter (19), check_typed_flag_before_use (18), unchecked_get_index (11), non_final_failure_check (11), unused_helper_functions (10), unused_variable (9), assign_then_return (6), fragile_last_exit_code (4), unchecked_cell_path_index (2). Decision: adopt nu-lint as an explicit maintainer command (yzx dev lint_nu) with a repo-specific .nu-lint.toml. Not CI-gated because relative import parse errors are a structural upstream false-positive blocker. Not pre-commit because maintainer-triggered is the right ergonomic fit. redundant_nu_subprocess (39) disabled because all hits are intentional process-isolation patterns in test harnesses, menu dispatch, and script runners. Deliverables: .nu-lint.toml at repo root, yzx dev lint_nu command.
close_reason: Completed: nu-lint adopted as maintainer command (yzx dev lint_nu) with repo-specific .nu-lint.toml. Policy decision documented.

title: Honor Zellij keybinds clear-defaults user-owned mode
description: Support advanced users who want the same native Zellij keybindings inside and outside Yazelix. Today the Zellij materializer extracts the user keybinds block body but drops the keybinds clear-defaults=true header and still appends Yazelix override keybinds, so clear-defaults cannot express full user ownership.
acceptance_criteria: When the managed Zellij config declares keybinds clear-defaults=true, the generated Yazelix config preserves keybinds clear-defaults=true and does not append Yazelix default override keybinds. Normal keybinds without clear-defaults keep the current merge behavior. Tests cover preserving the clear-defaults header, skipping Yazelix override injection, keeping ordinary merge behavior, and documenting that users must recreate any desired Yazelix integration bindings themselves in this mode.
close_reason: Implemented: generated Zellij config now preserves keybinds clear-defaults=true and skips Yazelix override keybind injection in full user-owned mode; ordinary keybind merges still append Yazelix overrides. Documented user responsibility for recreating desired integration bindings. Verified with yzx dev rust fmt --check, yzx dev rust test zellij_materialization, and yzx dev rust check.

title: Popup toggle-close still does not refresh Yazi git sidebar
description: The existing lazygit popup toggle-close fix was synced into the runtime, but in a fresh Yazelix window Alt+t still closes the managed popup without refreshing the Yazi git sidebar. Investigate whether the synced runtime is stale or the current background refresh implementation is not actually executing with the required Yazelix/Zellij context.
close_reason: Fixed: popup toggle-close refresh now works. The implementation was patched to refresh directly on popup close, and the real launch blocker was stale owner-runtime activation: fresh launches were rebuilding ~/.local/share/yazelix from an older profile-owned package until the local repo package was activated and rematerialized.

title: Scrub Ghostty GUI loader variables from workspace child shells
description:
Fix leaked Nix/Ghostty GUI loader environment variables that make host GUI apps such as Snap VS Code load incompatible Nix GIO/dconf modules when launched from inside Yazelix

## Context
Running `code .` from a Yazelix shell failed with `/nix/store/...-dconf.../lib/gio/modules/libdconfsettings.so: undefined symbol: g_assertion_message_cmpint`
The live environment had `GIO_EXTRA_MODULES` pointing at that Nix dconf module, plus Nix GUI `XDG_DATA_DIRS` and `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` entries
Ghostty itself may need those variables at launch time, but workspace child shells should not pass GUI loader variables to unrelated host GUI apps
design:
Separate terminal-emulator launch environment from workspace child environment
Scrub high-risk GUI loader variables before starting Zellij/shell processes inside the terminal, not before launching Ghostty itself
Keep Yazelix runtime variables and PATH intact
Prefer a small, explicit denylist for variables known to affect host GUI module loading
acceptance_criteria:
Yazelix startup/workspace children unset GIO_EXTRA_MODULES and related GUI loader variables inherited from the terminal launch wrapper
Ghostty launch still gets the environment it needs before child startup
A behavior-backed test covers the scrub helper or startup path
Document the decision in the bead and commit the fix
notes: Rust dependency decision: no new production or dev crates. Shell logic stays in-house/POSIX because the bug is inherited process environment hygiene. Rust is used only for an integration test that executes the checked-in startup shell path. Packaging impact: none; existing runtime shell files are already shipped.
close_reason: Completed: workspace startup now scrubs inherited GTK/GIO loader variables before handing off to yzx_control/Zellij children, with a regression test for the start_yazelix boundary.

title: Mirror grouped usage periods for Claude
description: Make claude_usage follow the same grouped period contract as codex_usage and opencode_usage. Add claude_usage_periods, render day/month/last with compact units, and remove the active-block meaning from the public claude_usage widget.
design: Only grouped provider usage widgets are public. Change claude_usage from active-block output to the same grouped day/month/last renderer used by Codex/OpenCode, backed by tokenusage commands that exclude Codex and Antigravity. Add zellij.claude_usage_periods to the config contract and Home Manager surface.
acceptance_criteria: claude_usage accepts configured periods from claude_usage_periods and renders one grouped segment. No active-block public meaning remains for claude_usage. Config/docs/defaults/Home Manager mention Claude periods alongside Codex/OpenCode. Tests cover grouped Claude rendering and provider refresh.
close_reason: Completed: claude_usage now mirrors grouped day/month/last usage periods, config/docs/Home Manager expose zellij.claude_usage_periods, and focused usage/config validation passes.

title: Bring Rio-based yazelix-terminal to Ghostty parity
description: Turn Rio into a first-party Rust terminal path for Yazelix, tentatively named yazelix-terminal, with Ghostty-grade cursor shaders, modern protocol coverage, and a Yazelix mode where Zellij remains the only in-session workspace owner.
design: Scope this as a cross-repo product epic, not a main-repo-only feature. The durable target is a Rio-derived yazelix-terminal that can be packaged and selected by Yazelix while preserving Ghostty and WezTerm as comparison oracles. Work should proceed through gates: define the Ghostty parity contract, create the fork/repo and source-absorption guardrails, prove cursor shader feasibility, implement Yazelix mode, build a conformance harness, close must protocol gaps, then package and document the experimental runtime. Keep direct Kitty source copying out of scope unless a separate GPL licensing decision explicitly approves it.
acceptance_criteria: The epic is complete when: 1. A yazelix-terminal Rio fork or equivalent owned repo exists with lineage, license, and upstream-sync policy documented. 2. A Ghostty parity contract defines must, should, and frontier capabilities. 3. Ghostty-compatible cursor shaders, including existing Yazelix cursor effects, run in yazelix-terminal with cursor-aware uniforms and animation semantics. 4. Yazelix mode launches one child command and does not depend on terminal-native tabs, splits, panes, sessions, or mux ownership. 5. A conformance matrix compares yazelix-terminal against Ghostty, WezTerm, and Kitty specs for graphics, keyboard, shell semantics, clipboard, notifications, parser robustness, and terminal identity. 6. Missing must protocols are implemented or explicitly rejected with a documented reason. 7. Frontier protocols are implemented or tracked behind clear should/cool gates. 8. Parser/fuzz, performance, and graphics-stability evidence show the runtime is credible against Ghostty. 9. Yazelix can package, launch, diagnose, and document the experimental yazelix-terminal runtime without making it the default before manual approval.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: 2026-06-02 audit: protocol, shader, packaging, conformance, parser, performance, frontier-decision, and integration child beads were closed. Epic remains open on the release-quality decision path: stack validation matrix, native/text/IME UX audit, and final release gate.

title: Define Ghostty parity contract for yazelix-terminal
description: Define the exact Ghostty parity target that Rio-based yazelix-terminal must meet before implementation work spreads across renderer, protocol, packaging, and docs.
design: Create a durable contract under docs/contracts/ if this becomes supported behavior. Separate must-have parity from should-have polish and frontier experiments. Must include cursor shader semantics, protocol matrix, source-absorption rules, terminal identity, security policy, Zellij/Yazi/Helix validation, and explicit non-goals around terminal-native multiplexing in Yazelix mode.
acceptance_criteria: The contract names the Ghostty features and local source paths used as references, classifies requirements as must/should/frontier, identifies acceptance tests or manual validation for each must, documents license/source-absorption rules for Ghostty, WezTerm, and Kitty, and states the pivot condition for abandoning Rio in favor of Ghostty/libghostty or a WezTerm-engine path.
close_reason: Completed in the child repo via docs/yazelix/ghostty_parity_contract.md, including must/should/frontier tiers, source references, validation expectations, license rules, and pivot criteria.

title: Implement Kitty OSC 66 text sizing in yazelix-terminal
description: Add Kitty text-sizing protocol support so yazelix-terminal can match Ghostty on this newer Kitty-era capability.
design: Use the official Kitty OSC 66 spec and Ghostty's parser/rendering behavior as references. Decide how Rio's text layout and renderer should represent scaled text while preserving terminal grid semantics, selection/copy behavior, clipping, and resize behavior.
acceptance_criteria: OSC 66 parses valid payloads, rejects malformed payloads with clear behavior, renders scaled text correctly enough for terminal UI use, preserves grid/selection invariants, includes tests/fixtures from the conformance harness, and documents unsupported edge cases.
close_reason: Completed: Kitty OSC 66 parsing, visual scaled/fractional rendering, multicell editing semantics, cursor visual extents, tests, and conformance fixture are recorded in child fork_feature_verification.md.

title: Implement OSC 133 semantic prompts and command zones in yazelix-terminal
description: Bring Rio-based yazelix-terminal up to Ghostty/WezTerm-style shell semantic prompt support for command boundaries and prompt/output awareness.
design: Use Ghostty and WezTerm as behavior references for OSC 133, and include OSC 7 working-directory reporting in the same shell-semantics pass if Rio lacks the right behavior. The feature should support future search, selection, command navigation, and agent state extraction without inventing Yazelix-only shell markers.
acceptance_criteria: OSC 133 prompt start/end/input/output/command status forms are parsed and reflected in terminal state, OSC 7 cwd reporting behavior is verified, command-zone selection/navigation behavior is either implemented or intentionally deferred, and tests cover valid, invalid, and escaped command-line payload cases.
close_reason: Completed: OSC 133 semantic zones and prompt/output navigation were implemented with production parser dispatch, row metadata, tests, and conformance fixture evidence in child fork_feature_verification.md.

title: Harden clipboard and notification protocols in yazelix-terminal
description: Define and implement a coherent security and UX policy for OSC 52, Kitty clipboard-style behavior, OSC 9/777 notifications, and Kitty OSC 99 notifications if adopted.
design: Ghostty already has useful policy surfaces around clipboard access and desktop notifications. Yazelix-terminal should avoid blindly trusting terminal output, support explicit config, give visible errors or prompts where appropriate, and keep notification UX restrained enough for an agent-heavy workspace.
acceptance_criteria: OSC 52 read/write behavior is gated by documented policy, Kitty clipboard behavior is implemented or explicitly rejected, OSC 9 and OSC 777 behavior is compatible with existing apps, OSC 99 is either implemented or tracked as a frontier protocol with rationale, and tests cover allowed, denied, malformed, and oversized payloads.
close_reason: Completed: OSC 52 policy hardening, OSC 99 notification parser/lifecycle/OS callbacks, inherited OSC 9/777 behavior, and OSC 5522 text-compatible clipboard decisions are implemented or explicitly bounded in child docs and tests.

title: Implement terminal identity capability and terminfo parity for yazelix-terminal
description: Make yazelix-terminal report capabilities honestly so applications know which modern protocols are actually available.
design: Cover DA, secondary DA, XTVERSION, XTGETTCAP, terminal name, terminfo, environment variables, and any Yazelix-specific identity. Bias toward truthful capability reporting over pretending to be Ghostty, xterm, Rio, or WezTerm when behavior differs.
acceptance_criteria: Capability responses match the implemented feature set, terminfo/environment story is documented, common apps receive enough information to enable graphics/keyboard/clipboard features correctly, false capability claims are removed, and conformance tests cover representative queries.
close_reason: Completed: the child repo uses conservative Rio identity with YAZELIX_TERMINAL_HOST for fork detection, scrubs inherited terminal identity/terminfo state in Yazelix mode, documents the terminfo story, and covers XTVERSION/XTGETTCAP fixtures.

title: Audit and close text rendering IME selection and native UX gaps
description: Close the non-protocol parts of Ghostty parity that users feel immediately: font quality, shaping, selection, copy/search, IME, high-DPI behavior, and native window polish.
design: Compare yazelix-terminal/Rio against Ghostty for ligatures, grapheme clusters, emoji/fallback fonts, bidi/RTL where feasible, IME, selection/copy/search, hyperlinks, high-DPI scaling, transparency/backgrounds, and platform-specific window behavior. Do not let protocol work mask poor daily terminal UX.
acceptance_criteria: A UX parity matrix exists with Ghostty and WezTerm baselines, major text/IME/selection gaps have implementation beads or fixes, high-DPI and resize behavior are validated, and any intentionally unsupported native-platform features are documented before experimental release.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: 2026-06-02 audit: no completed UX parity matrix found for font quality, shaping, selection/copy/search, IME, high-DPI, and native window behavior against Ghostty/WezTerm. Child repo has specific font/emoji and rendering fixes, but this broader UX gate remains open.

title: Evaluate Kitty multiple cursors for yazelix-terminal
description: Decide whether and when yazelix-terminal should implement the Kitty multiple-cursors protocol, based on real Helix/editor value in the Yazelix stack.
design: Use the official Kitty protocol specification and black-box Kitty behavior. Check whether Helix, Zellij, or Yazelix integrations can emit or benefit from real terminal-rendered secondary cursors. If no producer exists yet, record a clear deferral and create a later implementation bead only when a real workflow can use it.
acceptance_criteria: The decision either creates a concrete implementation bead for Kitty multiple cursors with parser, renderer, clearing, resize, scrollback, and selection requirements, or records a concrete deferral because no Yazelix workflow can use it yet. The decision must not copy Kitty GPL implementation code and unsupported behavior must not be advertised through capability reporting.
close_reason: Completed: Kitty multiple cursors moved beyond decision into implementation, with parser/state/query/color/clear behavior, renderer/shader support, conformance fixture, tests, and documented 256-visible-cursor shader limit.

title: Evaluate frontier Kitty protocols for yazelix-terminal
description: Decide which remaining modern Kitty-era protocols belong in yazelix-terminal after the must-have terminal path is stable.
design: Evaluate OSC 72 drag-and-drop, Kitty file transfer over TTY, arbitrary-region styling, and other frontier protocols for real Yazelix value, security boundaries, and implementation cost. Create implementation beads only for protocols that should actually ship.
acceptance_criteria: Each frontier protocol has a decision, security policy notes, conformance-fixture requirements, and either a follow-up implementation bead or a documented deferral. Unsupported protocols are not advertised through capability reporting.
close_reason: Completed: docs/yazelix/frontier_kitty_protocols.md records implementation/deferral decisions, security policy boundaries, fixtures, and follow-up scope for multiple cursors, file transfer, drag/drop, color/control, and related frontier protocols.

title: Package and integrate experimental yazelix-terminal runtime
description: Add yazelix-terminal as an experimental Yazelix runtime target once the fork, Yazelix mode, and shader gates are credible.
design: Integrate through the main repo without making it the default. Wire flake input or package source, generated config, terminal selection, launcher behavior, preflight/doctor diagnostics, docs, and Home Manager/desktop behavior as appropriate. Coordinate with yazelix-t3nkn so upstream-Rio launch support and forked yazelix-terminal support do not create duplicate terminal surfaces. Keep Ghostty as the stable default until manual testing approves a change.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix can build or select the experimental yazelix-terminal runtime, generated config is deterministic, missing/unsupported runtime errors are clear, doctor/preflight surfaces identify graphics/protocol limitations, docs state support level, and integration tests cover launch/config/schema behavior where applicable.
notes: Integrated main-repo opt-in runtime_variant="yazelix_terminal" consuming pushed child commit a79e53aabbeac4813dafa0377490527723fbfb9e from the child repo default branch main. Public flake attrs are #yazelix_terminal and #runtime_yazelix_terminal. Launch maps config id yazelix_terminal to child wrapper yazelix-terminal-desktop, passes generated config dir with YAZELIX_TERMINAL_CONFIG, and leaves child graphics wrapping owned by yazelix-terminal. Rust materialization derives generated config from runtime/share/yazelix-terminal/config.toml and injects terminal.transparency into [window].opacity/opacity-cells. Child packaging bug found during main runtime build was fixed in yzt-ppl by including conformance/shaders/ghostty_cursor_probe.glsl in the child Nix package fileset. Branch promotion completed: luccahuguet/yazelix-terminal main now points at a79e53aabbeac4813dafa0377490527723fbfb9e and the old remote yazelix-terminal-experiment branch was deleted. Validation run locally: child nix build .#yazelix-terminal-unwrapped --no-link passed, cargo fmt --check, cargo check -p yazelix_core -p yazelix_maintainer, focused Rust tests for preflight, launch argv, materialization, cursor scoping, and native config path, git diff --check, nix eval for x86_64-linux package/runtime/app and x86_64-darwin package, nix build .#runtime_yazelix_terminal --no-link passed before and after branch promotion. Local result_yazelix_terminal_runtime symlink is built for manual launch testing. Manual launch still pending maintainer test before push.
close_reason: Completed: main Yazelix exposes the experimental yzxterm/#runtime_yzxterm packaged runtime and Home Manager runtime_variant path; remaining stack validation, UX release gate, and crash/perf work stay tracked separately.

title: Run yazelix-terminal release gate against Ghostty baseline
description: Decide whether the experimental Rio-based yazelix-terminal is good enough to advertise as a serious Yazelix terminal path.
design: This is the final gate, not implementation. It should run conformance, shader, Zellij/Yazi/Helix, packaging, performance, and UX checks; update compatibility docs; and decide default/experimental/support status. Manual maintainer testing is required before promoting it beyond experimental.
acceptance_criteria: Release evidence includes conformance results, shader parity results, parser/fuzz robustness evidence, performance and graphics-stability measurements, screenshots or recordings for cursor effects and image previews, known gaps versus Ghostty, docs/matrix updates, and an explicit decision: keep experimental, promote, defer, or pivot away from Rio.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: 2026-06-02 audit: most implementation/protocol/pre-release subtasks were closed from child repo evidence. Keep the release gate open until yazelix-5br5o.9 stack matrix, yazelix-5br5o.14 UX parity audit, current crash/high-rate-output risk, docs/matrix updates, and an explicit keep-experimental/promote/defer decision are complete.

title: Adopt Ghostty-style parser fuzz and robustness strategy for yazelix-terminal
description: Bring Ghostty-grade parser and escape-sequence robustness discipline into yazelix-terminal, using source study and fixtures rather than ad-hoc protocol parsing.
design: Study Ghostty's parser/fuzz assets and WezTerm's parser tests as references, then define a Rust/Rio-appropriate fuzz and fixture strategy for OSC, CSI, DCS, APC/Kitty graphics, clipboard, prompt marks, malformed UTF-8, oversized payloads, and partial sequences. Use Kitty specs and black-box behavior for Kitty-owned protocols; do not copy GPL implementation code.
