acceptance_criteria: A parser robustness plan exists with initial fixtures or fuzz targets, malformed/oversized/incomplete sequence coverage is named, CI/local-run cost is documented, and at least one high-value parser path is protected by tests or fuzz corpus before release. Any new Rust tests include required Test lane and Defends/Regression/Invariant markers.
close_reason: Completed: docs/yazelix/parser_robustness_strategy.md defines the fuzz/fixture strategy and the child repo includes deterministic parser-noise coverage plus protocol parser tests and conformance fixtures.

title: Create yazelix-terminal Rio fork with lineage and license guardrails
description: Create the owned Rio-derived repository or equivalent fork workspace that will become yazelix-terminal, with explicit upstream lineage and legal boundaries before feature porting begins.
design: Prefer a separate repo named yazelix-terminal so the product name survives if the implementation eventually diverges from Rio. Preserve Rio upstream attribution, record the exact base commit, add remotes/branch policy, and document how Ghostty/WezTerm/Kitty source can be studied or ported. Do not copy GPL Kitty implementation code into the fork without an explicit licensing decision.
acceptance_criteria: The repo exists with upstream Rio remote and base commit recorded, license and attribution files are correct, AI/source-absorption policy is documented, branch/update strategy is clear, and maintainers can build or at least run the first upstream-equivalent check from the fork workspace.
close_reason: Completed: /home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-terminal exists as the Rio-derived repo with rio-upstream remote, MIT lineage, base commit, source-use policy, and build/check evidence recorded in docs/yazelix/lineage_and_guardrails.md.

title: Benchmark yazelix-terminal performance and graphics stability against Ghostty
description: Measure whether the Rio-based yazelix-terminal remains credible against Ghostty for startup, frame pacing, shader cost, resource use, and graphics-stack stability.
design: Benchmark after the experimental runtime can launch. Compare against Ghostty and, where useful, WezTerm on representative Yazelix flows: startup into Zellij, cursor shader animation, Yazi image preview, resize/high-DPI behavior, long-running idle, and protocol-heavy terminal output. Prefer cheap repeatable scripts first and record any manual visual checks explicitly.
acceptance_criteria: Results record terminal versions/commits, host graphics environment, startup timing, memory/CPU/GPU observations where available, shader overhead, frame pacing or visual smoothness evidence, image-preview stability, and known graphics-driver caveats. Release status cannot be promoted if performance or stability is visibly worse than Ghostty without an explicit acceptance decision.
close_reason: Completed: docs/yazelix/performance_and_graphics_benchmark.md records local Ghostty/Rio versions, host graphics environment, startup/scroll/frame evidence, CPU/RSS/GPU observations, shader overhead, harness commands, and caveats.

title: Build source-absorption workflow for Ghostty WezTerm and Kitty
description: Define how AI agents should study Ghostty, WezTerm, and Kitty to absorb behavior into yazelix-terminal without blind copying or license drift.
design: Use narrow feature dossiers: source paths inspected, behavior summary, protocol or UX contract, tests/fixtures to add, implementation plan for Rust/Rio, and license/attribution notes. Ghostty and WezTerm can be studied and selectively ported subject to permissive-license attribution; Kitty should be treated primarily as official specs plus black-box behavior unless a GPL decision says otherwise.
acceptance_criteria: There is a reusable source-absorption template and at least one completed sample dossier for Ghostty custom shaders. The workflow requires behavior contracts and tests before implementation, preserves attribution, flags GPL risk, and gives agents a repeatable way to compare yazelix-terminal against Ghostty, WezTerm, and Kitty.
close_reason: Completed in docs/yazelix/source_absorption_workflow.md with the reusable workflow and docs/yazelix/dossiers/cursor_shader_parity.md as the completed Ghostty shader dossier.

title: Prove Rio renderer can host Ghostty cursor shader parity
description: Run the smallest renderer spike that answers whether Rio can realistically support Ghostty-compatible cursor shaders without an architectural rewrite.
design: Inspect and prototype against Rio's Sugarloaf/wgpu/librashader cursor/effects paths and Ghostty's ShaderToy-style custom shader model. The spike should prove cursor-aware uniforms, post-process timing, current/previous cursor state, palette/color inputs, and animation hooks can be represented cleanly. If not, this bead must trigger the pivot path before broad implementation work continues.
acceptance_criteria: The bead records a clear go/no-go decision for Rio shader parity, with source paths, prototype evidence, rejected approaches, crate-vs-in-house dependency decision, packaging impact, and a pivot recommendation if the renderer seam is too invasive.
close_reason: Completed: docs/yazelix/dossiers/cursor_shader_parity.md records the go decision, renderer seams, source paths, validation evidence, rejected/pivot criteria, dependency impact, and packaging notes.

title: Implement Ghostty-compatible custom shader runtime in yazelix-terminal
description: Add the custom shader runtime needed for yazelix-terminal to run Ghostty-class cursor effects rather than only Rio's current cursor trails.
design: Implement configuration, loading, compilation/translation strategy, render-pass integration, animation timing, error reporting, and safety limits. Aim for practical Ghostty compatibility where possible: custom shader entries, optional animation, ShaderToy-style post-process semantics, cursor-aware uniforms, palette/color uniforms, focus/time uniforms, and current/previous cursor state.
acceptance_criteria: Existing Ghostty-style shader examples can be loaded or rejected with explicit actionable errors, cursor/focus/time/palette uniforms are available to shaders, multiple shader passes either work or are intentionally scoped out in the contract, bad shaders fail visibly without crashing the terminal, and focused renderer tests or golden visual checks defend the behavior.
close_reason: Completed in the child repo: Ghostty-compatible WGPU custom-shader runtime, cursor/focus/time/palette uniforms, shader parse errors, and multi-pass render ordering are recorded in fork_feature_verification.md and the shader dossier.

title: Validate Yazelix Ghostty cursor shaders in yazelix-terminal
description: Use the existing Yazelix Ghostty cursor shader pack as the real acceptance test for Rio-based shader parity.
design: Do not stop at synthetic shader demos. Run the maintained Yazelix cursor effects, compare them against Ghostty behavior, and identify any compatibility shim or intentional divergence. This is the deal-breaker gate before calling Rio the right base.
acceptance_criteria: The maintained Yazelix/Ghostty cursor shader set runs in yazelix-terminal or each non-working effect has a concrete blocker. Validation captures visual evidence, cursor movement/mode-change behavior, terminal resize behavior, error messages, and a final pass/fail recommendation for keeping Rio as the base.
close_reason: Completed: Yazelix Ghostty cursor shader presets were validated through the child WGPU shader path with generated shader assets, Naga compatibility shim, event-mode animation, and screenshot evidence recorded in the shader dossier and verification ledger.

title: Implement Yazelix mode without terminal-native workspace ownership
description: Add a yazelix-terminal mode that launches Yazelix/Zellij as the workspace owner and hides or disables Rio terminal-native tabs, splits, panels, and session behaviors that would duplicate Zellij.
design: The terminal may still keep a standalone mode, but Yazelix mode should be one outer terminal window, one configured child command, deterministic config, visible launch errors, and no terminal-native pane/session model exposed as part of Yazelix. If code is removed or compiled out, preserve a clean ownership boundary rather than scattering conditionals.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix mode launches exactly the configured child command, does not expose terminal-native tabs/splits/panels as a Yazelix workflow dependency, fails fast when required config is missing, keeps standalone behavior explicit if retained, and includes tests or manual validation proving Zellij remains the sole in-session workspace owner.
close_reason: Completed: docs/yazelix/yazelix_mode.md and fork_feature_verification.md document --yazelix host mode, exact child command launch, disabled Rio workspace ownership, app-id/env behavior, and tests.

title: Establish yazelix-terminal conformance harness against Ghostty WezTerm and Kitty
description: Create the test and comparison harness that lets implementation work prove behavior against Ghostty, WezTerm, and official Kitty specs instead of relying on anecdotes.
design: Start with high-signal fixtures for escape/protocol parsing, renderer-visible behavior, and integration flows. Include OSC, CSI, APC/Kitty graphics, keyboard encodings, clipboard, prompt marks, notifications, and identity/capability reporting. Keep Kitty implementation code out of the harness unless licensing is explicitly resolved; specs and black-box behavior are allowed.
acceptance_criteria: The harness can run a documented subset locally, records terminal/version/source commits under test, produces readable pass/fail output, and has initial fixtures covering custom shaders, Kitty graphics, Kitty keyboard, OSC 52, OSC 66, OSC 133, OSC 8, synchronized output, and terminal identity.
close_reason: Completed: tools/yazelix_conformance.py, conformance/fixtures/manifest.json, conformance_harness.md, shader probes, keyboard capture helpers, and protocol fixtures cover the initial required harness scope.

title: Validate core graphics and input protocols through Zellij Yazi and Helix
description: Prove the protocols Yazelix already depends on work through the real Yazelix stack, not only in isolated terminal demos.
design: Validate Kitty graphics including placeholders and virtual placements, Yazi image previews, Sixel and iTerm2 image behavior, Kitty keyboard and CSI-u/enhanced keyboard behavior through Zellij, focus events, bracketed paste, SGR mouse, and synchronized output. Isolate whether failures belong to yazelix-terminal, Zellij, Yazi, Helix, Chafa, or current Yazelix config.
acceptance_criteria: A matrix records pass/fail behavior for yazelix-terminal against Ghostty and WezTerm baselines under the same Yazelix/Zellij/Yazi/Helix flows. Any failure has an owner and follow-up bead. Kitty graphics placement through framed Zellij panes is explicitly covered.
notes: 2026-05-30 upstream Rio risk evidence: raphamorim/rio#1530 is still open as of latest checked state. Last comment, by dl09r on 2026-05-10, says: yazi is still not displaying images or pdf files. Earlier comments report Rio 0.3.11/0.4.2/0.4.3 differences between Yazi adapters Iip and Kgp, no image output outside tmux in some cases, tiny/chopped preview inside tmux, and overlapping/chopped image behavior. Treat this as a must-cover regression source for yazelix-terminal validation: Yazi image and PDF previews must be tested outside Zellij, inside Zellij, and through any tmux-like passthrough comparison if useful. The validation must distinguish terminal-side Rio bugs from Zellij/Yazi/Chafa/Yazelix config bugs before Rio can be considered viable.

Keep this bead open because acceptance still asks for a same-flow Ghostty/WezTerm matrix and explicit framed-pane Kitty graphics placement coverage; dependency yazelix-mpaus still records manual graphics validation pending.

title: Shrink the remaining internal yzx family rule engine until a Clap decision is honest
notes:
This epic is the post-2p32 path to a future honest Clap decision.

Guardrail:
- Do not add Clap during this epic by default
- The goal is to shrink or simplify the surviving `INTERNAL_NU_FAMILIES` rule engine until Clap would replace meaningful routing logic instead of wrapping a parser that is already small and schema-driven
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Rust-own the yzx config family end to end
close_reason: Completed: the full yzx config family is now Rust-owned through yzx_control, the shared public command surface metadata/help/externs reflect the new owner, and the surviving public Nu config owner file is deleted from the runtime contract.

title: Define the deletion budget and dependency gate for the yzx config owner cut
notes:
Working hypothesis:
- `yzx config` is the next strongest honest owner cut after `yzx home_manager`
- the public Nu owner is small, while config path and surface ownership already have Rust support in `active_config_surface`

Dependency gate:
- preserve `yzx config --path` and `yzx config reset` behavior exactly
- no new production crates by default unless a real parsing or time-format need appears that is more stable than in-house/std logic
Additional guardrails after plan review:
- do not count a Rust wrapper that shells back into `config_surfaces.nu` as an owner cut
- the decision must say whether Rust owns the user-visible config display surface directly, whether output stays text/TOML/record-compatible enough for current users, and how `yzx config reset` preserves confirmation, backup naming, and fail-fast behavior
- if exact output parity is too coupled to Nushell-only rendering, record that explicitly before implementation instead of discovering it halfway through the task
Decision:
- Rust should own the full public `yzx config` family directly; this is a real owner cut because config path discovery and active config surface loading already live in Rust and the remaining Nu owner is a thin command wrapper
- Production crates: no new crates; use existing `toml`, `serde_json`, and std facilities already in `yazelix_core`
- Dev-only crates: none
- In-house logic: implement config display rendering, confirmation flow, backup naming, and reset file operations in-house inside the existing control/runtime model
- Rejected alternatives: a Rust wrapper that shells back into `config_surfaces.nu` or keeps `config reset` in Nu would preserve the mixed owner seam and does not satisfy the deletion budget
- Packaging impact: delete `nushell/scripts/yzx/config.nu` and remove its export from the installed runtime once read and reset paths both land

Deletion budget:
- Move `yzx config` and `yzx config --path` to Rust control without behavior drift
- Move `yzx config reset [--yes] [--no-backup]` to Rust control with preserved confirmation, backup, and fail-fast semantics
- Remove the `config` family from `INTERNAL_NU_FAMILIES`
- Delete the surviving Nu public owner file and any stale contract/docs references

Parity decisions:
- `yzx config` will continue to print the active merged config as TOML text, not a Rust debug/JSON view
- `yzx config --path` must preserve the resolved active config file path behavior exactly
- `yzx config reset` must preserve confirmation by default, explicit `--yes`, explicit `--no-backup`, fail-fast IO errors, and a user-visible backup path message
- Backup naming should stay human-readable and stable rather than switch to opaque epoch-based suffixes
close_reason: Decision recorded: Rust owns the full yzx config family with no new crates; preserve TOML display, path, confirmation, backup, and fail-fast behavior while deleting the surviving Nu owner.

title: Implement the non-mutating yzx config read owner cut
notes:
Revised during plan review: this bead is now only the non-mutating side of the family.

Scope:
- move `yzx config` and `yzx config --path` onto the Rust-owned public route
- establish the family root on the Rust side without yet claiming the mutating reset path

Non-goal:
- do not delete `nushell/scripts/yzx/config.nu` in this bead unless the separate reset/delete bead is also complete
close_reason: Completed: yzx config and yzx config --path now route through yzx_control, the shared command surface classifies config as Rust-owned, and focused public CLI tests cover path and bootstrap behavior.

title: Implement the yzx config reset owner cut and delete nushell/scripts/yzx/config.nu
close_reason: Completed: yzx config reset is Rust-owned, nushell/scripts/yzx/config.nu is deleted, backup and no-backup behavior are covered by focused public CLI tests, and the installed-runtime contract no longer ships or expects the deleted Nu owner.

title: Collapse the smallest help and support internal yzx families
close_reason: Completed: the smallest help/support internal yzx families are collapsed; yzx why, yzx sponsor, and yzx keys are now Rust-owned and their surviving Nu owner files are deleted.

title: Define the batch boundary for yzx why and yzx sponsor
notes:
Initial batch boundary proposal:
- include `yzx why`, `yzx sponsor`, and `yzx keys*`
- exclude `yzx tutor*` for now because `yazelix-3jw` keeps product evolution open on that family
- exclude `yzx whats_new` for now because it still fronts the Nushell-owned `upgrade_summary` subsystem, so moving only the public leaf would be cosmetic rather than a real owner cut
Revised during plan review: `yzx keys` is no longer bundled into this smallest-family bead because it has its own alias and formatting parity surface.

This bead is now strictly about the tiny support leaf family in `yzx_support.nu`.

Still excluded here:
- `yzx tutor*` because `yazelix-3jw` keeps the product surface open
- `yzx whats_new` because it still fronts the Nushell-owned `upgrade_summary` subsystem and moving only the leaf would be cosmetic
Decision:
- `yzx why` and `yzx sponsor` are the smallest honest public-family Rust cut left in the help/support lane
- They should stay batched together because they share one tiny surviving Nu owner (`nushell/scripts/core/yzx_support.nu`) and do not carry the alias/table-formatting surface that makes `yzx keys` riskier
- `yzx keys*` stays out of this bead and remains its own decision lane because its alias set (`yzx`, `yazi`, `hx`, `helix`, `nu`, `nushell`) plus ANSI/table discoverability output are a separate parity problem
- `yzx tutor*` remains excluded because `yazelix-3jw` still owns product evolution there
- `yzx whats_new` remains excluded because it still fronts the Nushell-owned `upgrade_summary` subsystem and moving only the leaf would be cosmetic

Dependency and crate gate:
- Production crates: no new crates
- Dev-only crates: none
- In-house logic: keep static info rendering and opener probing in-house with std + existing control-plane structure
- Rejected alternative: keeping `yzx sponsor` in Nu just because it probes `xdg-open` / `open` would preserve a trivial mixed owner seam for no real benefit

Deletion budget:
- Move `yzx why` and `yzx sponsor` to Rust control
- Remove both leaves from `INTERNAL_NU_FAMILIES`
- Delete `nushell/scripts/core/yzx_support.nu`
- Remove installed-runtime expectations and stale docs/spec references to that deleted owner while preserving current copy and sponsor fallback behavior
close_reason: Decision recorded: yzx why and yzx sponsor stay batched as the smallest honest help/support Rust cut, while yzx keys remains a separate alias-heavy decision lane with no new crates.

title: Implement Rust ownership for yzx why and yzx sponsor and delete nushell/scripts/core/yzx_support.nu
notes: Revised during plan review: this bead now only owns the tiny support family. Keep the URL-opening behavior, fallback-to-print behavior, and current user-visible copy intact while deleting the Nushell owner.
close_reason: Completed: yzx why and yzx sponsor now route through yzx_control, the shared public command surface classifies both leaves as Rust-owned, nushell/scripts/core/yzx_support.nu is deleted, and focused public-route tests plus the installed-runtime contract validate the cut.

title: Define the deletion budget and alias parity gate for the yzx keys family
notes:
This bead exists because `yzx keys` is still small enough to be a plausible Rust owner cut, but it carries more alias and formatting surface than `yzx why` / `yzx sponsor`.

Guardrails:
- preserve all existing aliases (`yzx`, `yazi`, `hx`, `helix`, `nu`, `nushell`)
- preserve the current discoverability role as a keybinding/help surface, not a generic docs rewrite
- do not batch `yzx tutor*` into this family; its product evolution remains under `yazelix-3jw`
Decision:
- `yzx keys` remains a real next deletion lane after `yzx why` / `yzx sponsor`
- It is still small and self-contained enough to be Rust-owned end-to-end because the surviving owner is one public Nu file (`nushell/scripts/yzx/keys.nu`) rather than a larger subsystem seam
- It is not a trivial copy move because the family includes six public leaves and aliases (`yzx`, `yazi`, `hx`, `helix`, `nu`, `nushell`) plus human-oriented ANSI/table formatting and section structure

Crate and dependency gate:
- Production crates: no new crates by default
- Dev-only crates: none
- In-house logic: keep alias dispatch, ANSI text shaping, and simple table-like rendering in-house unless a later implementation proves that broad formatting crates buy materially safer parity than handwritten output
- Rejected default: do not add a terminal styling/table framework just to avoid writing a few formatted strings; that would weaken the deletion value of this lane

Deletion budget and parity gate:
- Preserve all existing aliases exactly: `yzx keys`, `yzx keys yzx`, `yzx keys yazi`, `yzx keys hx`, `yzx keys helix`, `yzx keys nu`, `yzx keys nushell`
- Preserve the current discoverability role and user-facing copy structure; this is still a keybinding/help surface, not a docs rewrite
- Preserve enough ANSI/table/readability quality that the Rust cut does not regress the human UX into a flat dump
- Count the migration only if the full public family becomes Rust-owned and `nushell/scripts/yzx/keys.nu` disappears
- If exact parity would require a broad crate or a bigger product rewrite than this bead justifies, record that stop condition before implementation instead of forcing a low-quality port
close_reason: Decision recorded: yzx keys is still a real next Rust owner cut, but only if it preserves the full alias family plus the current ANSI/table-style discoverability surface without adding broad new crates.

title: Implement Rust ownership for yzx keys and delete nushell/scripts/yzx/keys.nu
close_reason: Completed: the full yzx keys family now routes through yzx_control, the shared public command surface classifies keys as Rust-owned, nushell/scripts/yzx/keys.nu is deleted, and focused public CLI tests plus the installed-runtime contract validate the alias and discoverability surface.

title: Decide whether yzx doctor has an honest report-only Rust owner cut before Clap
close_reason: Completed: the honest report-only Rust doctor owner cut landed and the remaining doctor Nu work is private fix-only helper scope.

title: Define the yzx doctor report/fix split and the report-only go/no-go
notes: Decision: proceed with a report-only Rust owner cut. Scope: move public yzx doctor, summary/rendering, JSON emission, install/runtime/helix/config report aggregation, and live Zellij plugin-health reporting into Rust; keep only the side-effecting repair flow as a private Nushell helper. Crate gate: no new production crates and no new dev-only crates; use existing serde/serde_json/lexopt plus in-house std::process/std::fs path and command helpers. Rejected alternative: a thin Rust shim over the current Nushell doctor family would still leave the public owner mixed and does not count as deletion. Stop condition: if the Rust owner cannot delete nushell/scripts/core/yzx_doctor.nu and nushell/scripts/utils/doctor_report_bridge.nu, this bead should close as a no-go instead.
close_reason: Completed: the report-only Rust owner cut is honest because it deletes nushell/scripts/core/yzx_doctor.nu and nushell/scripts/utils/doctor_report_bridge.nu while leaving only a private fix helper.

title: Implement the yzx doctor report-only owner cut or record the explicit stop condition
notes: Implementation target: Rust-own the public yzx doctor report path end to end. Delete nushell/scripts/core/yzx_doctor.nu and nushell/scripts/utils/doctor_report_bridge.nu, move report aggregation/summary/rendering and live Zellij plugin-health checks into Rust, keep only a private Nushell doctor_fix.nu helper for side-effecting --fix actions, and leave install-ownership request shaping in a focused install_ownership.nu helper.
close_reason: Completed: yzx_control now owns the public doctor report path, doctor_fix.nu is the only surviving private repair helper, and the shared doctor report bridge is deleted.

title: Re-evaluate Clap after config and help-support collapse plus the doctor decision
notes:
Plan-review refinement:
- do not reopen Clap just because the config, support, and doctor beads exist
- reopen only if those lanes materially shrink the surviving internal-family routing surface, not merely move leaf text or wrap existing Nushell behavior
- if Clap is still a no-go, record the exact remaining families and the next honest deletion candidate instead of creating premature implementation beads
Decision on 2026-04-21:
- no-go for a Clap implementation lane now
- the public Rust root is already small and schema-driven
- the surviving internal-Nu families are cwd, desktop, dev, edit, enter, import, launch, menu, popup, restart, reveal, screen, tutor, and whats_new
- most of those families are still intentionally Nushell-owned because they are shell-heavy, process-heavy, or mostly product UX
- adding Clap now would mostly replace a small amount of family-classification logic while keeping or re-encoding the same internal-family table and family-specific fallback behavior
- the next honest public-family deletion candidate is core/yzx_workspace.nu through a joint yzx cwd / yzx reveal evaluation, tracked in yazelix-7nyk
close_reason: Completed: Clap re-evaluated as a no-go after the config, support, keys, and doctor report-owner cuts; next honest deletion candidate recorded.

title: Split installed-runtime and Nix package validators by domain
description: Continue the maintainer validator diet after the config-surface and upgrade-contract split. Split installed-runtime validation, Nix package/profile install validation, and helper IO into private domains, then delete redundant path/string checks only when covered by live package smoke checks.
design: Delete-first maintainer split. Keep validators in-repo. Preserve validator command behavior and error visibility. Do not delete live packaging/Home Manager checks unless a stronger live smoke check covers the same contract.
acceptance_criteria: repo_contract_validation.rs is smaller and delegates installed-runtime and Nix/package/profile domains to private modules; redundant validator trivia is deleted where behavior-equivalent smoke checks remain; focused maintainer validator tests/commands pass; Rust budget and inventory are updated; LOC delta and rejected candidates are recorded.
notes: Implementation: split repo_contract_validation into private helpers, installed_runtime, nix_interface, and nix_package domains. Deleted redundant positive substring checks for POSIX wrapper/runtime-tree internals while preserving path checks, forbidden legacy checks, package builds, and public CLI smoke. Replaced installed-runtime flake surface discovery from stale nix flake show JSON parsing with direct nix eval of current-system package/app keys. Verification: cargo check -p yazelix_maintainer --all-targets passed; cargo test -p yazelix_maintainer repo_contract_validation passed; validate-flake-interface passed; validate-nixpkgs-package passed; validate-rust-ownership-budget passed with existing hard-target warning. validate-installed-runtime-contract moved past the old flake-show failure but timed out after 10 minutes while building the temporary Home Manager activation package. validate-nix-customization-api hung in nix eval and was interrupted; no code failure was emitted.
close_reason: Completed: split validator domains, removed redundant static string checks, fixed installed-runtime flake surface evaluation, updated Rust budget/inventory.

title: Add compact overflow-safe agent usage rollup widgets
description: The individual daily/monthly/session usage widgets can exceed zjstatus right-bar width and disappear under format_hide_on_overlength, making it look like usage is broken even when status_bar_cache contains data. Design compact rollup widgets or an overflow-safe rendering contract for Codex/OpenCode usage, likely with shorter labels and selectable period/display modes, so users can see daily/monthly/session facts without manually balancing bar width.
close_reason: Completed compact combined usage widgets for Codex, Claude, and OpenCode Go; Claude now supports 5h/week token and quota shape with logged-out quota backoff.

title: Replace yzx screen Mandelbrot orbit with real deep zoom
description: Replace the current yzx screen Mandelbrot motion, which reads as an orbit/spinner with only modest apparent zoom, with a convincing terminal-native deep zoom. The quality target is inspired by Frankentui's VFX Mandelbrot screenshot: a clear Mandelbrot boundary at screen scale, high contrast, dense colorful edge detail, and visible inward motion over time. Do not copy Frankentui code or adopt its runtime; this belongs in the yazelix-screen child repo first.
design:
Start from first principles: a Mandelbrot VFX animation succeeds when the camera makes depth legible. The current implementation has a Seahorse Valley tour and iteration math, but the apparent motion is dominated by rotation/orbiting and the user does not perceive an awesome zoom. The fix should prioritize camera design before palette tweaks: choose a visually strong target boundary, begin from a wider recognizable view, zoom by enough orders of magnitude to feel like a dive, reduce rotational drift unless it actively improves the zoom, and keep the focal boundary in frame across common terminal sizes.

Visual style should stay terminal-native: character-cell rendering, ANSI/truecolor only if supported by the existing screen contract, no graphics protocol dependency, and no copied Frankentui implementation. Palette/glyph/dithering changes are welcome only if they support the deep-zoom feel and remain deterministic. Performance must be measured before this enters random/welcome defaults.

Ratatui is a legitimate implementation option, but not the core solution. yazelix-screen currently has a small crossterm-based runtime and custom ScreenFrame/ScreenCell line renderer. Before changing Rust code, record a crate-vs-in-house decision: keep the current in-house frame renderer if the work is mostly camera math plus deterministic string output; adopt Ratatui only if it clearly reduces custom rendering complexity, improves color/style correctness, provides a useful test backend, or helps future VFX composition without making standalone yzs harder to use.
acceptance_criteria:
- yzx screen / yazelix-screen Mandelbrot visibly performs a deep zoom rather than mostly spinning around
- The camera zoom ratio across the loop is large enough to be visually obvious on normal desktop terminal sizes, and this is covered by deterministic camera tests
- The focal boundary remains present in representative 80x24, 120x40, and wide terminal frames
- Render output has a high-contrast boundary and dense colored edge detail comparable in intent to the Frankentui VFX screenshot, while remaining terminal-native
- Before Rust implementation starts, the bead records a crate-vs-in-house decision that explicitly evaluates Ratatui versus the current crossterm/custom-frame path
- Performance is measured for at least one representative wide frame size before changing random or welcome defaults
- Implementation lives in the yazelix-screen child repo, with main repo consuming it only through the normal flake-lock release transaction after the child change is published
notes:
2026-05-22 implementation start / Rust dependency decision:
- Production crates: keep existing `crossterm`; add no new crates for the immediate Mandelbrot fix.
- Dev-only crates: none.
- Build in-house: camera path, zoom easing, frame scoring/color mapping, deterministic tests, and any tiny render-review helper needed for this bead.
- Rejected for this slice: Ratatui, because the observed failure is camera/motion/flicker, not widget composition; Ratty/RGP, because it is a separate optional terminal capability tracked in yazelix-pq674 and must not block Ghostty/WezTerm + Zellij behavior.
- Packaging impact: child repo source-only change first; main Yazelix should consume it later through the normal published child flake-lock transaction after manual visual approval.

Additional user observations:
- The current Mandelbrot flickers a bit. The implementation should reduce visible shimmer in addition to making zoom depth obvious.
- The visual route should expose the tentacles/tendrils, Seahorse Valley, and seahorse-like detail instead of diving into a filled interior.
- Render speed matters because nobody wants a slow welcome screen; measure representative frame cost and keep iteration caps conservative.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: Implemented locally in yazelix-screen commit 737594c (not pushed yet): Mandelbrot now starts from a recognizable overview, dives into a Seahorse Valley target, exposes dark valley/tendril negative space and dense seahorse/tentacle edge structure, removes the old rotational tour, removes percentile/rank remapping that contributed to shimmer, and exports mandelbrot_frame_delay() at 55ms for faster playback. Verification: cargo fmt --check; cargo test (28 lib + 4 bin tests); release render timings target/release/examples/render_once were 80x24 frame 105 0.00s, 120x40 frame 210 0.00s, and 240x62 frame 280 0.01s. Remaining before closing: user/manual visual approval, push child repo, update main Yazelix dependency/lock to the published child commit, and make the main welcome/screen path consume mandelbrot_frame_delay() instead of the old 110ms hardcode.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: Refined after visual feedback; child commit was amended to yazelix-screen c58c880 (not pushed). Changes from the first local pass: remove visible camera pan entirely by fixing the center on the Seahorse Valley target, shorten the loop from 420 to 120 frames, remove the max-zoom hold/freezing plateau, front-load the zoom so the normal short welcome sees valley/tentacle detail quickly, and reduce the deepest endpoint to avoid disappearing into a filled/static-looking patch. Verification after refinement: cargo fmt --check; cargo test (28 lib + 4 bin tests); cargo build --release --example render_once --bin yzs; release timings were 80x24 frame 36 0.00s, 120x40 frame 60 0.00s, 240x62 frame 90 0.02s.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: Refined Mandelbrot color after user approval of the fixed-center route. Child commit amended to yazelix-screen 9f34289 (not pushed): removed per-cell escape-depth hue cycling, dropped the loud cyan/lime ends of the palette, and made color selection intensity-led with a calmer violet/pink/orange/yellow ramp. A representative 120x40 frame now uses four related color codes instead of the previous seven-color cycling. Verification: cargo fmt --check; cargo test; cargo build --release --example render_once --bin yzs; release timings unchanged at 80x24 frame 36 0.00s, 120x40 frame 60 0.00s, 240x62 frame 90 0.02s.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: Refined motion after user feedback to keep the current recursive-feeling Seahorse/tendril target rather than chasing a new minibrot. Child commit amended to yazelix-screen 365cabd (not pushed): dive progress now uses an ease-in curve so the initial image starts slower and accelerates into the valley, while keeping the fixed-center/no-hold behavior. Main commit 21f23be7 changes the default welcome duration from 2.0s to 3.0s across settings_default.jsonc, the config contract, startup fallback, and tests so the slower start still reaches visible detail in the welcome window. Verification: child cargo fmt --check + cargo test; release build/timing at frame 54: 80x24 0.00s, 120x40 0.00s, 240x62 0.00s; main cargo test -p yazelix_core normalizes_default_config_with_parser_keys_and_transforms; cargo test -p yazelix_maintainer repo_sweep_runner; cargo run -p yazelix_maintainer --bin yzx_repo_validator -- validate-config-surface-contract.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: Manual visual feedback after the slower-start fixed-center version: user is satisfied. The final/deep negative-space hole resembles the starting Mandelbrot silhouette, which gives the animation a recursive feel without changing target or pursuing a true minibrot recurrence. Preserve this as a strength of the current route; do not keep tuning the target unless a future bead explicitly revisits recursive/self-similar Mandelbrot zooms.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: Recursive/endless zoom experiment implemented locally in yazelix-screen 46caa05 (not pushed). Preserved the satisfied baseline first: child branch preserve/mandelbrot_satisfied_state at 365cabd, main branch preserve/before_recursive_mandelbrot at 3c06abc. Web grounding: mathr's approximate self-similarity note describes looping Mandelbrot zooms by finding a baby-copy center and complex size r, with the limiting center c/(1-r); Wikibooks documents baby/minibrot copies, distortion, and size/orientation concerns. Prototype strategy: keep the existing fixed Seahorse target and non-returning inward zoom, then during the late seam project a scaled copy of frame 0 through the central dark recursive region and resolve completely to frame 0 before wrap. This is an illusion of affine recurrence rather than a full minibrot-center search. Verification: cargo fmt; cargo test; release build/timing for seam frame 108: 80x24 0.00s, 120x40 0.01s, 240x62 0.02s. If manual testing looks artificial, abandon this child commit and keep the preserved baseline.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: Second recursive experiment after visual rejection of the portal overlay. Child commit amended to yazelix-screen 5acc54d (not pushed). Dropped the screen-space frame projection entirely because it caused rectangular bars/artifacts and a hard-looking seam. New attempt is a pure scale-curve seam: zooms into the existing target, then smoothly eases back toward the overview during the final segment so the wrap is artifact-free. This is less mathematically ambitious than a true minibrot recurrence, but should be much smoother and has no copied-frame rectangles. Verification: cargo fmt --check; cargo test; cargo build --release --example render_once --bin yzs; seam timings 80x24 frame108 0.00s, 120x40 frame108 0.00s, 240x62 frame108 0.01s.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: Recursive finite-loop correction after visual rejection: the smooth scale-return seam was wrong because it produced back-and-forth motion, not an infinite zoom. I tried the mathematically grounded minibrot recurrence path (, ), but at terminal resolution and this escape-time renderer the endpoint became dense/artifact-prone rather than a convincing seamless recursive copy. Current local child change restores the approved Seahorse Valley route and removes the finite reset instead:  no longer wraps at 120, the camera remains fixed on the Seahorse target, and scale keeps decreasing past the old boundary. Tests now require frame 120 to differ from frame 0 and require 119 -> 120 -> 121 to keep moving inward. This is a monotonic infinite/deep zoom for the welcome/runtime path, not a perfect self-similar minibrot loop.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: Correction to previous comment: the shell ate the inline formulas. The rejected minibrot recurrence used center = c/(1-r) * (1-r^t) and scale = r^t. The retained local approach is explicitly monotonic: frame_index no longer wraps at 120, so the approved Seahorse Valley zoom continues inward instead of returning to the overview.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: Latest retry after standalone feedback: monotonic unbounded zoom was wrong for yzs because it eventually runs into floating-point / iteration-limit emptiness after repeated zooms. Reworked the local child patch into a bounded endless recursion illusion: the numeric camera still dives into the approved fixed Seahorse target, but the final third of each cycle grows a nested next-cycle Mandelbrot overview from the center using fresh Mandelbrot samples rather than copied screen rectangles. The phase then repeats before precision limits empty the screen. Tests now defend that frame 120 equals frame 0, frame 119 already reveals the next overview, and second-cycle frames remain populated.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: Refined recursive transition after manual feedback that the parent shape overlapped the baby Mandelbrot and then vanished, and that the end slowed almost to a halt. Child patch now carves the portal as a true hole: inside the growing opening the parent layer never draws, so the old shape is swallowed by the next zoom instead of overlapping it. The portal grows to cover the whole frame by the cycle boundary, and both the dive easing and portal easing keep a nonzero tail so the end still slows but does not stall. Added a regression covering the carved hole, full-frame endpoint coverage, and measurable late portal motion. Verification: cargo fmt --check; cargo test; cargo build --release --example render_once --bin yzs; release seam timings 80x24/120x40/240x62 frame 118 all reported 0.00s.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: Final easing tweak after manual testing: child commit fe49f8e keeps the carved recursive portal shape but reduces the seam stall by increasing the portal linear tail from 0.22 to 0.34, and makes the new cycle start a little faster by changing dive easing from tail 0.14 / pow 1.15 to tail 0.20 / pow 1.10. Verification in yazelix-screen: cargo fmt --check; cargo test; cargo build --release --example render_once --bin yzs; release render timing for 240x62 frames 0 and 118 reported 0.00s. Main flake.lock now consumes published yazelix-screen fe49f8e.

title: Fix yzx warp layout-name new-tab failure
description:
`yzx warp` can fail inside existing Yazelix windows with `Failed to load layout: IoError: The layout was not found, File: yzx_side`. The command passes `ZELLIJ_DEFAULT_LAYOUT=yzx_side` directly to `zellij action new-tab --layout`, but that action needs a resolvable layout path or layout-dir context.

Fix `yzx warp` so layout names resolve to the generated Yazelix layout path under `YAZELIX_STATE_DIR/configs/zellij/layouts/` when possible, while preserving absolute/path layout values and existing behavior for sessions that already provide a path.
notes:
Dependency decision, 2026-04-28:
- Production Rust crates: none new. Use std::env/path and existing CoreError helpers.
- Dev-only crates: none new. Existing workspace_commands unit tests cover structured argv behavior.
- In-house logic: resolve the current layout env to a Zellij new-tab layout path when it is a Yazelix layout name, preserving explicit path values.
- Rejected alternatives: shell wrapper around zellij, requiring users to restart all existing windows, or dropping the layout argument entirely and losing managed sidebar/editor tab shape.
- Packaging impact: none.
Implementation result, 2026-04-28:
- `yzx warp` now resolves `ZELLIJ_DEFAULT_LAYOUT` layout names such as `yzx_side` through `$YAZELIX_STATE_DIR/configs/zellij/layouts/<name>.kdl` before calling `zellij action new-tab --layout`.
- Explicit layout paths and `.kdl` values are preserved unchanged.
- If the generated layout path cannot be found, the old layout value is preserved so custom/session-specific Zellij behavior can still handle it.
- Added regression coverage for layout-name resolution and explicit path preservation.
- Verification: cargo fmt --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml --all -- --check; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core workspace_commands::tests::warp_; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core workspace_commands::tests::builds_structured_zellij_new_tab_args; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core; yzx_repo_validator validate-contracts.
close_reason: Resolved yzx warp layout names to generated layout paths

title: Define a supply-chain hardening policy for Yazelix tool surfaces
description: Define a lightweight supply-chain hardening policy for Yazelix so default-installed tool surfaces stay low-risk, optional higher-risk packs stay explicitly scoped, and future incidents have a repeatable triage and communication workflow.
design: Keep this lightweight and policy-first. The goal is not exhaustive auditing of every upstream tool; it is a practical safety model for Yazelix's maintained surfaces. Define the risk tiers for default-installed tools versus opt-in packs, prefer hermetic Nix-managed runtime paths over host package managers, require a small review checklist before enabling new default tools, and define a repeatable incident-response workflow for future supply-chain advisories. The result should help maintainers decide when a tool can be default-enabled, when it must remain opt-in, and how to communicate impact quickly when a dependency incident happens.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a short documented policy describing: 1. what package/tool surfaces Yazelix treats as default, opt-in, or higher-risk; 2. review criteria for enabling new default tools; 3. preferred runtime/update paths and discouraged package-manager paths; and 4. a repeatable incident triage workflow for future supply-chain advisories. The policy should give maintainers enough guidance to classify a new tool addition or a future supply-chain incident without ad-hoc reasoning each time.
close_reason: Completed: docs/contracts/supply_chain_hardening.md now defines the default/opt-in risk split, review checklist for default tool additions, Nix-managed runtime preference, and incident triage workflow. Verified by canonical contract structure and current repo references.

title: Publish a terminal editor shell compatibility matrix
description: Create a current compatibility matrix for Ghostty, WezTerm, Kitty, Alacritty, Foot, Helix, Neovim, shell choices, Linux/macOS, and install owners.
design: State supported, best-effort, and unsupported combinations. Separate cursor trails, image previews, launch support, managed editor integrations, Zellij web sharing, and install-owner behavior so users can reason about tradeoffs.
acceptance_criteria: Docs state exact support levels with validation date/owner, distinguish cursor trails from image previews and managed editor behavior, and avoid unsupported terminal/editor combinations being implied as first-class.

title: Prove the workspace UX layer runs without backend ownership
description: Take the boundary discussion from docs/specs into a real executable slice: the surviving workspace UX should run cleanly even when Yazelix is not provisioning or activating the environment. Focus on the tab-local and workspace-local flows that should remain coherent with host-provided tools on PATH, rather than on env/build/install behavior.
design: Treat this as a proof bead, not a speculative new edition. Start from the post-m5rk boundary: backend activation already has a clearer seam, so now prove that cwd/reveal/popup/menu/edit and other workspace UX flows can operate without depending on devenv ownership. Delete any remaining hidden backend assumptions in those flows instead of introducing compatibility shims. The result should make a future Core-style omission mode feel like deleting a layer, not reimplementing one.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete validated slice of workspace UX that runs with host-provided tools and without backend ownership. The surviving flows and their limits are explicit. Any remaining backend-coupled workspace behaviors are identified as follow-up seams rather than left implicit.
notes: Pipeline stage 4: the executable proof bead. This should only close after explicit-root cleanup, pane-orchestrator-owned workspace truth, and the honest capability split are in place. Its job is to prove that the surviving workspace UX actually works without backend ownership.
close_reason: Documented the runtime-root-only backend-free workspace slice and added regressions proving popup/menu/reveal workspace flows can run with host-provided tools.

title: Add Yazelix-managed per-shell user hook surfaces
description: Add explicit Yazelix-owned user hook files for managed shell startup instead of sourcing personal dotfiles in managed panes. Scope includes bash, zsh, fish, and nushell.
design: Preferred design: add optional per-shell hook files under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/shells/ (bash.sh, zsh.zsh, fish.fish, nu.nu) and source them at the end of each Yazelix-managed shell startup path. Managed panes and Yazelix env sessions stay self-contained by default; user hooks are an explicit extension surface inside Yazelix ownership, not personal dotfile sourcing. Do not auto-copy or auto-source ~/.bashrc, ~/.zshrc, ~/.config/fish/config.fish, or ~/.config/nushell/* for managed panes. Keep the order strict: runtime env, Yazelix initializers, managed shell config, then optional Yazelix user hook. Nushell should use a managed user hook file in the same spirit as the new managed pane wrapper, not the personal env.nu/config.nu path. Prefer one shared resolver/helper for shell hook file lookup rather than duplicated per-shell path logic.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Managed Yazelix shell startup for bash, zsh, fish, and nu can source an optional Yazelix-owned user hook file from ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/shells/. 2. Managed panes do not source personal shell dotfiles by default. 3. The feature does not reintroduce the pane-latency problem by importing large personal shell setups implicitly. 4. Missing user hook files are a normal no-op, not an error. 5. Tests defend at least one generated/runtime path and one startup path for the new shell hook contract.
notes: UX reasoning: users still need a place for aliases/functions/prompt tweaks inside Yazelix, but personal shell startup is too broad, slow, and surprising for managed panes. The explicit Yazelix-owned hook surface preserves self-containment while giving advanced users a clear extension point.
close_reason: Completed: managed per-shell user hook surfaces now exist for bash, zsh, fish, and nushell; the remaining stub-scaffolding follow-up stays in yazelix-5yg1.1.

title: Scaffold visible stubs for Yazelix-managed user config surfaces
description: Create lightweight empty/commented stubs for relevant Yazelix-managed user config surfaces so users can discover where customizations belong without reading docs first.
design: Scope this to lightweight, self-explanatory scaffolding for Yazelix-owned user config surfaces under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/. Prefer creating stubs automatically when a surface becomes relevant or is first managed, not a giant first-run dump of empty files. For shells, likely create ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/shells/ plus small commented stubs. For other managed surfaces, favor one-line comment stubs or empty directories only when they materially improve discoverability. These are user-owned runtime files under ~/.config/yazelix, not tracked repo files and not personal dotfiles outside Yazelix ownership.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Relevant Yazelix-managed user config surfaces are auto-created under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/ when they become relevant. 2. Scaffolding stays lightweight and does not create a noisy pile of unused files by default. 3. Stub files clearly say they are Yazelix-managed extension points. 4. No personal config files outside ~/.config/yazelix/ are created or modified by this feature. 5. The implementation does not add tracked repo-state pollution for these user-owned stubs.
notes:
Dependency decision, 2026-04-28:
- Production Rust crates: none new. Use std::fs/path and existing CoreError helpers.
- Dev-only crates: none new; existing tempfile test dependency is already available in the crate.
- In-house logic: small, explicit stub-file map for managed shell hooks, terminal overrides, and README-only discovery stubs for fallback-sensitive Zellij/Helix/Yazi surfaces.
- Rejected alternatives: creating actual Zellij config.kdl stubs, because that would disable native Zellij fallback; creating actual Helix config.toml stubs, because that would suppress import-discovery behavior; dumping every possible shell hook on first run; adding a template engine.
- Packaging impact: none. Stubs are created under user-owned ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs at runtime only when the corresponding surface is touched.
Implementation result, 2026-04-28:
- Added a Rust-owned managed_user_config_stubs helper. It writes only missing files under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs and never overwrites existing user content.
- Zellij, Helix, and Yazi now get lightweight README.md discovery stubs when their materialization surfaces run. Zellij config.kdl and Helix config.toml are intentionally not auto-created because doing so would change native fallback/import-discovery behavior.
- Shell startup now creates hook stubs only for the configured shell set (`bash.sh`, `zsh.zsh`, `fish.fish`, `nu.nu`) before generating shell initializers.
- Terminal materialization now creates terminal-native override stubs only for terminals with a live managed override contract: Ghostty, Kitty, and Alacritty. WezTerm and Foot do not get pretend override files.
- Verification: cargo fmt --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml --all -- --check; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core managed_user_config_stubs; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core.
close_reason: Implemented runtime-managed visible config stubs

title: Extract magician Kitty renderer to yazelix-screen
description: Move the generic Kitty graphics frame-sequence rendering used by the magician welcome/screen out of yazelix_core and into the yazelix-screen child crate. Keep Yazelix product policy in the main repo: style selection, runtime asset paths, environment/capability gating, and CoreError-facing messages. Follow the cross-repo release transaction: change and push yazelix-screen first, update the main repo dependency/lock/hash to that published rev, then validate the main runtime without local overrides.
acceptance_criteria: The extraction plan has child work for API boundary, child implementation, main integration, and release validation. Main repo runtime behavior remains unchanged: magician renders in Ghostty/Zellij, random pool includes magician, pane frames remain visible, and graphics cleanup works on exit.

title: Define magician renderer crate boundary
description: Decide the exact ownership boundary before code moves. yazelix-screen should own terminal drawing primitives: Kitty upload/delete command generation, image placement layout, z-index/inset policy, and frame-sequence playback loop. yazelix_core should retain Yazelix policy: style resolution, random pools, runtime asset discovery, Kitty/Zellij capability checks, and CoreError messages.
acceptance_criteria: Boundary notes list APIs exported from yazelix-screen and responsibilities intentionally kept in yazelix_core. The boundary decides whether random style resolution is child-owned or contract-tested for parity across yzs random, welcome random, and yzx screen random. Crate-vs-in-house decision is recorded: use existing child crate yazelix-screen, add no new production crates unless the bead is updated with explicit packaging impact.
notes: Boundary landed: yazelix_screen owns reusable Kitty PNG frame primitives (KittyFrameSequence, layout, upload/delete command generation, draw/play/cleanup loop) and shared random animation-family resolution. yazelix_core keeps Yazelix policy: style catalog enforcement, runtime magician asset path/checking, Kitty/Zellij capability gating, CoreError translation, image id selection, and welcome/screen routing. Crate decision: use the existing yazelix-screen child crate plus std/crossterm already present there; no new production or dev crates added, no extra Nix packaging inputs beyond the child source rev/hash.
close_reason: Boundary recorded and implemented in child/main split; no new crates added.

title: Implement Kitty frame sequence renderer in yazelix-screen
description: In the /home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-screen child repo, add a reusable Kitty frame-sequence renderer API for file-backed PNG frames. It should cover command generation, placement deletion, centered/inset layout, z-index below text, frame timing, keypress exit, and alternate-screen cleanup behavior. Keep the API generic enough that the child crate does not know about Yazelix assets or magician policy.
acceptance_criteria: yazelix-screen has focused tests for Kitty upload/delete command text, layout insets, z-index, and cleanup ordering. The child repo is formatted/tested and pushed before the main repo consumes the rev. Crate decision: no new production crates by default; if a crate is added, record production/dev-only status and Nix packaging impact here before implementation continues.
notes: Child repo luccahuguet/yazelix-screen commit 240ee378ddad37f2a033601a23e5557d26cd80fc adds the reusable Kitty frame sequence renderer. Child verification passed: cargo fmt --check, cargo check, cargo test, and nix build .#yzs --no-link.
close_reason: Published child renderer at luccahuguet/yazelix-screen@240ee378ddad37f2a033601a23e5557d26cd80fc with Rust and Nix checks passing.

title: Consume yazelix-screen magician renderer from main repo
description: Update yazelix_core to call the new yazelix-screen Kitty frame-sequence renderer for magician welcome and yzx screen magician. Keep main-repo code responsible for resolving runtime frame paths, asserting assets exist, checking Kitty/Zellij support, selecting random styles, and translating child IO/render errors into CoreError.
acceptance_criteria: front_door_render.rs deletes the local Kitty command/layout/playback implementation that moved to yazelix-screen. Existing magician behavior tests are preserved or replaced with main-repo boundary tests. Welcome random, yzx screen random, and yzs random use the same pool or have a contract-backed parity test. Cargo.toml/Cargo.lock and Nix vendor hashes point to the published child rev, not a local override.
notes: Main repo consumes published yazelix-screen rev 240ee378ddad37f2a033601a23e5557d26cd80fc through Cargo.toml, Cargo.lock, flake.lock, and the Nix vendor hash. front_door_render.rs delegates Kitty frame playback/cleanup and random family resolution to yazelix_screen while retaining runtime asset paths, asset validation, capability gating, CoreError mapping, and routing.
close_reason: Main repo integrated the published child renderer/random APIs and deleted duplicated Kitty/random implementation.

title: Validate and land magician renderer extraction
description: Complete the coupled release transaction after the child renderer is consumed: build the main runtime from the published child rev, run focused Rust tests and contract validators, manually test Ghostty/Zellij magician playback and cleanup, and update docs/contracts if the supported behavior changed.
acceptance_criteria: nix build .#yazelix_ghostty --no-link succeeds without overrides. Focused front-door tests, validate-contracts, and diff checks pass. Manual Ghostty/Zellij test confirms animation, pane frame visibility, and cleanup. Beads are closed with validation evidence and the main repo is pushed only after approval.
notes: Automated validation passed after moving magician source/frames to child rev 78aea588ea0e85954817eef1e90a2df044ceab0a: cargo fmt --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml --all -- --check; cargo check --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core random --lib -- --nocapture; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core magician --lib -- --nocapture; cargo run --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_maintainer --bin yzx_repo_validator -- validate-contracts; cargo run --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_maintainer --bin yzx_repo_validator -- validate-workspace-session-contract; git diff --check; git diff --cached --check; nix build .#yazelix_ghostty --no-link --print-out-paths; nix build .#yazelix_ratty --no-link --print-out-paths. Packaged Ghostty and Ratty runtime trees both link 198 magician frames from the child-owned yzs package. Main repo deletes the previously checked-in GIF and 198 PNG frames, about 11.62 MiB from HEAD. Manual packaged Ghostty/Ratty runtime review is still needed before closing this validation bead and pushing the non-trivial main repo change.

title: Add shared random mode to yazelix-screen
description: Add a random mode/option to the yazelix-screen child crate and CLI so it can choose from the same retained animation-family pool used by Yazelix: Game of Life, boids, Mandelbrot, and magician. Avoid duplicating random-pool policy between yazelix_screen and yazelix_core; either export a child-owned resolver/pool or add a contract-backed parity test in the main repo.
acceptance_criteria: The child yzs surface can run a random screen mode/option. The library exposes or documents the same random family pool used by Yazelix front-door random. Main repo welcome random and yzx screen random stay in parity with yzs random. Crate decision: use existing yazelix-screen code and std; add no new production crates unless this bead is updated with packaging impact first.
notes: Shared random mode is child-owned in yazelix_screen. The exported resolver rotates evenly across Game of Life, boids, Mandelbrot, and magician; yzs random uses it directly, and yazelix_core consumes the same resolver for welcome random and yzx screen random. Standalone yzs magician/random requires caller-supplied Kitty frame assets when the magician branch is selected.
close_reason: Shared random resolver landed in yazelix-screen and is consumed by yzs and main front-door random.

title: Clarify yzx update home_manager current-flake and input-name assumptions
close_reason: Completed

title: Write br-to-bd migration export/import tooling
description:
After the schema mapping is done, write a migration script (Nushell or Python) that:
1. Reads the authoritative .beads/issues.jsonl and .beads/config.yaml from the br workspace.
2. Transforms each br issue into bd-compatible create commands or Dolt SQL inserts.
3. Preserves all fields: id prefix migration (yazelix-xxx → bd-xxx or keep yazelix-xxx), title, description, acceptance_criteria, design, notes (concatenated into description or stored as bd comments), status mapping, priority mapping (br P0-P4 → bd priority numbering), issue_type mapping, labels, dependencies (parent-child, blocks, relates_to), external_ref (GitHub issue URLs), close_reason, compaction_level.
4. Handles dependencies in topological order so bd create doesn't fail on forward refs.
5. Verifies the migrated count matches and spot-checks critical beads.

Decide whether to keep the yazelix- prefix or adopt bd- IDs. Keeping the prefix avoids breaking external_refs and GitHub/Beads contract URLs in AGENTS.md.
close_reason: Obsolete after migration: the br-to-bd migration has already completed, so the one-off migration helper and tracked migration artifacts were deleted instead of retained as maintained tooling.

Verified cargo fmt --check, cargo check -p yazelix_core, config_ui unit tests, yzx_core_config_ui integration tests, upgrade contract, and git diff --check.

title: Improve architecture clarity from 84/100 review
description: Parent bead for the follow-up work from the architecture rating. The repo is strong overall, but the review identified surface-area gravity, stale legacy prose, onboarding/docs load, runtime ownership discoverability, remaining deterministic Nushell seams, subsystem validation discoverability, and pane-orchestrator sync risk as the main architecture drag.
acceptance_criteria: The child beads cover each concrete improvement area from the review; no implementation is required in this parent bead.
notes: Created from Codex architecture rating on 2026-05-09. Keep this backlog internal unless a child becomes user-visible enough to publish on GitHub.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Remove stale legacy config prose from current docs and templates
description: Audit current user-facing and maintainer-facing prose for stale references that conflict with the current settings.jsonc contract, especially wording that implies mutable yazelix.toml or cursors.toml are still supported runtime inputs. Replace stale migration-era wording with the current v16 contract: settings.jsonc is canonical, old mutable TOML inputs hard-error instead of migrating automatically, and generated state is repaired through startup or yzx doctor.
acceptance_criteria: No current docs/templates imply yazelix.toml or cursors.toml are supported runtime config sources; README style rules are preserved; config-surface validation still passes.
notes: Evidence from priming: yazelix_default.toml still has old header prose about yazelix.toml despite AGENTS and the current trimmed runtime contract saying old mutable TOML inputs are unsupported legacy surfaces.
