Historical and contract mentions remain only in migration/stale-config contexts. Verified README no longer mentions atuin or shell.enable_atuin, git diff --check, validate-docs-experience, and validate-readme-version.

title: Evaluate single-file Yazelix settings model
description: Evaluate whether Yazelix should converge most structured user intent into one canonical ~/.config/yazelix/yazelix.toml, with a config UI as the primary discovery/editing path, similar in spirit to editor settings UIs backed by settings files. This is separate from raw native escape hatches and from generated runtime state.
design:
Decision 2026-05-05: adopt one canonical Yazelix settings surface for semantic product settings, not one physical file for every customization surface.

Rule: a setting belongs in the canonical settings file when Yazelix owns the meaning, can validate it, can document it in schema metadata, can show it in the config UI, and can safely write it without understanding an upstream native grammar. A setting stays outside when it is native upstream config grammar, executable code, an opaque pass-through snippet, a secret, generated/runtime state, a large asset file, or a user-owned import that should remain portable to the upstream tool.

Classification of current flat user config files:

- yazelix.toml: migration/source input for the future canonical settings file. It currently contains the main_config_contract fields. The exact future format and filename are owned by yazelix-tlzg. It should not remain a long-lived alternate runtime settings path after the migration lands.
- cursors.toml: first-class canonical settings candidate and first-class Config UI tab. Cursor selection, effects, duration/glow, enabled cursor list, Kitty fallback, and custom preset definitions are Yazelix-owned structured settings. They may be stored in the canonical settings file if the accepted format makes that ergonomic, or kept as a dedicated backing file while still being presented as the Cursors tab. Either way, they should not be relegated to Advanced/raw-sidecar status. If migrated into settings.jsonc, cursors.toml should not remain a long-lived alternate runtime settings path.
- helix.toml: permanent native sidecar. It is Helix native TOML for managed Helix sessions. Yazelix-owned semantic editor choices stay in the canonical settings file, but raw Helix theme/keymap/config blocks stay sidecar.
- zellij.kdl: permanent native sidecar. Yazelix-owned Zellij settings such as widget tray, default mode, popup dimensions, and semantic keybinding ownership belong in the canonical settings file, but raw KDL, native keybinds, and advanced Zellij config stay sidecar.
- yazi.toml: permanent native sidecar. Common Yazi choices that Yazelix understands belong in canonical settings. Native Yazi override TOML stays sidecar.
- yazi_keymap.toml: permanent native sidecar. A future semantic Yazelix action keymap can be canonical, but raw Yazi keymap TOML stays upstream-shaped and sidecar.
- yazi_init.lua: permanent executable sidecar. Lua code must not be folded into the settings file.
- terminal_ghostty.conf, terminal_kitty.conf, terminal_alacritty.toml, terminal_foot.ini: permanent native sidecars. Common terminal selection/config-mode/transparency/cursor effects are canonical settings. Terminal-native snippets for fonts, padding, theme details, and other pass-through keys remain sidecars.
- shell_bash.sh, shell_zsh.zsh, shell_fish.fish, shell_nu.nu: permanent executable sidecars. shell.default_shell is canonical; shell startup code remains sidecar.

Config UI rule: organize by user intent, not file boundaries. The UI should show every schema field whether or not the raw settings file contains it, with state such as set, defaulted/unset, new, deprecated, invalid, and Home Manager-owned/read-only. Cursors should be a normal top-level tab. Raw native sidecars appear in Advanced or tool-specific sidecar sections with status such as absent, active, imported, native fallback, invalid, or managed override. The first UI should open or validate sidecars rather than pretending to safely round-trip arbitrary native grammars.

Save rule: default to delta saves that write only changed semantic settings. Offer explicit materialize-defaults/export modes for users who want a fully expanded settings file. Never silently rewrite user configs at startup just to add missing fields.

Home Manager rule: Home Manager manage_config owns only the canonical settings file unless a separate option explicitly owns a sidecar. If cursor settings are canonical, Home Manager should be able to represent them declaratively eventually, but it can lag behind UI support if that is documented. If Home Manager emits a generated settings file, comment preservation is not required in the generated file; comments and descriptions live in the schema/reference docs and UI. When the settings file is Home Manager-owned, the UI should either be read-only for that file or explain the required declarative edit path.

Import/adoption rule: native app configs remain explicit imports through yzx import or future UI import actions. Yazelix must not silently move or adopt ~/.config/zellij, ~/.config/helix, ~/.config/yazi, terminal configs, or shell rc files.

Migration rule: do not churn the just-flattened config layout until yazelix-tlzg picks the durable file format. Once migration is implemented, old Yazelix semantic settings inputs should use auto-migration-or-hard-error, not a long-lived old-format reader. If JSONC plus JSON Schema is accepted, convert ~/.config/yazelix/yazelix.toml and cursors.toml into the canonical settings file when safe and move originals to backups/migrated paths. If conversion is ambiguous, unsafe, Home Manager-owned, invalid, unreadable, or conflicting, hard-error with clear remediation. Existing sparse canonical settings configs must continue to work through defaults/schema discovery.
acceptance_criteria: Decision completed. The accepted model is one canonical schema-backed settings surface for Yazelix-owned semantic settings plus permanent sidecars for native grammars, executable code, pass-through snippets, and generated/runtime state. Every current flat config file is classified in the design. Cursors are first-class UI/config settings, with a dedicated Cursors tab. Config UI, save policy, Home Manager ownership, direct editing, import/adoption, and migration boundaries are defined. Follow-ups: yazelix-tlzg for format choice, yazelix-xz1z for schema-backed discovery implementation, and yazelix-ryx4 for config UI decision.
notes: Planning correction 2026-05-04: this decision should classify which Yazelix-owned structured settings belong in one canonical settings surface versus permanent sidecars. It should not decide the file format by accident. The durable TOML-vs-JSONC-vs-other decision is tracked separately in yazelix-tlzg and should consume this boundary decision before schema/UI implementation begins.
close_reason: Decision completed: use one canonical schema-backed Yazelix settings surface for semantic settings, keep raw native/executable sidecars, make Cursors a first-class config UI tab, and route format/schema/UI implementation through yazelix-tlzg, yazelix-xz1z, and yazelix-ryx4.

title: Add in-house Mandelbrot frame producer to yazelix_screen
description: Implement Mandelbrot as a CPU-generated yazelix_screen frame producer rather than pulling in a game engine or terminal UI framework.
design: Use the existing ScreenFrameProducer/ScreenAnimationContext seam. Start with deterministic CPU frames or cheap precomputed zoom/pan parameters, mapped to terminal-safe glyph/color output. Keep startup and runtime cost bounded. Do not add Bevy, Fyrox, Ratatui, image crates, or async runtimes for the first pass.
acceptance_criteria: Mandelbrot can render through yazelix_screen with deterministic frame output; frame dimensions and narrow-layout behavior are defended; runtime stays dependency-light and terminal-safe; if performance is poor, the bead records whether to reduce detail, precompute frames, or add a tiny in-house cache.
notes: Rust dependency gate: no new production or dev-only crates. Mandelbrot rendering is small deterministic CPU math and terminal frame mapping, so implement in-house/std inside yazelix_screen. Rejected alternatives: Bevy/Fyrox/Ratatui/image/async runtimes; they add packaging weight and do not improve the non-interactive terminal producer path.
close_reason: Completed: added dependency-free deterministic Mandelbrot frame producer in yazelix_screen, exposed it as yzx screen mandelbrot, and defended dimensions, math, and bounded iteration cost.

title: Document thin Yazelix Helix fork boundary
description: Explain in user and contract docs that the bundled Yazelix Helix Steel fork is intentionally thin and exists to add the config-directory launch support Yazelix needs for managed sessions.
acceptance_criteria: README and editor docs identify yazelix-helix as a thin Steel fork. The Helix managed config contract states that the fork boundary is limited to --config-dir/config-directory support. Docs are verified against local packaging and child-repo evidence.
notes: Source evidence: packaging/yazelix_helix.nix marks repo=luccahuguet/yazelix-helix, steel=true, configDirFlag=true; packaging/runtime_tool_registry.nix notes bundled mode uses the Yazelix-owned Helix Steel fork with --config-dir support; ../yazelix-helix HEAD is 745e2bb6 Add Yazelix config-dir override touching helix-loader/src/lib.rs, helix-term/src/args.rs, and helix-term/src/main.rs.
close_reason: Completed: README, editor configuration docs, and Helix managed config contract explain that yazelix-helix is a thin Helix Steel fork limited to config-directory launch support. Verified with local packaging/child-repo evidence, git diff --check, validate-contracts, and validate-readme-version.

title: zjstatus: allow a small user-configurable text slot
description: Give users a small piece of personal space in the Yazelix zjstatus bar where they can define a short custom string.
design: Keep this intentionally narrow. The likely placement is to the left of the `YAZELIX` segment. This should not become a full templating or arbitrary string-programming system for the entire status bar. A simple configurable text field in `yazelix.toml` is enough if the result feels stable and readable.
acceptance_criteria: Users can set a short custom string for zjstatus, and it appears in a defined place near the YAZELIX branding without destabilizing the rest of the status bar design. Add unit tests for config parsing and rendering helpers, width-budget or rendering checks, and end-to-end validation that logs the configured value, rendered placement, and truncation or hiding behavior on narrow layouts.
close_reason: Completed

title: Strengthen test governance against weak AI-generated tests
design: Replace the default-suite canonical test-count budget with structural governance that better resists low-value AI-generated tests. Enforce small explicit lane declarations on all test_*.nu files, require nearby justification markers for every canonical default-lane test entry, and forbid new generic _extended overflow files unless they are explicitly grandfathered. Keep the runtime budget as the real speed guard. Update AGENTS and the test governance spec so the repo, not prompt discipline, carries the enforcement.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The canonical test-count budget is removed from the default-suite validator and from the governance spec. 2. CI rejects test files without an allowed # Test lane declaration. 3. CI rejects default-lane canonical tests that lack a nearby # Defends:, # Regression:, or # Invariant: marker. 4. CI rejects new generic _extended overflow files unless they are explicitly grandfathered. 5. The runtime budget validator remains the speed guard for the default suite.
close_reason: Completed: removed the canonical test-count budget, added enforced test-lane declarations and canonical-test justification markers, and blocked new generic _extended overflow files while keeping the runtime budget as the real CI speed guard.

title: calling the lazygit popup from yazi fails to find the dir
description: Imported GitHub issue #598.
notes: Investigating Yazi-origin lazygit popup cwd loss. No new Rust crates expected; likely fix should reuse existing workspace/session cwd and yzpp popup request machinery, or child-owned Yazi plugin assets if the bug is in the Yazi adapter.
close_reason: Fixed by child repo yazelix-yazi-assets commit a9f8052, now locked in main. The lazygit Yazi plugin runs both git status and lazygit from cx.active.current.cwd instead of the launcher process cwd, so g l follows the active Yazi directory. Validated with child package build, git diff --check, cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_core_yazi_materialization, and nix build .#runtime_ghostty --no-link --no-write-lock-file.

title: Extend yzx edit to Yazi keymap and init.lua surfaces
description: Follow-up to the picker-based yzx edit surface. Add explicit support for the managed Yazi sub-surfaces beyond yazi.toml: keymap.toml and init.lua under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/yazi/. Keep the same canonical managed-surface policy and avoid exposing host-owned files.
design: Treat this as a narrow continuation of the current yzx edit surface, not as a generic file browser. Keep the supported targets limited to Yazelix-owned Yazi surfaces that are already part of the managed config contract. Reuse the existing target-selection machinery instead of adding one-off path logic, and keep the user-facing names explicit enough that scripting and printed-path flows remain predictable.
acceptance_criteria: yzx edit can target the managed Yazi keymap and init.lua surfaces in the same way it already targets config, packs, helix, zellij, and yazi.toml. Printed-path and interactive selection flows remain coherent, and the command still avoids exposing host-owned files outside Yazelix-managed paths.
notes:
2026-04-09 completion:
- Extended `yzx edit` to cover the managed Yazi `keymap.toml` and `init.lua` surfaces under `user_configs/yazi/`.
- Updated the printed-path regression and CLI docs so the new surfaces are part of the supported managed edit surface.

title: Audit nondefault test lanes file-by-file and remove dead weight

title: Evaluate reusable fuzzy target selection across the yzx CLI
description: Analyze the new picker/query pattern introduced for yzx edit and decide where else it should be reused across the CLI. Scope: inventory candidate commands, compare command-surface clarity vs subcommand sprawl, identify shared implementation opportunities, and recommend where fuzzy disambiguation improves UX without making scripting or discoverability worse.
design: Treat this as a command-surface audit, not as a blanket mandate to add fuzzy selection everywhere. The point is to identify where a small target catalog meaningfully simplifies the CLI and where it would instead add ambiguity or make automation worse. The output should include candidate commands, a recommendation on whether interactive selection, fuzzy query matching, exact aliases, or plain subcommands fit best, and whether the current yzx edit implementation should be extracted into a reusable helper at all.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation for which yzx commands should reuse the new target-selection pattern, which should not, and why. Any proposed reuse points are grounded in command-surface clarity and scripting impact rather than novelty. If a shared implementation seam exists, it is identified explicitly; if not, the bead says so instead of forcing abstraction.
close_reason: Completed: keep fuzzy target selection local to yzx edit and the command palette. Do not generalize it across the CLI today. yzx import, yzx keys, and yzx tutor read more clearly as explicit subcommands, and workspace commands like yzx cwd should keep plain path/query arguments rather than fuzzy target catalogs. There is no strong reusable implementation seam yet beyond the existing local edit target catalog.

title: Audit Rust codebase for canonical form after migration
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done

Yazelix has completed the Rust-forward migration and now has roughly 46.7k tracked Rust lines excluding build output. The risk has changed: the main danger is no longer missing Rust coverage, but carrying too much Rust from migration-era bridges, duplicate command surfaces, compatibility paths, oversized validators, weak tests, and maintainer-only machinery mixed into runtime code.

Perform a delete-first Rust inventory across every first-party Rust source file in rust_core/yazelix_core and rust_plugins/zellij_pane_orchestrator. Every file and major public/internal surface should be classified as canonical, delete, collapse, split, offload, or keep temporarily with an explicit deletion trigger. The goal is to reduce unnecessary LOC and complexity while preserving user-visible behavior and high-value contracts.
design: Use the Delete-First Protocol. First produce an ownership inventory and call-surface map, then execute deletion/refactor slices only where the inventory identifies real waste. Do not split large files just to make them smaller; split only when it deletes duplication, makes ownership clearer, or isolates maintainer-only code from runtime code. Treat tests like code: keep strong contract/regression tests, delete migration-only or duplicate tests once their risk has expired.
acceptance_criteria: Every tracked first-party Rust source file is classified with an owner and disposition; dead or obsolete Rust surfaces have follow-up beads or are deleted; duplicate/over-engineered subsystems have concrete deletion/refactor decisions; low-value tests are identified for deletion or demotion; any subsystem proposed for offloading has a first-principles keep-vs-offload decision.
notes:
Initial corrected inventory, excluding target/: 46,673 tracked Rust LOC across 98 .rs files; 43,003 LOC source and 3,670 LOC tests. Largest source files include repo_contract_validation.rs 3547, zellij_materialization.rs 1810, launch_commands.rs 1587, repo_update_workflow.rs 1438, front_door_render.rs 1360, public_command_surface.rs 1326, zellij_commands.rs 1302, bin/yzx_core.rs 1254, yazi_materialization.rs 1221, repo_validation.rs 1142, install_ownership_report.rs 1105, workspace_commands.rs 1102, repo_sweep_runner.rs 1064, runtime_contract.rs 1036.
2026-04-26 pass update: inventory doc now records 46,583 tracked Rust LOC across 92 tracked .rs files excluding target, with 42,913 source LOC and 3,670 test LOC. Closed 9opk.1 through 9opk.4; remaining follow-ups are maintainer split/offload and future LOC-budget policy.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Build Rust inventory and ownership map
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done

Before deleting Rust code, Yazelix needs a precise source inventory that distinguishes product runtime, maintainer tooling, validators, migration compatibility, tests, generated-state helpers, and the Zellij plugin. The current LOC profile shows several 1k+ line modules and many migration-era names, but raw size is not evidence of waste.

Create a file-by-file inventory for rust_core/yazelix_core and rust_plugins/zellij_pane_orchestrator that records each file's public entrypoints, callers, owned behavior, tests defending it, and disposition: canonical, delete, collapse, split, offload, or temporary.
design: Use mechanical probes first: cargo check with dead_code warnings where useful, cargo metadata, rustdoc/private-item scan if practical, rg-based callsite checks, binary entrypoint routing, and validator/test references. Then manually review every large or externally reachable file. Do not classify code as dead only because rg misses dynamic command names; verify command surfaces, generated metadata, and package/runtime entrypoints.
acceptance_criteria: Inventory covers every tracked first-party Rust .rs file excluding target/; each file has an owner/disposition; every delete/collapse/offload candidate has a bead or explicit no-op rationale; the inventory identifies at least the top 10 LOC modules and all public binaries; no deletion work starts before this classification exists.
notes:
Baseline: 98 tracked .rs files excluding target/. Current source/test split: source 81 files / 43,003 LOC, tests 17 files / 3,670 LOC. High-risk binary/bridge surfaces to classify include yzx, yzx_control, yzx_core, yzx_repo_maintainer, yzx_repo_validator, command_metadata extern bridge, and internal_nu_runner.
Completed 2026-04-26: added docs/rust_code_inventory.md covering all 92 tracked first-party Rust files excluding target, with current LOC totals, owner/disposition, migration-surface decisions, test audit outcome, and split/offload candidates.
close_reason: Rust inventory and ownership map added in docs/rust_code_inventory.md

title: Delete or justify migration-era Rust bridge surfaces
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done

The Rust migration intentionally introduced transitional seams: yzx_core helper commands, generated Nushell extern bridge support, internal Nushell runner routes, legacy config/migration surfaces, doctor repair bridges, and compatibility shims. Some are now canonical runtime boundaries, but others may only exist because Yazelix used to be mixed Rust/Nushell.

After the inventory bead classifies callers, audit all migration-era Rust bridge surfaces and either delete them, collapse them into the public Rust command owner, or document why they are still canonical.
design: Start from concrete surfaces, not vague naming: rust_core/yazelix_core/src/bin/yzx_core.rs, command_metadata.rs extern bridge, internal_nu_runner.rs, bridge.rs naming/API shape, doctor_fix/repair bridge references, legacy config/migration logic, and any record_demo/demo-only command surfaces if present. Apply delete-first: if a bridge only feeds removed Nu code or stale completions, delete it and its tests; if it remains because Nushell is still a supported shell surface, rename or document it as canonical rather than transitional.
acceptance_criteria: Every migration-era bridge/shim surface is either deleted, renamed/documented as canonical, or has a future deletion trigger; tests that only defend removed migration paths are deleted or converted to current contract tests; public command behavior remains covered by stronger route/behavior checks.
notes:
Initial rg candidates include command_metadata sync_yzx_extern_bridge, internal_nu_runner, bin/yzx_core command families, upgrade_summary migration_available handling, active_config_surface legacy root config handling, config_state legacy cache handling, zellij_materialization cleanup_legacy_popup_runner_artifacts, and install_ownership_report legacy wrapper diagnostics.
Completed 2026-04-26: classified migration-era Rust surfaces in docs/rust_code_inventory.md. Kept yzx_core/internal_nu_runner as temporary with explicit deletion triggers, kept command_metadata externs as canonical, kept legacy config/install diagnostics as user-safety guardrails, and removed dead pane-orchestrator plugin config parsing.
close_reason: Migration-era Rust surfaces classified; dead pane-orchestrator bridge config removed

title: Collapse duplicate and overbuilt Rust runtime modules
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done

Several Rust modules are large because they own real behavior, but size can also hide duplication and over-engineering left by the mixed implementation era. The highest-risk files are materializers, command dispatchers, runtime contracts, workspace command helpers, and validator/report builders.

Review the large runtime-facing modules for duplicate data flow, repeated path/env resolution, unnecessary wrapper structs, split ownership between plan/render/materialize layers, and compatibility logic that no longer pays for itself. Delete or simplify what does not survive first-principles scrutiny.
design: Target concrete modules from the LOC inventory: zellij_materialization.rs, launch_commands.rs, front_door_render.rs, public_command_surface.rs, zellij_commands.rs, yazi_materialization.rs, install_ownership_report.rs, workspace_commands.rs, runtime_contract.rs, doctor_commands.rs, config_normalize.rs, control_plane.rs, and runtime_materialization.rs. The goal is not cosmetic splitting; each change must reduce duplicate behavior, remove a stale seam, or make ownership more direct.
acceptance_criteria: For each high-LOC runtime module, record whether it is canonical as-is, should be deleted/simplified, should be split, or should be merged with a sibling; implement the top safe deletions/refactors; verify exact changed commands; do not add new abstraction layers unless they delete more code than they add.
notes:
Initial top runtime LOC candidates: zellij_materialization.rs 1810, launch_commands.rs 1587, front_door_render.rs 1360, public_command_surface.rs 1326, zellij_commands.rs 1302, yazi_materialization.rs 1221, install_ownership_report.rs 1105, workspace_commands.rs 1102, runtime_contract.rs 1036, doctor_commands.rs 942, config_normalize.rs 871.
Completed 2026-04-26: reviewed high-LOC runtime owners in docs/rust_code_inventory.md. Implemented the top safe deletion by removing unused pane-orchestrator widget/custom/sidebar config flow from Zellij materialization and plugin state; left large cohesive owners canonical or split-candidate with rationale.
close_reason: High-LOC runtime modules classified; top safe duplicate plugin config flow deleted

title: Audit Rust tests for real contract value
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done

The Rust migration added useful coverage, but some tests may now only defend transitional behavior, command-existence trivia, stale migration paths, or duplicate snapshots. Yazelix should keep strong tests and delete weak tests that inflate LOC or slow feedback without protecting real behavior.

Review all Rust tests, including inline module tests and integration tests under rust_core/yazelix_core/tests plus pane-orchestrator tests. Classify tests by contract strength, current relevance, and uniqueness. Delete or demote tests that no longer defend a live user-visible behavior, maintained invariant, or known regression.
design: Use the existing test strength rules, but apply them aggressively. Keep tests that defend config/runtime/session contracts, exact failure modes, or real regressions. Question tests that only prove a command exists, only protect removed migration history, only duplicate another stronger test, or only assert implementation trivia.
acceptance_criteria: Every Rust test file and inline test cluster is classified; weak or expired migration-only tests have deletion/demotion beads or are removed; remaining tests have clear contract markers and defend current behavior; focused test commands are documented for each changed area.
notes:
Initial test LOC: 17 tracked test files / 3,670 LOC plus inline #[test] blocks in source. Large candidates include tests/yzx_core_config_normalize.rs 1190, tests/yzx_control_workspace_surface.rs 532, tests/yzx_control_runtime_surface.rs 509, tests/yzx_core_yazi_materialization.rs 219, and migration/bridge-oriented tests around yzx_core command metadata and upgrade contracts.
Completed 2026-04-26: audited Rust integration and inline test clusters in docs/rust_code_inventory.md. No contract tests were deleted; yzx_core_config_normalize.rs is a split candidate, not dead code. Disabled the pane-orchestrator native bin test harness so package-level cargo test runs the canonical library contracts.
close_reason: Rust tests audited; pane-orchestrator package test target corrected

title: Separate or offload maintainer-only Rust tooling
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done

A large share of Rust LOC appears to be maintainer tooling, validators, update workflows, sweep runners, repo sync, release bumping, and packaging checks. Some of this belongs in Yazelix because it protects the product, but some may be better isolated from the user runtime crate or eventually moved to another repo/tooling crate.

Evaluate whether maintainer-only Rust subsystems should stay in yazelix_core, move into a separate crate inside this repo, or be offloaded to a separate repository.
design: Reason from first principles: runtime users should not pay conceptual or packaging complexity for maintainer-only machinery unless the shared code genuinely benefits product safety. Consider repo_contract_validation.rs, repo_update_workflow.rs, repo_sweep_runner.rs, repo_issue_sync.rs, repo_plugin_build.rs, repo_version_bump.rs, yzx_repo_maintainer, and yzx_repo_validator. Do not offload code just to reduce local LOC if it makes releases less reproducible or splits contracts across repos.
acceptance_criteria: There is a keep/split/offload decision for each maintainer-only Rust subsystem; any accepted split has a concrete implementation bead; any rejected offload has a short rationale; runtime package impact is explicit.
notes:
Initial maintainer-heavy candidates by LOC: repo_contract_validation.rs 3547, repo_update_workflow.rs 1438, repo_validation.rs 1142, repo_sweep_runner.rs 1064, repo_issue_sync.rs 670, repo_test_runner.rs 591, repo_plugin_build.rs 476, repo_version_bump.rs 469, bin/yzx_repo_maintainer.rs 283, bin/yzx_repo_validator.rs 243.
Completed 2026-04-26: decision recorded in docs/rust_maintainer_tooling_boundary.md. Keep maintainer-only Rust tooling in this repository, reject external offload for now, and split it into an in-repo yazelix_maintainer crate. Created implementation bead yazelix-9opk.5.1 and made yazelix-9opk.6 wait for that split so the Rust LOC budget codifies the post-split shape.
close_reason: Decision recorded: split maintainer Rust tooling into an in-repo crate; reject external offload for now

title: Split Rust maintainer tooling into an in-repo crate
description: Move maintainer-only Rust modules and binaries out of the product runtime crate without offloading them to another repository. The target crate is rust_core/yazelix_maintainer, depending on yazelix_core for product contract APIs while yazelix_core remains independent of maintainer tooling.
design: Mechanical crate split: move repo_* modules, workspace_session_contract.rs, yzx_repo_validator, and yzx_repo_maintainer into yazelix_maintainer; update CI, nushell/scripts/yzx/dev.nu, docs, and packaging references to use -p yazelix_maintainer for maintainer commands; update packaging/rust_core_helper.nix so runtime builds/tests target only yazelix_core and shipped helper binaries.
acceptance_criteria: yazelix_core no longer exposes repo_* maintainer modules or yzx_repo_* binaries; yzx_repo_validator and yzx_repo_maintainer command names still work through the new crate; runtime package helper builds only product binaries; CI and yzx dev maintainer routes use the maintainer crate; cargo test -p yazelix_core and cargo test -p yazelix_maintainer pass; docs/rust_maintainer_tooling_boundary.md remains accurate.
notes:
Created from the yazelix-9opk.5 boundary decision. Do not create an external repository for this step.
Rust dependency gate: do not add new crates for the split. Reuse the existing yazelix_core dependency set from the moved maintainer modules, and depend on yazelix_core by path only where product contract APIs are needed. Rejected alternatives: offloading to another repository now, adding a CLI framework, or duplicating product runtime modules inside the maintainer crate.
close_reason: Completed: split repo validators, maintainer workflows, workspace-session validator, and yzx_repo_* binaries into rust_core/yazelix_maintainer; updated CI, yzx dev, docs, and runtime package helper.

title: Add Rust ownership and LOC budget after canonical cleanup
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done

Yazelix already has a Nushell budget because shell LOC was a known risk. After Rust becomes canonical and the cleanup audit deletes obvious waste, Rust needs a lightweight guardrail too, or LOC can grow again through validators, wrappers, duplicated command metadata, and maintainer-only helpers.

Add a Rust ownership/budget manifest and validator only after the audit establishes the canonical shape. The validator should prevent accidental Rust surface growth without freezing useful product work.
design: Do not add this before cleanup, because it would lock in current bloat. Use the final inventory to define families such as runtime_commands, materialization, config_contracts, workspace_session, maintainer_tooling, zellij_plugin, and tests. Prefer budgets per ownership family over one blunt global ceiling.
acceptance_criteria: A Rust ownership/budget manifest exists with measured LOC/file counts by family; a repo validator checks unexpected files and no-growth ceilings; the validator is wired into the appropriate maintainer gate; ceilings reflect post-cleanup canonical code, not the pre-audit baseline.
notes: 2026-04-26 implementation start: no new crates. Use existing repo validator patterns and a checked-in manifest; budgets should be family-based and advisory enough to prevent accidental growth without freezing product work.
close_reason: Completed: added config_metadata/rust_ownership_budget.toml, validate-rust-ownership-budget, CI/static gate wiring, docs, and regression coverage for unexpected files plus LOC growth.

title: Load user Nushell shell hook from Yazelix wrapper
description: Yazelix Nushell sessions launch with the managed config but do not source user_configs/shells/nu.nu, so personal Nushell functions such as mi are missing inside Yazelix while Bash/Fish/Zsh hooks are loaded.
close_reason: Fixed by generating an XDG-aware Nushell startup config that sources the managed config and then user_configs/shells/nu.nu when present.

title: Sidebar focus should be explicit, not latent
description: Make the managed sidebar behave like an explicit auxiliary surface rather than a normal focus target. A closed sidebar should not remain focusable, closing it while focused should move focus back to the main workspace, and opening it should not steal focus unless the action explicitly targets the sidebar.
design: Use the managed sidebar as an explicit auxiliary surface, but only if the behavior can be implemented cleanly within the current pane-orchestrator model. Closing it should clear any latent focus on it and return focus to the main workspace; opening it should preserve current focus unless the user explicitly targeted the sidebar. If this requires a large amount of extra focus bookkeeping or layout-specific complexity, it is not worth doing.
acceptance_criteria: When the sidebar is closed it is no longer focusable, and if it was focused the active focus returns to the main workspace/editor pane. Reopening the sidebar does not steal focus by default. Add focused tests for close-while-focused and reopen-without-focus-steal behavior only if the implementation stays small and coherent; otherwise leave the current explicit-focus model in place.
close_reason: Sidebar visibility and focus are now explicit; closed sidebars are not reachable through explicit or incidental movement.

title: Add yzx keys command for Yazelix bindings and tool discoverability
description: Add a small `yzx keys` surface that shows Yazelix-owned keybindings by default and offers tool-specific discoverability helpers like `yzx keys yazi` and `yzx keys hx`. Keep it intentionally narrow: the default output should focus on the Yazelix layer, while per-tool subcommands explain how to view upstream keybindings inside Yazi or Helix instead of duplicating their full keymaps.
close_reason: Implemented yzx keys with Yazelix defaults plus yazi and hx discoverability helpers

title: improve doctor
description:
Imported GitHub issue #589.

### Details

use this text as inspiration, and find the smaller changes we could do that would bring the greatest improvements to yzx doctor

(or a separate command)

"Every good cli tool should have a "doctor mode," which is a special command for the tool that knows all the various things that can go wrong and how to fix them in a safe, reversible way.

A good doctor mode is both a diagnostics engine for checking everything and seeing what's "healthy" (normal) and what looks broken or corrupted, and also an automated resolution engine for actually fixing all those problems.

Often there are various ways to go about fixing problems that vary in how radical the "therapy" is.

For example, you might want to try to repair an existing SQLite database file used by your tool. Or you might decide to start from scratch and regenerate the SQLite file from a corresponding jsonl file. 

Either way, you want to be careful to first back up the existing SQLite database so that you avoid catastrophic, irreversible data loss! A great doctor mode never leaves the patient worse off than at the start, abiding by the doctor's maxim: "do no harm."

My newest skill, /world-class-doctor-mode-for-cli-tools, automates the process completely, making it easy for you to get a truly excellent doctor mode for your cli tool. You can get it on my skills site ($20/month) here:

[https://jeffreys-skills.md/skills/world-class-doctor-mode-for-cli-tools](https://t.co/QerHoeuWNM)

If your tool already has a doctor mode, the skill will diagnose it and figure out how to improve it. If you don't have a doctor mode at all, the skill will design one for you and implement it for you.

The skill is based on my actual, real-world experience designing and using doctor modes in all my various tools, including cass, dcg, ubs, br, bv, rch, and more. I've analyzed where doctor commands fall short and how to make them maximally agent-intuitive.

Here is GPT-5.5's take on what makes this skill particularly useful and compelling:

---

I read the skill package, including SKILL.md, the kernel, phase playbook, scoring rubric, CLI surface, mutate chokepoint, worked example, meta-doctor docs, and current changelog. I also ran its read-only meta-validator: validate-skill .sh passes.

What makes it special is that it treats doctor as a recovery contract for a future agent, not as a loose diagnostic command. 

The central idea in SKILL.md is unusually strong: detect first, fix only through one audited mutate() path, back up before every mutation, record hashes and actions, and require byte-for-byte undo. That turns “self-healing CLI” from a vibe into an enforceable protocol.

The innovative part is the synthesis. None of the individual ingredients are unheard of: health checks, backups, JSON output, fixtures, scorecards, artifact logs. But this skill combines them into one coherent operating system for CLI repair:

- capabilities --json, robot-docs, stable exit codes, stdout-data/stderr-progress, and --robot-triage make the tool legible to agents.

- mutate() plus actions.jsonl, backups, hashes, locks, and undo make automated repair safe enough to run without hovering over it.

- The ten-dimension rubric makes quality measurable instead of taste-based.

- The phase loop mines real incidents from CASS, git history, beads, and changelogs, then converts them into detector, fixer, fixture, test tuples.

- The safety harness proves reversibility, idempotence, crash recovery, concurrency behavior, and detector repeatability.

- The meta-doctor validates the skill itself, so the method applies recursively."

The compelling thing is that it attacks a very real bottleneck in agentic development: agents lose huge amounts of time rediscovering broken local state, ambiguous CLI errors, stale locks, corrupt caches, bad config, partial migrations, or “run this five-command manual playbook” lore. 

This skill says: every time that happens twice, absorb it into the binary. The next agent should run one command, get structured findings, get an exact safe next step, and leave an artifact for the next pass.

It is also deeply accretive. A normal fix resolves one bug. This turns each bug into durable infrastructure: a detector, a safe fixer or refusal, a regression fixture, a scorecard row, a history entry, and sometimes an update to a related manual skill that now points to the doctor first. Over time, the project’s support burden collapses into a self-describing recovery surface.

My short verdict: this is not just a skill for adding doctor. It is a methodology for converting recurring operational pain into compounding product leverage.
notes: 2026-05-22 idea triage: split the broad doctor-mode ambition into focused first slices instead of one large rewrite. Concrete child beads now cover install-owner diagnostics (yazelix-a1ue.1), runtime graphics diagnostics (yazelix-a1ue.3), and a guarded repair framework (yazelix-a1ue.2). Keep yazelix-cfjqc nearby as release/install validation pressure, but do not overload doctor with expensive CI work.

title: Add install-owner diagnostics for profile, Home Manager, and desktop entries
description: Add a focused diagnostic surface for current Yazelix install ownership: default Nix profile package, Home Manager package, unmanaged one-shot runtime, and desktop entry Exec path.
design: Report the active owner, the exact update command for that owner, whether the desktop launcher points at the expected runtime, and what to do when ownership is mixed. This should diagnose first and avoid mutation in the first slice.
acceptance_criteria: Doctor/status output distinguishes profile-owned, Home Manager-owned, unmanaged store/runtime-root, and stale desktop-entry states. It prints exact repair or update commands, covers profile and Home Manager fixtures, and performs no mutation in this bead.
close_reason: Completed: doctor now emits install-owner diagnostics for profile, Home Manager, unmanaged runtime roots, desktop launcher targets, and exact owner-specific update/repair commands. Verified with cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core install_ownership_report::tests.

title: Add guarded doctor repair framework
description: Design and implement the safe mutation layer for future yzx doctor repair actions, separate from read-only diagnostics.
design: Doctor fixes should be explicit, auditable, reversible where practical, and isolated behind a small mutation chokepoint. Prefer diagnose-only checks until a repair action has a clear backup/rollback story and fixture coverage.
acceptance_criteria: A future repair action has a standard structure for preflight, backup or rollback evidence, clear user consent, stable JSON output, and idempotence checks. No broad automatic fix behavior is introduced without a specific detector/fixer pair.
notes: Isomorphism card: preserve current doctor --fix action set, text output, unsupported --json --fix behavior, and nonzero exit semantics. Add guarded repair framework structure only; no broad new auto-fix actions. Dependency decision: production crates none; dev-only crates none; in-house/std plus existing serde types is enough for repair plan metadata and tests; rejected workflow/CLI crates because repair execution remains internal doctor orchestration; packaging impact none.
close_reason: Completed: added a guarded DoctorRepairPlan/DoctorRepairAction framework behind the existing yzx doctor --fix flow. Each automatic action now has explicit consent metadata, preflight requirements, backup/rollback evidence, idempotence checks, and a stable JSON event id, and repair execution goes through run_doctor_repair_action. No new repair actions were introduced; --json --fix remains unsupported. Verified cargo fmt --check, cargo test -p yazelix_core doctor_commands --lib, cargo check -p yazelix_core. LOC scorecard HEAD~1..HEAD: runtime +240 total, delta_code +228; product growth accepted for safety infrastructure, payback tracked in yazelix-a1ue.2.1.

title: Pay down doctor repair framework boilerplate
description: The guarded doctor repair framework intentionally added runtime LOC to standardize repair consent, preflight, rollback evidence, idempotence checks, and stable event metadata. After the framework shape proves useful, collapse duplicated metadata/execution boilerplate or move it behind a smaller typed module without changing doctor repair behavior. Acceptance: reduce doctor repair framework LOC or clearly isolate it in a smaller module; preserve current --fix text behavior and unsupported --json --fix contract; focused doctor repair framework tests still pass; include LOC scorecard.
close_reason: Closed as obsolete: Yazelix no longer uses payback beads for accepted LOC growth. The repo policy now records rationale and affected ownership instead of creating separate payback beads.

title: Add runtime graphics diagnostics for terminal, Zellij, and Yazi previews
description: Add a doctor/status command or section that reports whether high-quality image previews should work in the current Yazelix runtime.
design: Report terminal identity, Zellij fork/upstream marker, Yazi version/source, Chafa probe safety, Kitty graphics passthrough expectation, and the current preview strategy. Keep Chafa ghost-keypress safety separate from high-quality image-preview support.
acceptance_criteria: Diagnostics distinguish safe/unsafe Chafa probe states from graphics capability states, explain unsupported terminal/Zellij/Yazi combinations without guessing, and have tests for representative runtime markers.
close_reason: Completed: doctor runtime report now separates Kitty graphics bridge status from Chafa probe safety and validates Ghostty/Ratty runtime markers plus packaged Zellij/Yazi commands. Verified with cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core doctor_runtime_report::tests.

title: Define active runtime identity and update-status contract
description:
Define the user-facing and machine-readable contract for explaining which Yazelix runtime is active and how it should be updated or recovered.

Design:
Cover runtime root, config root, state root, install owner, runtime variant, feature markers, current executable path, profile/Home Manager/desktop ownership, stale shell or profile state, and unknown/dev runtime cases. This should complement install-owner diagnostics and whats_new rather than duplicating them: doctor/status should explain identity and repair/update ownership; whats_new should explain release-note deltas once identity is known. Keep checks read-only in this decision.

Acceptance Criteria:
The decision names the fields that yzx status/doctor expose, the JSON shape or equivalent typed surface, user-facing messages for stale or mixed ownership, how update owner commands are selected, how runtime_feature markers such as zellij_kitty_passthrough are represented, and which follow-up implementation beads are needed. It must distinguish package runtime, Home Manager, profile install, dev/source checkout, and unknown store/runtime-root states without guessing silently.
notes: Decision recorded in docs/contracts/active_runtime_identity.md. Follow-up sequencing: the parent doctor/status work should consume this contract for broader status output; yzxterm crash reproduction should use the new terminal_launch logs if it reproduces the incident; no extra Home Manager ownership bead is needed because this change updates the install-ownership detector.
close_reason: Recorded the active runtime identity contract, including owner/update fields, Home Manager ownership without managed config, generated-state checks, runtime features, and follow-up sequencing in Beads notes; validated with contract and focused Rust checks.

title: Clarify host-sourced Yazi graphics doctor support
description: Host-sourced Yazi is an allowed advanced runtime_tool_sources choice, but Ghostty/Ratty graphics diagnostics must not mark it as equivalent to the bundled Yazelix KGP Yazi path unless a real capability check proves that. Doctor should allow host Yazi, warn/degrade for Kitty bridge support, and keep missing host command warnings actionable.
close_reason: Completed: host-sourced Yazi remains allowed, but Ghostty/Ratty graphics diagnostics now report it as degraded instead of equivalent to bundled Yazelix KGP Yazi. Host runtime command detection now requires executable files. Verified with yzx dev rust test doctor_runtime_report, yzx dev rust check core, and yzx dev rust fmt --check.

title: Extract shared normalize_path_entries and platform name helpers from runtime_contract_checker.nu and doctor_runtime_report.nu
description: runtime_contract_checker.nu and doctor_runtime_report.nu both define near-identical normalize_path_entries and platform-name helpers. Extract these into common.nu or a shared runtime utility so the bridge layer stops duplicating deterministic path-string logic across separate report modules.
close_reason: Extracted normalize_path_entries and get_runtime_platform_name into common.nu, deleting duplicates from runtime_contract_checker.nu (-25), doctor_runtime_report.nu (-25), and terminal_launcher.nu (-10). Net reduction 35 lines. All syntax validation and targeted tests pass.

title: Audit superseded v14/devenv-era specs and archive or relabel stale contracts
description: The specs directory still contains many documents that explicitly describe superseded v14-era, devenv-era, installer-primary, migration-preserving, Rust-first-v15, or two-lines-transition semantics. Audit that corpus and make the spec surface honest for the current product direction: v15.0 is the live trimmed non-Rust line, config migrations are being deleted from the live contract, later v15.x may gain selective Rust slices, v16 is the Rust-forward release, and historical contracts should be clearly marked, archived, or linked as superseded references instead of reading like live planning inputs.
design: Do not delete useful project history casually. Prefer a clear active-v15-versus-historical split: keep genuinely useful old specs as explicitly historical references, but stop letting them masquerade as current design contracts or as evidence that Yazelix still supports a parallel Classic line, config-migration safety as a standing feature, or a Rust-first v15.0 release.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit inventory of historical versus active specs; superseded v14/devenv-era, migration-preserving, Rust-first-v15, and two-lines-transition contracts are archived, relabeled, or otherwise clearly marked; the docs surface points maintainers toward current v15.0 planning documents first; and future work no longer has to guess whether an old spec is still live.
close_reason: Completed: added a specs inventory, marked migration-era specs historical, updated live v15 specs for no config-migration engine and v15.0 non-Rust framing, and linked the inventory from the spec workflow.

title: Auto-migrate moved Ghostty cursor fields during repair
description:
Fix runtime-materialization.repair so stale terminal.ghostty_* cursor fields from yazelix.toml are migrated into user_configs/yazelix_cursors.toml instead of blocking activation after the cursor sidecar split

## Context
Home Manager activation hit unsupported_config for terminal.ghostty_mode_effect, terminal.ghostty_trail_color, terminal.ghostty_trail_duration, terminal.ghostty_trail_effect, and terminal.ghostty_trail_glow
Those fields were intentionally moved out of yazelix.toml into yazelix_cursors.toml
A repair command should be able to perform this narrow migration because both files are Yazelix-owned config files under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs
design:
Keep strict unknown-field validation for ordinary startup and unrelated schema errors
Add a targeted repair step that detects only moved terminal.ghostty_* cursor fields, writes equivalent sidecar settings, and removes the old keys from yazelix.toml
Do not silently accept the old fields after repair; the old fields should be gone from the main config
Preserve user-owned sidecar choices unless the old main-config field provides the explicit migration source
acceptance_criteria:
runtime-materialization.repair migrates moved terminal.ghostty_* fields from yazelix.toml into yazelix_cursors.toml
runtime-materialization.repair removes the moved terminal.ghostty_* fields from yazelix.toml
Unrelated unknown config fields still block repair
A behavior-backed regression test covers the migration path
notes:
Rust dependency decision: implement this migration in-house with the existing toml crate and small line-preserving helpers. Production crates: no new crates. Dev-only crates: unchanged. Build in-house: targeted extraction of terminal.ghostty_* values, line-preserving removal from yazelix.toml, and line-preserving settings updates in yazelix_cursors.toml. Rejected alternatives: toml_edit dependency for one migration path, broad tolerant config normalization, and local-only config edits. Packaging impact: none.
Implementation: runtime-materialization.repair now runs a narrow pre-plan migration for moved terminal.ghostty_* cursor fields. It writes equivalent [settings] values into user_configs/yazelix_cursors.toml, bootstrapping from yazelix_cursors_default.toml when the sidecar is missing, validates the resulting cursor registry before writing, removes the moved keys from user_configs/yazelix.toml, and then continues through the existing strict repair planner. Unrelated unknown fields still block repair. Verification: yzx dev rust fmt --check; yzx dev rust check; yzx dev rust test runtime_materialization_repair; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_core_config_normalize; yzx dev rust test ghostty_cursor_registry; yzx_repo_validator validate-rust-test-traceability; git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: repair now auto-migrates moved Ghostty cursor settings into the cursor sidecar before strict runtime materialization validation.

title: Define a first-class one-command install UX for Yazelix
description:
Users should have a first impression closer to modern tools like uv: one obvious copy-paste install path, not a repo-bootstrap explanation. Evaluate concrete install surfaces such as `curl | sh`, `nix run`, or another thin first-party bootstrap that installs the real runtime cleanly without reviving the old clone-under-~/.config/yazelix assumption.

Design:
Treat this as install-product design, not as a docs polish task. Compare the trust, portability, maintenance cost, update story, rollback story, and failure modes of each candidate. Be explicit about what the command installs, where runtime assets live, how user config is initialized, and how the installed `yzx` command and desktop entry are produced. Prefer a surface that feels copy-paste simple for users while still preserving Yazelix's real runtime model and Nix/devenv constraints.

Acceptance Criteria:
A single recommended install UX is chosen and justified against the main alternatives. The bead records the exact user-facing command shape, ownership of runtime/config/state paths, and the minimum supporting implementation work needed to ship it. The result should make the install guide shorter and more copy-paste friendly, not just more carefully worded.
notes:
Decision recorded in docs/specs/one_command_install_ux.md.

Recommendation: Yazelix should ship a first-class one-command install UX built around a thin first-party Nix installer app, not a hosted shell installer.

Canonical front door once implemented:
  nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install

Important boundary: the GitHub flake source path used during `nix run` is bootstrap-only. The installer must materialize a persistent runtime tree, initialize user config if missing, install the stable `yzx` executable, and optionally install the desktop entry. User config remains under `~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/`; generated state remains under `~/.local/share/yazelix`.
close_reason: Install UX decision recorded and follow-on implementation identified

title: Install UX: evaluate a first-party nix run bootstrap surface
description:
Evaluate whether the cleanest one-command install UX for Yazelix is a first-party `nix run` surface that bootstraps the real runtime without requiring users to understand the repo layout.

Design:
Assess how thin the required flake interface can stay, what it would install or initialize, whether it can produce `yzx`, desktop entry assets, and runtime placement cleanly, and how it would behave on Linux vs macOS. Be explicit about whether `nix run` is just a bootstrap helper or the long-term user-facing install command.

Acceptance Criteria:
The bead records whether `nix run` is a viable first-class install entrypoint, the exact user-facing command shape if yes, and the concrete implementation prerequisites if Yazelix chooses this path.
notes:
Outcome: viable and preferred.

The cleanest one-command install surface for Yazelix is a thin first-party installer app exposed through a top-level flake:
  nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install

Why it wins:
- matches Yazelix's real Nix/devenv architecture
- gives users a copy-paste command without inventing a second bootstrap ecosystem
- keeps trust and auditability better than a hosted shell installer
- can stay thin if it only bootstraps a persistent installed runtime and then hands off to the stable `yzx` entrypoint

Critical constraint: the flake source path itself must not become the runtime root. The installer must install or materialize a persistent runtime tree and point `yzx` and desktop assets at that installed runtime.

Implementation follow-on belongs to `yazelix-2ex.1.4.2`.
close_reason: Evaluation complete; first-party nix run installer app chosen as viable

title: Install UX: evaluate a hosted curl-pipe bootstrap installer
description:
Evaluate whether Yazelix should ship a hosted bootstrap command in the style of modern tools: a short curl-pipe installer that fetches or installs the real runtime cleanly without teaching users the repo layout first.

Design:
Compare trust, auditability, update behavior, rollback story, macOS/Linux portability, and how the installer would hand off to Nix, devenv, runtime assets, yzx wrapper creation, and desktop integration. Be explicit about what must stay transparent to avoid a sketchy installer story.

Acceptance Criteria:
The bead records whether a hosted installer is a viable first-class install surface, the exact command shape if yes, and the minimum infrastructure or release discipline Yazelix would need before shipping it.
close_reason: Duplicate of yazelix-aa24.3

title: Install UX: evaluate a hosted curl-pipe bootstrap installer
description:
Evaluate whether Yazelix should ship a hosted bootstrap command in the style of modern tools: a short curl-pipe installer that fetches or installs the real runtime cleanly without teaching users the repo layout first.

Design:
Compare trust, auditability, update behavior, rollback story, macOS/Linux portability, and how the installer would hand off to Nix, devenv, runtime assets, yzx wrapper creation, and desktop integration. Be explicit about what must stay transparent to avoid a sketchy installer story.

Acceptance Criteria:
The bead records whether a hosted installer is a viable first-class install surface, the exact command shape if yes, and the minimum infrastructure or release discipline Yazelix would need before shipping it.
notes:
Outcome: not the preferred first move.

A hosted `curl | sh` installer could eventually wrap a mature packaged install path, but it should not be Yazelix's first-class front door yet.

Why it loses now:
- adds a second trust surface immediately
- adds release-hosting and cross-platform bootstrap burden
- still has to hand off to Nix and the pinned `devenv` story anyway
- risks looking simpler than it really is while hiding important runtime constraints

Reconsider only after the packaged runtime and first-party installer app are solid.
close_reason: Evaluation complete; hosted curl installer not chosen as first-class front door

title: Install UX: choose the canonical one-command Yazelix install story
description:
Choose the single install story Yazelix should optimize for after comparing the leading options. This is where the project stops debating surfaces and commits to one first-class command users can copy and paste.

Design:
Use the comparison work to choose one canonical path and demote the others to secondary or maintainer-oriented options. Record the user-facing command, supported platforms, runtime/config/state ownership, how yzx becomes available, and what documentation or packaging changes are required.

Acceptance Criteria:
One install path is clearly recommended as the front-door UX. The bead records the chosen command, why it beat the alternatives, and the concrete follow-on implementation work needed to ship it.
notes:
Decision: optimize for a first-party Nix installer app as the canonical install front door.

Chosen command shape:
  nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install

Why it beat the alternatives:
- honest about Yazelix being a Nix-first product
- short enough to feel like a real copy-paste install command
- aligns with runtime-root and package-ready work already underway
- avoids creating a separate hosted-installer product before the packaged path is stable

Minimum follow-on work:
- add a thin top-level flake interface
- expose an `install` app
- install or materialize a persistent runtime tree
- install the stable `yzx` executable and optional desktop assets
- make update behavior work without a live Git clone
- rewrite installation docs around this front door
close_reason: Canonical one-command install story chosen

title: Recover status cache path across zjstatus command env boundary
description: After launch-scoped status cache env and plain stdout fixes, the bar can still omit dynamic widgets even though the Zellij server has YAZELIX_STATUS_BAR_CACHE_PATH and the cache file is valid. The cache reader command can run with a stripped environment from zjstatus, so status-cache-widget falls back to empty output. Acceptance: status-cache-widget resolves the launch-scoped cache path when direct env is missing but the parent Zellij process has it, without sharing cache state across multiple Yazelix windows.
close_reason: Fixed status-cache-widget path resolution by preserving direct env behavior and adding a Linux parent-process env fallback for zjstatus command execution boundaries; regression covers explicit and derived cache paths from environ bytes.

title: Keep Zellij pane cwd aligned with restart directory
description: Imported GitHub issue #500. Restart currently boots the sidebar from the requested directory but was rewriting Zellij's own default cwd to HOME, so native new panes ignored the restart directory.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Add fast KGP package contract checks
description: Add cheap flake checks or maintainer validators for KGP Yazi/Zellij package-override contracts so agents can verify source-coupled ownership without rebuilding the full runtime. The checks should cover cargoDeps ownership, cleared consumer patch hooks, and Zellij install-check ownership using poisoned consumer attrs. They should be suitable as the default investigation gate before the final runtime build.
close_reason: Completed: added checks.<system>.kgp_package_contracts with poisoned consumer Zellij/Yazi attrs covering cargoDeps ownership, cleared patch hooks, and Zellij install-check ownership. Verified with nix build .#checks.x86_64-linux.kgp_package_contracts --no-link --no-write-lock-file.

title: Add full Yazelix version diagnostics for issue reports
description:
Add a user-facing command that prints the Yazelix release version plus packaged runtime/source identity suitable for GitHub issue reports, and update the GitHub issue template to ask reporters for that output.

Acceptance criteria:
- `yzx --version-full` prints copy/pasteable diagnostic output including release version, runtime dir, runtime variant, source revision when packaged metadata has it, and first-party input revisions relevant to packaged runtime debugging.
- Packaged runtime tree writes the identity metadata from flake self/input revisions.
- Root command help and classifier know the new flag.
- GitHub issue template asks for `yzx --version-full` output.
- Focused tests cover the public root route/output contract.
close_reason: Completed: added `yzx --version-full` as a Rust root route that prints copy/pasteable JSON with release version, runtime dir, runtime variant, main source revision, and first-party input revisions. Packaged runtimes now write runtime_identity.json from flake self/input revision metadata, and the GitHub issue template asks reporters for the command output. Verification passed: cargo fmt --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml --all -- --check; cargo check --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_root_version; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core public_command_surface; nix build .#runtime_ghostty --no-link --no-write-lock-file --print-out-paths; packaged store-path yzx --version-full smoke with stale-wrapper redirect disabled.

title: Add repeatable runtime closure size measurement tooling
description:
Runtime closure investigations currently require ad-hoc `nix path-info`, `nix-store -q`, and shell pipelines. Add a repeatable maintainer measurement surface that reports package closure size, largest NAR paths, direct-reference closure sizes, and optional output-delta summaries without accidentally launching broad rebuilds.

Motivation:
- Initial runtime/cache investigation needed several manual commands.
- A broad `nix build --expr` alternate-shape measurement stayed silent for about two minutes and was killed to avoid an accidental heavyweight build.
- Future cache and packaging work needs reliable before/after size reports.

Acceptance criteria:
- Provide a maintainer command or script that measures an already-built output path and prints:
  - total closure size,
  - largest individual NAR paths,
  - direct-reference closure sizes,
  - optionally duplicate package-name families such as Mesa/LLVM/Python.
- Avoid triggering expensive builds by default; require an explicit flag to build a flake output first.
- Document the command in package-size or maintainer docs.
- Run it on the current installed `yzx` package and record output in the related investigation bead.
- Keep the implementation small and avoid adding runtime dependencies.
close_reason: Added shells/posix/yazelix_runtime_size_report.sh, documented it in package-size docs, ran it on installed yzx and the current git+file default package with --build, and validated docs/config/Nix customization surfaces.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: Implemented `shells/posix/yazelix_runtime_size_report.sh`. It measures realized outputs by default and requires explicit `--build` to realize a flake attr. Report includes total NAR/closure size, closure path count, largest NAR paths, direct references by closure size, and duplicate store basename families. Verified with `sh -n`, `--help`, installed `$(command -v yzx)`, and `--build git+file:///home/lucca/pjs/yazelix#packages.x86_64-linux.yazelix`. Current `--build` default package output measured 4,323,440,624 bytes closure and 884 paths.

title: Investigate idle screen saver not opening
description: The opt-in Zellij idle screen saver settings appear not to work in practice: enabling zellij.screen_saver_enabled should open a floating `yzx screen` pane after zellij.screen_saver_idle_seconds of input inactivity, but observed behavior suggests no pane opens.
design: Focus on live runtime behavior before changing the settings surface. Check generated Zellij config contains screen_saver_enabled, screen_saver_idle_seconds, and screen_saver_style values; verify the pane orchestrator receives those plugin configuration keys; inspect timer scheduling after the central timer refactor; confirm Event::InputReceived/Timer behavior is actually delivered by Zellij; verify permissions and open_command_pane_floating path; and check whether `yzx screen --style <style>` can run from the plugin runtime cwd. Keep the setting opt-in and fail visibly if config reaches an unsupported state.
acceptance_criteria: With zellij.screen_saver_enabled=true and a short valid idle threshold, a fresh Yazelix session opens the configured `yzx screen` floating pane after inactivity; any user input closes or resets it as designed; invalid style/threshold errors remain clear; focused tests or validators cover the timer plan/config propagation; tracked wasm is rebuilt and synced if pane-orchestrator code changes.
notes:
Investigation: manual Zellij probes showed ordinary floating command panes and `yzx screen mandelbrot` can open from the active Yazelix session. The reported symptom matched the screen saver using Zellij floating panes: opening a new floating screen pane can reveal the existing hidden floating layer, so stale floating panes appear instead of a clean screen-saver surface.
Implementation: changed the pane-orchestrator screen saver to hide the floating layer first, open `yzx screen <style>` as a normal command pane, fullscreen that pane, and restore the floating layer on dismissal only if it had been visible before the screen saver opened. This keeps hidden stale floating panes hidden and avoids using the floating layer for the screen saver itself.
Verification: cargo test --manifest-path rust_plugins/zellij_pane_orchestrator/Cargo.toml screen_saver passed; cargo check --manifest-path rust_plugins/zellij_pane_orchestrator/Cargo.toml passed; yzx dev rust fmt --check passed; yzx dev build_pane_orchestrator --sync rebuilt and synced the wasm; validate-pane-orchestrator-sync passed. Manual live probe also confirmed `yzx screen mandelbrot` opens in a Zellij command pane. A fresh Yazelix session is required to load the updated plugin.
close_reason: Completed: screensaver now avoids the floating layer by opening yzx screen as a fullscreen tiled command pane, with wasm rebuilt and synced.

title: Make new tabs inherit the active workspace root
description: New tabs currently inherit a stale bootstrap root, so Alt+m can open panes in the original launch directory even when the current tab visually starts at HOME. Tighten the workspace model so new tabs inherit the active tab's workspace root and tab names follow that root immediately, including HOME-backed tabs.
close_reason: Completed: fresh sidebar tabs now bootstrap the tab workspace root from the actual Yazi launch directory, so Alt+m follows the tab start directory instead of stale launch cwd state

title: Evaluate opt-in idle yzx screen screensaver
description: Evaluate whether Yazelix should activate yzx screen automatically after a session has been idle for a configured amount of time. This is the idle/screensaver workflow for GitHub #420; wallpaper images are only one possible visual payload.
design: Keep idle activation separate from wallpaper packaging. The screensaver should be generic over yzx screen styles so existing animations can work even if wallpaper support is not ready. The yzx screen walls slideshow bead can become a strong visual mode for this workflow later, but idle detection itself remains separate. Until Zellij graphics protocol support is reliable, any wallpaper-based screensaver payload should use the same terminal-safe rendering baseline as yzx screen walls rather than real bitmap passthrough.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear go/no-go decision or implementation for an opt-in idle screensaver that launches yzx screen after a configured idle duration and exits cleanly on user input. The result documents how idle is detected, how the selected yzx screen style is chosen, how users disable it, and which terminal or Zellij caveats apply. If implemented, it must avoid interrupting active commands or editor interaction and include focused verification for activation and cancellation behavior.
notes:
2026-05-01: Connected to the wallpaper image work under yazelix-f71j. The wallpaper slideshow path can supply a future yzx screen visual mode, but idle activation should stay generic and must not depend on wallpaper assets.
2026-05-01: Wallpaper screensaver payloads should use terminal-safe yzx screen rendering while Zellij Kitty graphics/image passthrough remains unresolved upstream. Real bitmap output is a future enhancement, not a dependency for idle activation.
2026-05-05: The settings and pane-orchestrator implementation exist, but the maintainer reports the idle screen saver does not appear to open in practice. Runtime investigation is tracked in child bug yazelix-ah7t.

title: Prepare yazelix_ratconfig for child-repo extraction
description: Do the final boundary pass before extracting the reusable ratatui config editor surface from Yazelix. The current model/editor/render/apply split is close, but the public boundary should keep generic config editing separate from Yazelix settings, Home Manager, generated refresh, and runtime apply behavior.
design: Start from the private yazelix_ratconfig module boundary created in yazelix-w032.12. Identify the generic ratatui config editor core: field model, section navigation, editing actions, validation diagnostics, rendering primitives, and test fixtures. Keep Yazelix-specific adapters in the main repo: settings.jsonc contract/schema mapping, JSONC patch writes, Home Manager/native config status, generated config refresh, pane-orchestrator apply/reload behavior, and Yazelix command routing. Decide crate/repo name, public API shape, dependency policy, fixture ownership, docs shape, and whether extraction should be child repo now or an internal crate first.
acceptance_criteria: A boundary decision and/or patch states exactly which modules move to yazelix_ratconfig, which remain Yazelix adapters, what public API the config editor exposes, how tests are split, and what main-repo LOC should disappear during extraction. Follow-up extraction beads are created if the decision accepts public child-repo extraction.
notes:
2026-05-07 start: doing private internal boundary pass only. Rust dependency gate: no new crates planned; in-house module split/model cleanup only; packaging impact none.
2026-05-07 completion: kept ratconfig private. The private reusable model no longer owns ConfigUiRequest/runtime paths, no longer stores RuntimeApplyMode, and receives native config status through ConfigUiNativeStatus display rows supplied by the Yazelix adapter. Yazelix config_ui.rs keeps settings/schema loading, Home Manager ownership/read-only handling, native status classification, JSONC patching, generated refresh, and runtime apply. Public child-repo extraction is not accepted yet because the adapter is still substantial. LOC scorecard for touched files: +19 net lines, accepted as boundary cleanup rather than deletion; no budget ratchet. Verification: cargo fmt --all --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml --check; cargo check --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --all-targets; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core config_ui; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_core_config_ui; yzx_repo_validator validate-contracts; git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: private ratconfig boundary tightened; public extraction not accepted because the Yazelix adapter is still substantial.

title: Epic: Modular Yazelix runtime packaging and granular Nix customization
description: Epic for GitHub issue 585: support granular Yazelix runtime packaging so advanced users can keep Yazelix's integrated workflow while using selected host-managed tools from PATH instead of bundling duplicate runtime packages. The original host-tool request remains tracked by child bead yazelix-ak2d.2 and GitHub issue 584. Default behavior remains fully bundled and reliable out of the box.
design: Use a typed runtime tool registry as the source of truth. Home Manager is the polished granular UX; lib.mkYazelix is the advanced flake-builder UX; overlays are package-set integration; public flake packages stay curated instead of becoming a large package matrix. Runtime PATH already prepends Yazelix toolbin and then falls through to inherited host PATH, so host mode should omit package/toolbin exports and rely on doctor diagnostics for missing host commands.
acceptance_criteria: Implementation slices cover the public Nix customization contract, runtime tool registry, lib.mkYazelix builder, Home Manager runtime_tool_sources, doctor diagnostics for host-sourced tools, overlay support, curated-preset decision, component-toggle evaluation, docs, and validation. The GitHub issue maps to this single epic bead through external_ref.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Define runtime tool registry for hostable tools
description: Replace the flat runtime dependency/exported-command lists with a typed Nix registry for runtime tools. Each tool should declare its package, exported commands, whether host PATH is supported, whether disabling is allowed, and any feature notes.
design: Keep required bootstrap tools bundled. Start with hostable tools such as lazygit, helix, neovim, yazi, fzf, zoxide, starship, carapace, macchina, mise, tombi, git, jq, fd, and ripgrep. Treat nushell, zellij, nix, terminal packages, core POSIX utilities, and graphics wrappers as non-hostable until a separate contract says otherwise.
acceptance_criteria: packaging/runtime_deps.nix and packaging/mk_runtime_tree.nix are driven by one registry source of truth; default output matches the current full bundled runtime; unsupported host/off settings fail clearly at Nix evaluation time.
close_reason: Completed: runtime dependencies and exported commands now come from packaging/runtime_tool_registry.nix; default export surface matches the previous bundled runtime, host mode omits bundled commands, and unsupported host modes fail during Nix evaluation.

title: Document granular Yazelix install patterns
description: Document the supported granular install and package customization patterns once runtime tool sources and the public Nix builder are defined.
design: Docs should show Home Manager as the friendly path, lib.mkYazelix as the advanced flake path, overlays as package-set integration, and default flake packages as curated batteries-included outputs. Avoid README sprawl; prefer installation or dedicated Nix customization docs.
acceptance_criteria: Docs include working snippets for Home Manager runtime_tool_sources, lib.mkYazelix, overlays.default, and the reason default flake packages remain curated. Docs call out doctor behavior for host-sourced tools and storage-saving tradeoffs.
close_reason: Completed: documented Home Manager runtime_tool_sources, advanced lib.mkYazelix usage, overlays.default, curated package policy, doctor behavior, and storage tradeoffs.

title: Validate public Nix customization API
description: Add validation for the granular Nix API so flake packages, lib.mkYazelix, overlays, and Home Manager do not drift.
design: Prefer cheap Nix eval checks and targeted package builds over broad expensive CI. Validate that default lib.mkYazelix output matches the default package path behavior, overlays.default exposes yazelix, and invalid runtime tool/component modes fail with clear messages.
acceptance_criteria: Repo validators or CI checks cover lib.mkYazelix, overlays.default, default flake package, Home Manager package construction, and invalid option failures. Checks are scoped so Nix CI remains light.
