close_reason: Completed: added validate-nix-customization-api, wired it into CI, and made validate-flake-interface avoid expensive outPath forcing while still guarding default package shape.

title: Expose runtime_tool_sources for host PATH tools in Home Manager
description: Add the supported Home Manager UX for choosing bundled, host, or off sources for hostable Yazelix runtime tools.
design: Expose Home Manager first instead of creating a broad public flake matrix. Proposed shape: programs.yazelix.runtime_tool_sources.<tool> = "bundled" | "host" | "off" for supported tools. Default is bundled. Host removes the package/toolbin export and relies on inherited PATH. Off removes the package/toolbin export and marks dependent features unavailable or warned.
acceptance_criteria: Home Manager has typed options for supported runtime tool sources; default Home Manager output remains fully bundled; the option passes through to the Yazelix package/runtime builders; docs explain that public flake packages stay bundled by default.
close_reason: Completed: Home Manager exposes typed programs.yazelix.runtime_tool_sources, passes it through the shared mkYazelix package path, and rejects unsupported host modes via the runtime registry.

title: Teach doctor to validate host-sourced runtime tools
description: Make yzx doctor explain missing host PATH tools when Home Manager config asks Yazelix not to bundle them.
design: Have the runtime build write a small manifest recording each tool source and exported commands. Doctor should read that manifest and check host-sourced commands against the active PATH. Missing host tools should be warnings or errors depending on whether the feature is required by current settings.
acceptance_criteria: A runtime manifest records bundled/host/off tool decisions; yzx doctor reports clear findings for missing host-sourced commands; default bundled installs produce no new warnings; tests cover at least a missing host lazygit case.
close_reason: Completed: runtime_tools.json now records required_commands, and yzx doctor reads host-sourced tools from the manifest, warning when required host commands such as lazygit are absent while bundled defaults stay quiet.

title: Evaluate modular Yazelix component toggles
description: Evaluate whether extracted or optional Yazelix components should become Home Manager-toggleable modules after runtime tool source modes land.
design: Decision: runtime tool source modes are ready for Home Manager now, but product component toggles should stay future-only until each component has a real disable path. Current classification: required now: core yzx, yzx_core, yzx_control, Nushell/Zellij/bootstrap runtime; optional future: yzs/screen, yzc/cursors, AI usage helper packages, future yazelix_bar/status pieces; not component toggles: external hostable tools such as Helix/Yazi/lazygit, which belong to runtime_tool_sources. Storage impact estimate: host-sourcing leaf tools saves the most immediately because it removes full Nix packages from the runtime closure. Disabling yzs/yzc will not materially save space until the Rust helper stops compiling those crates by default or moves them behind Cargo features/separate packages. Doctor/docs behavior: disabled components must be visible in runtime_components.json; doctor should report disabled feature commands as unavailable rather than broken; docs should keep defaults enabled and explain component toggles as an advanced storage/support tradeoff. Default: no component disabled by default.
acceptance_criteria: Decision notes classify components as required, optional, or future-only; storage-saving impact is estimated; doctor/docs behavior is specified for disabled components; no component is disabled by default.
close_reason: Completed: classified current components, estimated storage impact, and specified future doctor/docs behavior. Component disabling stays future-only until real Cargo/package disable paths exist.

title: Define public Nix customization contract
description: Define the supported granular Nix surfaces for Yazelix so Home Manager, lib builders, overlays, and curated flake outputs do not diverge.
design: The decision should state that default flake packages remain curated and batteries-included; Home Manager is the polished granular UX; lib.mkYazelix is the advanced granular package-builder UX; overlays are package-set integration; curated flake presets are limited and demand-driven.
acceptance_criteria: A contract or durable Beads decision documents supported granular Nix surfaces, unsupported surfaces, naming, compatibility expectations, and sequencing. The decision explicitly prevents a large public flake package matrix by default.
close_reason: Documented the public Nix customization surfaces contract

title: Expose lib.mkYazelix granular package builder
description: Expose an advanced Nix API for users who want flake-based granular Yazelix packages without relying on Home Manager.
design: Proposed shape: inputs.yazelix.lib.${system}.mkYazelix { inherit pkgs; runtimeToolSources = { lazygit = "host"; }; components = { screen = false; }; }. The builder should reuse the same package path as Home Manager and default flake packages, not a second implementation.
acceptance_criteria: flake lib exposes mkYazelix per supported system; callers can pass pkgs, runtime variant, runtimeToolSources, components, and extraRuntimePackages; default arguments produce the same package as .#yazelix; invalid tool/component modes fail clearly at Nix evaluation time.
close_reason: Completed: exposed lib.<system>.mkYazelix, reused it for default Yazelix packages, threaded runtimeToolSources/components through the package path, and added strict component validation so unsupported disabling fails clearly.

title: Make Home Manager consume lib.mkYazelix
description: Refactor the Home Manager module to construct Yazelix through the same granular package builder exposed to advanced flake users.
design: Home Manager should not maintain a separate package construction path. Once lib.mkYazelix exists, programs.yazelix options should translate into the builder arguments, including runtime_variant, runtime_tool_sources, components, and extra runtime packages.
acceptance_criteria: Home Manager uses the shared builder path; default Home Manager output still matches the default bundled package; Home Manager-specific options remain declarative and typed; tests or validators catch divergence between default flake package and Home Manager package construction.
close_reason: Completed: the flake Home Manager module now passes the same mkYazelix builder into home_manager/module.nix, with a direct-import fallback for path imports. Verified Home Manager config evaluation, flake show, and nix build .#yazelix.

title: Expose default Yazelix overlay
description: Expose a conventional flake overlay for users who integrate Yazelix into a package set instead of installing a flake package directly.
design: overlays.default should provide the default full Yazelix package in final.yazelix. If practical, also expose a helper or documented pattern for calling lib.mkYazelix with final pkgs. Avoid hiding granular behavior in overlay magic; the builder remains the explicit customization API.
acceptance_criteria: flake overlays.default exists; overlay-provided final.yazelix matches the default flake package behavior; docs show how advanced users can combine the overlay with lib.mkYazelix when they need granular runtime tool sources.
close_reason: Completed: exposed overlays.default and overlays.yazelix with the default full Yazelix package, leaving granular customization in lib.<system>.mkYazelix.

title: Evaluate curated granular flake presets
description: Decide whether Yazelix should expose a tiny set of curated granular flake package outputs after Home Manager and lib.mkYazelix exist.
design: Decision: do not add curated granular flake presets now. Keep public packages small and predictable: default, terminal runtime variants, standalone yzs/yzc, and maintainer-oriented agent_tools. Granular storage-saving combinations belong to Home Manager and lib.<system>.mkYazelix because the exact right host/off mix depends on each user's PATH and package manager. Rejected now: .#yazelix_minimal because component disabling is not real yet; .#yazelix_host_tools because it would encode arbitrary host assumptions and create support confusion; a large matrix because every package name becomes a public support promise. Revisit only after repeated user demand converges on one exact combination with a clear name, exact modes, doctor behavior, and docs.
acceptance_criteria: Decision records whether any curated flake presets should exist; if yes, names and exact tool/component modes are specified; if no, docs explain that granular customization lives in Home Manager and lib.mkYazelix.
close_reason: Completed: no curated granular flake presets for now; granular customization lives in Home Manager and lib.<system>.mkYazelix until demand converges on one supportable preset.

title: Evaluate explicit import for user Yazi plugin directories
description: Users who set yazi.plugins in Yazelix expect matching plugin directories from native ~/.config/yazi/plugins to be available in the generated Yazelix Yazi runtime. Automatic startup copying would silently take ownership of external user-managed config, so evaluate or implement an explicit import path instead.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix does not silently copy from ~/.config/yazi/plugins during startup; users get a clear supported path to bring native Yazi plugin directories into Yazelix-managed state; the behavior is documented; GitHub issue #594 has the canonical Beads tracking comment and an explanation of the ownership boundary.
notes: Implemented the nested managed Yazi home shape: yzx import yazi imports yazi.toml, keymap.toml, init.lua, package.toml, plugins/, and flavors/ from ~/.config/yazi/ into ~/.config/yazelix/yazi/. Runtime materialization reads those managed inputs, copies imported plugins/flavors into generated YAZI_CONFIG_HOME, and rejects old flat ~/.config/yazelix/yazi.toml, yazi_keymap.toml, yazi_init.lua, and yazi_plugins paths with a clear config error instead of silently ignoring or adopting them. Config UI, doctor/native-status inventory, reset preservation, contracts, README, and CLI docs now use the same managed Yazi-home vocabulary. Verification: cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core import_commands::tests; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core yazi_materialization; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core native_yazi_and_helix_configs_are_available_to_import; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_core_config_ui; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_core_command_metadata; cargo run --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_maintainer --bin yzx_repo_validator -- validate-docs-experience; cargo fmt --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml --all --check; git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: explicit native Yazi import now uses the managed Yazi home at ~/.config/yazelix/yazi/ for config, package, plugin, and flavor state; materialization and docs share that contract.

title: Improve terminal emulator comparison matrix
description: Expand the terminal emulator comparison table with modern protocol and feature columns based on the current supported terminal set: Ghostty, Yazelix Terminal, WezTerm, Ratty, and Kitty. Include graphics protocols, cursor shader support, keyboard protocols, multiplexing posture, first-party package status, and Ratty inline 3D graphics.
close_reason: Updated docs/terminal_emulators.md with a 100-point terminal emulator comparison matrix: 25 criteria at 4 points each, expanded protocol coverage, Ratty inline 3D/RGP coverage, and removed implementation-language and multiplexer-overlap scoring.

title: Evaluate replacing the popup-runner plugin with Zellij 0.44 CLI pane control
description:
Post-Zellij-0.44 follow-up: evaluate whether Yazelix can replace the custom popup-runner plugin with pure Zellij CLI control for popup lifecycle management.

Why this exists:
- Zellij 0.44 expanded CLI automation with `zellij action list-panes` (including JSON output) and pane IDs returned from pane/tab creation commands
- Yazelix currently uses a dedicated popup-runner plugin plus a shell toggle wrapper to discover, focus, and close the managed popup pane
- If the new CLI surface is sufficient, Yazelix may be able to remove one Rust/WASM plugin and simplify popup lifecycle logic
design: Treat this as a simplification evaluation first, not an automatic rewrite. Start from the current popup contract: one managed popup identity, toggle=open/focus/close semantics, no duplicates, and active-tab scoping. Validate whether Zellij 0.44 CLI surfaces can cover that contract reliably without plugin state: list panes in the active/current session, identify the managed popup pane by stable metadata, and focus/close it without racing the open path. Preserve the current shell-side `yzx popup` launcher if it remains the most reliable creation path. Success can be either a real plugin removal plan or a documented rejection if active-tab targeting or popup identity still requires plugin state.
acceptance_criteria: The evaluation ends with a concrete decision grounded in verified Zellij 0.44 behavior. Either: 1. Yazelix adopts or prototypes a CLI-only popup lifecycle path that preserves active-tab scoping, toggle semantics, and no-duplicate guarantees, with regression coverage at the popup seam; or 2. the bead records a clear rejection explaining which popup invariants still require the custom plugin. In either case, leave behind a short implementation plan or rejection note rather than vague future intent.
close_reason: Superseded by pane-orchestrator transient-pane ownership; the separate popup-runner plugin was removed instead of being replaced by a CLI-only popup lifecycle path

title: Make Yazelix Zellij Popup config KDL-native and extract standalone repo
notes: Naming follow-up: public repo/project name is yazelix-zellij-popup. yzpp remains the short Zellij plugin alias, wasm artifact, and bin target only.
close_reason: Completed local extraction: yzpp now lives as a standalone local repo with KDL-native popup specs, raw JSON retained for generated callers, Nix wasm package and tests. Created yazelix-ijlr for manual Zellij smoke test and remote publication.

title: Split managed config contract tests by subsystem ownership
close_reason: Completed

title: Evaluate Ratty as a Yazelix terminal lane
description:
## Decision
Pending. Evaluate whether Yazelix should support Ratty as an experimental terminal lane, and define what "support" means before implementation.

## Research Summary
Ratty is a GPU-rendered terminal emulator by Orhun Parmaksiz, built with Rust/Ratatui and Bevy, with a custom Ratty Graphics Protocol (RGP) for inline 3D objects.

Primary sources:
- Ratty announcement: https://blog.orhun.dev/introducing-ratty/
- Ratty repo: https://github.com/orhun/ratty
- RGP spec: https://github.com/orhun/ratty/blob/main/protocols/graphics.md

Key findings:
- Ratty uses a normal PTY and terminal parser for text, then renders terminal state through a GPU/3D presentation layer. Running Zellij inside Ratty should mostly work as ordinary terminal output.
- Ratty's inline 3D feature is not a normal text-grid feature. RGP uses APC sequences shaped like `ESC _ ratty;g;<verb>[;<key=value>...] ESC \`.
- Apps inside Zellij that emit RGP are unlikely to work today because Zellij does not provide generic APC passthrough or reverse response forwarding. This is the same class of bottleneck as Kitty graphics passthrough.
- RGP support would require forwarding Ratty-specific APC commands from pane output to the Ratty host terminal, and forwarding Ratty support-query replies back to the pane app. That becomes a Zellij protocol/capability problem, not a Yazelix terminal-config problem.
- Ratty also supports Kitty image protocol, but image support inside Zellij remains subject to the existing Zellij graphics passthrough/Sixel limitations.
- Ratty's creator explicitly describes it as experimental and not necessarily a daily-driver terminal. Treat it as an experimental lane, not a default runtime replacement.

## First-Principles Framing
Yazelix should not adopt Ratty because it is Rust or visually exciting alone. Ratty is interesting if it gives Yazelix a stronger terminal-native visualization story while preserving the integrated Zellij workspace. If the integration only works when Ratty is outside Zellij and RGP cannot cross panes, the first support level should be modest: launch/config support and documented limitations.

## Options
1. No support yet: keep watching Ratty until it matures.
2. PATH-provided experimental terminal: allow `ratty` in `terminal.terminals`, generate minimal config only if the surface is stable, and document that RGP inline 3D does not cross Zellij yet.
3. Packaged runtime variant: add `#yazelix_ratty` only after Ratty is packaged and stable enough in nixpkgs or a clean flake input.
4. Deep integration: pursue Zellij generic APC passthrough/reverse replies so RGP apps can render inside panes. This belongs with the Zellij graphics/passthrough decision, not terminal config alone.

## Recommended Direction
Start with an evaluation-only bead and then a small experimental support bead if Ratty can be launched/configured without destabilizing the default runtime. Do not make Ratty default. Do not fork Ratty or Zellij just for RGP until the generic passthrough path is clearer.

## Acceptance Criteria
A decision is complete when it states whether Yazelix will support Ratty as unsupported docs-only, PATH-provided experimental terminal, packaged runtime variant, or deep RGP integration. The decision must name required config/schema/docs changes, packaging source, expected platform support, Zellij limitations, and a manual verification path.
close_reason: Decision: support Ratty as an experimental Linux terminal lane with both PATH-provided selection and a packaged #yazelix_ratty / #runtime_ratty flake variant. Ratty is available from nixpkgs as ratty 0.3.0 with Linux platforms and exposes the needed --config-file, --title, and -e command surface. Keep Ghostty as the default, do not claim Ratty Graphics Protocol passthrough inside Zellij, and require manual runtime verification before pushing.

title: Implement disableable screen and welcome component
description: Implement the accepted screen component toggle from the Home Manager optional-component evaluation. Advanced users who always skip welcome/screen behavior should be able to build a runtime that omits screen/welcome rendering where possible.
design: Start from front_door_render.rs, welcome screen settings, yzx screen command routing, zellij screen_saver settings, packaging/runtime_component_registry.nix, and home_manager/module.nix. Disabled mode should require core.skip_welcome_screen=true, reject zellij.screen_saver_enabled=true, and make yzx screen fail fast with an unavailable-component error. Package savings probably require Cargo feature gating around yazelix_screen or an equivalent Rust split; do not add a cosmetic toggle that leaves the same binary and assets.
acceptance_criteria: Home Manager and lib.mkYazelix expose a typed screen component setting whose default preserves current behavior. Disabling the component removes real screen-related package/runtime surface or the bead records why feature gating is impossible, generated settings cannot enable welcome/screen saver behavior, yzx screen reports a clear disabled-component error, and tests cover default enabled behavior plus disabled validation.
close_reason: Implemented optional component toggles for cursors/screen and off mode for optional runtime helper tools, with runtime manifests, diagnostics, docs, and focused verification.

title: evaluate making the welcome screen skip on any key press
description: Imported GitHub issue #483.
close_reason: GitHub issue #483 is closed; tracker synced.

title: Narrow packaged runtime bin surface to avoid Home Manager collisions
description:
## Problem

When Yazelix is installed through Home Manager or a profile, the packaged Yazelix output currently exposes many runtime dependency binaries through the package's public `bin/`, not just the intended user-facing `yzx` entrypoint. That creates `buildEnv` collisions whenever the user's profile also contains a different derivation that exports one of the same binary names.

Issue #560 is the first concrete report of this class:

```
two given paths contain a conflicting subpath:
  /nix/store/...-helix-wrapped-.../bin/hx
  /nix/store/...-yazelix/bin/hx
```

But the mechanism is broader than Helix. Any bundled runtime tool that Yazelix re-exports publicly can collide with a separately managed package in the user's profile.

## Root Cause

`packaging/mk_runtime_tree.nix` currently symlinks runtime dependency binaries into the packaged Yazelix public `bin/`. The Home Manager module then adds that full package to `home.packages`, so Yazelix participates in normal profile-level binary merging as if all of those tools were part of its public package surface.

That is a packaging-boundary mistake. The real public package surface is `yzx`. The rest of the runtime toolchain is internal product machinery that Yazelix should use inside its own managed runtime environment, not export as profile-wide binaries by default.

## Important Constraint

Users should not have to change `editor.command`, remove Home Manager-managed Helix/Neovim, or avoid wrapped packages just to install Yazelix cleanly. The fix should narrow the public package surface and keep the managed runtime behavior intact.
design:
Treat this as a package-boundary cleanup, not an editor-specific patch.

Recommended direction:
- Shrink the packaged Yazelix public `bin/` surface to the canonical user-facing entrypoint: `yzx`.
- Move runtime dependency executables into a private runtime tool directory instead of exporting them all through the package's public `bin/`.
- Keep the package/runtime root self-contained: Yazelix-managed shells, `yzx run`, popup/editor flows, doctor checks, tutor helpers, and startup wrappers should resolve runtime tools through the private Yazelix runtime PATH, not through public profile-wide package binaries.
- Update both POSIX bootstrap and Nushell runtime-env construction so the private runtime tool dir is prepended to PATH inside Yazelix-managed execution.
- Keep the editor contract stable. `editor.command = "hx"` / `"nvim"` and existing managed-editor logic should continue to work by name inside the Yazelix runtime env; do not push path-specific exceptions through Helix/Neovim helpers unless required.
- Update package/install validation and docs/specs that currently assume public runtime binaries under package `bin/` beyond `yzx`.

Design consequences to audit explicitly:
- `YAZELIX_NU_BIN` bootstrap resolution currently assumes `bin/nu`; that needs a private-runtime equivalent.
- Package/install smoke tests currently assert the presence of many public runtime binaries; they should be rewritten around the narrowed public surface and the private runtime PATH contract.
- Helper flows such as `yzx tutor hx`, `yzx doctor`, version probes, and managed editor launch must still work under the private runtime PATH without depending on public `bin/hx`.

Delete-first principle:
- Do not keep exporting a large public package `bin/` and then special-case collisions one tool at a time.
- First delete the accidental public ownership of runtime tools.
- Then make the remaining private-runtime contract explicit and well-defended.
acceptance_criteria: The packaged Yazelix public `bin/` exports only the intended user-facing package surface, centered on `yzx`; runtime tools such as `hx`, `nvim`, `zellij`, `yazi`, `nu`, shells, and other bundled helpers no longer collide with separately managed Home Manager/profile packages through public package-path ownership; inside `yzx` commands and Yazelix-managed sessions, those runtime tools still resolve through the private Yazelix runtime PATH; startup/bootstrap, doctor, tutor, popup/editor, and managed-editor flows continue to work without requiring user config changes; validation defends both the narrowed public package surface and the still-working private runtime tool contract.
close_reason: Completed: the packaged public surface was narrowed to yzx, bundled runtime tools moved behind private runtime directories, and validation/docs now defend the non-colliding package boundary.

title: Package boundary: narrow Yazelix public bin surface to yzx
description: Change the packaged Yazelix runtime layout so the public package `bin/` surface is reduced to the intended user-facing command, `yzx`, instead of re-exporting the full bundled runtime toolset into profile-level package merging.
design:
Primary scope:
- `packaging/mk_runtime_tree.nix`
- any packaging helpers or package-shape assumptions directly coupled to the runtime tree layout

Goal:
- Stop exporting bundled runtime tools such as `hx`, `nvim`, `zellij`, `yazi`, `nu`, shells, and helper binaries through the package's public `bin/`.
- Introduce a private runtime tool directory owned by the Yazelix runtime root.
- Keep `bin/yzx` as the canonical public package entrypoint.

Non-goal:
- Do not yet solve every lookup path in bootstrap and runtime code here; that belongs to the bootstrap/runtime child bead.
acceptance_criteria: The packaged Yazelix public `bin/` exports `yzx` and no longer re-exports the bundled runtime toolset into profile-level package merging; bundled tools are still present in a private runtime-owned location for later runtime PATH wiring; package shape changes are minimal and do not reintroduce installer-era wrapper complexity.
close_reason: Completed: packaged Yazelix now exports only bin/yzx and moves bundled runtime tools into a private libexec surface.

title: Runtime bootstrap: resolve bundled tools through private runtime PATH
description: Update POSIX bootstrap and Nushell runtime-env construction so Yazelix-managed commands and sessions resolve bundled tools through the new private runtime tool directory instead of depending on public package `bin/` exports.
design:
Primary scope:
- `shells/posix/runtime_env.sh`
- `shells/posix/yzx_cli.sh`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/runtime_env.nu`
- bootstrap/runtime helpers that currently assume `bin/nu` or public `bin/<tool>` lookups

Goal:
- Prepend the private runtime tool dir to PATH for Yazelix-managed execution.
- Resolve `YAZELIX_NU_BIN` and other runtime-owned tool lookups from the new private runtime layout.
- Preserve current command behavior for `yzx run`, startup wrappers, doctor/tutor helpers, popup/editor flows, and managed editor integration.

Constraint:
- Keep the editor contract stable. `hx` / `nvim` should keep working by name inside the Yazelix runtime env rather than by scattering new full-path special cases through editor helpers.
acceptance_criteria: Inside `yzx` commands and Yazelix-managed sessions, bundled tools still resolve correctly through the private runtime PATH; POSIX bootstrap and Nushell runtime env agree on the new runtime tool layout; startup, popup/editor, doctor, tutor, and managed-editor flows keep working without user config changes.
close_reason: Completed: POSIX bootstrap and Nushell runtime env now resolve bundled tools through the private runtime PATH and agree on the libexec layout.

title: Validation and docs: defend narrowed public runtime surface
description: Refresh validation, tests, and documentation so they defend the narrowed public package surface and the still-working private runtime tool contract instead of assuming the old public runtime `bin/` layout.
design:
Primary scope:
- package/install validation
- Home Manager regression coverage for package-surface collisions
- docs/specs/install docs that currently describe the old runtime `bin/` surface

Goal:
- Replace tests that assert many public package binaries with tests that assert the narrowed public surface plus private-runtime-path behavior.
- Add regression coverage for the collision class exposed by issue #560.
- Update docs/specs so the package boundary is accurately described.

Constraint:
- Prefer behavior-level checks over brittle file-inventory noise where possible.
acceptance_criteria: Validation and tests defend the narrowed public package surface and the private runtime tool contract; regression coverage covers the Home Manager/profile collision class behind issue #560; docs/specs no longer describe the old broad public runtime `bin/` surface as if it were the intended package contract.
close_reason: Completed: validation, regression checks, and specs now defend the narrowed public package surface and the private runtime tool contract.

title: Audit KGP Yazi wrapper and unwrapped closure shape
description:
Initial closure investigation saw both wrapper and unwrapped Yazi store paths in the installed runtime. Before treating this as waste, audit whether the current KGP Yazi package shape is expected wrapper structure or accidental duplicated dependency ownership.

Initial evidence:
- Direct runtime references include `/nix/store/l5v2...-yazi-26.5.6` with about 503.2 MiB closure and near-zero wrapper NAR.
- Recursive closure also includes `/nix/store/g17p...-yazi-26.5.6` with about 27.6 MiB NAR.
- KGP Yazi is produced by `packaging/yazelix_kgp_yazi.nix` and installed through the graphics package overlay.

Questions:
1. Is the wrapper/unwrapped pair the normal nixpkgs Yazi package shape?
2. Does KGP override accidentally retain a base Yazi or duplicate cargo/vendor/runtime closure?
3. Are any heavy Yazi dependencies introduced only for preview helpers that could be optional?
4. Does host-sourcing Yazi remain an acceptable storage escape hatch despite reduced graphics support?

Acceptance criteria:
- Trace references from packaged `yazi` to `yazi-unwrapped`, cargo/vendor paths, preview helpers, and graphics bridge dependencies.
- Compare current KGP package shape to nixpkgs Yazi package shape.
- Record whether there is actual removable duplication.
- Create a focused implementation bead only if there is a safe package simplification.
- Preserve KGP image-preview behavior unless an explicit reduced mode is selected.
close_reason: Traced KGP Yazi wrapper/unwrapped references and compared with base nixpkgs Yazi; wrapper plus unwrapped is expected package shape, so no deletion bead was created.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: Audit complete. Current runtime has wrapper plus unwrapped Yazi paths: `libexec/yazi` points at wrapped Yazi, `libexec/ya` points at the KGP unwrapped Yazi. The wrapper references the KGP unwrapped binary and preview helpers (`ffmpeg-headless`, `file`, `chafa`, `jq`, `poppler-utils`, `imagemagick`, `fd`). A base nixpkgs Yazi package from the same flake input has the same wrapper/unwrapped shape. Conclusion: this is normal nixpkgs Yazi packaging, not accidental duplicate ownership. No implementation bead created for wrapper deletion; storage escape hatch remains `runtime_tool_sources.yazi = "host"` with the documented KGP preview tradeoff.

title: Make first-run Zellij plugin permissions clean
description: Investigate and implement a cleaner first-run plugin permission UX so users do not have to focus the broken-looking transparent zjstatus pane and press y. Web/upstream finding: Zellij plugins request permissions from their own load path with request_permission(), and Zellij caches grants by plugin location. zjstatus documents the current focus-pane-and-press-y flow. Therefore one Yazelix pane-orchestrator popup probably cannot directly bundle zjstatus permissions unless Zellij upstream changes the permission model or Yazelix owns/forks the status plugin. Preferred product path: pre-seed the required permission blocks for both stable Yazelix runtime plugin paths before first launch, using the existing zellij-materialization seed_plugin_permissions path or a narrower first-run materialization step. Keep explicit user trust semantics: document exactly what permissions are seeded and provide doctor/inspect visibility. Sources: https://zellij.dev/documentation/plugin-api-permissions, https://zellij.dev/documentation/plugin-api-commands, https://zellij.dev/tutorials/developing-a-rust-plugin/, https://github.com/dj95/zjstatus/wiki/2-%E2%80%90-Permissions
design: Do not try to fake a single cross-plugin permission popup unless Zellij upstream exposes a supported aggregate flow. Instead, make Yazelix first-run generated-state materialization seed the exact permission cache entries for the stable runtime paths of zjstatus and yazelix_pane_orchestrator before launching the first session. Acceptance should include: no broken top zjstatus pane on first run, no manual focus+press-y instructions in first-run copy, doctor/inspect can report seeded permissions, zellij updates still preserve or reseed permissions for stable runtime paths, and denied/revoked cache states remain recoverable with yzx doctor --fix.
notes: 2026-04-26 implementation: runtime materialization now requests managed Zellij permission seeding before every launch, so first run and cache deletion repair write the bundled zjstatus and pane-orchestrator permission blocks before the visible session. Explicit zellij-materialization seeding now also runs on the generated-state reuse path, fixing doctor --fix when configs are reusable. Welcome/install copy no longer tells first-run users to walk to a broken zjstatus pane, while troubleshooting keeps the manual zjstatus/orchestrator recovery path for deleted or stale cache states. Verification: focused runtime materialization test, doctor permission regression, Rust traceability validator, Rust ownership budget validator, Nushell syntax validator, git diff --check.
close_reason: Implemented first-run bundled Zellij permission seeding with troubleshooting fallback retained

title: Unify status/keys CLI visual language
notes:
Dependency decision
- no new crates
- reuse existing `crossterm` only
- build a small shared CLI styling helper in-house so `yzx status`, `yzx status --versions`, and `yzx keys*` share color/TTY behavior without forcing the same layout everywhere

Acceptance target
- `yzx keys*` keeps its table layout but adopts the same visual language and color gating as `yzx status`
- redirected/non-TTY output should avoid raw ANSI noise
- `yzx status --versions` should use the same visual language where practical
close_reason: Moved CLI styling into a shared crossterm helper, aligned yzx status, yzx status --versions, and yzx keys visual language without flattening keys tables, and verified the status/keys command surfaces.

title: Polish Yazelix Terminal user-facing wording
close_reason: Replaced user-facing 'child-owned' phrasing for Yazelix Terminal cursor shader/wrapper docs with packaged Ghostty-style cursor shader and packaged wrapper wording.

title: Specify canonical config surface and launch-profile contract
description: Define the canonical config surfaces and launch-profile contract for Yazelix so downstream work can stop guessing which configuration path owns what behavior and how launch entrypoints are supposed to compose.
design: This is a high-leverage contract bead. Clarify the relationship between direct config, Home Manager, generated runtime state, launch profiles, and any environment-specific entrypoints. The goal is not just documentation; it is a stable contract that downstream beads can depend on when reducing duplication or adding validators. Prefer sharp ownership rules and explicit derivation paths over fuzzy precedence.
acceptance_criteria: There is a documented canonical contract for config surfaces and launch profiles that downstream work can reference. The contract states what each surface owns, how launch profiles compose with configuration, and where parity or validation is required. Dependent beads such as duplication reduction and parity validation can proceed without redefining the model.
close_reason: Completed: added the canonical config-surface and launch-profile contract spec and linked it from the architecture map.

title: Add launch-profile freshness diagnostics to yzx doctor
description: Teach `yzx doctor` to report whether the current cached Yazelix launch profile is valid, stale, or missing for the active rebuild-relevant config and tracked devenv inputs so users get actionable guidance before launch/reuse confusion sets in.
design: Keep this user-facing and runtime-local. Reuse compute_config_state and the existing refresh-reason logic instead of reimplementing launch-profile validity in doctor. The result should distinguish at least: healthy profile, stale profile because rebuild-relevant config changed, stale profile because tracked devenv inputs changed, and missing verified launch profile. It should suggest the right recovery path such as `yzx refresh` or rerunning without `--reuse`. Do not turn `yzx doctor` into a repo-maintainer parity validator; that stays in the cheap validator lane.
acceptance_criteria: Running `yzx doctor` includes a clear launch-profile health result. When the cached profile matches the current rebuild-relevant config and tracked inputs, doctor reports it as healthy. When the profile is stale or missing, doctor reports the reason using the existing config-state logic and points the user at the right next command. The check relies on the canonical launch-profile contract rather than duplicating its rules ad hoc.
notes:
2026-04-09 implementation:
- Added launch-profile freshness classification to `yzx doctor` by extending the canonical config-state/launch-state contract instead of reimplementing freshness heuristics inside doctor.
- `doctor` now distinguishes healthy cached launch profile, stale due to rebuild-relevant config changes, stale due to tracked runtime/devenv input changes, and missing verified launch-profile evidence.
- Added a strong default-lane regression covering healthy, stale-config, stale-input, and missing-profile states through the real `yzx doctor --verbose` command.
- Hardened the new check so it skips cleanly when the active config cannot yet be parsed into canonical config state; doctor still reports stale/migration-aware config instead of crashing.
close_reason: Completed: yzx doctor now reports launch-profile freshness states via the canonical config-state and launch-state contract.

title: Retire legacy shell-block migration checks once the old installer path is dropped
description: Yazelix now carries transitional cleanup and doctor detection for legacy managed shell blocks written into host startup files by older installer-owned releases. This should not become a permanent compatibility surface.
design: Do not use a time-based TODO. Keep one explicit tracked retirement seam instead. Remove the shell-block takeover cleanup and stale-shell-block doctor warning once Yazelix intentionally drops support for upgrades from installs that wrote managed host-shell blocks, the docs/troubleshooting surface no longer routes users through that migration path, and at least one stable release cycle has passed without needing that bridge.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Decide explicitly that pre-profile/Home Manager installs with managed host-shell blocks are no longer an in-scope upgrade path. 2. Remove the transitional shell-block cleanup and doctor checks, plus any code comments pointing at this seam. 3. Verify the remaining install-ownership and doctor paths still describe only supported update/install contracts.
notes: 2026-04-20 delete-first rewrite follow-up: this is now a direct shipped-Nushell shrink lever for install_ownership.nu. Leaving the legacy shell-block upgrade path open forever forces ulb2.4.1 either to preserve dead compatibility logic in Rust or to keep a large Nu ownership island alive. Treat this as a boundary decision, not distant cleanup.
close_reason: Completed: legacy managed shell-block detection, doctor warnings, and home_manager prepare shell-block archiving removed as part of yazelix-ulb2.4.1; upgrade path no longer tracked in product code.

title: Define a package-first macOS launcher surface
description: Yazelix does not ship a supported macOS launcher surface today. `yazelix-0nvb` deliberately removed the stale clone-era `assets/macos` bundle instead of keeping a half-supported path. If Yazelix wants Spotlight/Launchpad/Dock integration later, it should be rebuilt as a package-first surface rather than revived from the deleted clone workflow.
design:
Treat this as new product work, not compatibility cleanup.

Requirements for a first-class macOS launcher surface:
- package-first ownership instead of copying an app bundle from a repo checkout
- launcher resolves the active installed Yazelix runtime correctly
- explicit behavior for default-profile installs and Home Manager installs
- an honest install/uninstall path owned by Yazelix commands or documented package output
- a clear Gatekeeper/signing/notarization stance, or an explicit unsigned-app story
- a defended manual macOS smoke gate

Delete-first guidance:
- do not resurrect the deleted `assets/macos` bundle by patching old paths
- start from the current package/runtime contract and decide what launcher artifact should exist, if any
- if the right answer is not an `.app` bundle, say so explicitly

Sequencing:
- only do this after the macOS support floor is stable
- not required before the Rust rewrite
acceptance_criteria:
1. Yazelix has a concrete package-first decision for whether it ships a macOS launcher artifact.
2. If a launcher artifact exists, it resolves the active installed runtime without clone-era paths like `~/.local/bin/yzx` or repo-checkout assumptions.
3. The install/uninstall ownership model is explicit for both default-profile and Home Manager installs.
4. The docs and manual smoke procedure are aligned with the real launcher behavior on macOS.
notes:
Potential implementation directions to evaluate:
- a generated `.app` bundle built from the installed runtime or flake package output
- a `yzx macos install-launcher` / `uninstall-launcher` surface if Yazelix should own deployment into `/Applications`
- a documented unsigned-app path first, with signing/notarization deferred

Manual acceptance likely needs real macOS hardware:
- install Yazelix
- install the launcher artifact
- launch from Spotlight, Launchpad, Finder, and Dock
- verify it resolves the installed runtime and reaches `yzx desktop launch` or an equivalent owned entrypoint
- verify failures are visible and actionable when the runtime is missing
2026-04-19 decomposition:
- `yazelix-b63b.1` owns the package-first macOS launcher MVP
- `yazelix-b63b.2` owns launcher productization as a real macOS app
- keep `yazelix-b63b` as the umbrella/decision surface instead of one oversized implementation bead
close_reason: Completed: the package-first macOS launcher surface is defined by the closed preview/productization children, with install/uninstall ownership, unsigned preview stance, active profile-wrapper resolution, and actionable failure behavior documented and tested. Verified with macos_preview launcher tests.

title: Experimental package-first macOS launcher preview
description: Build a best-effort package-first macOS launcher preview for community testing. Because Yazelix does not currently have maintainer-owned macOS hardware, this bead must not claim a fully supported launcher UX. The goal is to create an explicit opt-in preview that adventurous macOS users can test and report on.
design:
Scope this as an experimental/community launcher path, not a supported MVP.

Must include:
- a package-first launcher artifact or installation path
- correct resolution of the active installed Yazelix runtime
- explicit behavior for both default-profile installs and Home Manager installs when that can be reasoned about from the current contract
- visible, actionable failure behavior when the runtime is missing or stale
- docs that clearly mark the launcher as experimental and maintainer-unverified on macOS hardware
- a community feedback loop: users should know this preview may break and should file issues if it does

Must not include by default:
- support claims that require maintainer macOS verification
- code signing
- notarization
- DMG packaging
- native-grade polish claims

Delete-first guidance:
- do not recreate the deleted clone-era `assets/macos` flow
- build from current package/runtime ownership only
- prefer the smallest launcher artifact that community testers can realistically try
acceptance_criteria:
1. Yazelix has an explicit opt-in package-first macOS launcher preview with an install/uninstall path or equivalent preview flow.
2. The launcher resolves the active installed runtime without clone-era paths or repo-checkout assumptions.
3. Runtime-missing or stale-launcher failures are visible and actionable.
4. Docs clearly label the launcher as experimental/community-tested and not maintainer-validated on macOS hardware.
notes:
Target outcome:
- curious macOS users can intentionally install a Yazelix-owned launcher preview after installing Yazelix
- the launcher reaches the installed runtime reliably enough for community testing
- docs tell the truth: experimental, best-effort, not maintainer-validated on macOS hardware

Likely work areas:
- launcher artifact definition
- `yzx` macOS launcher install/uninstall preview surface or equivalent
- docs/spec updates with explicit experimental wording
- a community-test checklist and bug-report prompt
close_reason: Completed: added explicit yzx desktop macos_preview install/uninstall preview that writes a Yazelix-marked app bundle, resolves the default/Home Manager profile yzx wrapper, surfaces missing/stale launcher failures, and documents the feature as experimental/community-tested rather than supported.

title: Productionize the macOS launcher as a real app
description: Productionize the macOS launcher so it behaves like a real owned macOS app rather than a functional MVP. This is the polish and operationalization layer on top of the package-first launcher surface.
design:
Treat this as launcher productization, not initial support.

Likely scope:
- code-signing stance and release ownership
- notarization decision and workflow if Yazelix chooses to own it
- Gatekeeper behavior and user-facing install friction
- launcher packaging polish such as app-bundle metadata and release artifact shape
- stronger release/maintainer validation for the macOS launcher path

This bead should build on a working package-first launcher MVP. It should not be the first place where launcher runtime resolution is invented.

Delete-first guidance:
- do not broaden this into generic macOS parity work
- do not duplicate MVP launcher logic here
- keep the work focused on what turns a working launcher into a polished macOS app surface
acceptance_criteria:
1. Yazelix has an explicit production stance for the macOS launcher: unsigned, signed, or signed plus notarized.
2. The release/install story for the launcher matches that stance and is documented honestly.
3. Gatekeeper-facing behavior and maintainer release steps are explicit instead of accidental.
4. The launcher surface feels like an owned macOS app path rather than an MVP-only utility surface.
notes:
Potential outputs to evaluate:
- signed `.app` bundle
- notarized distribution story
- release automation for launcher artifacts
- explicit unsupported/unsigned path if notarization is deferred

This bead may be operationally heavier than it is code-heavy.
close_reason: Completed: documented the macOS launcher production stance as an unsigned preview with a supported-app promotion gate, tightened preview app metadata, and added focused launch-command coverage for owned bundle metadata.

title: Use Yazi as a floating picker from Helix
description: Add a Yazelix-native flow where Helix can open Yazi as a floating picker pane, select a target, and return the chosen path back to the editor. Keep the first version tightly scoped: it should feel fast and integrated inside the existing Zellij workspace rather than spawning an unrelated external file picker.
design: Keep the first slice tightly scoped around a fast file-selection loop from Helix using a floating Yazi surface inside the existing Zellij workspace. Define invocation, selected-path return, cancellation, and what happens when the persistent sidebar is disabled. Avoid letting this turn into a generic command-palette replacement or a second unrelated file-tree system.
acceptance_criteria: From Helix, a user can open a Yazi-based floating picker, choose a path, and return that path cleanly to the editor without breaking the existing workspace flow. The first slice is explicitly scoped and remains compatible with the narrower sidebar-disabled variant tracked separately.
close_reason: Rejected as not planned: the managed Yazi sidebar already owns Yazelix's primary picker loop; a floating Yazi pane would duplicate the sidebar unless reframed later as a general path-picking API or editor return-value contract.

title: Split CI into fast and integration lanes with path-gated heavy install validation
description: Restructure GitHub Actions so strong always-on branch protection remains in place while the current single serial CI job stops paying for heavy install/runtime validation on every push; the main target is to stop validate_flake_install.nu from dominating Beads/docs and other unrelated changes without losing real install/runtime/packaging/Home Manager coverage.
design: Current state: .github/workflows/ci.yml is one serial validate job; cheap validators finish quickly, validate_default_test_budget is about 60s, Check Home Manager flake is about 15-20s, and validate_flake_install.nu dominates runtime with roughly 4m cold-success paths and >10m hangs/cancels. Recommended architecture: keep an always-on fast required lane for cheap branch-protection checks; move heavy install/runtime coverage into a separate integration lane gated internally by changed-file detection rather than workflow-level paths filters; keep nix flake check ./home_manager --no-build in fast unless later timing proves otherwise; preserve visibility so skipped integration work concludes cleanly as not-needed instead of disappearing into pending required-check state. Likely heavy-lane file patterns: flake.nix, shells/posix/install_yazelix.sh.in, yazelix_runtime_package.nix, mk_runtime_tree.nix, locked_devenv_package.nix, home_manager/**, nushell/scripts/setup/**, nushell/scripts/core/**, nushell/scripts/utils/**, and configs/** when they affect installed/generated runtime content.
acceptance_criteria: 1. CI no longer consists of one fully serial validate job that always runs the heavy install smoke on every push. 2. There is an always-on fast lane preserving current cheap branch-protection checks. 3. Heavy flake-install/runtime integration validation runs only when relevant install/runtime/packaging/Home Manager files change, or under another explicitly documented narrow trigger. 4. The design avoids the GitHub Actions trap where skipped required workflows remain pending and block merges. 5. Heavy-lane skip decisions are explicit and observable in CI output. 6. Workflow comments or nearby docs explain which checks belong to fast vs integration and why. 7. Routine CI runtime and Actions spend are materially reduced for non-runtime changes.
notes: Relevant repo surfaces: .github/workflows/ci.yml, nushell/scripts/dev/validate_flake_install.nu, nushell/scripts/dev/validate_default_test_budget.nu, home_manager/flake.nix. External references informing the design: GitHub Docs on skipped-but-required checks staying pending and on passing job outputs between jobs, which supports a detector-job to gated-integration-job design. Observed timings from recent runs: default-suite runtime budget about 60s, Home Manager flake about 15-20s, flake install smoke 4m timeout-triggered failure on cold runner after the fail-fast patch and >10m when previously hanging/cancelled.
close_reason: Implemented: split CI into fast and integration lanes with explicit internal change gating for flake install smoke

title: Clean up tests after recent extraction and boundary splits
description: Audit tests after the recent child-repo extractions and private boundary splits. Delete or rewrite tests that defend old file/module structure, generated trivia, or implementation details, while keeping behavior tests for config generation, launch behavior, bar layout, and adapter contracts.
design: Delete weak tests, do not lower real coverage. Use the repo strength rules: every kept governed test should defend a user-visible behavior, regression, invariant, or durable contract. Prefer fewer stronger tests over broad command-discovery or string-trivia checks.
acceptance_criteria: A test inventory identifies weak tests tied to old structure, obsolete tests are deleted or rewritten, remaining tests pass the governed strength/lane rules, focused behavior gates pass, Rust inventory/budget are updated if Rust test LOC changes, and the bead records net LOC and coverage risk.
notes: Completed deletion-only cleanup: removed generated extern leaf enumeration, duplicated yzx keys output/alias integration checks already covered by keys_commands unit tests, and help-copy checks for onboard/restart. Kept behavior-backed public tests for sponsor fallback, cursor config surface, and reset-config preservation. Net Rust raw lines: -177; tokei Rust code LOC: -161. Verification: cargo fmt --all -- --check; cargo test -p yazelix_core --test yzx_core_command_metadata --test yzx_control_public_commands; cargo test -p yazelix_core keys; cargo test -p yazelix_core parse_restart_args_accepts_skip_aliases; cargo test -p yazelix_core onboard_config_is_valid_current_main_config; yzx_repo_validator validate-rust-ownership-budget; git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed deletion-only test cleanup and Rust budget rebaseline

title: Evaluate headless desktop-launch failure notification
description: After moving Linux desktop entries away from Terminal=true, successful desktop clicks are more reliable because yzx desktop launch owns the configured terminal spawn. The remaining UX gap is failures that happen before the configured terminal can be spawned from a headless desktop click; stdout/stderr may be invisible. Evaluate whether Yazelix should add a small graphical notification/dialog/log handoff without reintroducing desktop-environment Terminal=true dependence.
acceptance_criteria: Decision records whether headless desktop-launch failures need notify-send, a dedicated graphical dialog helper, a terminal-based fallback, structured logs plus doctor guidance, or no extra surface. It must preserve the non-Terminal=true desktop-entry contract and avoid broad optional fallback behavior that masks real launch errors.

title: Add a maintainer refresh path for vendored Yazi plugins
description: Yazelix vendors Yazi plugin code from upstream repositories, but it currently lacks a clear maintainer refresh path comparable to the vendored zjstatus update flow. Add a deterministic maintainer update path so vendored plugin drift is explicit and reproducible.
design: Treat vendored Yazi plugin refresh as a source-management problem, not a user runtime feature. Inventory which shipped plugins come from which upstreams: git.yazi is from the yazi-rs/plugins monorepo, while starship.yazi and lazygit.yazi are separate upstream repositories. Add an explicit source map that records the upstream repo, pinned revision, source path, and target vendored path for each intentionally shipped plugin. Then add one maintainer refresh command that pulls those sources into configs/yazi/plugins/, preserving any clearly-marked Yazelix-local patches instead of relying on manual copy steps or user-side ya pkg resolution. Keep the runtime deterministic: the refresh happens in the repo, maintainers review the vendored diff, and end users just consume the committed plugin code.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The repo has an explicit source-of-truth mapping for each vendored upstream Yazi plugin Yazelix ships. 2. Maintainers can run one documented command to refresh vendored Yazi plugins from those pinned upstream sources. 3. The refresh path makes it explicit which plugins come from yazi-rs/plugins versus separate upstream repositories. 4. The resulting vendored files under configs/yazi/plugins/ are reproducible and do not depend on user-side ya pkg resolution or network access. 5. Focused maintainer validation or regression coverage defends the source-map/update seam so vendored plugin drift is easier to detect.
close_reason: Completed: added a pinned source map for vendored Yazi plugin runtime files, a refresh helper with overlay patch support and dirty-worktree protection, integrated it into yzx dev update, and added a maintainer regression. The first slice intentionally manages runtime files (main.lua) rather than full README synchronization.

title: Full docs stale wording pass
description: Audit README and docs for stale post-v17 surfaces: old keybindings, old layout-family language, old popup/config UI wording, old Beads/bd references, old config paths, old first-party child repo ownership claims, and outdated runtime/package statements. Patch high-confidence user-facing stale docs and verify.
close_reason: Completed stale docs pass: restored the 10-minute Nix install promise, refreshed v17/current runtime wording, clarified ratconfig/config UI and popup docs, updated workspace/keybinding docs, replaced stale pane-orchestrator verification paths, and validated docs/contracts/workspace-session gates.

title: When opening many tabs, the tabs bar stops working correctly
description: Imported GitHub issue #549. With several tabs open and long repo-based tab names, the zjstatus tab bar can scroll the rendered tabs out of view instead of keeping the active tab visible. In practice the bar can end up showing only truncated tail fragments or an effectively empty tab area, leaving the user guessing which tab is focused.
close_reason: Completed: capped the visible zjstatus tab window before overflow while keeping tab indexes visible.

title: Pass nixgl through the Home Manager Yazelix package build
description: The Home Manager module imports yazelix_package.nix with only pkgs, while the flake package output passes nixgl too. On Linux this can produce a Home Manager-installed Yazelix runtime that lacks the packaged nixGL wrapper path used by Ghostty desktop launches, so Home Manager users get a weaker runtime than .#yazelix.
design: Keep one package contract. The Home Manager module should consume the same nixgl-aware package path as the flake output instead of rebuilding a variant that silently drops nixgl. Do not solve this in user configs. After the repo fix, refresh the local Home Manager lock and verify the installed runtime contains the expected libexec nixGL wrapper and updated doctor/runtime checks.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Home Manager builds Yazelix with the same nixgl-aware package contract as the flake package output. 2. A Home Manager build from the local flake produces an installed Yazelix runtime with the packaged Linux graphics wrapper when applicable. 3. The fix is verified by rebuilding the local Home Manager config and checking the resulting runtime/doctor surface.
close_reason: Completed

title: Refresh AGENTS config guidance for settings.jsonc
description: AGENTS.md still says configuration is handled through yazelix.toml and yazelix_default.toml. After the settings.jsonc move, that stale durable instruction can mislead future agents even if user-facing docs are correct. Refresh the agent-only project structure notes so they describe settings.jsonc as the canonical semantic config surface and distinguish generated defaults/templates from legacy migration surfaces.
design: Keep this edit narrow. AGENTS.md should stay an operational guide, not a duplicate user manual. Update only the stale project-structure/config bullets and any adjacent wording that would cause agents to target old TOML paths. Preserve the broader workflow rules: sync Home Manager when defaults change, fail fast, do not silently adopt external configs, and use bd for planning.
acceptance_criteria:
- AGENTS.md names ~/.config/yazelix/settings.jsonc as the current canonical semantic config surface.
- Old yazelix.toml and cursors.toml are described only as legacy/migration context if mentioned at all.
- Default/template references are accurate for the current generator/schema architecture.
- No unrelated AGENTS.md prose churn or workflow-policy changes are introduced.
close_reason: Completed: AGENTS project-structure guidance now names settings.jsonc as the canonical semantic config surface, keeps old TOML paths as legacy migration inputs only, and points defaults/templates at the current schema/contract generation files.

title: Clear magician welcome cells after black playback
description:
The ASCII magician style plays correctly as a standalone screen, but on the startup welcome surface it leaves black cell-background artifacts behind the following welcome text. Root cause: the Kitty frame playback wrapper forces ANSI background black, the child cleanup clears the screen while black is active, and the wrapper then restores default background without repainting existing cells.

Acceptance criteria:
- After magician welcome playback finishes, the terminal cells are repainted/cleared with default background before the logo/welcome text is printed.
- Standalone magician playback still uses black background during playback.
- Add/adjust a focused regression test for the ANSI cleanup sequence if practical.
close_reason: Fixed by clearing magician playback cells after restoring the terminal default background; verified with focused Rust regression tests.

title: Render split cursor preview in cursor widget
description: Improve the cursor status-bar widget preview glyph so it reflects mono vs split Ghostty cursor presets instead of always rendering one full block. The widget should stay compact and terminal-safe while giving users a closer visual hint of the active cursor palette.
design:
Use the launch-scoped cursor facts already cached for the status bar. Extend the materialization/env/cache path only as much as needed to carry cursor family, divider, and two display colors for split presets. Rendering contract:
- mono -> █ using the current single foreground color
- split vertical -> ▌ using foreground/background colors
- split horizontal -> ▀ using foreground/background colors
- fallback, none, n/a, missing split metadata -> current █ behavior

This is a symbolic one-cell preview, not a soft-gradient renderer. Soft split transitions in the Ghostty shader should still render as a hard split in the bar because terminal cells cannot represent the shader gradient accurately.
acceptance_criteria:
- Cursor widget keeps the current compact segment shape and does not add extra bar spacing.
- Mono cursor facts render with █ and the active cursor color.
- Split vertical cursor facts render with ▌ and foreground/background colors.
- Split horizontal cursor facts render with ▀ and foreground/background colors.
- Missing, invalid, n/a, none, or non-Ghostty cursor facts keep the current safe █ fallback.
- Tests cover mono, split vertical, split horizontal, and fallback rendering.
- Documentation that describes the cursor widget is updated if the visible behavior changes.
close_reason: Completed: cursor widget renders mono block previews, split vertical/horizontal glyph previews, and block fallback for missing or invalid split metadata.

title: Fill missing yzx subcommand help descriptions
description: Audit the yzx command surface and add explicit descriptions for user-facing subcommands that currently show blank help entries in Nushell help output. Treat the command surface as a product contract: help text should be present, concise, and useful for every supported subcommand instead of relying on users to guess from the name.
design: Treat this as command-surface contract cleanup, not help-text polishing for its own sake. Audit the real user-facing yzx subcommands that still show blank descriptions, fill them with concise task-level descriptions, and prefer one clear source of truth so help metadata does not drift from the command tree. Avoid verbose marketing copy and avoid testing help-text trivia unless a check defends the maintained command-metadata contract itself.
acceptance_criteria: 1. User-facing yzx subcommands no longer show blank built-in descriptions in Nushell help output. 2. Descriptions stay concise, specific, and aligned with the actual supported command behavior. 3. The implementation does not introduce a second hand-maintained command tree just to improve help text.
notes: Filled blank Nushell help descriptions for exported yzx commands by adding concise comments at the command definitions, not by introducing a second command tree. Added test_yzx_exported_commands_have_help_descriptions to query Nushell's live exported command metadata and fail on blank yzx descriptions. Updated the default-suite count budget and v15 post-trim matrix from 89 to 90 for this deliberate new contract check. Verification: no blank yzx descriptions, run_core_canonical_tests passed 17/17, full test_yzx_commands.nu passed 90/90, validate_default_test_count_budget.nu passed, validate_default_test_traceability.nu passed, validate_syntax.nu passed, and git diff --check passed.
close_reason: Completed: exported yzx commands now have concise help descriptions and the live metadata contract is tested.

title: Simplify startup by making inner startup own generated-layout handling
description:
Follow-up cleanup after the cold-start generated-layout fix. The current behavior is correct, but start_yazelix.nu now participates in managed generated-layout repair before inner startup runs.

This should likely be simplified so the outer startup layer only resolves runtime/config, validates the working directory, bootstraps the environment, and hands off. Generated-layout materialization, effective layout resolution, and final layout validation should live in one place.
design:
Preferred direction:
- remove generated-layout ownership from start_yazelix.nu
- let start_yazelix_inner.nu remain the single owner of regenerate_runtime_configs, layout resolution, and require-existing-layout checks
- keep clear failure behavior for custom or unmanaged layout overrides
- preserve the yzx doctor --fix repair surface independently
acceptance_criteria:
1. start_yazelix.nu no longer contains managed generated-layout repair logic
2. start_yazelix_inner.nu is the single owner of generated-layout materialization and validation
3. cold first-run startup still succeeds when only managed generated layouts are missing
4. custom or unmanaged layout overrides still fail clearly when unresolved
5. the regression coverage added for issue #558 remains green
close_reason: Completed

title: Update Cachix cache publisher action for Node 24
description: Publish Nix Cache is green but emits a hosted annotation because cachix/cachix-action@v15 targets deprecated Node 20. Upgrade the action to the current major line so the cache publishing lane stays warning-clean under GitHub's Node 24 transition.
acceptance_criteria: Publish Nix Cache workflow uses cachix/cachix-action@v17; workflow YAML parses locally; hosted Publish Nix Cache run succeeds without the cachix Node 20 annotation.
close_reason: Completed: updated Publish Nix Cache from cachix/cachix-action@v15 to @v17 after hosted run showed a Node 20 annotation. Verified workflow YAML parses with python3/PyYAML.

title: Proper agent command center IDE for teams of running agents
description: Create a real agent command-center surface for coordinating multiple running agents across one or more monitors, with fast visibility into agent state and the ability to pop open related tools.
design:
Treat this as the main strategic Yazelix product epic: a terminal-native agent mission-control workspace, not a fancier Codex wrapper.

Yazelix already has the substrate: reproducible runtime, Zellij pane identity, Yazi/editor orchestration, right agent sidebar, Beads, usage widgets, install/runtime diagnostics, and session contracts. The product leap is to make Yazelix the place where a developer starts, monitors, resumes, routes, reviews, and shares multiple coding-agent tasks across repo worktrees and Zellij sessions.

Core direction:
- Local-first task lanes. A task lane ties together a bead or GitHub issue, isolated git worktree or branch, Zellij tab/session, agent command, logs, status, usage, validation results, and eventual PR/review state.
- Mission-control surface. Add a `yzx agents` or command-center TUI/popup/tab that lists active and archived agent lanes, shows idle/running/blocked/action-required state, and offers focus/attach/open-diff/run-validation/stop/archive/resume actions.
- Passive-first observation. V1 should observe named agents and route the user to the right pane/worktree. It should not become arbitrary fleet remote control or full scrollback capture. Send-input automation requires explicit policy gates later.
- Beads and GitHub integration. Beads remain the local planning source. GitHub issue/PR refs can enrich state when available. Agent lanes should be able to start from `br` issue ids, GitHub issue URLs, or freeform prompts.
- Zellij-native workspace integration. Agent lanes must use existing workspace/session truth instead of pane scanning. Session resurrection and Zellij web sharing are secondary extensions once local mission control works.

Strategic rationale:
Current terminal and coding-agent tools are moving toward multi-agent supervision, background task lanes, worktrees, and shared workspaces. Yazelix can compete by being a reproducible, keyboard-first, local terminal IDE that makes that workflow explicit instead of scattering agents across random tabs and shells.
acceptance_criteria:
The epic is successful when Yazelix has a credible first version of an agent mission-control workflow:

1. A durable local task-lane registry records at least: lane id, source bead/issue/prompt, agent kind/command, repo root, worktree or branch, Zellij session/tab identity where available, status, last activity, log/evidence paths, and validation summary.
2. A first command surface can start an agent lane from a bead id or prompt, creating or selecting an isolated worktree/branch and launching the configured agent in a managed Zellij surface without disturbing unrelated user work.
3. A mission-control UI or popup lists lanes and supports high-confidence actions: focus/attach to the lane, open diff/status, run configured validation, stop/archive, and resume when the underlying agent supports it.
4. The implementation is passive-first: it observes process/session/log/git state and routes the human to the right surface. Any send-input or autonomous fleet control is deferred behind a documented policy gate.
5. Beads/Git/GitHub state is linked but not conflated: Beads own local planning metadata, git owns branches/worktrees/diffs, GitHub owns public issue/PR lifecycle when present.
6. Doctor or maintainer diagnostics can identify stale, missing, or broken agent lanes and give actionable cleanup guidance.
7. Tests cover task-lane state transitions, action routing, stale-lane diagnostics, and worktree/session safety. End-to-end validation logs visible lanes, derived status, and invoked workspace actions.
notes:
2026-05-21: V1 tab-local agent sidebar shipped under yazelix-gxti / GitHub #587. Current baseline: host-installed Codex launched by yzx agent, managed right sidebar toggled with Alt Shift L, editor/right-agent focus on Ctrl Shift Y, left Yazi/editor focus remains Ctrl y, and generalized role/placement config is deferred. Keep this issue open for the broader multi-agent command-center / mission-control design, not for the basic single-agent sidebar.

2026-05-22 Frankenterm evaluation outcome from yazelix-lytcv: borrow ideas, do not adopt. The useful direction is a small Yazelix-native, Zellij-centered, passive-first agent state/control contract with clear policy gates. Do not import a WezTerm-only watcher, full scrollback capture/search, or arbitrary send-input fleet control as the next step. New child yazelix-bl3.2 captures the minimal observation-contract decision.

2026-05-23 strategic promotion: this is now the highest-leverage big product change candidate for Yazelix. The thesis is that Yazelix should become a terminal-native mission control workspace for multiple coding agents. Evidence from current tooling direction: Codex app, Warp, Cursor background agents, and GitHub coding-agent surfaces all point toward supervising multiple agents, background tasks, worktrees, and task progress. Yazelix already owns the local terminal workspace substrate better than most wrappers: Zellij session identity, managed panes, Yazi/editor handoff, Beads, usage widgets, and reproducible Nix runtime.

First slice should stay boring and strong: local task-lane registry, worktree-backed launches, mission-control listing, focus/attach/diff/validate/archive actions, and stale-lane diagnostics. Do not begin with generic remote-control automation, agent-provider abstraction sprawl, or team sharing. Those only become valuable after local lanes are reliable.

title: Decide mission-control tab vs tab-local agent sidebar
description: Decide whether Yazelix agent management should first ship as a dedicated mission-control tab or a tab-local sidebar surface, with current bias toward mission control as the higher-value and lower-hanging first slice.
design: Reason from scope first. A mission-control tab is probably the better first slice because it has more room for agent state, actions, and future orchestration, while avoiding premature sidebar overloading. Treat a tab-local agent sidebar as a possible later adjunct, not the default first target. This decision work should define which concerns are tab-local versus session-global, whether agents belong in a narrow side surface, and how a future sidebar would relate to the broader command-center story. Keep session-management sidebar ideas out of the first decision unless they materially change the surface choice.
acceptance_criteria: There is a documented decision on whether the first real agent-management surface should be a mission-control tab, a tab-local agent sidebar, or both in staged order. The decision explicitly classifies which agent operations are tab-local versus global, explains why the chosen first surface is the better product slice, and leaves a clear follow-up path for any deferred sidebar idea without hardcoding the current sidebar system around Yazi forever.
notes: Decision superseded/answered by the completed role-placement architecture decisions: first implementation should be tab-local agent in right_sidebar, not a mission-control-first surface. Mission-control remains future/global agent-management work under the parent epic, but it should not block the first Codex agent pane slice. See docs/role_based_pane_placement_decision.md and docs/role_placement_config_decision.md.
close_reason: Completed by h0kp decision set: first agent implementation is tab-local agent in right_sidebar. Mission-control remains future/global agent-management work under the parent epic and should not block the first right_sidebar Codex pane slice.

title: Define minimal Yazelix agent command-center observation contract
description: Define the smallest Yazelix-native state/control contract for a future agent command center, borrowing the useful Frankenterm ideas without adopting Frankenterm or adding a WezTerm-only watcher.
design: Start from Yazelix's existing Zellij role/placement model: a managed right-side agent pane, role identity, status-bar agent usage widgets, and explicit focus/toggle actions. V1 should be passive-first and session-local: expose which managed agent panes exist, their role/title/tab/session identity, rough liveness/status if already available, and safe focus/toggle/reveal actions. Do not capture and index all scrollback, do not send arbitrary input to agents, do not require WezTerm, do not introduce a long-running observer daemon unless a later design proves it is needed.
acceptance_criteria: The decision names the V1 JSON/status surface, supported actions, trust boundaries, redaction/privacy rules, and non-goals. It states whether any action besides focus/toggle/reveal needs an approval gate. It explicitly compares against Frankenterm-style fleet capture/search/send and rejects any surface that would duplicate Zellij or require a terminal-specific backend.

title: Decide explicit full Yazelix config policy
description: Evaluate whether Yazelix should move away from comment/uncomment setting discovery and toward explicit mandatory settings in the user-facing config. The goal is a clearer config surface where users can see the real active values without guessing which commented examples matter.
design:
Decision 2026-05-04: use staged explicit main-config materialization, not mandatory runtime fields.

Yazelix should move new/generated main configs toward a fully explicit ~/.config/yazelix/yazelix.toml where users can see the real active values without uncommenting examples. This should apply to first-run bootstrap, yzx reset config, and onboard/config generation. It should not make every field mandatory at runtime: older and hand-written partial configs must continue to parse, validate, and receive defaults from the main config contract.

Preserve comments as product documentation. Do not solve explicitness by round-tripping through a generic TOML serializer that drops the explanatory comments in yazelix_default.toml. The durable implementation should use a canonical commented template or schema/template renderer that keeps comments near the values they describe.

Keep sidecars opt-in. cursors.toml, helix.toml, zellij.kdl, yazi.toml, yazi_keymap.toml, yazi_init.lua, terminal_*, and shell_* should not all become mandatory just because the main config becomes explicit. Materialize a sidecar only when that sidecar already has a product reason to exist.

Existing users should not be silently rewritten. Doctor or normalize may offer an explicit completion/update path for sparse configs, but startup should not rewrite a user config just to expand defaults.

Home Manager manage_config should follow the explicit value contract where possible. If Home Manager cannot preserve rich comments in generated TOML, the module/docs should say that clearly and point users at the shipped commented reference.

The future Ratatui/config-editor work should treat the main config contract and comments/reference text as one source of truth. Saving through a UI must either preserve comments or keep comments in a generated/reference companion with a deliberate tradeoff.
acceptance_criteria: Decision completed. Policy: staged explicit materialization for the main config; tolerant runtime parsing for partial/older configs; comments preserved as documentation; sidecars stay opt-in; existing configs are not silently expanded; Home Manager behavior must be explicit about comment limitations. Follow-up implementation tracked in yazelix-xz1z. Ratatui/config-editor evaluation remains in yazelix-ryx4.
