description:
Problem:
The current pin/update flow tracks Yazelix and devenv version movement, but it does not treat the host/bootstrap Nushell version as an explicit pinned surface. Recent installer breakage on Nushell 0.108 showed that Nushell version drift matters during bootstrap and should be part of the maintained update story.

Design:
Define Nushell version as an explicit pinned tool surface in the same workflow that updates maintained pins, and extend the maintainer-facing update automation so `yzx dev update` (or the relevant subcommand in that surface) can refresh the Nushell pin and any corresponding lock/config references intentionally. The workflow should make it obvious when Yazelix runtime behavior depends on a host/bootstrap Nu floor versus the managed environment Nu version.

Acceptance Criteria:
- The maintained pin/update workflow explicitly includes Nushell version handling.
- `yzx dev update` (or the relevant maintainer update path) can update the Nushell pin without manual scattered edits.
- The implementation keeps Home Manager/default config/runtime pin metadata coherent where applicable.
- The command/docs make clear which Nushell version is bootstrap-sensitive versus runtime-managed.
- The workflow fails clearly if the Nushell pin cannot be updated consistently.
close_reason: Completed: maintainer update now syncs both PINNED_NIX_VERSION and PINNED_NUSHELL_VERSION from the locked nixpkgs input, with docs and maintainer regression coverage.

title: Make boids sprites point with filled triangles
description: The dotted V-shaped boids are too abstract. Update the boids sprite vocabulary so creatures visibly point in their movement direction using filled triangle glyphs such as ◀, ▶, ▲, ▼, and filled corner triangles for diagonals, while keeping predator/prey color and size distinctions.
design: Keep the existing 8-way direction classifier and sprite renderer. Replace abstract braille-only prey/predator glyph tables with filled geometric triangle glyphs. Cardinal directions use ◀, ▶, ▲, and ▼; diagonals use ◤, ◥, ◢, and ◣. Preserve the larger predator footprint and existing behavior tests, updating visual tests to assert filled directional glyphs.
acceptance_criteria: 1. East and west boids visibly point with ▶ and ◀ glyphs. 2. North and south boids visibly point with ▲ and ▼ glyphs. 3. Diagonal directions use distinct filled corner triangles. 4. Predator remains visually larger than prey. 5. Boids tests pass.
close_reason: Completed: boids now use filled directional triangle prey glyphs with ◀ ▶ ▲ ▼ and filled corner diagonals, while predator uses a larger red body-plus-tip sprite with a large circle body. Tests defend cardinal and diagonal glyph selection, predator/prey visual size split, and frame dimensions.

title: Move sidebar Yazi identity fully into the pane orchestrator
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done

The current workspace model still keeps a sidebar Yazi cache on disk because the pane orchestrator does not fully own Yazi instance identity and cwd. That cache is explicitly treated as an integration cache in the workspace contract, which means the architecture already wants it to be secondary rather than permanent.

Explore the path to making sidebar Yazi identity and cwd fully plugin-owned so the filesystem cache can be deleted or reduced to a narrow fallback. The goal is to simplify the workspace model and remove another source of state drift between the live session and the cache layer.
acceptance_criteria: Sidebar Yazi pane identity and cwd are owned or derived by the pane orchestrator; any remaining filesystem cache is narrow and secondary; tab-locality is defended so one tab cannot pick another tab sidebar state by recency; current Yazi file tree behavior remains unchanged for users.
notes: 2026-04-26 implementation start: no new crates. Use existing pane-orchestrator/session-state Rust surfaces; keep current user Yazi behavior unchanged while removing recency-based sidebar cache as the primary identity source.
close_reason: Completed: sidebar Yazi identity/cwd now publish only into the pane orchestrator; the old filesystem identity cache was removed from the Yazi adapter and the ownership docs were updated

title: Move managed Zellij config into user_configs/zellij/config.kdl
description: Make Yazelix-managed Zellij config self-contained under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/zellij/config.kdl. In managed mode, stop implicitly reading ~/.config/zellij/config.kdl as the default input surface. Instead, treat native Zellij config as an explicit import/migration source or escape hatch. Keep the generated merged runtime config in ~/.local/share/yazelix/configs/zellij/config.kdl. Update config loading, migration/doctor flows, docs, and verification so the user mental model matches Yazi and the broader user_configs architecture. Include strong behavior tests for managed-path loading, native-config migration/import, and generated runtime output.
close_reason: Completed

title: Allow Home Manager to install extra bundled terminal variants
description: Home Manager currently selects one packaged terminal runtime variant, but users may want a yzxterm primary runtime while also installing Ghostty as a bundled Yazelix-managed terminal fallback. Add a declarative HM option for extra bundled terminal variants that installs terminal packages without adding duplicate yzx wrappers.
close_reason: Completed: added programs.yazelix.extra_terminal_variants to install additional bundled terminal emulator packages beside the primary Home Manager runtime without duplicate yzx wrappers, passed the yzxterm+Ghostty default order through generated settings, documented the option, and added Nix customization validation. Verified with cargo fmt --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml --all --check, cargo check --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_maintainer, two focused nix evals, validate-nix-customization-api, validate-config-surface-contract, validate-flake-interface, and validate-upgrade-contract.

title: Add off mode for optional runtime helper tools
description: Extend runtime_tool_sources beyond host mode for optional helper tools where turning a tool off saves closure size without pretending the feature still exists. This covers helper tools rather than child repos, but it is the clearest near-term storage win from the optional-component evaluation.
design: Start with tools that are not bootstrap-critical: macchina, p7zip, poppler, resvg, and possibly lazygit. For each accepted off-capable tool, define generated-config behavior and doctor/status language. For example, macchina off should require or imply core.show_macchina_on_welcome=false; Yazi preview helpers off should adjust previews or report intentional absence instead of leaving generated config that calls missing commands. Keep host mode unchanged.
acceptance_criteria: runtime_tool_sources supports off for a documented first slice of optional helper tools. Disabled tools are omitted from runtime packages/exports, runtime_tools.json records source=off, generated config or preflight does not reference intentionally disabled helpers without a clear diagnostic, yzx doctor distinguishes disabled from missing, and focused tests or Nix eval checks cover host/off/bundled validation.
close_reason: Implemented optional component toggles for cursors/screen and off mode for optional runtime helper tools, with runtime manifests, diagnostics, docs, and focused verification.

title: Add configurable Ghostty cursor trail glow levels
description: Add a first-class config option for the glow around Ghostty cursor trails and related cursor effects. Use 'glow' as the canonical term rather than 'intensity' or 'halo'. The setting should control the spatial aura around the cursor/trail, not the underlying trail color itself. A value of 'none' should keep the trail color/effect but remove the surrounding glow. Proposed levels: none, low, medium, high. Proposed default: medium, matching the current feel closely enough that existing visuals still feel like Yazelix by default.
design: Treat this as a Ghostty shader/config contract first, not a vague visual-preference knob. The setting should only control the outer glow or aura around the cursor/trail, while preserving the underlying trail color/effect. Use 'glow' as the user-facing term. Start with a small discrete enum: none, low, medium, high. The current visual behavior should map roughly to medium so existing Yazelix visuals do not suddenly become more aggressive by default. The implementation should work across the supported Ghostty trail and mode effects, or clearly document any effect families that cannot share the same glow semantics without awkward one-off hacks.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix exposes a documented Ghostty cursor-trail glow setting with values none, low, medium, high. Setting glow to none keeps the cursor/trail color effect while removing the surrounding spatial glow. Medium is the default and should preserve the current feel closely enough that the default user experience does not regress unexpectedly. Add focused validation for config parsing and generated Ghostty config or shader selection, and document which Ghostty effect families honor the glow setting if any exceptions remain.
close_reason: Completed

title: Fix pane-orchestrator permission limbo after plugin reload
description: Document and fix the failure mode where changing the pane-orchestrator plugin can trigger Zellij's permission popup inside an existing Yazelix window in a broken state that cannot be accepted. After that partial reload, launching Yazelix again can land in a blank-screen limbo because the session/plugin state is inconsistent.
design: Treat this as a plugin-lifecycle and recovery bug, not just a one-off UX glitch. Verify exactly when the permission dialog appears during pane-orchestrator reloads, whether the trigger is plugin hash changes, permission re-requests, stale plugin instances, or Zellij reload semantics, and why the popup becomes unusable inside the managed Yazelix window. The fix should prioritize clean recovery: avoid leaving the session in a blank-screen limbo, fail fast with a recoverable path when permissions are not granted, and make plugin reload/update flows deterministic instead of depending on manual session luck. If the root cause is a Zellij limitation, document the exact safe reload workflow and add guardrails in Yazelix rather than pretending the reload is seamless.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear reproduction path or documented trigger for the permission-limbo state, Yazelix no longer gets stuck in a blank-screen session after the pane-orchestrator plugin reload/update path fails, and there is an explicit recovery path for maintainers/users when plugin permissions are missing or stuck. Add focused validation for the detected failure mode, plus logs or diagnostics that show whether the plugin is loaded, permissioned, and responding after reload attempts.
close_reason: Handled by the restart/recovery workflow and current plugin update guidance; the permission-limbo issue is no longer actionable as a separate task.

title: Plan gradual Rust insertion across v15.x toward a Rust-forward v16 release
description: Create a long-term plan for introducing Rust into Yazelix only after v15.0 ships, and then only gradually during v15.x where it buys real safety, maintainability, or performance, while keeping the release identity honest: v15.0 remains the trimmed rebooted non-Rust release, and v16 is the release where Rust becomes a major visible architectural fact. The goal is not rewriting for aesthetics; it is getting a more explicit, testable, and maintainable system with clear behavior contracts and a cleaner boundary between deterministic core logic and the surviving shell/Nix glue.
design: Treat this as a current-v15 architecture-and-process initiative with explicit release framing. Do not make Rust a prerequisite for shipping v15.0 and do not market v15.0 as the Rust release. Rust can land incrementally behind later v15.x point releases when a slice is mature enough, but the roadmap should keep that work honest and narrow. The rewrite plan should define what belongs in a Rust core, what remains Nushell or Nix glue, and how feature behavior becomes spec-driven by default. Every user-visible feature should have a Markdown spec and tests that map to the spec's acceptance cases. The sequence should start with deterministic, reusable logic, not interactive shell glue.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete plan for post-v15.0 gradual Rust insertion during v15.x and for a more explicitly Rust-forward v16 release. The plan identifies the first migration slices, the surfaces that should remain Nushell or Nix, the parity strategy for each slice, and the support boundary between Rust and the remaining glue so work can proceed incrementally without confusing release identity or turning Rust into a prerequisite for the initial v15 release.
notes:
Updated 2026-04-13: release framing is now explicit. v15.x may gain Rust incrementally, but v16 is the release where Rust becomes a major visible architectural fact.
2026-04-19 deletion-discipline update:
- Every Rust bead must record temporary seams introduced, the exact Nushell or CI glue expected to disappear, and the earliest bead where deletion is required.
- A Rust slice that only adds helper code without shrinking any surviving Nushell owner seam is suspect and should not be treated as done unless it files an explicit deletion follow-up.
- Parity tests stay only while they defend migration risk or a permanent public contract. Transition-only parity coverage should be deleted once the replacement boundary is stable.
2026-04-26 successor note: the Rust insertion/migration plan is effectively complete enough that the next architecture risk is Rust bloat, stale bridge seams, and weak migration-era tests. Successor cleanup work is tracked under yazelix-9opk, which audits the Rust codebase for canonical form after migration.
close_reason: Completed: the gradual Rust insertion plan and first-slice sequence are done, all kt5 child beads are closed, and successor canonical cleanup was tracked under yazelix-9opk.

title: Define the Rust migration matrix and first-slice order after boundaries are proven
description: Produce an explicit Rust migration matrix for the current v15 codebase in a form that supports gradual v15.x insertion and a later Rust-forward v16 release. Classify the major surfaces by Rust fitness, parity risk, expected payoff, and whether they should stay in Nushell, move behind a Rust shim, or move fully into a Rust core. Ground the matrix in the actual hot modules carrying product complexity today: config parsing and validation, runtime contract checking, doctor and preflight logic, environment bootstrap, launch/bootstrap state, config generation, and the mixed yzx command surface.
design: This is the gate between 'Rust is safer/faster' sentiment and an implementation sequence that actually improves the live v15 product without confusing release identity. Build directly on the current architecture work: runtime/helper trims, pane-orchestrator cleanup, migration-layer deletion, runtime dependency/preflight contracts, and shared checker work. The output should prefer delete-first simplification before translation. Explicitly identify what should remain Nushell or Nix glue even in a Rust-heavier future, and separate slices that can land quietly during v15.x from slices that are more naturally part of the later v16 push.
acceptance_criteria: There is a written Rust migration matrix covering the main surviving Nushell surfaces with a recommended keep/bridge/rewrite decision for each. The matrix identifies which slices can land during v15.x, which should likely wait for the broader v16 Rust-forward push, and which should likely remain Nushell or Nix. The recommendation is grounded in current hot spots and the live v15 product contract rather than in generic preference for Rust.
notes:
Current sequencing note after the 2026-04-13 product decision: treat this as planning for the live v15 line after the current trim and boundary-hardening work, not as a placeholder for a parallel future branch. Do not use the Rust matrix to bypass the remaining cleanup lanes; it should start only after the key ownership and product-boundary work is stable enough that Rust effort will clarify the system instead of freezing transitional clutter into a new implementation.
2026-04-17 sequencing note: do not treat revival of a broad config-migration engine as a default Rust slice. The cheaper first move is desktop-launch visibility and visible config failure, then only consider a tiny schema-versioned Rust migration adjunct if the pain remains and the product promise justifies permanent machinery.
2026-04-18 first-slice recommendation after the startup/profiling cleanup lane:
- Treat the first Rust slice as the config/runtime core, not launch/bootstrap orchestration, shellhook glue, doctor reasoning, or a broad clap-first CLI rewrite.
- Recommended first-slice order:
  1. yazelix.toml loading, defaults, and schema-backed normalization
  2. config-state hashing / invalidation
  3. generated runtime materialization planning and managed writes
  4. a stable Nushell-callable helper/CLI seam for the slice
  5. preserving the same startup profile/report step names so before/after comparisons stay honest.
- Use this sequence as the default recommendation in the migration matrix unless later boundary work proves a smaller delete-first move.
2026-04-19: Added docs/specs/rust_migration_matrix.md and registered it in the spec inventory. Decision: first Rust implementation sequence is config.normalize via kt5.2.1, config_state.compute via kt5.2.2, then runtime_materialization plan/apply via kt5.2.3. Keep public yzx UX, launch/bootstrap process orchestration, host integration, and most text-heavy renderers in Nushell unless later beads prove a narrower Rust subcore is worth extracting.
close_reason: Completed: Rust migration matrix added in docs/specs/rust_migration_matrix.md and registered in spec inventory.

title: Define the Rust/Nushell bridge contract and incremental crate layout
description: Define how Rust code will be integrated incrementally into the current Nushell-driven repo so small slices can land after v15.0 during v15.x without forcing a big-bang rewrite or pretending v15.0 itself is the Rust release. Cover the helper-binary versus library boundary, JSON/stdout-stderr contracts, error-classification handoff, repo/workspace crate layout, build/distribution implications, and how Nushell command surfaces should call into Rust while preserving current UX.
design: This is the missing bridge between Rust planning and actually shipping incremental Rust slices in the live v15 repo. The repo already contains Rust Zellij plugins, but those wasm crates do not answer how a future Rust core should coexist with Nushell commands, Nix packaging, and generated-config flows. Prefer a thin typed Rust helper/binary model first, with stable machine-readable output consumed by Nushell shims. Do not jump straight to replacing the whole CLI with clap, and keep the bridge compatible with a longer v15.x insertion path that feeds into v16.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation for the incremental Rust integration strategy: where Rust code lives, how Nushell invokes it, what machine-readable contract is used, how errors/logging are surfaced, and how the build/distribution story stays coherent with the current repo. The result is specific enough that later Rust slices can share one integration pattern across v15.x and into the broader v16 push instead of inventing their own ad hoc shims.
notes:
2026-04-18 bridge note for the first recommended Rust slice:
- This bead is now the concrete prerequisite for the first Rust config/runtime slice.
- Start with a stable helper/CLI surface that Nushell can call for config/runtime-core operations; do not treat the bridge as permission to replace the whole yzx CLI with clap.
- Preserve current startup profile/report step names and semantics at the Nushell boundary so Rust-vs-Nushell before/after comparisons use the same labels and reports.
2026-04-19: Added docs/specs/rust_nushell_bridge_contract.md and registered it in the spec inventory. Decision: use a private runtime-root-resolved Rust helper binary behind Nushell, keep yzx as the public command surface, use strict JSON envelopes and stable error classes, keep startup profile labels at the Nushell boundary, and place helper-oriented Rust under rust_core/ separate from rust_plugins/.
close_reason: Completed: bridge contract added in docs/specs/rust_nushell_bridge_contract.md and registered in spec inventory.

title: Port the surviving config contract, parsing, and validation core behind a Rust shim after migration deletion
description: Plan and later implement a Rust-backed core for the deterministic config logic that survives after the v15 migration layer is deleted. The target is the current config contract, parsing, schema validation, and state logic, not the old migration machinery. The goal is not to rewrite every config-facing command immediately, but to move the most structured, failure-prone surviving logic into a safer typed core while keeping a thin Nushell compatibility layer during migration.
design: Treat this as a good early Rust slice once the migration matrix and Rust/Nushell bridge strategy are ready and once the config-migration deletion bead has landed. This logic is highly structured, heavily reused, and benefits from strong types, deterministic serialization, and richer tests. Keep the UX and command surface stable by exposing the Rust logic through a narrow helper binary or shim first. Do not mix this with launch orchestration or package-backend work in the same slice, and do not preserve deleted migration behavior just because the old bead used to mention it.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear migration plan for moving the surviving config contract/parsing/validation/state core behind a Rust shim without breaking the current command surface. The slice defines parity fixtures for the live config contract, identifies which Nushell modules become thin wrappers versus being deleted, and is concrete enough to implement incrementally during v15.x or as part of the broader v16 push.
notes:
Primary target modules after migration deletion: config_parser.nu, config_contract.nu, config_schema.nu, config_state.nu, config_diagnostics.nu, and any tightly coupled surviving helpers they require. Explicit non-goal: porting config_migrations.nu or migration transaction machinery, because that layer is being removed from the live product.
2026-04-17 scope note: if Yazelix later revisits config migration, the likely fit is a very small Rust-first adjunct on top of this surviving config core, with explicit schema_version and deterministic structural transforms only. Do not broaden this slice into the historical migration engine without a separate product decision.
2026-04-18 first-slice scope note:
- Treat kt5.2 as the first recommended implementation slice once kt5.1 / kt5.1.1 are ready.
- Immediate scope for that slice:
  1. yazelix.toml loading, defaults, and schema-backed normalization
  2. config-state hashing / invalidation
  3. generated runtime materialization planning and managed writes
- Keep text-heavy Yazi/Zellij template rendering in Nushell unless a narrower structured subcore clearly proves worth extracting; the Rust core should first own typed config/state/materialization decisions and machine-readable outputs.
- Preserve existing startup profile/report step names across the slice so the current profiler stays comparable before/after.
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before implementing this config-core Rust slice, record a crate-vs-in-house plan in the bead or linked spec. The plan must separate production crates, dev-only crates, in-house domain logic, rejected alternatives, and Nix packaging impact.
2026-04-19 kt5.2.1 landed as the packaged Rust config.normalize core and parity seam, not as live startup adoption. Added child yazelix-kt5.2.4 for the explicit Nushell activation boundary; kt5.2.2 and kt5.2.3 can continue using the Rust library/core without waiting for live parser adoption unless their implementation needs startup traffic switched first.
2026-04-19 temporary seam ledger:
- Current temporary seams from kt5.2.1 and kt5.2.2 live in config_parser.nu helper discovery and rendering, config_state.nu bridge wrappers, transition-heavy cases in test_yzx_generated_configs.nu, and CI helper export/build wiring.
- Earliest deletion checkpoint is kt5.2.3. That slice should reassess which planning/state wrappers still need to exist and which parity cases only defended the Nu-to-Rust migration.
- Do not close kt5.2 on additive Rust LOC alone. At least one surviving Nu owner seam in the config/state/materialization path should be deleted or explicitly demoted to a permanent thin bridge.
close_reason: Completed: the config-core Rust slice is finished; config normalization, config-state hashing, runtime materialization planning/apply, live parser adoption, fallback deletion, legacy cache deletion, and bridge-glue cleanup children are all closed.

title: Rust first slice: port yazelix.toml loading, defaults, and schema-backed normalization
description: Implement the first concrete Rust sub-slice for config input handling. Port the live v15 yazelix.toml loading, default application, and schema-backed normalization path now owned by config_parser.nu, config_contract.nu, config_schema.nu, and tightly coupled helpers into a typed Rust core behind the agreed Nushell shim. The goal is to make the normalized config record deterministic and reusable before porting hash/invalidation or runtime materialization logic.
design: Keep config-migration machinery out of this slice. The source of truth remains the canonical config contract plus generated template/Home Manager defaults. Rust should expose machine-readable normalized output that Nushell can consume without changing the current user-facing command surface. Preserve current defaults, enum validation, parser-key shaping, and failure-class boundaries as closely as practical.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete plan or implementation target for moving yazelix.toml loading, defaults, and schema-backed normalization into Rust behind the shared shim. Parity fixtures cover the current live config contract and defaults, the slice explicitly excludes deleted migration behavior, and the output is specific enough to feed the later hash/invalidation slice.
notes:
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before coding config.normalize, record the crate-vs-in-house plan. Decide explicitly which TOML/JSON/error/argv/test crates are allowed, what config-domain logic stays in-house, and what Nix packaging or vendoring work the crate set creates.
2026-04-19 crate-vs-in-house decision for config.normalize:
- Production crates: serde/serde_json for bridge envelopes and typed output; toml for parsing yazelix.toml, defaults, and main_config_contract.toml; thiserror for stable helper error classes; lexopt for a small private-helper argv parser.
- Dev-only crates: tempfile for fixture roots, pretty_assertions for readable parity diffs, assert_cmd for helper-binary contract tests once the binary exists.
- Build in-house: schema comparison, enum/range/list validation, parser_key shaping, compact_badge_text, empty_string_to_null, bool_to_string, bridge envelope data model, and Yazelix-specific diagnostics.
- Rejected for this slice: clap because yzx_core is private and not a public CLI; anyhow/miette because error classes must stay explicit and machine-readable; toml_edit because this slice reads/normalizes and does not preserve or rewrite user-authored TOML; schemars/config-rs because the canonical schema is Yazelix's TOML contract metadata.
- Packaging impact: add a repo-local rust_core Cargo workspace and build a native yzx_core helper for the runtime libexec path; users should not need Rust installed. Hashing crates are deferred to kt5.2.2.
2026-04-19 implementation: added rust_core Cargo workspace and private yzx_core helper with config.normalize. The helper reads explicit config/default/contract TOML paths, applies contract defaults, emits deterministic normalized_config JSON with parser_key shaping and current parser transforms, and returns a schema_version=1 success/error envelope. Packaged helper is installed at runtime libexec/yzx_core via packaging/rust_core_helper.nix and mk_runtime_tree.nix. Live Nushell startup is intentionally not switched in this bead; follow-up yazelix-kt5.2.4 owns the adoption boundary so source checkouts do not depend on an unbuilt helper and packaged runtimes do not silently mask helper failures.
2026-04-19 verification: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test, nix build #runtime, nix build #yazelix, runtime-output probe for libexec/yzx_core config.normalize, Rust-vs-Nushell default-config parity diff, validate_specs.nu, git diff --check, and nix flake show --all-systems all passed. During verification the filesystem hit 100%; cleaned only rust_core/target and ran nix store gc to remove unreferenced Nix store paths, freeing about 146 GiB before rerunning the runtime/package probes.
2026-04-19 packaging follow-up: threaded fenixPkgs through the first-party Home Manager module and made rust_core_helper fall back to pkgs.rustPlatform for direct imports such as the nixpkgs draft package. Verified flake runtime/package output, direct packaging/nixpkgs/default.nix build, helper presence in both package paths, and Home Manager module argument evaluation.
2026-04-19 final cleanup: tightened yzx_core error envelopes so unsupported helper commands report the requested command, added an integration test for that contract, and excluded rust_core/target from the Nix clean source filter. Final verification reran cargo fmt --check, clippy -D warnings, cargo test, flake runtime/package builds, direct nixpkgs draft package build with helper presence check, Rust-vs-Nushell default-config parity, validate_specs.nu, git diff --check, Home Manager module argument evaluation, and nix flake show --all-systems.
close_reason: Implemented packaged Rust config.normalize core with parity tests and runtime libexec helper; live Nushell adoption split to yazelix-kt5.2.4.

title: Rust first slice: port config-state hashing and invalidation
description: Move the rebuild-hash and invalidation logic from config_state.nu into the Rust core after normalized config output exists. This includes rebuild-required field extraction, current-versus-cached hash computation, runtime hash composition, refresh reasoning, and managed-surface state recording semantics.
design: Treat this as typed deterministic state math, not launch orchestration. Preserve live v15 invalidation behavior for rebuild-required keys and runtime changes. Do not broaden this slice into migration machinery, shellhook code, or text-heavy generator template logic.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete plan or implementation target for Rust-owned config-state hashing and invalidation that preserves current refresh/no-refresh outcomes, hash/input boundaries, and machine-readable output for Nushell shims. Parity coverage defends unchanged inputs, runtime-only changes, config-only changes, and ignored non-rebuild keys.
notes:
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before coding config_state.compute, record the crate-vs-in-house plan. Hashing/serialization crates must be justified against deterministic parity needs; state classification and refresh reasoning should default to in-house domain logic unless a crate has a clear narrow payoff.
2026-04-19 crate-vs-in-house decision for config_state.compute:
- Add production crate: sha2 for SHA-256 parity with Nushell `hash sha256`; implement lowercase hex encoding in-house to avoid a separate hex crate.
- Reuse existing production crates: serde/serde_json for bridge envelopes and state JSON; toml for parsing config and contract metadata; thiserror/lexopt for existing helper error and argv seams.
- Build in-house: rebuild-required key extraction, nested TOML projection, config/runtime hash composition, legacy string-cache compatibility, structured cache comparison, refresh-reason classification, and managed-main-surface recording rules.
- Keep out of this slice: Yazi/Zellij rendering, generated file materialization planning, config migration machinery, implicit Cargo builds in startup, and broad launch orchestration.
- Nix impact: yzx_core grows one small hashing dependency but remains packaged into runtime libexec/yzx_core; users still do not need Rust installed.
2026-04-19 implementation outcome:
- Added Rust-owned config_state.compute/config_state.record in yzx_core and rewired config_state.nu to use the helper instead of local Nushell hashing/state-write logic.
- Preserved legacy string-cache compatibility while writing structured {config_hash,runtime_hash} state for managed main configs only.
- Added helper CLI subcommands and Rust integration/unit coverage for compute/record envelopes and refresh semantics.
- Added shared dev helper resolution for source-checkout validators/tests so fake runtime fixtures explicitly use a built yzx_core helper instead of assuming packaged libexec state.
- Verified with: cargo test (Rust unit + integration), validate_config_surface_contract.nu, run_generated_config_canonical_tests, direct source-checkout compute_config_state probe, nix shell nu-lint on changed Nu files, nix build .#runtime .#yazelix, and git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: config-state hashing and invalidation now route through yzx_core with parity checks and packaged helper coverage.

title: Rust first slice: port generated runtime materialization planning and managed writes
description: After normalized config output and config-state hashing exist in Rust, move the generated-runtime materialization plan and managed-write ownership out of generated_runtime_state.nu. Target the decision layer that decides what should be materialized, when warm paths can skip rewrites, how managed state is recorded, and how atomic managed writes are coordinated.
design: Do not force all text-heavy renderers into Rust in this slice. The likely first move is a Rust owner for planning, invalidation, and write orchestration while Yazi and Zellij text assembly may remain Nushell until kt5.5 is decided. Preserve current startup profile/report step names at the boundary so before/after comparisons stay meaningful.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete plan or implementation target for Rust-owned generated-runtime materialization planning and managed writes that keeps warm-path skip behavior honest, records applied state deterministically, and preserves comparable startup profile labels before/after. The slice explicitly states which renderer surfaces stay Nushell for now.
notes:
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before coding runtime_materialization plan/apply, record the crate-vs-in-house plan. Atomic writes, path handling, renderer boundaries, and plan data structures should be explicitly assigned to crates versus Yazelix-owned code before implementation starts.
2026-04-19 deletion ledger:
- Temporary seams expected to shrink here: generated_runtime_state.nu planning and managed-write orchestration, adjacent atomic_writes.nu call plumbing where Rust can own the decision/apply boundary, and transition-only parity cases in test_yzx_generated_configs.nu.
- Earliest acceptable survivors are text renderers that kt5.5 has not moved: yazi_config_merger.nu, zellij_config_merger.nu, and terminal_configs.nu.
- Must delete by close of kt5.2.3: either remove a real Nu orchestration seam in generated_runtime_state.nu or file explicit follow-up beads naming each surviving duplicate or shim and why it could not be deleted yet.
- Suspicion rule: if kt5.2.3 lands with new Rust plan/apply code but no net shrink in the surviving Nu orchestration path, treat the bead as incomplete.
2026-04-19 kt5.2.3 dependency decision:
- Production crates: keep the current helper stack only (`serde`, `serde_json`, `toml`, `sha2`, `thiserror`, `lexopt`). No new production crate is justified for runtime-materialization planning or apply verification.
- Dev-only crates: keep the existing Rust test helpers (`tempfile`, `pretty_assertions`, `assert_cmd`) only.
- In-house logic: materialization-plan modeling, expected-artifact resolution, missing-artifact classification, generated-state apply verification, and any bridge JSON shaping specific to Yazelix stay Yazelix-owned.
- Rejected alternatives: no filesystem helper crate, no atomic-write helper crate, no clap, no path-diff/path-absolutize convenience layer. The slice should reuse std fs/path plus existing helper patterns.
- Packaging impact: expected to stay within the current packaged `yzx_core` helper flow with no new Nix dependency surface beyond rebuilding the helper.
2026-04-19 implementation landed:
- Rust helper now owns runtime-materialization planning and apply verification via `runtime-materialization.plan` and `runtime-materialization.apply` in `yzx_core`.
- This slice intentionally kept Yazi and Zellij text rendering in Nushell; renderer ownership was not reopened here.
- Verified with `cargo test`, `nu-lint`, `validate_config_surface_contract.nu`, the canonical generated-config tests, `git diff --check`, `nix build .#runtime .#yazelix`, and a packaged-helper probe against the built runtime.
- The deletion suspicion rule still matters: ownership moved, but `generated_runtime_state.nu` remains too thick because of surviving helper-exec, JSON-envelope, and path-plumbing boilerplate. Follow-up `yazelix-kt5.2.7` tracks deleting that bridge glue before this boundary is considered clean.
close_reason: Completed: Rust now owns runtime-materialization planning and apply verification behind `yzx_core` while Nu keeps renderer ownership for now. The remaining generated_runtime_state.nu bridge boilerplate is tracked separately in `yazelix-kt5.2.7`.

title: Wire live Nushell config parser to Rust config.normalize helper
description: Adopt the packaged yzx_core config.normalize helper from yazelix-kt5.2.1 in the live Nushell config parser path once helper discovery is safe for installed runtimes and source checkouts. This is the activation step, not another parser port.
design: Keep the command surface unchanged. Prefer runtime-local libexec/yzx_core when YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR resolves to a packaged runtime. For source checkouts, do not make startup depend on an unbuilt local Rust binary unless there is an explicit maintainer/dev path. Preserve the current Nushell parser as a deliberate compatibility path only where the Rust helper cannot be expected to exist yet, and make that boundary explicit instead of silently masking helper failures in packaged runtimes.
acceptance_criteria: Packaged/runtime Yazelix startup obtains normalized config through yzx_core config.normalize. Source-checkout behavior is explicitly defined and tested. Failures from the Rust helper produce clear config-class errors instead of silent fallback in packaged runtimes. A parity test proves the live parser returns the same normalized default config shape as the Rust helper.
notes:
Implementation 2026-04-19: live parse_yazelix_config now resolves the active runtime and uses runtime-local libexec/yzx_core config.normalize when present. Packaged runtimes without yzx_core now fail fast as host-dependency errors instead of falling back to the legacy Nushell parser. Source checkouts keep an explicit Nushell fallback until the maintainer/source helper story is resolved.

Verification: nu load check; source-checkout parse_yazelix_config against yazelix_default.toml; nix build #runtime plus packaged-runtime parse through libexec/yzx_core; run_generated_config_canonical_tests; nu-lint on changed Nu files; git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: live config parsing now uses runtime-local yzx_core in packaged runtimes, fails fast when the packaged helper is missing, and preserves only an explicit source-checkout fallback tracked by yazelix-kt5.2.5.

title: Delete the legacy Nushell config parser fallback
description: After yazelix-kt5.2.4, packaged runtimes use libexec/yzx_core for config.normalize, but source checkouts still keep the legacy Nushell parser as an explicit fallback. This bead is the deletion step for that remaining fallback once the maintainer/source-checkout helper story is good enough.
design: First choose the source-checkout helper strategy: checked-in/dev-built yzx_core, explicit maintainer bootstrap, or another low-friction path. Do not make normal source checkout startup run cargo builds implicitly. Once source checkouts can reasonably execute yzx_core, remove parse_yazelix_config_with_nushell_fallback and the legacy contract parsing helpers from nushell/scripts/utils/config_parser.nu. Update tests so source-checkout parsing also exercises the Rust helper instead of preserving the fallback.
acceptance_criteria: Source checkout and packaged runtime config parsing both go through yzx_core config.normalize. The legacy Nushell parser fallback and its dead helper functions are deleted. Missing helper errors remain explicit and actionable. run_generated_config_canonical_tests and nu-lint pass after the deletion.
notes:
Implementation 2026-04-19: deleted the legacy Nushell config parser fallback and all local contract parsing helpers from config_parser.nu. parse_yazelix_config now always resolves a yzx_core helper and calls config.normalize. Packaged runtimes use runtime-local libexec/yzx_core first; source-checkout/maintainer flows use YAZELIX_YZX_CORE_BIN or a prebuilt rust_core/target helper, with clear host-dependency errors when no helper exists. maintainer_shell.nix now exports YAZELIX_YZX_CORE_BIN from the Nix-built rust_core helper before runtime_env parsing.

Verification: focused source/package helper tests; run_generated_config_canonical_tests with yzx_core helper; maintainer shellHook probe via nix develop; nu-lint on changed files; nix build #runtime #yazelix; packaged runtime parser probe; git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: deleted the legacy Nushell config parser fallback; source and packaged runtimes now require yzx_core config.normalize with explicit maintainer/source helper resolution.

title: Delete legacy rebuild_hash string-cache compatibility
notes:
2026-04-19 scope:
- Remove support for pre-2026-02-06 raw-string rebuild_hash cache files.
- Treat malformed or non-structured cache state as missing/stale instead of legacy-compatible.
- Delete the now-useless cached_hash surface and verbose launch print.
- Verify with malformed-cache probe, Rust tests, nu-lint, contract validator, and package build.
close_reason: Completed: raw-string rebuild_hash compatibility removed; malformed cache now behaves like missing state and the cached_hash surface was deleted.

title: Delete remaining runtime-materialization bridge glue from generated_runtime_state.nu
description: kt5.2.3 moved generated-runtime materialization planning and apply verification into the Rust yzx_core helper, but the surviving Nushell wrapper in generated_runtime_state.nu still carries a fair amount of helper invocation, JSON-envelope parsing, and path/override plumbing. Delete or centralize that remaining bridge glue so the surviving Nushell layer is materially thinner and more obviously a wrapper rather than a second owner.
design: Prefer deleting or centralizing repeated yzx_core invocation boilerplate across config_state.nu and generated_runtime_state.nu instead of adding more one-off wrapper code. A good outcome is one small shared helper runner plus a slimmer generated_runtime_state.nu that only preserves the public profile step names and calls the Nu renderers that still own text assembly. Do not reopen renderer ownership here; this is boundary cleanup after kt5.2.3, not kt5.5.
acceptance_criteria: There is a materially thinner surviving Nushell wrapper for runtime materialization. generated_runtime_state.nu no longer owns repeated helper-exec/envelope-parsing/path-plumbing boilerplate beyond what is strictly necessary for public profile labels and Nu renderer calls, and the resulting surface is clearly a thin bridge rather than a parallel implementation owner.
notes:
2026-04-19 concrete cleanup targets after kt5.2.3:
- Centralize the repeated `yzx_core` helper invocation and JSON-envelope parsing instead of leaving `compute_runtime_materialization_plan` to assemble and decode the helper call inline.
- Delete or centralize the layout-override and path/argv plumbing that still lives in `generated_runtime_state.nu`; the surviving Nu layer should not own that much bridge setup.
- Keep only the public profile step names and the Nu-owned text renderers in this file. Do not reopen Yazi/Zellij renderer ownership here; kt5.2.7 is boundary cleanup, not kt5.5.
- A good end state is one shared helper runner plus thin `regenerate_runtime_configs` / `record_current_materialized_state` wrappers around `generate_merged_yazi_config`, `generate_merged_zellij_config`, and the Rust helper commands.
2026-04-19 implementation landed:
- Added shared `yzx_core` bridge helpers in `config_parser.nu` for JSON-returning commands and fire-and-forget commands, keeping helper-path lookup, command execution, envelope parsing, and error rendering in one place.
- Moved `get_materialized_state_path` into `common.nu` so `config_state.nu` and `generated_runtime_state.nu` no longer duplicate the rebuild-hash path logic.
- Slimmed `config_state.nu` onto the shared bridge and reduced `generated_runtime_state.nu` to command-arg builders plus the public profile-labeled flow and Nu-owned renderers.
- Verified with `nu-lint`, import/parse smoke for the edited modules, `git diff --check`, and `run_generated_config_canonical_tests` \(all passing\).
- Net cleanup for this bead: 107 insertions, 127 deletions across the touched Nushell bridge files.
close_reason: Completed: the surviving Nushell bridge is now thinner and shared `yzx_core` invocation/envelope handling lives in `config_parser.nu` instead of being duplicated across config_state.nu and generated_runtime_state.nu.

title: Port runtime dependency checking and doctor reasoning into a Rust core
description: Once the runtime dependency contract and shared checker are defined in Nushell, evaluate and stage a Rust-backed implementation for the dependency/preflight reasoning that launch and doctor share. Focus on the classification engine and structured results, not on rendering every doctor message in Rust on day one. This is a candidate for gradual v15.x insertion if the slice becomes mature enough, otherwise it can remain part of the larger v16 Rust-forward push.
design: This should follow the contract work, not replace it. First prove the dependency model and user-visible semantics in the current v15 codebase. Then move the checker core into Rust if it materially simplifies correctness, structured diagnostics, and future performance or maintainability. Keep doctor and launch presentation in Nushell initially if that reduces churn.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete plan for moving the shared runtime contract checker into Rust, with a clear boundary between structured result generation and user-facing rendering. The plan identifies parity cases, launch-time performance constraints, expected machine-readable output, and how doctor will consume the same results without reintroducing duplicate logic. It also states honestly whether this slice belongs in a v15.x point release or should wait for the broader v16 push.
notes:
Primary target logic lives around doctor.nu and the future shared runtime checker introduced by yazelix-hv2m. This slice should not start until j4qv and hv2m have clarified the contract in the current codebase.
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before any doctor/preflight Rust implementation, record the crate-vs-in-house plan. Structured finding classification should default to Yazelix-owned domain types; add crates only for narrow parsing, serialization, or test value.
2026-04-19 deletion ledger:
- Temporary seams expected in this slice: doctor.nu and launch-time preflight should become formatting and exit-policy consumers, not parallel owners of dependency classification.
- Deletion targets are duplicated reasoning branches in runtime_contract_checker.nu, doctor.nu, doctor_install_artifacts.nu, doctor_helix.nu, runtime_distribution_capabilities.nu, and related launch preflight helpers once Rust findings become the shared source.
- Must delete by close of kt5.3: any duplicated classification path that still computes findings separately in Nushell, or file explicit follow-up beads naming the leftover duplicates.
- Keep prose-heavy rendering in Nushell if useful, but do not keep two reasoning engines.
2026-04-19 concrete first slice for kt5.3:
- Rust target is the shared structured-finding engine behind `runtime_contract_checker.nu`, not the whole `yzx doctor` surface.
- Keep in Nushell for now: doctor rendering, `--fix` actions, desktop-entry and shell-shadowing audits from `doctor_install_artifacts.nu`, Helix-specific diagnostics from `doctor_helix.nu`, runtime distribution tier reporting from `runtime_distribution_capabilities.nu`, and live Zellij session/plugin checks. Those are either prose-heavy, install-surface-specific, or session-local rather than shared launch blockers.
- First Rust command shape should be one batch evaluation per surface \(startup, launch, doctor shared-preflight\), not one helper subprocess per individual check. The launch/startup performance constraint is to avoid turning simple preflight into several extra helper process launches.
- First parity set to port: working-directory validation, runtime script existence, generated-layout existence, terminal availability, and the Linux Ghostty desktop-graphics warning logic that currently lives in `runtime_contract_checker.nu`. Layout-path resolution can stay in Nushell initially and feed the resolved path into Rust.
- Honest v15.x boundary: this slice is reasonable for v15.x if it stays at the shared finding engine plus bridge consumption. Pulling in install-artifact doctoring, Helix runtime analysis, or session-local plugin health should wait for later beads or the broader v16 push.
2026-04-19 kt5.3 dependency decision:
- Production crates: keep the existing helper stack only \(`serde`, `serde_json`, `toml`, `sha2`, `thiserror`, `lexopt`\). No new production crate is justified for runtime/preflight evaluation; PATH scanning, filesystem checks, and OS/env inspection should stay on `std`.
- Dev-only crates: keep the current Rust test helpers only \(`tempfile`, `pretty_assertions`, `assert_cmd`\).
- In-house logic: runtime-check domain types, severity/failure-class modeling, terminal candidate detection for the supported Yazelix terminal list, nixGL wrapper ownership detection, and batch request/response shaping stay Yazelix-owned.
- Rejected alternatives: no `which` crate, no `clap`, no broader system-information crate, no command-runner crate. The contract is narrow enough that extra dependencies would mostly hide simple logic.
- Packaging impact: should stay within the existing packaged `yzx_core` helper, with one new helper command and Nushell bridge updates in launch/startup/doctor call sites.
2026-04-19 landed v15.x shared Rust preflight boundary:
- Added `yzx_core runtime-contract.evaluate` with batch request/response types for startup, launch, and doctor shared-preflight.
- Ported shared structured findings for working-directory validation, runtime-script existence, generated-layout existence, terminal availability/candidate selection, and the Linux Ghostty desktop-graphics warning into `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/runtime_contract.rs`.
- Reworked `runtime_contract_checker.nu` into a Rust-backed bridge while keeping Nushell ownership of rendering, `require_runtime_check`, and doctor/launch exit policy.
- Startup and new-window launch now use one batch preflight call on the main path; direct helper seams like `validate_startup_working_dir`, `validate_launch_working_dir`, and terminal-candidate helpers were restored as thin wrappers so workspace/test contracts stayed stable.
- Tightened runtime-root resolution so repo-sourced code prefers the checkout over ambient installed-runtime pointers from `/nix/store` or `~/.local/share/yazelix/runtime/...`, while explicit synthetic runtime roots still win for tests and real runtime surfaces.
- Updated `docs/specs/runtime_dependency_preflight_contract.md` to record the v15.x Rust/Nushell boundary and helper verification surface.
Verification:
- `nix shell nixpkgs#nu-lint -c nu-lint nushell/scripts/utils/common.nu nushell/scripts/utils/config_parser.nu nushell/scripts/utils/runtime_contract_checker.nu nushell/scripts/utils/doctor.nu nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix.nu nushell/scripts/core/launch_yazelix.nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_doctor_commands.nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_shell_managed_config_contracts.nu`
- `YAZELIX_YZX_CORE_BIN=/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix/rust_core/target/debug/yzx_core nu -c "source nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu; run_workspace_canonical_tests"`
- `YAZELIX_YZX_CORE_BIN=/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix/rust_core/target/debug/yzx_core nu -c "source nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_doctor_commands.nu; run_doctor_canonical_tests"`
- `nu -c "source nushell/scripts/dev/test_shell_managed_config_contracts.nu; run_shell_managed_config_contract_tests"`
- `nix shell nixpkgs#cargo nixpkgs#rustc -c cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml`
- `nu -c "source nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu; main"`
- `git diff --check`
close_reason: Completed: shared runtime preflight reasoning now runs through the Rust yzx_core batch helper, with Nushell kept as the rendering and fix-policy surface.

title: Port launch/bootstrap state and backend adapter logic into Rust after the seam is extracted
description: After the backend adapter seam is clearer, and only after v15.0 has shipped, evaluate moving the launch/bootstrap state machine and backend-calling logic out of environment_bootstrap.nu, launch_state.nu, and related yzx env/run/launch/refresh helpers into a Rust core. The target is safer orchestration and clearer state transitions, not a generic backend framework. This is a candidate only for a later v15.x point release if the seam is truly stable; otherwise it belongs in the broader v16 Rust-forward push.
design: Do not start here and do not treat this as part of shipping v15.0. This is a later slice because it mixes process spawning, environment shaping, state reuse, and user-facing behavior. Only move it once the backend contract and command-surface audit have made the boundary explicit and after the earlier Rust slices have proven the integration strategy. A likely good outcome is a Rust core that owns the state machine and structured actions while Nushell stays as a thin CLI and host-integration layer. If the boundary still feels porous after the earlier work, defer this slice to v16 instead of forcing it into v15.x.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete migration plan for the launch/bootstrap/backend adapter path that respects the extracted backend seam and does not re-entangle product semantics. The plan identifies which Nushell modules become shims or are removed, how parity will be verified for env/run/refresh/launch behavior, why this slice is worth doing after the earlier ones, and whether it is realistically safe for a later v15.x point release or should wait for the broader v16 push.
notes:
Primary target modules: environment_bootstrap.nu, launch_state.nu, yzx/env.nu, yzx/run.nu, yzx/launch.nu, and any helper code that still encodes backend-specific state transitions.

Sequencing note after the 2026-04-05 architecture review:
- Treat `yazelix-m5rk.1` as the concrete precondition that makes the runtime state machine explicit before this slice moves into Rust.
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before any launch/bootstrap Rust implementation, record the crate-vs-in-house plan. Process, shell, and terminal seams are high-risk; justify any framework or process-management crate before adding it.
2026-04-19 deletion ledger:
- Temporary seams expected in this slice: environment_bootstrap.nu, launch_state.nu, start_yazelix.nu, start_yazelix_inner.nu, launch_yazelix.nu, and the yzx env/run/launch helpers should not all remain full behavior owners after Rust state-machine adoption.
- The intended steady state is a thin Nushell CLI and host boundary over a Rust-owned launch state machine. If the slice would leave both sides owning the same state transitions, defer instead of merging additive glue.
- Must delete by close of kt5.4: at least one real Nu state-machine or decision layer, or file explicit follow-up beads per surviving duplicate orchestration seam.
- Permanent Nu ownership is acceptable for shell-specific argv/env handoff and host integration, not for duplicated launch state logic.
2026-04-19 start note: claimed for launch/bootstrap Rust migration planning. Rust dependency gate for kt5.4: keep the existing yzx_core production crate set only \(serde, serde_json, toml, sha2, thiserror, lexopt\); add no process, shell, async, terminal, PATH, or command-runner framework crates for the first slice. Keep current dev-only crates \(assert_cmd, pretty_assertions, tempfile\) for helper/fixture tests. Build in-house/std: launch/bootstrap state classification, canonical runtime-env planning, shell/terminal handoff plan records, env-var cleanup lists, and bridge request/response shaping. Rejected alternatives: clap for public CLI rewrite, tokio/async-process/process-control crates, which crate, shell-escape crates, terminal abstraction crates. Packaging impact: no new dependency closure or vendoring/hash change; any implementation must stay inside the private packaged libexec/yzx_core helper and leave Nushell/POSIX as the host/process execution boundary until a narrower deletion target is proven.
2026-04-19 planning completion:
- added spec docs/specs/launch_bootstrap_rust_migration.md to define the live v15 launch/bootstrap ownership map and the narrow Rust insertion plan
- current first Rust slice is `runtime-env.compute`; `runtime_env.nu` is the first Nushell decision layer slated to shrink into a bridge
- explicit first execution child: `yazelix-kt5.4.1`
- historical `devenv_backend`, `launch_state`, launch-profile reuse, and public `yzx refresh` remain out of scope for this plan
close_reason: Completed: concrete launch/bootstrap Rust migration plan captured in docs/specs/launch_bootstrap_rust_migration.md and first execution child yazelix-kt5.4.1 created

title: Move canonical runtime env planning into yzx_core and collapse runtime_env.nu into a bridge
description: Move the first execution slice under `yazelix-kt5.4` by moving canonical runtime-environment planning behind `yzx_core` and shrinking `runtime_env.nu` to a bridge. This slice covers the shared env computation used by `yzx env`, `yzx run`, popup/helper launches, and startup wrappers. It does not port terminal launch execution or startup orchestration.
design:
- Add a private `runtime-env.compute` helper command under `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/runtime_env.rs`
- Pass explicit runtime root, home/XDG-derived roots, current PATH entries, and normalized config fields that shape the runtime env
- Delete local decision helpers from `nushell/scripts/utils/runtime_env.nu` and keep only the bridge plus `activate_runtime_env` / `run_runtime_argv`
- Leave `shells/posix/runtime_env.sh`, `launch_yazelix.nu`, `terminal_launcher.nu`, `start_yazelix.nu`, and `start_yazelix_inner.nu` as the process/orchestration owners in v15.x
- Coordinate with `yazelix-0dra`: either absorb the remaining explicit nested-editor regression coverage into this slice or keep that bug open until equivalent coverage exists
acceptance_criteria: `runtime_env.nu` no longer owns PATH curation, editor-kind detection, Helix-runtime resolution, or layout-env decision logic locally. A Rust helper returns the canonical runtime env consumed by `yzx env`, `yzx run`, popup/helper launches, and startup wrappers without changing the public command surface. Regression coverage defends `VISUAL == EDITOR`, Helix wrapper and curated PATH behavior, `yzx run` child-argv pass-through, and unchanged launch/startup behavior. `cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/yazelix_core/Cargo.toml` and the targeted Nushell tests for popup, Helix managed-config, core commands, and workspace launch paths pass.
notes:
2026-04-19 start note: implementation claimed. Rust dependency gate for kt5.4.1 stays on the existing helper crate set only: production crates remain serde, serde_json, toml, sha2, thiserror, and lexopt; dev-only crates remain assert_cmd, pretty_assertions, and tempfile. Build in-house/std: PATH normalization and runtime-owned entry filtering, editor-kind detection, managed Helix wrapper selection, Helix runtime override handling, and the runtime-env request/response types. Rejected for this slice: clap, process-management crates, which, shell-escape, async runtimes, and terminal abstraction crates. Packaging impact: keep the helper private under libexec/yzx_core with no new dependency closure changes.
2026-04-19 implementation completion:
- added `runtime-env.compute` to `yzx_core` as a JSON-request helper command and new `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/runtime_env.rs` decision module
- collapsed `nushell/scripts/utils/runtime_env.nu` into a bridge over the helper while keeping `activate_runtime_env` and `run_runtime_argv` as Nushell-owned execution helpers
- kept POSIX/runtime bootstrap and launch/startup orchestration in Nushell/POSIX; no terminal execution logic moved into Rust
- updated popup and Helix contract tests to use the built helper explicitly where fake runtime roots are involved
- validation run: `cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml`, `nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_helix_managed_config_contracts.nu`, `nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_popup_commands.nu`, targeted `yzx run` passthrough regressions from `test_yzx_core_commands.nu`, and targeted startup bootstrap env probe from `test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu`
close_reason: Completed: runtime-env.compute now owns canonical runtime env planning behind yzx_core, runtime_env.nu is a bridge, and the targeted Rust/Nushell contract tests pass

title: Decide which config generators should stay Nushell versus move to Rust
description: Evaluate the major generated-config surfaces such as yazi_config_merger.nu, zellij_config_merger.nu, terminal_configs.nu, and related template helpers to decide whether they are good Rust targets or should stay in Nushell. The goal is to avoid rewriting text-heavy, low-risk template glue just because v16 will be Rust-forward, while still identifying any generator slice that would genuinely benefit from stronger modeling. v15.0 should ship without Rust becoming the owner of these surfaces; any generator move would be a later v15.x or v16 decision.
design: Use the migration matrix to separate structured generator logic from simple template stitching. Favor keeping straightforward text/template assembly in Nushell unless there is repeated correctness pain, complex state reconciliation, or a clear typed model that would become substantially safer in Rust. Do not let this bead become a license to port all generators indiscriminately just because Rust is on the roadmap or because v16 is intended to be Rust-forward. The default should be to keep v15.0 and probably much of v15.x generator ownership in Nushell unless a narrower structured subcore proves worth extracting.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear keep-versus-port recommendation for the main config generators, with concrete reasons tied to defect history, complexity, and expected payoff. The output identifies any generator slice worth porting, explicitly names the ones that should remain Nushell, and states honestly whether any surviving Rust candidate belongs in a later v15.x point release or should wait for v16.
notes:
Likely keep-in-Nushell candidates today: zellij_config_merger.nu, yazi_config_merger.nu, and much of terminal_configs.nu unless a narrower structured core emerges that is worth extracting.

Sequencing note after the 2026-04-05 architecture review:
- `yazelix-jheg` is the likely non-Rust improvement path for `zellij_config_merger.nu`; if that typed render-pipeline work lands first, reevaluate whether any narrower structured subcore is still worth porting instead of assuming the whole generator should move.
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before any generator-port Rust implementation, record the crate-vs-in-house plan. Template/text rendering should stay Nushell or in-house unless a crate clearly reduces real parser/renderer risk.
2026-04-19 deletion discipline:
- This bead is allowed to conclude that most generators stay Nushell. The goal is to avoid cargo-cult rewrites.
- If any generator slice is approved for Rust, the recommendation must also name the exact Nushell template or glue expected to disappear and the earliest deletion bead for it.
- Reject ports that only add a Rust planner while leaving the same text ownership and branching duplicated in Nushell.
- The default outcome remains deletion of unnecessary bridge ideas, not automatic generator porting.
2026-04-19 renderer ownership decision:
- Do not port Yazi/Zellij/terminal/Helix/shell initializer/layout/extern text renderers to Rust just to reduce Nushell LOC.
- Rust owns the structured decision layer already behind yzx_core: normalized config, rebuild-state hashing, refresh classification, expected artifact planning, and materialized-state recording.
- Nushell owns generated text/template output, plugin/layout copying, user override discovery, per-launch Ghostty random cursor rerolls, shader sync, shell initializers, and the fingerprinted Nushell extern bridge.
- runtime-materialization.apply should remain limited to materialized-state recording and expected-artifact verification unless a future renderer-specific bead explicitly moves one generated file family to Rust.
- Revisit only for repeated renderer correctness defects, need for a real parser/AST model, shared structured renderer logic that is cheaper in Rust, or a later v16 public-CLI ownership change.
- No follow-up Rust renderer port bead is recommended now; this bead resolves by locking the boundary in docs/specs/rust_migration_matrix.md.
close_reason: Completed: renderer/template ownership is locked in the Rust migration matrix; generated text/template surfaces stay Nushell for v15.x and Rust remains the structured config/state/materialization decision owner.

title: Validate first-party Cargo git dependency source hashes
description:
Child repos such as yazelix-screen can be pinned in both flake.lock and rust_core/Cargo.lock. buildRustPackage also requires matching fixed-output cargoLock.outputHashes in packaging/rust_core_helper.nix. A child release can therefore update the flake input and Cargo rev while leaving the package hash stale; nix build catches it late as a fixed-output hash mismatch.

Acceptance criteria:
- validate-child-release-transaction or a sibling maintainer validator checks first-party Cargo git dependencies in rust_core/Cargo.lock against packaging/rust_core_helper.nix outputHashes.
- The check reports a clear actionable error when a first-party git dependency rev changed but its output hash did not.
- The implementation stays narrow to Yazelix-owned child repos and does not become a broad generic Nix linter.
- The validator is documented or named clearly enough to run during coupled child-release transactions.
close_reason: Completed: validate-child-release-transaction now verifies first-party Cargo git dependencies against packaging/rust_core_helper.nix outputHashes using nix flake prefetch, reports stale/missing hashes clearly, and the current transaction passes for published child rev 32657f86.

title: Isolate KGP package overrides from consumer hooks
description: KGP package overrides replace upstream sources and therefore must own source-coupled package metadata. They should not inherit consumer nixpkgs patch hooks, Cargo vendor deps, or install checks that target a different source shape.
close_reason: Completed: KGP Yazi and KGP Zellij overrides now clear inherited consumer patch hooks; KGP Zellij also owns the install-check phase that was coupled to upstream postPatch. Validator checks poison inherited cargoDeps/patch hooks/installCheckPhase. Verified with targeted Nix eval, Rust maintainer fmt/check, and nix build .#runtime_ghostty --no-link --no-write-lock-file.

title: Investigate how to make session resurrection excellent
description: Investigate what a genuinely strong resurrection story should look like for Yazelix sessions, including Zellij session restore, workspace cwd/state recovery, and any plugin-managed pane context that users would expect to survive restarts.
design: Do not reduce resurrection to merely reopening a Zellij session. Identify what users actually mean by a great restore experience: tabs, cwd, managed panes, sidebar/file-tree state, popup state, editor context, and whether resurrection should be explicit or automatic. Then determine which pieces are already available from Zellij, which belong in Yazelix, and which are too fragile to promise.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear target definition for great resurrection in Yazelix, with supported versus unsupported restored state called out explicitly. The bead leaves behind narrower follow-up beads for the gaps that matter instead of keeping resurrection as a vague aspiration.
notes:
Investigation result, 2026-04-28:
- Great resurrection in Yazelix should be explicit and session-scoped, not automatic by default. Users can keep many Yazelix windows open, so invisible auto-attach/resurrect behavior risks crossing window boundaries.
- Zellij should own low-level process/tab/pane serialization and explicit save/restore primitives: session_serialization, save-session, session-manager listings, and restored commands/cwds.
- Yazelix should own the coherence layer around restored sessions: versioned per-window config snapshot identity, runtime version, tab-local workspace root/source, managed editor/sidebar pane identity, sidebar Yazi identity/cwd when live, layout/sidebar state, and clear diagnostics when restored state is stale or incomplete.
- Supported target state: Zellij tabs/panes/cwds/running commands when Zellij can resurrect them; Yazelix tab workspace root/source; managed pane identity after pane-orchestrator observation; runtime/config snapshot diagnostics; user-triggered explicit save/restore surface.
- Unsupported by default: resurrecting transient popup/menu panes, stale floating panes, editor-internal unsaved buffer state beyond the editor/session tool, and implicit adoption of unrelated existing Zellij sessions.
- Current code already has the important in-session seam: get_active_tab_session_state plus yzx dev inspect_session. The missing piece is a product contract and UX around explicit session save/restore and how restored pane-orchestrator state is rehydrated or diagnosed.
- Created follow-up beads for the remaining narrower work.
close_reason: Completed investigation. Defined explicit, session-scoped resurrection target and split remaining work into yazelix-p7qs and yazelix-l38n.

title: Ratconfig adapter thinning phase 2
description: Continue moving generic app state, navigation, editor behavior, and render helpers into the private yazelix_ratconfig boundary while leaving Yazelix settings, JSONC, Home Manager, native status, cursor config, and apply behavior local.
design: Private boundary only. Do not create a public child repo. Prefer moves that make config_ui.rs more clearly a Yazelix adapter and delete wrapper duplication. Reject moves that drag Yazelix product policy, action registry behavior, or config file ownership into ratconfig.
acceptance_criteria: config_ui.rs is smaller and delegates more generic state/navigation/editor/render behavior to yazelix_ratconfig; product policy remains local; focused config UI tests pass; Rust budget and inventory are updated; LOC delta and rejected candidates are recorded.
close_reason: Completed: moved generic config UI JSON path/value helpers into the private yazelix_ratconfig model boundary and collapsed duplicate choice movement. config_ui.rs dropped from 2749 to 2647 raw lines; total Rust budget dropped by 16 raw lines. Rejected moving apply-mode wording, JSONC patching, Home Manager ownership, cursor config, and Zellij action-registry details because those remain Yazelix adapter policy.

title: Migrate Yazelix planning tracker to beads_rust
description: Replace the Go/Dolt bd workflow with beads_rust br across repo guidance, maintainer shell packaging, GitHub issue sync, CI contract validation, and local .beads metadata. Preserve .beads/issues.jsonl as the durable shared planning state.
acceptance_criteria: br can import the tracked .beads/issues.jsonl and list ready issues; yzx dev sync_issues dry-run uses br; CI installs/initializes br instead of bd; AGENTS/docs command references use br; maintainer shell packages br; old bd flake input is removed.
notes:
Migrated live repo workflow from Go/Dolt bd to beads_rust br 0.2.11. br imports the tracked .beads/issues.jsonl into local SQLite cleanly, maintainer shell packages br from crates.io with Fenix nightly because fsqlite-types requires portable_simd, yzx dev sync_issues dry-run uses br and reports no GitHub/Beads mutations, and CI workflow installs br instead of bd.

Post-migration rationale, 2026-05-22:
Public criticism of the Go/Dolt bd direction matches Yazelix's experience that bd could become slow, invasive, and operationally heavy. Useful references:
- Alexander Holbreich's Beads review says bd's AGENTS.md auto-creation is invasive and that the Dolt-first direction, Dolt commits/push/pull, server mode, and Gas Town-adjacent orchestration feel overkill for solo or small-agent workflows: https://alexander.holbreich.org/posts/2026/beads-ai-native-issue-tracking/
- The official Beads README confirms bd init/setup can create or update AGENTS.md and other agent integration files by default unless skipped: https://github.com/gastownhall/beads
- A Beads+Dolt operations guide documents real bd/Dolt operational failure modes, including bd blocked hanging on >1k active issues, stale sync-state warnings, and JSONL/Dolt consistency problems: https://gist.github.com/l0g1x/dbae96d6af273832271386302c177438
- A Dolt server collision repair guide documents versions where multiple projects could silently connect to the wrong Dolt server/database, creating cross-project issue leakage risk: https://gist.github.com/PabloLION/827beb43e766a3f3bb28b12baf9715ed
- A broader community critique collects complaints about bloat, daemons, hooks, core.hooksPath ownership, pre-commit/pre-push behavior, sync branches, and config mutation: https://random.qmx.me/posts/2026/01/04/on-beads-bloat-and-breaking-points/
- The beads_rust README frames br as the classic SQLite + JSONL architecture with explicit git operations, no daemon, manual hook installation, smaller footprint, and fewer moving parts: https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_rust

Yazelix conclusion: keep the Beads idea, not the Go/Dolt control plane. The durable value is the local dependency-aware issue graph, ready queue, claims, and JSONL-in-git planning state. br gives Yazelix that value while avoiding automatic repo/agent-file ownership, Dolt/server complexity, daemon/hook behavior, and slow or surprising sync paths. This migration is therefore an architecture simplification, not just a CLI swap.
close_reason: Completed: repo guidance, .beads metadata, maintainer shell packaging, GitHub issue sync, docs, and CI contract workflow now use beads_rust br. Verified br import/status/ready, Nix maintainer-shell br 0.2.11, Rust formatting, repo_issue_sync unit tests, and yzx dev sync_issues --dry-run.

title: Adopt nixpkgs-unstable as Yazelix package-set baseline
description:
Evaluate and, if accepted, migrate Yazelix from `github:NixOS/nixpkgs/nixos-unstable` to `github:NixOS/nixpkgs/nixpkgs-unstable` as the default flake package-set policy.

This is broader than the Yazi/Chafa ghost-keypress fix. The strategic question is whether Yazelix should favor fresher terminal/TUI/runtime packages because the project depends on fast-moving Zellij, Yazi, terminal graphics/input behavior, Rust TUI tooling, and first-party plugin packaging.
design:
Working thesis:
- Yazelix is closer to an application/runtime distribution than a conservative NixOS system profile.
- Fresh terminal/TUI packages matter more to Yazelix than conservative NixOS module gating, because many key issues live at the edge of Yazi, Zellij, terminal emulator, graphics protocol, and Rust plugin behavior.
- `nixpkgs-unstable` may better match this vision because it usually carries application/package updates sooner than `nixos-unstable`.

Risks to evaluate before landing:
- This changes the whole package universe, not just Yazi. Runtime tools, build tools, Rust dependencies, Home Manager inputs, and child repos that follow the main `nixpkgs` input can all move.
- Binary cache behavior may change for some packages.
- Some NixOS-gated confidence is traded for fresher application packages.
- The Home Manager module and runtime package should keep one coherent package-set story.

Implementation shape, if accepted:
- Change `flake.nix` from `nixos-unstable` to `nixpkgs-unstable`.
- Update `flake.lock` with the new input revision.
- Record the before/after versions for the runtime-sensitive packages: Yazi, Chafa, Zellij, Nushell, WezTerm, Ghostty if packaged, and the Rust/plugin build inputs that materially affect Yazelix.
- Run focused runtime/package validation before any broad CI sweep.
- Decide whether `yazelix-q82i` is fixed by the migration or still needs a narrow Yazi overlay/guard.
- Document the package-set policy so future maintainers do not accidentally switch back during lockfile cleanup.
acceptance_criteria:
A completed decision/implementation result must show:
- `flake.nix` intentionally uses `github:NixOS/nixpkgs/nixpkgs-unstable`, or the bead explains why the migration was rejected
- `flake.lock` is updated and committed if the migration lands
- before/after package versions are recorded for Yazi, Chafa, Zellij, Nushell, WezTerm, and other runtime-sensitive packages
- `.#runtime` builds without local flake overrides
- the Home Manager package path still evaluates or builds through the flake-provided module path
- `yazelix-q82i` is explicitly resolved, superseded, or kept open with a narrower remaining fix
- docs or maintainer notes explain why Yazelix tracks `nixpkgs-unstable` rather than `nixos-unstable`
notes:
Package-set migration impact captured during implementation:

Before (`nixos-unstable`, root nixpkgs rev 4c1018dae018162ec878d42fec712642d214fdfa):
- yazi 26.1.22
- chafa 1.18.1
- zellij 0.44.1
- nushell 0.111.0
- wezterm 0-unstable-2026-03-31
- ghostty 1.3.1

After (`nixpkgs-unstable`, root nixpkgs rev 4a29d733e8a7d5b824c3d8c958a946a9867b3eb2):
- yazi 26.5.6
- chafa 1.18.1
- zellij 0.44.3
- nushell 0.112.2
- wezterm 0-unstable-2026-03-31
- ghostty 1.3.1

This package-set change should carry the upstream Yazi Chafa `--probe off` fix needed by yazelix-q82i through the normal runtime package path instead of a local Yazi overlay.
Verification evidence:
