title: Evaluate unified Yazelix extension and config contract
description:
Capture the architecture decision space for reducing extension/config fragmentation across the Yazelix stack.

Context:
- Zellij uses KDL for config/layouts and WebAssembly for plugins.
- Helix uses TOML today and upstream is pursuing a Scheme/Steel plugin direction, while at least one Helix fork already explores Lua.
- Yazi uses TOML plus Lua plugins.
- WezTerm uses Lua configuration/plugins.
- Terminal emulators also fragment around graphics, keyboard, and control protocols.

The core problem is not just syntax fragmentation. It is that each tool exposes a different lifecycle, event model, permission model, reload story, config schema, and extension packaging surface.
design:
Current take:
- Do not force every Yazelix tool to share one native language or file format. That would create fork maintenance without solving the deeper semantic mismatch.
- Keep settings.jsonc as the durable Yazelix user-facing semantic config because it is schema-friendly, Ratconfig-friendly, Home Manager-friendly, and easy for AI agents to edit safely.
- Treat generated KDL/TOML/Lua/terminal config files as implementation outputs, not the primary Yazelix mental model.
- Use Lua as the likely best common human/agent scripting layer because Yazi and WezTerm already use it, a Helix Lua fork exists, and the Lua ecosystem is much larger and easier to generate than Steel/Scheme.
- Keep Zellij WebAssembly for sandboxed workspace plugins because Wasm is still the better fit for multiplexer UI/plugin isolation and capability control.
- Add a shared Yazelix action/event API as the real unification point: editor.open(path), file_tree.reveal(path), workspace.focus(role), workspace.toggle_surface(surface), workspace.popup(slot, role), git.open_client(), terminal.graphics.capabilities(), etc.
- Let adapters call that same API from Yazi Lua, WezTerm Lua, a possible Helix Lua fork, Zellij Wasm plugins, and Rust runtime helpers.

Preferred final shape:
settings.jsonc -> durable declarative user intent
Lua scripts -> user/agent-authored behavior
Wasm plugins -> sandboxed Zellij workspace components
Yazelix action bus -> shared semantic contract across all tools
Generated native config -> KDL/TOML/Lua/terminal files as outputs

Fork stance:
- Fork Zellij only if it buys real product capability such as terminal graphics passthrough or better workspace/plugin hooks.
- Avoid forking Yazi unless blocked; its Lua surface is already compatible with the desired direction.
- Consider a Helix Lua fork only if it becomes stable enough and Yazelix needs editor-side integration that upstream Helix/Steel cannot provide cleanly.
- Do not fork terminals for config-language uniformity; terminal forks need a protocol/rendering reason.
acceptance_criteria:
This decision bead is useful when it produces one of:
- a written Yazelix extension/config contract proposal
- a spike plan for a Lua action-bus adapter across Yazi/WezTerm/Helix fork
- a decision to keep only generated native config and postpone extension unification
- a concrete fork/no-fork recommendation for Zellij, Helix, Yazi, or terminal surfaces

Any implementation bead derived from this must keep Yazelix main config JSONC-first and must avoid adding duplicate per-tool extension logic where a shared action/event contract would work.
notes: Side-conversation note, 2026-05-22: the attractive architecture is not all tools using Lua. It is one Yazelix semantic contract with JSONC for durable intent, Lua for common scripting where available, Wasm for Zellij plugin isolation, and generated native configs as outputs.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text:
Zed-inspired ideas worth keeping, 2026-05-30:

- Keep Helix as the terminal-native editor base. Do not fork Zed and strip the GUI; Zed is GPUI/desktop-first, so that path is effectively a new editor project.
- Use Zed as an architecture reference for extensions: manifest-first packaging, explicit capabilities, extension API versioning, stable host contracts, curated extension categories, and structured language-server provisioning.
- Treat Wasm as the long-term sandboxed extension ABI where it buys isolation or distribution safety; do not force it into simple config/plugin cases where Steel, Lua, or native generated config is enough.
- Prefer explicit command registration and namespace hygiene. Public commands should be intentionally exported; internal helpers should not leak into completion or user command lists.
- Zed Helix mode is a UX benchmark for modal polish, selections, keymap feel, and command discoverability. It should raise Yazelix expectations, not change the base editor choice.
- The durable Yazelix target remains one action/event contract with adapters into Helix/Steel, Yazi Lua, terminal hooks, Zellij plugins, and runtime helpers.

title: Design Zed-inspired Yazelix extension manifest and capability model
description:
Define a future Yazelix extension contract inspired by the useful parts of Zed without adopting Zed as the runtime base.

Scope:
- Manifest-first extension packaging for Yazelix plugin bundles.
- Explicit capability declarations for access to process execution, network/downloads, filesystem/workspace data, editor actions, terminal actions, and MCP/server integrations.
- Extension API versioning and stable host contracts.
- Curated extension categories such as languages, themes, command/actions, editor helpers, workspace tools, terminal integrations, and MCP/server tools.
- A rule that public commands must be registered intentionally, while internal helpers stay out of completion and user-visible command surfaces.

Acceptance criteria:
- Propose a minimal manifest shape with name, version, api version, categories, entrypoints, capabilities, and source/provenance fields.
- Define an initial capability vocabulary and which capabilities are denied by default.
- Decide where Wasm is useful versus where Steel, Lua, TOML/KDL generation, or Rust runtime code is the simpler fit.
- Define how the Yazelix action/event bus exposes stable commands to adapters without leaking tool internals.
- Explicitly record why terminal-Zed is not a base strategy and why Zed remains an architecture reference only.

Non-goal: implementing an extension runtime in this bead.

title: Bring the default suite back under the 75s runtime budget
description: The runtime-budget validator no longer hangs, but it now exposes a real default-suite runtime overrun: the profiled default suite takes about 82.37s against the explicit 75.0s cap. The current profile is dominated by the generated-config and doctor lanes, so CI now fails clearly on budget rather than stalling on leaked live Zellij subprocesses.
design: Treat this as a real budget-reduction or re-baselining decision, not a harness bug. Start from the measured profile now emitted by validate_default_test_budget: generated about 25.24s, doctor about 24.70s, core about 13.57s, workspace about 10.64s, with the other lanes much smaller. Prefer deleting avoidable expensive work from default-lane tests, moving slower but lower-signal coverage to the right nondefault lane, or narrowing repeated heavy regeneration paths before touching the cap. Only raise the 75s budget if the remaining work is truly high-signal and the slower profile is an intentional product decision.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The default-suite runtime budget validator passes under the intended cap, or the cap is intentionally changed with a documented rationale and updated validator. 2. The chosen fix is driven by the measured lane profile rather than guesswork. 3. Expensive work removed from the default lane is either deleted or moved to an appropriate nondefault lane with maintained coverage. 4. The follow-up keeps the validator deterministic and does not reintroduce live Zellij subprocess leaks.
notes: Observed profile after fixing yazelix-v6vs: generated 25.24s, doctor 24.70s, core 13.57s, workspace 10.64s, yazi 3.20s, refresh 2.21s, popup 1.58s, screen 1.12s. Total about 82.37s.
close_reason: Completed: deleted weak overlapping default-lane tests from the core, generated, doctor, and yazi tails until the budget validator passed again. validate_default_test_budget now completes successfully at about 74.09s <= 75.0s.

title: Preserve pane-relative cursor placement when Zellij replays Kitty APC frames
description: Kitty graphics passthrough itself is working in the Yazelix Zellij/Yazi fork path: APC frames can reach the terminal again. The remaining bug is narrower: the Zellij replay path captures Kitty graphics APC frames but does not preserve the pane-relative cursor position where the APC was emitted. Replayed frames can therefore start at the pane origin, so `yzx screen magician` inside framed or rounded Zellij panes can ignore the inset/padding that the screen renderer produced and draw against pane chrome. Preserve the original pane-relative cursor placement, or emit an equivalent cursor move before replaying each completed Kitty APC frame, so graphics placement survives redraws, tab switches, and framed-pane rendering.
design: Fix this in the Yazelix Zellij KGP passthrough fork, not as a Yazelix main-repo or `yazelix-screen` workaround. The current fork captures APCs in the terminal pane/grid path and re-emits them as raw VTE output from the pane rendering path. That replay path currently positions output at the pane origin before writing the captured APC frame. Extend the captured Kitty frame state to include the pane-local cursor coordinates at APC completion time, then replay that cursor movement immediately before the APC frame. Keep the existing passthrough behavior and memory cap intact. Prefer focused unit coverage around capture/replay serialization plus one real Yazelix runtime/manual check for `yzx screen magician` in a framed pane.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Existing Kitty graphics passthrough behavior remains working for Yazi image previews and `yzx screen magician`. 2. Replayed Kitty APC frames use the pane-relative cursor cell where the APC was originally emitted, rather than always replaying from the pane origin. 3. `yzx screen magician` in a framed or rounded Zellij pane keeps its intended inset away from pane borders after normal render and after a redraw/tab-switch style re-render. 4. The fix lands through the Yazelix Zellij fork release path and the main `flake.lock` consumes the published child commit. 5. Verification records both the focused automated check and the real runtime/manual graphics check used to close the bead.
notes:
Rust dependency decision: no new crates. Production change stays in the existing Zellij grid/terminal pane code using std-owned cursor coordinates and existing VTE render plumbing. Dev-only crates: none. Rejected alternatives: adding a terminal/Kitty parser crate would broaden the fork dependency surface for a small state-carrying fix; a Yazelix main-repo or yazelix-screen workaround would duplicate ownership and leave redraw replay wrong. Packaging impact: child Zellij fork commit plus main flake.lock update only.

Implementation evidence: child repo luccahuguet/yazelix-zellij commit bfbd06d5737ab4dffcc5cfb2ae3cd5fe1bda24f9 stores the captured pane-local cursor cell with each Kitty APC chunk and emits a cursor move before replaying each chunk. Published branch: yazelix_kgp_preview. Published tag: yazelix-kgp-preview-1.

Automated validation completed: cargo test -p zellij-server kitty_apc_replay -- --nocapture; nix build .#checks.x86_64-linux.kgp_package_contracts --no-link --no-write-lock-file --accept-flake-config; nix build .#yazelix_kgp_zellij --no-link --no-write-lock-file --accept-flake-config; nix build .#runtime_ghostty --no-link --no-write-lock-file --accept-flake-config. Manual graphics validation still pending before closing/pushing the main repo.

title: Evaluate Lenia welcome style later
design: Defer Lenia until the welcome-style system exists and the cost/readability tradeoffs are proven. Prefer a pre-rendered or precomputed frame-sequence evaluation first; only consider live Lenia later if it proves clearly worth the complexity. This bead is for evaluating whether Lenia should join the animated pool later, not for forcing a live simulation into startup prematurely.
acceptance_criteria: The evaluation documents whether Lenia should be live, precomputed, or rejected for startup use, and only proceeds after the shared welcome_style selector exists.

title: Profile desktop and managed-terminal startup as first-class scenarios
description:
yazelix-3n1 upgraded the current-terminal profiling surface, but desktop launch and managed new-window launch remain distinct user-felt startup paths with their own prelaunch logic, wrapper repair behavior, and terminal-selection contracts. They need first-class profiling scenarios instead of being inferred from the enter path.

Design:
Extend the profiling surface carefully instead of creating a second profiler. Add explicit scenario coverage for desktop launch and managed new-window launch so reports can attribute time to prelaunch work, visible bootstrap paths, terminal-wrapper repair, and the transition into the actual Yazelix session. Keep the same structured report schema and avoid speculative instrumentation that does not correspond to real ownership boundaries.

Acceptance Criteria:
1. The profiling surface can produce structured reports for desktop or managed new-window startup, not only current-terminal entry. 2. The measured phases include any prelaunch work that users actually wait on in those flows. 3. Reports stay comparable with the existing current-terminal profiling schema. 4. Documentation explains which scenarios are covered and how to invoke them.
notes:
opus 4.6 review:

CRITICAL BUG — profiler invocation pattern is wrong for both new scenarios.

run_desktop_profile_command runs:
  ^$nu_bin desktop.nu "desktop launch"

run_launch_profile_command runs:
  ^$nu_bin launch.nu "yzx" "launch"

But desktop.nu has export def "yzx desktop launch" (no def main), and launch.nu has export def "yzx launch" (no def main). Running these as direct scripts with arguments will NOT dispatch to the exported commands. Nushell only dispatches to def main or export def main for script execution.

The production path in yzx_cli.sh uses a fundamentally different pattern:
  nu -c "use desktop.nu *; yzx desktop launch"

The test code also uses this pattern:
  ^nu -c $"use \"($desktop_script)\" *; yzx desktop launch" | complete

The profiler commands would load the scripts, define all functions, and exit without executing any profiled code. The report would have no steps or fail on report loading.

Fix: change both profiler commands to use the nu -c "use ... *; command" invocation pattern matching the production dispatcher and tests.

OTHER ISSUES:

2. Dead code in run_desktop_profile_command — the variable start_script is defined as the path to core/start_yazelix.nu but never used. Copy-pasted from run_dev_profile_harness which does use it.

3. Inconsistent env key lists — launch.nu propagate_profile_env propagates 6 keys (missing YAZELIX_SHELLHOOK_SKIP_WELCOME). launch_yazelix.nu launch_terminal_candidates propagates 7 keys (includes YAZELIX_SHELLHOOK_SKIP_WELCOME). Not a functional bug right now since the var propagates via process env inheritance, but an inconsistency that could bite if the architecture changes to explicit env blocks. Should either add the missing key to launch.nu or extract both lists into a shared constant.

4. No tests added — the implementation brief says to add focused tests defending scenario selection and the presence of desktop/launch-specific profile steps in structured output. None were added.

5. No documentation changes — acceptance criterion 4 requires documenting which scenarios are covered and how to invoke them. No docs were updated.

WHAT IS GOOD:

- The instrumentation approach is correct — profile_startup_step wrapping at ownership boundaries users actually wait on, not speculative internals.
- Step names are well-chosen: resolve_runtime_dir, build_launch_env, fast_path_handoff, parse_config, terminal_handoff, compute_config_state, validate_working_dir, resolve_terminals, generate_terminal_configs, reroll_ghostty_cursor, launch_terminal. These map cleanly to real wait phases.
- Profile env propagation through launch_terminal_candidates in launch_yazelix.nu is correctly implemented with the explicit key list.
- The desktop_fast_path vs launch component distinction in launch_yazelix.nu correctly attributes steps to the right scenario.
- Scenario naming (desktop_launch, managed_launch) is clear and compatible with the existing schema.
- The --desktop, --launch, --terminal, --verbose flag additions to yzx dev profile are natural extensions of the existing interface.
- Correct IN_YAZELIX_SHELL guard on both new profiler commands.
- Normal non-profile launch paths are behaviorally unchanged — profile_startup_step is a no-op when profiling env is absent.

VERDICT:

Do not ship as-is. The invocation bug means both new scenarios produce no useful data. Fix the invocation to use nu -c "use ... *; command" pattern, remove the dead start_script variable, align the env key lists, add tests, and update docs.
2026-04-18 implementation fix note:

Reviewed the first implementation and found two blockers: `yzx dev profile --desktop`/`--launch` invoked leaf modules as plain script files, so Nushell loaded them and exited without running `yzx desktop launch` or `yzx launch`; and detached terminal profiling rendered the report before the spawned startup path had necessarily written `inner.zellij_handoff_ready`.

Fix shape:
- `yzx dev profile --desktop` and `--launch` now invoke leaf commands via `nu -c 'use <module> *; yzx ...'`, matching the stable CLI wrapper command model.
- Desktop, launch, and launch_yazelix now share startup-profile env propagation, including the shellhook skip flag, so child terminal startup writes into the same JSONL report.
- The profile harness waits boundedly for `inner.zellij_handoff_ready` before summarizing detached desktop/managed launch reports.
- `terminal_launcher.nu` records `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe` around the spawn/probe wait and early-death capture seam.
- Docs now list `--desktop`, `--launch`, and `--terminal` profiling scenarios.

Defended by focused maintainer tests for desktop/launch profiler invocation and handoff waiting, terminal-launcher probe instrumentation, full maintainer lane, workspace canonical lane, syntax validation, traceability validation, upgrade-contract validation, nu-lint, and `git diff --check`.
2026-04-18 spec note: added live spec docs/specs/startup_profile_scenarios.md and listed it in docs/specs/spec_inventory.md so the desktop/managed-launch profile scenario contract is durable for the Rust rewrite baseline.
2026-04-18 runtime fallback fix note: 🚀 Profiling warm Yazelix startup from the current shell...

title: Track upstream Zellij stacked-pane ordering fix
description: Track upstream Zellij issue #4084, where stacked panes change order when NewPane is called from the middle of a stack. This matters directly to Yazelix pane pinning: if upstream preserves stacked pane order correctly, Yazelix-side editor pane pinning and pane-drift work may become unnecessary or should be reduced to validation only.
design: Local Yazelix pane-pinning mechanics are intentionally dropped. Treat upstream Zellij issue #4084 as the right owner for the stacked-pane ordering bug. Preferred sequence: first wait for upstream movement; if the bug blocks Yazelix strongly enough, prepare a focused PR to Zellij; if release timing blocks users after a fix exists or the PR needs a proving path, consider a thin temporary Zellij fork that carries only this patch and has an explicit removal gate. Avoid reviving Yazelix-side editor pinning unless upstream rejects the behavior and a local workaround is the only viable product path.
acceptance_criteria: Zellij #4084 is checked after upstream movement or a Zellij version bump. When upstream fixes stacked-pane ordering in a consumable release, Yazelix validates pane creation, focus changes, workspace retargeting, and editor/sidebar stack behavior, then closes this tracker. If the issue remains important and stale, this bead should produce either a focused upstream PR plan or a thin-fork plan with a removal gate; it should not reopen local pane-pinning mechanics by default.
notes:
2026-05-01 decision: dropped local pane-pinning beads yazelix-3hnk and yazelix-2ex.1.7.7. The product direction is upstream-first: wait for Zellij, contribute the Zellij fix, or temporarily carry a minimal Zellij fork with only that fix.
Plan repair 2026-05-03: priority lowered from P1 to P3 because this is an upstream tracker with no immediate local action unless Zellij issue #4084 moves, a Zellij version bump lands, or pane ordering becomes a current release blocker again. Keep the tracker open, but do not let it dominate ready-work selection.

title: Refresh architecture map and subsystem LOC inventory for current v15 repo shape

Update architecture_map.md to define the real current subsystems and ownership boundaries, then regenerate subsystem_code_inventory.md with a fresh code-only LOC breakdown that matches those subsystem boundaries. The result should make it easy to answer both 'what subsystems does Yazelix have now?' and 'how much does each subsystem weigh?' without relying on stale v14-era buckets or counts.
close_reason: Completed

title: Let Home Manager install Yazelix without owning yazelix.toml
description: Home Manager currently always generates ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/yazelix.toml through xdg.configFile, so users who install Yazelix with Home Manager cannot edit their normal Yazelix config file directly. This couples package/runtime ownership to config ownership and creates bad UX for users who want HM to install the tool but still want Yazelix config to be mutable through yzx edit or their editor. Add an explicit Home Manager config ownership mode so the module can install/activate Yazelix while leaving the main yazelix.toml user-managed by default or by option.
acceptance_criteria: Home Manager users can choose not to generate ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/yazelix.toml; when unmanaged, Yazelix bootstraps/validates the normal mutable config file; generated runtime repair still works; docs explain the tradeoff between Home Manager-owned declarative config and user-managed mutable config; existing fully declarative Home Manager behavior remains available behind an explicit option.
notes:
Local workaround validated in /home/lucca/.config/home-manager/home.nix: set xdg.configFile."yazelix/user_configs/yazelix.toml".enable = lib.mkForce false while keeping programs.yazelix.enable = true. Home Manager switch succeeded; activation removed the managed symlink and Yazelix bootstrapped a normal writable ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/yazelix.toml from the shipped default template. This confirms the repo feature can be implemented as a first-class Home Manager option rather than forcing full config ownership.
Implemented first-class Home Manager option programs.yazelix.manage_config. Default remains true for compatibility with existing declarative Home Manager users; setting it false keeps the package/runtime/desktop entry Home Manager-owned while leaving ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/yazelix.toml mutable and Yazelix-bootstrapped. Validator now exercises both activation modes and checks managed mode produces a symlink while unmanaged mode does not. Local Home Manager config switched from xdg.configFile mkForce workaround to programs.yazelix.manage_config = false, flake.lock updated to commit 867fa1f6, home-manager switch succeeded, yzx why works, and yazelix.toml is a normal file.
close_reason: Completed: added programs.yazelix.manage_config with validator coverage for both config ownership modes; local Home Manager setup now uses manage_config=false.

title: Hide sidebar after first editor pane creation
description: When hide_sidebar_on_file_open is true and a tab starts as the single Yazi/sidebar pane, the pre-open hide command runs before Zellij has enough panes for the managed closed layout. If opening the file creates the managed editor pane, run one post-create hide pass so the initial sidebar pane collapses after the editor exists. Keep existing pre-open hide behavior for tabs where the editor pane already exists.
notes: Implemented local fix: when hide_sidebar_on_file_open creates the editor pane from the initial single-Yazi/sidebar state, yzx_control now runs one post-create hide pass after a short layout-settle wait. This preserves the existing pre-open hide for tabs with an existing editor while covering the first-open case where Zellij cannot apply the closed swap layout until the editor pane exists. Added regressions for open-editor and open-editor-cwd first-pane paths. Verification passed: yzx dev rust fmt --check; yzx dev rust check core; focused open-editor/open-editor-cwd hide tests; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_control_workspace_surface.
close_reason: Implemented and verified post-create hide pass for first editor pane creation

title: Evaluate pane-orchestrator artifact ownership boundary
description: Evaluate whether the pane orchestrator should remain a separate source repo with a synced tracked wasm in Yazelix, move to a locked first-party artifact/package consumption model, or return to the main repo. The goal is to reduce source/wasm drift and runtime breakage without adding duplicate implementation code or making main repo ownership heavier than needed.
design:
Decision: move pane-orchestrator ownership from tracked copied wasm toward locked child package consumption. Keep the current copied wasm plus sync stamp only as the interim release-safe guardrail until the child repository publishes a package output.

Current synced-wasm model:
- Strengths: works today, requires no child flake/package, supports fast local plugin iteration, and has useful guardrails: clean-source sync, source hash/commit/remote stamp, tracked wasm hash, and validate-pane-orchestrator-sync.
- Weaknesses: main repo still owns a binary artifact that is not lock-file provenance; CI on artifact-only checkouts can validate stamp/hash consistency but does not rebuild the child source; release and rollback require coordinating child commit, copied wasm, sync stamp, main repo commit, and runtime validation; drift risk remains procedural rather than architectural.

Locked child artifact/package consumption:
- Strengths: child source repo owns source, wasm build, package checks, artifact name, and standalone examples; main flake.lock becomes the normal provenance and rollback surface; regular users consume a selected artifact without adjacent mutable checkouts; CI can build both child package and integrated runtime; local development can use explicit flake overrides instead of committing copied binary drift.
- Weaknesses: pane-orchestrator first needs a child flake/package output and a stable package artifact path; local iteration needs explicit override commands; release ordering must update child first, then main flake.lock; Nix package checks must cover wasm32-wasip1 reliably.

Merge back to main repo:
- Strengths: one repository would make source, tests, main runtime integration, and rollback atomic.
- Weaknesses: it would reabsorb a useful standalone Zellij plugin boundary, grow main-repo Rust ownership, weaken the child repo adoption story, and still leave the runtime ABI as wasm. It solves cross-repo coordination by deleting a valuable ownership split rather than by fixing artifact consumption.

Recommendation:
1. Do not merge the pane orchestrator back into Yazelix now.
2. Do not treat tracked copied wasm as the durable architecture.
3. Add a package output to yazelix-zellij-pane-orchestrator that exposes a stable wasm artifact for the Yazelix runtime name, e.g. share/yazelix_zellij_pane_orchestrator/yazelix_pane_orchestrator.wasm or an equivalent documented path.
4. Add a locked main flake input for the child package and teach packaging/mk_runtime_tree.nix to place that artifact into configs/zellij/plugins/yazelix_pane_orchestrator.wasm.
5. Keep local maintainer development through explicit override-input or source-dir flows, with no local-only path leakage into committed runtime definitions.
6. After package consumption is live, delete configs/zellij/plugins/yazelix_pane_orchestrator.wasm and its sync stamp from main repo source ownership, and retarget validation from copied-artifact stamp checks to flake/package/runtime artifact checks.

Evaluation details:
- Local testability is best with locked package consumption plus override-input: maintainers can test adjacent child changes without publishing them, while committed main state stays lock-driven.
- Release ordering should be child source/package first, main flake.lock second, integrated runtime validation third, Beads/GitHub closure last.
- CI coverage should build the child package and main runtime, not only compare a copied binary to a stamp.
- User runtime closure should still contain only the wasm artifact and generated Zellij integration, not child source.
- Zellij permission prompts are mostly independent of provenance model; recent generated-state repair and permission upsert logic remains necessary in every option.
- Rollback is cleanest as flake.lock rollback plus runtime rebuild; copied wasm rollback requires binary/stamp/main coordination; merge-back rollback is atomic but at the cost of larger main ownership.
- Source/wasm drift risk is lowest when flake.lock selects the child source revision and Nix builds the artifact, rather than when humans copy wasm into the main repo.
acceptance_criteria: Produce an evaluation-only recommendation that compares current synced-wasm ownership, locked child artifact consumption, and merge-back-to-main; evaluates local testability, release ordering, CI coverage, user runtime closure, permission prompts, rollback behavior, and source/wasm drift risk; records a recommended boundary and rejected alternatives; does not implement migration or code changes.
notes: Evaluated against docs/contracts/first_party_zellij_plugin_wasm_ownership.md, docs/contracts/artifact_first_child_integration.md, docs/contracts/pane_orchestrator_component.md, configs/zellij/plugins/yazelix_pane_orchestrator.sync_stamp, packaging/mk_runtime_tree.nix, rust_core/yazelix_maintainer/src/repo_plugin_build.rs, rust_core/yazelix_core/src/zellij_materialization.rs, and rust_core/yazelix_core/src/workspace_asset_contract.rs. Current child repo ../yazelix-zellij-pane-orchestrator has Cargo sources but no flake/package output; ../yazelix-zellij-popup already has flake.nix and is closer to the target package model.
close_reason: Completed: recorded the artifact ownership boundary decision. Recommendation is locked child package consumption for pane-orchestrator wasm, with current copied wasm plus sync stamp retained only as an interim guardrail until the child repo publishes a package output. Rejected merge-back because it grows main-repo ownership and weakens standalone plugin value while still leaving the runtime ABI as wasm. Compared current synced-wasm, locked package consumption, and merge-back across local testability, release ordering, CI coverage, user runtime closure, permission prompts, rollback, and source/wasm drift risk.

title: Migrate from beads_rust (br) to gastownhall/beads (bd) for stable issue tracking
description:
The current Rust br (beads_rust v0.1.38) has chronic fsqlite DB corruption: writes corrupt pages, dotted-ID lookups fail for show/update while list succeeds, parent-child dep add falsely detects cycles, and the DB must be rebuilt from JSONL after write-heavy sessions. The upstream maintainer has no open issues and no v0.1.39 release, and the fsqlite engine is the root cause across 15+ closed issues (#138, #156, #223, #227, #228, #230, #233, #234, #235).

gastownhall/beads (bd, Go, v1.0.0, 20.6k stars) uses embedded Dolt instead of fsqlite, has proper single-writer locking, native hierarchical ID support, and a stable v1.0 release. The AGENTS.md workflow already references bd-style command patterns (br create, br dep add, br close, bv --robot-triage) which bd shares with compatible semantics.

This epic tracks the full migration: assessment, data export, schema mapping, AGENTS.md rewrite, Home Manager packaging, and validation.
close_reason: Completed: all tracked child beads are closed and the parent scope is done.

title: Delete the remaining br-to-bd migration scaffolding once bd retirement is fully validated
description: The repo still carries leftover migration scaffolding such as scripts/migrate_br_to_bd.py and scattered historical br references. Once the bd workspace is fully validated and br retirement is complete, delete the remaining repo-local migration helper code and trim any non-historical references that are no longer serving an active recovery path.
design: Treat this as a post-validation cleanup gate, not a premature deletion pass. Keep genuinely useful historical narrative where needed, but remove live tooling or workflow references that imply br is still part of the normal maintainer surface.
acceptance_criteria: After gm39 is satisfied, the remaining br-to-bd migration scaffolding is either deleted or explicitly justified; live maintainer workflows no longer reference br except for clearly historical context; and the repo no longer carries dead migration helper code by inertia alone.
close_reason: Completed: removed tracked br-to-bd migration artifacts, switched live maintainer issue sync and CI validation from br to bd, and updated docs/AGENTS references to the bd workflow.

title: Evaluate centralizing more shared data into constants.nu and whether it now needs refactoring
description: Audit the current role of nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu and decide whether more shared data should be centralized there or whether the file has reached the point where further growth would be counterproductive. Scope: inventory existing constants-like data that is still duplicated elsewhere, identify what truly benefits from centralization, and recommend whether constants.nu should stay as-is, absorb more shared data, or be split into smaller focused modules.
design: Treat this as a focused audit, not as a mandate to turn constants.nu into a dumping ground. Identify which duplicated values are genuinely constants-like and shared across multiple real consumers, and which ones should stay near the subsystem that owns them. The output should distinguish between centralizing more data into constants.nu, leaving the file mostly as-is, or splitting it into smaller focused modules if its current role has become too mixed.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation for constants.nu: keep as-is, centralize a specific shortlist of additional shared data, or refactor the file into smaller modules. The recommendation is grounded in actual duplicated data ownership sites, not taste alone. Any suggested next steps leave the repo with clearer ownership of shared values, not more indirection.
notes: Completed the constants audit on v15. Decision: keep constants.nu as a focused shared-values module rather than splitting it or growing it into a subsystem registry. The file is still small (~311 lines), and most remaining lists are subsystem-owned or test fixture data. One concrete centralization was worth doing: production paths that still hard-coded the existing DEFAULT_SHELL/DEFAULT_TERMINAL values now import those constants instead. Verified with validate_syntax, the full yzx default suite, and git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed constants audit and centralized the existing shell/terminal defaults without broadening constants.nu.

title: Remove yzx warp command surface
description: Remove the yzx warp command because Yazelix no longer needs a dedicated warp workspace navigation surface.
design: Delete-first command-surface cleanup. Remove the public command metadata, routing, docs, tests, and palette/menu references for yzx warp instead of keeping a compatibility alias. Preserve lower-level workspace retarget/open-tab helpers only if other command surfaces still use them.
acceptance_criteria: yzx warp is no longer listed in public help, command metadata, docs, or palette categories; yzx_control routing no longer accepts the warp command; tests that only defend warp are removed or retargeted to surviving workspace behavior; focused command-surface and contract validators pass.
close_reason: Completed: removed the yzx warp command surface from Rust routing, public command metadata, docs, contracts, tutor text, and stale docs validation; kept surviving cwd/reveal/popup workspace behavior and verified focused command/docs/contract checks.

title: Delete simple Zellij command wrappers through direct pane-orchestrator actions
description: Audit outcome: several files in nushell/scripts/zellij_wrappers only translate a Zellij keybinding into one pane-orchestrator pipe command. Rust-owned Zellij materialization should be able to emit direct pane-orchestrator actions for those simple cases instead of spawning tiny Nu wrapper programs.
design: Target first: next_layout_family.nu, previous_layout_family.nu, toggle_sidebar_layout.nu, and open_workspace_terminal.nu. Retained behavior: exact keybinding behavior, pane-orchestrator session-local target, error visibility, and plugin permission requirements. Candidate surviving owner: Rust Zellij materialization emits direct Zellij actions; pane-orchestrator remains the live state owner. Stop condition: keep a wrapper when Zellij KDL cannot express the action without losing failure visibility or session-local plugin targeting.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The simple wrapper set is confirmed against generated Zellij config call sites. 2. Direct actions preserve existing pane-orchestrator command names and session-local behavior. 3. Wrappers that no longer own behavior are deleted from runtime materialization, docs, and tests. 4. Non-trivial wrappers such as launch_sidebar_yazi.nu and popup/menu wrappers are explicitly kept or split into later beads. 5. Verification includes Zellij materialization tests and any wrapper contract tests affected by the deletion.
close_reason: Completed

title: Step 5: record the seam, decision, and follow-up ownership
description: For candidate refactors under `yazelix-790`, capture the final seam, deleted assumptions, and any remaining owner beads or spec updates after the simpler contract is verified. The goal is to make the refactor durable in project memory instead of leaving the new boundary implicit.
notes:
2026-04-08 recording guidance:

- In this lane, that includes explicitly noting large-but-acceptable files such as `ascii_art.nu`, `core/yazelix.nu`, and the current `yazi_config_merger.nu` generator, so the audit does not reopen the same questions immediately.
close_reason: Completed

title: Fix broken zjstatus widget tray command rendering
description: The zjstatus widget tray already supports workspace, AI activity, and token widgets, but generated layouts call bare `yzx_control` from plugin commands. The Yazelix runtime intentionally keeps `yzx_control` under `libexec/`, so these widgets can render as empty bracket shells such as `[workspace ] [ai ] [tokens ]`. Fix generated command paths to use resolved runtime helpers and make status-bus widgets hide themselves when they have no data instead of reserving empty brackets.
acceptance_criteria: Generated zjstatus templates call resolved Yazelix runtime helper paths, not bare `yzx_control`; workspace/AI/token widgets render complete segments only when they have data; empty command output does not leave `[workspace ]`, `[ai ]`, or `[tokens ]` shells; focused Rust tests defend the generated template and widget command output behavior.
close_reason: Fixed. Generated zjstatus commands now resolve runtime `libexec/yzx_control`/`libexec/nu` paths instead of relying on bare PATH lookup, and workspace/AI/token status-bus commands now emit complete zjstatus segments only when they have data. Empty AI/token facts produce no stdout, so the bar no longer leaves `[ai ]` or `[tokens ]` shells. Verified with `yzx dev rust fmt --check`, `yzx dev rust check`, `yzx dev rust test zjstatus`, `yzx dev rust test status_bus`, and `cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_bar`.

Remove launch-time shell-hook ownership from the normal runtime path instead of widening Home Manager heuristics.
