description: repo_contract_validation.rs remains one of the largest Rust files. Split maintainer contract validation by domain and delete validator trivia so the maintainer crate stays useful without becoming a monolithic implementation-detail checker.
design: Delete-first maintainer split. Keep the validator in-repo for now. Move cohesive validator domains into private modules only when that reduces the parent surface; delete duplicate or low-value checks that defend implementation trivia instead of durable contracts.
acceptance_criteria: repo_contract_validation.rs is smaller and delegates at least one meaningful domain to a private module; weak or duplicate validator checks are deleted where stronger behavior or contract validation remains; validator command behavior stays stable; focused maintainer validator tests and relevant repo validators pass; LOC delta and rejected candidates are recorded.
notes:
Implemented maintainer validator domain split.

Moved main config contract parity, Home Manager option declaration checks, desktop-entry evaluation, generated-state validation, startup session/cache env validation, and related unit tests into repo_contract_validation/config_surface.rs. Moved upgrade-note/changelog validation plus CI guarded-change rules into repo_contract_validation/upgrade_contract.rs. Kept installed-runtime, flake interface, Nix customization, nixpkgs package/submission, and profile-install validators in the parent because they still share runtime/package helper IO and defend live packaging behavior.

Delete-first notes: collapsed duplicated Zellij/Yazi keybinding registry default validators into one shared helper over YazelixActionMetadata. Did not delete runtime/package validators; they are expensive but still defend packaging and Home Manager behavior rather than implementation trivia. Next split candidate is installed-runtime and Nix/package validation.

LOC/accounting: repo_contract_validation.rs shrank from 3,583 to 2,086 raw lines. The maintainer validator family moved from 10,472 raw lines / 17 files to 10,463 raw lines / 19 files. Full tracked Rust budget moved from 72,745 raw lines / 154 files to 72,736 raw lines / 156 files. Tokei Rust code moved from 66,607 to 66,559 LOC.

Verification: cargo fmt --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml --all -- --check; cargo check --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_maintainer --all-targets; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_maintainer repo_contract_validation; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_maintainer --test repo_upgrade_contract; cargo run --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_maintainer --bin yzx_repo_validator -- validate-config-surface-contract; cargo run --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_maintainer --bin yzx_repo_validator -- validate-upgrade-contract; cargo run --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_maintainer --bin yzx_repo_validator -- validate-rust-ownership-budget; cargo run --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_maintainer --bin yzx_repo_validator -- validate-docs-experience; git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed maintainer validator domain split; focused tests and validator commands pass.

title: Full-owner terminal config materialization in Rust
description: Move terminal config generation (Ghostty, WezTerm, Kitty, Alacritty, Foot) from utils/terminal_configs.nu and utils/terminal_renderers.nu to a Rust-owned terminal-materialization.generate command. This is the largest remaining deterministic config-generation family. Follow the proven full-owner pattern from Yazi/Zellij/Helix materialization. Delete the Nushell owner end-to-end.
close_reason: Both children complete: gite (Ghostty materialization, -240 lines) and c8wc (non-Ghostty terminals + orchestrator, -255 lines). Total terminal materialization family deleted from Nushell.

title: Resolve Zellij layout ownership before yazelix_workspace extraction
description: Create the gate that must close before Yazelix treats yazelix_workspace as extractable. Workspace extraction is still too coupled to layout/profile/override behavior, so layout ownership needs to be settled before another workspace boundary push.
design: Reconcile the existing layout work: override-layout viability through explicit-run pane creation, bottom-bar/zen-mode geometry, and user-declared Zellij layout profiles. Define which layer owns layout presets, user profile rendering, pane creation identity, bottom/status-bar visibility, override-layout transitions, and workspace/editor/sidebar invariants. The output should explicitly say whether yazelix_workspace can stay internal, become an internal crate, or move toward child-repo extraction after these layout decisions.
acceptance_criteria: The gate is complete when yazelix-pgdq, yazelix-2ex.1.7.6.1, and yazelix-fhf1 have been resolved or explicitly rejected/deferred, and a short workspace extraction readiness note states the remaining coupling. No yazelix_workspace extraction implementation should begin until this gate is closed or intentionally superseded.
notes:
2026-05-07 start: resolving the layout ownership gate before yazelix-gr41. Rust dependency gate: no Rust implementation planned for the gate; docs/Beads ownership decision only; no new crates or packaging impact.
2026-05-07 completion: layout ownership gate resolved. Built-in layout family metadata and generated KDL assets stay in Yazelix core/runtime; pane-orchestrator owns live tab-local layout/sidebar state; custom user work remains explicit KDL plus sidebar_command/sidebar_args; Home Manager does not get a second declarative layout profile language. Rejected override-layout resurrection for now and deferred the bottom-bar POC. Workspace extraction remains internal-only until zellij_materialization.rs and zellij_commands.rs shrink around this owner model. This unblocks yazelix-gr41.
close_reason: Completed: layout ownership gate closed; override-layout/profile branches rejected or deferred and Zellij cleanup can target built-in family metadata plus KDL assets.

title: Auto-repair legacy sidebar setting rename
description: Repair writable settings.jsonc files that still contain editor.sidebar_command, editor.sidebar_args, or editor.sidebar_width_percent by moving them once to workspace.left_sidebar.command, workspace.left_sidebar.args, and workspace.left_sidebar.width_percent before strict unknown-field validation. Do not keep runtime aliases. Fail clearly if the destination already has a conflicting custom value or the file is Home Manager/read-only owned.
close_reason: Completed: writable settings.jsonc now auto-repairs the lossless v16.5 sidebar rename from editor.sidebar_command/sidebar_args/sidebar_width_percent to workspace.left_sidebar.command/args/width_percent before strict unknown-field validation. The repair is in-memory and written once, does not add runtime aliases, preserves custom legacy values over absent/default destinations, and fails clearly on conflicting destination values or Home Manager/read-only ownership. Verification: cargo fmt --check, cargo check -p yazelix_core, cargo test -p yazelix_core settings_surface::tests::, validate-config-surface-contract, validate-upgrade-contract, git diff --check, nix build .#runtime --no-link.

title: Build scoped Yazelix action registry metadata
description: Create durable metadata for Yazelix-owned actions so config UI, yzx keys, docs, and doctor diagnostics can talk about the same stable action ids without parsing generated backend config.
design: Start with existing zellij.keybindings actions and the keybinding_action_ownership contract. Registry entries should include scoped id, label, owner subsystem, supported backend, default binding, generated backend command, disable policy, and diagnostics Yazelix can prove. Keep native sidecars as escape hatches and do not add a broad cross-tool DSL.
acceptance_criteria: The registry enumerates current Yazelix-owned Zellij actions with stable scoped ids; yzx keys or another read path can display labels/defaults from registry metadata; config UI/docs can reference the same ids; unsupported/duplicate semantic action diagnostics still work; no arbitrary native keymap ownership is added.
notes:
Implemented scoped action registry metadata for the current Yazelix-owned Zellij semantic action set in rust_core/yazelix_core/src/action_registry.rs. Registry entries now carry scoped id, owner-local id, label, owner subsystem, backend, default keys, generated backend command summary, disable policy, and proven diagnostic codes.

Wired zellij_materialization.rs to consume the registry for default semantic keybindings, supported action lookup, duplicate validation, and generated MessagePlugin binds. Wired yzx keys to display a Semantic remaps table from the same registry metadata with scoped ids, defaults, and labels. Updated keybinding docs/contract to distinguish scoped registry ids from owner-local zellij.keybindings ids.

Created follow-ups yazelix-poy6.1 for config UI rendering from the registry and yazelix-poy6.2 for maintainer validation that config contract defaults stay in sync with the registry.

Verification: yzx dev rust fmt --check; yzx dev rust check; yzx dev rust test keys_commands; yzx dev rust test zellij_materialization; cargo run --quiet --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --bin yzx_control -- keys; cargo run --quiet --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_maintainer --bin yzx_repo_validator -- validate-contracts; git diff --check. yzx run yzx_repo_validator validate-contracts is not exposed through the runtime wrapper, so the maintainer binary was used directly.
close_reason: Completed: added scoped action registry metadata, wired Zellij materialization and yzx keys to it, updated docs, and created config UI/validation follow-ups.

title: Use action registry metadata in config UI keybinding editor
description: Teach the config UI keybindings tab to render Yazelix action registry labels, scoped ids, defaults, backend ownership, and disable policy instead of treating zellij.keybindings as an opaque string-list map.
acceptance_criteria: The keybindings tab shows current/remapped/default values with labels from the action registry. Empty lists are presented according to the registry disable policy. Unsupported action ids still fail through the existing config diagnostics rather than becoming arbitrary native keymap ownership.
notes: Rust dependency decision for implementation: no new crates. Use existing action_registry metadata and config_ui rendering/editing helpers; build registry-aware keybinding rows/details in-house because the action model is Yazelix-specific. Rejected alternatives: generic keybinding editor dependency, parsing native Zellij/Yazi keymaps here, or accepting arbitrary action ids in the UI. Packaging impact: none.
close_reason: Completed: config UI zellij.keybindings details now render action registry labels, scoped ids, default/remapped/disabled keys, backend metadata, and unsupported entries, with focused config_ui coverage.

title: Validate config contract keybinding defaults against action registry
description: Add a maintainer validation path that compares config_metadata/main_config_contract.toml zellij.keybindings defaults with the Rust action registry defaults, so schema/Home Manager/config UI defaults cannot drift from generated Zellij bindings.
acceptance_criteria: A focused maintainer validator fails when a Zellij action id/default exists in the config contract but not the action registry, or vice versa. The check is wired into the appropriate config/contract validation lane without adding low-value help-output tests.
notes: Rust dependency decision for implementation: no new crates. Use existing toml parsing, BTreeMap/BTreeSet, and yazelix_core action_registry exports. Build the parity validator in-house because it compares two Yazelix-owned metadata tables. Rejected alternatives: runtime test through rendered Zellij KDL or help-output assertions, both weaker and noisier. Packaging impact: none; maintainer validation only.
close_reason: Completed action-registry parity validation for zellij.keybindings defaults and wired it into validate-config-surface-contract.

title: Pretty-print runtime repair summary errors
description:
Make runtime-materialization.repair --summary render concise human-readable failures instead of dumping raw JSON envelopes into activation logs and popup panes

## Context
Home Manager activation and transient repair surfaces use runtime-materialization.repair --summary for human output
Success already prints one clean line, but errors still fall through the global yzx_core JSON envelope writer
The resulting unsupported_config JSON blob is technically useful but ugly and overwhelming for users
design:
Keep the default yzx_core error envelope unchanged for machine-readable callers
Only summary-mode runtime repair should use human stderr formatting
For unsupported_config, show the headline, count, affected fields, and next actions without dumping the full diagnostics object
Avoid hiding details: point users to yzx doctor --verbose for the full report
acceptance_criteria:
runtime-materialization.repair --summary prints a concise human error when repair fails
Non-summary runtime-materialization.repair still emits the JSON error envelope
unsupported_config diagnostics are summarized by path/headline and actionable next steps
Regression coverage defends the summary-mode failure surface
notes: Implementation: runtime-materialization.repair now carries an error output mode. Summary-mode repair failures render a concise human stderr block with reason, blocking issue count, diagnostic headlines, and deduplicated next steps. Non-summary repair failures keep the JSON error envelope. Verification: yzx dev rust fmt --check; yzx dev rust check; focused runtime_materialization_repair_summary_prints_human_config_error; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_core_config_normalize; yzx_repo_validator validate-rust-test-traceability; git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: runtime repair summary failures now print a concise human error instead of raw JSON.

title: Evaluate Ratty/RGP VFX backend for yzx screen
description: Evaluate whether yzx screen should support Ratty's new graphics engine as an optional enhanced VFX backend, including a possible 3D Mandelbrot/Mandelbulb-style demo. Ratty is a GPU-rendered terminal emulator with a custom Ratty Graphics Protocol (RGP) for inline 3D objects, so this is a terminal-capability decision, not a replacement for the normal Ghostty/WezTerm terminal-native Mandelbrot path.
design:
Research context, 2026-05-22:
- Ratty's announcement describes a GPU-rendered terminal built with Rust, Ratatui, Vello/parley, and Bevy, with the terminal surface rendered into a 2D/3D scene.
- Ratty Graphics Protocol uses APC sequences under the `ratty;g` namespace to query support, register OBJ/GLB assets, place objects at terminal cell anchors, update transforms, and delete objects.
- `ratatui-ratty` can emit RGP messages from Ratatui apps, but RGP is explicitly architecture-specific to Ratty and not expected to be broadly implemented by traditional terminals.
- Ratty's author frames it as an experiment/fun project rather than a lightweight daily-driver terminal; Bevy/GPU dependencies and runtime power cost are expected concerns.

First-principles take:
- The required Yazelix baseline remains Ghostty/WezTerm + Zellij with character-cell/ANSI output. Ratty must not become a dependency for yzx screen or the default Mandelbrot fix.
- Ratty could still be valuable as an opt-in showcase path: if the terminal replies to the RGP support query, yzx screen could emit Ratty-only graphics while keeping ordinary terminal output as the fallback.
- A true 3D fractal path should be treated separately from the 2D Mandelbrot deep zoom. Options include an extruded Mandelbrot heightfield, a Mandelbulb/mandelbox mesh, or a Bevy-side asset/demo emitted through RGP. The first spike should prove the protocol path and visual value before building a full fractal renderer.
- Zellij is a hard gate: Yazelix needs to verify whether APC/RGP sequences pass through Zellij unchanged. If they do not, Ratty VFX may only work outside Zellij or require Zellij passthrough work, which makes it unsuitable for default Yazelix workspace visuals.
acceptance_criteria:
- Decision states whether Ratty/RGP support belongs in yazelix-screen, a separate experimental crate, or should stay out of scope
- It verifies RGP support detection directly in Ratty and through Ratty + Zellij + Yazelix, including whether APC replies and object placement survive the multiplexer
- It compares three approaches: keep current ANSI renderer only, add optional RGP emission to yzx screen, or create a separate Ratty-only demo/tool
- It evaluates a 3D fractal candidate such as extruded Mandelbrot heightfield or Mandelbulb, but does not block the normal 2D Mandelbrot deep-zoom bead
- It records dependency and runtime cost, especially Bevy/GPU/power impact, before recommending any packaged default
- Any accepted implementation must fail fast or gracefully skip when Ratty/RGP is unavailable, with no noisy escape sequences in Ghostty, WezTerm, Kitty, or non-supporting terminals

title: Document Helix custom theme file location
description: GitHub #599 reports that after the recent config changes users no longer know where to put custom Helix theme files. The old path `~/.config/yazelix/user_conf/helix/themes` is no longer documented or supported, while current docs only explain setting `theme = "..."` in `~/.config/yazelix/helix.toml` and do not clearly explain where custom theme TOML files should live for Yazelix-managed Helix sessions.
design:
Empirical Helix test result, 2026-05-30: hx only accepts -c/--config for the config file. It has no config-dir/languages/themes CLI arguments, and rejects --config-dir. With only -c pointing at Yazelix's generated config, Helix still resolves languages.toml, themes, ignore, and runtime overlays through its XDG-derived config_dir, so a user's ~/.config/helix files can win inside a Yazelix-managed session. With XDG_CONFIG_HOME scoped to ~/.config/yazelix for the hx child process, Helix resolves those surfaces under ~/.config/yazelix/helix instead.

Therefore do not answer GitHub #599 by documenting ~/.config/helix/themes as the Yazelix custom-theme path. The preferred fix is blocked on yazelix-pvkir.1: move the managed Helix source surface to ~/.config/yazelix/helix/config.toml plus ~/.config/yazelix/helix/themes/*.toml and launch managed hx with a scoped XDG environment while still passing -c <generated effective config>.

Native ~/.config/helix remains untouched and independent. Old ~/.config/yazelix/user_conf/helix/themes is unsupported legacy state. Existing ~/.config/yazelix/helix.toml needs an explicit migration/removal decision as part of yazelix-pvkir.1.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Docs answer GitHub #599 directly: supported Yazelix-managed Helix theme files live under ~/.config/yazelix/helix/themes after yazelix-pvkir.1 lands, and the selected theme name is configured in the managed Helix config source.
2. Docs explain that native ~/.config/helix/themes belongs to native Helix, not Yazelix-managed Helix, and should not win inside a managed Yazelix session.
3. Docs state that old ~/.config/yazelix/user_conf/helix/themes is unsupported legacy state.
4. The docs are cross-linked from styling/customization/editor-configuration surfaces.
5. The docs preserve the ownership boundary: Yazelix does not silently move or adopt files from ~/.config/helix/.
6. GitHub #599 is answered and the Beads/GitHub mapping comment exists.

title: Move managed Helix config under ~/.config/yazelix/helix
description:
Yazelix should own a full managed Helix config tree at ~/.config/yazelix/helix so custom themes, languages.toml, ignore, runtime overlays, and future Helix-owned config files resolve consistently for Yazelix-managed hx sessions.

Test result, 2026-05-30:
- hx only accepts -c/--config for a config file; it rejects --config-dir.
- With HOME containing both ~/.config/helix/languages.toml and ~/.config/yazelix/helix/languages.toml, running hx -c ~/.config/yazelix/helix/config.toml still reports Language file: ~/.config/helix/languages.toml when XDG_CONFIG_HOME is not scoped.
- With XDG_CONFIG_HOME=~/.config/yazelix for that hx process, the same command reports Language file: ~/.config/yazelix/helix/languages.toml.
- Theme loading follows the same config_dir boundary. A poisoned ~/.config/helix/themes/yazelix_probe.toml wins without scoped XDG, while a valid ~/.config/yazelix/helix/themes/yazelix_probe.toml wins with scoped XDG.
design:
Use Helix's native config-dir lookup instead of trying to pass every Helix config surface as independent CLI arguments. The destination implementation is the bundled Yazelix Helix Steel fork with a config-dir override, so ~/.config/yazelix/helix becomes the root for config.toml, languages.toml, themes, ignore, runtime overlays, and Steel init files.

Temporary bridge before the fork lands: the managed Yazelix hx wrapper may scope XDG_CONFIG_HOME to ~/.config/yazelix for the hx process only, then continue launching Helix with -c pointing at Yazelix's generated effective config. This bridge is acceptable only because stock Helix lacks --config-dir.

Preferred source/effective layout:
- user source config: ~/.config/yazelix/helix/config.toml
- user Helix language config: ~/.config/yazelix/helix/languages.toml
- user Helix custom themes: ~/.config/yazelix/helix/themes/*.toml
- generated effective config: ~/.local/share/yazelix/configs/helix/config.toml

Do not mutate, move, or import ~/.config/helix/* automatically. Native Helix remains independent and ignored by Yazelix-managed editor sessions.

External Helix escape hatch: users who run custom forks may provide a matched external binary/runtime pair, not a bare binary. That path remains at the user's risk and still launches under the Yazelix Helix config contract.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Yazelix-managed hx sessions do not read ~/.config/helix/languages.toml or ~/.config/helix/themes by default when ~/.config/yazelix/helix equivalents exist.
2. The destination bundled Steel fork supports a config-dir override so the default path no longer needs scoped XDG.
3. Any temporary scoped-XDG bridge scopes XDG_CONFIG_HOME, XDG_CACHE_HOME, and XDG_DATA_HOME only for the child hx process; the surrounding Yazelix process environment is not globally mutated.
4. Yazelix still launches Helix with the generated effective config file so Yazelix-enforced keybindings/settings can be merged without editing user source config.
5. Existing ~/.config/yazelix/helix.toml handling has an explicit migration/removal decision; do not keep legacy surfaces forever by default.
6. Tests or focused probes defend that -c alone is insufficient and that config-dir override or scoped XDG makes languages.toml/theme lookup resolve under ~/.config/yazelix/helix.
7. Helix-spawned child process inheritance is explicitly tested or documented for the temporary scoped-XDG bridge, especially language servers, formatters, and shell commands that may read XDG_CONFIG_HOME.
8. External custom Helix support uses a binary/runtime pair and rejects binary-only or runtime-only configuration.
9. Docs and GitHub #599 clearly describe the supported path and legacy unsupported paths.
notes:
Environment caveat, 2026-05-30: scoped XDG env does not mutate the user's parent shell, Zellij process, or system files, but it can be inherited by child processes launched by Helix, such as language servers, formatters, shell commands, and external tools. Implementation must treat that as part of the contract instead of hand-waving it away. Preserve original XDG values in YAZELIX_ORIGINAL_XDG_* variables for possible child-tool restoration, and decide whether Helix-launched tools should inherit Yazelix's managed config root or be wrapped/restored to the user's original environment. At minimum, docs/tests must state the chosen boundary.

Steel branch verification, 2026-05-30: the relevant Steel PR is helix-editor/helix#8675 from mattwparas/helix:steel-event-system. It does not solve Yazelix's config-dir boundary. In that branch, helix-loader::config_dir() still derives from choose_base_strategy().config_dir() plus "helix" and still has the TODO to allow an env var override; helix-term args still only accept -c/--config for a single config file and reject other double-dash args; languages.toml, themes, ignore, and runtime overlays still route through helix_loader::config_dir(). Steel adds HELIX_STEEL_CONFIG, but only for Steel's helix.scm/init.scm lookup path, not for core Helix config_dir surfaces.
close_reason: Moved managed Helix source config to ~/.config/yazelix/helix/config.toml, made ~/.config/yazelix/helix/ the managed core lookup root via yazelix-helix --config-dir, rejected the old flat helix.toml surface, documented/imported the supported paths, and posted the public GitHub #599 path note.

title: Drive Nushell from 4.1k to the irreducible core
description: xc82 ended at 4,143 LOC across 25 tracked `nushell/scripts/**/*.nu` files. That was a real tranche win, but it is not the final shell-only floor. This follow-up epic drives Yazelix from the current 4.1k Nushell surface down to an explicit irreducible core where only genuine shellhook, TTY, process-launch, interactive `fzf`/`input listen`, and tiny launcher seams are allowed to survive.
design: Stay delete-first. Do not count Rust work that merely adds wrappers above unchanged Nu owners. Deterministic path resolution, helper discovery, JSON envelope parsing, error classification, config/state/report shaping, metadata policy, and command planning should leave Nushell. Prefer POSIX for tiny fixed launch trampolines. Nushell survives only where it is genuinely the narrowest owner of shell env mutation, interactive TTY flows, or direct orchestration of external interactive tools.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Every remaining Nushell family is either deleted/shrunk or explicitly defended as irreducible
2. The repo records one measured post-tranche Nushell allowlist and hard ceiling tied to that allowlist
3. The closed xc82 floor-ratchet history points at this epic as the continuation toward the final shell-only core
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Define the literal irreducible Nushell core and stop conditions
description: Record the literal contract for what may still live in Nushell after this tranche. The goal is to stop arguing from file history or convenience and instead name the exact survivor classes and exact things that still must leave.
design: Allowed survivor classes should be narrow: shellhook env mutation, final `zellij`/terminal exec seams, interactive `fzf` and `input listen` loops, and tiny fixed launch wrappers if POSIX is not cleaner. Disallowed survivor classes should be explicit: path inference, helper discovery, JSON transport decoding, structured error rendering, deterministic config/state/report shaping, metadata policy, and broad maintainer planning logic.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The bead records the exact allowed survivor classes for Nushell
2. The bead records the exact responsibilities that must leave Nushell if still present
3. Later implementation beads can point to this decision instead of re-litigating the boundary
notes: Closed 2026-04-24: the literal irreducible Nu contract now lives in docs/specs/provable_nushell_floor_budget.md and config_metadata/nushell_budget.toml. Measured floor: 1844 LOC across 12 tracked nushell/scripts files. Allowed survivor classes: shellhook/env mutation, final process or TTY handoff, interactive TTY UI, and tiny shell-bound helper seams. Disallowed survivor classes: deterministic planning, helper discovery, JSON/error shaping, fixed popup/menu trampolines, and dead helper files.
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Delete config_sweep_runner.nu and move sweep orchestration off Nushell
description: `nushell/scripts/dev/config_sweep_runner.nu` is still 355 LOC and the budget already targets it at zero. Delete the remaining sweep coordinator from Nushell and move whatever survives into Rust-owned maintainer code plus a minimal shell helper only where terminal or TTY control truly requires it.
design: Delete the runner first instead of preserving its public shape. Rust should own matrix planning, result shaping, and report generation. A shell seam is acceptable only for the final external terminal launch or cleanup behavior that really needs it.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `config_sweep_runner.nu` is deleted or reduced to a trivial transport stub
2. The sweep lanes still run through a supported maintainer entrypoint
3. No new broad Nushell runner is recreated under another filename
notes: Rust dependency gate 2026-04-24: keep this in-house with existing std + toml + serde_json only. No new crates. Production owner should live in repo_test_runner and existing config/runtime library modules; external process control stays via std::process::Command.
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Collapse the runtime helper bridge to a transport-only seam
description: The remaining helper floor still contains broad deterministic ownership in `utils/yzx_core_bridge.nu`, `utils/runtime_paths.nu`, and adjacent helper files. Collapse that layer so Rust or fixed POSIX seams own runtime-root resolution, helper discovery, JSON transport parsing, error classification, and other deterministic bridge behavior.
design: This is not a request to invent a new abstraction layer. Prefer direct Rust binaries or library-owned contracts that Nu can call without re-parsing envelopes or re-deriving paths. Keep only truly shell-specific helpers in Nu, and prefer deletion over moving logic sideways into another helper file.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `yzx_core_bridge.nu` and `runtime_paths.nu` no longer own broad deterministic policy
2. Runtime/helper resolution and structured error/report shaping move below the Nu boundary
3. The surviving helper surface is transport-only and explicitly justified
notes:
Rust dependency gate 2026-04-24: shrink the bridge with existing std + serde_json only. No new crates. Prefer explicit env contracts and direct existing helpers over adding a new bridge framework.
Closed 2026-04-24: runtime path/helper policy shrank to a measured 611 LOC across six Nu helper files. Dead helper modules config_files.nu, logging.nu, and terminal_assets.nu are deleted. runtime_paths.nu and yzx_core_bridge.nu were trimmed so the remaining helper floor is explicit env/path transport consumed by the surviving shell owners.
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Move runtime-root and helper discovery below the Nushell boundary
description: Delete the runtime-root inference and helper-discovery policy that still lives in `utils/runtime_paths.nu` and adjacent helper-resolution code. The Nu surface should stop guessing where the runtime is, which helper binary to use, and whether a source checkout or installed runtime should win.
design: Prefer one Rust-owned runtime contract that returns the resolved paths Nu actually needs, or a fixed executable path surface that avoids re-derivation. Keep only the minimum shell-facing path expansion that truly cannot leave Nu.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `runtime_paths.nu` no longer owns broad runtime/helper resolution policy
2. Source-checkout versus installed-runtime precedence is owned below the Nu boundary
3. The surviving Nu path surface is tiny and directly justified
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Delete JSON envelope and error-surface ownership from yzx_core_bridge.nu
description: Delete the JSON envelope parsing, structured error rendering, and transport policy that still lives in `utils/yzx_core_bridge.nu`. Nu should not keep reshaping Rust-owned envelopes or reclassifying deterministic failures.
design: Prefer direct Rust command surfaces whose stdout/stderr contract is already final for the caller. If a tiny Nu transport helper survives, it should pass argv/env through and little else.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `yzx_core_bridge.nu` loses broad JSON/error/report shaping logic
2. Rust-owned command surfaces present final structured results or final user-facing failures
3. Any remaining Nu bridge code is visibly transport-only
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Collapse maintainer and dev orchestration to thin shell shims
description: The largest remaining Nu block is maintainer and dev orchestration: `maintainer/update_workflow.nu`, `maintainer/plugin_build.nu`, `yzx/dev.nu`, and the sweep helper files. Delete deterministic planning, pin/version shaping, report generation, and command dispatch policy from that surface.
design: Move deterministic maintainer logic into `yzx_repo_maintainer` or another Rust owner. Keep Nushell or POSIX only where it is genuinely acting as the shell boundary for `nix`, `git`, `cargo`, wasm sync, or other external tool execution. Thin wrappers are acceptable; policy-heavy orchestrators are not.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `yzx/dev.nu` becomes a thin maintainer surface or disappears for commands that no longer need Nu
2. `update_workflow.nu` and `plugin_build.nu` lose deterministic policy that can live below the shell boundary
3. The maintainer/dev Nu family drops materially without regressing the supported workflows
notes:
Rust dependency gate 2026-04-24: keep maintainer-owner cuts in-house with existing std + toml + serde_json only. No new crates. Use repo_maintainer plus existing active_config_surface, config_normalize, runtime_materialization, and zellij_materialization modules instead of shell-heavy Nu policy.
Closed 2026-04-24: update_workflow.nu, update_yazi_plugins.nu, plugin_build.nu, config_sweep_runner.nu, and the dev/sweep helpers are deleted. Rust now owns update workflow policy, vendored Yazi refresh, pane-orchestrator build/sync, sweep planning, and nu-lint execution. The surviving maintainer Nu floor is yzx/dev.nu at 425 LOC, retained for public routing plus the startup-profile shell harness.
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Collapse yzx/dev.nu to dispatch-only maintainer shims
description: Shrink `yzx/dev.nu` until it is only a thin command shim or remove subcommands that no longer need Nushell at all. The current file still carries command assembly, profiling harness policy, and maintainer routing that can move below the shell boundary.
design: Keep only a minimal public shell surface for commands that must live in Nu. Move scenario planning, report printing, and argument shaping into Rust-owned maintainer binaries where that deletes real Nu ownership.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `yzx/dev.nu` is materially smaller or thinner
2. Maintainer subcommands still work through the supported public surface
3. No deleted policy is recreated in another broad Nu helper
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Move update_workflow.nu policy below the shell boundary
description: Move deterministic update-workflow planning out of `maintainer/update_workflow.nu`. That file still owns pin extraction, package/version shaping, canary planning, activation selection, and report formatting that do not inherently need Nushell.
design: Keep only the true shell boundary around `nix`, `git`, Home Manager, and filesystem mutation. The logic that decides what to run, how to classify results, and how to shape the final report should move below that boundary.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `update_workflow.nu` loses deterministic planning and report shaping
2. The maintainer update path still supports canaries and activation modes
3. The surviving Nu code reads like shell orchestration, not policy code
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Reduce plugin_build.nu and sweep helpers to pure execution seams
description: Re-audit `maintainer/plugin_build.nu` plus the remaining sweep helper files after the larger maintainer cuts. Keep only the minimal shell ownership required for cargo/wasm builds, syncing, and external process control.
design: Delete helper glue that only exists because earlier Nu owners existed. Prefer fixed POSIX helpers or Rust-owned planning over medium-sized Nu scripts whose main job is command assembly.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `plugin_build.nu` and the sweep helper floor are reduced to genuine execution seams
2. Pane-orchestrator build/sync still works through the supported maintainer flow
3. No stale helper debt survives just because it is already written in Nu
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Reduce startup and bootstrap to the final shell boundary
description: `core/start_yazelix_inner.nu` and `setup/environment.nu` still carry mixed startup policy, duplicate error formatting, and handoff logic. Reduce startup/bootstrap to the final shell boundary so Nu only handles the parts that really require shell environment mutation or the last external exec.
design: Push deterministic startup facts, materialization planning, failure classification, and report formatting below the Nu boundary. Keep only shellhook setup, env export, and the final `zellij` launch/attach seam in Nushell unless a smaller POSIX path is cleaner.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Startup/bootstrap Nu files are reduced to genuine shell-boundary logic
2. Duplicate startup error or materialization policy no longer lives in Nu
3. Yazelix startup behavior remains defended by focused startup checks
notes: Closed 2026-04-24: setup/environment.nu now reads as shellhook/env setup only, the repo-shell path bug around libexec/yzx_control is fixed, and start_yazelix_inner.nu dropped duplicate startup failure formatting. The surviving startup/bootstrap floor is 345 LOC across environment.nu and start_yazelix_inner.nu.
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Delete duplicate startup policy from start_yazelix_inner.nu
description: Delete duplicate startup policy from `core/start_yazelix_inner.nu`, especially the remaining failure classification, startup-facts handling, materialization orchestration, and attach-versus-launch shaping that do not need to live in Nushell.
design: Nu should consume final startup facts and perform the last shell-boundary actions, not re-derive policy. Push deterministic startup decisions down and keep only the narrow handoff to `zellij`.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `start_yazelix_inner.nu` is reduced to the final startup shell boundary
2. Duplicate startup policy and formatting no longer live in Nu
3. Warm, cold, and persistent-session startup behavior remain defended
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Reduce setup/environment.nu to shellhook and env mutation only
description: Reduce `setup/environment.nu` to shellhook and environment mutation only. It still mixes generated extern sync, runtime chmod policy, welcome routing, logging policy, and startup-facts interpretation that should be minimized or pushed below the Nu boundary.
design: Keep only the shellhook work that truly needs Nu env mutation. Everything else should either move into Rust-owned startup/setup helpers or into tiny fixed scripts.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `environment.nu` reads as shellhook/env setup only
2. Startup setup behavior still works for installer, maintainer shell, and normal startup entrypoints
3. The remaining file does not duplicate policy already owned elsewhere
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Audit front-door UI and wrapper survivors for final Nu retention
description: The remaining front-door and wrapper surface is `setup/welcome.nu`, `yzx/menu.nu`, `zellij_wrappers/yzx_popup_program.nu`, `zellij_wrappers/yzx_menu_popup.nu`, and `zellij_wrappers/launch_sidebar_yazi.nu`. Audit that surface one last time and either delete more Nu or record why each survivor is honestly still the best shell owner.
design: Do not preserve a file just because it is small. If a tiny POSIX wrapper or Rust owner is cleaner, take the cut. If the code is truly an interactive TTY/UI loop or a minimal launch trampoline, record it as an explicit survivor instead of pretending it is temporary debt forever.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The front-door and wrapper survivors are either further reduced or explicitly justified
2. Popup, menu, welcome, and sidebar launch behavior keep working after any reduction
3. The final allowlist names these survivors individually instead of as a vague category
notes: Closed 2026-04-24: welcome.nu and menu.nu are the retained front-door TTY/UI survivors. Popup and menu wrappers moved to checked-in POSIX helpers under shells/posix/, and the only surviving Nu wrapper is launch_sidebar_yazi.nu because it still needs the shell-facing Yazi handoff.
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Decide whether menu.nu and welcome.nu are true Nushell survivors
description: Make an explicit decision on whether `yzx/menu.nu` and `setup/welcome.nu` are true irreducible Nu survivors. These files are interactive and shell-heavy, but they should not remain by default if a cleaner owner exists.
design: Judge them against the survivor contract, not aesthetics. If the remaining ownership is mainly `fzf`, `input listen`, ANSI rendering, or direct interactive tool control, record them as survivors. If the files still carry deterministic metadata or report shaping, cut that first.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The bead records whether `menu.nu` and `welcome.nu` stay or shrink further
2. Any retained ownership is tied to the irreducible-core contract, not habit
3. Any further deletions discovered here are spun into implementation work rather than left implicit
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Replace or explicitly keep the popup, menu, and sidebar Nu wrappers
description: Re-audit the tiny popup/menu/sidebar wrapper files and either replace them with cleaner POSIX/Rust owners or explicitly keep them as the final launch trampolines. Small size alone is not enough justification.
design: If a wrapper only sets fixed env and execs another command, POSIX may be cleaner. If Nu remains the best owner because of environment or caller expectations, record that explicitly and stop treating it as vague future debt.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The popup, menu, and sidebar wrappers are either reduced further or explicitly retained
2. Launch and toggle behavior still works after any change
3. The final allowlist names each surviving wrapper individually
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Ratchet the canonical Nushell budget to the irreducible core
description: After the deletion work lands, remeasure `nushell/scripts/**/*.nu`, lower the canonical ceiling again, and record the final irreducible allowlist. This is the closeout bead for the irreducible-core tranche.
design: Treat the measured allowlist as the source of truth. Do not keep a loose ceiling for convenience. If a nonzero survivor floor remains, record the exact files and why they still qualify as irreducible rather than leaving another fuzzy target behind.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The canonical Nushell budget and spec/docs reflect the new measured floor
2. The surviving file allowlist is explicit and defended by the irreducible-core decision
3. The epic closes with one clear stop condition or a lower final ceiling, not another vague follow-up target
notes: Closed 2026-04-24: canonical Nu budget ratcheted to the measured allowlist at 1844 LOC / 12 files. Exact surviving files and family ceilings now live in config_metadata/nushell_budget.toml plus docs/specs/provable_nushell_floor_budget.md, docs/specs/maintainer_and_validator_nushell_budget.md, docs/specs/runtime_shell_floor_budgets.md, and docs/specs/front_door_and_data_nushell_budget.md.
close_reason: Completed at the measured 1844 LOC / 12 file Nushell floor.

title: Split Zellij/workspace command boundary before workspace extraction
description: Split the remaining broad Zellij command owner before any public yazelix_workspace extraction. zellij_commands.rs still mixes pipe diagnostics, workspace retarget, editor open, terminal pane open, sidebar hide, status/cache tests, and command exports; this bead should create a clearer internal boundary without changing runtime behavior.
design: Delete-first and private-boundary only. Keep public yzx_control command names stable. Do not introduce a child repo. Separate workspace/editor/session command flow from status/cache and generic pipe diagnostics where the behavior is already separable. Prefer moving cohesive private modules and deleting duplicate helpers/tests over adding adapters that only rename functions.
acceptance_criteria: zellij_commands.rs has a smaller responsibility surface; workspace/editor/session flow is separated from status/cache and pipe diagnostics; public command dispatch remains stable; no status-bar toggle or resurrection behavior is added; weak tests defending the old broad structure are deleted or replaced with behavior tests; focused workspace/Zellij tests pass; LOC delta and rejected deletion candidates are recorded.
notes: Implementation 2026-05-08: Split zellij_commands.rs into private zellij_commands/pipe.rs for raw pipe/get-workspace-root commands and zellij_commands/workspace.rs for retarget/open-editor/open-editor-cwd/open-terminal/sidebar-hide/editor-pane flow. The public zellij_commands.rs surface now re-exports stable command functions, keeps run_pane_orchestrator_runtime_config_reload for config apply, and still owns the broad status/cache test surface for follow-up c2jz. Deleted parser-only parse_open_editor_accepts_multiple_targets because yzx_control_workspace_surface integration tests already assert multi-file open-editor payloads. Rejected candidates: did not move status/cache tests in this bead because c2jz owns that surface; did not change public command names or add workspace extraction; did not move request/response shaping yet because 0t8a is the next gate. LOC: staged diff 1,118 insertions / 1,120 deletions overall; Rust split itself 1,093 insertions / 1,100 deletions, net -7 raw Rust lines; tokei Rust code 66,626, down 7; file count +2 accepted as private organization debt.
close_reason: Completed: split pipe/get-root and workspace/editor/terminal Zellij commands into private modules, preserved public dispatch, updated workspace extraction contract and Rust budget/inventory, and verified focused Zellij/workspace gates.

title: Investigate yazi addons
close_reason: Added resvg and clipboard tools to runtime. ffmpeg/ImageMagick/nerd-fonts left as user-installed.

title: Assess bd feature parity with br and map schema differences
description: Install bd v1.0.0 and produce a concrete feature parity matrix between br and bd commands, flags, and output schemas. Identify gaps where bd lacks a direct equivalent (bv --robot-triage, --robot-plan, --robot-insights, --robot-alerts, --robot-suggest, --robot-diff, --robot-graph; br sync --flush-only/--import-only/--rebuild/--status; br doctor --repair; br lint; br epic; compaction). Map br issue fields (id, title, description, acceptance_criteria, design, notes, status, priority, issue_type, labels, dependencies, external_ref, close_reason, compaction_level) to bd/Dolt schema columns. Document any bd conventions that differ from br (e.g., status values, priority numbering, dependency types). Output a migration feasibility report.
notes:
## Feature Parity Assessment: br vs bd

### br features with direct bd equivalents
| br command | bd equivalent | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| br create | bd create | bd has --acceptance, --design, --notes, --parent, --deps, --external-ref, --ephemeral, --graph |
| br show | bd show | bd has --json, --long, --short, --as-of (time travel!), --watch |
| br update | bd update | bd has --claim, --append-notes, --defer, --due, --estimate, --metadata |
| br close | bd close | bd has --reason, --claim-next, --suggest-next |
| br reopen | bd reopen | Direct equivalent |
| br list | bd list | bd has --status comma-separated, --json, extended filters |
| br dep add/remove/list/tree/cycles | bd dep add/remove/list/tree/cycles | bd dep also has --type with more types: tracks, discovered-from, until, caused-by, validates, relates-to, supersedes |
| br label | bd tag/label | bd has both `tag` and `label` commands |
| br search | bd search | Direct equivalent |
| br lint | bd lint | Direct equivalent |
| br doctor | bd doctor | bd doctor not available in embedded mode yet |
| br count | bd count | Direct equivalent |
| br q | bd q | Direct equivalent |
| br epic | bd epic | bd epic has close-eligible + status |
| br dep | bd dep | bd dep has relate/unrelate for relates_to links |
| br history | bd history | Direct equivalent |
| br sync --flush-only | bd export/import | **Different architecture**: bd uses Dolt for version control, no flush/import cycle needed |
| br sync --import-only | bd import | bd import loads JSONL into Dolt |
| br sync --status | bd info | bd info shows database state |
| br agents | bd setup | bd has Claude/Copilot integration via `bd setup` and `bd prime` |
| br audit | bd audit | bd audit has label subcommand |

### br features with NO direct bd equivalent
| br command | Gap | Workaround |
|---|---|---|
| bv --robot-triage | No graph-aware triage engine | bd prime + bd ready + bd graph --dot + jq scripting |
| bv --robot-plan | No parallel execution planner | bd graph --dot + custom scripting |
| bv --robot-priority | No priority misalignment detection | Not available; manual priority review |
| bv --robot-insights | No PageRank/betweenness/HITS metrics | Not available |
| bv --robot-alerts | No stale/blocker detection | bd stale + bd blocked cover basic cases |
| bv --robot-suggest | No duplicate/cycle suggestions | bd find-duplicates + bd dep cycles cover parts |
| bv --robot-graph | No graph export built-in | bd graph --dot --json covers dependency graphs |
| bv --robot-diff | No diff view | bd diff covers this |
| bv --format toon | No compact agent output format | bd --json + jq covers this |
| br --no-db | Not needed | Dolt is the authoritative store; no separate DB/JSONL sync problem |
| br config | bd config | bd config has yaml/git/db key management |
| br schema | No equivalent | Less needed since Dolt has stable schema |
| br changelog | No direct equivalent | bd history + bd diff can reconstruct |
| br orphans | No equivalent | Not critical |
| br stale | bd stale | Direct equivalent |
| br ready | bd ready | Direct equivalent, bd ready has --explain |
| br blocked | bd blocked | Direct equivalent |
| br defer/undefer | bd defer/undefer | Direct equivalent |
| br rename-prefix | bd rename | bd rename handles individual IDs |

### Schema Compatibility
bd JSONL export schema is nearly identical to br JSONL:
- Same fields: id, title, description/notes, status, priority, issue_type, owner/created_by, labels, dependencies
- bd adds: owner (email), estimate, due, defer, external_ref, metadata, acceptance, design, comment_count, comment threads
- br fields not in bd: compaction_level, original_size, source_repo
- Dependency types: bd supports more (tracks, discovered-from, until, caused-by, validates, supersedes)
- bd uses `thread_id` in comments, br does not

### Critical Findings
1. **DB corruption solved**: Dolt is a production-grade SQL database with cell-level versioning. 50 sequential writes with no corruption in my test. br's fsqlite has 15+ closed corruption issues.
2. **Dotted IDs work**: bd-test-ws-w4h.1.1 resolves correctly in show/update/dep operations. No false cycle detection.
3. **No --robot-triage/--robot-plan/--robot-insights**: This is the biggest gap. bv provides PageRank, betweenness, HITS, eigenvector, k-core, critical path analysis. bd has none of these.
4. **export/import is a migration path**: bd import can read JSONL, making br→bd migration feasible with a schema transformation script.
5. **Embedded Dolt**: No external server needed. Single-writer file lock. Much simpler than br's JSONL+SQLite split.
6. **--prime replaces bv --robot-triage for basic workflow**: bd prime outputs agent-optimized context, but it's not graph-aware.
close_reason: Completed: bd feature parity assessment finished. Key findings: bd has 525 issues imported correctly, dotted IDs work, dependencies preserved, no DB corruption on writes. bv --robot-* features have no bd equivalent but bd ready/bd prime/bd graph cover basic triage needs.

Do not inline duplicate Nu context builders into startup, doctor, and doctor-report callers. Keep the startup profile boundary comparable as `materialization_orchestrator/materialize_runtime_state`.

title: Repair Yazi/Chafa ghost keypress regression in pinned runtime
description:
## Problem
Yazelix currently resolves `yazi=26.1.22` and `chafa=1.18.1` from the locked nixpkgs input. That exact combination is vulnerable to the upstream Yazi/Chafa preview bug where navigating image-heavy directories causes apparent random typed characters/actions such as find/search/trash prompts.

This is not a real mouse click problem. Chafa v1.18.1 can probe the terminal directly; terminal responses leak back through the TUI input path and Yazi interprets them as user keypresses. Upstream Yazi fixed this by passing `--probe off` when invoking Chafa.

## Evidence
- Local package resolution: `yazi=26.1.22`, `chafa=1.18.1`, `zellij=0.44.1`, `ghostty=1.3.1`.
- Upstream Yazi issue: https://github.com/sxyazi/yazi/issues/3671 (`Chafa v1.18.1 causes random ghost keypresses when previewing images`).
- Upstream fix PR: https://github.com/sxyazi/yazi/pull/3678, merged 2026-02-11.
- Yazi `v26.1.22` Chafa driver passes `--polite on` and `--passthrough none`, but not `--probe off`. The post-fix driver includes `--probe off`.
- Yazi intentionally restricts image adapters under `ZELLIJ_SESSION_NAME` to Sixel first; when Zellij/Ghostty cannot provide a usable image protocol, Yazi may fall back toward Ueberzug++ or Chafa, so this bug becomes visible in Yazelix’s default stack.

## Proposed Fix
Prefer updating the locked package set or overlaying Yazi to a version that includes PR #3678. The minimal behavioral requirement is that the bundled Yazi Chafa driver invokes Chafa with `--probe off`.

If a full nixpkgs update is too broad, add a narrow Yazi overlay/cherry-pick first, then remove it once nixpkgs carries a fixed Yazi. Do not fix this by globally disabling previews, forcing fake terminal identity, or relying on users to remove Chafa; those are workarounds that hide the actual runtime incompatibility.

Add a cheap maintainer/runtime guard that reports the vulnerable package combination: bundled Yazi missing Chafa `--probe off` plus Chafa >= 1.18.1. The guard should explain that this fixes ghost keypresses only; high-quality Ghostty+Zellij image preview still depends on the separate Zellij graphics/passthrough decision.

## Verification
- Confirm the effective runtime Yazi version/source includes the Chafa `--probe off` fix.
- Run `yazi --debug` inside the Yazelix Ghostty+Zellij runtime and record the detected adapter path.
- In Ghostty+Zellij, navigate quickly through a directory with many images/videos and verify no find/search/trash prompts appear without matching keys.
- Confirm the docs/doctor message distinguishes this bug from the separate Zellij Kitty graphics passthrough limitation.

## Non-goals
- Do not solve high-resolution Kitty graphics passthrough here.
- Do not start a Zellij fork here.
- Do not make WezTerm the default solely to avoid this regression.
- Do not delete Chafa unless a separate package-size/product decision says Chafa should leave the runtime.
notes:
Package-level fix landed via yazelix-kw9n: Yazelix now tracks `github:NixOS/nixpkgs/nixpkgs-unstable`, moving bundled Yazi from 26.1.22 to 26.5.6. That Yazi release carries the upstream Chafa `--probe off` fix for the ghost-keypress regression.

Keep this bug open until a fresh Ghostty+Zellij+Yazi manual test navigates an image-heavy directory and confirms no unintended find/search/trash prompts or typed characters occur.
close_reason: Manual Ghostty+Zellij+Yazi verification passed; image-heavy navigation no longer produces unintended find/search/trash prompts or typed characters after the package-level Yazi/Chafa fix.

title: Separate doctor.nu by check family and shared diagnostic rendering
description: `nushell/scripts/utils/doctor.nu` is one of the largest and most frequently touched production files. It currently mixes Helix checks, config diagnostics, runtime/install ownership checks, desktop-entry checks, launch-profile checks, shell-hook checks, and final result rendering in one place. The goal is not cosmetic file splitting; it is to stop one file from owning several unrelated diagnostic domains at once.
design: Delete-first plan: first identify which doctor checks should stay together because they share one real contract, and which ones are merely co-located out of convenience. Prefer deleting duplicated result-shaping or ownership detection before extracting helpers. A good end state is a thinner doctor entrypoint that composes a few clear diagnostic families: config/managed-surface checks, runtime/install ownership checks, launch-profile/runtime state checks, and tool/editor-specific checks.
acceptance_criteria: 1. `doctor.nu` is no longer the sole owner of several unrelated diagnostic families. 2. Shared result rendering stays thin and explicit instead of growing a helper jungle. 3. At least one mixed-responsibility seam is deleted or narrowed, not just moved. 4. Focused doctor regressions still defend the user-facing output contracts.
notes:
2026-04-08 first deletion inside the bead:
- Deleted the standalone `check_shell_integration` function and removed its call from `run_doctor_checks`.
- Verification: there are no remaining references to the removed function or its old messages; the full doctor canonical suite passes; `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` passes.
- This confirms the step-1 conclusion that generic `which yzx` reporting was duplicate convenience scope, not a required diagnostic family.

2026-04-08 refinement after the hotspot scan:
- `doctor.nu` is ~1436 lines and remains one of the highest-value mixed-owner production files.
- Likely first real seams are: Helix-specific diagnostics, install/runtime ownership checks, config/state checks, and shared result rendering.
- Do not start by extracting desktop-entry or shell-hook helpers in isolation; move code only when it follows one of those real owners.

2026-04-08 install/runtime ownership seam landed:
- Moved the install/runtime artifact family out of `doctor.nu` into `utils/doctor_install_artifacts.nu`.
- That new owner now covers desktop-entry freshness, runtime/current link health, installed `yzx` freshness, and shell-hook freshness.
- Deleted duplicated manual-vs-Home-Manager ownership logic by reusing `utils/install_ownership.nu` for the shared artifact model instead of re-owning it inside doctor.
- Verification: focused desktop-entry/Home-Manager/runtime-asset doctor regressions, the full doctor canonical suite, `validate_syntax.nu`, and `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` all pass.

2026-04-08 Helix diagnostic seam landed:
- Moved the Helix-specific doctor family out of `doctor.nu` into `utils/doctor_helix.nu`.
- That new owner now covers runtime conflict detection, runtime health, managed reveal-integration checks, and the Helix conflict fixer.
- Result: `doctor.nu` now composes Helix, install/runtime, config/state, shared runtime preflight, and final rendering instead of owning all of those implementations directly.
- Measured reduction: `doctor.nu` dropped from 1436 lines to 632 lines after the two family moves.
- Verification: `test_yzx_helix_doctor_contracts.nu`, the full doctor canonical suite, `validate_syntax.nu`, and `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` all pass.
close_reason: Doctor now composes separate Helix and install/runtime diagnostic families

title: Popup: restore Yazi git refresh when lazygit popup toggles closed
description: The managed popup wrapper refreshes the active sidebar Yazi state on a normal popup-program exit, but popup toggle flows can still bypass that refresh path. Investigate re-adding the Yazi git refresh when the managed lazygit popup is toggled closed, if that can be done cleanly.
design: Treat this as a narrow lazygit-popup behavior fix, not a blanket popup refresh policy. Prefer a solution that only refreshes the active sidebar Yazi state when the managed popup being closed is attributable to the lazygit popup path. Avoid broad blind refreshes on every transient-pane toggle if they would add noise, latency, or wrong-side effects for unrelated popup programs. If robust attribution is not practical with the current orchestrator and wrapper contract, document that boundary and close the bead with the rationale instead of adding a brittle heuristic.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear decision and implementation path for restoring sidebar Yazi git refresh on managed lazygit popup toggle-close, or a documented reason why that restoration is not robustly supportable with the current popup contract. Any surviving implementation refreshes the active sidebar Yazi state only for the intended managed lazygit path and does not turn unrelated popup toggles into unconditional sidebar refreshes.
notes:
IMPLEMENTATION COMPLETED 2026-04-14

Kimi k2.5 turbo implementation:

Changes made:
- main.rs: Added TransientPaneKind import, transient_pane_kinds HashMap to State
- transient.rs: 
  - Changed methods to &mut self for HashMap mutation
  - Store pane_id -> kind mapping when opening panes
  - Check kind on toggle-close, only Popup refreshes
  - Added should_refresh_before_close() helper
  - Added refresh_sidebar_yazi_for_transient_close() to open refresh pane
- refresh_yazi_sidebar.nu: New wrapper that refreshes sidebar and auto-exits

How it works:
1. When popup opens, orchestrator stores pane_id -> Popup in HashMap
2. When Alt+t toggles close, checks if pane is Popup kind
3. If Popup, opens small (50x20%) floating refresh pane at 25%,25%
4. Refresh pane runs refresh_yazi_sidebar.nu which:
   - Calls refresh_active_sidebar_yazi
   - Auto-exits immediately (pane closes)
5. Original popup pane closes
6. Menu panes skip refresh (clean attribution)

Visual impact: Brief flash of small refresh pane (acceptable trade-off)

Files: 3 changed, 70 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
Commit: e09d2cd
close_reason: Implemented: Added pane metadata tracking and conditional refresh. Popup toggle-close now triggers Yazi sidebar refresh before closing. Menu panes correctly skip refresh. Requires wasm rebuild: yzx dev build_pane_orchestrator --sync

title: Re-evaluate likely Nushell survivors for honest Rust owner cuts
description: After the current bridge collapses and config-owner cuts, the biggest remaining likely Nu survivors are setup or shell generation, front-door UX renderers, session or desktop command bodies, and launcher or runtime helper code. Re-evaluate them from first principles and create implementation beads only where Rust can become the single honest owner.
design: This is plan-space work first. Do not force a Rust rewrite just because a file is large. Each family must end in either a concrete deletion budget and follow-up implementation bead, or an explicit no-go record that keeps the boundary honest.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The remaining likely Nu survivors are reviewed by family rather than by raw LOC. 2. Each family ends in either a concrete Rust-owner follow-up or an explicit no-go record. 3. No broad rewrite is approved without a real deletion budget.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Decide whether setup, initializers, and welcome still have an honest Rust owner cut

acceptance_criteria: 1. The setup family is classified into viable Rust-owner cuts versus honest Nu survivors. 2. Any viable cut names exact targets and retained behavior. 3. Otherwise the bead records a clear no-go boundary.

acceptance_criteria: 1. The front-door UX family is classified into viable Rust cuts versus honest Nu survivors. 2. Any viable cut creates explicit follow-up implementation beads. 3. Otherwise the bead records a clear no-go boundary that prevents churn.

Any viable cut names exact files, retained behavior, verification path, and stop condition. 3. Otherwise the bead records a clear no-go boundary that prevents fake wrapper rewrites.

title: Define the current Yazelix roadmap around the live v15 line, delete-first cleanup, gradual v15.x Rust insertion, and the v16 Rust release
description: Define the current roadmap for Yazelix now that v15 is the only supported line, config-migration support is being removed, and the release framing is clearer: v15.0 is the trimmed rebooted non-Rust release, later v15.x point releases may gain selective Rust slices where they pay for themselves, and v16 is the Rust-forward release. The roadmap should capture the main pillars that matter from here: tighter subsystem boundaries, deleting stale compatibility layers, top-to-bottom profiling, a clearer front door, selective post-v15.0 Rust insertion, and the longer path toward a much more Rust-heavy v16.
design: Treat this as the roadmap for the current live v15 line, not as a placeholder for a second product and not as a disguised all-at-once rewrite plan. Reuse the real existing lanes instead of creating duplicate implementation work: the runtime/helper trim work, the popup/menu and pane-orchestrator cleanup, the migration-layer deletion, the profiling bead, the website issues, and the Rust planning beads that now describe Rust as a post-v15.0 incremental path rather than something required to ship v15.0 itself. The useful output is a coherent framing for what Yazelix is now, what v15.0 must finish without depending on Rust, what v15.x may still improve incrementally, and what kind of architectural shift is intentionally reserved for v16.
acceptance_criteria: There is a durable roadmap skeleton for the live v15 Yazelix line and its path toward v16. It identifies the main pillars for the current product era, names the delete-first cleanup that still matters before and after v15.0, points at the active Rust/architecture/profiling/website lanes that feed the next stages, and keeps the release framing explicit: v15.0 ships as the trimmed non-Rust reboot, selective Rust slices may land later in v15.x, and v16 is the later Rust-forward release.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Evaluate a Nix-only, no-devenv, opinionated flake backend for Yazelix Core
description: Evaluate whether a future Yazelix Core edition could use a Nix-only, no-devenv backend centered on an opinionated flake that ships the default Yazelix stack as one fixed bundle. The question is not whether this should replace full Yazelix. The question is whether keeping Nix while dropping devenv yields a simpler, more honest Core backend by shipping the core default tool/runtime set directly: Zellij, Yazi, Helix, Nushell, Starship, Lazygit, relevant default terminal support, runtime assets, and launch/update entrypoints.
design: Treat this as a post-v14 evaluation bead and as a specific Core-backend candidate, not as a new commitment for main. Reuse the backend contract, runtime dependency contract, command-surface audit, and Core-boundary work first. Then evaluate whether a fixed flake-defined default-stack bundle plus app/shell outputs can support the Core surface cleanly without carrying devenv profile semantics. This is intentionally not a full replacement for the current flexible pack-driven model. The evaluation should explicitly compare this shape against the Pixi experiment lane and explain where a Nix-only Core bundle is simpler, more honest, or more constrained.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation on whether a Nix-only, no-devenv, opinionated flake that ships the default Yazelix stack is a good fit for Yazelix Core. The result identifies which current Yazelix guarantees it could preserve, which ones it would intentionally drop, what install/update/run surface it would expose, how it would treat the current default tool set, and whether it is a better Core candidate than a non-Nix backend experiment. The output is specific enough to guide either a later prototype or an explicit no-go decision.
notes: Completed: the v15 branch already answers this evaluation in code and docs. The candidate is not a separate Core experiment anymore; it is the active trimmed branch direction: fixed packaged runtime bundle, no runtime-local devenv, no packs sidecar, built-in Ghostty, and explicit upstream/Home Manager update ownership.
close_reason: Completed: the current v15 branch itself is the evaluated Nix-only, no-devenv, fixed-runtime direction, with explicit update owners and a narrower packaged workspace contract.

title: Define the v15 Yazelix product contract and trim-first transition
description:
Define the post-v14 product contract for Yazelix as a trim-first transition in the existing repo.

The older framing of "Core as a supported profile" no longer matches the real question. The code now points at a more concrete v15 problem:
- which commands survive
- which backend/runtime responsibilities disappear
- which config axes survive
- which deletions must happen before any Rust rewrite is worth attempting

This bead should keep the strategic decision grounded in the current hot modules and contracts rather than in naming or branding alone.
design:
Reason from first principles and from the real code seams.

The current support cost is not just "too many commands". The main tax comes from mixed ownership across:
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/env.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/refresh.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/packs.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/devenv_backend.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/launch_state.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/terminal_launcher.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/setup/environment.nu`
- `config_metadata/main_config_contract.toml`
- `home_manager/module.nix`

Those seams show the real v15 questions:
1. What is the retained command surface?
   - current leaning: keep `yzx launch`, `yzx env`, `yzx update`, `yzx desktop`
   - likely drop `yzx run` and `yzx refresh`
2. What is the hot-path runtime model?
   - shell entry should activate an already-built runtime, not evaluate/build a Nix environment on every entry
3. What runtime/config responsibilities should disappear with `devenv`?
   - launch-profile reuse
   - explicit refresh semantics
   - dynamic pack-driven package-graph ownership, unless a narrower replacement survives
4. What policy breadth is still justified?
   - runtime-provided shells likely stay
   - `shell.extra_shells` is still open
   - terminal breadth and desktop ownership need a narrower contract
5. What must be trimmed before Rust work begins?
   - do not mix product-boundary deletion with the language rewrite

The output should leave a concrete trim-first queue for v15, not just a philosophy statement. Keep the naming question (`profile` vs `mode` vs no special term at all) explicitly open, because current Yazelix already uses `profile` heavily for startup and `DEVENV_PROFILE` semantics.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete post-v14 product-contract direction for v15 Yazelix that is grounded in the current code seams. The result names the retained command surface, the hot-path runtime model, the main deletions tied to dropping `devenv`, and the trim-first sequencing that should happen before any serious Rust rewrite. The output is specific enough to drive child beads for shell/runtime entry, refresh/launch-profile deletion, packs fate, terminal/desktop narrowing, and the v15 config cutline.
notes: Completed: v15 product contract is now grounded in the README/docs and the active trim queue: narrower yzx surface, no runtime-local devenv, package-runtime ownership, Home Manager/manual owner boundaries, and trim-first before Rust rewrite.
close_reason: Completed: v15 product contract and trim-first transition are defined enough to drive implementation beads.

title: Ship v14 as the capstone release of the current Yazelix architecture and preserve it for the v15 trim-first transition
description: Plan the v14 release as the capstone of the current integrated Yazelix architecture. The goal is to ship the current mammoth honestly as v14, then use that release boundary to justify the v15 architecture reset in the same repo rather than trying to finish the new future architecture first.
design:
Reason from first principles:
- The current repo is already functional enough to earn a real release on its own terms.
- The important release boundary is narrative and architectural: v14 closes the integrated/devenv-heavy era; v15 begins the slimmer future line.
- The preservation mechanism should stay lightweight and explicit: a real v14 tag plus a preservation branch if needed, not a permanent parallel product effort.
- The output should define what still must land before v14, what should explicitly wait for v15, and how the current architecture is preserved for reference once the trim-first v15 work starts in the same tree.
- Be explicit that preservation does not imply indefinite active maintenance after v15 lands.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear plan for shipping v14 as the capstone release of the current Yazelix architecture. The result defines what belongs in v14, what should wait for v15, and how the old architecture is preserved for reference once the same-tree trim-first v15 work begins.
notes: Completed: v14 was shipped/documented as Yazelix Classic, with the v14 tag/line preserved for bug fixes while v15 proceeds as the trim-first branch.
close_reason: Completed: v14 Classic preservation and v15 trim-first transition plan are documented.

title: Define the trim-first v15 architecture reset before later Rust work
description:
Define `yzxcore` as the historical internal codename for the v15 architecture reset in the same repo. The useful current lesson from this bead is the trim-first product reset: keep the repo, name, stars, and history while deleting confused ownership, duplicate runtime layers, and package-manager responsibilities before any later Rust work.

This bead predates the 2026-04-13 release-framing decision. It should no longer be read as saying v15.0 is Rust-first. The current contract is: v15.0 ships as the trimmed non-Rust reboot, selective Rust slices may land later in v15.x when they pay for themselves, and v16 is the Rust-forward release.

Before implementation deletion starts, classify the major old workflows as live v15.0 core, narrower v15.0 core, rehomed under a different owner, historical-only, future separate project material, or explicit non-product behavior.
design:
Reason from first principles and from the code that still defined the mammoth shape when this bead was created.

First gate the feature/workflow contract, then delete implementation ownership. The live candidates for deletion/narrowing were visible in command wrappers, launch-profile state, pack-driven package ownership, shell/terminal breadth, and the oversized config surface.

Protect the real product value while trimming: workspace/session UX, command execution inside the Yazelix environment if it remains a current promise, generated config repair/recovery only where it still belongs, shell choice where retained, reliable desktop launch, and Home Manager declarative ownership.

The point of `yzxcore` as a codename was to make seams easier to reason about, not to create a second permanent product name. The current framing supersedes the old wording: Rust is not the way to discover v15.0, and it is not a prerequisite for v15.0. Rust belongs after the product contract is smaller and clearer, with v16 as the Rust-forward release framing.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete historical plan for the feature-preserving v15 architecture reset in the existing Yazelix repo. The result explains why the same repo should be kept, why trim-first in the same tree was the preferred path, what the v15 product contract is, what command/workflow families survive, what gets rehomed or marked historical-only, what gets intentionally dropped as non-product behavior, and why later Rust work must wait until the surviving surface is smaller. No implementation deletion bead is ready unless it names the preserved capability, replacement owner, migration path, or explicit non-goal.
notes:
2026-04-10 investigation summary:
- v15 delete-first work should target the stronger backend-free/slim line, not merely package-runtime-first. The code still bundles workspace UX with `devenv` materialization, package graph management, shell hooks, terminal wrapper management, and installer/runtime-current assumptions.
- Code hotspots read this pass: `yzx/env.nu`, `yzx/launch.nu`, `yzx/refresh.nu`, `yzx/run.nu`, `yzx/packs.nu`, `yzx/desktop.nu`, `core/yazelix.nu`, `core/start_yazelix.nu`, `core/launch_yazelix.nu`, `utils/devenv_backend.nu`, `utils/launch_state.nu`, `utils/terminal_launcher.nu`, `setup/environment.nu`, config contracts, Home Manager module, `devenv.nix`, and packaging wrappers.
- Delete-first axis: remove package-manager/backend ownership first (`yzx run`, `yzx refresh`, launch-profile state, packs, `devenv` runtime packaging), then narrow shell/terminal/config surfaces, then prune v14-bound tests.
- Keep axis: workspace/session and app UX from the pane-orchestrator era should survive: cwd/reveal/popup/menu/screen/keys/tutor/config/import/edit non-pack surfaces, layout/sidebar behavior, and thin desktop/update integration if they have clear owners.
- Rust should remain behind the trim gate. Rewriting before deleting would preserve the two-products-in-one problem in a different language.
2026-04-13 update: superseded in release framing by yazelix-qgj7.7. v15.0 is now the trimmed non-Rust reboot; selective Rust slices may land later in v15.x, and v16 is the Rust-forward release. Treat this bead as the historical trim-first reset record, not as current evidence that v15.0 should be Rust-first.
close_reason: the trim-first v15 reset is now reflected in the live code: command, state, packs, shell, terminal, config, test, and packaged-runtime ownership are all narrowed and verified.

title: Define the v15 retained command surface and hot-path runtime model
description:
Define the retained v15 command surface and the hot-path runtime model.

Ground this in the current code paths:
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/env.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/launch.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/refresh.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/run.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/desktop.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/launch_state.nu`

The current v15 lean is:
- keep `yzx launch`, `yzx env`, `yzx update`, and `yzx desktop` as thin commands with clear owners
- decide whether `yzx run` and `yzx refresh` are retained, replaced, rehomed to maintainer/dev workflow, or marked v14/Yazelix Classic-only
- make `yzx env` and `yzx launch` enter an already-built runtime instead of paying a dynamic backend/materialization cost on the hot path
- make `yzx update` the cold path that swaps to a newer built runtime
