title: Delete residual transient-pane duplication after menu/popup parity
description: After the helperless shared control model, geometry parity, and menu toggle are in place, do the delete-first cleanup pass. Remove stale wrappers, duplicate pane identity helpers, duplicated tests, and stale docs/spec text that describe menu and popup as separate transient-pane mechanisms.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Remove or rewrite leftover wrappers/scripts/tests/docs that only exist because menu and popup used separate transient-pane paths. 2. Keep product-specific menu and popup behavior where it is real, and document why any remaining separation survives. 3. Validate the final code inventory shows less transient-pane duplication than before this series. 4. Run focused popup/menu tests, generated Zellij config tests, syntax validation, and traceability validation.
notes: Follow-up cleanup remains after the shared transient-pane move: the old popup-runner plugin is still shipped/loaded even though popup/menu now route through the pane orchestrator, and docs/spec text such as docs/specs/floating_tui_panes.md still describe the older popup-runner/helper ownership model.
close_reason: Completed

title: Trim overlapping high-cost runtime behavior tests after the v15 surface cleanup
description: The runtime and maintainer test buckets are still heavy after the command-surface and ownership cleanup. Audit the largest behavior tests delete-first, remove stale or overlapping cases that no longer defend a distinct surviving contract, and keep only the stronger tests that still protect v15 semantics.
acceptance_criteria: 1. At least one stale or overlapping high-cost runtime or maintainer test path is deleted, merged, or narrowed. 2. The surviving test set still defends the explicit v15 contracts that remain after the trim. 3. Test governance and traceability validators still pass for the surviving suite.
close_reason: Deleted the overlapping low-level legacy-root relocation parser tests from test_yzx_generated_configs.nu and kept the stronger entrypoint-preflight migration tests as the surviving contract owners; syntax and test-governance checks still pass after the trim.

title: Delete the config migration and legacy relocation layer from the live v15 line
description: The live v15 line should no longer carry config migration, migration preview, migration transaction, or legacy relocation behavior as part of its product contract. The project has gone through a real rebirth, and preserving old config-shape transitions now adds more confusion and code than value. Delete config_surfaces.nu relocation behavior, config_migrations.nu, config_migration_transactions.nu, config_migration_preview.nu, and any startup or doctor flows that still present migration support as a living feature, while preserving the parsing and validation needed for the current managed config surface.
design: This is no longer an audit-for-retention bead. It is a delete-first removal bead. Fail fast on unsupported old config shapes instead of trying to repair them through migration machinery. Keep only the config parsing, schema, and validation that defend the current v15 surface. Update related docs/specs/tests so they stop describing migration support as part of the live product.
acceptance_criteria: The config migration and legacy relocation layer is removed from the live v15 line; startup and doctor no longer present migration repair as part of the product contract; the surviving config surface fails clearly on unsupported old shapes; and the remaining config code/tests/docs defend only the current managed config contract.
close_reason: Completed: deleted the live v15 config migration/relocation engine, made legacy config surfaces fail fast, and updated diagnostics, upgrade-summary guidance, validators, and tests around the no-migration v15 contract.

title: Simplify the remaining doctor, front-door, and session-helper hotspots
description: The latest inventory still shows a clear hotspot cluster around ascii_art.nu, doctor.nu, zellij_plugin_paths.nu, and integrations/zellij.nu. Those files are not obviously dead, but they are dense enough that another delete-first pass is likely to find ownership overlap, helper duplication, or branches that survived older product shapes. Audit that cluster as one seam and trim it without losing popup/menu/sidebar/workspace behavior or the current doctor guarantees.
design: Start by classifying each large helper path into keep, drop, and unsure. Prefer deleting duplicate ownership or split-brain logic before refactoring surviving code. Treat popup/menu transient behavior, permission repair, workspace retargeting, and doctor output as defended contracts, not casual collateral damage.
acceptance_criteria: The hotspot cluster has an explicit keep/drop/unsure map; any dead or duplicate helper paths in that cluster are removed; surviving responsibilities are smaller or more clearly separated; and focused regressions still defend doctor, transient-pane, sidebar, and workspace behavior.
notes:
Audit map 2026-04-13:
- Keep: doctor.nu current health/fix output, especially config diagnostics, runtime preflight, desktop freshness, Helix runtime checks, and pane-orchestrator permission/sidebar/editor checks.
- Keep: setup/zellij_plugin_paths.nu stable wasm paths, permission seeding, legacy popup-runner cleanup, and generation-state artifact hashing. These defend live popup/menu/sidebar and plugin permission recovery.
- Keep: integrations/zellij.nu pane-orchestrator pipe transport, popup/menu transient pane open, workspace root resolution, retarget_workspace, active sidebar state, and managed editor launch/open helpers.
- Keep: ascii_art.nu implemented welcome/screen styles: static, logo, boids, game_of_life, and random. These are user-visible front-door surfaces in v15.
- Drop candidates: integrations/zellij.nu set_tab_cwd/set_workspace_for_path/update_tab_workspace are no longer imported anywhere; mandelbrot welcome style is only a reserved enum plus a logo fallback, not a real renderer.
- Unsure / leave for separate bead: deeper ASCII renderer split or pane-orchestrator component reorganization. Those are organization changes, not safe delete-first work inside this bead.
Implemented trim:
- Removed unused zellij workspace setter path: update_tab_workspace, set_tab_cwd, set_workspace_for_path. The surviving retarget_workspace path remains the active workspace contract.
- Removed the placeholder mandelbrot welcome style because it had no renderer and only replayed the logo fallback. Updated default config, Home Manager text, and config contract allowed values.
- Reused remove_runtime_plugins_by_prefix for pane-orchestrator stale runtime wasm cleanup instead of keeping a duplicate scanner in sync_pane_orchestrator_runtime_wasm.
Verification: test_yzx_screen_commands.nu, test_zellij_plugin_contracts.nu, test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu, test_yzx_generated_configs.nu, test_yzx_doctor_commands.nu, validate_config_surface_contract.nu, validate_syntax.nu, and git diff --check all passed.
close_reason: Completed hotspot trim: recorded keep/drop/unsure map, removed unused zellij workspace setters, removed placeholder mandelbrot welcome style, and deduplicated pane-orchestrator stale plugin cleanup; focused tests and syntax checks passed.

title: Re-audit remaining high-cost maintainer test overlap after the latest runtime trims
description: The first maintainer-test trim pass removed a lot, but the current inventory still leaves most of the repo's largest files in the maintainer suite. Revisit the biggest surviving runtime-behavior and generated-config test files after the popup/menu unification, runtime ownership cleanup, and bd migration work, and identify where strong contracts are still defended twice or where old scenario splits no longer buy enough signal.
design: Do not reduce contract strength just to make the suite smaller. Start with a replacement matrix for the biggest surviving files, classify assertions into keep, merge, move, or delete, and only cut coverage when another stronger check already defends the same behavior. Prefer fewer stronger tests over broad overlapping scenario copies.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit overlap matrix for the highest-cost surviving maintainer tests; duplicate or stale coverage is either removed or merged into stronger defending checks; the resulting suite keeps the real product contracts defended; and the final lane placement stays honest about what still matters.
notes:
Completed audit and trim:
- Added the qgj7.4.7 overlap matrix to docs/v15_post_trim_test_coverage_matrix.md.
- Consolidated three removed-surface parser tests into test_parse_yazelix_config_rejects_removed_surfaces_without_rewriting while keeping legacy [ascii], removed shell.enable_atuin, and legacy [packs] cases.
- Consolidated two removed enum schema tests into test_config_schema_rejects_removed_enum_values.
- Narrowed the yzx status assertion away from a brittle unwrapped full-path check while preserving the status-summary contract.
- Hardened workspace/Yazi fake zellij fixtures to match pipe command names by argv value instead of brittle $6 position checks.
- Default canonical suite count moved from 92 to 89; the stale count budget was reset to 89 so future growth is guarded again.
Verification: test_yzx_generated_configs.nu, test_yzx_core_commands.nu, test_yzx_commands.nu, validate_default_test_count_budget.nu, validate_default_test_traceability.nu, validate_specs.nu, validate_syntax.nu, and git diff --check passed.
close_reason: Completed test-overlap trim: documented the replacement matrix, consolidated removed-surface and enum rejection coverage, hardened brittle fake Zellij fixtures, restored the default-suite count budget to the post-trim actual count, and verified the default suite and governance validators.

title: Adopt v15 as the sole supported Yazelix line and treat v14 as historical
description: Yazelix v15 is no longer a future branch experiment. The trimmed runtime, explicit update ownership, built-in Ghostty path, and the shared fast transient popup/menu workspace flow are coherent enough that v15 should become the only supported Yazelix line. v14 remains historical release context and migration input, not an actively supported parallel product.
design: This is a product-boundary decision, not just a branch preference. Consequences: stop recommending v14 as the line users should stay on; stop planning for ongoing Classic maintenance as a parallel promise; keep compatibility code only when it directly helps users move onto the current v15 product; and rewrite docs, specs, and roadmap beads that still describe a two-lines strategy. The current v15 identity should center on the trimmed workspace product plus the enhanced, very fast popup/menu-driven workspace UX.
acceptance_criteria: The decision is recorded durably in Beads; the roadmap/docs cleanup beads can now assume v15 is the live line; and stale planning assumptions about preserving or recommending v14 are superseded rather than left ambiguous.
close_reason: Decision recorded: v15 is the sole supported Yazelix line; v14 is historical only.

title: Treat v15 as a config-surface rebirth and delete config migrations
description: Yazelix v15 should no longer carry config-migration machinery or legacy relocation behavior as part of its normal product contract. The project has been through a real product rebirth, and preserving old config-shape transitions now adds more complexity than value. The live line should converge on one current managed config surface, with explicit breakage if someone brings ancient assumptions back in rather than another layer of migration logic.
design: This is a delete-first product decision. Consequences: remove config_migrations, migration preview/transaction machinery, and startup or doctor flows that speak in migration language; stop treating old config shapes as first-class upgrade targets; keep only the config parsing and validation that defends the current surface; and update docs/specs/beads that still assume migration support is part of the living contract.
acceptance_criteria: The decision is recorded durably in Beads; cleanup beads can now assume config-migration support is to be removed rather than audited for retention; and future roadmap work no longer treats migration machinery as part of the stable v15 product surface.
close_reason: Decision recorded: v15 no longer carries config-migration support as part of the live product contract.

title: Use gradual Rust insertion in v15.x and treat v16 as the Rust-forward release
description: Yazelix v15.0 should ship as a trimmed non-Rust release focused on the rebooted product and its UX cleanup. Rust is allowed only as a later v15.x point-release implementation strategy where it clearly pays for itself; it is not part of what defines the initial v15 release. The release-era framing should stay simple: v15.0 is the trimmed reboot, later v15.x may gain selective Rust slices, and v16 is the release where Rust becomes a major visible architectural fact with substantially broader adoption.
design: This is a roadmap and release-framing decision. Consequences: do not present v15.0 as 'the Rust release'; do not make Rust a prerequisite for shipping v15.0; allow incremental Rust slices to land only in later v15.x point releases when they clearly improve the codebase; and shape the Rust planning beads around a gradual insertion path that culminates in a more explicitly Rust-heavy v16. The planning language should separate implementation cadence from release identity.
acceptance_criteria: The decision is recorded durably in Beads; roadmap and Rust beads can now distinguish the initial non-Rust v15.0 release from later v15.x incremental Rust insertion and the later v16 Rust-forward release framing; and future planning no longer conflates 'some Rust lands later' with 'v15 itself is the Rust release.'
close_reason: Decision recorded: v15.x may gain Rust incrementally, while v16 is the Rust-forward release.

title: Document actionable Zed architecture lessons
description: Turn the prior Zed repo investigation into durable Yazelix documentation. Read the relevant Zed settings, action/keybinding, command palette, and UI architecture surfaces, then document what Yazelix should copy conceptually, what it should avoid, and which follow-up beads should inherit those lessons.
acceptance_criteria: A docs or contract note summarizes concrete Zed patterns useful to Yazelix, especially typed settings inventory, settings UI/search, default-vs-user config display, action/keymap registry, keybinding conflict diagnostics, and command palette shape. The note must distinguish inspiration from code reuse, include repo/file references, and link or update follow-up beads for config UI/action keymap work.
notes: Related prior decision: yazelix-7tpo concluded that Zed should be used as architecture inspiration, not direct code reuse. This bead is for durable project documentation and extracting reusable lessons into the Yazelix config/action planning track.
close_reason: Completed: documented actionable Zed architecture lessons in docs/zed_architecture_lessons.md and linked the findings into config UI/action keymap follow-up beads.

title: Add Alt+<number> keybindings to yzx keys documentation
description: The yzx keys command should document the Alt+1 through Alt+9 keybindings that Yazelix provides for direct tab navigation. These keybindings already exist in the Zellij config (yazelix_overrides.kdl) but are not shown in the yzx keys output.
notes: Already satisfied by the current keybinding docs: yzx keys reports Alt+1..9 as direct tab navigation, README.md documents Alt+1..9, and docs/keybindings.md documents Alt+1 through Alt+9. No code change needed for this bead.
close_reason: Already documented in yzx keys, README, and keybindings docs.

title: Fix Ctrl+y helper pane stealing focus context and slowing toggle
description: GitHub issue #553: Ctrl+y currently launches the transient helper pane named toggle_editor_sidebar_focus. Because the pane orchestrator only preserves previous focus context for helper panes whose titles start with yzx_, the helper steals editor/sidebar context before the toggle command is evaluated. That makes Ctrl+y keep targeting the sidebar instead of returning to the editor, and the repeated sidebar path makes the interaction feel much slower than it should. Keep this delete-first: fix the helper identity contract at the generated Zellij config seam before considering bigger changes to the wrapper path.
close_reason: Fixed Ctrl+y by renaming the transient helper pane to the yzx_* namespace so the pane orchestrator preserves previous focus context; added generated-config regression coverage.

title: Rust-own yzx cwd and yzx reveal and delete core/yzx_workspace.nu
description: Delete the surviving workspace command owner by making Rust the single public owner for yzx cwd and yzx reveal, then removing nushell/scripts/core/yzx_workspace.nu. This lane only counts if Rust directly owns the pane-orchestrator and Yazi CLI interactions needed by those commands instead of shelling back into the old Nu workspace module or recreating session-state reconstruction.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Define the deletion budget and dependency gate for the yzx cwd / yzx reveal Rust owner cut
description: Record the exact contract for the workspace command owner cut: Rust must become the single public owner for both commands, preserve the current workspace/sidebar behavior, and delete nushell/scripts/core/yzx_workspace.nu without reviving Nu session-state reconstruction or broad new dependencies.
notes:
Deletion budget and gate on 2026-04-21:
- success means Rust becomes the single public owner for both yzx cwd and yzx reveal
- success also requires deleting nushell/scripts/core/yzx_workspace.nu and removing both commands from INTERNAL_NU_FAMILIES
- do not count a Rust shim that shells back into integrations/zellij.nu, integrations/yazi.nu, or core/yzx_workspace.nu
- preserve current contracts: outside-Zellij failure for yzx cwd, zoxide-or-path target resolution for yzx cwd, managed editor retarget status, plugin-owned sidebar sync truth, sidebar-disabled reveal copy, direct Yazi reveal plus sidebar focus behavior, and public-root routing through yzx_control
- crate-vs-in-house decision: use in-house/std plus existing serde/serde_json only; reuse existing command/bridge error types; reject clap, duct, shell-escape helper crates, and zellij/yazi client crates because this cut only needs a few fixed command invocations and JSON payloads
close_reason: Completed: recorded the delete-first budget, behavior gate, and in-house crate decision for the yzx cwd / yzx reveal Rust owner cut.

title: Implement the Rust-owned yzx cwd owner cut without reviving Nu session-state reconstruction
description: Move the public yzx cwd command onto the Rust-controlled path. Preserve outside-Zellij failure behavior, zoxide-or-path target resolution, managed editor retarget status, and plugin-owned sidebar sync truth while avoiding a Rust shim over integrations/zellij.nu or core/yzx_workspace.nu.
close_reason: Completed: public yzx cwd is now Rust-owned through yzx_control, routes directly through the pane orchestrator, preserves target resolution/editor retarget/sidebar sync behavior, and no longer depends on the deleted workspace Nu owner.

title: Implement the Rust-owned yzx reveal owner cut and delete nushell/scripts/core/yzx_workspace.nu
description: Move the public yzx reveal command onto the Rust-controlled path, preserve sidebar-enabled checks, direct path resolution, Yazi reveal plus sidebar focus behavior, and then delete nushell/scripts/core/yzx_workspace.nu once both workspace commands are Rust-owned.
close_reason: Completed: public yzx reveal is now Rust-owned through yzx_control, uses the pane-orchestrator session snapshot plus direct Yazi reveal/focus actions, and deletes nushell/scripts/core/yzx_workspace.nu once both workspace commands are Rust-owned.

title: Evaluate cross-repo release transaction policy
description: Evaluate how Yazelix should treat changes that span child repos and the main repo as one release transaction. Recent workspace-widget work required child bar, child pane-orchestrator, synced wasm, main flake.lock, Beads state, and CI; the release policy should reduce broken intermediate states without adding heavyweight process for trivial changes.
design:
## Decision

Treat any change that requires both a child repository revision and a main-repo `flake.lock` update as a coupled cross-repo release transaction. Local override validation is useful for development, but it is not release validation because it can pass against unpublished child commits.

## Recommended Policy

1. Classify the change before closing work:
- Trivial child-only change: affects only child docs, tests, CI, or package internals that are not consumed by a new main-repo lock update. It can be built/tested/pushed in the child repo by itself.
- Coupled cross-repo runtime change: main Yazelix will consume a new child artifact, package output, command surface, generated asset, plugin wasm, runtime config, or behavior through `flake.lock`. Treat this as one transaction across repos.

2. Development order for coupled changes:
- Implement and test the child repo first.
- Use local `--override-input` builds only as a smoke test while developing.
- Commit the child repo locally after child tests/package builds pass.
- Do not close the main bead or push a main lock update yet.

3. Publish and lock order:
- Push the child repo commit first, after maintainer approval if the child change is non-trivial.
- Update the main repo `flake.lock` to the pushed GitHub revision, not to a local path.
- Run the main validation without local overrides, especially `nix build .#runtime --no-link` or the exact package/app surface affected.
- Only the no-overrides build proves downstream Home Manager and user flakes can resolve the graph.

4. Manual test and closure timing:
- For non-trivial runtime changes, get maintainer manual test approval before pushing the main repo.
- Keep coupled beads open or in progress until the child commit is pushed, the main lock references that published revision, and no-overrides validation passes.
- Close Beads in the same local completion batch as the main repo commit, after validation and manual approval.
- Run `bd dolt push` with the final main push so planning state does not advertise completion ahead of published code.

5. CI gates:
- CI should validate the main repo from the committed lock file only; it must not rely on adjacent child checkouts or local overrides.
- Child CI/package builds are necessary for child health, but main CI/no-overrides validation is the release gate for integrated Yazelix behavior.
- If CI later gains cross-repo checks, they should fetch the locked GitHub revs from `flake.lock`, not workspace-local paths.

6. Rollback and failure handling:
- If the child push succeeds but the main lock update or no-overrides build fails, do not push the main repo lock. Leave the child commit published but unused, or push a child fix/revert only if the child default branch itself is broken for direct consumers.
- If the main push succeeds and a downstream issue appears, prefer reverting the main lock/update first because that removes user exposure without rewriting child history.
- If both child and main need rollback, revert main first, then revert or fix the child only if the child package is independently harmful.

7. Documentation location if this becomes durable policy:
- Put the concise mandatory rule in `AGENTS.md` near `Rust Plugin Workflow` or as a general `Cross-Repo Release Transactions` subsection before child-specific workflows.
- Put expanded rationale/checklists in maintainer docs only if repetition justifies it, likely `docs/rust_maintainer_tooling_boundary.md` or a small child-repo release note under `docs/`.
- Do not create a product contract unless the behavior affects supported user-facing runtime semantics; this is primarily maintainer workflow policy.

## Rejected Alternatives

- Rely on local `--override-input` validation alone: rejected because it can pass with unpublished child commits and break downstream flakes.
- Commit a local path input in the main lock: rejected because users and Home Manager flakes cannot resolve maintainer-local paths.
- Close Beads after local child/main smoke only: rejected because planning state would claim completed integrated work before the published graph is reproducible.
- Require heavyweight release machinery for every child-only cleanup: rejected because trivial child-only changes do not need a cross-repo transaction.
acceptance_criteria: Produce an evaluation-only policy recommendation covering push order, flake.lock update timing, Beads closure timing, Dolt push, local manual test gates, CI gates, rollback strategy, and what counts as a trivial child-only change versus a coupled cross-repo runtime change; identify where this should live in AGENTS.md or docs if implemented later; do not edit policy files or scripts.
close_reason: Completed: recorded an evaluation-only cross-repo release transaction policy covering child-first push order, GitHub flake.lock timing, no-overrides runtime validation, Beads/Dolt closure timing, manual and CI gates, rollback strategy, trivial child-only classification, coupled runtime classification, and recommended durable documentation locations.

title: Register active Yazi sidebar state during file open
description: Implement the option-2 architecture cleanup from the sidebar-state startup-delay investigation: when yzx_control zellij open-editor is invoked from the Yazi sidebar, use the current YAZI_ID, ZELLIJ_PANE_ID, and cwd to opportunistically register sidebar state with the pane orchestrator before open-editor asks for active layout/sidebar state. This makes the file-open path independent from the delayed sidebar-state startup registration and reduces the chance that early opens need to wait for the async startup hook.
notes: Implemented option 2 locally. yzx_control zellij open-editor/open-editor-cwd now passes the active Yazi sidebar identity through the existing retarget_workspace payload when YAZI_ID and ZELLIJ_PANE_ID are present. The pane orchestrator accepts that inline sidebar_yazi registration only when the reported pane id matches the active tab's managed sidebar pane, then retarget_workspace returns that state for existing sidebar sync. This removes the file-open path's dependency on the delayed sidebar-state startup registration without adding another Zellij pipe. Verification: yzx dev rust fmt --check; yzx dev rust check core; yzx dev rust check pane_orchestrator; yzx dev rust test yzx_control_zellij_open_editor_passes_current_yazi_state_to_retarget; yzx dev rust test pane_orchestrator; yzx dev build_pane_orchestrator --sync; ./rust_core/target/debug/yzx_repo_validator validate-pane-orchestrator-sync.
close_reason: Completed option 2: file-open retarget payload now carries current Yazi sidebar state and pane orchestrator accepts it after pane identity validation.

title: Use typed terminal control APIs across Yazelix repos
description:
Replace direct raw ANSI/CSI escape strings with typed terminal-control APIs such as crossterm wherever the behavior is standard terminal control: colors, background reset, cursor movement, screen clearing, alternate screen, raw mode, and similar. Keep raw protocol strings only where the protocol is not covered by the typed layer, such as Kitty graphics image placement/deletion.

Scope should include Yazelix main and child repos that emit terminal control sequences, especially yazelix-screen.

Acceptance criteria:
- Audit Yazelix main and first-party child repos for raw ANSI/CSI strings.
- Standard terminal controls use typed APIs or small shared helpers instead of ad hoc strings.
- Kitty graphics and other unsupported extension protocols remain clearly isolated and named as protocol-specific code.
- Tests cover any helper that replaces escape-string behavior, without testing trivia beyond maintained terminal behavior.
- Repo guidance or a contract note documents when raw terminal protocol strings are acceptable.
close_reason: Completed: standard terminal controls now route through typed helpers in Rust/Nushell, Kitty graphics remains isolated as protocol-specific raw strings, documented the terminal-control boundary, pushed yazelix-screen 32657f86, and validated with cargo tests/checks plus main runtime Nix build. Local child-flake Nix build was interrupted after idle hang; the main runtime build consumed the published child package successfully.

title: Experiment: evaluate a Pixi-backed Yazelix branch
description: Evaluate whether Yazelix could support a separate Pixi-backed experiment branch without turning main into a multi-backend runtime. Focus on a narrow prototype: Helix, Yazi, Zellij, Nushell, and core CLI flows such as yzx env, yzx run, and launch/bootstrap. The goal is to determine whether Pixi is credible for a lower-support branch, what major gaps remain compared with the current devenv/Nix model, and whether the branch should exist at all.
design: Treat this as an exploration task, not a commitment to multi-backend support in main. Keep the scope narrow: prototype a separate Pixi-backed branch that covers the core Yazelix stack plus the command slice that still matters after the backend-contract and command-surface work lands. The task should identify what maps cleanly from the current runtime model to Pixi, what becomes unsupported or degraded, and whether the resulting branch would be honest enough to maintain as a lower-support option. Avoid solving generic backend abstraction unless the prototype proves the branch is worth pursuing first.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation on whether Yazelix should maintain a Pixi-backed experiment branch. The result includes: a validated package-availability check for the core toolchain; a clear list of current devenv/Nix assumptions that would need replacement; a small prototype or spike proving the core launch/env/run flow is either viable or not; and a documented capability matrix showing which features would work, degrade, or remain unsupported. Leave behind enough notes and logs that the branch decision is reproducible instead of intuition-driven.
close_reason: Not planned: Nix serves Yazelix well and we do not want to spend backlog space on a Pixi-backed experiment branch.

title: Home Manager clean-room refresh should materialize generated Zellij layouts before yzx enter
close_reason: Completed: Home Manager activation now materializes generated Zellij/Yazi runtime configs during switch, refresh rebuilds regenerate them, and no-op yzx refresh now repairs missing generated runtime configs without forcing a rebuild.

title: Retire temporary old-config migration gates after transition windows
description: Eventually delete temporary config migration support introduced by yazelix-07ta and yazelix-xz1z. This covers nested user_configs -> flat sidecar migration and old mutable yazelix.toml / cursors.toml -> settings.jsonc migration. This is not immediate cleanup; it should happen only after enough tagged-release exposure that remaining legacy-path usage is better handled as a clear hard error.
design:
Retirement heuristic:
- Track the first tagged release and date that shipped each migration family: flat sidecars from yazelix-07ta, then settings.jsonc from yazelix-xz1z.
- For each family, wait at least two subsequent tagged releases and at least 90 days from the first release carrying the migration.
- Before deletion, search GitHub issues/discussions and recent maintainer notes for reports mentioning old paths, settings.jsonc migration, stale_old_settings_input, duplicate config files, or reset-config recovery.
- Prefer permanent fail-fast diagnostics over hidden compatibility. After the window, stale legacy input should produce a clear actionable error instead of another automatic migration pass.
- Keep the two migration families separable if their first tagged-release dates differ.
acceptance_criteria:
- First tagged release/date for each temporary migration family is recorded in this bead before removal starts.
- The retirement heuristic is satisfied, or the bead is explicitly deferred with evidence from current support reports.
- Old nested user_configs auto-migration helpers are removed where ready.
- Old TOML -> settings.jsonc migration helpers are removed where ready.
- Tests that only defend temporary migration behavior are removed or rewritten to defend the permanent hard-error contract.
- Documentation mentions old paths only as historical changelog or recovery context.
- Current settings.jsonc bootstrap, doctor, reset, Home Manager, edit, and materialization validation still pass.
notes: 2026-05-07: Policy changed from delayed migration retirement to immediate delete-first cleanup. Removed automatic old TOML to settings.jsonc conversion, embedded cursor settings rewrite, and user_configs sidecar relocation. Surviving behavior is fail-fast diagnostics for old yazelix.toml/cursors.toml/user_configs surfaces plus explicit manual cleanup guidance. Ratcheted Rust budget to 74,409 raw lines after updating tests to use canonical settings.jsonc fixtures.
close_reason: Completed: automatic legacy config rewrites are gone. Old yazelix.toml/cursors.toml/user_configs and embedded cursor settings now fail fast with explicit cleanup guidance; docs/contracts/tests/budget were updated and verified.

title: Split yzx menu from config/edit/open surfaces and thin the command palette
description: The current yzx menu module carries too many unrelated command families. It should be thinned so the command palette UI and popup behavior are separated from config/edit/open/reset/migrate implementations.
design: Keep yzx menu as the palette and popup wrapper only, but stop treating palette eligibility as a tiny hand-filtered subset. Prefer a small explicit command-category model that allows most user-facing yzx commands into the palette by default while excluding commands whose semantics are inherently pane-scoped, tab-scoped, or otherwise unsuitable for palette dispatch. Move config/edit/open/reset/migrate implementations out of menu.nu into dedicated leaf modules. Prefer one declarative metadata source for grouping, labels, and palette eligibility instead of mining help output and then layering ad hoc filters.
acceptance_criteria: 1. yzx menu remains the palette surface, but config/edit/open/reset/migrate implementations no longer live in nushell/scripts/yzx/menu.nu. 2. Palette eligibility is driven by explicit command categories or metadata rather than a fragile manual name filter. 3. Most user-facing yzx commands are eligible by default, while pane-scoped and tab-scoped commands are explicitly excluded or handled outside the palette contract. 4. Popup and normal menu flows keep working without regressing the lightweight dispatcher path. 5. Focused regressions defend the moved command surfaces or palette dispatch seam without weak help-output tests. 6. Net result is a meaningfully smaller and less mixed menu.nu.
close_reason: Split config/edit/open ownership out of menu.nu, added explicit palette categories and eligibility, and kept menu as a thinner palette wrapper.

title: Define yzx command categories and palette eligibility
design: Write a small spec that classifies the public yzx command surface into practical UX categories for the command palette. The goal is not to restate backend-coupling buckets; it is to define which commands are palette-eligible by default, which are explicitly excluded, and how categories drive grouping and labels. Prefer broad inclusion of user-facing commands, with explicit exclusions for pane-level and tab-level commands and any commands whose interaction model is not suitable for palette dispatch.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The spec defines concrete command categories for the public yzx surface with palette-eligibility rules. 2. The spec makes explicit that pane-scoped and tab-scoped commands are not normal palette items. 3. The spec explains how categories differ from backend-coupling buckets and how the two models relate. 4. The spec names the current command families or concrete commands that belong in each category. 5. The spec includes traceability back to yazelix-qrnq and validation through the normal spec validator.
close_reason: Specified yzx command categories and palette eligibility in docs/specs/yzx_command_palette_categories.md and validated specs.

title: Broader Rust/clap public CLI rewrite after deletion gates
description: Track the eventual broader Rust public `yzx` rewrite as an explicit blocked execution bead instead of an implied future idea. This is a v16-or-later path only if Rust becomes the single public command-tree owner for the control-plane families that matter and deletes real Nushell public-owner seams rather than layering clap over the existing public Nushell tree.
design: Follow docs/specs/v16_rust_cli_rewrite_evaluation.md. The rewrite is only worth doing if it deletes the public Nushell command registry, reduces or removes the generated extern bridge as a public discovery seam, and gives Rust real ownership of parsing/help/completions for migrated families. Do not start this bead as a thin argv shim above the current Nushell tree. Keep pane-orchestrator session truth work separate; it is not part of the public CLI rewrite.
acceptance_criteria: The bead stays blocked until the documented go/no-go gates are materially satisfied. When it becomes actionable, the implementation plan must name the exact Nushell public-owner modules that disappear, the parity harness for help/completion/exit behavior, and the command families that become Rust-owned without carrying the old public Nushell tree forward in parallel.
notes:
2026-04-20 public-CLI rewrite reset:
- ulb2.2 is complete, so the original first deletion gate is cleared.
- this bead remains blocked in practice on three public-owner gates: 1) Rust-owned command metadata/help/completion parity, 2) deletion of Nushell extern-scan discovery as a public owner, and 3) deletion of the public export-use registry in core/yazelix.nu.
- qsb5.1, qsb5.2, and qsb5.3 now track those gates explicitly.
- do not start implementation by layering a Rust root above the unchanged Nushell public tree.
close_reason: Completed as the public-CLI gate tracker: qsb5.1, qsb5.2, and qsb5.3 are all complete, the control-family Rust root cut landed, and any later expansion to additional mixed families should be tracked as a new follow-up bead instead of leaving this gate umbrella open.

title: Rust-own yzx command metadata and delete extern-scan discovery as a public owner
description: Before a serious public Rust yzx rewrite can happen, Yazelix needs one static Rust-owned source of truth for command metadata, help/completion structure, and migrated-family ownership. Today nushell/scripts/utils/nushell_externs.nu still scans the Nushell command tree and treats generated extern output as part of public command discovery. That keeps the public CLI split alive even after helper-backed Rust moves. This bead exists to replace that discovery model with Rust-owned command metadata so the extern bridge becomes compatibility glue instead of an owner seam.
design: Delete-first boundary: do not add a Rust metadata file while still treating Nushell scope commands or generated extern scans as authoritative. The end state should let the extern bridge render from Rust-owned metadata or shrink to a small compatibility surface. Keep intentionally Nushell-owned commands possible, but they must stop making the Nushell command tree the source of truth for public discovery. This bead should also define or implement the parity harness for help/completion/exit behavior that qsb5 needs.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Public command discovery/help/completion metadata is no longer authored by scanning the Nushell command tree. 2. nushell_externs.nu stops being the authoritative source of public command discovery and shrinks materially or changes role to compatibility-only. 3. A parity harness exists for help/completion/exit behavior for migrated families. 4. The work names the exact public owner seam deleted, not just a new Rust metadata source added in parallel.
close_reason: Completed by e92c63b: yzx_core now owns public command metadata/help/extern rendering, nushell_externs.nu is compatibility-only startup glue instead of authoritative command discovery, and focused Rust/Nu parity checks cover root help, externs, and menu inventory/dispatch.

title: Delete the public Nushell yzx registry and make Rust the single root/help/completion owner
description: The broader Rust yzx rewrite only becomes real once Rust, not core/yazelix.nu plus export-use scanning, owns the public command root. This bead tracks the actual registry deletion step: remove the public export use ../yzx/*.nu tree as the command registry, stop shelling back into use nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu *; yzx ..., and make the Rust yzx binary the single parser/help/completion owner for the migrated public surface.
design: Delete-first boundary: a Rust root layered above the unchanged Nushell public tree is not acceptable. Rust may dispatch to internal Nushell helpers for commands intentionally kept Nushell-owned, but the public root/help/completion owner must be singular and Rust-owned. This bead should name the exact Nushell modules that stop being public owners and the command families that remain internal Nu helpers. Detached shell execution, renderers, and explicitly Nushell-owned UX commands stay out of scope unless their owner boundary is separately justified.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The public export-use command registry in nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu is deleted or demoted from public-owner status. 2. Rust becomes the single root/help/completion owner for the migrated command surface. 3. The implementation does not shell back into the old public yzx Nu tree for migrated commands. 4. The deleted Nushell public-owner modules and remaining internal Nu helper modules are explicitly named.
notes:
2026-04-20 narrowed starting point from `yazelix-qsb5.3`:
- initial migrated public root surface should be the already Rust-owned control-plane family: `yzx env`, `yzx run`, and `yzx update*`
- success means Rust owns public root parsing/help/completion/dispatch for that family without shelling back into `use nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu *; yzx ...`
- leave `launch` / `enter` / `restart`, `status` / `doctor`, and other Nu-owned families on explicit internal helper paths until they get their own deletion budget
2026-04-20 implementation outcome:
- `shells/posix/yzx_cli.sh` is now bootstrap-only and no longer routes command families itself.
- The public root parser and dispatcher moved to `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/bin/yzx.rs`.
- `yzx env`, `yzx run`, and `yzx update*` stay on the Rust-only public path through `yzx_control`; the root no longer falls back to `use nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu *; yzx ...` for that surface.
- The surviving Nushell-owned families now sit behind one explicit internal entrypoint: `nushell/scripts/core/yzx_internal_dispatch.nu`.
- Remaining internal Nu helper owners after the cut: `nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu` only for `why`, `sponsor`, `cwd`, `reveal`, `status`, `restart`, and `doctor`; plus explicit internal helper modules under `nushell/scripts/yzx/` for launch, enter, desktop, menu, popup, config, edit, keys, tutor, screen, whats_new, home_manager, import, and dev.
- Packaging now ships `libexec/yzx` alongside `yzx_control`, and validators/tests defend that new root seam.
close_reason: Completed: the public yzx root is now Rust-owned via rust_core/yazelix_core/src/bin/yzx.rs, yzx_cli.sh is bootstrap-only, env/run/update stay on the Rust-only public path through yzx_control, and the old generic core/yazelix.nu root fallback was replaced by one explicit internal Nu entrypoint for the remaining Nushell-owned families.

title: Choose and narrow the first mixed public family for the Rust yzx root
description: A serious Rust public yzx rewrite still needs one mixed command family beyond env/run where Rust can own the public contract without inheriting fuzzy shell/process ownership. This bead exists to force that choice explicitly instead of pretending the root rewrite can happen in a vacuum. The likely candidates are launch/enter/restart or status/doctor, but the answer must be driven by real owner deletion and exact boundary narrowing, not by which family sounds more important.
design: Compare the remaining mixed families against docs/specs/v16_rust_cli_rewrite_evaluation.md and docs/specs/launch_bootstrap_rust_migration.md. Pick one family only if the public Rust path can delete real Nushell public owners while leaving shell-bound execution, renderers, and prose in the right layer. Good outcomes are: 1) a concrete family-level execution plan with exact Nu modules that disappear or stop being public owners, or 2) an explicit no-go result for a family that is still too shell-bound. Avoid broad hand-wavy 'migrate launch later' planning without a deletion story.
acceptance_criteria: 1. One mixed family is chosen as the first serious public Rust candidate, or explicitly rejected with reasons. 2. The internal boundary and remaining Nushell helper role are named concretely. 3. The exact public Nushell owner modules that disappear or are demoted are listed. 4. The family-level plan is strong enough to satisfy qsb5's go/no-go gate about mixed command families.
notes:
2026-04-20 candidate refresh: yzx update is now a serious mixed-family candidate alongside launch and status or doctor. The public block in core/yazelix.nu is sizable but bounded, and yzx_cli.sh already has the direct yzx_control dispatch pattern from env and run.
2026-04-20 audit outcome:
- Chosen first mixed public family for the Rust yzx root: the already migrated control-plane surface `yzx env`, `yzx run`, and `yzx update*`.
- Why: `shells/posix/yzx_cli.sh` already dispatches that family directly to `yzx_control`; `nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu` no longer exports `yzx update*`; and Rust command metadata already owns help, externs, and menu inventory for the family.
- Explicit no-go for the first root cut: `launch` / `enter` / `restart` remain shell-orchestration heavy, and `status` / `doctor` still keep substantial Nushell bridge, rendering, and repair ownership.
- Follow-on scope for `yazelix-qsb5.2`: make Rust the single public root/help/completion owner for `env` / `run` / `update*`, delete the public registry role of `core/yazelix.nu` for that surface, and keep remaining Nu-owned families behind explicit internal helper entrypoints until they have their own deletion story.
close_reason: Completed: chose the first mixed public family for the Rust yzx root as the already migrated control-plane surface (yzx env / yzx run / yzx update*), documented the no-go reasons for launch and status/doctor, and narrowed qsb5.2 to the concrete root-deletion cut.

title: Ghostty: restore dead-key IM fallback in the runtime launch path
description: The provided first-party Ghostty path regressed: dead keys no longer work. Yazelix used to ship a Wayland input-method fallback for Ghostty in commit `c82780e` (`fix(ghostty): auto-fallback IM module for dead keys on Wayland`), but that logic disappeared when the old `devenv.nix` terminal wrapper layer was trimmed out. Restore an equivalent fix in the current runtime-owned architecture.
design: Do not revive the old `devenv.nix` wrapper model. Reintroduce the dead-key fix through the current Ghostty ownership seam, most likely the runtime-owned Ghostty launch path in `nushell/scripts/utils/terminal_launcher.nu` or a dedicated runtime wrapper it calls. Preserve the old behavior intent from `c82780e`: when Ghostty is launched on Linux/Wayland with stale or missing IM configuration, fall back to a simple GTK input method so dead keys and compose input work again; when a real `ibus` or `fcitx` daemon is alive, do not blindly override it. Be explicit about host-independence: the old logic used `pgrep`, but the trimmed runtime no longer obviously ships that helper, so the fix may need either a runtime-owned process probe dependency or a different robust detection strategy. Do not perturb macOS or non-Ghostty terminals.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix restores dead-key behavior for the provided Ghostty launch path on the affected Linux/Wayland setups. The fix lives in the current runtime architecture rather than the deleted `devenv.nix` wrapper layer. Valid running IM-daemon setups are not clobbered unnecessarily, and non-Ghostty or macOS launch paths are unchanged. The launch-env decision is defended by focused tests or another explicit verification path tied to the surviving Ghostty launch seam.
close_reason: Completed: restored the Ghostty dead-key IM fallback through a runtime-owned Linux launch wrapper, added focused wrapper tests, and verified the packaged runtime install path.

title: Define the Yazelix Core edition boundary and support story
description: Define whether a no-package-management Yazelix edition should exist, what it would own, and what it would explicitly stop promising. Treat this as a product-boundary bead, not an implementation promise. The likely shape is a Yazelix Core edition that keeps the workspace UX layer, generated config ownership, and selected CLI flows while dropping environment/package provisioning and managed backend guarantees. The output should be explicit enough to say whether Core should be a branch, package, or later mode at all.
design: Reason from the current architecture and command inventory rather than from branding first. Use the backend contract and command-surface audit to determine whether a Core edition is actually coherent. Be explicit about what Core would keep, what it would drop, what failure modes would become user responsibility, and whether it should be a separate package, branch, or not pursued at all. The name can stay provisional until the product boundary is proven; the important part is the support story, not the label.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear recommendation on whether Yazelix Core should exist and, if so, what it would own and explicitly stop promising. The decision is grounded in the backend contract, runtime dependency contract, and command-surface classification rather than generic appetite for a lighter edition. The result is specific enough to guide later implementation or a deliberate no-go decision.
notes:
2026-04-09 completion:
- Added `docs/specs/yazelix_core_boundary.md` with the product-boundary decision.
- Current recommendation: do not ship a separate Yazelix Core edition now; revisit it later only as a narrower same-repo mode after more seam reduction.
close_reason: Completed

title: Structured config UI map editors
description: Replace raw one-line editing for keybinding maps with a structured config UI editor, and audit/fix other config fields that expose complex maps through unreadable raw JSON editing.
close_reason: Completed: keybinding maps now expose per-action rows instead of raw object editing; complex arrays/objects without dedicated editors no longer open one-line JSON editing.

title: Make yzx dev update activation explicit for installer vs Home Manager users
description:
`yzx dev update` currently always finishes by running `nix run .#install` unless `--no-install-runtime` is used. That conflates repo update with installer activation and is the wrong default for Home Manager users, who should reapply their Home Manager config instead of re-running the manual installer. Add an explicit activation mode so the maintainer update flow can target installer activation, Home Manager activation, or no activation at all.

Design:
Keep this delete-first. Do not add another vague boolean like `--hm` on top of the current surface. Replace the ambiguous post-update install step with an explicit activation choice, ideally something like `--activate installer|home_manager|none`. The installer mode should keep the current `nix run .#install` behavior. The Home Manager mode should skip the installer path and guide or perform the local Home Manager reapply path against the maintainer's configured Home Manager flake input. The none mode should make the current `--no-install-runtime` intent first-class. The goal is one update command that does not accidentally mix the manual installer model with the Home Manager ownership model.

Acceptance Criteria:
1. `yzx dev update` no longer assumes installer activation as the only meaningful post-update path.
2. There is an explicit supported activation mode for Home Manager users.
3. The command surface clearly distinguishes installer activation, Home Manager activation, and no activation.
4. Maintainer docs/help text explain which activation mode to use for local Home Manager testing without pushing.
close_reason: Implemented explicit yzx dev update activation modes with required --activate for real updates, Home Manager activation helpers, docs, and maintainer regressions.

title: Fix cursor rename Nix hash and generated README drift
close_reason: Fixed stale Cargo lock output hash key/hash for the renamed yazelix_cursors git dependency and regenerated the README latest tagged release block from docs/upgrade_notes.toml. Verified validate-readme-version, validate-upgrade-contract --ci, validate-flake-interface, cargo fmt --all --check, git diff --check, and nix build .#runtime --no-link.

title: Make Yazelix installs and Home Manager switches fast
description: Reduce the cost and failure surface of installing Yazelix from the flake and switching Home Manager configurations. The user install path should avoid maintainer validation work and should prefer downloaded binaries when available.
design: Treat Home Manager switch and flake install as product consumption paths, not maintainer verification paths. Keep correctness gates in CI, checks, and yzx dev maintainer commands. Start with removing package-time Rust tests from runtime builds, then add a binary cache, then evaluate dependency-layered Rust builds if local Nix rebuilds remain slow.
acceptance_criteria: A fresh flake install or Home Manager switch does not run repo-maintainer Rust tests; supported systems can consume cached Yazelix binaries from CI/cache when configured; local maintainers have a documented fast path and a final-gate path; compile-time changes do not weaken CI coverage.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Do not run Rust tests during user package builds
description: Make the Yazelix runtime/package build used by flake install and Home Manager switch skip Cargo tests by default. User activation should install the product, not validate the repository.
design: Change the runtime/package Rust helper so product package derivations use doCheck = false or an equivalent test-free mode. Move package-time Rust validation into explicit flake checks, CI, and yzx dev maintainer commands. Keep the build restricted to product binaries, not maintainer binaries.
acceptance_criteria: Home Manager activation-package builds no longer execute cargo test for yazelix_core; CI or explicit checks still run the relevant Rust tests; previous package-time host-tool failures cannot block user installs; documentation or maintainer command help makes the final-gate test path clear.
notes: 2026-04-26 implementation start: keep production/development crates unchanged. Packaging change only: user-facing runtime/package derivation should skip cargo tests; existing cargo/yxz maintainer commands and CI remain the verification owners.
close_reason: Completed: runtime Rust helper builds only the product crate and disables package-time Cargo tests; explicit cargo tests still pass outside the package path, and docs now name CI/maintainer commands as Rust verification owners.

title: Publish cached Yazelix flake package builds
description: Add a binary-cache path for release/install users so supported systems download Yazelix package outputs instead of compiling Rust during normal flake installs or Home Manager switches.
design: Use CI to build packages for the supported first-party systems that are practical for the runner set, then push the resulting store paths to a cache such as Cachix or Attic. Keep cache configuration documented but optional. Do not make local development depend on the external cache being writable.
acceptance_criteria: A documented cache can serve packages.${system}.yazelix and runtime outputs for at least x86_64-linux; CI publishes cache artifacts for configured systems; users can opt into the cache from Nix/Home Manager; a cache miss still builds correctly from source.
close_reason: Completed: x86_64-linux user-facing package outputs are published to the yazelix Cachix cache by .github/workflows/publish_nix_cache.yml; workflow run 25268152912 succeeded and pushed Yazelix runtime/package paths; README, docs/installation.md, and home_manager/README.md document opt-in substituter/key configuration and Home Manager usage.

title: Evaluate dependency-layered Rust Nix builds
description: Measure and, if justified, move the Rust package build to a dependency-layered Nix builder so local source edits do not rebuild third-party crates during Nix/package iteration.
design: Compare the current buildRustPackage path with a crane-style or equivalent cargoArtifacts split. Adopt it only if it materially improves repeated local Nix builds without making packaging harder to understand. Keep the product package test-free by default and keep full tests in checks/CI.
acceptance_criteria: A before/after timing note exists for clean and incremental package builds; if adopted, dependency artifacts are cached separately from Yazelix source; package output remains identical from the user perspective; if rejected, the bead records the measured reason.
notes: Evaluation recorded in docs/specs/rust_nix_dependency_layering_evaluation.md. Current buildRustPackage path already builds only yazelix_core with package tests disabled. Measured current invalid-output package build after source changes at 0:20.88 and warm no-op build at 0:00.39. Rejected crane/dependency-layered migration for now; revisit only if repeated Home Manager/package timings show source-only Rust rebuilds above 60s or cargo-vendor-dir is a material wall-time share.
close_reason: Completed: measured current Nix package path, recorded rejection of a crane-style migration in docs/specs/rust_nix_dependency_layering_evaluation.md, and set a reopen threshold.

notes:
Completed in the current worktree:

title: yzx reveal latency is too high
description: Track `yzx reveal` taking around three seconds to reveal the current file in the managed Yazi sidebar. This is a reveal-path performance bug, not a popup bug and not a correctness regression. The work should identify where the cost actually lives: target-path resolution, sidebar state discovery, Yazi `ya emit-to reveal`, pane focus/orchestrator work, or related coordination on the reveal path.
design: Treat this as a measurement-first bug. Reproduce the latency with a concrete reveal action and instrument the reveal path from `yzx reveal` through target resolution, sidebar lookup, Yazi emit, and focus/orchestrator work. Keep the scope on reveal latency only. Prefer deleting unnecessary work or reusing state over adding more coordination layers.
acceptance_criteria: 1. There is a reproducible measurement of reveal latency before and after the fix. 2. The main source of reveal-path cost is identified and documented. 3. `yzx reveal` becomes substantially faster in normal use without regressing correctness or focus behavior. 4. Add one focused regression or benchmark guard at the seam that actually caused the latency.
close_reason: Completed

title: Home Manager clean-room refresh should materialize generated Zellij layouts before yzx enter
description: On a clean-room Home Manager install, the ownership surfaces validate and `yzx refresh` successfully builds a fresh profile, but it still leaves ~/.local/share/yazelix/configs/zellij/layouts/yzx_side.kdl missing. As a result, `yzx enter --skip-refresh` fails immediately with `Missing Yazelix generated Zellij layout` even after a successful refresh. This leaves the first Home Manager startup path under-materialized and makes `yzx doctor` report a real startup blocker instead of a finished first-run state.
close_reason: Completed

title: Define child-repo Beads ownership policy
description: Decide when work should be tracked in the main Yazelix Beads database, in child-repo Beads databases, in both, or through a more explicit cross-repo protocol. The policy should cover public GitHub issues, child-repo implementation slices, release transactions, cross-repo blockers, and how agents discover canonical planning state before coding.
design: Start from the current rule: main Yazelix owns product planning and release transactions, while child repos own their source and package behavior. Decide whether child repos should gain their own Beads for implementation details, whether main beads should remain parent epics, and how cross-repo issue links should be represented without creating stale duplicate state.
acceptance_criteria: 1. A durable policy or contract explains which repo owns planning for cross-repo work. 2. The policy covers child-only implementation, main-repo integration, coupled release transactions, GitHub issue mirroring, and local agent workflow. 3. The policy avoids duplicate unclear issue state while still making child-repo work discoverable from the relevant repo. 4. Existing AGENTS.md cross-repo release rules are either confirmed or updated to point at the new policy.

title: Separate stable pane-orchestrator client API from dead zellij debug exports
description: `nushell/scripts/integrations/zellij.nu` mixes the stable workspace/pane API with several debug-only exports. Repo search shows `debug_write_literal`, `debug_send_escape`, and `debug_open_file_via_plugin` are currently only referenced at their own definitions. Delete-first goal: remove or isolate dead debug probes before doing broader API splitting so the surviving module reflects the real pane-orchestrator client contract.
notes:
2026-04-08 implementation:
- Deleted the dead `debug_write_literal`, `debug_send_escape`, and `debug_open_file_via_plugin` exports from `nushell/scripts/integrations/zellij.nu`.
- Verification: repo-wide search now finds no remaining references to those names; importing the surviving pane-orchestrator client functions still succeeds; `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` passes.
- This confirms the step-1 requirement cut: those debug probes were not part of the supported zellij-integration contract.
close_reason: Completed: deleted the dead zellij debug exports and kept the supported pane-orchestrator client API.

title: Evaluate standalone yazelix_bar Zellij plugin path
description: Evaluate whether yazelix_bar should become a user-facing standalone Zellij bar offering, and choose the first shippable shape. The current in-repo yazelix_bar crate renders zjstatus segments; it is not a Zellij WASM plugin. The current top bar is generated KDL plus vendored zjstatus.wasm plus Yazelix status-cache/status-bus helpers.
design:
Decision, 2026-05-05:

Recommendation: ship a standalone `yazelix_bar` zjstatus preset/bundle first. Do not fork zjstatus, and do not build a real `yazelix_bar.wasm` plugin until the preset hits a hard runtime limitation.

Why:
- Current Yazelix bar behavior is mostly zjstatus configuration: mode, tabs, session, right-side command widgets, and formatting. The in-repo `yazelix_bar` crate is a pure renderer for zjstatus strings, not a plugin runtime.
- Upstream zjstatus is active enough to avoid fork ownership for now; `gh repo view dj95/zjstatus` reported latest release v0.23.0 published 2026-04-18, MIT licensed, and described as a configurable Zellij statusbar plugin.
- Public value for non-Yazelix users is the tastefully styled bar, compact/full tab labels, sane overflow choices, and a small preset they can install without adopting Yazelix.
- A fork would create recurring vendoring/security/update work while solving no current hard limitation.
- A real WASM plugin would be cleaner long term only if we need native provider scheduling, richer adaptive layout, dark/light theme switching beyond zjstatus config, or fewer command-widget footguns.

First shippable shape:
- Package or publish a `yazelix_bar` preset containing pinned/vendored `zjstatus.wasm` plus a generic KDL snippet generated from the `yazelix_bar` crate.
- Generic default must not require `yzx_control`, `IN_YAZELIX_SHELL`, pane-orchestrator, Yazelix status cache, Nushell, tokenusage, or Yazelix runtime paths.
- Minimum generic widgets: mode, tabs, session or custom static text, datetime, optional static editor/shell/terminal labels, optional user-supplied command widgets.
- Optional Yazelix provider boundary: when running inside Yazelix, users can enable workspace, cursor, Claude/Codex/OpenCode usage, CPU, and RAM widgets through existing `yzx_control zellij status-cache-widget ...` and runtime helper commands. These are an integration preset layer, not the generic default.

Implementation size estimates:
- Standalone preset/bundle: roughly +120 to +300 net LOC for package output, docs, generated KDL fixture, and validator/tests.
- Generator/CLI polish on top of the preset: roughly +250 to +600 net LOC if we add a small `yazelix_bar` command that renders preset variants.
- Real `yazelix_bar.wasm` plugin: roughly +900 to +1,800 net LOC for Zellij plugin SDK wiring, application-state reads, config parsing, provider scheduling, tests, packaging, and migration docs.
- zjstatus fork: unknown upstream churn cost plus patch carry; only justify if upstream rejects a required capability and the preset cannot work around it.

Migration path from current Yazelix internals:
1. Keep Yazelix's managed bar on zjstatus for now.
2. Continue moving pure bar formatting into the `yazelix_bar` crate, as with widget tray and tab label formats.
3. Add the standalone preset package in `yazelix-r9n1.1`.
4. Use the same renderer for Yazelix-managed KDL and standalone KDL so behavior stays aligned.
5. Evaluate a real plugin only after the preset proves real external demand or exposes a hard zjstatus limitation.

Rejected alternatives:
- Fork zjstatus now: too much maintenance for mostly configuration-level product changes.
- Real WASM plugin now: higher LOC and test burden before we have evidence that standalone users need more than a preset.
- Publish the current Yazelix-managed bar as-is: not acceptable because it requires Yazelix runtime helpers by default and would fail for plain Zellij users.
acceptance_criteria: Capture a recommendation for preset/bundle vs real plugin vs fork; define the generic standalone user value; list minimum generic widgets and optional Yazelix provider boundary; estimate implementation size and migration path from current zjstatus layout generation.
notes: Supersedes the stale imported bead yazelix-jy4x for the current product/architecture evaluation. Initial take: do not fork zjstatus; prototype as a zjstatus preset if we want fast public value, or as an internal yazelix_bar.wasm POC if we want a true replacement.
close_reason: Decision completed: ship a standalone zjstatus preset/bundle first, keep zjstatus upstream, and defer a real yazelix_bar.wasm until a hard limitation appears.

title: Extract yazelix_bar as a standalone Zellij bar preset
description: Package yazelix_bar as a standalone Yazelix-branded Zellij/zjstatus bar offering for users who want the bar experience without adopting the full Yazelix workspace. Selected name: yazelix_bar. Alternatives: yazelix_zjstatus_bar, yazelix_status_bar.
design: Target users: Zellij users who like Yazelix's status bar; AI-heavy terminal users who want Codex/Claude/opencode usage, reset, budget, and activity widgets; Yazelix users who want the bar decoupled from the main runtime. Included scope: branded theme/preset generation, mode/tabs/session/time, CPU/RAM, AI usage widgets as first-class high-value widgets, command widgets, and optional Yazelix-aware workspace widgets. Excluded scope: requiring full Yazelix install, requiring the pane orchestrator by default, and hiding Yazelix-specific widgets inside the generic path. Split the package into generic widgets, AI widgets, and Yazelix-only widgets with clear feature flags or config switches.
acceptance_criteria: Decision/implementation defines selected name plus alternatives, standalone install shape, minimal Zellij config snippet, AI widget data-source contract, generic-vs-Yazelix-specific widget boundary, packaging/release process, and how the main Yazelix repo consumes the extracted output without copying generated artifacts manually.
close_reason: Completed: added .#yazelix_bar package with bundled zjstatus.wasm, standalone preset/template, docs/yazelix_bar.md, standalone distribution contract, and flake overlay/package output. Verified nix build .#yazelix_bar, cargo test -p yazelix_bar, and validate-contracts.

title: Canonical contract-driven development protocol for Yazelix
description: Define and adopt a stricter, public-facing contract-driven development protocol so Yazelix converges toward canonical form: one owner per behavior, no missing layer, no extra layer, no duplicate owner, no weak tests, and no surviving Nushell without an explicit owner reason. This is a planning-first lane: audit code/specs/tests before implementation, question each requirement, create deletion beads from findings, and avoid changing product code during the audit pass.
design: Use indexed subsystem contract items rather than indexing every prose line. Each live normative item should have a stable ID, type, owner, verification method, and status. Tests should trace to live contract items, concrete regressions, or maintained invariants; orphan or weak tests should be deleted, demoted, or quarantined with an explicit bead. Public docs must explain the protocol and cite external references.
acceptance_criteria: The protocol is ready only when the schema decision is closed, public protocol docs exist with references, validators enforce the agreed contract/test rules in migration-safe ratchet mode, the governed test suite has been inventoried and weak/orphan tests have explicit delete/demote/quarantine outcomes, subsystem audits have produced feature-preserving owner maps and deletion beads, and a ranked Nu deletion budget exists. No child may recommend deleting code, tests, aliases, or docs without naming the retained behavior, canonical owner, and verification path or explicit no-go reason.
notes:
Reference sources to carry into public docs: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook appendix on requirements traceability and verification matrices: https://www.nasa.gov/reference/system-engineering-handbook-appendix/ ; Google Testing Blog, Test Sizes: https://testing.googleblog.com/2010/12/test-sizes.html ; Google Testing Blog, Just Say No to More End-to-End Tests: https://testing.googleblog.com/2015/04/just-say-no-to-more-end-to-end-tests.html ; Rust Book test organization: https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch11-03-test-organization.html ; Cargo test docs: https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/commands/cargo-test.html ; cargo-nextest docs: https://nexte.st/ ; cargo-mutants docs: https://docs.rs/crate/cargo-mutants/latest ; cargo-mutants limitations: https://mutants.rs/limitations.html ; Rust Fuzz Book: https://rust-fuzz.github.io/book/
Planning review 2026-04-21: parent acceptance was tightened because a mere child work plan would close the epic too early. The epic now closes only after protocol docs, enforcement, audits, test outcomes, and the ranked deletion budget exist.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Define the canonical contract item schema and ID policy
description: Decide the strict contract unit Yazelix will use before broad audit or validator work. The schema should distinguish normative contract items from rationale, examples, non-goals, historical notes, and implementation notes so the project does not turn every prose line into bureaucracy.
design: Candidate fields: stable ID, subsystem, type, normative text, owner, status, verification method, defended-by tests or validators, source/rationale, and deletion/bridge implications. Candidate statuses: live, planning, historical, deprecated, quarantine. Candidate types: behavior, invariant, boundary, ownership, failure mode, non-goal, manual verification.
acceptance_criteria: The decision records the final schema, ID naming rules, allowed statuses, allowed verification methods, migration rules for existing specs, and the rule for when a test may reference a contract item versus a concrete regression. It explicitly rejects indexing every prose line, requires that normative live contract items distinguish automated tests, validators, manual checks, and intentionally-unverified items with reasons, and includes feature-preservation rules for delete-first work. It cites the NASA requirements/verification matrix reference and explains why Yazelix uses lightweight Markdown rather than a heavyweight requirements tool.
notes:
Reference: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook appendix on requirements traceability and verification matrices: https://www.nasa.gov/reference/system-engineering-handbook-appendix/
Planning review 2026-04-21: schema must preserve functionality as a first-class constraint. The unit should be a normative contract item, not every sentence in a spec. Non-goals, rationale, examples, and historical notes should be addressable but should not require one-to-one tests.
Completed 2026-04-21: added docs/specs/canonical_contract_item_schema.md and indexed it in docs/specs/spec_inventory.md. The decision now defines the contract-item unit, ID format, statuses, types, verification modes, test reference rules, migration rules, and delete-first feature-preservation constraints.
close_reason: Completed: canonical contract item schema now lives in docs/specs/canonical_contract_item_schema.md and is indexed in docs/specs/spec_inventory.md

title: Publish the public Yazelix contract-driven development protocol
description: Create or update public-facing docs that explain Yazelix's stricter contract-driven development protocol: how specs, Beads, tests, validators, and delete-first subsystem audits fit together, and how external references inform the protocol.
design: The docs should be practical and non-bureaucratic: define the canonical contract item, explain when specs are required, explain test traceability, explain quarantine/delete/demote paths for weak tests, and include a references section with the source list from the parent bead.
acceptance_criteria: Public docs exist in an appropriate docs/specs or docs architecture location, reference the canonical schema decision and schema pilot, list the source references with links, and explain how contributors should add or change a spec, contract item, or test without creating weak/spec-less coverage. The docs must distinguish normative contract items from rationale/history, describe quarantine/delete/demote paths, preserve AGENTS.md as the short durable workflow entrypoint, and follow README prose style if README is touched.
notes:
References to include: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook appendix: https://www.nasa.gov/reference/system-engineering-handbook-appendix/ ; Google Test Sizes: https://testing.googleblog.com/2010/12/test-sizes.html ; Google E2E guidance: https://testing.googleblog.com/2015/04/just-say-no-to-more-end-to-end-tests.html ; Rust Book test organization: https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch11-03-test-organization.html ; Cargo test docs: https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/commands/cargo-test.html ; cargo-nextest: https://nexte.st/ ; cargo-mutants: https://docs.rs/crate/cargo-mutants/latest ; Rust Fuzz Book: https://rust-fuzz.github.io/book/
