close_reason: Completed: public contract-driven development docs landed and now point contributors to the schema, pilot, and traceability protocol

title: Enforce contract IDs across specs and governed tests
description: Add mechanical validation so contract-driven development is enforced instead of aspirational. Specs should expose indexed live contract items. Governed tests should reference live contract IDs, concrete regressions, or approved invariants. Broad file-level references like only defending test_suite_governance.md should not be sufficient for default-lane tests.
design: Extend or add validators after the schema decision lands. The validator should parse Markdown contract item IDs and Nu/Rust test markers, detect orphan tests, detect missing verification for live normative items, and produce actionable errors. Keep false positives low and allow explicit quarantine during migration.
acceptance_criteria: Validation fails for new default-lane governed tests that lack a live contract/regression/invariant mapping. Validation reports live contract items with no verification or explicit manual/unverified reason. The first implementation uses a migration-safe ratchet: existing unmapped tests are reported and can be quarantined behind bead IDs, while new or touched tests must satisfy the stricter rule. Broad policy-only defenses such as only referencing test_suite_governance.md are not sufficient for new/touched default-lane tests. The validator is documented in the public protocol docs.
notes:
References: NASA verification matrix guidance supports unique IDs and verification mapping. Google Test Sizes supports enforceable lane boundaries. Cargo test docs define the base Rust test runner behavior. cargo-nextest is a candidate runner for faster isolated Rust test execution, not a required dependency until evaluated.
Planning review 2026-04-21: enforcement should be strict for new/touched work but migration-safe for the existing suite, otherwise the protocol turns into a big-bang cleanup that risks losing regression memory.
Completed 2026-04-21: extended validate_specs.nu to parse indexed contract items and verify required fields/statuses/verification, added contract_traceability_helpers.nu plus docs/contract_traceability_quarantine.toml for a migration-safe file-level ratchet, taught the default and Rust test traceability validators to validate nearby Contract markers and reject policy-only nearby traceability, added pilot Contract markers to the config/runtime tests, updated docs/contract_driven_development.md with the mechanical validator surfaces, and fixed pre-existing default-runner and Rust-test metadata debt exposed by the stricter validators.
close_reason: Completed: contract-item and test traceability ratchet is now mechanically enforced in migration-safe mode

title: Canonicalize the governed test suite from contract traceability
description: Audit the current Nu and Rust tests from a delete-first point of view. The target end state is zero surviving governed Nushell tests. Port only strong contract, regression, and invariant coverage to Rust; delete weak or orphan coverage; and record blocked surfaces as future owner-cut or harness work instead of accepting them as long-term Nu survivors. First-party Rust tests should run under nextest by default, with cargo test retained only for doctests and any explicit unsupported exception buckets.
design: Use the existing strength rubric as an input, but make contract traceability and strength the hard gates. Rust becomes the canonical home for surviving governed tests, and nextest becomes the default execution surface for first-party Rust test buckets. Nushell tests are transitional debt only; if a behavior cannot be defended cleanly in Rust yet, record the blocking owner cut or harness gap instead of preserving a Nu test by habit.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The governed suite has an inventory of strong Rust-port, weak-delete, and blocked candidates. 2. Surviving default-lane tests are 7+/10 and mapped to live contracts, regressions, or invariants. 3. No open migration bead treats a governed Nu test as a permanent survivor; blocked cases point at explicit owner-cut or harness work instead. 4. First-party Rust test lanes are nextest-first by default, with cargo test reserved only for doctests and explicit exceptions. 5. No valuable regression memory is lost without a strong Rust replacement or explicit contract retirement.
notes:
References: Google E2E guidance favors fast, reliable, isolated feedback loops and a small number of E2E tests; cargo-mutants can probe whether Rust tests actually check behavior; Rust Fuzz Book applies to parser/serializer/merge style logic.
Planning review 2026-04-21: delete-first for tests means deleting noise, not deleting regression memory. Feature preservation and contract retirement must be explicit.
Planning review 2026-04-22: no governed Nushell test is an accepted steady-state owner. Strong tests port to Rust; weak tests delete; blocked cases need explicit owner-cut or harness beads.
close_reason: Completed: all governed test canonicalization children are closed; zero governed Nu tests remain and Rust/nextest is the canonical first-party test lane.

title: Inventory governed tests and map them to live contract items
description: Read every governed Nu and Rust test file and produce a contract traceability inventory. Classify each test as mapped, regression-only, invariant-only, duplicate, implementation-trivia, weak, dead, or quarantine.
acceptance_criteria: The inventory identifies tests currently defended only by broad policy docs, tests without precise live contract mapping, redundant tests, likely 9/10 keepers, and tests that are the only known defense for live behavior. It records the minimum spec/contract IDs needed before deletion or demotion work starts, and separates Rust-owned deterministic logic tests from Nu-owned shell/process/integration tests.
notes:
Current rough counts from 2026-04-21 planning pass: 121 Nu files, 42 Rust files under yazelix_core, about 195 Nu tests and 119 Rust tests, with 12 Nu test files top-level defending test_suite_governance.md. Re-verify counts during implementation.
Planning review 2026-04-21: inventory must identify 'only known executable defense' tests so later deletion work does not accidentally remove the last guard for real behavior.
Completed inventory in docs/specs/governed_test_traceability_inventory.md. Counts: 18 governed Nu test files, 198 Nu def test_* functions, 38 first-party Rust files with tests, 146 Rust #[test] functions. Validator status: default Nu traceability passes with five quarantined default-component file-level debts; Rust traceability passes cleanly. Outcome: no immediate safe mass deletion; yazelix-rdn7.4.2 should use the inventory to delete/demote only after contract IDs or explicit quarantine cover the retained behavior.
close_reason: Completed governed test traceability inventory and validated Nu/Rust traceability ratchets.

title: Delete, demote, or quarantine weak and orphan governed tests
description: After the test inventory exists, remove tests that do not defend a live contract, concrete regression, integration boundary, or maintained invariant. Demote tests to sweep/manual lanes when they are valuable but too heavy or environment-sensitive for default. Quarantine only when deletion would risk losing unmodeled regression memory.
acceptance_criteria: Every deleted test has an explicit reason or a replacement contract/test mapping. Default-lane orphan tests are gone or quarantined with bead IDs and exit criteria. Tests that are the only known executable defense of live behavior are kept until replacement verification exists or the behavior is explicitly retired. Demotions preserve the verification path in the appropriate lane. No new generic overflow test files are created.
notes:
Reference: Google E2E guidance says tests should create a fast, reliable, failure-isolating feedback loop; weak tests that mostly assert existence/help/implementation trivia should not survive by inertia.
Planning review 2026-04-21: this bead is not permission for a mass test purge. It is a controlled delete/demote/quarantine pass after inventory and validator ratchet are in place.
Unblocked by yazelix-rdn7.4.1 inventory. Start from docs/specs/governed_test_traceability_inventory.md. Important constraints: five default component files are still quarantined for broad file-level traceability; do not delete only-known defenses listed in the inventory; prioritize draining item-ID gaps via yazelix-7upo before pruning command-discovery, generated-config duplicate, or historical byte-compatibility tests.

title: Evaluate Rust test hardening tools for high-value owned logic
description: Decide whether Yazelix should add targeted Rust test hardening tools such as cargo-mutants, cargo-fuzz, or cargo-nextest, and define the narrow surfaces where they provide signal without becoming noisy process weight.
design: Evaluate tools against current Rust-owned deterministic logic: config normalization, route planning/public command surface, materialization render plans, TOML/KDL/text generation, runtime contract evaluation, and report JSON shaping. Default to targeted use, not full-suite blanket enforcement.
acceptance_criteria: The decision records keep/reject for cargo-mutants, cargo-fuzz, and cargo-nextest; packaging impact; expected command entrypoints; which modules are eligible; CI/local lane placement; and explicit stop conditions for tools that are too slow, flaky, or broad. Any adopted Rust test tool must include the crate-vs-in-house/dev-dependency decision required by AGENTS.md, including rejected alternatives and packaging impact. If adopted, follow-up implementation beads are created.
notes:
References: cargo-mutants docs: https://docs.rs/crate/cargo-mutants/latest ; cargo-mutants limitations on hermetic tests: https://mutants.rs/limitations.html ; Rust Fuzz Book: https://rust-fuzz.github.io/book/ ; cargo-nextest docs: https://nexte.st/ ; Cargo test docs: https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/commands/cargo-test.html

Completed 2026-04-22 with docs/specs/rust_test_hardening_tools_decision.md. Decision: keep cargo test as canonical baseline, adopt cargo-nextest narrowly as an optional maintainer/CI lane, reject cargo-mutants and cargo-fuzz for now, and create follow-up bead yazelix-fkgs for the nextest pilot.

Cover the helper output shape, configured yazi/ya overrides, sidebar enablement, and managed editor kind resolution without rebuilding another broad integration suite. description: The biggest remaining Nushell bucket is dev and test code. Large Nu test files still defend behavior now owned by Rust, especially generated-config, materialization, public Rust-owned command families, and helper payload shaping. Port only strong deterministic coverage to Rust where Rust already owns the behavior, delete weak Nu tests, and treat any not-yet-portable remainder as blocked on a future owner cut or Rust harness lane rather than a Nu survivor. Migrated first-party Rust suites should land nextest-compatible and nextest-owned by default.

Count progress only when strong Rust contract coverage lands, the resulting Rust suite runs cleanly under nextest by default, and Nu test files or helper surfaces materially shrink.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The large deterministic Nu test files are split into strong Rust-port, weak-delete, and blocked-on-owner-or-harness buckets. 2. New Rust coverage is added only where Rust already owns the behavior and the test defends a live contract, regression, or invariant. 3. Replaced or weak Nu tests are deleted rather than kept or demoted. 4. Migrated first-party Rust test buckets are nextest-first by default, with cargo test retained only for doctests and explicit exceptions. 5. Any blocked remainder has explicit follow-up owner-cut or harness beads instead of an accepted keep-in-Nu status.

Surviving Nu validator floor is limited to syntax plus Nix/package/install probes: validate_syntax.nu, validate_flake_interface.nu, validate_flake_install.nu, validate_nixpkgs_package.nu, and validate_nixpkgs_submission.nu.

title: Define the honest owner boundary for validate_installed_runtime_contract.nu
description: Separate the deterministic contract checks inside validate_installed_runtime_contract.nu from the parts that are still genuinely live runtime or shell probes before promising a Rust validator cut.
design: Do not oversell a full port if the installed-runtime lane still needs a narrow Nu or shell probe boundary.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The deterministic versus live-probe boundary is explicit. 2. The follow-up implementation lane is explicit. 3. Any required surviving Nu or shell probe layer is justified.
notes: Boundary decision: validate_installed_runtime_contract.nu has no honest surviving Nu owner. Repo file checks, flake surface checks, built-output inspection, and built yzx smoke are all fixed external probes that Rust can own directly. The follow-up should port the validator end-to-end to Rust and keep any remaining nix/build/cli probes explicit and fixed-argv inside Rust.
close_reason: Completed

title: Port the deterministic installed-runtime contract checks or record the explicit Nu stop condition
description: Port validate_installed_runtime_contract.nu end-to-end to Rust. The fixed nix/build/output/smoke probes stay explicit, but they do not justify a surviving Nu owner.
design: Keep the installed-runtime validator inside the Rust repo-validator surface. External probes such as nix flake show, nix build, and the built yzx smoke command remain fixed argv subprocesses owned by Rust rather than a broad Nu harness.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Installed-runtime contract ownership no longer lives in broad Nu validator code. 2. Any remaining external command probes are explicit fixed-argv Rust subprocesses. 3. The Nu owner is deleted or reduced to a thin compatibility shim only if that shim still buys something real.
notes: Ported the installed-runtime contract validator to Rust in yzx_repo_validator validate-installed-runtime-contract. The retained Nix and built-yzx smoke probes are fixed argv calls inside Rust; deleted nushell/scripts/dev/validate_installed_runtime_contract.nu.

title: Adopt cargo-nextest as the default runner for first-party Rust tests and define explicit cargo test exceptions
description: The earlier hardening decision and pilot treated cargo-nextest as an optional lane. After the pilot and the current Rust-first test direction, that posture is too soft. Adopt nextest as the default runner for first-party Rust test buckets and define the exact cases that should remain on cargo test, such as doctests or any other unsupported exception surface.
design: This is a policy and ownership bead, not a broad tooling experiment. The goal is one clear default for Rust tests, less runner ambiguity, and fewer duplicated invocation paths. Exceptions should be explicit, narrow, and tied to real tool limitations rather than habit.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Nextest is the default runner for first-party Rust test buckets. 2. The cargo test exception list is explicit and narrow. 3. Existing rollout beads are updated to implement the policy instead of the older optional-pilot posture. 4. The policy does not authorize weaker tests or duplicated runner surfaces.

title: Run recursive delete-first subsystem canonicalization audits
description: Perform a no-code-change audit pass across Yazelix subsystems. For each subsystem, read the code, specs, and tests; question every requirement; identify the canonical owner; name missing or extra layers; find duplicated code and glue; classify surviving Nushell as delete now, bridge to collapse, big Rust-port target, or likely survivor; then create precise implementation beads.
design: This is the recursive entry point for subsystem-by-subsystem canonicalization. The output should be beads and contract updates, not opportunistic code edits. Each audit child should produce a short owner map, bridge burn list, DRY/deletion opportunities, test/spec gaps, and proposed next beads.
acceptance_criteria: Every major subsystem has an audit bead with clear scope and acceptance criteria. A shared audit template and feature-preservation gate exists before subsystem audits start. Audit results name must-not-lose behavior, current verification, canonical owner map, concrete deletion lanes, no-go survivor reasons, and follow-up beads. Audits avoid vague 'clean up code' tasks. No implementation changes happen inside audit beads unless a child explicitly switches from planning to implementation after acceptance criteria are clear.
notes:
Current 2026-04-21 rough split: product/runtime Nu excluding dev and maintainer is about 9,763 LOC; Nu dev/test harness is about 17,223 LOC; maintainer Nu is about 2,138 LOC. Re-measure during audit and separate product deletion from test/dev harness deletion.
Planning review 2026-04-21: added yazelix-rdn7.5.8 as the common audit-template gate so recursive audits preserve functionality and produce comparable outputs.
close_reason: Completed all recursive subsystem audit children and produced follow-up deletion beads.

It records which live specs need normative contract items and which tests/validators currently defend them.

acceptance_criteria: A reusable audit template exists in the bead notes or linked docs. Each audit child must name must-not-lose behavior, current verification, proposed canonical owner, deletion class, and stop conditions. The template explicitly says no code/test/doc deletion is allowed from an audit unless retained behavior and verification are named.

The template now forces must-not-lose behavior, current verification, candidate surviving owner, deletion class, stop conditions, and explicit no-deletion-inside-the-audit rules before future subsystem audit beads proceed.

title: Convert canonical audits into a ranked Nu deletion budget
description: After schema/test/subsystem audits land, consolidate the findings into a ranked deletion budget for remaining Nushell and redundant glue. This should separate product/runtime Nu, integration Nu, launch/session Nu, dev/test Nu, maintainer Nu, and likely survivors.
design: The budget should not be a LOC vanity metric. Each candidate must state what behavior remains, the canonical Rust/Nu/plugin/POSIX owner after deletion, required tests/contracts, and whether deletion is now, bridge-collapse, big Rust port, or no-go survivor.
acceptance_criteria: A ranked deletion budget exists with concrete follow-up beads for high-value work. Each item states expected deleted files/LOC, retained functionality, canonical owner after deletion, required contract IDs or contract gaps, verification plan, and stop condition. The budget separates product/runtime Nu from integration, launch/session, dev/test, maintainer, and likely-survivor Nu. It does not wait for all weak-test cleanup to be implemented, but it must consume the test inventory so deletion lanes know whether verification is already strong or needs prerequisite work. It includes a public-facing summary that avoids promising deletion where shell/process ownership is genuinely irreducible.
notes:
This is the bridge between plan-space and implementation-space. Do not start broad rewrites until the audit shows deletion value and contract coverage.
Planning review 2026-04-21: dependency narrowed from completed test cleanup/tool decisions to the test inventory. The deletion budget is a planning output; individual implementation beads can depend on test cleanup or tool hardening when needed.
Completed 2026-04-22 with docs/specs/ranked_nu_deletion_budget.md plus public-facing inventory updates in docs/subsystem_code_inventory.md. The ranked remaining lanes are nuj1, p18h, jkk3, 4ucy, and l533; jkk3 was created as the missing integration follow-up bead.

title: Pilot the contract schema on one subsystem before broad rollout
description: Apply the schema decision to one representative subsystem before public docs, validators, and broad audits harden around it. Use the pilot to prove the schema is sharp enough to preserve behavior, expose duplicate owners, and map tests without becoming bureaucracy.
design: Use config/runtime/control-plane as the preferred pilot unless the schema decision names a better subsystem. The pilot should produce a small contract-item set, mapped tests or verification statuses, and a list of lessons for the public protocol and validators. Do not change product behavior during the pilot.
acceptance_criteria: One representative subsystem has a draft contract-item mapping with owner, status, verification, and deletion implications. The pilot records schema adjustments or confirms the schema as-is. It identifies at least one weak/orphan test or duplicate owner if present, but does not delete anything during the pilot. Public docs and validators use the pilot result rather than an untested schema.
notes:
Planning review 2026-04-21: adding this pilot avoids overfitting a validator and public protocol to a theoretical schema.
Completed 2026-04-21: added docs/specs/config_runtime_control_plane_contract_item_pilot.md as the mixed-subsystem pilot. The pilot mapped five concrete config/runtime/control-plane items, marked the surviving Nu bridge layer as quarantine debt, identified broad default-lane traceability debt in test_yzx_generated_configs.nu/test_yzx_core_commands.nu/test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu, and confirmed the canonical contract-item schema works as-is without product changes.

Verified git diff --check, validate-upgrade-contract, validate-readme-version, validate-flake-interface, validate-nix-customization-api, and flake attr absence for old cursor outputs.

title: Design host or optional graphics-wrapper mode for Linux runtimes
description:
The current Linux Yazelix runtime bundles runtime-owned NixGL graphics wrappers. Initial closure investigation found `nixGLMesa`/`nixVulkanMesa` pull in duplicated LLVM and Mesa paths, dominating the yzxterm runtime closure. Design and validate a safer mode that can reduce this weight without breaking desktop launch reliability.

Context:
- Current yzxterm runtime includes runtime-owned `nixGLMesa` and `nixVulkanMesa`.
- Local installed Yazelix closure is about 3.6 GiB.
- Largest NAR paths include two LLVM libs around 540 MiB and 528 MiB, plus two Mesa paths around 272 MiB and 264 MiB.
- Ownership trace reaches those paths through NixGL/Vulkan wrapper packages.
- Current contract treats graphics wrappers as bootstrap/runtime-owned, not hostable/disableable via `runtime_tool_sources`.

Questions:
1. Should graphics wrappers become an explicit package option, such as bundled, host, or none?
2. Which runtime variants actually need OpenGL and/or Vulkan wrappers?
3. Can yzxterm avoid an outer wrapper because its child package already handles runtime libraries?
4. What launch/doctor failures should users see when wrappers are host-sourced or disabled?

Acceptance criteria:
- Record the current contract paths that require runtime-owned NixGL.
- Measure closure impact of removing or host-sourcing graphics wrappers for at least yzxterm.
- Propose the exact option surface, likely Home Manager and `lib.mkYazelix`, or reject the option with evidence.
- If accepted, create an implementation bead with tests for launch command rendering and doctor diagnostics.
- Do not change default behavior until desktop-launch reliability is preserved.
close_reason: Recorded current NixGL contract paths and yzxterm closure impact; accepted explicit Linux graphics wrapper source mode with default bundled behavior unchanged; created implementation bead yazelix-linux-graphics-wrapper-source-mode-6nhcg.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: Design complete. Current contract paths: `packaging/runtime_tool_registry.nix` imports Linux NixGL and includes GL/Vulkan wrapper packages; `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/launch_commands/terminal.rs` resolves runtime or host wrappers and prepends them for Ghostty/WezTerm/Kitty/Ratty while yzxterm intentionally avoids an outer wrapper; `docs/contracts/terminal_launch_contract.md` records Ratty Vulkan wrapper preference and yzxterm no-outer-wrapper behavior; `docs/contracts/nix_customization_surfaces.md` currently keeps graphics wrappers bundled until a separate contract. Measured yzxterm graphics wrappers at 2,160,340,584 bytes combined unique closure, leaving an approximate 1,740,980,504 byte no-wrapper yzxterm closure. Accepted design: add explicit Linux graphics wrapper source mode (`bundled`, `host`, `none`) outside `runtime_tool_sources`, default unchanged. Follow-up implementation bead: `yazelix-linux-graphics-wrapper-source-mode-6nhcg`.

title: Investigate Codex and Claude quota widgets
description: Investigate whether Yazelix should add separate quota/rate-limit widgets for Codex and Claude, distinct from local token/cost usage widgets. Evaluate tokenusage --official-limits, Codex/Claude auth/API requirements, refresh cadence, cache shape, failure modes, privacy/network implications, and display formats such as codex 5h/week remaining. Acceptance: document whether quota facts are safe to collect in the pane-orchestrator background path, recommended refresh interval, provider-specific commands/APIs, and whether to implement codex_quota/claude_quota widgets.
design:
Decision: quota/rate-limit widgets should be separate from token/cost usage widgets and must not run provider or network probes from zjstatus. Quota facts are account-level, network/auth-sensitive, and can be rate limited independently of local log scanning.

Recommended architecture:
- Add a shared cross-window quota cache under the Yazelix cache directory, not the per-window status-bar cache, so multiple Yazelix windows do not duplicate OAuth/API probes
- Widgets read only cached quota facts and render nothing or a stale/error marker from cache; they never invoke tu, Codex, Claude, or network clients directly
- Refresh in the pane-orchestrator background path only when quota widgets are enabled, with per-provider locks, atomic writes, and per-provider freshness/backoff
- Use a much longer refresh cadence than token/cost usage: 10-15 minutes normal TTL, 30+ minutes backoff after 429/unauthorized/network errors
- Store per-provider facts as official values only: provider, plan, captured_at, source, five_hour_remaining_percent, five_hour_resets_at, weekly_remaining_percent, weekly_resets_at, credits when available, status/error

Provider findings:
- Codex: OpenAI Codex source parses rate-limit headers into primary/secondary windows and /status displays 5h and weekly remaining plus credits. tokenusage exposes structured JSON through `tu blocks --active --json --official-limits --no-claude --no-antigravity`; local probe returned official_codex with plan_type, primary/secondary used percents, window minutes, reset timestamps in about 1s. codex-cli-usage is a small Codex-only alternative that calls the same ChatGPT backend usage API and caches `~/.codex/usage-limits.json`, but Yazelix already packages tu.
- Claude: official Claude Code statusline stdin now includes `rate_limits.five_hour` and `rate_limits.seven_day` after the first API response for Claude.ai subscribers. That is the best source because it avoids extra OAuth calls. tokenusage can also probe Claude, but its implementation drives `/usage` through a PTY, then falls back to OAuth/cache; local probes hit 429/unauthorized, so this should not be a frequent Yazelix background poll.

Display recommendation: keep labels compact and remaining-oriented, for example `[codex q 5h49 wk80]` or `[claude q 5h85 wk62]`; reset timers can be an optional verbose mode or popup detail, not the default bar text.
close_reason: Completed: investigated current quota sources, benchmarked tokenusage official Codex JSON, confirmed Claude tokenusage probing can rate-limit/unauthorize locally, recorded the shared-cache recommendation, and filed implementation follow-ups yazelix-3urp and yazelix-2pnd.

title: Remove bundled mise and tombi from the default Yazelix runtime
description:
The default Yazelix runtime currently bundles `mise` and `tombi`, but both are host/maintainer-adjacent tools rather than core runtime-owned binaries. `mise` is a host tool manager and should not be owned by the packaged runtime by default; `tombi` should be audited the same way before remaining in the default closure. Runtime size measurements show the bundled `mise-2026.5.12` direct reference contributes about 498 MiB of closure weight in the current default package shape.

Context:
- `runtime_tool_sources.mise = "host"` and `runtime_tool_sources.tombi = "host"` are already supported escape hatches.
- The lean Home Manager/package-builder profile gains significant closure reduction by host-sourcing these leaf tools alongside editor/sidebar/helper tools.
- Generated shell initialization, TOML tooling, maintainer docs, and runtime docs may currently assume bundled availability.

Acceptance criteria:
- Remove `mise` and `tombi` from default bundled runtime ownership, or replace either with an explicit opt-in surface if bundled mode remains justified.
- Audit generated shell initializers, TOML tooling paths, runtime manifests, `yzx env`, doctor diagnostics, and docs for assumptions that these commands are always bundled.
- Preserve clear behavior for users who want host-managed commands; missing host commands must produce actionable diagnostics instead of silent degradation.
- Measure before/after closure impact with `shells/posix/yazelix_runtime_size_report.sh`.
- Update Home Manager and installation docs to describe the new ownership boundary.
- Run the focused package/runtime validation ladder for touched Nix and runtime surfaces.
close_reason: Changed default runtime tool sources so mise and tombi are host optional integrations, preserved explicit bundled mode, updated doctor/docs/contracts, verified runtime manifest/toolbin, and measured #yazelix at 3.0 GiB / 816 paths.

title: Reproduce yzxterm crash under high-rate Codex TUI output
description:
A Yazelix Terminal window reportedly disappeared after Codex began updating very quickly, while the Zellij session survived. Reproduce or falsify a high-rate Codex/Zellij/Rio output crash and identify whether the culprit is Codex output, Zellij relay behavior, or yzxterm/Rio rendering/window handling.

Evidence so far:
- User observed Codex moving very fast immediately before the crash.
- The older `likable-muskrat` Zellij server survived the window loss and still had active Codex processes.
- No coredump, kernel segfault/OOM/GPU fault, or fresh Rio log was captured for the incident.

Questions:
1. Can a high-rate Codex TUI stream make yzxterm/Rio exit, freeze, or close?
2. Does the same workload reproduce in vanilla Rio, Ghostty, WezTerm, or Kitty?
3. Does the issue require Zellij in the middle?
4. Is there a specific escape sequence, viewport update pattern, cursor-effect path, or renderer state that correlates with the failure?

Acceptance criteria:
- Define a repeatable high-output TUI stress scenario approximating Codex fast updates.
- Run it in yzxterm with logging enabled and record whether the terminal exits.
- Compare against at least one non-yzxterm terminal if practical.
- Capture enough logs or terminal transcript data to file the right upstream/fork issue.
- If the failure reproduces, create a focused implementation/upstreaming bead with minimized evidence.
- If it does not reproduce, document residual risk and required user-captured evidence for the next incident.

title: Trim config UI tab spacing and header padding
description: The config UI header needs slightly more lateral breathing room, and the tabs should not reserve an empty bordered row before the settings body.
acceptance_criteria: Header content has horizontal padding from the terminal edge; tabs render as one unbordered row; no blank divider row appears between tabs and the body.
close_reason: Completed: header content now has lateral padding and tabs render as a single unbordered row.

title: Audit and prune the default-lane test suite
close_reason: Completed

title: Thin Yazi writer boundary before public extraction
description: Follow up the private Yazi writer split by reducing Yazelix-specific policy inside yazi_materialization/writer.rs before any public Yazi integration extraction. The goal is a thinner writer contract, not a new child repo yet.
design: Delete-first. Identify whether runtime placeholder rendering, opener preservation, asset sync, and generated file headers belong in the writer or adapter. Move policy toward adapter only when it makes the writer more reusable without duplicating logic. Do not rebuild broad caching from yazelix-8h9y unless the invalidation story is naturally simple.
acceptance_criteria: The bead records a responsibility map for yazi_materialization.rs versus writer.rs, deletes or moves policy only when behavior remains equivalent, keeps generated yazi.toml/keymap.toml/init.lua/asset tests passing, updates Yazi boundary docs and Rust budget, and records whether public extraction remains deferred.
notes: Completed Yazi writer thinning. Responsibility map: yazi_materialization.rs owns Yazelix config normalization, managed user override path resolution, legacy override errors, semantic Yazi action keybinding validation, and parsing managed user yazi.toml/yazi_keymap.toml/yazi_init.lua into optional overlays; yazi_materialization/writer.rs owns bundled base config reads, overlay merging, theme/keymap/init generation, static asset sync, runtime placeholder rendering, managed output permissions, and atomic writes. Public extraction remains deferred because generated-file headers, runtime placeholder ownership, and asset-sync policy are still Yazelix-shaped. Deleted duplicate writer-internal tests for keymap-section preservation and placeholder rendering because yzx_core_yazi_materialization integration tests defend the generated behavior end-to-end. Scorecard: 145 insertions / 168 deletions, net -23 raw Rust lines; tokei Rust code LOC 66641 -> 66621 (-20); config/materialization family 20684 -> 20661. Verification: cargo fmt --all -- --check; cargo check -p yazelix_core --all-targets; cargo test -p yazelix_core --test yzx_core_yazi_materialization --test yzx_core_yazi_render_plan yazi; cargo test -p yazelix_core yazi_materialization; validate-rust-ownership-budget; validate-docs-experience; git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: thinned Yazi writer override boundary

title: Audit and prune the full Yazelix test surface for signal
design: Treat this as an aggressive pruning program, not a test-preservation exercise. First inventory every test file by lane, contract, score, and likely verdict. Then audit the default lane test-by-test using the new strength rubric and remove or demote weak/default-inappropriate cases. After that, audit nondefault lanes file-by-file to decide whether each file still deserves to exist. Finish by applying deletions, demotions, and runner simplifications so the repo is smaller and the suite stays easier to reason about.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Every test file under nushell/scripts/dev/test_*.nu is inventoried with lane, rough contract, and a keep/demote/remove/rewrite verdict. 2. The default lane is reviewed test-by-test and at least one weak, redundant, or mis-laned test is removed, demoted, or rewritten if such a case exists. 3. Nondefault lanes are reviewed file-by-file and dead-weight files or tests are removed or narrowed where justified. 4. The resulting runner/validator surfaces remain coherent and all affected validation lanes pass. 5. The audit leaves behind a concise written inventory or report so future pruning starts from reality instead of memory.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add Yazelix zen mode for chrome-free focused work
description: Provide a first-party Yazelix zen mode that hides Yazelix chrome while keeping the active work surface recoverable. Zen mode should hide the top Yazelix zjstatus bar, the bottom Zellij hints/status surface, and the managed sidebar for focused work.
design: Treat zen mode as an explicit layout/chrome state transition, not as a raw pane hide/show trick. Use Zellij override-layout or Yazelix-owned layout-family arithmetic for tiled bars and sidebar geometry, because Zellij plugin hide_pane_with_id suppresses panes and later re-adds them rather than restoring a stable bottom-row slot. The pane orchestrator should own per-tab zen state, remember the previous chrome/sidebar state, and restore it deliberately. Prefer an on-demand floating hints surface while zen is active.
acceptance_criteria: A user can toggle zen mode for the active Yazelix tab. Entering zen hides the top bar, bottom hints/status row, and managed sidebar while preserving the active editor/shell pane. Exiting zen restores the previous top/bottom/sidebar visibility state or fails clearly with actionable diagnostics. The implementation does not rely on hide_pane_with_id/show_pane_with_id for stable tiled bar geometry. yzx inspect or the pane-orchestrator debug surface reports zen state and the saved restore target. Runtime validation records visible pane geometry before entering, during zen, and after restoring.
notes: 2026-04-28 scope correction: this bead is intentionally future-facing and should not be implemented before proving the smaller bottom-bar toggle POC. Zen mode remains a product direction, but the implementation gate is yazelix-2ex.1.7.6.1: first prove active-tab bottom-row hide/restore with override-layout or equivalent layout arithmetic, including geometry evidence. Only after that should zen mode add top-bar and sidebar restore semantics.

title: Trim stale devenv wording and maintainer-root heuristics from live v15 code
description: Remove stale devenv-era wording and unnecessary maintainer heuristics from live code. Scope: update startup/environment/initializer comments so they describe the current runtime contract, stop requiring devenv-specific repo-root hints where a normal repo-root check is enough, and keep only the maintainer-specific devenv references that are still genuinely required.
close_reason: Completed: removed stale devenv-era wording from live runtime code, simplified repo-root detection away from DEVENV_ROOT, and updated maintainer tests to match the current checkout contract.

title: Make random welcome rotate equally across GOL boids and Mandelbrot
description: Current welcome randomization does not give equal exposure to the three live screen families. Mandelbrot is currently screen-only, while random welcome resolves through the retained Game of Life pool plus existing boids behavior. The desired UX is a fair 1/3 chance for Game of Life, boids, or Mandelbrot when welcome_style = random.
design: Keep explicit welcome styles supported. Change only the random welcome selection contract. Random should choose one family with equal probability: Game of Life, boids, Mandelbrot. For the Game of Life family, either pick a stable/default GOL variant or split the family share across existing GOL variants; document whichever rule is chosen. Use deterministic index/seed plumbing already present in front_door_render tests so tests can assert the rotation without host randomness. Do not make random choose static or logo.
acceptance_criteria: 1. welcome_style = random has a 1/3 family split across Game of Life, boids, and Mandelbrot. 2. Mandelbrot is accepted as an internal welcome style when selected explicitly or by random, while public yzx screen behavior remains unchanged. 3. Config metadata, default config comment, Home Manager option docs, and CLI/docs style lists are updated consistently. 4. Existing explicit styles still work. 5. Tests defend the fair family rotation and exclude static/logo from random.
notes: Dependency decision before implementation: use existing Rust std/in-house renderer logic only. No new production crates, no new dev crates. Random-family selection should reuse the existing deterministic seed/index plumbing in yazelix_screen/front-door tests. Packaging impact: none.
close_reason: Completed: welcome random now rotates evenly across Game of Life, boids variants, and Mandelbrot while yzx screen random remains Game-of-Life-only.

title: Audit install and desktop refresh flows against the installed runtime
description: Verify the real effects of the flake install and desktop install paths against the installed runtime. Audit which state they are expected to update, then confirm whether runtime/current, ~/.local/bin/yzx, generated configs, shell hooks, and the desktop entry are actually refreshed or left stale.
close_reason: Audit completed: install and desktop install now refresh the installed runtime coherently; stale desktop contract replaced by stable yzx launcher path.

title: yzx onboard
description: Guide first-time users through creating the supported Yazelix config set instead of making them hand-edit configuration from scratch. Keep yazelix.toml as the front door, but allow onboarding to emit optional sidecars such as yazelix_packs.toml when that is the supported architecture.
design: Treat yzx onboard as an interactive first-run config generator, not as a generic tutorial surface. The command should ask the user a focused set of high-value questions and then write a sensible configuration outcome based on the answers. The interaction model should feel like a lightweight terminal questionnaire: arrow-key navigation for single-choice prompts, and explicit multi-select prompts where space toggles choices and enter confirms. Keep the scope tight around the settings that matter most for getting productive quickly rather than exposing every config knob up front. Question areas should likely include terminal preference, shell choice, editor choice, whether the sidebar is enabled, whether sessions are persistent, and a small set of useful packs or defaults. The output should stay aligned with the supported config architecture rather than hardcoding everything into yazelix.toml forever; if pack config lives in yazelix_packs.toml, onboarding should generate that file instead of collapsing pack answers back into the main file.
acceptance_criteria: `yzx onboard` can guide a first-time user through creating a valid supported Yazelix configuration with a small number of high-signal prompts. Single-choice prompts support arrow-key selection, and multi-select prompts support toggling choices before confirmation. The generated output is valid, readable, and aligned with the same supported options as the normal config path, including optional sidecars where the architecture calls for them. Add behavior tests for prompt flow and generated-config output, including at least one multi-select case, one interrupted-or-aborted flow, one case that writes the main yazelix.toml only, and one case that writes a pack sidecar when pack answers require it.
notes: 2026-04-26 scope correction during implementation: current trimmed Yazelix does not have a supported yazelix_packs.toml sidecar or pack graph, so yzx onboard must not recreate one. Use current supported main-config fields only; multi-select coverage should target current config such as zellij.widget_tray.
close_reason: Completed: added Rust-owned yzx onboard with interactive single/multi-select prompt state, current main-config generation, overwrite guard, docs, command metadata, and focused tests. Scope intentionally excludes removed packs sidecar.

title: Harden flake onboarding for host tool bootstrap
description:
Problem:
The flake install surface still assumes too much about host bootstrap tools. Recent failures showed that host Nushell version drift can break install/setup even when the packaged runtime is correct.

Design:
Track the remaining onboarding hardening work as a small subtree focused on bootstrap-tool assumptions, especially host Nushell dependence and explicit pinned-tool onboarding behavior.

Acceptance Criteria:
- The remaining host-tool assumptions in the flake onboarding path are explicit and tracked.
- Host Nushell dependence is addressed separately from broader pinned-tool update workflow.
- The onboarding story for pinned bootstrap tools is coherent.
close_reason: Completed

title: Make flake install/bootstrap independent of host Nushell version
description:
Problem:
The flake installer and bootstrap path still expose users to host Nushell compatibility issues. Recent install failures on Nushell 0.108 proved that Yazelix bootstrap is not yet sufficiently isolated from the user's ambient Nu version.

Design:
Audit and reduce every bootstrap-sensitive path that still depends on host Nushell parsing or runtime behavior, and ensure the installer uses the packaged Nushell/tooling path consistently enough that host Nu version drift no longer breaks first-run onboarding.

Acceptance Criteria:
- The flake install/bootstrap path no longer fails because of host Nushell version drift in the supported onboarding flow.
- Bootstrap-sensitive scripts either avoid host Nu entirely or are explicitly compatible with the supported host floor.
- The maintained smoke/install lane covers the intended bootstrap contract.
close_reason: Completed

title: Clarify pinned bootstrap-tool onboarding responsibilities
description:
Problem:
Yazelix now pins and manages some bootstrap-adjacent tools, but the onboarding story is still fuzzy about what the installer should install directly versus what it should merely assume or validate.

Design:
Define the onboarding contract for pinned bootstrap tools such as devenv, and clarify what is in scope for the installer versus what must already exist on the host.

Acceptance Criteria:
- The onboarding contract clearly states which bootstrap tools Yazelix installs or refreshes itself.
- The docs/specs distinguish installer-managed bootstrap tools from host prerequisites.
- The result does not blur into unrelated release/update automation work.
close_reason: Completed

title: Investigate remaining duplicate shellhook contexts on cold profiled startup
description: The Yazelix-owned refreshed startup path no longer re-enters `devenv shell` after rebuild, but a truly cold `yzx dev profile --cold --clear-cache` run still shows two shellhook PID contexts. Warm startup does not show this duplication. This follow-up tracks the remaining deeper investigation separately from the completed refreshed-startup cleanup.
design: Treat this as a narrower cold-path investigation, not a redo of the refreshed startup ownership cleanup. The remaining duplicate shellhook contexts survive after the rebuilt-profile activation simplification, so first determine whether they come from upstream `devenv build shell` behavior, a profiling harness interaction, or another cold-only shell activation boundary. Prefer an explicit explanation and the smallest correct fix over more startup-path branching.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The remaining duplicate shellhook contexts on truly cold profiled startup have a concrete root-cause explanation. 2. If Yazelix owns the duplication, remove or narrow it without regressing visible rebuild behavior or startup correctness. 3. If the duplication is upstream or intentionally unavoidable, document that contract clearly in the bead and keep the startup profile output interpretable. 4. Warm startup remains unchanged and the existing refreshed-startup regression continues to pass.
notes: Root cause was not an unavoidable duplicate shellhook entry. The refreshed-startup path was resolving runtime-owned launch state to the .devenv/gc/shell script path itself instead of extracting the embedded DEVENV_PROFILE. Because direct activation was using a devenv-shell script path rather than the real devenv-profile, cold profiling and refreshed startup behavior became misleading. Fix: resolve shell-script candidates through readlink first, extract the embedded profile path robustly, and defend it with a workspace regression that uses a real .devenv/gc/shell fixture. After reinstalling the runtime, cold yzx dev profile --cold --clear-cache no longer records shellhook contexts at all; it goes straight from bootstrap.devenv.build_shell into inner startup. Warm profiling remains clean.
close_reason: Fixed runtime-owned profile resolution; cold startup profiling no longer shows duplicate shellhook contexts

title: add a link to my personal home manager files in the contributing section
description: Imported GitHub issue #601.

title: Migrate flake outputs to flake-parts
design: Refactor flake.nix to use flake-parts for per-system structure without changing public outputs. Start with packages, apps, devShells, and checks. Keep package implementation files under packaging/ unchanged. Preserve existing overlays, Home Manager modules, mkYazelix API, package names, app names, and checks. Avoid bundling unrelated runtime or Home Manager behavior changes.
acceptance_criteria: flake-parts is added as an input. packages/apps/devShells/checks are expressed through flake-parts perSystem or equivalent modules. Existing public flake output names still evaluate: yazelix, runtime_ghostty, runtime_wezterm, yazelix_kgp_yazi, yazelix_zellij_bar, apps.yzx, devShells.default, devShells.ci, and checks. validate-flake-interface and validate-nix-customization-api pass. No package implementation behavior changes are mixed into the refactor.

title: Publish KGP Yazi and Zellij runtime packages to binary cache
description: Add CI/cache publication for the expensive KGP Yazi and KGP Zellij runtime package outputs so routine runtime_ghostty validation and user installs substitute them instead of rebuilding large Rust packages locally. This should complement the existing generic cache-hit verification bead and should document expected fallback behavior when a revision is uncached.
close_reason: Completed: exposed #yazelix_kgp_yazi and #yazelix_kgp_zellij package outputs, added them to the Cachix publication workflow before the wrapper packages, and documented fallback behavior plus cache verification for uncached revisions.

title: Organize the repository root and clarify root-level ownership
description: Audit the repository root and make root-level ownership legible. The goal is not cosmetic churn; it is to decide which files truly belong at the repo root, which should move under docs/ or a more specific subsystem directory, and which root artifacts need explicit documentation because they are part of the product or maintainer contract.
design: Treat the repo root as a high-signal surface. Root-level files should exist only when they are one of: repository entrypoints, packaging/build metadata, maintainer workflow files, user-facing discovery docs, or source-of-truth config/templates that genuinely belong at the top level. Everything else should either move under a more specific directory or be documented as intentionally root-level. Prefer delete-first reasoning over aesthetic reshuffling: remove stale or redundant root artifacts, group related files when a clearer home exists, and avoid broad moves that break discoverability without a real ownership win.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Inventory the current root-level files and classify them into intentional root ownership, should-move, or should-delete/document categories. 2. At least one real root-level ambiguity, redundancy, or stale artifact is either removed or re-homed. 3. The remaining root-level surfaces have a clearer ownership story consistent with current Yazelix architecture and maintainer workflow. 4. Any user-facing or maintainer-facing moves are reflected in docs or workflow references. 5. Leave behind a short summary of what root artifacts are intentionally kept and why.
notes:
Completed with a conservative first slice focused on root discoverability rather than broad reshuffling.

Removed:
- deleted the unreferenced docs_explore/ root directory and its stale exploratory Helix keybinding reference because current maintained guidance already lives under docs/keybindings.md and docs/helix_keybindings.md.

Intentionally kept at root:
- entrypoint/user-facing docs: README.md, CHANGELOG.md, AGENTS.md, LICENSE, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md
- build and packaging entrypoints: flake.nix, devenv.nix, devenv.yaml, flake.lock, devenv.lock, locked_devenv_package.nix, mk_runtime_tree.nix, mk_yazelix_package.nix, yazelix_package.nix, yazelix_runtime_package.nix
- maintainer workflow/config: .pre-commit-config.yaml, .taplo.toml, .nu-lint.toml, .github/, .beads/
- source-of-truth runtime templates: yazelix_default.toml, yazelix_packs_default.toml

Documentation follow-up:
- added a short root-ownership rule to docs/contributing.md
- repaired nearby stale docs/specs path references so maintainer workflow docs no longer point at a local config path
close_reason: Completed a conservative root-ownership cleanup slice: removed the stale docs_explore/ root subtree and documented what intentionally belongs at the repo root.

title: Verify Home Manager zjstatus first-install and upgrade path on macOS
description: A macOS/Home Manager tester reported that older Yazelix versions made zjstatus painful after install or upgrade and required manual intervention. This may be obsolete in v16, but the report is valuable enough to turn into a focused smoke/regression check.
acceptance_criteria: A v16+ Home Manager install or switch on macOS confirms that the status bar assets/config work without manual zjstatus steps, or a current bug is filed with the exact failing path and recovery guidance.

title: Fix cursor settings editing paths
description: Ratconfig refuses cursor settings as too complex and yzx edit cursors does not open the cursor settings surface. Make both cursor editing paths work against the canonical cursor settings file.
close_reason: Fixed cursor editing: yzx edit cursors opens the cursor sidecar, ratconfig exposes enabled_cursors and trail as pickers, and cursor registry rows point to direct file editing. Verified with cargo fmt --check, cargo check -p yazelix_core, focused config_ui/edit_commands tests, and yzx_control edit cursors --print.

title: Merge README editor pane orchestration into workspace model
description: The standalone README Editor Pane Orchestration section overlaps with Workspace Model. Merge the non-duplicated editor-pane behavior into Workspace Model and remove the separate section.
acceptance_criteria: README has no Editor Pane Orchestration heading; Workspace Model still explains managed editor targeting, reuse, pane creation when missing, Helix/Neovim coverage, and editor.command configuration without duplicating prose.
close_reason: Completed: removed the standalone README Editor Pane Orchestration section and merged the non-duplicated managed editor reuse/create behavior into Workspace Model. Verified the heading is gone, git diff --check, validate-docs-experience, and validate-readme-version.

title: Evaluate scoped Clap ownership for the Rust yzx root router
description: Evaluate whether Clap should replace the hand-rolled public Rust yzx root router, and implement only if it deletes enough parser/help/router code without duplicating command metadata or weakening explicit internal Nu route boundaries. The scope is the existing Rust root surface in rust_core/yazelix_core/src/bin/yzx.rs: help/version, yzx_control-backed leaves, yzx status, and explicit internal Nu routes for intentionally Nu-owned families.
design: Delete-first scope: this lane is not pre-approval for Clap. It must first prove that Clap removes meaningful hand-rolled parser/help/router ownership without creating a second public command registry beside command_metadata.rs. If Clap only becomes a nicer shim over the same manual route table, or if it obscures direct Nu helper routing, record a no-go and close the implementation bead instead of adding the dependency. Preserve all current user-visible behavior: root help/version, usage errors, env/run/update/status Rust routing, direct internal Nu module routes, aliases, subcommands, and argv passthrough/quoting.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The lane records a go/no-go decision for Clap before implementation. 2. The decision names the exact parser/help/router code that can disappear and the exact code that must remain. 3. The lane keeps command_metadata.rs or an explicit successor as the single public command metadata source; no parallel Clap-only registry is allowed. 4. Nu-owned command bodies remain explicit internal routes and are not silently reclassified as Rust-owned. 5. A parity harness exists before any router rewrite. 6. If Clap is not deletion-positive, the implementation bead closes with the stop condition instead of adding the crate.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Define the Clap go/no-go, deletion budget, and metadata ownership
description: Decide whether Clap is justified for the existing Rust-owned yzx root router before implementation. This bead may approve a scoped rewrite or record a no-go if Clap would add dependency/duplication risk without deleting enough current ownership.
design: Record the Rust dependency gate: production crates, dev-only crates, in-house logic, rejected alternatives, packaging impact, and why a broad CLI crate is justified for a public binary if accepted. The decision must compare at least three shapes: keep in-house parsing; Clap for top-level routing only while command_metadata.rs remains the help/extern/menu source; and Clap as, or feeding, the single command metadata source. Name the exact current code in rust_core/yazelix_core/src/bin/yzx.rs that can disappear or shrink: RootRoute/classify_root_route, root help/version flag matching, top-level command-family matching, unknown-command handling, and any duplicated help surface. Also name the code that must stay unless a separate owner cut exists: resolve_rust_control_path, resolve_nu_bin, resolve_internal_nu_module_path, render_nu_command_script/render_nu_arg shell-bound quoting, run_child, and explicit internal Nu module routing for Nu-owned families.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Records a go/no-go decision for Clap, not just an assumption. 2. Lists production crates, dev-only crates, in-house logic, rejected alternatives, and packaging impact. 3. Proves the chosen shape keeps a single source for public command metadata/help/extern/menu data. 4. Lists exact yzx.rs code to delete or shrink and exact code to preserve. 5. Lists every behavior that must remain parity-protected before implementation: no-arg help; help/-h/--help; version flags -V/--version/-v/--version-short; unknown top-level command error; env/run/update/status yzx_control routing; required-subcommand errors for desktop; unknown-subcommand errors for dev/import; config reset, edit config, home_manager prepare, keys aliases, tutor aliases; direct Nu module routing; argv passthrough for spaces, quotes, and --. 6. If the deletion budget is not enough, it defines the stop condition and instructs downstream beads to close without implementation.
notes:
Decision: no-go for a scoped Clap rewrite in the current shape.

Compared shapes:
1. Keep in-house parsing in rust_core/yazelix_core/src/bin/yzx.rs
- Production crates: existing serde, serde_json, thiserror, lexopt in workspace; no new crate required for yzx root
- Dev-only crates: existing assert_cmd and fixture/test crates only
- Logic kept in-house: RootRoute/classify_root_route, explicit NuRoutePlan planning, helper resolution, Nu argv quoting, direct internal Nu route handoff
- Packaging impact: none
- Result: preserves single metadata source and explicit route ownership with no new dependency

2. Clap for top-level routing only while command_metadata.rs remains the help/extern/menu source
- Production crate candidate: clap
- Rejected alternative for now
- Why rejected: deletes classify_root_route and some manual help/version handling, but still requires a parallel route definition for top-level commands, aliases, grouped subcommands, and direct Nu family dispatch. command_metadata.rs would remain a separate public metadata registry, so Clap becomes a second command surface description instead of a real owner cut.
- Packaging impact: adds a broad public-CLI dependency for limited deletion

3. Clap as, or feeding, the single metadata source
- Rejected as out of scope
- Why rejected: would require redesigning command_metadata.rs, render_yzx_help, yzx-command-metadata.externs, and menu/palette metadata around Clap or a new shared schema. That is no longer a scoped router rewrite; it is a broader public command metadata ownership rewrite.

Deletion budget outcome:
- Code that could shrink with Clap: RootRoute/classify_root_route, root help/version flag matching, top-level command-family matching, some unknown-command handling, and some required/unknown subcommand matching inside plan_internal_nu_route
- Code that must remain unless a separate owner cut exists: resolve_rust_control_path, resolve_nu_bin, resolve_internal_nu_module_path, render_nu_string_literal/render_nu_arg/render_nu_command_script, run_child, and the explicit internal Nu module routing for config/cwd/desktop/dev/doctor/edit/enter/home_manager/import/keys/launch/menu/popup/restart/reveal/screen/sponsor/tutor/whats_new/why
- Net result: not enough real deletion. The surviving direct-route table and single-source metadata constraint dominate the surface.

Required parity surface if revisited later:
- no-arg help
- help/-h/--help
- version flags -V/--version/-v/--version-short
- unknown top-level command error class/code
- env/run/update/status Rust routing
- required-subcommand errors for desktop
- unknown-subcommand errors for dev/import
- config reset, edit config, home_manager prepare
- keys aliases, tutor aliases
- direct Nu module routing
- argv passthrough for spaces, quotes, and --

Explicit stop condition:
Do not add Clap unless a future plan also deletes the separate route-definition duplication by making command_metadata.rs or an explicit successor the single source for both public metadata and parser shape, or otherwise removes a materially larger portion of the direct internal route table than this scoped lane can.
close_reason: Completed: recorded a no-go decision because a scoped Clap rewrite would duplicate command metadata and explicit route definitions without deleting enough current ownership.

title: Defend current yzx root parity before any Clap rewrite
description: Add or tighten high-signal parity coverage for the current Rust yzx root router before changing it. The goal is to prevent the Clap rewrite from losing aliases, subcommands, direct module routing, or argv passthrough behavior.
design: Use the parity matrix from yazelix-rxym.1. Prefer focused Rust unit tests for pure parser/planner behavior and existing Nushell CLI probe tests for launcher/direct-module behavior. Do not add weak help-output inventory tests that only duplicate command lists. Defend real behavior: route class, error class/code, direct module path, command_name, tail argv, and rendered Nu invocation quoting for spaces/quotes/--. Keep test lane and strength markers compliant with AGENTS.md.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Current root parser/planner behavior has focused parity coverage before the rewrite. 2. The coverage defends no-arg help, help/version flags, unknown top-level command errors, yzx_control routing, direct internal Nu route planning, grouped subcommands, aliases, required/unknown subcommand errors, and argv passthrough/quoting. 3. Existing direct-module CLI probe tests remain aligned with the route plan. 4. New tests follow lane and strength-marker rules. 5. Cargo test for the yzx root tests and relevant Nushell CLI probe tests pass.
close_reason: Closed without implementation: the go/no-go decision rejected the scoped Clap rewrite, so a pre-rewrite parity harness is not needed for this lane.

title: Implement or explicitly reject the scoped Clap root router rewrite
description: After the go/no-go decision and parity harness, either implement the scoped Clap root router rewrite or close with the explicit no-go stop condition.
design: If yazelix-rxym.1 approves Clap, implement only the scoped rewrite in rust_core/yazelix_core/src/bin/yzx.rs and related tests/docs. Preserve all existing command behavior and keep Nu-owned command bodies as explicit internal helper routes. Do not port launch, enter, restart, doctor, menu, edit, import, desktop, dev, tutor, keys, popup, screen, or other Nu-owned command bodies as part of this bead. Do not introduce a second command metadata registry: command_metadata.rs or an explicit successor remains the single source for root help, externs, and menu/palette metadata. If yazelix-rxym.1 records no-go, close this bead by recording the stop condition rather than adding Clap.
acceptance_criteria: 1. If implemented, the hand-rolled parser/help/router code named by rxym.1 is deleted or materially simplified. 2. If not implemented, the bead records the no-go stop condition and leaves code unchanged. 3. No command family loses behavior, aliases, subcommands, direct Nu module routing, or argv passthrough. 4. command_metadata/help/extern/menu ownership remains single-source. 5. Cargo fmt/check/test and the parity tests from rxym.2 pass. 6. Any new Rust tests follow lane and strength-marker rules.
close_reason: Closed without implementation: the scoped Clap rewrite failed the deletion-budget gate and must not add Clap in the current shape.

title: Evaluate Ratatui config editor for Yazelix settings
description: Evaluate a terminal UI for editing Yazelix settings without making users manually hunt through TOML comments. The UI should make common settings discoverable and safe while still editing the real Yazelix config contract rather than becoming a second source of truth.
design:
Decision: build a Yazelix config UI, and use Ratatui as the first full-screen implementation layer. Ratatui fits Yazelix better than a GUI, prompt-only flow, or higher-level TUI framework because Yazelix already ships Rust+crossterm, runs inside terminals/Zellij, needs custom tabs/detail panes/validation states, and should stay keyboard-native. Ratatui is a maintained tui-rs successor and provides the right low-level widget/layout base without forcing an application architecture that fights Yazelix.

Rejected alternatives:
- Prompt-only dialoguer/inquire/cliclack: good for onboarding questions, not enough for browsing all settings, showing defaults, comparing sidecars, or editing cursor registries.
- Cursive: simpler view model, but a larger framework boundary and less aligned with the current Rust/crossterm path.
- tui-realm or Elm/React-style TUI frameworks: interesting if app state becomes too complex, but premature for the first slice and adds a second architecture on top of Ratatui.
- Native GUI/web UI: better widgets, worse fit for SSH/terminal-native Yazelix and much larger packaging burden.

Accepted product shape:
- Command surface should be a future yzx config tui/config edit UI path, with direct JSONC editing still supported for power users.
- Source of truth remains ~/.config/yazelix/settings.jsonc plus config_metadata/yazelix_settings.schema.json and main_config_contract.toml.
- The UI must show every schema-known setting, including settings absent from the raw JSONC file. Each field has state: explicit, defaulted/unset, changed, invalid, deprecated/removed, new, Home Manager-owned/read-only.
- Tabs are user-intent tabs, not storage-file tabs: General, Workspace, Editor, Terminal, Appearance, Cursors, Status Bar, File Manager, Keybindings, Shell, Advanced. The schema metadata needs to grow from the current coarse tabs to this UI taxonomy before implementation.
- Save policy: do not wipe comments or reorder the file as the default path. The implementation needs a lossless JSONC patcher for common scalar/list/object edits, preserving comments and surrounding formatting where possible. If a safe patch cannot be produced, the UI must show a preview and require explicit rewrite confirmation.
- Defaulted values solve stale-config discoverability by appearing in the UI even when absent from settings.jsonc. Saving should write changed/touched values by default, with an explicit action to materialize all defaults for a tab or the whole file.
- Home Manager-owned settings are read-only inside the UI by default; the UI can show the relevant option path and suggested Home Manager edit surface.
- Advanced is for raw sidecar/native files and generated previews: open/create/import/validate/view-generated, not arbitrary safe round-tripping of KDL/Lua/native terminal grammars.

Implementation sequencing:
1. Expand schema/UI metadata first.
2. Build a lossless JSONC settings patcher for the first supported value shapes.
3. Build a read-only Ratatui browser/validator.
4. Add writes only after the patcher can preserve comments or force explicit preview-confirmed rewrite.

Source notes checked 2026-05-05: Ratatui official docs describe it as a fast, lightweight Rust terminal UI library; Ratatui docs also state it is the maintained fork/successor path from tui-rs. Existing Yazelix already depends on crossterm in yazelix_core, so the packaging delta is limited to Ratatui and focused helper crates when the implementation bead starts.
acceptance_criteria: Decision defines whether to build a Ratatui or other config UI for the accepted settings.jsonc plus JSON Schema model, which settings are in the first useful slice, how validation/save/preview works, and how it interacts with direct JSONC editing, Home Manager-managed settings, stale sparse settings.jsonc files, old-format migration diagnostics, and raw sidecar files. It must explicitly handle schema-backed discovery of settings absent from settings.jsonc, save policy for defaulted values, comment/reference preservation, first-class Cursors tab behavior, and Advanced sidecar actions. It must not treat yazelix.toml or cursors.toml as long-lived alternate runtime settings sources.
notes:
Config UI direction update 2026-05-05 after format and migration correction:

The config UI should be built against ~/.config/yazelix/settings.jsonc plus JSON Schema metadata. Keys remain snake_case. The UI should show every current semantic setting from the schema whether or not the raw JSONC file contains it. State labels should include set, defaulted/unset, new, deprecated, invalid, and Home Manager-owned/read-only.

Tabs from the design discussion: General, Workspace, Editor, Terminal, Appearance, Cursors, Status Bar, File Manager, Keybindings, Shell, Advanced. Tabs are organized by user intent, not by storage file. Cursors is a first-class tab, not an Advanced/raw sidecar screen.

Advanced should surface raw native sidecar files with status such as absent, active, imported, native fallback, managed override, invalid, or not validated. It should provide open/create/import/validate/view-generated actions where safe. It should not pretend to safely round-trip arbitrary native grammars.

The UI should be taught as the primary discovery/editing path, similar in spirit to VS Code/Zed settings UIs backed by settings files. Raw settings.jsonc editing remains supported for power users and automation. Old yazelix.toml and cursors.toml are migration surfaces only: the UI may show migration success, pending safe auto-migration, or hard-error blockers, but should not treat them as editable alternate runtime settings sources.

Single-file boundary from yazelix-8x3x: move Yazelix-owned structured intent into the canonical settings surface where the UI can validate it, while keeping separate files for raw native grammars, executable code, secrets, large assets, and generated state. Canonical candidates include core UX, editor orchestration, terminal selection/simple prefs, cursor settings and presets, status widgets, common Yazi prefs, semantic keybinding policy, and XDG/native integration mode. Permanent sidecars include zellij.kdl, helix.toml for native/fork config, yazi_keymap.toml, yazi_init.lua, terminal native snippets, shell hooks, shaders/assets/themes, secrets, logs/profiles/generated configs.
close_reason: Decision completed: use Ratatui for the terminal-native config UI, keep settings.jsonc/schema as source of truth, require comment-preserving JSONC patching before writes, define first slice as schema-backed read-only browser, and create implementation follow-ups for metadata, patching, and browser UI.

title: Expand settings schema UI metadata
description: Add the metadata the future config UI needs on top of the current settings.jsonc schema. The current schema has coarse tabs; the accepted config UI needs user-intent groups, labels, descriptions, advanced/read-only markers, default-source links, deprecation/new-field state, and sidecar actions without making the UI infer product semantics from raw section names.
acceptance_criteria: config_metadata/yazelix_settings.schema.json or adjacent generated metadata can drive the planned tabs: General, Workspace, Editor, Terminal, Appearance, Cursors, Status Bar, File Manager, Keybindings, Shell, Advanced. Each schema-known setting has enough metadata for label, help text, grouping, value editor kind, default source, write support, and Home Manager/read-only behavior. Metadata generation or validation keeps it in sync with main_config_contract.toml and cursor defaults.
notes:
Zed lesson link 2026-05-05: docs/zed_architecture_lessons.md says this metadata should be curated by user intent, not raw schema shape. Include labels, help text, JSON path, default source, owner/read-only state, editor kind, search text, and sidecar action metadata so the config UI does not infer product semantics from section names.
Implemented 2026-05-05: added config_metadata/config_ui_metadata.toml as the curated UI metadata layer, kept parser semantics in main_config_contract.toml, aligned yazelix_settings.schema.json tab_order with the planned user-intent tabs, and made yzx config ui load/fail-fast on this metadata instead of inferring tabs from raw path prefixes. Added coverage that metadata fields exactly match visible config UI fields and that tabs/editors stay in the allowed contract.
close_reason: Completed: added curated config UI metadata, schema tab alignment, fail-fast UI metadata loading, and metadata coverage tests.

title: Implement lossless JSONC settings patcher
description: Build the save engine the config UI needs so common settings edits do not destroy comments, formatting, or the deliberate settings.jsonc section order. The config UI should not default to parse-and-pretty-print rewrites because the comments in settings.jsonc are part of the user experience.
acceptance_criteria: A Rust-owned patcher can update supported settings.jsonc paths for scalar values, arrays of strings, and simple objects while preserving unrelated comments and formatting. Unsafe edits return a clear preview/rewrite-required result instead of silently rewriting the whole file. Tests cover preserving comments around edited fields, adding an absent defaulted field in the right section, rejecting unsupported structures, and round-tripping through existing config validation.
notes: Implementation decision: use the existing jsonc-parser crate with its cst feature enabled instead of hand-written byte insertion logic. No new crate is added; packaging impact is limited to compiling the already-present dependency feature. The patcher will build in-house policy around that CST API: dotted settings paths only, supported JSON value shapes only, validate the patched JSONC, and return clear rewrite-required errors for unsupported structures.
close_reason: Completed: added a comment-preserving settings.jsonc CST patcher for supported set/unset operations, wired yzx config set/unset through contract validation, documented the command surface, and covered parser, patcher, command, metadata, and full yazelix_core tests.

title: Implement read-only Ratatui config browser
description: Create the first shippable config UI slice as a read-only Ratatui browser/validator over settings.jsonc, schema metadata, defaults, Home Manager ownership, and advanced sidecar status. This gives users discoverability before introducing writes.
acceptance_criteria: A yzx config UI command opens a Ratatui view that lists every schema-known setting by user-intent tab, shows explicit versus defaulted/unset values, highlights invalid or stale fields, shows the active settings path, and exposes Advanced sidecar status read-only. The first slice must not mutate files. Tests cover field inventory construction, tab grouping, defaulted/unset display state, Home Manager/read-only state, and invalid config diagnostics.
notes:
Zed lesson link 2026-05-05: docs/zed_architecture_lessons.md says the first config browser should include search and visible state for explicit/defaulted/unset/overridden/read-only values. Search candidates should include tab, section, label, help text, and settings.jsonc path.
Rust dependency decision for first config UI slice: use ratatui as the one new production TUI crate, backed by the existing crossterm dependency, because the requested surface is a terminal-native settings browser and hand-rolling layout/widgets would add more private UI code than value. Reuse existing jsonc-parser/serde_json/settings normalization code for parsing and inventory; no new parser or schema crates. Build in-house: schema-to-field inventory, explicit/defaulted/unset state, sidecar/read-only status, diagnostics model, and navigation/search state. Rejected for this slice: mutable writes before the JSONC patcher bead lands, hand-written crossterm-only UI, web/native GUI config editors, and broad TUI frameworks beyond ratatui. Packaging impact: one focused Rust dependency in yazelix_core; no separate binary or user runtime package split.
close_reason: Implemented read-only Ratatui config browser via yzx config ui. The UI builds its field inventory from the main config contract plus cursor schema metadata, shows explicit/defaulted/unset/invalid state, active path ownership/read-only status, stale diagnostics, and managed sidecar status without mutating files. Verification: cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/yazelix_core/Cargo.toml --test yzx_core_config_ui; yzx dev rust fmt --check; yzx dev rust check; noninteractive yzx_control config ui exits with the expected terminal-required error.

title: Survey Ratatui config UI references
description: Research existing Ratatui projects and references that implement or approximate config/settings UIs, then record the patterns Yazelix should reuse for its settings.jsonc config UI and action/keymap direction.
acceptance_criteria: Research notes list relevant Ratatui config/settings UI projects or adjacent references, include source URLs, summarize which patterns matter for Yazelix, and link the findings to the active config UI follow-up beads.
notes:
Research completed 2026-05-05. Sources checked by web search and repo/docs pages: Ratatui official config template and best-practices discussion, olafkfreund/r-hyprconfig, LargeModGames/spotatui, neiii/bridle, erickochen/purple, pythops/bluetui, cubismod/hypr-showkey, and ratatui/awesome-ratatui.

1. r-hyprconfig - closest full analog. Source: https://github.com/olafkfreund/r-hyprconfig and https://libraries.io/cargo/r-hyprconfig. It is a Ratatui Hyprland config editor with logical panels, real-time hyprctl apply, in-place editing with validation, search, save/reload/import/export, profiles, and NixOS/Home Manager export. Useful for Yazelix: user-intent tabs, type-specific editors, live validation, search across settings, explicit save/reload, and declarative export boundary.
