title: Build KGP Zellij from unwrapped nixpkgs package
close_reason: Fixed KGP Zellij packaging to override pkgs.zellij.unwrapped instead of the nixpkgs wrapper package, and disabled package-time Cargo tests for this user runtime dependency. Verified validate-flake-interface, git diff --check, and nix build .#runtime --override-input nixpkgs github:NixOS/nixpkgs/e9a7635a57597d9754eccebdfc7045e6c8600e6b --no-link.

title: Auto-apply safe config migrations at entrypoints while keeping yzx config migrate
description:
Imported GitHub issue #478.

Yazelix should automatically apply deterministic config migrations when the user enters the product through the normal interactive entrypoints, instead of forcing them to manually run `yzx config migrate --apply` for every stale-but-safe case. This should make startup, launch, restart, and interactive `yzx env` feel self-healing for safe rewrites while preserving explicit control and clear diagnostics for ambiguous cases.

Do not remove `yzx config migrate`. It should remain the canonical preview/apply/debug surface for migrations. The change here is to make the common entrypoints invoke the same safe migration engine automatically when that is clearly safe.
design:
Reason from first principles:
- Deterministic rewrites with backups are product-maintenance work, not user work, so Yazelix should usually handle them automatically when the user is trying to launch or enter the environment.
- Read paths must stay read-only. Do not hide file mutations inside `parse_yazelix_config` or other helpers used by widgets, config generation, or general state inspection. Ordinary reads should not rewrite user config.
- Keep the existing split between safe `auto` migrations and `manual_only` migrations. Automatic entrypoint behavior must only apply the `auto` subset and must leave manual-only items explicit.

Scope:
- Add a migration preflight for interactive entrypoints such as `yzx launch`, `yzx restart`, normal Yazelix startup, and interactive `yzx env`.
- If the migration plan has only deterministic auto rewrites, apply them automatically with backups, then continue into the normal entrypoint flow and print a short high-signal notice.
- If the plan has both auto and manual-only items, apply only the auto rewrites, then continue while printing the remaining manual follow-up clearly.
- If the situation is ambiguous or ownership-sensitive, keep failing fast with the current actionable diagnostics instead of guessing.
- In non-interactive or read-only contexts, do not mutate automatically.

Non-goals:
- Do not remove `yzx config migrate`.
- Do not make `parse_yazelix_config` mutate files.
- Do not auto-apply `manual_only` migrations.
- Do not silently resolve duplicate or conflicting ownership cases that still require user intent.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Interactive entrypoints (`yzx launch`, `yzx restart`, normal Yazelix startup, and interactive `yzx env`) run a migration preflight before normal config consumption.
2. When the migration plan contains only `auto` changes, Yazelix applies them automatically with backup and continues into the entrypoint flow without requiring the user to run `yzx config migrate --apply` manually.
3. When the plan contains both `auto` and `manual_only` items, Yazelix applies only the `auto` subset, surfaces the remaining manual items clearly, and still fails fast when those manual-only items require user intent before startup can continue.
4. When the config state is ambiguous or ownership-sensitive, Yazelix still fails fast with clear guidance instead of auto-mutating.
5. Non-interactive/read-only paths such as widget rendering, general config parsing helpers, and other routine readers do not mutate config files.
6. `yzx config migrate` remains available as the explicit preview/apply/debug surface and still reports the same plan semantics.
7. Add focused regression coverage for: auto-apply on at least one interactive entrypoint, mixed auto/manual plans, non-interactive no-mutation behavior, and continued fail-fast handling for ambiguous ownership conflicts.
close_reason: Completed: interactive entrypoints now auto-apply deterministic config migrations with backups, still fail fast on remaining manual-only follow-up, and keep parse/read paths non-mutating.

title: Remove tracked .vscode repo state
description: Remove the tracked .vscode/settings.json Kiro-local preference, ignore .vscode/, and keep editor-local repo settings out of git by default.
close_reason: Resolved earlier: .vscode is ignored and no .vscode files are tracked.

title: Clarify unmanaged store runtime update path
description: When Yazelix is launched from an unmanaged Nix store runtime, nix profile upgrade cannot update it and yzx update upstream should explain that the runtime is neither default-profile-owned nor Home-Manager-owned. Fix the error surface and docs so users do not run nix profile upgrade --refresh yazelix manually.
close_reason: Update command now explains unmanaged store runtimes and README documents the profile-owned recovery flow instead of the brittle nix profile upgrade yazelix selector.

title: Add visual evidence harness for yazelix-screen VFX styles
description: Add a lightweight visual evidence and performance harness for yazelix-screen VFX styles so visual changes can be reviewed without relying on chat screenshots or subjective memory. The immediate driver is Mandelbrot deep zoom, but the harness should also help evaluate future styles such as alien sea slug particles.
design: Keep this small and useful. Prefer a repo-local command, example, or test helper that renders deterministic representative frames at selected sizes/frame indices, can emit plain text and ANSI captures, and reports basic frame timing. Avoid broad snapshot-framework bloat or huge binary fixtures. This should make it easy to compare before/after frames and prove that VFX styles are not blank, not dominated by one glyph, and not too expensive for terminal use.
acceptance_criteria:
- Maintainers can render deterministic evidence frames for at least Mandelbrot at representative terminal sizes
- The harness can preserve enough ANSI output to review color/palette changes and can also emit stripped text for cheap checks
- It records or reports simple frame timing for representative sizes
- It avoids large checked-in artifacts unless they defend a real contract
- The harness is documented in the yazelix-screen repo and is referenced by future VFX quality beads

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: Reference: FrankenTUI comparison section (https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/frankentui#how-frankentui-compares). Useful takeaways for the yazelix-screen visual evidence harness: prioritize deterministic buffer/frame captures, simple diffable outputs, one-writer/flicker discipline, timing evidence, and optional snapshot-style before/after review. Do not treat FrankenTUI as an immediate dependency for yazelix-screen VFX: its own README says the public API is evolving fast, and the current Yazelix need is a small child-repo harness for deterministic frames and performance, not a broad TUI kernel migration.

title: Re-verify Alt y does not re-enable pane frames zellij-wide
description: GitHub issue #484 reports that pressing Alt y makes pane frames appear again across the Zellij session and reintroduces the shaky pane-frame bug. Current code history includes a fix removing the old frame-toggle hook, but this needs live behavioral verification before the issue can be closed.
close_reason: Fixed the Alt+y pane-frame regression; the remaining sidebar-reopen bug is tracked separately.

title: Trim shell-out indirection from yzx status --versions
description: yzx status --versions still shells out to a second nu process and builds a source string just to run version_info.nu. Delete that indirection and call the version-info helper in-process. Keep the visible output the same while making the status path smaller and easier to verify.
close_reason: Completed: yzx status --versions now calls version_info in-process instead of shelling out to a second nu process.

title: Fix leaked __YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR__ placeholder in Zoxide editor launcher
description: Opening a file from the Zoxide editor flow currently fails because the generated command still contains the literal __YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR__ placeholder instead of a real runtime path. The failure shows up as a command like 'nu __YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR__/nushell/scripts/integrations/zoxide_open_in_editor.nu ...', so the editor pane never opens and the runtime contract is broken.
design: Treat this as a runtime-generation and command-templating bug, not a user-local recovery. Identify where the Zoxide editor command is supposed to resolve the runtime path, remove any duplicate placeholder plumbing that can leave __YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR__ unsubstituted, and make the launcher consume one canonical runtime-path source. If substitution is missing, fail fast with a clear runtime-path error instead of emitting a broken command string into the live session.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The Zoxide editor flow never emits a literal __YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR__ path in the launched command. 2. Opening a file from the Zoxide/Yazi editor surface works in a normal managed Yazelix session. 3. The runtime path used by this launcher comes from one explicit canonical source instead of ad hoc placeholder substitution. 4. A focused regression defends the seam that previously emitted the broken command.
notes: Resolved by the current generated-Yazi plugin runtime placeholder rendering path. The shipped plugin template still contains __YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR__ intentionally, but sync_bundled_yazi_assets renders bundled plugin files into the generated Yazi surface using get_yazelix_runtime_dir. Existing regression test test_generate_merged_yazi_config_renders_runtime_placeholders_in_plugins verifies that zoxide-editor.yazi/main.lua contains the real zoxide_open_in_editor.nu path and no literal placeholder. Verification: generated-config canonical runner passed 28/28.
close_reason: Already fixed and verified: generated zoxide-editor plugin renders a real runtime path and no literal placeholder.

title: Fix right sidebar first-open placement from stacked panes
description: When the right agent sidebar is opened for the first time while the active pane is inside a Zellij stack with more than one pane, the new agent pane initially opens in that stack instead of the right-sidebar slot. Pressing the right-sidebar keybinding again fixes placement by routing through the existing-agent path. Investigate and fix the pane-orchestrator creation path so first-open placement lands in the managed right sidebar position.
acceptance_criteria: The pane-orchestrator creates the right sidebar in the correct right-side slot on first open even when the active pane is inside a stack. Existing-agent open/close behavior remains unchanged. Child repo tests cover the placement command sequence or state transition. Main repo consumes the published child revision and validates the packaged runtime without local overrides before push.
notes: Implemented child repo fix in ../yazelix-zellij-pane-orchestrator and published child commit 9df68b4ffb029b104603cac84c56aff09190ce37. Local child checks passed: cargo fmt --all -- --check, cargo test --lib, cargo check, git diff --check, nix build .#yazelix_zellij_pane_orchestrator --no-link. Main flake.lock consumes the published child revision, and the local main integration is committed together with the status-bar runtime update. Main validation passed: nix build .#runtime --no-link, cargo run --quiet --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_maintainer --bin yzx_repo_validator -- validate-workspace-session-contract, nix build .#yazelix_ghostty --no-link. Installed local profile runtime from git+file:///home/lucca/pjs/yazelix#yazelix_ghostty and refreshed the desktop entry for manual testing. Main push and bead close are waiting for maintainer manual test approval.

title: Make boids render as V-shaped dotted creatures
description: Replace the current arrow-like boids glyphs with V-shaped dotted creatures so the welcome animation reads more organic while preserving predator/prey colors and deterministic rendering.
design: Keep the existing in-house boids simulation and scoped renderer. Add an 8-direction visual direction classifier so diagonals have distinct dotted V silhouettes. Use small V-shaped prey glyphs and a distinct larger/denser predator glyph without adding heavy media assets. Update tests to defend diagonal glyph selection and predator/prey visual identity.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Boids no longer render with arrow glyphs. 2. Boid visual direction supports diagonals. 3. Predator-mode prey keep one shared prey color. 4. Predator glyphs remain visually distinct from prey glyphs. 5. Existing frame dimensions and deterministic animation tests still pass.
close_reason: Completed: boids now render as dotted V-shaped sprites with 8-way direction selection, predator sprites use a larger two-row dotted footprint rendered above prey, arrows are removed, and tests defend diagonal visuals, frame dimensions, predator/prey split, and deterministic motion.

title: Align yazelix package meta.platforms with documented macOS support
description:
The current tree exposes darwin package/app outputs in `flake.nix`, and the repo docs describe the first-party Yazelix package/runtime story as working on Linux and macOS. But the shared package builder in `packaging/mk_yazelix_package.nix` still hardcodes `meta.platforms = pkgs.lib.platforms.linux`, so `nix profile add github:luccahuguet/yazelix#yazelix` is refused on darwin even though `packages.aarch64-darwin.yazelix` and `packages.x86_64-darwin.yazelix` exist.

This is not just a missing platform string. The same shared package builder is currently being used for two different surfaces with different scope promises:
- the first-party top-level flake package, which the repo currently presents as Linux + macOS
- the draft nixpkgs submission package under `packaging/nixpkgs/`, whose notes still frame the first submission as Linux-first and intentionally not claiming darwin yet

That shared ownership made the narrower nixpkgs-submission metadata leak into the broader first-party flake contract.
design:
Delete-first recommendation: stop making the shared package builder own platform policy.

Do not solve this by blindly adding darwin to the shared `meta.platforms` line in `packaging/mk_yazelix_package.nix`, because that would silently broaden the draft nixpkgs submission contract at the same time. The slimmer and more honest fix is to delete the shared platform-policy ownership and let each wrapper own its own claim:
- `yazelix_package.nix` should claim the supported first-party flake package systems
- `packaging/nixpkgs/yazelix_package.nix` should keep the narrower submission scope if Linux-first is still the intended upstream boundary

An acceptable implementation shape is:
1. remove or parameterize the hardcoded `meta.platforms` from `packaging/mk_yazelix_package.nix` so it is no longer the canonical policy source
2. set the top-level first-party package platforms in `yazelix_package.nix`, preferably matching the explicit four flake systems already exported today rather than claiming a broader unverified universe
3. set the draft nixpkgs-submission package platforms in `packaging/nixpkgs/yazelix_package.nix` to the intended Linux-only submission scope, if that scope is still current
4. update the live package spec/docs so the distinction is explicit instead of accidental drift
5. add a cheap metadata-only validator that would have caught this exact mismatch without requiring darwin builds

Important constraints:
- do not delete darwin flake outputs or narrow the docs unless there is an explicit product decision to retract first-party macOS support
- do not widen the nixpkgs submission scope by accident just because the first-party flake contract is broader
- prefer one small validator over a large matrix of packaging tests; `nix eval` metadata checks are enough here

Rewrite sequencing: do this before the Rust rewrite. This is package-surface truthfulness and contract cleanup, not a Rust-shaped problem.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The top-level first-party flake package no longer reports as unsupported on the darwin systems it explicitly exports today.
2. The draft nixpkgs submission package keeps its intended Linux-first/Linux-only claimed scope unless a separate product decision broadens it.
3. `docs/specs/nixpkgs_package_contract.md` and `packaging/nixpkgs/submission_notes.md` make the first-party-versus-submission scope split explicit.
4. A cheap maintainer validator or equivalent metadata check fails if a future exported flake package system is not actually available according to `meta.platforms`.
notes:
opus 4.6 review:

The Nix packaging fix is clean and correct. The doc/spec changes are clear. But the validator has a critical bug that makes it a no-op — it would not have caught the original bug it was designed to catch.

CRITICAL BUG — validator passes a list instead of a package to lib.meta.availableOn.

The validate_package_platforms function extracts platforms as a list and passes it to availableOn:

  platforms = pkg.meta.platforms or [];
  lib.meta.availableOn platformEntry platforms;

But availableOn expects a derivation/package as the second argument, not a raw list. When given a list, it checks list ? meta.platforms which evaluates to false (lists have no attributes). The negation !false = true makes availableOn always return true regardless of actual platform content.

Proof from nix eval:

  fakePkg = { meta.platforms = ["x86_64-linux"]; };
  linuxOnlyList = ["x86_64-linux"];
  platformEntry = lib.systems.elaborate { system = "x86_64-darwin"; };

  lib.meta.availableOn platformEntry linuxOnlyList;  # → true  (WRONG: darwin not in list)
  lib.meta.availableOn platformEntry fakePkg;         # → false (CORRECT)

The validator would have given a green checkmark on the original linux-only bug. Fix: pass pkg directly to availableOn instead of extracting the platforms list first:

  check = system:
    let
      pkg = flake.packages.${system}.yazelix;
      platformEntry = lib.systems.elaborate { system = system; };
    in
      lib.meta.availableOn platformEntry pkg;

OTHER ISSUES:

2. EXPECTED_FLAKE_SYSTEMS constant is defined at the top of validate_flake_interface.nu but never referenced. The Nix expression hardcodes the same system list as an inline string. Either use the constant to generate the Nix expression or remove the unused constant.

WHAT IS GOOD:

- The Nix packaging architecture is exactly right: metaPlatforms parameter with null default on the shared builder, first-party wrapper claiming the four exported systems explicitly, nixpkgs wrapper claiming Linux-only. Clean delete-first fix that removes shared ownership of platform policy.
- yazelix_package.nix explicitly lists the four systems rather than using a broad lib.platforms set. This is honest and matches the flake outputs.
- packaging/nixpkgs/yazelix_package.nix has a clear comment explaining why its scope is narrower and pointing to the first-party wrapper.
- The spec additions (acceptance cases 8 and 9, the Platform scope subsection) clearly document the scope split.
- submission_notes.md expansion correctly explains the independence of the two surfaces.
- The validator design is right (metadata-only nix eval, no darwin build requirement, graceful skip on eval failure). Only the availableOn call site is wrong.

VERDICT:

The Nix packaging fix and documentation are correct — ship those. Fix the validator before shipping: pass pkg instead of the extracted platforms list to lib.meta.availableOn, and either use or remove the unused EXPECTED_FLAKE_SYSTEMS constant.
close_reason: Completed: first-party flake package platform metadata was split from the narrower nixpkgs submission scope, the validator was fixed to check package availability correctly, commit 0236b08 landed on main, and GitHub issue #562 is now closed.

title: Add workspace session contract validator
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done

The live Yazelix workspace layer depends on exact built-in layouts, wrapper script paths, tracked plugin artifacts, and the internal yzx_control zellij subcommand seam. Those contracts are currently defended mostly by runtime behavior and scattered tests, which leaves room for silent repo drift.

Add a repo validator that checks the built-in workspace/session assets and the shipped Yazi/Zellij integration references, centralize the internal zellij subcommand list so help text and validators use one source of truth, and add Rust tests for the validator contract.
notes: Implemented the workspace/session contract validator, wired it into yzx_repo_validator and the static maintainer checks, and added Rust coverage for the internal zellij command surface and bundled Yazi entrypoint validation.
close_reason: Added workspace/session contract validator and maintainer gate

title: Extract yazelix_ratconfig into a child repository
description: Publish the reusable ratatui config editor as a first-party child repository after the final boundary pass confirms that generic config editing can be separated cleanly from Yazelix settings and apply behavior.
design: Do not start this until the preparation bead accepts a public boundary. The child repo should own reusable ratatui config editor primitives, model/editor/render tests, and standalone documentation. The main Yazelix repo should keep only adapter code for settings.jsonc, Home Manager/native status, generated refresh, and runtime apply/reload. The extraction should remove meaningful main-repo LOC or explicitly record what deletion remains blocked.
acceptance_criteria: A first-party child repo exists or the extraction is explicitly rejected after the boundary pass. If implemented, Yazelix consumes the child repo through normal package/crate inputs, reusable config editor tests move with the child repo, main-repo adapter tests remain focused, and the LOC scorecard reports before/after impact.
notes: 2026-05-07 boundary outcome from yazelix-ajho: do not extract publicly now. The private model/editor/render boundary is cleaner, but config_ui.rs remains a substantial Yazelix adapter for settings/schema loading, Home Manager ownership, native status classification, JSONC patching, generated refresh, and runtime apply. Reopen or replace this only after the adapter thins enough that extraction removes meaningful main-repo LOC.
close_reason: Rejected for now after boundary pass: keep ratconfig private until the Yazelix adapter is thin enough to extract with real LOC reduction.

title: Rebaseline Rust ownership budget after child repo extractions
description: The manual rust_ownership_budget validator is stale after recent config/apply/ratconfig/pane-orchestrator work and the yazelix_bar extraction. Rebaseline ownership families deliberately, decide whether ceilings should move or stay as warnings, and keep the budget aligned with the LOC/extraction scorecard instead of silently accepting growth.
notes: Decision: rebaseline measured/max ceilings to the current post-yazelix-bar main-repo Rust surface instead of keeping stale failures. The current tracked surface is 75,562 raw Rust lines across 137 files. Existing lower target_loc values and hard_target_loc=60000 remain intact, so the budget is a no-growth ceiling plus shrink warning rather than permission to grow. Assigned action registry and pane-orchestrator client/session parsing to workspace/pane integration; config apply mode, native config status, and private ratconfig files to config UI/materialization; repo_rust_commands to maintainer tooling; pane-orchestrator runtime_config files to the pane plugin family.
close_reason: Completed: rebaselined config_metadata/rust_ownership_budget.toml to the current post-child-repo Rust surface, assigned the previously unowned config/apply/ratconfig/action/workspace/maintainer/pane-orchestrator files to explicit families, kept hard_target_loc at 60000 with lower family target LOCs, and refreshed docs/rust_code_inventory.md. Verified validate-rust-ownership-budget, validate-docs-experience, and git diff --check.

title: Fix sweep config generator drift against the live Ghostty mode-effect contract
description: The non-visual sweep lane is currently red because nushell/scripts/dev/sweep/sweep_config_generator.nu still emits terminal.ghostty_mode_effect = null, while the live config contract rejects that value. This is generator drift, not a product fallback problem.
design: Keep this delete-first and narrow. Do not add parser fallbacks or special-case sweep-only config tolerance. Make the sweep generator emit only values allowed by the live config contract, keep the generated sweep configs minimal, and preserve the purpose of the sweep lane as a real contract check instead of a stale-config compatibility test.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Sweep config generation no longer emits terminal.ghostty_mode_effect = null or any other value rejected by the live config contract. 2. The non-visual sweep lane gets past config parsing for the generated sweep configs again. 3. No parser fallback or validator bypass is added just to tolerate stale sweep config shapes. 4. The retained sweep config still stays minimal and representative of the supported Ghostty surface.
close_reason: Completed

title: Stop exporting source-checkout runtime identity through ambient YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR
description: Delete the source-shell export of YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR from devenv enterShell so repo-loaded code and generated wrappers stop inheriting the installed runtime by accident. Keep explicit runtime env only where cross-process handoff genuinely needs it, and defend the source-checkout launch path against a conflicting installed-runtime env. Split shellHook runtime identity so repo-local shells keep DEVENV_ROOT as the live source surface while internal setup and config-state bookkeeping can still use an explicit runtime root when needed.
close_reason: Completed: repo-local devenv enterShell no longer exports ambient YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR or YAZELIX_DIR; repo shells now keep DEVENV_ROOT as the live checkout identity while internal shellHook setup still passes an explicit runtime root only where needed. Managed Nushell config is defended through IN_YAZELIX_SHELL without requiring a fake runtime-root export.

title: Repair stale GitHub/Beads validator and sync contract
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done:
The GitHub/Beads contract sync path still calls a stale Nushell validator entrypoint that imports a removed module, so the contract cannot be verified or synced cleanly through the maintained flow.

What needs to be done:
Restore the validator entrypoint so it delegates to the current owner, run the sync flow to repair lifecycle drift, and re-verify the contract.
close_reason: Completed

title: Split install, update, and doctor into honest full-vs-core capability tiers
description: Define and implement an honest capability split so a narrower Yazelix mode can say what it does not own. The important part is not branding; it is that install/update/doctor stop implying provisioning or runtime-repair promises when those capabilities are intentionally absent.
design: Use the existing runtime contract and Core-boundary decisions as inputs. Separate workspace/config diagnostics from provisioning/runtime-repair diagnostics, and do the same for install/update surfaces. Avoid fake fallbacks or soft wording that still suggests Yazelix can manage runtime ownership in a narrowed mode. If the right outcome is only a clearer contract plus smaller command behavior, take that path instead of inventing a full product switch.
acceptance_criteria: Install, update, and doctor have an explicit capability split between full Yazelix behavior and a narrowed workspace-only/Core-style mode. The surfaces fail or omit behavior honestly instead of pretending to provision tools or repair runtime ownership. The durable contract is documented clearly enough to guide later Core decisions.
notes: Pipeline stage 3 on the capability-contract branch. Install/update/doctor need an honest full-vs-core split before the repo can claim a backend-free workspace slice without fake fallbacks.
close_reason: Implemented explicit runtime/distribution capability tiers for update and doctor, added regressions, and documented the contract.

title: Refine boids predator visuals and motion
description: The first behavior-backed boids pass added predator, schools, and flow variants, but the current UX needs a tighter visual and motion follow-up. Predator/prey colors should remain distinct, all prey should share one prey color, the creatures should read as directional creatures rather than plain block cells, flow should be removed because it looks weird, and boids motion should feel faster.
design: Refine the boids renderer around the variants that survive actual product taste. Keep predator and prey visually distinct, but do not assign arbitrary per-prey colors in predator mode. Replace or supplement block glyphs with a directional glyph system, preferably dot-built arrows or another terminal-safe direction cue that makes movement legible without relying on heavy assets. Remove boids_flow from public style resolution, docs, config comments, and random boids-family selection. Increase movement speed in a bounded way that still preserves readable flocking and deterministic tests. Use the existing in-house boids engine; no new crates expected.
acceptance_criteria:
1. boids_predator renders predators and prey with different colors, while all prey share the same prey color.
2. Boids render with a cooler directional creature glyph instead of only plain block cells, with tests defending orientation or glyph selection behavior.
3. boids_flow is removed from public screen/welcome style resolution, docs/config comments, and random boids-family selection.
4. Random boids-family selection is rebalanced across the remaining boids variants.
5. Boids movement is visibly faster while keeping deterministic frame output and bounded terminal cost.
6. Tests defend the predator/prey color contract, flow removal, remaining random selection, and faster movement behavior.
close_reason: Completed: restored the 2s welcome default, removed boids_flow from the public style surface, made boids/predator use dot-arrow directional glyphs with shared prey color and faster motion, rebalanced random boids selection across predator/schools, rendered boids welcome as an unframed broad surface, and trimmed inline welcome padding to avoid terminal autowrap artifacts.

title: Explore ideal yazelix-terminal Rust terminal strategy
description:
Evaluate whether Yazelix should eventually own a first-party terminal, tentatively `yazelix-terminal`, by forking a Rust terminal such as Alacritty, WezTerm, Rio, Ratty, or another suitable base.

The goal is not to start the fork yet. The goal is to decide whether a Yazelix-owned terminal would materially improve the stack compared with continuing to target Ghostty and WezTerm as first-class terminals.
design:
First-principles framing:
- The point is to explore the perfect terminal for Yazelix: a super-modern Rust terminal with modern protocols, rich visual features, and a first-party Yazelix feel.
- Yazelix still treats Zellij as the in-session workspace/multiplexer, but that should not flatten the terminal ambition. A future yazelix-terminal can have a standalone mode with tabs/splits/session niceties while keeping a Yazelix mode that subordinates those features to Zellij.
- The terminal should optimize for rendering correctness, input correctness, graphics protocol behavior through Zellij, startup speed, predictable config, modern visuals, agent ergonomics, and tight fit with the Yazelix runtime.
- Rust stack coherence matters because Yazelix already leans on Rust/Zellij/Ratatui-style tooling, and a Rust terminal is easier for the project to inspect, patch, package, theme, and eventually integrate deeply.
- A fork or custom terminal is worth exploring positively, not only defensively. The decision should identify the best dream base, the best mature base, and the feature-lab inspirations before deciding whether Yazelix should build, fork, upstream, or defer.

Modern capability checklist to evaluate:
- GPU renderer quality, frame pacing, high-DPI behavior, transparency/backgrounds, shader/effect hooks, and graphics-stack diagnostics.
- Text shaping and UX quality: ligatures, grapheme clusters, emoji/fallback fonts, bidi/RTL, IME, selection/copy/search, hyperlinks, shell integration, and prompt/command marks.
- Protocol coverage: Kitty graphics, Sixel, iTerm2 images, Kitty keyboard, CSI-u/enhanced keyboard, OSC 8 hyperlinks, OSC 52 clipboard, bracketed paste, focus events, synchronized output, truecolor, light/dark notifications, and cursor-shape sequences.
- Yazelix-specific features: cursor trails/mode effects, Yazi image previews inside Zellij panes, clean generated config, terminal-side notifications, structured state export, safe input-injection boundaries, and agent workflow affordances.

Candidate bases and inspirations to compare:
- Rio: likely best dream-base candidate; Rust, modern, graphics-forward, protocol-rich, active.
- WezTerm: likely best mature-base candidate; Rust, complete protocol surface, proven, but mux/session-heavy.
- Kaku: WezTerm-fork case study for polished AI-coding defaults and trimming.
- Ratty: feature-lab for Rust/Ratatui adjacency, GPU/3D ideas, Kitty graphics, and RGP.
- Alacritty and alacritty_terminal-derived apps such as COSMIC Term/Horizon: small engine/custom-app direction, but protocol gaps must be explicit.
- Ghostty: non-Rust benchmark for polish, native UX, Kitty graphics/keyboard, and cursor effects.
- Frankenterm: agent-control and observability inspiration; not a renderer base.
- Zellij upstream/fork path: still relevant if the real blocker is mux/protocol passthrough rather than terminal rendering.

Hard maintainer constraints:
- Ghostty-style cursor shader/effect parity is a deal breaker. Any serious base must either already expose enough renderer/shader hooks for Yazelix cursor trails and mode effects, or be patchable enough that Yazelix can add them cleanly.
- For any WezTerm-like base, terminal-native mux/session/tab code should be treated as removable or optional in Yazelix mode. Zellij owns the Yazelix workspace; terminal multiplexing must not become a second workspace owner.
acceptance_criteria:
A decision-quality bead update exists that:
- defines the ideal Yazelix terminal capability checklist with must/should/cool categories
- compares Ghostty, WezTerm, Alacritty/alacritty_terminal, Rio, Ratty, Kaku, Frankenterm, and the Zellij upstream/fork path from the standpoint of a perfect modern Yazelix terminal
- separates image/graphics protocol behavior from keyboard/input behavior
- identifies which annoyances are hard blockers, which are product preferences, and which are opportunities for Yazelix-native polish
- ranks at least the best dream base, best mature base, best feature-lab inspiration, and best agent-control inspiration
- recommends one of: keep targeting existing terminals, upstream fixes, fork a terminal, build a new Rust terminal shell around an engine, fork Zellij, or defer
- defines the smallest compelling MVP for a super-modern Rust yazelix-terminal, not merely the smallest safe terminal
