notes:
2026-05-22 Frankenterm evaluation from yazelix-lytcv: do not treat Frankenterm as a promising yazelix-terminal base. It is a large WezTerm fork plus AI-fleet control platform, not a small terminal emulator that cleanly preserves Yazelix's Zellij-as-mux model. Frankenterm remains useful as architecture research for passive observation, structured JSON state, and policy-gated control, but not as a dependency, fork base, or optional terminal lane.

2026-05-30 initial terminal comparison:

Concrete Yazelix terminal requirements:
- Terminal is the display/input host; Zellij remains the multiplexer. A yazelix-terminal MVP should not own tabs, splits, pane management, session restore, or mux semantics.
- Keep launch and config deterministic: generated Yazelix config, explicit terminal argv, visible early failures, and no dependence on ambient user terminal config unless terminal.config_mode=user is explicit.
- Preserve Yazi image previews through Zellij. The current product uses temporary Yazelix Zellij/Yazi forks for Kitty graphics passthrough, so image bugs must be separated into terminal, multiplexer, Yazi/Chafa, and protocol-placement causes.
- Preserve keyboard correctness. Alt+Shift placement keys avoid control-key ambiguity; Ctrl+Shift structural/focus bindings need careful testing with Kitty keyboard protocol. Ghostty supports Kitty keyboard; WezTerm requires config.enable_kitty_keyboard=true; Zellij's support_kitty_keyboard_protocol should not become globally default until the matrix proves it stable.
- Favor Rust for inspectability/patchability, but do not fork just for stack aesthetics. A fork is justified only by a Yazelix-critical terminal-side blocker that cannot be solved through config or upstream patches.

Graphics vs input split:
- Graphics blockers: Kitty graphics through Zellij/Yazi and cursor/placement behavior are the real product risks. Sixel is useful but not a blocker while the Yazi path is Kitty graphics. Ratty Graphics Protocol is terminal-native 3D behavior and is outside the current Zellij integration contract.
- Input blockers: Ctrl+Shift-letter ambiguity and terminal/mux keyboard negotiation are the real product risks. Current keymap already mitigates the common case with Alt+Shift defaults; opt-in Kitty keyboard testing remains required.

Candidate fit:
- Ghostty: best current default for Yazelix UX because it has strong native UX, GPU rendering, Kitty graphics, Kitty keyboard, and Yazelix cursor trail integration. Downsides are Zig rather than Rust and terminal-side tabs/splits that Yazelix mostly does not need. Keep as default unless evidence shows Ghostty itself is the failing layer. Not a fork base.
- WezTerm: best mature Rust terminal target and stable alternate. It is cross-platform, GPU accelerated, supports iTerm2 images, Kitty graphics, Sixel, Lua config, and Kitty keyboard when enabled. Its built-in mux/session/tabs are duplicate surface for Yazelix, but generated config can keep them mostly out of the way. Prefer upstream/config work over a fork unless upstream cannot support a required fix.
- Alacritty: best philosophical fit because it is small, Rust, fast, cross-platform, and intentionally avoids tabs/splits. It is a poor near-term fork base because the inspected official README/features and GitHub code search do not show a current Kitty/Sixel image protocol path comparable to Ghostty/WezTerm/Rio/Ratty. A fork would likely mean building the graphics path first.
- Rio: most interesting Rust graphics-forward fork candidate. It is active, Rust, cross-platform, GPU/WebGPU-oriented, and current repo/docs show Kitty/iTerm2/Sixel image support plus Kitty keyboard code. Risks are maturity, packaging/operational history, and terminal-native splits/window features that duplicate Zellij. Keep as a research candidate, not a default or immediate fork.
- Ratty: interesting Yazelix-adjacent experiment because it is Rust/Ratatui-adjacent and already packaged experimentally on Linux. It is still young, Bevy/wgpu/Vulkan-heavy, Linux-first in Yazelix, and its own RGP 3D protocol does not solve Zellij image passthrough. Keep as experimental lane, not fork base.
- Frankenterm: reject as a terminal base. Current repo describes a terminal hypervisor/control plane for AI agent fleets, requires WezTerm CLI/mux for live pane/session operations, vendors/derives from WezTerm semantics, and explicitly does not ship non-WezTerm mux backend support. Useful ideas: passive observation, structured JSON state, policy-gated control, condition-based waits. Not useful: renderer base, dependency, optional terminal lane.
- Zellij upstream/fork path: highest leverage if the failing behavior is graphics passthrough or keyboard negotiation through the mux. Since Yazelix already pins temporary Zellij/Yazi forks for Kitty graphics, first isolate and upstream/fix that bridge before considering a terminal fork.

Recommendation:
Defer yazelix-terminal. Keep Ghostty as default, WezTerm as stable packaged alternate, and Ratty as experimental Linux lane. Next work should be a small reproducible matrix for Ghostty/WezTerm/Rio/Ratty under Zellij that separates Kitty graphics placement, image preview behavior, Sixel, and Kitty keyboard. Revisit terminal forking only if a bug is terminal-side, Yazelix-critical, and rejected or structurally impossible upstream.

Smallest possible yazelix-terminal MVP if reopened:
Rust, Linux-first, no mux/tab/session features, launch exactly one child command, deterministic generated config, Kitty keyboard support, Kitty graphics enough for Yazi-through-Zellij validation, clear failure diagnostics, and Ghostty-style cursor shader parity as a v1 requirement.

Primary sources checked:
- Ghostty README and feature docs: https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty and https://ghostty.org/docs/features
- WezTerm README and feature/keyboard/image docs: https://github.com/wezterm/wezterm, https://wezterm.org/features.html, https://wezterm.org/imgcat.html, https://wezterm.org/config/lua/config/enable_kitty_keyboard.html
- Alacritty README/features/code search: https://github.com/alacritty/alacritty
- Rio README/site/source tree: https://github.com/raphamorim/rio and https://rioterm.com
- Ratty README/protocol/config: https://github.com/orhun/ratty
- Frankenterm README/repo metadata: https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/frankenterm
- Zellij README/local upstream clone: https://github.com/zellij-org/zellij and /home/lucca/pjs/open_source/yazelix_related/zellij

2026-05-30 correction after maintainer feedback:
The previous recommendation was too conservative and missed the point of the bead. This is not only a risk gate about whether to fork now. It is an exploration of the ideal Yazelix terminal: a super-modern Rust terminal that makes Yazelix feel first-party, polished, protocol-complete, and fun.

Correct north-star question:
What would the perfect terminal for Yazelix look like if Yazelix owned the full outer terminal experience while still letting Zellij own the in-session workspace/mux model?

North-star requirements:
- Rust-first implementation with a renderer/protocol codebase Yazelix can realistically inspect, patch, package, and eventually theme deeply.
- Modern GPU rendering, preferably wgpu/Vulkan/Metal/DX12-style portability, excellent frame pacing, high-DPI correctness, and explicit diagnostics when the graphics stack is not available.
- Modern text shaping: ligatures, grapheme clusters, emoji/fallback fonts, bidi/RTL where feasible, IME correctness, and stable copy/selection semantics.
- Modern protocols: Kitty graphics, Sixel, iTerm2 inline images where practical, Kitty keyboard protocol, CSI-u or equivalent enhanced key encoding, OSC 8 hyperlinks, OSC 52 clipboard, bracketed paste, focus events, synchronized output, truecolor, shell integration hooks, working-directory reporting, command-status/prompt marks, light/dark theme notifications, and cursor-shape sequences.
- Yazelix-native visual identity: cursor trails/shaders, mode-change effects, coherent themes, transparent/background options, image previews that behave inside Zellij panes, and maybe optional Ratty-style 3D/inline object experiments.
- Yazelix-native agent ergonomics: terminal-side notifications, pane/session observation hooks, structured state export, safe input injection boundaries, and policy-gated control ideas borrowed from Frankenterm, without letting the terminal become the Zellij workspace manager.
- Two product modes: Yazelix mode hides or subordinates terminal tabs/splits/session features because Zellij owns the workspace; standalone mode can still expose terminal-native tabs/splits if the base already has them.

Candidate ranking for the aspirational terminal:
1. Rio is the most interesting modern Rust terminal base for the dream. It is Rust, graphics-forward, cross-platform, active, has Kitty/iTerm2/Sixel image support in the source/docs, includes Kitty keyboard code, and is less weighed down by a mature built-in mux than WezTerm. If the goal is a stylish, modern Yazelix terminal rather than a conservative production alternate, Rio deserves the deepest next look.
2. WezTerm is the strongest mature Rust protocol base. It already has GPU rendering, Kitty graphics, iTerm2 images, Sixel, Lua customization, Kitty keyboard support when enabled, and a large production track record. Its downside is that it is also a full mux/session platform. A Yazelix fork from WezTerm would be powerful but would need deliberate product trimming or a strict Yazelix mode. Kaku shows that a WezTerm fork can become a polished AI-coding terminal with sharper defaults, so this path is more credible than the earlier note made it sound.
3. Ratty is the coolest experimental inspiration, not the safest base. Rust/Ratatui adjacency, Bevy/wgpu presentation, Kitty image support, and Ratty Graphics Protocol make it valuable for visual ambition. It is a source of ideas for VFX, inline objects, and playful terminal graphics. It is probably too young to be the main terminal base yet.
4. Alacritty is still the cleanest small Rust terminal philosophy, but it is too protocol-thin for the perfect Yazelix terminal unless Yazelix wants to build modern graphics/input protocols itself. The better Alacritty-related path may be using alacritty_terminal as an engine inside a custom app, as COSMIC Term and Horizon demonstrate, rather than forking stock Alacritty directly.
5. Horizon is not a direct Yazelix fit because it moves toward an infinite terminal canvas, which overlaps Zellij/Yazelix workspace ownership. It is useful as modern product inspiration: Rust, GPU, agent panels, session persistence, interactive file/URL affordances, and a rich terminal surface built around AI coding.
6. Warp is a product inspiration, not a fork base. Its Rust agentic terminal direction proves the market expectation has moved beyond plain VT emulation, but Yazelix should not import Warp's product model wholesale because Yazelix already has Zellij/Yazi/agent panes as its workspace substrate.
7. Frankenterm should be reconsidered as architecture inspiration, not dismissed as irrelevant. It is not a renderer/base terminal for Yazelix, but its structured JSON robot mode, passive pane capture, condition-based waits, policy-gated send, and fleet/agent observability are exactly the kind of modern terminal-adjacent capability Yazelix may want around agent workflows.

Corrected recommendation:
Do not end the exploration at "defer." The real next step is a positive design comparison between Rio and WezTerm as the two serious bases for a beautiful Rust yazelix-terminal, with Ratty, Horizon, Kaku, Warp, COSMIC Term, Ghostty, and Frankenterm used as inspiration sources for specific capabilities. The current hypothesis is: Rio is the better dream-base; WezTerm is the better mature-base; Ratty is the feature-lab; Frankenterm is the agent-control idea mine.

Next evidence to gather before closing again:
- Build a feature matrix scored for the ideal Yazelix terminal, not only current Yazelix launch support.
- Inspect Rio's actual Kitty graphics, Sixel, iTerm2 image, Kitty keyboard, renderer, config, and platform code closely.
- Inspect WezTerm's forkability: renderer/protocol boundaries, mux separability, default config trimming, and cost of a Yazelix mode.
- Inspect Kaku as a WezTerm-fork case study: what it trimmed, what it kept, and whether its AI-coding defaults map to Yazelix.
- Inspect Ratty's terminal conformance and protocol surface to decide whether any RGP/VFX ideas belong in yazelix-terminal.
- Define a real "perfect terminal" capability checklist with must/should/cool categories before making a final fork/base recommendation.

2026-05-30 maintainer requirement: Ghostty cursor shader parity is a deal breaker for any serious yazelix-terminal path. The future terminal must support the same class of cursor shaders/effects Yazelix currently gets through Ghostty/yazelix-ghostty-cursors, or have a credible renderer hook that lets Yazelix implement them cleanly. Treat this as a hard product requirement, not decorative polish.

This changes candidate evaluation:
- Renderer hackability and shader/effect injection are must-have criteria alongside Kitty graphics, Sixel, and keyboard protocols.
- A base with a modern GPU pipeline but no practical cursor/effect hook is incomplete for Yazelix, even if the VT/protocol layer is strong.
- Ghostty remains the visual benchmark because cursor trails/mode effects are part of the Yazelix identity.
- Rio should be inspected specifically for where cursor rendering, shader passes, and animation hooks live.
- WezTerm should be inspected specifically for whether cursor trail/shader parity can be added without fighting its renderer abstractions.

Maintainer direction on mux scope: if a WezTerm-like base is chosen, Yazelix should be willing to throw away, compile out, or permanently hide terminal-native multiplexing code for Yazelix mode. Zellij owns the in-session workspace. Terminal-native mux/session/tabs are useful only as optional standalone-terminal behavior, not as a dependency of the Yazelix runtime.

2026-05-30 Rio vs WezTerm comparison pass 1:
Local reference clones added under /home/lucca/pjs/open_source/yazelix_related/: rio at 6a11aa3 and wezterm at 577474d.

High-level result:
- Rio is currently ahead as the dream Yazelix-terminal base because the source already has the exact kind of cursor/effects seam Yazelix cares about: config-level effects.custom-mouse-cursor and effects.trail-cursor, a front-end trail cursor implementation, per-frame cursor destination/animation/draw calls, and Sugarloaf wgpu/librashader filter support. This does not prove Ghostty shader parity yet, but it changes the risk from "invent cursor effects from scratch" to "generalize/replace an existing cursor effect path."
- WezTerm is still ahead as the mature protocol/terminal-engine base. Its docs and term crate explicitly cover multiplexing, tabs/panes/windows, SSH/serial domains, iTerm2 images, Kitty graphics, Sixel, OSC 8 hyperlinks, keyboard/mouse encoding, and a broad cell model. The tradeoff is that mux is not incidental: wezterm-gui builds a Mux at startup, registers clients, sets active workspace/default domain, and renders PositionedPane/Tab/Window structures throughout the GUI.

Cursor/effect finding:
- Rio: frontends/rioterm/src/screen/mod.rs:3473-3520 draws custom mouse cursor and trail cursor each frame; rio-backend/src/config/effects.rs exposes custom-mouse-cursor and trail-cursor; rio-backend/src/config/renderer.rs exposes wgpu-only renderer.filters; sugarloaf/src/sugarloaf.rs:485-503 installs librashader filters. This is the strongest current evidence for Yazelix cursor shader feasibility.
- WezTerm: wezterm-gui/src/termwindow/render/screen_line.rs:306-421 renders cursor as a line-quad/cache sprite path via glyph_cache.cursor_sprite, with cursor blink/easing and color controls elsewhere; wezterm-gui/src/termwindow/prevcursor.rs tracks cursor movement time but does not implement a trail/effect path. Cursor shader parity looks possible only as a renderer patch, not as an existing product seam.

Mux removal finding:
- Rio: has panels/splits/navigation and per-panel rendering, but no separate mux crate in the inspected tree. It looks like a terminal app with splits, not a mux/session platform. A Yazelix mode could plausibly disable/hide tabs/splits/navigation and keep one child command per window.
- WezTerm: mux is central. docs/features.md lists local/remote multiplex panes/tabs/windows, local multiplexer server, remote SSH/TLS mux, tabs, panes, and SSH client. wezterm-gui/src/main.rs:676-718 builds the initial mux even for local GUI startup. The mux crate owns Window, Tab, Pane, domains, local panes, tmux/ssh/serial integration, and renderable state. Throwing it away means a serious fork or a custom GUI around wezterm-term, not a small config trim.

Protocol finding:
- WezTerm remains more proven and documented on breadth: docs/features.md names Kitty graphics, Sixel, iTerm2 images, multiplexing, SSH/serial, hyperlinks, searchable scrollback, rich text attributes, and hot-reload Lua config; term/README.md describes the terminal engine as full-featured with escape parsing, keyboard/mouse input encoding, scrollback, Sixel/iTerm2 image support, OSC 8, and rich attributes; enable_kitty_keyboard is available but default false.
- Rio has the right modern pieces in source: Kitty graphics protocol, Kitty virtual placements, Sixel, iTerm2 image protocol, Kitty keyboard bindings, GPU image overlay handling, and a Sugarloaf renderer. It needs deeper conformance/runtime testing before calling it as mature as WezTerm.

Current ranking for the specific Rio vs WezTerm choice:
1. Best dream base: Rio. Better fit for Ghostty-style cursor effects and lower mux-removal burden.
2. Best mature base: WezTerm. Better protocol/documentation/production maturity, but cursor parity and mux deletion are larger fork work.
3. Most plausible WezTerm-derived alternative: do not fork all of WezTerm as-is; either create a hard Yazelix mode that bypasses mux surfaces as much as possible, or build a new GUI around wezterm-term/termwiz if the engine is the part we actually want.

Next checks before final recommendation:
- Compare Ghostty/yazelix-ghostty-cursors shader model against Rio's trail_cursor plus Sugarloaf filter pipeline and identify the minimal patch for exact effect parity.
- Run or build Rio locally enough to confirm renderer.filters, trail-cursor, Kitty graphics, and Kitty keyboard behavior under Zellij/Yazi.
- Inspect whether WezTerm's cursor quad/sprite path can support multi-frame cursor trails without destroying line-quad cache behavior.
- Decide whether a WezTerm path means fork WezTerm, fork wezterm-term/termwiz only, or reject because mux removal is too large for Yazelix's Zellij-owned workspace model.

2026-05-30 protocol comparison pass 2:

Official modern protocol baseline:
- Kitty now extends beyond the older graphics/keyboard pair: terminal graphics protocol, comprehensive keyboard handling, text sizing, drag-and-drop, multiple cursors, file transfer over the TTY, desktop notifications, mouse pointer shapes, richer clipboard, color control, arbitrary-region styling, and miscellaneous terminal-state controls. Source: https://sw.kovidgoyal.net/kitty/protocol-extensions/
- Kitty multiple cursors are real and were added in Kitty 0.43.0. The protocol lets terminal programs request real terminal-rendered extra cursors at specific cells/rectangles, rather than faking extra cursors with Unicode glyphs. Source: https://sw.kovidgoyal.net/kitty/multiple-cursors-protocol/
- Kitty text sizing is also real and newer than the classic fixed-cell model. It was added in Kitty 0.40.0 and uses OSC 66 to render text at larger/smaller sizes while preserving grid compatibility. Source: https://sw.kovidgoyal.net/kitty/text-sizing-protocol/
- Kitty drag-and-drop was added in Kitty 0.47.0 and is still marked under development. File transfer over TTY and OSC 99 desktop notifications are separate modern protocols, but they are less central to the first Yazelix-terminal MVP than graphics, keyboard, shell semantics, and cursor effects.

Rio implementation evidence from local clone /home/lucca/pjs/open_source/yazelix_related/rio at 6a11aa3:
- Strong core graphics/input set: Kitty graphics protocol including virtual placements/placeholders, Sixel parser/tests, iTerm2 OSC 1337 images, Kitty keyboard bindings, focus tracking, SGR mouse, bracketed paste, synchronized output DECSET 2026, OSC 8 hyperlinks, OSC 52 clipboard, OSC 9/777 notifications/progress, OSC 22 mouse pointer shapes, XTGETTCAP capability answers, and a Rio Glyph Protocol APC 25a1 path.
- No source evidence found for Kitty multiple cursors, OSC 66 text sizing, OSC 72 Kitty drag-and-drop protocol, OSC 99 Kitty desktop notifications, or Kitty file transfer over TTY.
- The Rio Glyph Protocol path looks interesting but should not be treated as equivalent to Kitty text sizing until verified; it appears to be a Rio-specific APC/glyph-registration protocol in the inspected source.

WezTerm implementation evidence from local clone /home/lucca/pjs/open_source/yazelix_related/wezterm at 577474d:
- Strong mature protocol set: Kitty graphics, Sixel, iTerm2 images, Kitty keyboard protocol behind enable_kitty_keyboard=false by default, CSI-u/fixterms docs, OSC 8 hyperlinks, OSC 52 clipboard, synchronized rendering, XTGETTCAP/DA/XTVERSION behavior, focus tracking, bracketed paste, SGR mouse, OSC 133 semantic prompt zones, OSC 1337 user vars/shell integration, and OSC 777/9 notifications.
- No source evidence found for Kitty multiple cursors, OSC 66 text sizing, OSC 72 Kitty drag-and-drop protocol, OSC 99 Kitty desktop notifications, or Kitty file transfer over TTY.

Protocol verdict:
- WezTerm wins protocol completeness today. It is better documented, broader in shell semantics, and more battle-tested as a terminal engine.
- Rio covers the protocols most critical to current Yazelix runtime behavior: Kitty graphics for Yazi-through-Zellij, Kitty keyboard, Sixel/iTerm2 images, OSC 8, OSC 52, focus/bracketed/SGR mouse, and synchronized updates. Its gap is less core graphics and more newer Kitty-era extras plus shell semantic polish.
- Multiple cursors are a real modern Kitty protocol, but neither Rio nor WezTerm currently appears to implement it. For Yazelix, treat it as a strong should/cool feature tied to Helix/editor polish, not as the first blocker unless Helix/Zellij/Yazi can actually emit/use it in the target stack.
- If Yazelix wants the best mature protocol matrix immediately, WezTerm wins. If Yazelix wants the better base for adding modern visual protocols plus Ghostty-style cursor shader parity, Rio remains the stronger dream-base candidate.

2026-05-30 working pick: choose Rio as the preferred yazelix-terminal base.

Decision:
- Pick Rio, not WezTerm, for the first serious yazelix-terminal exploration.
- Keep WezTerm as the maturity/protocol benchmark and as a possible engine reference, but do not make it the preferred fork base unless Rio fails the cursor-shader or runtime-conformance proof.

Reasoning:
- The goal is not only to get the most protocol-complete Rust terminal today. The goal is to get one base, bring it to Ghostty cursor shader parity, remove or hide duplicate terminal-mux behavior, then fill the missing protocols.
- Rio has the better shape for that goal: it already has cursor/effect seams, per-frame cursor rendering, trail/custom cursor behavior, wgpu/Sugarloaf renderer ownership, and librashader/filter hooks.
- Rio has enough of the core protocol layer to be a credible starting point: Kitty graphics, Kitty keyboard, Sixel, iTerm2 images, OSC 8, OSC 52, focus events, bracketed paste, SGR mouse, synchronized output, notifications/progress, pointer-shape hooks, and XTGETTCAP.
- Rio is less structurally tied to terminal-native multiplexing than WezTerm. Its tabs/splits/panels look more like app features that can be hidden or trimmed for Yazelix mode. WezTerm's mux is a central architecture boundary, so deleting it means a much larger fork or a new GUI around wezterm-term/termwiz.
- WezTerm is still the stronger mature terminal/protocol codebase. That makes it a conformance target, not the preferred product base for a first-party Yazelix terminal.

Selection criteria beyond protocols:
- Cursor shader parity risk: highest priority. Rio currently looks lower risk than WezTerm because cursor/effect seams already exist.
- Mux removal risk: Rio lower risk; WezTerm high risk because mux is central to GUI startup, pane/window/tab state, remote domains, and renderable state.
- Protocol debt: WezTerm lower risk; Rio has debt, but the debt is additive and easier to sequence than mux surgery.
- Renderer ownership: Rio better fit because Yazelix wants visual identity, shader effects, and first-party polish.
- Runtime fit: Rio better fit for one-window/one-child Yazelix mode with Zellij owning the workspace.
- Conformance maturity: WezTerm better; use it as comparison oracle for escape/protocol behavior where possible.
- Product direction: Rio better match for a modern Rust terminal that can feel first-party rather than a powerful terminal-mux platform with Yazelix layered over it.

Roadmap priority:
1. Prove Ghostty cursor shader parity in Rio. If this fails, Rio loses its main advantage.
2. Define and implement Yazelix mode: one child command, generated config, no terminal-native workspace ownership, and hidden/disabled Rio tabs/splits/navigation unless explicitly running standalone.
3. Build a conformance matrix against Ghostty, Kitty, and WezTerm for graphics, keyboard, shell semantics, clipboard, focus/mouse, and synchronized output.
4. Fill missing must protocols before chasing newer Kitty frontier work.
5. Add should protocols once the runtime path is stable.
6. Add cool/frontier protocols only when they create visible Yazelix value or upstream Helix/Zellij/Yazi can use them.

Must-have protocol/features for the fork:
- Ghostty-style cursor shader/trail/mode-effect parity.
- Kitty graphics, including Unicode placeholders/virtual placements, validated through Zellij/Yazi.
- Kitty keyboard and CSI-u/enhanced keyboard correctness through Zellij.
- Sixel and iTerm2 image support.
- OSC 8 hyperlinks.
- OSC 52 clipboard.
- Bracketed paste, focus events, and SGR mouse.
- Synchronized output / DECSET 2026.
- XTGETTCAP, DA, and XTVERSION-style capability reporting.
- OSC 7 working-directory reporting and OSC 133 semantic prompt zones.
- Clear terminal identity/terminfo story for Yazelix mode.

Should-have protocols/features:
- OSC 1337 user vars for richer shell/agent state.
- A coherent notification policy for OSC 9, OSC 777, and Kitty OSC 99 rather than blindly passing everything to the desktop.
- OSC 22 mouse pointer shapes.
- Light/dark/theme notifications if useful for Yazelix theme synchronization.
- Structured terminal state export for agent workflows.
- Policy-gated input injection and passive observation ideas inspired by Frankenterm, without importing Frankenterm's WezTerm/mux architecture.
- Explicit protocol passthrough testing with Zellij, Helix, Yazi, Chafa, and image-preview workloads.

Cool / modern / frontier protocols:
- Kitty multiple cursors, especially if Helix or other editor workflows can emit it.
- Kitty text sizing / OSC 66 for polished terminal UI affordances.
- Kitty drag-and-drop / OSC 72.
- Kitty file transfer over TTY.
- Arbitrary-region styling.
- Rich inline objects or Ratty-style graphics experiments, only after the core terminal is stable.

Current decision status:
- Working pick: Rio.
- Fallback if Rio fails cursor-shader parity or runtime conformance: reconsider WezTerm-derived engine work, likely around wezterm-term/termwiz rather than a full WezTerm fork.
- Do not close the decision bead until Rio's cursor parity feasibility and basic runtime/protocol proof are checked directly.

2026-05-30 Rio vs Ghostty comparison and source-absorption strategy:

Reference clone:
- Ghostty cloned read-only under /home/lucca/pjs/open_source/yazelix_related/ghostty at c4eba3da3.

Comparison result:
- Ghostty beats Rio as a terminal today. Its own docs/source show a mature native terminal with Metal/OpenGL rendering, native macOS/GTK platform surfaces, tabs/splits/windows, libghostty, a strong xterm/conformance posture, Kitty graphics/keyboard, synchronized rendering, light/dark notifications, OSC 52, OSC 8, OSC 133, OSC 66 Kitty text sizing, OSC 9/777 notifications, strong parser/fuzz/test assets, and a mature custom ShaderToy-style shader path.
- Rio still remains the preferred yazelix-terminal base if Rust ownership is a real requirement. The reason is not current terminal quality; the reason is project shape. Rio is Rust, closer to Yazelix's stack, less native-platform/mux-shaped, and already has cursor/effect seams plus a wgpu/Sugarloaf renderer that looks patchable toward Ghostty-style shader parity.
- If the Rust requirement is dropped, Ghostty/libghostty becomes a much more serious base or embedded-engine candidate. It already solves many things Yazelix wants. But that would make yazelix-terminal a Zig/C/Swift/GTK-adjacent project rather than a Rust terminal project, and it would change maintainer ergonomics and packaging ownership substantially.

Ghostty advantages over Rio:
- Mature terminal behavior and conformance discipline. README states Ghostty has done a comprehensive xterm audit and positions standards/xterm/origin-terminal behavior as the compatibility rule.
- Custom shader model is already much richer than Rio's current visible cursor/effect path. Ghostty's `custom-shader` uses ShaderToy-style shaders, supports multiple post-processing passes, compiles GLSL to SPIR-V and then GLSL/MSL, and exposes cursor-aware uniforms: current/previous cursor rectangle, current/previous cursor color, cursor style, cursor visibility, cursor change time, focus time, palette, foreground/background/cursor/selection colors.
- Protocol breadth is stronger. Source shows OSC 66 Kitty text sizing parser/tests, OSC 9 and OSC 777 desktop notifications, OSC 133 semantic prompts, OSC 7/report pwd, OSC 52, OSC 8, mouse shape, Kitty graphics, Kitty keyboard, synchronized output, and capability/version behavior.
- Native UX is a product moat: platform-native macOS/GTK surfaces, platform windowing, AppleScript/AppIntents on macOS, secure keyboard entry, Quick Terminal, themes, ligatures, grapheme clustering, and robust font/platform integration.
- libghostty is a real boundary: it creates an embeddable terminal-engine option that Rio does not currently offer in the same mature form.

Rio advantages over Ghostty:
- Rust and Yazelix stack fit. Forking Rio keeps the main terminal project closer to Yazelix/Zellij/Ratatui/Rust maintainership and easier for existing agents to patch coherently.
- Lower identity mismatch for a Rust yazelix-terminal. Ghostty would make the best terminal today, but it would not make the best Rust-first Yazelix-owned terminal unless the Rust requirement is relaxed.
- Renderer patchability for Yazelix effects still looks credible: Rio already has trail cursor/custom cursor concepts and Sugarloaf/wgpu/librashader hooks, so the first job is to generalize this into Ghostty-compatible custom shader semantics.
- Less terminal-workspace ownership to fight than WezTerm. Rio's tabs/splits/panels look more removable/hidable than WezTerm's mux, and Ghostty's platform-native tabs/splits are excellent but still not the Yazelix workspace owner.

Updated working decision:
- Keep Rio as the fork/base pick while Rust-first remains a hard product constraint.
- Treat Ghostty as the primary behavioral and visual benchmark. Rio should not merely have cursor trails; it should aim for Ghostty custom-shader compatibility where practical so existing Ghostty/Yazelix cursor shaders can run with minimal changes.
- If Rio cannot reach Ghostty shader parity without invasive renderer rewrites, reopen the base decision and seriously evaluate using libghostty or a Ghostty-derived path despite the non-Rust stack.

Source absorption strategy:
- Use Ghostty, WezTerm, and Kitty as read-only source oracles, but do not turn this into blind copying.
- For permissive projects such as Ghostty and WezTerm, direct code study and selective porting can be legally and technically viable if license notices/attribution are preserved and the code is actually understood.
- For Kitty, be much more careful because the project is GPL-3.0. Use Kitty's official protocol specifications and black-box behavior as the main source; do not copy implementation code into a non-GPL Yazelix terminal unless the license decision is explicit.
- AI agents are useful here if each feature has a narrow brief: read source, identify behavior, extract tests/contracts, map data structures, then implement the Rust/Rio version with reviewable diffs. The expected artifact should be a behavior contract and tests first, then implementation.
- The safest pattern is: source reading -> protocol/behavior summary -> conformance fixtures -> independent Rust implementation -> cross-terminal comparison against Ghostty/Kitty/WezTerm -> attribution/license audit.

First absorption targets from Ghostty for Rio:
1. Custom shader compatibility: `custom-shader`, `custom-shader-animation`, ShaderToy-style post-process passes, cursor-aware uniforms, focus/time/palette/color uniforms, and cursor movement timing.
2. OSC 66 Kitty text sizing parser and rendering behavior.
3. OSC 133 semantic prompt handling and selection/navigation behavior around prompt/output zones.
4. Clipboard/security policy around OSC 52 and Kitty clipboard-style behaviors.
5. Parser/fuzz corpus strategy for OSC/CSI/APC robustness.
6. Terminal identity/capability reporting discipline from Ghostty's xterm/origin-compatibility posture.

Practical conclusion:
- Ghostty is the benchmark and feature donor.
- Rio is the Rust base.
- The success condition for Rio is not "better than Ghostty today". It is "can absorb Ghostty-grade shader/protocol behavior fast enough while staying Rust-first and Yazelix-shaped." If yes, Rio is the right path. If no, Ghostty/libghostty becomes the next serious contender.
close_reason: Decision completed: Rio was selected as the Rust-first yazelix-terminal base, with Ghostty as the behavioral benchmark and fallback criteria recorded; subsequent child-repo shader/runtime work validated the decision.

title: Collapse config_parser.nu generic wrapper duplication into one transport seam
description: config_parser.nu exports five near-identical generic wrappers (run_yzx_core_json_command, run_yzx_core_request_json_command, run_yzx_core_command, run_yzx_core_json_command_with_error_surface, run_yzx_core_command_with_error_surface) that differ only in whether they parse JSON, accept a request record, or surface errors. Consolidate these into 2-3 generic exports so the bridge foundation stops carrying repetitive transport boilerplate.
close_reason: Consolidated 5 near-identical yzx_core transport wrappers into 3 canonical exports: run_yzx_core_json_command and run_yzx_core_command now own their full bodies directly, deleting the two _with_error_surface intermediate exports. Also fixed test_yzx_popup_commands.nu fixture to include missing build_record_yzx_core_error_surface and run_yzx_core_request_json_command stubs. Lines reduced from 373 to 356 (-17). All targeted tests pass.

title: Narrow terminal launcher ownership to transport plus canonical launch metadata
description: Shrink nushell/scripts/utils/terminal_launcher.nu so it stops owning stale terminal metadata, dead exports, or duplicated command-shape policy that could live in a smaller canonical data surface, while keeping real shell/process launch behavior visible and explicit.
acceptance_criteria: terminal_launcher.nu keeps only the shell/process boundary and the live launch contract, stale exports or duplicated metadata owners are deleted or demoted, config_mode=user still never takes over external user configs, and desktop/new-window failure feedback stays explicit.
notes: Deletion class: bridge_collapse. Created from yazelix-rdn7.5.5 after yazelix-p18h. Stop if the work would just hide the same shell command assembly behind a fake Rust wrapper instead of deleting real Nu owner logic.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Define the retained terminal-launch contract and metadata deletion budget
description: Name the true shell/process behaviors terminal_launcher.nu must keep, identify which metadata and command-shape tables are live versus stale, and decide whether the surviving non-transport data should become a static manifest or another canonical owner.
acceptance_criteria: The surviving transport boundary is explicit, dead exports and stale metadata are named, the candidate data owner is named, and the deletion budget states exactly which terminal_launcher.nu responsibilities must disappear before the lane counts as success.
notes:
Deletion class: bridge_collapse. Preserve detached-launch visibility, nixGL selection, terminal config_mode policy, and platform-specific launch behavior. Do not rely on local-only host fixes and do not take ownership of user-managed terminal configs outside Yazelix-owned paths.
Defined the retained terminal-launch contract and zlt2.2 deletion budget: keep shell/process launch transport, config_mode=user fail-fast non-takeover, Ghostty platform flags, and detached-launch visibility; delete duplicate terminal discovery, stale exported nixGL context, one-caller display wrapper, and TERMINAL_METADATA ownership inside terminal_launcher.nu.
close_reason: Completed

title: Delete stale terminal-launch metadata owners without fake Rust launch wrappers
description: After the launch contract lands, delete or demote stale terminal-launch metadata and dead wrapper ownership so terminal_launcher.nu is mostly transport and explicit launch behavior.
acceptance_criteria: terminal_launcher.nu is materially smaller, dead exports or duplicated tables are gone, the surviving launch transport still passes the current launch and detached-probe regressions, and config_mode=user remains an explicit non-takeover boundary.
notes:
Deletion class: bridge_collapse. Success is measured by deleting Nu owner logic, not by wrapping the same shell command matrix inside Rust while Nushell still owns the real behavior.
Completed terminal-launch metadata owner deletion: terminal_launcher.nu no longer imports TERMINAL_METADATA, no longer exports duplicate terminal detection helpers, no longer exposes nixGL launch-context resolution, and keeps only retained launch-boundary helpers. launch_yazelix.nu now derives the display label from resolved terminal info at the caller seam without introducing fake Rust launch wrappers. Verified with validate_syntax, test_yzx_generated_configs.nu, targeted detached-launch maintainer checks, validate_specs, and validate_installed_runtime_contract.
close_reason: Completed: deleted stale terminal-launch metadata owners and validated retained launch contract.

title: Keep repo workspace root when Yazi opens files into Helix
description:
Yazi-to-Helix file opens can sometimes retarget the Yazelix workspace to the opened file's containing folder instead of preserving the current repo/workspace root. This breaks project-scoped status, naming, and follow-up commands after opening files from nested folders.

Acceptance criteria:
- Opening a file from Yazi into Helix whose path is inside the current workspace/repo root keeps the workspace root unchanged.
- Opening or warping to an explicit different directory/project can still retarget intentionally.
- The Yazi-to-Helix handoff may use the file parent as editor-local context only when appropriate, but must not rewrite the Yazelix workspace root.
- Add focused regression coverage for nested in-repo Yazi-to-Helix opens and an intentional cross-root retarget case.
close_reason: Completed: Yazi-to-Helix file opens now retarget workspace state to the resolved editor workspace root, preserving Git repo roots for nested files; added focused regression coverage and updated the workspace-session contract.

title: Evaluate whether yzx cwd should be removed, narrowed, or kept
description: Evaluate whether the yzx cwd command still deserves to exist as a first-class command surface, and if so what narrower contract it should have.
design: Reason from user-facing intent rather than implementation habit. Determine what real job yzx cwd is doing today: tab naming, workspace retargeting, cwd synchronization, or a compatibility shim for older flows. Then compare that against overlapping surfaces and recent bugs. If the command is confusing or too easy to misuse, prefer narrowing or deleting it with a clear migration story rather than preserving it out of inertia.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit decision to keep, narrow, rename, or remove yzx cwd. The bead records the user-facing contract if it survives, or the replacement and migration story if it does not. Any decision accounts for overlaps with newer workspace and tab-cwd flows.
close_reason: Completed: keep yzx cwd as the explicit current-tab workspace-retarget command. The narrowed contract is not "generic navigation" or "open a new project tab"; it is tab-local workspace-root mutation plus managed editor/sidebar sync. Docs and command descriptions were tightened around that contract, while a future warp-style new-tab flow remains a separate feature idea rather than a replacement.

title: Move remaining generic config UI behavior into yazelix-ratconfig
description: Continue the ratconfig extraction by moving generic config UI reducer/edit/test/field-row behavior from the Yazelix adapter into the reusable yazelix-ratconfig child crate. Keep Yazelix-specific schema loading, Home Manager/native status, validation, file IO, keybinding registry policy, cursor registry composition, and runtime apply behavior in the main repo.
design: Delete-first boundary pass after the JSONC-first extraction. The child crate should own generic UI mechanics and reusable field-row construction where it can do so without importing Yazelix policy. Main should shrink to an adapter that handles product state and side effects. Do not move behavior just to move lines; reject any candidate that would make ratconfig know about Yazelix paths, commands, or apply modes.
acceptance_criteria: Main repo deletes additional generic config UI logic and tests while preserving config UI behavior. Child repo gains reusable reducer/intents and any neutral field-row helpers with project-agnostic tests. Yazelix consumes the pushed child revision through normal Cargo/Nix metadata. Focused child and main tests pass, validators pass, and LOC scorecards are recorded.
notes: 2026-05-22 Frankentui evaluation from yazelix-gnflf: the remaining ratconfig direction is hardening, not framework replacement. Do not reintroduce generic UI code into main or swap ratconfig onto a broad external runtime. If more work is needed, it should be narrow cleanup/snapshot/focus/a11y checks under yazelix-0ytzl.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Move generic config UI reducer and write intents into ratconfig
description: Move generic config UI input handling and edit-mode transitions out of the Yazelix adapter and into yazelix-ratconfig. Ratconfig should own keyboard reduction for navigation, search, bool toggle, scalar edit, single-select, multiselect, and generic save/unset requests. Yazelix should receive semantic intents and remain responsible for editability checks, JSONC writes, validation, reload, and runtime apply behavior.
design: Introduce a project-agnostic action/reducer API in the child crate. Avoid coupling it to crossterm if a small neutral action enum is enough. Main maps crossterm keys into the reducer and handles emitted intents. Preserve existing UI behavior and notices.
acceptance_criteria: Main config_ui/app.rs loses generic reducer/edit-mode logic and keeps terminal lifecycle plus Yazelix side effects. Child crate has project-agnostic tests covering navigation, search, bool quick edit, scalar single-select, multiselect, save, unset, cancel, and exit intents. Focused main config UI tests still pass.
close_reason: Moved generic reducer/intents, picker/parser tests, neutral field-row construction, and schema/UI metadata parsing into yazelix-ratconfig. Main now consumes published ratconfig 90b0609333b9c03395cff40366d1efec058a0ff1. Verification: child cargo test; main cargo check, config_ui tests, yzx_core_config_ui tests, repo validators, nix build .#runtime. LOC scorecard: runtime net -646, total code -600.

title: Move generic config UI behavior tests into ratconfig
description: After the generic reducer/intents move, relocate tests that defend generic picker, parser, and edit behavior from the Yazelix adapter into yazelix-ratconfig. Keep only Yazelix-specific tests in the main repo.
design: Delete duplicated main tests that only prove child-owned behavior. Preserve or rewrite main tests that defend Home Manager ownership, cursor composition, keybinding metadata, apply modes, stale diagnostics, and write/apply integration.
acceptance_criteria: Child tests cover generic picker/parser/reducer behavior. Main config_ui/tests.rs shrinks and no longer retests child-owned behavior except through high-value integration cases. Focused tests pass in both repos.
close_reason: Moved generic reducer/intents, picker/parser tests, neutral field-row construction, and schema/UI metadata parsing into yazelix-ratconfig. Main now consumes published ratconfig 90b0609333b9c03395cff40366d1efec058a0ff1. Verification: child cargo test; main cargo check, config_ui tests, yzx_core_config_ui tests, repo validators, nix build .#runtime. LOC scorecard: runtime net -646, total code -600.

title: Move neutral config field row construction into ratconfig
description: Move the neutral part of Yazelix's ConfigUiField row construction into yazelix-ratconfig behind a project-agnostic field spec. Yazelix should supply paths, kind, current/default values, descriptions, allowed values, validation, apply status, and edit behavior. Ratconfig should compute display state, rendered values, edit values, and invalid/defaulted/explicit classification where that logic is not Yazelix-specific.
design: Do not move schema loading, config diagnostics, cursor registry, Home Manager status, native status, keybinding expansion, runtime apply-mode choice, file IO, or validation. Accept the bead only if it deletes meaningful main adapter logic without making the child API awkward.
acceptance_criteria: Main model_builder.rs delegates neutral field-row composition to the child crate and shrinks. Child tests cover explicit/defaulted/unset/invalid state construction and edit/display values. Main config UI model tests continue to pass.
close_reason: Moved generic reducer/intents, picker/parser tests, neutral field-row construction, and schema/UI metadata parsing into yazelix-ratconfig. Main now consumes published ratconfig 90b0609333b9c03395cff40366d1efec058a0ff1. Verification: child cargo test; main cargo check, config_ui tests, yzx_core_config_ui tests, repo validators, nix build .#runtime. LOC scorecard: runtime net -646, total code -600.

title: Move generic schema and UI metadata parsing into ratconfig
description: Ratconfig should own project-neutral JSON Schema field discovery and config UI metadata parsing. Yazelix should keep file IO, CoreError wording, apply-mode mapping, cursor-specific enrichment, and runtime policy.
acceptance_criteria: Existing Yazelix config UI tests pass with behavior unchanged.
close_reason: Moved generic reducer/intents, picker/parser tests, neutral field-row construction, and schema/UI metadata parsing into yazelix-ratconfig. Main now consumes published ratconfig 90b0609333b9c03395cff40366d1efec058a0ff1. Verification: child cargo test; main cargo check, config_ui tests, yzx_core_config_ui tests, repo validators, nix build .#runtime. LOC scorecard: runtime net -646, total code -600.

title: Order settings.jsonc sections with cursors last
description: The generated settings.jsonc file puts the heavy cursors object near the top because JSON object serialization sorts keys lexicographically. Keep the public key as cursors, but render top-level settings sections in a deliberate human-readable order with cursors last.
design: Do not rename cursors or introduce a prefixed key just to influence alphabetical sorting. This is a rendering/readability issue, not a schema/model issue. Apply the same root-section order in Rust-generated files and Home Manager-generated files: core, helix, editor, shell, terminal, zellij, yazi, cursors. Keep nested object rendering handled by the existing JSON serializer.
acceptance_criteria: Rust-generated settings.jsonc prints cursors after yazi when both sections exist. Home Manager manage_config-generated settings.jsonc prints cursors last. The canonical schema and runtime key remain cursors with no migration or alias. Focused tests or eval checks defend the root order.
close_reason: Completed: Rust and Home Manager settings.jsonc renderers keep the public cursors key but place it after normal semantic sections; focused Rust and Nix eval checks passed.

title: Manually validate Yazelix installation and startup through Home Manager for the first time
description: Run a real first-time Home Manager install of Yazelix on a clean user setup, verify runtime/current ownership, desktop entry, yzx shim, launch flow, and managed config generation, then record any gaps between the documented Home Manager path and actual user experience.
design: Treat this as a real user-path validation bead, not as another flake-check proxy. The important thing is to exercise the documented Home Manager install story from a clean user perspective and record where setup, runtime ownership, generated configs, desktop entry integration, or launch behavior diverge from the intended contract. Keep the validation focused on the canonical Home Manager path rather than maintainer-only local shortcuts.
acceptance_criteria: There is a manual validation record for the Home Manager install/startup path covering first-time installation, runtime/current ownership, yzx wrapper behavior, generated config presence, desktop entry path, and successful launch. Any gaps found are either fixed or split into explicit follow-up beads, leaving the Home Manager story better defined than before.
notes:
Validation on 2026-04-08:
- Clean-room Home Manager switch against the root flake now succeeds without pre-existing ~/.bashrc or ~/.config/nushell/config.nu.
- Home Manager activation now materializes generated runtime-owned Yazi and Zellij config surfaces during switch.
- Verified runtime/current ownership, managed TOML surfaces, generated Zellij layouts, generated Yazi config, Home Manager-owned yzx profile path, successful `yzx doctor`, successful `yzx run which uv`, successful `yzx run which tokei`, and successful `yzx enter --skip-refresh` preflight in an isolated HOME.
close_reason: Completed

title: Make the Yazelix desktop entry stable across runtime updates
description: The desktop entry should not keep going stale whenever the installed runtime store path changes. Replace the current runtime-targeted desktop-entry contract with a stable launcher path so normal installs and updates do not require rerunning yzx desktop install just to keep the desktop entry working.
design: Treat the current stale-desktop-entry detection as a guardrail, not the final UX. The real contract problem is that the desktop file points at a specific runtime store path, so normal runtime updates can invalidate it. Replace that with a stable launcher surface such as ~/.local/bin/yzx or ~/.local/share/yazelix/runtime/current so desktop launches remain valid across installs and updates. Preserve clear diagnostics, but the goal is to stop needing repeated yzx desktop install repairs in normal use.
acceptance_criteria: A previously installed Yazelix desktop entry continues to work after normal runtime updates without needing yzx desktop install again. The desktop entry uses a stable launcher path instead of a specific runtime store path. Doctor and launch-time checks may still detect genuinely broken installs, but normal runtime churn should not make the desktop entry stale.
close_reason: Completed

title: Evaluate migrating TOML tooling from Taplo to Tombi
description: Evaluate whether Yazelix should replace Taplo with Tombi for TOML formatting, validation, editor/LSP guidance, and managed config-support packaging. Upstream check on 2026-04-27: Taplo's GitHub page does not show it as archived, but its latest listed Taplo CLI release is 0.10.0 from 2025-05-23; Tombi presents itself as a TOML formatter/linter/language server and lists latest release v0.9.24 from 2026-04-26.
design: Decision-first scope. Audit every Yazelix Taplo surface before changing dependencies: runtime_deps.nix, mk_runtime_tree exported commands, repo_source.nix, .taplo.toml shipping/sync, active_config_surface managed Taplo support, doctor/onboard messaging, docs, and tests. Compare Tombi CLI/LSP/config semantics against current Taplo behavior, especially formatter output stability on yazelix_default.toml, config_metadata/*.toml, docs/upgrade_notes.toml, and Home Manager examples. Prefer a migration only if Tombi can preserve or improve the maintained contract without broad formatter churn or weaker validation. If migration is chosen, rename managed support concepts deliberately instead of leaving Taplo-named paths in the product surface.
acceptance_criteria: Produce a written recommendation with evidence from upstream status, package availability in the locked nixpkgs input, CLI command parity, formatting diff size, schema/config support, LSP/editor impact, and package/runtime closure impact. Include a no-migration option if Taplo remains acceptable. If migration is recommended, file follow-up implementation beads with exact file scopes, compatibility notes, docs changes, and validation commands. Do not replace Taplo in the same bead.
notes: Decision recorded in docs/specs/toml_tooling_taplo_tombi_evaluation.md. Initial outcome was a staged migration, not a blind dependency swap. Follow-ups yazelix-zz0k.1, yazelix-zz0k.2, and yazelix-zz0k.3 are complete: the bounded Tombi formatter corpus is accepted, managed support naming is generic TOML tooling, the runtime now packages/exports Tombi, tombi.toml is the managed support config, and .taplo.toml is removed from the shipped source.
close_reason: Completed

title: Prototype Tombi config and formatting migration
description: Prototype a Tombi config and measure formatter churn across the repo TOML corpus without changing the shipped runtime dependency.
design: Work in a dedicated formatting/prototype bead. Compare Tombi against current repo state and current Taplo output, decide whether to accept a one-time TOML formatting commit, and document unsupported Taplo formatting knobs explicitly.
acceptance_criteria: Temporary or committed prototype clearly reports touched files and diff size; no runtime package dependency changes; recommendation says whether a later package swap is safe.
notes: Prototype completed. Added tombi.toml with a bounded Yazelix-owned TOML corpus: root TOML, config_metadata, docs/upgrade_notes.toml, and user_configs; excluded vendored Yazi flavors and Rust Cargo manifests. Applied Tombi formatting to the accepted corpus. Full default Tombi run would touch 36/49 tracked TOML files; configured corpus avoids unrelated vendor/Cargo churn. Verified tombi format --check, validate-specs, and git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed

title: Rename Taplo support to generic TOML tooling support
description: Rename Taplo-specific managed support concepts so Yazelix does not expose Taplo-shaped state names after a future TOML tooling swap.
design: Audit active_config_surface, doctor/onboard diagnostics, fixtures, docs, and runtime-tree support paths. Prefer generic TOML tooling support names while preserving explicit, actionable errors.
acceptance_criteria: Taplo-specific names remain only where the current tool is intentionally referenced; user-facing support paths and diagnostics are ready for a future Tombi swap without stale Taplo wording.
close_reason: Completed generic TOML tooling support rename; concrete runtime tool remains unchanged for the follow-up swap bead

title: Swap runtime TOML tool from Taplo to Tombi
description: Replace the shipped runtime TOML tool with Tombi after the formatter prototype and support-surface rename gates pass.
design: Scope packaging/runtime_deps.nix, packaging/mk_runtime_tree.nix, packaging/repo_source.nix, docs, package-size notes, managed support file shipping, and validation commands. Do not carry both Taplo and Tombi in the default runtime.
acceptance_criteria: Runtime package exports Tombi instead of Taplo; docs and diagnostics no longer claim Taplo support; package closure delta is recorded; formatter/lint commands are validated against the accepted repo TOML corpus.
close_reason: Completed runtime TOML tooling swap: Tombi is packaged/exported, tombi.toml is the managed config, .taplo.toml is removed, docs/specs updated, and runtime build verified
